stem:
A 14-month-old child with Kawasaki disease, treated with IVIG and aspirin, presents at age 18 months
for routine immunization. Which of the following vaccines should not be administered at this time?
explanation:
• High-dose IVIG (2 g/kg) used in Kawasaki disease provides passive antibodies that can neutralize
live attenuated vaccines.
• Varicella is a live vaccine and should be delayed for 11 months after IVIG.
• Inactivated vaccines (DTaP, Hep A, Hib) are not affected and should be given on schedule.
• Giving live vaccines during this window may result in ineffective immunization.
Clinical pearls:
• Defer MMR and Varicella for 11 months after IVIG administration.
• Document the exact date of IVIG to plan vaccine scheduling.
• No delay needed for inactivated vaccines post-IVIG.
• If live vaccine is given too soon, consider serologic testing or repeat vaccination.
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 536; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th E., p. 215; Osama Naga p. 207
☆ Infectious Diseases: Live Vaccine Timing After IVIG
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 536; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th E., p. 215; Osama Naga p. 207

stem: Which of the following is true regarding nocturnal enuresis in children?
explanation:
• Up to 70% of children with nocturnal enuresis have a positive family history, most often in
a parent who also experienced it
• Primary enuresis (child has never been dry) is much more common than secondary
• Boys are more commonly affected than girls (approx. 2:1 ratio)
• Many cases resolve spontaneously — not all require treatment, especially if child is <7 years and
asymptomatic
Clinical pearls:
• Strong genetic component in enuresis
• Primary monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis is the most common type
• Initiate treatment only if child is ≥5 years, and/or family is concerned
★ Nocturnal Enuresis:
• Definition
o Involuntary urination during sleep in child ≥5 years old
o Occurs ≥2 times/week for ≥3 months
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
Types
o Primary: never dry for >6 months
o Secondary: recurrence after 6 months of
dryness
Causes
o Delayed bladder maturation (most common)
o Nocturnal polyuria (↓ ADH at night)
o Small functional bladder capacity
o Constipation
o Psychological stress (especially in secondary)
o Sleep arousal disorder
More common in boys (ratio 2:1).
Family history plays a significant role:
o One enuretic parent → 44% chance in child.
o Two enuretic parents → 77% chance in child.
Spontaneous remission occurs at a rate of 15% per year.
Red Flags
o Daytime incontinence, urgency, frequency
o Poor urinary stream, dribbling
o Constipation, UTIs
o Abnormal gait or lumbosacral findings
Workup
o History + physical exam
o Urinalysis (rule out UTI, diabetes)
o Assess for constipation
o No imaging/labs unless red flags
First-Line Management
o Reassure parents — usually self-limited
o Voiding before bed
o Fluid restriction in evening
o Positive reinforcement
o Treat constipation if present
o Most Effective Treatment
 Enuresis alarm
• Long-term cure, behaviorally conditioned
• Use nightly for 3–6 months
o Pharmacologic Therapy
 Desmopressin (DDAVP)
• Quick response, high relapse if stopped
• Use for >6 yrs or special events (sleepovers)
 Monitor for hyponatremia
 Oxybutynin: only if daytime symptoms or detrusor overactivity
Referral
o Failure of therapy after 6 months
o Non-monosymptomatic enuresis
o Neurologic or anatomical concern
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 551; Pocket Pediatrics
☆ Nephrology: Nocturnal Enuresis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 551; Pocket Pediatrics

stem:
A 5-day-old neonate is brought by his mother due to poor feeding and lethargy. There is a positive
family history of metabolic disorders, and his older sibling died in the neonatal period. The mother
recalls that the sibling's urine had a strong odor resembling sweaty feet. What is the most likely
diagnosis?
explanation:
• Isovaleric acidemia (IVA) is caused by deficiency of isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase, leading to
accumulation of isovaleric acid.
• It presents in the first few days of life with poor feeding, vomiting, lethargy, and metabolic
acidosis.
• The hallmark is a "sweaty feet" odor, due to isovaleric acid excretion.
• Phenylketonuria (PKU) causes a musty/mousy odor, not sweaty feet.
• Propionic acidemia may resemble sepsis and cause severe metabolic
derangements but has no characteristic odor.
• Maple syrup urine disease (MSUD) features a sweet, maple syruplike urine odor and is associated with branched-chain ketoacid
dehydrogenase deficiency.
Clinical pearls:
• Sweaty feet odor is specific for Isovaleric acidemia.
• Always consider inborn errors of metabolism in neonates with lethargy, vomiting, and acidosis,
especially with positive family history.
• Initial labs: anion gap metabolic acidosis, hyperammonemia, ketonuria, and hypoglycemia.
• Treatment: protein restriction, glycine + carnitine supplementation, glucose infusion,
and hemodialysis in severe cases.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 97; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 50
☆ Genetic/Metabolic: Organic Acidemias
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 97; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 50

stem:
A 4-year-old child is admitted to the ICU with sepsis and purpura fulminans. Laboratory studies
confirm a diagnosis of disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). Which of the following
coagulation factors is characteristically low in DIC?
explanation:
• In DIC, there is widespread activation of coagulation with consumption of platelets and clotting
factors, including fibrinogen.
• Fibrinogen levels are typically decreased due to conversion to fibrin during ongoing intravascular
clotting.
• Factor VIII is often normal or elevated in DIC because it is an acute-phase reactant.
• Factor VII may be low but is not specific and is not the hallmark.
• Factor XIII is not routinely measured and is less relevant to the initial diagnosis or monitoring of DIC.
Clinical pearls:
• Low fibrinogen, thrombocytopenia, elevated PT/aPTT,
and elevated D-dimer are key diagnostic clues in DIC.
• DIC is a consumptive coagulopathy—look for bleeding +
clotting simultaneously.
• In neonates and children, sepsis (especially meningococcemia) is a leading cause of DIC.
• Treatment includes managing the underlying cause, and supportive care with FFP,
cryoprecipitate, and platelets as needed.
High-Yield Labs in Coagulopathies:
• Isolated ↑ PT → Think early vitamin K deficiency or Factor VII deficiency.
• Isolated ↑ aPTT → Think Hemophilia A/B, vWD (↑ bleeding time), or lupus anticoagulant.
• ↑ PT + ↑ aPTT + ↓ fibrinogen + ↑ D-dimer → Classic for DIC.
• Low platelets only, normal PT/aPTT → Think ITP.
• Mixing test corrects aPTT → Suggests factor deficiency (e.g., hemophilia).
• Mixing test fails to correct aPTT → Suggests inhibitor (e.g., lupus anticoagulant).
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 417; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide, p. 178
☆ Hematology: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 417; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide, p. 178

stem: Which of the following is true about the test shown below?

options.label: A
options.text: It is performed using an intradermal injection

options.label: B
options.text: It is the gold standard for diagnosing food allergies

options.label: C
options.text: A positive test alone confirms clinical allergy

options.label: D
options.text: It is a useful screening test for IgE-mediated allergic conditions

answer: D. It is a useful screening test for IgE-mediated allergic conditions
explanation:
• Skin prick testing is a percutaneous (not intradermal) test used to detect IgE-mediated
hypersensitivity to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, animal dander, and foods.
• Oral food challenge is the gold standard for food allergy.
• A positive test only indicates sensitization, not clinical
allergy — correlation with clinical history is essential.
Clinical pearls:
• Skin prick tests are quick, safe, and widely used in allergy
clinics.
• Antihistamines should be stopped at least 72 hours before
testing.
• False positives and negatives can occur; interpretation
must be clinically guided.
• Intradermal testing is more sensitive but has higher
false-positive rates and is used for drug or venom allergy.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1175–1176;
UpToDate, “Overview of allergy testing; Pocket Pediatrics 2024
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Allergy Testing
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1175–1176;

stem:
A 14-year-old girl presents with 4 months of weight loss, amenorrhea, and lanugo hair. On
examination, her heart rate is 40 bpm. What is she likely to have?
explanation:
• Anorexia nervosa is characterized by low body weight (BMI<18.5 kg/m2), fear of becoming fat, and
distorted body image. Unlike other patterns of restrictive eating (eg, bulimia nervosa), patients with
anorexia nervosa experience complications of progressive clinical starvation, such as bradycardia,
osteoporosis, and amenorrhea.
• This is a classic presentation of anorexia nervosa: weight loss, bradycardia, hypothermia, lanugo
hair, and amenorrhea
• Bradycardia (HR 40) is common due to increased vagal tone and electrolyte disturbances
• Patients are at risk for prolonged QT due to hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and malnutrition
→ Risk of torsades de pointes and sudden death
Remember in an ECG:
1 small box = 0.04 sec (40 ms)
1 large box = 0.20 sec (200
ms) = 5 small boxes
5 large boxes = 1 sec
ECG in a patient with anorexia showing severe prolonged QTc
- Sinus rhythm, HR 59 bpm, QT interval 0.7s (700 ms)
How to read QT interval on ECG:
- Identify lead II or V5/V6 — use the one
with a clearly defined T-wave
- Measure QT (in ms): From start of QRS
to end of T wave (not the U wave)
- Measure RR interval (peak of one R to next
R), in seconds
- Apply Bazett’s formula (corrected QT):
QTc = QT / √RR
→ QTc < 440 ms (<2.2 large boxes) = normal
→ QTc 440–460 ms = borderline
→ QTc > 460 ms = prolonged (in M&F)
→ QTc ≥ 500 ms = high risk for torsades
Clinical pearls:
• Anorexia nervosa can lead to cardiac complications, including bradycardia, hypotension, QT
prolongation, and pericardial effusion
• EKG is essential in evaluation — check for QTc >460–470 ms (>2.3 big boxes)
• Cardiac instability (bradycardia, prolonged QT, orthostasis) =
indication for inpatient admission
• Monitor electrolytes and initiate nutritional rehabilitation
cautiously to avoid refeeding syndrome
• Right axis deviation is not a typical feature in anorexia and would
be more suggestive of congenital or pulmonary cardiac pathology
• Short PR interval – more commonly seen in pre-excitation
syndromes (e.g., WPW)
• Left ventricular hypertrophy – rare in malnourished patients,
more in athletes or HTN
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 395
☆ Adolescent Medicine: Eating Disorders
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 395

stem:
A 9-year-old child presents with diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) and is admitted for management. As part
of the risk assessment for cerebral edema, which of the following is the most significant risk factor?
explanation:
Cerebral edema is the most feared complication of DKA in children. The strongest
independent risk factors associated with the development of cerebral edema in DKA include:
• High blood urea nitrogen (BUN) at presentation — marker of severe dehydration.
• Low PaCO₂, indicating more severe acidosis and potential for rapid shifts.
• Age <5yrs is a recognized risk factor, but elevated BUN is more specific and predictive.
As per UpToDate:
★ Diabetic Ketoacidosis: (very high yield for wards & boards)
• Approximately 25% of children with T1DM present in diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA).
• Pathophysiology: Triggered by inadequate insulin/increased
insulin need (stress from infection, steroids, etc.)
o ↓ insulin → glycogenolysis + gluconeogenesis + lipolysis
o ↑ glucose → polyuria → dehydration
o ↑ lipolysis leads to creation of ketone bodies, if ↑↑ → ↓
pH → electrolyte abnormalities
• Patients with DKA present initially with polyuria, polydipsia,
nausea, and vomiting.
o Abdominal pain occurs frequently and can mimic an
acute abdomen.
o The abdomen may be tender from vomiting or
distended secondary to a paralytic ileus.
• Diagnosis: Glucose >200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L); Moderate–
large ketones in urine, betahydroxybutyrate in blood; AGAP metabolic acidosis
General principles of DKA Management:
• Initial labs: glucose, VBG, Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, HCO₃⁻, BUN, Cr, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, phosphate, urinalysis (ketones)
• Monitor: neuro status, vitals hourly, glucose q1hr & electrolytes q2–4h
• Start with fluids — insulin comes after volume resuscitation begins
o 1st start w/ a NS bolus 10 mg/kg
o 2nd continuous IV fluids at 1.5–2× maintenance (to rehydrate and avoid dehydration)
 DO NOT USE HYPOTONIC FLUIDS – cerebral edema risk
 ↑ dextrose-containing fluids once blood glucose <300 and ↓ nondextrose fluids
• Insulin infusion – Regular insulin (0.1 u/kg/hr) – start 1 hr after initial NS bolus
o Goal to ↓ glucose by 50–100 mg/dL/hr (2.8–5.6 mmol/L/hr)
• At risk of initial hypokalemia and hypophosphatemia 2º to urine losses, hypernatremia 2º to dehydration
(may present as pseudohyponatremia)
o insulin shifts K⁺ and PO4 shift → hypokalemia & hypophosphatemia risk [total body depletion]
o K added to IV fluids [typically 20 mmol/L potassium phosphate + 20 mmol/L potassium chloride or
acetate]
o PO4 replaced only if symptomatic (e.g., muscle weakness, arrhythmia, hemolysis)
• Avoid bicarbonate unless pH <6.9 with life-threatening instability – risk of cerebral edema
Mild DKA: pH 7.2 – <7.3, HCO3 10 – <15
Usually well-appearing, alert
Fluids:
10–20 mL/kg NS over 1–2 hr
Maintenance + deficit (5%) over 36–
48 hrs
Insulin:
Moderate DKA: pH 7.1 – <7.2, HCO3 5 – <10
More dehydration, possible lethargy
Fluids:
10–20 mL/kg NS over 1–2 hr
Maintenance + 7–8% deficit over 48 hrs
Insulin:
Start 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr IV regular
insulin 1 hour after fluids
Severe DKA: pH <7.1, HCO3 <5
High risk for cerebral edema, may be
obtunded or confused
Fluids:
10–20 mL/kg NS over 1–2 hr
Replace 10% deficit over 48 hrs,
using 0.9% or 0.45% NaCl
depending on corrected Na⁺
-
Start 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr IV
regular insulin 1 hour after fluids
Potassium: Add once urine output
confirmed
Glucose: Add D5% when BG < 250–300
mg/dL (13.9–16.7 mmol/L)
Potassium: Monitor K⁺ closely — total body
K⁺ is low even if serum is normal
Glucose: Add D5% when BG < 250–300
mg/dL (13.9–16.7 mmol/L)
Insulin:
Start 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr IV
regular insulin 1 hour after fluids
Potassium: Monitor K⁺ closely — total
body K⁺ is low even if serum is normal
Glucose: Add D5% when BG < 250–
300 mg/dL (13.9–16.7 mmol/L)
Avoid bicarbonate unless pH <6.9
with cardiac instability or coma
Complications:
• Cerebral edema (0.5% to 1%)
o Signs/Sx: Headache, incontinence, altered mental status, emesis, hypertension, bradycardia, irregular
respirations
o Risk factors: age <5 yo, severe acidosis (pH <7.1, bicarb <5), severe dehydration (elevated BUN), severe
hyperosmolarity (corrected Na >155, glucose >1,000mg/dL [55.5 mmol/L]), longer duration of sx, new-onset
diabetes, aggressive initial fluid resuscitation, treatment with bicarbonate
o Management: Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg intravenously over 20 min or hypertonic saline (3% NS) 5–10 mL/kg over 30
min
 repeated if there is no response in 30 minutes
o Respiratory compromise – Rare respiratory failure
• Electrolyte abnormalities
o Cardiac dysrhythmias
o Rapid correction has risk for CNS fluid shifts
When is the patient considered out of DKA?
• Venous pH > 7.3
• Bicarbonate ≥ 15 mEq/L
• No ketonuria or ketonemia
• Alert mental status
• Hemodynamically stable and well hydrated
• Tolerating oral intake
Next steps:
• Start subcutaneous insulin once DKA has
resolved and the child is eating
• Give SC insulin 30–60 minutes before stopping
the insulin drip to avoid rebound hyperglycemia
DKA mnemonic: The most important findings of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) are: Delirium/psychosis,
Dehydration, Kussmaul respirations, Abdominal pain/nausea/vomiting, fruity (Acetone) breath odor.
Clinical pearls:
• Always start DKA management with isotonic fluid bolus (10–20 mL/kg) based on severity
• Do not give bicarbonate unless pH <6.9 with life-threatening symptoms (e.g., arrhythmia)
• Replace deficit + maintenance over 36–48 hours; add insulin only after 1–2 hours of fluids
• Monitor glucose, electrolytes, pH, and mental status closely for cerebral edema
• Most cases of DKA-related cerebral edema occur 4–12 hours after therapy begins.
• Early signs include headache, irritability, vomiting, and altered mental status.
• Risk factors: Severe acidosis at presentation, Substantially elevated BUN at presentation,
Severe hypocapnia
o Young child (<5 years) and/or new onset of diabetes are not independent risk factors but
are markers for more severe DKA because they are associated with delayed diagnosis
• Rapid fluid administration or bicarbonate use may increase risk.
• Suspected cerebral edema should be treated empirically with hypertonic saline (3%) or mannitol,
without delay.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 264–265; Osama Naga 3rd ed., p. 173; Oncall
Pediatrics; Pocket Pediatrics 2024; UpToDate: Diabetic ketoacidosis in children: Cerebral edema
☆ Endocrinology: Diabetic Ketoacidosis – Cerebral Edema
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 264–265; Osama Naga 3rd ed., p. 173; Oncall

stem:
A previously healthy 10-year-old presents with fever, cough, and anosmia during a global respiratory
illness outbreak. A nasopharyngeal PCR test confirms infection with the virus responsible for the
COVID-19 pandemic. Which of the following pathogens is the causative agent of COVID-19?
explanation:
• SARS-CoV-2 (Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2) is the virus responsible for the
global COVID-19 pandemic, first identified in late 2019.
• SARS-CoV-1 caused the SARS outbreak in 2002–2003 but is not responsible for COVID-19.
• H1N1 Influenza A caused the 2009 swine flu pandemic and is a completely different virus
(orthomyxovirus).
• MERS-CoV (Middle East Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus) emerged in 2012 but is not related to
COVID-19 epidemiologically.
Clinical pearls:
• SARS-CoV-2 is a single-stranded RNA virus from the coronavirus family.
• Pediatric cases often have milder symptoms, but watch for MIS-C (Multisystem Inflammatory
Syndrome in Children).
• Most common symptoms in children: fever, cough, sore throat, and anosmia.
• PCR from nasopharyngeal swab is the gold standard for diagnosis.
• Vaccination against COVID-19 is now approved for pediatric age groups ≥6 months (age group
may vary by country).
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 186–188; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, 3rd ed., p. 126
☆ Infectious Diseases: COVID-19
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 186–188; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, 3rd ed., p. 126

stem:
A 3-year-old boy presents with a history of recurrent oral thrush, recurrent otitis media, and
sinusitis. On examination, he has dysmorphic facial features and a thoracotomy scar. There is no
family history of immunodeficiency. Which of the following immunologic abnormalities is most likely
associated with this child’s condition?
explanation:
This is a classic presentation of DiGeorge syndrome (22q11.2 deletion syndrome).
• DiGeorge syndrome results from a 22q11.2 deletion causing failure of development of the 3rd
and 4th pharyngeal pouches
• About 90% of cases are de novo mutations, meaning they occur spontaneously without a family
history. 10% of cases are inherited, and when they are, it follows an autosomal dominant pattern
• Key Clinical Features (mnemonic: CATCH-22):
o Cardiac defects → conotruncal anomalies (e.g., tetralogy of Fallot, truncus arteriosus,
VSD) → explains thoracotomy scar
o Abnormal facies → low-set ears, micrognathia
o Thymic hypoplasia → ↓ T-cells, recurrent viral/fungal infections (oral thrush)
o Cleft palate
o Hypocalcemia → due to parathyroid hypoplasia → seizures
•
•
•
•
o 22q11.2 deletion
Immunologic findings:
o ↓ or absent T cells, normal or reduced B cells
 Thymus is essential for T-cell maturation; absence leads to increased risk of viral
and fungal infections
 B-cell numbers are typically normal, but antibody production may be reduced due
to lack of T-cell help
o Recurrent infections begin in infancy (esp. viral, fungal, Pneumocystis)
Other associated features:
o Speech delay, learning disabilities, psychiatric disorders (e.g., schizophrenia risk later in life)
Diagnosis:
o FISH or microarray to detect 22q11.2 deletion
o CXR may show absent thymic shadow
Management:
o Calcium and vitamin D for hypocalcemia
o Avoid live vaccines if severe T-cell deficiency
o Severe immunodeficiency → consider thymic transplant or HSCT
o Early developmental & speech therapy
Clinical pearls:
• Suspect DiGeorge in infants with hypocalcemic seizures, cardiac anomalies, facial
dysmorphism, and chronic infections.
• May also present with speech delay, learning difficulties, and psychiatric disorders in later life.
• Infections begin early and are mostly viral, fungal, or Pneumocystis jirovecii
• Diagnosis confirmed via FISH for 22q11.2 deletion
• Live vaccines are contraindicated if T-cell deficiency is severe
• Severe cases may require thymic transplant or bone marrow transplant
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 542–543
☆ Allergy & Immunology: DiGeorge Syndrome (22q11.2 Deletion)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 542–543

stem:
A child known to have shrimp allergy is scheduled to undergo CT imaging with iodinated contrast,
and the parents express concern about a possible allergic reaction to the contrast due to the
seafood allergy. What is the most appropriate management?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassurance, as the allergy risk is negligible

options.label: B
options.text: Perform allergic test for the contrast material

answer: A. Reassurance, as the allergy risk is negligible
explanation:
• There is no immunologic cross-reactivity between shellfish allergy and iodinated contrast
media. The belief that iodine in seafood is linked to contrast allergies is incorrect; iodine is not an
allergen. Shellfish allergies are to tropomyosin (a muscle protein), not iodine.
• The risk of contrast reaction in patients with seafood allergy is no higher than in the general
population, and this has been clinically proven.
• Premedication or skin testing is unnecessary unless the patient has a history of prior contrastinduced reaction.
Clinical Pearls:
• Shellfish or iodine allergy is not a contraindication to iodinated contrast.
• Contrast reactions are non-IgE-mediated and idiosyncratic.
• True contrast allergy warrants premedication with corticosteroids and antihistamines (e.g.,
prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg 13, 7, and 1 hour prior + diphenhydramine 1 hour before).
• Low-osmolar or iso-osmolar contrast agents reduce reaction risk.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2696; UpToDate: Patient evaluation prior to oral or
iodinated IV contrast for CT
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Contrast Allergy
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2696; UpToDate: Patient evaluation prior to oral or

stem:
A 7-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with new-onset confusion, lethargy, and
difficulty walking for the past day. His symptoms started five days after a viral upper respiratory tract
infection. On examination, he is febrile, has bilateral lower extremity weakness, and shows
hyperreflexia. MRI of the brain reveals multifocal hyperintense lesions in the white matter on T2weighted images. Cerebrospinal fluid shows lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein.
What is the underlying cause of this condition?
explanation:
• Acute Disseminated Encephalomyelitis (ADEM) is an immune-mediated demyelinating disorder
• Typically follows a viral infection or vaccination (1–3 weeks prior)
o Often follows measles, influenza, or EBV, or after MMR or DTP vaccination
• Caused by autoimmune attack on CNS myelin, likely due to molecular mimicry
• Clinical signs: encephalopathy, ataxia, weakness, seizures, optic neuritis
• Diagnosis:
o MRI shows diffuse, bilateral, patchy white matter lesions
o CSF: mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, ↑ protein, normal glucose
o Negative infectious and autoimmune workup
o No oligoclonal bands (vs MS)
• Management:
o High-dose IV methylprednisolone x 3–5 days
o If refractory: IVIG or plasmapheresis
o Supportive care: manage seizures, hydration, ICU if needed
• Prognosis:
o Good in most cases — complete recovery in weeks
o Rarely, children may have residual neurologic deficits or recurrent episodes (multiphasic
ADEM)
Clinical pearls:
• Always consider ADEM in child with new neurologic symptoms + recent illness or vaccine
• Must differentiate from multiple sclerosis (MS rarely presents with encephalopathy)
• MRI = best diagnostic tool
•
•
CSF may show mild lymphocytic pleocytosis and ↑ protein
Viral encephalitis can mimic ADEM but is more likely to present with fever, seizures, and focal
deficits. MRI is often localized, not multifocal white matter lesions.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 275–276
☆ Neurology: Demyelinating Disorders
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 275–276

stem:
A 7 year old boy with cystic fibrosis presents with recurrent pneumonia and chronic productive
cough. A sputum culture is obtained to evaluate the causative pathogen. What is the most common
organism responsible for pulmonary infections in this child?
explanation:
• As cystic fibrosis progresses, Pseudomonas aeruginosa
becomes the dominant pathogen in the respiratory tract,
especially after early childhood.
• Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA): most common
respiratory pathogen in children with CF under 5 years of age.
• Pseudomonas aeruginosa: becomes most common after age
5, and clearly dominant after age 10.
o Pseudomonas is associated with chronic infection,
lung damage, and increased morbidity and mortality in
older children and adolescents with CF.
•
H. influenzae is common in early CF but less so with age.
• S. pneumoniae is not a common CF pathogen.
Cystic fibrosis presenting signs mnemonic: CF PANCREAS
Chronic cough and wheezing
★ Cystic Fibrosis:
Failure to thrive
• Autosomal recessive, mutation in CFTR gene
Pancreatic insufficiency (malabsorption, steatorrhea)
Alkalosis and hypotonic dehydration
(Chr 7), most common = ΔF508.
Neonatal intestinal obstruction (meconium ileus)/ Nasal polyps
• CFTR defect → impaired chloride transport
Clubbing of fingers/ Chest radiograph with characteristic changes
→ thick, dehydrated secretions.
Rectal prolapse
Electrolyte elevation in sweat, salty skin
• Newborns: suspect with meconium
Absence or congenital atresia of vas deferens
ileus (pathognomonic – bowel obstruction +
Sputum with Staph or Pseudomonas (mucoid)
ground-glass on AXR).
• Pulmonary symptoms: chronic cough, recurrent pneumonia, bronchiectasis, digital clubbing.
• Early colonization: Staph aureus (infancy/early childhood).
• Later colonization: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (mid-childhood/adolescence), Burkholderia
cepacia and Stenotrophomonas maltophilia (severe prognosis).
• GI: pancreatic insufficiency → steatorrhea, failure to thrive, ADEK vitamin deficiency.
• Intestinal: DIOS (Distal Intestinal Obstruction Syndrome) = recurrent obstruction in older children.
• Liver: focal biliary cirrhosis → portal hypertension.
• Reproductive: azoospermia/infertility in males (absent vas deferens), thick cervical mucus in
females.
• Electrolytes: hypochloremic, hyponatremic metabolic alkalosis (esp. in hot weather).
• Diagnosis:
o Sweat chloride ≥60 mmol/L (gold standard)
o 2 pathogenic CFTR mutations
o Nasal potential difference (if equivocal)
• Treatment:
o Airway clearance: chest physiotherapy, hypertonic saline, dornase alfa (DNAse).
o Antibiotics:
•
•
 Early: anti-staphylococcal (e.g., cephalexin, TMP-SMX).
 Chronic Pseudomonas: inhaled tobramycin, aztreonam.
o CFTR modulators:
 Ivacaftor (G551D),
 Lumacaftor/Ivacaftor (ΔF508 homozygous),
 Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (Trikafta – ΔF508 hetero/homo).
o PERT + ADEK vitamins.
Complications: hemoptysis, pneumothorax, CF-related diabetes (CFRD), osteopenia.
Surveillance: annual OGTT (CFRD), DEXA (bone health), sputum cultures (track colonization), liver
US.
Clinical Pearls:
• Pseudomonas colonization in CF is linked to worse outcomes and requires early eradication.
• Chronic Pseudomonas infection may require inhaled tobramycin, aztreonam, or ciprofloxacin.
• Burkholderia cepacia and Stenotrophomonas maltophilia are less common but indicate poor
prognosis.
• Airway clearance techniques and regular sputum cultures are crucial in CF management.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1496–1499; UWorld; UpToDate: “Cystic fibrosis:
Microbiology and treatment of lung disease”
☆ Pulmonology: Cystic Fibrosis
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1496–1499; UWorld; UpToDate: “Cystic fibrosis:

stem:
A 6-year-old child is identified during a school TB screening. He has a positive PPD (tuberculin skin
test) but is asymptomatic and his chest X-ray is normal. There is no known contact with an active TB
case. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• A positive PPD with negative chest X-ray and no symptoms indicates latent tuberculosis
infection (LTBI).
• Children <5 years are at higher risk of progression to active TB, so treatment is always
indicated even if asymptomatic.
• The recommended treatment is Isoniazid (INH) 10 mg/kg/day (max 300 mg) for 9 months.
• Reassurance alone is inappropriate due to risk of progression.
• Repeating the PPD is not indicated if already positive.
• RIPE therapy is reserved for active TB, which this child does not have.
★ Pediatric Tuberculosis:
• Transmission & Risk Factors
o Spread via airborne droplets, usually from a household contact
o Infants and children <5 yrs are at highest risk for progression to active disease
• TB Testing: PPD vs IGRA
o PPD (Mantoux)
 Positive if ≥10 mm in high-risk children (e.g., close contacts, age <4, foreign-born)
 Positive if ≥5 mm in immunocompromised, close contacts, or abnormal CXR
IGRA (Quantiferon)
 Preferred in BCG-vaccinated children ≥2 years
 Not reliable in <2 years
 Does not distinguish active vs latent TB
Clinical Features of Active TB
o Pulmonary TB
 Chronic cough, weight loss, fever, fatigue
 Hilar lymphadenopathy, pleural effusion on imaging
o Extrapulmonary TB
 CNS (meningitis), miliary TB, lymph nodes, bones (Pott
disease)
 Infants and toddlers are at high risk for disseminated TB
Diagnosis of Active TB
o Start with chest X-ray
o Sputum AFB smear/culture if age ≥10 or able to expectorate
o Gastric aspirates x3 if <10 years old
o Consider CSF or lymph node biopsy if extrapulmonary TB
o Always screen for HIV
Latent TB Infection (LTBI)
o Defined as positive PPD or IGRA, no symptoms, normal CXR
o Treatment options
 INH x 9 months
 Rifampin x 4 months
 INH + Rifapentine weekly x 3 months (if age >2 yrs, HIV-negative)
Treatment of Active TB
o RIPE for 2 months: Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol
o
•
•
•
•
o
o
o
•
Then INH + Rifampin for 4 months
Total treatment: 6 months, extend to 9–12 months for CNS or bone TB
Use Directly Observed Therapy (DOT) to ensure adherence
Mothers with TB can breastfeed using expressed milk only
Clinical pearls:
• Positive PPD in children ≥ 5 years: ≥ 10 mm
• Positive PPD in children < 5 years or high-risk (e.g., contacts, immunocompromised): ≥ 5 mm
• Always treat LTBI in children <5 years with a positive TST and negative CXR.
• Preferred regimen: INH for 9 months, or Rifampin daily for 4 months as an alternative.
• Monitor for hepatotoxicity, especially in adolescents and with long courses.
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 821–822; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 399; Osama Naga
Pediatric Board Review, 3rd ed., p. 223
☆ Infectious Diseases: Latent Tuberculosis Infection (LTBI)
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 821–822; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 399; Osama Naga

stem:
A newborn boy presents with bilateral choanal atresia, micropenis with cryptorchidism, and
dysmorphic ears. A cardiac murmur is noted on examination. Based on the suspected syndrome,
what congenital heart defect is most likely associated?
explanation:
This infant’s presentation suggests CHARGE syndrome, which includes:
• Coloboma
• Heart defects
• Atresia of the choanae
• Retarded growth/development
• Genital anomalies (e.g., micropenis, undescended testes)
• Ear anomalies
® Among cardiac defects, conotruncal anomalies (including tetralogy of Fallot, truncus arteriosus,
and double outlet right ventricle) are the most common in CHARGE syndrome (31–42%). These
defects result from abnormal development of the cardiac outflow tract.
® Atrioventricular septal defects (AVSDs; 13–17%) with associated or isolated PDA and aortic arch
abnormalities are also seen.
Clinical pearls:
• Conotruncal defects are strongly associated with CHARGE, DiGeorge, and 22q11 deletions.
• Suspect CHARGE in neonates with choanal atresia, ear anomalies, and genital abnormalities.
• Diagnosis is confirmed by CHD7 gene mutation testing.
• Bilateral choanal atresia is a neonatal emergency due to obligate nasal breathing.
📚 Source: PubMed: Congenital heart defects in CHARGE syndrome
☆ Genetics: CHARGE Syndrome and Congenital Heart Disease
source: PubMed: Congenital heart defects in CHARGE syndrome
topic: CHARGE Syndrome and Congenital Heart Disease

stem:
A15-year-old adolescent girl presents with significant weight loss. She reports excessive exercise,
restricts food intake, and has a fear of gaining weight, believing she is overeating. On exam, her BMI
is 16, temperature is 35.8°C, heart rate is 54 bpm, and blood pressure drops from 94/60 (supine) to
73/48 (standing). Which of the following findings is the most immediate indication for hospital
admission?
explanation:
• Orthostatic hypotension, defined as a drop in systolic BP ≥20 mmHg or
diastolic ≥10 mmHg, especially with symptoms, is a sign of hemodynamic
instability in anorexia nervosa, and is a clear admission criterion.
• Bradycardia (<50 bpm in adolescents) and hypothermia (<36°C) are also
concerning, but hemodynamic compromise (i.e., orthostatic changes) is
more acutely life-threatening.
• Low BMI or weight loss alone, without systemic compromise, does not
mandate hospitalization unless BMI <14 or rapid weight loss occurs.
Clinical pearls:
• Hospitalization in anorexia nervosa is indicated for:
o Orthostatic hypotension (drop in BP or HR with position change)
o Bradycardia (<50 bpm daytime, or <45 bpm at night)
o Hypothermia (<36°C)
o Electrolyte disturbances, arrhythmias
o BMI <14 or acute weight loss (>1 kg/week)
• Adolescents with vital sign instability are at risk of sudden cardiac death due to electrolyte
imbalance and cardiac atrophy.
• Early multidisciplinary intervention improves long-term outcomes.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 87; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 334
☆ Adolescent Medicine: Anorexia Nervosa and Admission Criteria
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 87; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 334

stem:
A 12-year-old boy presents with a 6-month history of progressive dysphagia, initially to solids and
now to liquids. He has episodes of regurgitation, weight loss, and nocturnal coughing. On physical
exam, he is thin but stable, and halitosis is noted. A barium swallow is performed and shown below.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Achalasia is a primary esophageal motility disorder caused by impaired
relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) and loss of peristalsis in
the esophageal body.
• The bird-beak sign on barium swallow is pathognomonic.
• Children typically present with progressive dysphagia, regurgitation, weight
loss, and possibly aspiration pneumonia.
Clinical pearls:
• Gold standard diagnosis: Esophageal manometry (shows absence of peristalsis and incomplete
LES relaxation).
• Common in children with Allgrove syndrome (triple A syndrome): achalasia, alacrima, adrenal
insufficiency.
•
•
Treatment: Heller myotomy or pneumatic dilation; botulinum toxin is temporary.
Differentiate from GERD: GERD typically presents with heartburn, crying with feeds, and
epigastric pain.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1966–1968; First Aid for the USMLE; UWorld
☆ Gastroenterology: Achalasia
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1966–1968; First Aid for the USMLE; UWorld

stem:
A 9-year-old boy is admitted with signs of acute renal failure following severe dehydration and prolonged
NSAID use. Labs reveal decreased urine output, elevated BUN and creatinine. Which of the following is
the most common electrolyte abnormality in acute renal failure?
explanation:
• In acute renal failure (ARF), the kidneys lose the ability to effectively excrete potassium,
making hyperkalemia the most common and dangerous electrolyte abnormality. Potassium builds
up quickly in oliguric or anuric phases of renal failure, risking arrhythmias.
• Hypercalcemia is uncommon; calcium tends to be low or normal.
• Hypophosphatemia is more typical in refeeding syndrome or DKA recovery, not ARF.
• Hypernatremia is possible but less common; more likely in dehydration without ARF.
Clinical Pearls:
• Monitor ECG in all patients with suspected hyperkalemia.
• Management includes calcium gluconate, insulin with glucose, β-agonists, and dialysis if severe.
• Other ARF complications: metabolic acidosis, hyperphosphatemia, hypocalcemia.
When K+ shifts out of the cell (hyperK), it's a BAD LOSS!
– Beta blockers, Acidosis, Digoxin,
Lysis, hyperOsmolality, high Sugar, Succinylcholine
Degree of hyperkalemia
Potassium level (mmol/L)
Mild
5.3 – 6.0
Moderate
6.0 – 6.9
Severe
≥ 7.0
Hyperkalemia management:
C BIG K DROP
Calcium gluconate
Beta agonists
Insulin + Glucose
Kayexalate
Diuretics (furosemide)
Removal Of Potassium
(dialysis)
Tall, symmetrically peaked T waves.
This patient had a serum K+ of 7.0.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2462–2464; UWorld; ECG Library: Hyperkalemia
☆ Nephrology: Acute Kidney Injury, Hyperkalemia
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2462–2464; UWorld; ECG Library: Hyperkalemia

stem:
A 5-month-old preterm infant who is exclusively breastfed and has not received any supplements
presents for routine labs. Investigations show:
- Calcium: Normal
- Phosphate: Normal
- ALP: 1200 U/L
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Osteopenia of prematurity (OOP) is common in preterm infants, especially those not receiving
mineral or vitamin D supplementation.
• ALP > 800–1000 U/L is a sensitive early marker of bone turnover and suggests metabolic bone
disease in neonates.
• Osteopenia of prematurity (metabolic bone disease of prematurity) typically presents
in preterm infants due to inadequate mineral stores and insufficient calcium and phosphorus
intake, especially in exclusively breastfed infants not receiving supplementation
• Lab findings:
o Normal calcium and phosphate early
o Elevated alkaline phosphatase (>800–1000 U/L) is the earliest and most sensitive
marker
• Vitamin D deficiency often shows low calcium/phosphorus
• Transient hypophosphatemia is usually seen acutely in refeeding syndrome or early postnatal
days
•
Familial hypophosphatemia presents with low phosphate and normal/elevated ALP, but not
typically in this setting
Clinical pearls:
• Supplement preterm infants with calcium, phosphorus, and vitamin D to prevent metabolic
bone disease
• Monitor ALP and radiographs to assess severity
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 233
☆ Neonatology / Endocrinology: Osteopenia of Prematurity
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 233

stem:
A 14 years old girl with painless breast mass that increases in size during menses. On examination:
Mass is firm, non-tender. What is most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
A fibroadenoma is the most common benign breast mass in adolescent girls.
• Painless, mobile, firm, rubbery mass
• Non-tender and well-circumscribed
• Increases slightly in size premenstrually due to hormonal sensitivity
• Common in teens and early 20s
• No skin changes or nipple discharge
• Often found incidentally during self-exam or routine check
• Ultrasound = first-line imaging
• Observe 4–6 months if <5 cm and classic features
• Biopsy if >5 cm, rapid growth, or atypical findings
• Regresses spontaneously in many adolescents
• Surgical excision for giant fibroadenoma or phyllodes tumor suspicion
Other options:
• Fibrocystic changes → More common in older reproductive-age women, usually presents
with bilateral breast pain and multiple cystic lesions.
• Adenoma → Less common and usually difficult to distinguish clinically from fibroadenoma.
• Malignancy → Rare in this age group; would be hard, fixed, with skin/nipple changes, and
sometimes with systemic signs.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 805; Osama Naga Pediatrics Board Review 2024,
Breast Disorders Section
☆ Adolescent Medicine: Benign Breast Disorders
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 805; Osama Naga Pediatrics Board Review 2024,

stem:
A DNR-designated neonate with a known terminal condition is not to undergo invasive procedures. The
baby experiences severe pain and is given morphine, after which he develops respiratory failure. What
is the most appropriate next step?
explanation:
• In the context of a Do Not Resuscitate (DNR) order and palliative care, the focus is on comfort
measures, not life-prolonging interventions. The use of morphine in terminal care is accepted even
•
•
•
•
if it hastens death, under the principle of double effect — the intent is pain relief, not euthanasia.
Since the DNR order excludes invasive interventions (such as intubation), and respiratory
depression is a known and acceptable risk in this setting, the appropriate response is to console
and support the family.
Intubate – Inappropriate, violates DNR and palliative care principles.
Give antidote (e.g., naloxone) – Not aligned with the comfort-focused care plan.
Console the family – Appropriate, respects DNR, provides compassionate care.
Do nothing – Incomplete; support and empathy must be offered.
Clinical Pearls:
• DNR ≠ Do Not Treat: It means avoiding resuscitative/invasive measures, not withholding
symptom relief.
• Principle of Double Effect: Using medication like opioids for comfort is ethically acceptable even if
it may shorten life.
• Palliative care is about maximizing quality of life, not prolonging suffering.
• Always ensure clear documentation of goals of care and family communication.
Please note:
The original question collection mentioned “counsel” instead of “console” however this linguistically
changes the correct answer and meaning of each option. By definition being:
•⁠ ⁠console: to comfort someone at a time of grief or disappointment
•⁠ ⁠counsel: advice, especially that given formally
Thus, the exam question was likely using the term “console” meaning to comfort the family.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 112–115
☆ Palliative Care & Ethics: DNR and Comfort Management
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 112–115

stem:
A 5-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his mother for evaluation of a patch of hair loss on his scalp that
has been worsening over the past 2 weeks. She notes the area began as a small scaly patch and has
now expanded, with more noticeable hair loss and crusting. The child sometimes scratches the area,
and she reports no improvement despite using over-the-counter antibacterial ointment. On exam, you
note a well-demarcated, erythematous, scaly alopecic plaque on the parietal scalp with black dot
appearance (broken hairs at follicular openings). There is no fever, and lymphadenopathy is absent.
What is the most appropriate next step in treatment?
explanation:
• This is a classic presentation of tinea capitis, a dermatophyte infection of the scalp. The black dot
pattern indicates endothrix infection, most commonly caused by Trichophyton tonsurans.
• Topical antifungals do not reach the hair follicle, so systemic antifungal therapy is required.
• Oral griseofulvin (20–25 mg/kg/day for 6–8 weeks) is the first-line treatment, especially in
younger children.
Other options:
• Fluconazole cream: Topical therapy is ineffective for scalp infections due to poor follicular
penetration.
• Topical steroid: Not indicated in fungal infections; may suppress immune response and worsen the
condition.
•
Oral cephalexin: Unnecessary unless there is clear secondary bacterial infection; does not treat
dermatophytes.
Clinical pearls:
• Tinea capitis often presents with scaling, hair loss, black dots, and
sometimes kerion (inflammatory mass).
• Treat with oral griseofulvin (20–25 mg/kg/day for 6–8 weeks) or oral
terbinafine (alternate).
• Selenium sulfide or ketoconazole shampoo can reduce transmission.
• Examine and treat household contacts if symptomatic.
• Confirm diagnosis with KOH prep or fungal culture if uncertain.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2755–2757; UpToDate: “Tinea capitis: Clinical
features and diagnosis”
☆ Dermatology: Tinea Capitis
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2755–2757; UpToDate: “Tinea capitis: Clinical

stem:
A child is able to walk up and down stairs with alternating feet, speak in 3-word sentences, draw a
circle, run well, and build a 7-block tower. What is the most likely age of this child?
explanation:
These developmental milestones are consistent with a 3-year-old (36 months):
• Walks up and down stairs with alternating feet
• Speaks in 3-word sentences
• Draws a circle (a key 3-year motor skill)
• Runs well
• Builds a tower of 6–8 blocks
Other options:
• 18 months: Typically walks, builds a 3–4 block tower, says a few single words, not yet using
sentences.
• 24 months (2 years): Can run, walk up stairs with help, build ~4–6 blocks, say 2-word phrases.
• 30 months: May build 6–7 blocks and use simple phrases, but typically cannot draw a circle or use
alternating feet on stairs yet.
Clinical pearls:
• Drawing a circle, alternating stairs, and 3-word sentences are classic 36-month (3-year)
milestones.
• Tower building is a reliable gross marker of fine motor development (by age):
o 15 months = 2 blocks
o 18 months = 3 blocks
o 24 months = 4–6 blocks
o 36 months = up to 8 blocks
• Developmental screening tools like ASQ or Denver II can help track progress.
Red flags in development:
• 9 months: not sitting alone
• 12 months: not passing objects hand to hand
• 15 months: abnormal pincer grasp
• 18 months: not walking alone
• 24 months: not saying words
• Hearing assessment: Should be done by 3 months.
•
•
•
•
•
•
Neonatal breast hypertrophy (Gynecomastia): Resolves at 1 year.
Teeth eruption: Normal up to 18 months.
Toe walking: Normal up 3 years, after that:
o Abnormal speech > ASD
o Normal speech > Short Achillis tendon
Thumb sucking: Stop at 4 years.
Handedness: Should start at 2-4 y (before that check nerves and muscles or perinatal stroke)
Head banging: Normal up to 5 years.
📚 Source: Osama Naga – Developmental Milestones; Nelson – Growth & Development Tables
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: Developmental Milestones
source: Osama Naga – Developmental Milestones; Nelson – Growth & Development Tables

stem:
When reviewing the chemistry panel of a newly diagnosed patient with acute lymphoblastic leukemia
who is lethargic, complaining of flank pain, and experiencing nausea and vomiting, which of the
following you expect to see?

options.label: A
options.text: Potassium 4.5 mmol/L, phosphorus 8mg/DL, uric acid 7mg/dL, calcium 9mg/dL, BUN 12mg/dL

options.label: B
options.text: Potassium 6.5 mmol/L, phosphorus 8mg/DL, uric acid 9mg/dL, calcium 10mg/dL, BUN 14mg/dL

options.label: C
options.text: Potassium 4 mmol/L, phosphorus 9mg/DL, uric acid 10mg/dL, calcium 10mg/dL, BUN 10mg/dL

options.label: D
options.text: Potassium 7 mmol/L, phosphorus 12mg/DL, uric acid 10mg/dL, calcium 7mg/dL, BUN 25mg/dL

answer: D. Potassium 7 mmol/L, phosphorus 12mg/DL, uric acid 10mg/dL, calcium 7mg/dL, BUN
explanation:
25mg/dL
Explanation:
• Tumor lysis syndrome is common in newly diagnosed leukemia patients after initiation of
therapy.
• This is because patients with hematologic malignancies that have high tumor cell burdens or rapid
turnover rates are at high risk for electrolyte abnormalities following the initiation of cytotoxic
chemotherapy due to the massive lysis of tumor cells.
•
Laboratory results consistent with TLS include hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia,
hyperuricemia, and hypocalcemia.
These patients may also have kidney injury evidenced by elevated BUN and/or creatinine.
•
★ Tumor Lysis Syndrome (TLS):
•
•
•
•
•
•
Definition: TLS is a potentially life-threatening oncologic emergency caused by rapid lysis of
tumor cells, leading to release of intracellular contents into the bloodstream.
Etiology (common triggers):
o High-grade lymphomas (especially Burkitt lymphoma)
o Acute leukemias (ALL, AML with high WBC)
o Large tumor burden or rapid cell turnover
o After chemotherapy, corticosteroids, or spontaneously → may be the initial presentation
of cancer
Core Electrolyte Abnormalities (mnemonic: PUKED):
o Phosphatemia (↑ PO₄)
o Uric acid (↑ UA)
o Kalemia (↑ K⁺)
o sEcondary hypoCalcemia (↓ Ca²⁺)
o Dehydration & renal failure risk
Clinical Features:
o Vomiting, lethargy, muscle cramps, seizures, tetany
o Arrhythmias from hyperkalemia or hypocalcemia
o Acute kidney injury due to uric acid or calcium phosphate nephropathy
Diagnostic Criteria (Cairo-Bishop):
o Laboratory TLS = ≥2 of the following within 3 days before or 7 days after chemotherapy:
 Uric acid > 8 mg/dL (or 25% increase)
 Potassium > 6 mEq/L (or 25% increase)
 Phosphate > 6.5 mg/dL (children)
 Calcium < 7 mg/dL (or 25% decrease)
o Clinical TLS = Lab TLS + one of the following:
 Renal insufficiency
 Cardiac arrhythmia/sudden death
 Seizure
Prevention (most high-yield concept):
o IV hydration 2–3x maintenance (start before chemo)
o Allopurinol to prevent uric acid buildup
 Prevents formation of uric acid, does not lower existing uric acid.
 Start: 1–2 days before chemotherapy in moderate-risk TLS patients.
o Rasburicase in high-risk patients to lower uric acid quickly
 Treatment and prevention: rapidly degrades uric acid into allantoin, which is
•
•
more water-soluble and easily excreted.
 Start: at first sign of hyperuricemia or for high-risk TLS even before hyperuricemia
appears.
 Contraindicated in G6PD deficiency → risk of hemolysis and methemoglobinemia
o Note: Avoid simultaneous use of allopurinol and rasburicase: allopurinol inhibits xanthine
oxidase → co-administration reduces efficacy and increases risk of nephropathy
o Close monitoring: electrolytes, renal function, urine output
Management:
o Aggressive hydration
o Correct electrolyte abnormalities:
 Hyperkalemia: calcium gluconate, insulin/glucose, albuterol
 Hyperphosphatemia: phosphate binders, avoid phosphate intake
 Hypocalcemia: treat only if symptomatic (seizure, tetany)
o Dialysis if refractory hyperkalemia, severe AKI, or fluid overload
High-Risk Features for TLS (prophylaxis needed):
o WBC >100,000/mm³ in leukemia
o LDH >2x normal
o Preexisting renal dysfunction
o Burkitt lymphoma or T-cell ALL
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1874–1876; Pocket Pediatrics 2024
☆ Hematology/Oncology: Oncologic Emergencies
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1874–1876; Pocket Pediatrics 2024

stem:
A 2-week-old term infant presents with progressive cyanosis and tachypnea. There is no
improvement in oxygen saturation with 100% oxygen. Physical examination reveals a single S2, mild
subcostal retractions, and no murmur. A chest radiograph was done, and is shown below:
What is the most likely diagnosis?
answer: D. Total Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Return (TAPVR)
explanation:
• TAPVR presents in neonates with cyanosis and tachypnea,
especially when pulmonary veins fail to connect to the left
atrium
• The hallmark clinical clue is cyanosis unresponsive to oxygen
• The absence of a murmur and presence of single S2 suggests no
significant outflow tract obstruction, consistent with TAPVR
• TAPVR requires right-to-left shunting (via PFO or ASD) for survival
• TGA presents with cyanosis but typically has narrow mediastinum on imaging
• Tetralogy of Fallot often presents later and has a systolic murmur
• Truncus arteriosus presents with bounding pulses and single arterial trunk
Normal anatomy
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 397–398; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd Ed., p. 374–375
☆ Cardiology: Cyanotic Congenital Heart Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 397–398; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 12-year-old girl with a known diagnosis of autoimmune hepatitis for the past 3 years is
maintained on immunosuppressive therapy. She now presents with evidence of clinical relapse.
On further history, you learn that she has missed multiple school days, lives only with her father,
and is inconsistent with follow-up. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: B
options.text: Increase the dose of the medication

explanation:
• This child is presenting with disease relapse, and the history strongly suggests non-adherence or
psychosocial barriers to care.
• Before escalating medical therapy or performing invasive investigations, it's essential to identify
and address underlying social factors, such as:
Single-parent household
School absenteeism
Possible poor medication compliance or lack of support
A thorough social assessment may reveal modifiable causes and allow targeted interventions.
o
o
o
•
Clinical Pearls:
• Non-compliance is a leading cause of relapse in adolescents with chronic illness
• Immunosuppression escalation or biopsy should be considered only after evaluating adherence
• CT is not helpful in autoimmune hepatitis unless specific complications are suspected
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 625–626; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 702–703
☆ Gastroenterology: Autoimmune Hepatitis, Social Determinants of Health, Medication compliance
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 625–626; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 702–703
topic: Autoimmune Hepatitis, Social Determinants of Health, Medication compliance

stem:
A 6-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to chronic constipation for several
months. He has infrequent bowel movements, sometimes going 4–5 days without passing stool,
and recently, they noticed soiling of underwear despite regular toilet use. On physical examination,
there is a palpable mass in the left lower quadrant and mild abdominal distension. He is
otherwise healthy with no vomiting, fever, or weight loss. What is the most appropriate next step in
management?
explanation:
• This is classic for functional constipation with overflow fecal incontinence (encopresis)
o retentive encopresis is associated with functional constipation (80%)!
• The palpable mass is likely a large stool burden in the rectum or colon
• Soiling occurs due to liquid stool leaking around impacted feces and is almost always
associated with severe functional constipation.
• Imaging is not needed when the diagnosis is clinically obvious, and no red flag signs are present.
• Immediate treatment is disimpaction (e.g., high-dose PEG or enemas), followed by maintenance
laxatives.
• Reassurance alone is inappropriate without addressing the underlying constipation.
★ Pediatric Constipation:
• Definition
o <2 bowel movements per week or hard, painful stools
o ± fecal incontinence, large-diameter stools, or stool withholding
• Most common type
o Functional constipation (>95% of cases)
o Onset: during toilet training, starting school,
or diet change
o No underlying organic disease
• Red flags (suggest organic cause)
o Onset <1 month of age
o Delayed meconium (>48 hrs) → think
Hirschsprung
o Bilious vomiting, failure to thrive
o Neuro signs (weak legs, abnormal reflexes) → think spinal dysraphism
o Severe abdominal distension
o Anal stenosis or ectopic anus
• Common Causes
o Functional withholding (fear of pain → retention cycle)
•
•
•
•
o Low fiber, high dairy diet
o Inadequate water intake
o Hypothyroidism, Hirschsprung, spinal abnormalities (less common)
Clinical Features
o Infrequent, large, hard stools
o Stool withholding behavior (e.g., leg crossing, stiff posture)
o Soiling (encopresis)
o Abdominal pain, decreased appetite
o Palpable fecal mass
Diagnosis
o Clinical in most cases
o No imaging or labs unless red flags
o Abdominal X-ray only if uncertain or severe fecal load suspected
Treatment Approach
o Step 1: Disimpaction
 Oral PEG 3350 (1–1.5 g/kg/day x 3–6 days)
 Or rectal enemas (if oral fails)
o Step 2: Maintenance therapy
 Oral PEG 3350 (0.4–0.8 g/kg/day) for months
 Add fiber, fluids, toilet routine (sit after meals)
o Step 3: Wean slowly after 6 months of symptom-free period
Follow-up
o Monitor stool frequency, abdominal symptoms, and adherence
o Relapse common if treatment stopped early
Clinical pearls:
• Most common cause of encopresis is chronic functional constipation
• Treatment requires:
o Disimpaction → maintenance → behavioral/dietary changes
o Use PEG 3350 (1–1.5 g/kg/day) for disimpaction
• Stool softeners must be continued for months to prevent recurrence
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 398–400
☆ Gastroenterology: Functional Constipation and Encopresis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 398–400

stem:
A 6-year-old boy presents with difficulty walking following a febrile upper respiratory tract
infection one week ago. He prefers not to walk and complains of pain in his calves. On exam, he is
afebrile, alert, and has tenderness of the calf muscles without joint swelling or neurologic deficits.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Viral myositis commonly occurs in school-aged children, often 1–2 weeks after a viral
illness, particularly influenza.
• Key findings:
o Recent viral illness, bilateral calf pain, tenderness, refusal to walk
o Normal neurologic exam and afebrile child
• Serum CK is elevated due to muscle inflammation and helps confirm diagnosis
• Condition is self-limiting, resolves in a few days with supportive care
• No need for imaging or invasive testing unless atypical signs present
★ Viral Myositis:
• Definition: Acute inflammation of skeletal muscle caused by viral infection, often after viral
prodrome (especially influenza)
o Etiology: Influenza A/B (most common), Coxsackievirus, Adenovirus, Parainfluenza,
Enterovirus
• Typical Age: 3–9 years (school-age children), boys > girls
• Clinical Features:
o Fever, malaise after URI
o Sudden calf pain, reluctance/refusal to walk
o Tender gastrocnemius, normal neuro exam
o Often bilateral but may be asymmetric
o No significant joint involvement
• Labs:
o Elevated CK (can be >10,000 IU/L)
o Mild leukocytosis
o Normal inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP)
o Urine myoglobin may be positive (without renal failure)
• Diagnosis: Clinical, supported by elevated CPK
o Rule out: rhabdomyolysis, Guillain-Barré, septic arthritis, osteomyelitis
• Management:
o Supportive: hydration, rest, NSAIDs
o Full recovery in 3–7 days
o No antibiotics or antivirals needed unless other complications
• Red Flags (Not typical):
o Focal neurological signs
o Persistent or worsening symptoms
o Very high CK with dark urine → evaluate for rhabdomyolysis
o Tachycardia out of proportion, hypotension, or chest discomfort → think viral
myocarditis
 Always consider ECG and cardiac markers if systemic symptoms suggest
myocardial involvement
Clinical pearls:
• Acute viral myositis = flu + calf pain + refusal to walk
• Normal neuro exam, resolves spontaneously
• CK elevation is often 1000–5000 U/L
• Rule out rhabdomyolysis if severe or if dark urine
• Differentiate from GBS (which causes weakness, not pain)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1042–1043
☆ Neurology: Viral Myositis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1042–1043
topic: • Definition: Acute inflammation of skeletal muscle caused by viral infection, often after viral

stem:
A 7-year-old boy presents with bedwetting that has occurred nightly since toilet training. He has no
daytime incontinence, no dysuria, and normal growth and development. His physical exam is
unremarkable, and there is no family history of renal or neurologic disease. What is the most
appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• In a child ≥5 years with primary monosymptomatic nocturnal enuresis, the first step is to rule out
•
•
•
•
secondary causes with a screening urinalysis.
Urinalysis can help detect infection, glycosuria (diabetes mellitus), or specific gravity
abnormalities (e.g., diabetes insipidus).
Reassurance alone is not sufficient before ruling out secondary causes.
Renal ultrasound is not needed unless physical exam or urinalysis is abnormal.
Desmopressin is a treatment option, but only after evaluation and behavioral strategies are
considered.
Clinical pearls:
• Primary enuresis is defined as persistent nighttime wetting in a child who has never been dry for
≥6 months.
• Monosymptomatic = no daytime symptoms.
• Initial steps: urinalysis, then educational and behavioral therapy (e.g., fluid restriction, scheduled
voiding, enuresis alarm).
• Most effective therapy = enuresis alarm.
• Pharmacologic treatment (e.g., desmopressin or imipramine) is reserved for motivated families or
older children.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 551; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 178
☆ Nephrology: Nocturnal Enuresis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 551; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 178

stem:
A child with fat malabsorption due to chronic cholestatic liver disease is evaluated for nutritional
support. You are asked which vitamin would be important to supplement to aid in fat absorption.
explanation:
• Vitamin E is a fat-soluble vitamin (along with A, D, and K) that depends on bile salts and
pancreatic enzymes for proper absorption.
• In conditions with fat malabsorption (e.g., cystic fibrosis, biliary atresia, cholestasis), vitamin E
deficiency is common and requires supplementation in a water-soluble or micellized form to
improve absorption.
Other options:
• Vitamin C – water-soluble, absorbed without the need for fat.
• Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) – water-soluble, unrelated to fat absorption.
• Vitamin B12 – requires intrinsic factor, not fat for absorption.
Clinical Pearls:
• Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) should be supplemented in children with cholestasis, CF, or short
bowel syndrome.
• Vitamin E deficiency can lead to ataxia, areflexia, and hemolytic anemia, particularly in neonates
or those with chronic malabsorption.
• Monitoring fat-soluble vitamin levels is essential in children with long-term TPN or liver disease.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 296–297
☆ General Pediatrics: Fat-Soluble Vitamin Deficiencies
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 296–297

stem:
A 13-year-old previously healthy girl is brought to the clinic by her parents due to an 8 kg weight
loss over the past few months. She has no medical complaints, her growth parameters are within
normal range, and her physical exam is unremarkable. She reports going to the gym daily, and says she
is “just trying to stay fit.” Her parents are concerned about the rapid weight loss. What is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.label: B
options.text: Reassure and advise reducing physical activity

options.label: D
options.text: Refer to endocrinology for hormone panel

answer: A. Refer to dietitian and psychologist
explanation:
• Although the child appears physically well, unintentional or excessive weight loss in adolescents,
especially in the context of increased exercise, raises concern for early or atypical eating disorders
(e.g., anorexia nervosa, atypical anorexia).
• Early multidisciplinary intervention with psychological support and nutritional guidance is
essential for diagnosis and prevention of progression.
Other options:
• Reassure and reduce physical activity – Inappropriate without addressing potential psychological
drivers and dietary concerns.
• Nutritional shakes – May be supportive later, but not a first-line action without assessing the
underlying cause.
• Endocrinology referral – Not indicated at this stage without evidence of hormonal dysfunction.
Clinical Pearls:
• Rapid or significant weight loss (>5% in 1 month or >10% in 6 months) in adolescents warrants full
evaluation.
• Eating disorders may present with normal vitals and labs, especially in early stages or atypical
presentations.
• High-yield red flags include excessive exercise, body image preoccupation, and restrictive eating
behaviors.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 86–87
☆ Adolescent Medicine: Eating Disorders
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 86–87

stem:
A 10-year-old boy presents with recurrent episodes of microscopic, painless hematuria found on
routine urinalysis. He is otherwise asymptomatic. There is no recent illness, dysuria, or edema. What is
the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Familial benign hematuria (thin basement membrane disease) presents as persistent or
recurrent painless microscopic hematuria, often identified incidentally.
• It is inherited, usually autosomal dominant, and often associated with a family history of
hematuria, but may coexist with renal stones in some families.
• Hypercalcemia is not typically a cause of hematuria; rather, hypercalciuria is. This makes
hypercalcemia an incorrect distractor.
• Note: Idiopathic hypercalciuria is the most common cause of recurrent painless microscopic
hematuria in children, especially with a family history of nephrolithiasis.
• Urinary tract infections usually present with dysuria, frequency, or fever, not isolated hematuria.
• PSGN presents with gross hematuria, edema, hypertension, and low complement levels following
a recent streptococcal infection.
Isolated microscopic hematuria (>5 RBC/hpf; asymptomatic):
® Most common cause of transient asymptomatic
hematuria in children: fever, exercise or menstruation
® Most common cause of persistent asymptomatic
hematuria: familial benign hematuria, hypercaliuria
® However be careful: if there is FHx of hearing deficit or
eye problems then look for Alport syndrome
® If present, redo urinalysis in a few days
Clinical pearls:
• Benign familial hematuria typically causes isolated microscopic hematuria, with normal renal
function and no proteinuria or hypertension.
• Often diagnosed based on family history and exclusion of other causes.
• Renal biopsy (if done) shows uniformly thinned glomerular basement membranes.
• No treatment is needed, and prognosis is excellent.
• Always consider hypercalciuria in painless hematuria with family history of kidney stones.
o Idiopathic hypercalciuria is more common than familial benign hematuria — even in the
absence of a family history of kidney stones.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 508–509; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 149
☆ Nephrology: Hematuria
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 508–509; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 149

stem:
A 9-year-old child presents with a recurrent, itchy skin rash that has been appearing and disappearing
over the past 2 weeks. Each episode lasts for a few hours and resolves without scarring or
pigmentation. The child recently recovered from a viral upper respiratory tract infection. On
examination, there are transient, erythematous wheals on the trunk and extremities, with no systemic
symptoms. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Acute urticaria is defined as transient wheals lasting <24 hours, with total duration of <6 weeks,
commonly triggered by viral infections, foods, or medications.
• The 2-week recurrent history still qualifies as acute, not chronic
urticaria.
• Chronic urticaria is diagnosed when episodes occur daily or neardaily for ≥6 weeks.
• Urticarial vasculitis presents with painful lesions lasting >24 hours,
often with bruising, systemic signs, or residual pigmentation.
• Pityriasis alba is a non-pruritic, hypopigmented patch, not wheals,
and has a chronic course.
Clinical pearls:
• Acute urticaria in children is often post-viral and resolves spontaneously within days to weeks.
• Treat with second-generation antihistamines (e.g., cetirizine, loratadine).
• Avoid potential triggers if identified, but often no clear cause is found.
• Systemic involvement or progression to angioedema warrants further workup.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 737–738; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 249
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Acute Urticaria
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 737–738; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 249

stem:
A 10-year-old boy presents with a history of recurrent sinusitis, pneumonia, and otitis media over the
past 2 years. His infections are frequent, requiring multiple courses of antibiotics annually. He
had normal growth and development until age 7, but recently has had fatigue and poor school
attendance due to illness. There is no family history of immunodeficiency. Physical exam reveals nasal
congestion, post-nasal drip, and crackles in the lungs. Chest X-ray shows mild bronchiectasis. His
vaccines are up to date. What is the most appropriate initial laboratory test?
explanation:
• This is a classic presentation of Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID):
o Onset in late childhood/adolescence
o Recurrent sinopulmonary infections
o Normal growth early in life, then progressive infections and bronchiectasis
• The first-line diagnostic step is to check quantitative immunoglobulin levels (IgG, IgA,
IgM) and antibody titers to protein and polysaccharide vaccines (e.g. tetanus, pneumococcus).
• CVID is characterized by low IgG and either low IgA or IgM, and poor vaccine response.
Other options:
• CH50 – Screens for complement deficiency; more relevant for recurrent bacterial meningitis or
Neisseria infections.
• Phagocytic burst – Tests neutrophil oxidative burst (e.g., for CGD); used in deep-seated abscesses,
not primarily airway disease.
• Sweat chloride test – To rule out cystic fibrosis; useful but not first-line if recurrent ENT +
bronchiectasis in older child without failure to thrive or GI symptoms.
Clinical Pearls:
• Evaluate quantitative immunoglobulin levels and vaccine titers in any child with recurrent
sinopulmonary infections.
• Bronchiectasis in children should always prompt evaluation for underlying immunodeficiency,
CF, or primary ciliary dyskinesia.
• CVID can present after age 2 with low IgG ± IgA/IgM, poor vaccine response.
• Usually presents 10-30 yrs
• Treatment: Lifelong IVIG or SCIG replacement therapy
• Increased risk of autoimmunity, lymphoid hyperplasia, and GI disease
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1097–1099
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Antibody Deficiency and Recurrent Infections
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1097–1099

stem:
A 2-year-old child is being evaluated for delayed motor milestones. He has increased muscle tone in
both legs, brisk deep tendon reflexes, and a scissoring gait. MRI of the brain shows periventricular
leukomalacia. What is the most common subtype of cerebral palsy?
explanation:
• Spastic cerebral palsy is the most common subtype, accounting for ~70–80% of all CP cases.
• It is characterized by hypertonia, hyperreflexia, and pyramidal tract signs. It often results from
periventricular leukomalacia, especially in preterm infants.
Other options:
• Athetoid (Dyskinetic CP) – Less common; involves involuntary writhing movements, usually due to
basal ganglia injury (e.g., kernicterus).
• Ataxic – Rare; involves coordination and balance problems.
• Hypotonic – Often transitional or associated with genetic/metabolic conditions; not a distinct
classic CP subtype.
Clinical Pearls:
• Spastic diplegia is most common in preterm infants, especially with PVL.
• Spastic hemiplegia usually occurs after stroke or perinatal insult.
• Dyskinetic CP is often due to hyperbilirubinemia and kernicterus.
• MRI brain is the preferred imaging to identify the etiology and pattern of brain injury in CP.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 3160–3162; UWorld
☆ Neurology: Cerebral Palsy Subtypes and Diagnosis
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 3160–3162; UWorld

stem:
A 9-month-old infant is brought for evaluation of motor delays and stiffness. On examination, the
child has hyperreflexia, hypertonia in all four limbs, exaggerated deep tendon reflexes, and
scissoring of the legs when held upright. What is the most likely type of cerebral palsy?
explanation:
• Spastic quadriplegic cerebral palsy is the most severe form, involving all four limbs with
hypertonia, hyperreflexia, and often scissoring of the legs due to increased adductor tone.
• Diplegic CP typically affects the lower limbs more than upper, often seen in preterm infants.
• Dystonic CP (a type of dyskinetic CP) is characterized by involuntary muscle contractions,
fluctuating tone, and movement disorders, not spasticity.
• Hemiplegic CP involves one side of the body and is often due to perinatal stroke.
Clinical pearls:
• Spasticity + hyperreflexia → strongly suggests spastic CP subtype.
• Quadriplegia indicates diffuse brain injury, often due to perinatal asphyxia or severe brain
malformation.
• MRI may show periventricular leukomalacia, cortical atrophy, or diffuse white matter injury.
• Management includes PT/OT, spasticity control (e.g., baclofen, botulinum toxin), and monitoring
for comorbidities (seizures, feeding issues).
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 256–257; Osama Naga Pediatric Board, 3rd ed., p. 168
☆ Neurology: Spastic Cerebral Palsy Subtypes
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 256–257; Osama Naga Pediatric Board, 3rd ed., p. 168

stem:
A 4-month-old infant presents with episodes of repetitive jerking of the neck, upper extremities,
and trunk, often occurring in clusters. The events are followed by brief loss of tone. The infant is
developmentally normal. EEG is reported as normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Benign neonatal/infantile sleep myoclonus presents in the first few months of life with:
o Repetitive, jerky movements during sleep, typically involving limbs or trunk
o Episodes stop when the infant is awakened
o Normal EEG, development, and neurologic exam
• Infantile spasms usually occur between 3–12 months, are associated with developmental
regression and abnormal EEG (hypsarrhythmia)
• Myoclonic epilepsy and focal seizures would also show EEG changes and developmental
concerns
Clinical pearls:
• Benign sleep myoclonus: no treatment needed, resolves spontaneously
• Always confirm EEG is normal during events if diagnosis is uncertain
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 238–239
☆ Neurology: Benign Sleep Myoclonus
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 238–239

stem:
A child presents with recurrent episodes of staring, sometimes associated with lip smacking and
squinting. These episodes occur multiple times per day. Neurologic examination is normal. EEG is
attached below.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Absence seizures present with brief staring spells, lasting <30 seconds, often multiple times per
day
• Duration is <15 seconds
• No post ictal phase
• Can have simple automatisms → lip smacking, eye fluttering, head nodding
• Can be induced by hyperventilation
• Absence seizures are characterized by momentary lapses in consciousness (~10 seconds)
associated with a classic 3-Hz spike-wave pattern on electroencephalogram.
•
Tx:
o
o
o
1st line = ethosuximide (Zarontin)
 Mechanism: blocks T-type calcium
channels in thalamic neurons
 Highly effective with fewer side
effects compared to alternatives.
2nd line or if associated with generalized
seizures = sodium valproate (Depakene)
3rd line = lamotrigine
Other options:
• Daydreaming is not associated with automatisms or interruption of activity when called
• Pseudoseizures (non-epileptic events) often involve dramatic movements and occur in older
children
• Myoclonic seizures involve sudden, brief muscle jerks — not staring or automatisms
Clinical pearls:
• Triggered by hyperventilation in clinic
• First-line treatment: ethosuximide
• Typical onset: age 4–10 years
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 271; UWorld.
☆ Neurology: Absence Seizures
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 271; UWorld.

stem:
A 7-year-old boy is brought in for evaluation of abnormal movements during sleep, described by the
parents as gurgling sounds and facial twitching, especially involving the mouth and tongue. Episodes
occur during light sleep and last for less than 2 minutes. The child is otherwise healthy and has normal
development. EEG shows centrotemporal (mid-temporal) spikes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Benign Rolandic Epilepsy (Benign Epilepsy with Centrotemporal Spikes) is the most common
focal epilepsy of childhood.
• Seizures are brief, typically occur during sleep or upon awakening, and involve facial twitching,
drooling, or guttural noises.
• EEG is diagnostic: shows centrotemporal spikes, often activated by sleep.
• Absence seizures are generalized, occur during wakefulness, and involve staring spells, not focal
motor symptoms.
• Nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy has complex motor automatisms, often confused with
parasomnias, and EEG may be normal or show frontal activity.
• Lennox-Gastaut syndrome presents with multiple seizure types, cognitive impairment, and slow
spike-and-wave pattern on EEG.
Clinical pearls:
• Rolandic epilepsy typically remits by adolescence and often requires no treatment if seizures are
infrequent.
• If treated, low-dose carbamazepine or levetiracetam is effective.
• Cognitive development is usually normal, but reading/language delays can occur in some.

ed., p. 173; Learning EEG

stem:
A 10-month-old infant is brought to your primary care clinic with a history of intractable seizures,
currently uncontrolled despite two anti-epileptic medications. The mother also reports dermatitis
around the mouth and genital area, and recent onset of alopecia. What is the most likely deficiency in
this child?
explanation:
• Biotinidase deficiency is a rare inherited metabolic disorder that leads to impaired
recycling of biotin, an essential cofactor for several carboxylase enzymes.
• Classic features include:
o Refractory seizures or hypotonia
o Eczematous periorificial dermatitis
o Alopecia
o Developmental delay
o Metabolic acidosis
• Pyruvate carboxylase deficiency causes lactic acidosis,
failure to thrive, and neurologic symptoms but not alopecia or dermatitis.
• Beta-methylcrotonyl-CoA carboxylase deficiency is biotin-dependent, but typically presents
with metabolic acidosis and lethargy, and does not explain all the findings.
• Pantothenic acid (vitamin B5) deficiency is extremely rare and usually seen in severe
malnutrition; may cause burning feet syndrome, but not seizures or alopecia.
• Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) deficiency may cause seizures, but not alopecia or dermatitis.
Clinical pearls:
• Biotinidase deficiency is screened for in many newborn screening programs.
