• Diagnosis is confirmed by low biotinidase enzyme activity.
• Treatment is oral biotin supplementation (5–10 mg/day), which is dramatically effective.
• Without treatment, symptoms can progress to hearing loss, vision problems, and
neurodevelopmental delay.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 97; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 49
Additionally, an interesting case walk-through: https://www.davidbender.co.uk/metabonline/central/biotin/biotin17.html#answer
☆ Genetic/Metabolic: Biotinidase Deficiency
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 97; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 49

stem:
A dehydrated child is brought in with vomiting and receives IV fluids and antibiotics. Shortly after, the
child develops seizures that are unresponsive to anti-epileptic medications. A CT scan reveals
central pontine myelinolysis. What is the most likely underlying cause?
explanation:
• Central pontine myelinolysis (CPM), now often called osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS),
occurs due to rapid correction of chronic hyponatremia, especially in cases of severe
dehydration or malnutrition.
• Overly aggressive IV fluid correction (>8–10 mEq/L of sodium in 24 hours) leads to osmotic injury
to brain cells, particularly in the pons.
• Presents with seizures, altered consciousness, and potentially locked-in syndrome.
• Hypernatremia causes brain shrinkage, but not CPM.
• Hypokalemia or hypocalcemia may cause seizures but are not associated with pontine
demyelination.
★ Hyponatremia:
• Defined as Na <135
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
Clinical Manifestations
o Nonspecific: Nausea, headache, lethargy, ataxia, disorientation, seizure, coma
(due to cerebral edema)
o Sx more likely if [Na] Δ is rapid (vs. abs level of [Na]). Usually don’t see until [Na] <125
Workup
o Acute vs. chronic (>48 hr); (2) neuro status; (3) check BMP, plasma Osm, Uosm, UNa, can
check lipids and protein (r/o pseudohypoNa)
Fluid Status
o Hypovolemic: Net loss of Na in excess of the net loss of free water
 Intravascular volume depletion: stimulates ADH synthesis → renal water retention
o Euvolemic: Gain of free water
o Hypervolemic: Greater gain of free water relative to sodium with overall positive balance of
both
 Often a decrease in effective blood volume due to third space fluid loss, vasodilation,
or poor cardiac output → ADH and aldosterone production
Basic Treatment
o Hypovolemic: Isotonic fluid replacement
o Euvolemic: Fluid restriction
o Hypervolemic: Fluid + Na restriction
Loop diuretics to increase water excretion for more rapid response
Vasopressin antagonists block action of ADH causing a water diuresis, helpful in pts with heart
failure or cirrhosis (Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics. 20th ed. 2016:346– 384)
Rate of Na correction in chronic hypoNa should not exceed 12 mEq/L/24 hr to avoid osmotic
demyelination
Clinical pearls:
• In hyponatremic dehydration, sodium should be corrected slowly:
o Target rise of <0.5 mEq/L/hour, max 8–10 mEq/L/day
• Risk factors for CPM: chronic hyponatremia, malnutrition, liver disease, hypokalemia
• Always calculate corrected sodium and monitor serum sodium levels closely during rehydration.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 274, 447; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p.
181; Pocket Pediatrics 2024: Hyponatremia
☆ Emergency Medicine: Electrolyte Correction Complications
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 274, 447; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p.

stem:
A 9-year-old boy presents to the ER with new-onset focal seizures and fever. He is confused and has
trouble answering questions. A lumbar puncture reveals cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) with lymphocytic
pleocytosis, elevated protein, normal glucose, and increased red blood cells (RBCs) without trauma
during the tap. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• HSV encephalitis in children and adolescents classically presents with:
o Acute altered mental status, focal neurologic signs, seizures
o CSF: Lymphocytic predominance, elevated RBCs, elevated protein,
normal glucose
 Can be red in color (bloody) or yellow (xanthochromic) due to RBC
breakdown
 Caused by hemorrhagic necrosis of the temporal lobe
 However, the CSF profile can be normal early in the course of the disease and
therefore is not diagnostic.
• Temporal lobe seizure in HSV encephalitis often presents with focal seizures, altered mental
status, hallucinations, behavioral changes, and confusion.
• Cause:
o Most commonly HSV-1 in children and adolescents
o HSV-2 in neonates (often perinatally acquired)
• Diagnosis:
o CSF PCR for HSV DNA = gold standard
o MRI: hyperintensity in temporal lobes
 MRI classically shows temporal lobe edema because the virus enters the brain via
the olfactory tract and travels to the cortex (located in the medial temporal lobe).
o EEG: periodic sharp waves in temporal areas
• Treatment:
o Empiric IV acyclovir immediately (do not wait for PCR)
o Duration: 14–21 days
o Monitor renal function (acyclovir nephrotoxic)
• Prognosis:
o High mortality if untreated
o Survivors may have neurodevelopmental impairment, especially if diagnosis delayed
Other options:
• Enteroviral meningitis usually has lymphocytosis but lacks focal signs and RBCs
• TB meningitis is subacute with very high protein and low glucose
• Bacterial meningitis typically has neutrophilic predominance and low glucose

stem:
A neonate is diagnosed with non-obstructive (communicating) hydrocephalus after evaluation for
increasing head circumference. Which of the following anatomic sites is typically involved in this
condition?
explanation:
Communicating (non-obstructive) hydrocephalus is due to impaired absorption of
cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) at the arachnoid villi or subarachnoid space, not due to a blockage within the
ventricles themselves. Thus, the ventricular system remains open, including the aqueduct of Sylvius, 3rd,
and 4th ventricles. In this condition:
• All ventricles are symmetrically enlarged.
• There is no focal obstruction, so the 4th ventricle remains open, and flow obstruction does not
occur at the level of the aqueduct or foramina.
® The lesion is not in the 4th ventricle — the 4th ventricle is still
open, and CSF flows through it, proving it's not obstructive.
Other options:
• Aqueduct of Sylvius → this is a common site for obstructive
(non-communicating) hydrocephalus, especially in
congenital aqueductal stenosis.
• Lateral ventricle and 3rd ventricle → can become dilated in
both types but are not the primary site responsible for
pathophysiology in communicating hydrocephalus.
Clinical pearls:
• CSF production → choroid plexus (mostly in lateral ventricles).
• CSF flow → Lateral → 3rd → Aqueduct → 4th → Subarachnoid space.
• CSF absorption → Arachnoid granulations in the superior sagittal sinus (NOT inside ventricles).
• Non-obstructive hydrocephalus = enlarged ventricles due to defective CSF absorption, usually
post-infectious (e.g., meningitis), post-hemorrhagic, or idiopathic.
o 4th ventricle dilation = suggests communicating hydrocephalus.
• Obstructive hydrocephalus = CSF flow is blocked inside the ventricular system (e.g., at the
aqueduct or foramina).
o Aqueductal stenosis is the most common congenital cause of obstructive hydrocephalus.
o Dandy-Walker Malformation → cystic dilation of 4th ventricle + cerebellar vermis
hypoplasia.
• Symptoms: bulging fontanelle, increased head circumference, sunsetting eyes (classic in
infants), irritability, and vomiting.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2984–2986; UWorld
☆ Neurology: Hydrocephalus
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2984–2986; UWorld

stem:
A 4-year-old boy is brought by his parents due to episodes of screaming and sweating during sleep.
These occur 1–2 hours after falling asleep. During the episodes, he appears terrified, is difficult to
console, and does not recognize his parents. He returns to sleep spontaneously and the next morning
has no memory of the event. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Night terrors are a type of parasomnia that occur during non-REM (N3) sleep, typically in the first
third of the night.
• Children may scream, sweat, sit up in bed, or appear fearful, but are unresponsive and have no
memory of the event the next morning.
• Nightmares, by contrast, occur during REM sleep, later in the night, and are usually remembered.
• Nocturnal seizures often have stereotyped motor activity and may require EEG to distinguish.
• Obstructive sleep apnea is associated with snoring, gasping, and daytime fatigue—not isolated
screaming or amnesia.
Clinical pearls:
• Reassurance is the mainstay of management
• Usually resolves with age
• Maintain good sleep hygiene; no medications are typically needed
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 136
☆ Neurology: Pediatric Sleep Disorders; Night Terrors
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 136

stem:
A 2-year-old child is brought to clinic after episodes where he begins crying intensely, turns cyanotic,
and loses consciousness briefly, especially when angry or frustrated. The mother also reports
occasional jerking movements during these episodes, which resolve spontaneously within a minute.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Breath-holding spells are involuntary, benign events that occur in toddlers in response to
emotional triggers (e.g., anger, pain)
• The child cries, exhales, then holds breath in expiration, leading to cyanosis, brief loss of
consciousness, and sometimes seizure-like movements (anoxic seizure)
• There is rapid recovery, and no post-ictal state
• Temper tantrums do not involve cyanosis or loss of consciousness
• Atonic seizures occur without a clear trigger and often have no emotional provocation
• Conversion disorder is a diagnosis of exclusion in older children/adolescents, not toddlers
Clinical pearls:
• Breath-holding spells are benign, peak at 1–2 years, and resolve by age 5
• Two types:
o Cyanotic (most common) – after crying
o Pallid – after minor trauma or fear
• EEG is not required unless spells are atypical
• Check for iron deficiency anemia → low ferritin is associated with more frequent spells
• Management: Reassurance, iron supplementation if needed
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 254–255; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 105
☆ General Pediatrics: Breath-Holding Spells
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 254–255; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 105

stem:
A 4-year-old child is undergoing behavioral therapy for temper tantrums. The pediatrician advises the
parents to implement a “time-out” strategy. What is the appropriate time duration for a time-out in
this child?
explanation:
The general rule for time-out duration is 1 minute per year of age. Therefore, for a 4-year-old,
the recommended time-out is 4 minutes. This helps reinforce cause-effect behavior while being
developmentally appropriate.
• A and B are underestimations for a 4-year-old.
• D is acceptable but may exceed attention span or frustrate the child, potentially reducing
effectiveness.
Clinical Pearls:
• Time-out is most effective when used consistently and immediately after undesired behavior.
• The location should be neutral (not fun or scary).
• Avoid excessive talking or emotional reactions during time-out.
• The child should know why they are being given a time-out.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 55
☆ Developmental & Behavioral: Discipline Strategies
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 55

stem:
A 16-year-old boy presents with fever, fatigue, and sore throat. Physical exam reveals exudative
pharyngitis, generalized lymphadenopathy, and mild splenomegaly. Laboratory studies show
elevated WBC count with atypical lymphocytes. What is the best next action?

options.label: A
options.text: No participation in contact sports for the next 2–4 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: 2-week treatment with oral acyclovir and prednisone

answer: A. No participation in contact sports for the next 2–4 weeks
explanation:
• This clinical picture is classic for Epstein-Barr Virus (EBV) infectious mononucleosis.
• Atypical lymphocytes, pharyngitis, and splenomegaly are hallmark features.
• The most dangerous complication in the acute phase is splenic rupture, especially in adolescents
engaged in contact sports.
• Therefore, the most important management step is to restrict contact sports for at least 3–4
weeks, or until splenomegaly resolves.
• Steroids may be used only for life-threatening airway obstruction or severe thrombocytopenia —
not routine.
• Acyclovir has no proven clinical benefit in EBV.
• Penicillin is not indicated unless there is coexisting streptococcal infection.
• Strict bed rest is not required — activity as tolerated, except for contact sports
In EBV infectious mononucleosis:
• Blood smear → atypical lymphocytosis (>10% atypical lymphocytes).
• Other findings: mild ↑ transaminases, ± thrombocytopenia.
•
•
•
After entering the bloodstream through the pharyngeal mucosa and tonsillar crypts, the EpsteinBarr virus (EBV) preferentially infects B lymphocytes by binding to CD21 cell surface receptors.
EBV-infected B lymphocytes then activate cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CD8+) through the
presentation of viral antigens on major histocompatibility complex class I molecules.
These reactive (atypical) CD8+ T lymphocytes are the primary immune response to EBV and
clonally expand to destroy virus-infected cells.
Atypical lymphocytes make up more than 10% of the cells in a peripheral blood smear in IM, and
most (>95%) of those cells are CD8+ T lymphocytes.
o They classically appear much larger than quiescent lymphocytes, with abundant basophilic
cytoplasm and a cell membrane that conforms to the borders of neighboring cells.
Clinical pearls:
• Rash after amoxicillin in EBV is classic — indicates misdiagnosis as strep pharyngitis.
• Avoid palpation of the spleen aggressively during physical exams.
• Avoid contact sports 2–4 weeks due to risk of splenic rupture → if occurs will present with Kehr
sign: referred shoulder pain from LUQ.
• Diagnosis is confirmed by positive Monospot or EBV serologies.
• Symptoms may last weeks, but prognosis is excellent.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 404; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 128
☆ Infectious Diseases: Epstein-Barr Virus (Infectious Mononucleosis)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 404; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 128

stem:
A 13-year-old child with persistent asthma is currently on a medium-dose inhaled corticosteroid
plus long-acting beta agonist. Despite this, his symptoms remain uncontrolled, with frequent night
awakenings and limitation in daily activity. What is the next best step in management?
answer: B. Step up to high-dose ICS + LABA
explanation:
• According to GINA and NHLBI guidelines, the next step after failure of medium-dose ICS + LABA:
o Step up to high-dose ICS + LABA (Step 5)
• SABA (short-acting beta agonists) are for rescue use, not for maintenance escalation
• Systemic corticosteroids are reserved for exacerbations or short-term control, not chronic use
due to adverse effects
• Immunomodulators (e.g., omalizumab, dupilumab) are considered only after failure of high-dose
ICS + LABA and confirmation of allergic or eosinophilic phenotype
Clinical pearls:
• Evaluate medication adherence, technique, and trigger
exposure before stepping up therapy
• Consider spirometry and FeNO testing if not already done
• Step-down therapy can be considered once asthma is controlled for ≥3 months
• High-dose ICS must be used cautiously and monitored for growth suppression in children
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 453–454; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 147
☆ Pulmonology: Stepwise Management of Asthma
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 453–454; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 147

stem:
A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after falling backward while skateboarding. He
did not lose consciousness and is alert and oriented. There is no vomiting or seizure. On physical
examination, he has bruising over the mastoid area and blood behind the right tympanic membrane.
No focal neurologic deficits are noted. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Basilar skull fracture involves the bones at the base of the skull (e.g., petrous portion of
temporal bone)
• Key signs include:
o Hemotympanum (blood behind tympanic membrane)
o Battle sign (postauricular ecchymosis), raccoon eyes, CSF rhinorrhea or otorrhea
o May have cranial nerve deficits (CN VII palsy, hearing loss)
o CSF otorrhea
• Halo sign: Blood-tinged fluid from nose/ear forms a central red spot with a clear outer ring on
gauze, suggesting a CSF leak, typically from a basilar skull fracture.
• Even in absence of LOC or vomiting, hemotympanum in this setting is highly suggestive of basilar
skull fracture
• CT head is the preferred diagnostic modality
• Requires monitoring for CSF leaks and complications like meningitis
Clinical pearls:
• Avoid placing NG/ET tubes blindly in suspected basilar skull fracture
• Admit for observation even if GCS is normal if signs of basilar fracture are present
• Neurosurgery consultation may be warranted
• Monitor for CSF leaks and signs of meningitis
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 307–308; LANGE Q&A Pediatrics, 1st ed., p. 287
☆ Emergency Medicine: Skull Fractures
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 307–308; LANGE Q&A Pediatrics, 1st ed., p. 287

stem:
A child presents to the emergency room after ingesting an unknown number of pills. He is
unresponsive, bradypneic, bradycardic, febrile, and has pinpoint pupils. What is the most
appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• This is a classic presentation of opioid toxicity, which includes:
o Bradypnea (respiratory depression)
o Bradycardia
o Pinpoint pupils (miosis)
o Decreased level of consciousness
• Although fever is not classic for opioids, it can occur with hypoventilation and hypoxia.
Naloxone is an opioid antagonist and the drug of choice in this situation. It rapidly reverses
respiratory depression and CNS effects.
•
•
•
Flumazenil is the antidote for benzodiazepine overdose but is not appropriate here. It also risks
seizures in mixed overdose.
Sodium bicarbonate is used for TCA (tricyclic antidepressant) overdose, which typically presents
with widened QRS and anticholinergic symptoms.
Activated charcoal could be considered, but airway protection and antidote come first in a
comatose child.
Clinical Pearls:
• Opioid toxidrome = miosis + respiratory depression + CNS depression.
• Always secure the airway first in comatose or bradypneic patients.
• Naloxone can be repeated or infused depending on the opioid’s half-life.
• Consider polydrug ingestion and supportive ICU care if severe.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 432–434; UpToDate, "Opioid intoxication in children"
☆ Emergency Medicine: Toxicology and Poisoning
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 432–434; UpToDate, "Opioid intoxication in children"

stem:
A 7-year-old previously healthy boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to a sudden onset of
behavioral changes. Over the past 48 hours, he has developed obsessive hand-washing rituals and
frequent blinking tics. His teacher reports he has become anxious and easily distracted in school. He
had a sore throat about 10 days ago that resolved spontaneously and was never treated. On
examination, the child is afebrile, alert, and oriented with no focal neurological deficits. What is the
most likely associated organism?
explanation:
• Explanation:
This is a classic presentation of PANDAS (Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders
Associated with Streptococcal infections).
• It is characterized by the sudden onset of OCD or tics following Group A Streptococcus
(GAS) infection, such as pharyngitis.
Other options:
• Echovirus may cause viral meningitis or hand-foot-mouth disease but not linked to OCD.
• Hepatitis viruses are associated with liver disease, not neuropsychiatric changes.
• Coronavirus (including SARS-CoV-2) may have neuroinflammatory complications, but not
classically OCD behavior like seen in PANDAS.
Clinical Pearls:
• PANDAS involves autoimmune response where anti-streptococcal antibodies cross-react with
basal ganglia.
• Diagnostic criteria: abrupt onset OCD/tics, prepubertal age, temporal association with GAS, and
neuropsychiatric symptoms.
• Treatment includes antibiotics (penicillin/azithromycin), psychiatric support, and in some
cases, immunomodulatory therapy (IVIG or steroids).
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2973–2975; UpToDate, "PANDAS: Clinical features
and diagnosis"
☆ Rheumatology: PANDAS (Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with
Streptococcus)
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2973–2975; UpToDate, "PANDAS: Clinical features
topic: PANDAS (Pediatric Autoimmune Neuropsychiatric Disorders Associated with

stem:
A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to recurrent episodes of lightheadedness and fatigue.
His physical examination is unremarkable. An ECG is conducted and provided below.
What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.label: B
options.text: Second-degree AV block Mobitz type I (Wenckebach)

options.label: C
options.text: Second-degree AV block Mobitz type II

answer: B. Second-degree AV block Mobitz type I (Wenckebach)
explanation:
• Mobitz I (Wenckebach): Progressive PR interval
prolongation followed by a non-conducted P wave
(dropped beat)
• In the attached ECG there is:
o QRS complexes clustered in groups, separated
by non-conducted P waves.
o The P:QRS conduction ratio varies from 5:4 to 6:5.
o Note the difference in PR interval between the first
and last QRS complex of each group.
• Common in athletic or vagally dominant children; often benign
• Typically asymptomatic or may present with fatigue/light-headedness
• No treatment if asymptomatic; monitor with ECG or Holter
Clinical pearls:
• First-degree AV block: Prolonged PR interval, every P wave followed by a QRS
• Mobitz II: Constant PR interval with intermittent dropped QRS, risk of progressing to complete
block
• Third-degree AV block: Complete AV dissociation (P and QRS independent)
o Neonatal SLE is associated with congenital 3rd degree AV block
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 268; Osmosis; ECG library
☆ Cardiology: Pediatric Arrhythmias & Conduction Disorders
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 268; Osmosis; ECG library

stem:
A 12-year-old boy with a known history of asthma is brought to clinic due to worsening cough,
wheezing, and intermittent low-grade fever for the past month. He has had multiple asthma
exacerbations over the past year despite adherence to inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators.
His mother also reports brownish sputum and occasional hemoptysis. On examination, he is
afebrile, with bilateral expiratory wheezes. Laboratory testing reveals eosinophilia and markedly
elevated total IgE. Chest X-ray is provided.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• ABPA is a hypersensitivity reaction to Aspergillus fumigatus seen in children with asthma or
cystic fibrosis
• Presents with chronic cough, wheezing, recurrent infiltrates, and lobar collapse or mucus
plugging
• Risk factors: Cystic fibrosis, asthma
• Diagnostic Criteria (Modified ISHAM)
o Asthma or CF
o Total serum IgE > 1000 IU/mL
o Positive Aspergillus-specific IgE or IgG
o Pulmonary infiltrates (transient or fixed)
 CXR may show transient or fixed infiltrates, lobar
collapse, or band-like opacities due to mucus impaction
 Finger-in-glove appearance (mucus-filled bronchi)
 Upper lobes > lower lobes
 Right upper lobe is most commonly involved
o CT chest (gold standard): Central bronchiectasis with normal peripheral bronchi
o Peripheral eosinophilia >500/µL
• Treatment
o First-line: oral corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone 0.5 mg/kg/day)
o Adjunct: itraconazole or voriconazole to reduce fungal burden; indication: severe
disease refractory to solo steroid therapy
o Other treatments: omalizumab (Xolair, MoA: anti-IgE antibody) may reduce exacerbations
o Monitor IgE levels to assess response
• Complications
o Irreversible bronchiectasis
o Pulmonary fibrosis
o Progressive decline in lung function
Other options:
• Bronchial asthma alone does not cause lobar collapse or significant eosinophilia
• Cystic fibrosis may have chronic cough but usually shows diffuse bronchiectasis, not isolated
lobar bands and not such high IgE
• Primary ciliary dyskinesia presents with recurrent sinopulmonary infections, situs inversus,
and low nasal nitric oxide, not with eosinophilia or high IgE
Clinical pearls:
• Suspect ABPA in any child with asthma or CF + recurrent infiltrates + high IgE
• Diagnosis confirmed with IgE >1000 + positive Aspergillus-specific antibodies + radiologic
findings
• Treated with oral corticosteroids ± antifungal therapy (e.g. itraconazole)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 481–482; UWorld.
☆ Pulmonology: Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis (ABPA)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 481–482; UWorld.

stem:
A 6-year-old child with known chronic liver disease presents to your clinic. He has steatorrhea, poor
weight gain, and low serum levels of fat-soluble vitamins. The dietitian asks you for
recommendations on nutritional support to improve nutrient absorption. What is the most appropriate
dietary modification for this child?

options.label: A
options.text: Supplement with vitamin E to enhance fat absorption

options.label: B
options.text: Increase long-chain triglycerides to support energy needs

options.label: C
options.text: Provide medium-chain triglycerides in the diet

options.label: D
options.text: Restrict all dietary fats to minimize steatorrhea

answer: C. Provide medium-chain triglycerides in the diet
explanation:
• Explanation:
Children with chronic liver disease often develop fat malabsorption, especially of long-chain fatty
acids, due to impaired bile production.
• Medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) are absorbed directly into the portal circulation without the
need for bile salts, making them ideal for patients with cholestasis or impaired fat digestion.
Other options:
• Vitamin E supplementation helps with deficiencies but does not aid absorption directly.
• Long-chain triglycerides require bile salts for digestion—ineffective in cholestasis.
• Fat restriction worsens caloric intake and growth—only modify fat type, not remove fat entirely.
Clinical pearls:
• MCTs are important in biliary atresia, Alagille syndrome, and other cholestatic liver diseases.
• Supplement all fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) in water-miscible forms.
• Monitor for growth failure and bone disease in chronic liver failure.
• Consider enteral nutrition support in severe cases with poor oral intake.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1917–1920
☆ Gastroenterology: Chronic Liver Disease & Nutrition
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1917–1920

stem:
A 7-month-old infant is brought to clinic for a routine visit. The parents ask if they can begin using a baby
walker to help the child learn to walk. They promise to supervise the infant closely during use. What is
the most appropriate recommendation?

options.label: A
options.text: Use only with protective gear such as a helmet

explanation:
• Baby walkers are not recommended by the American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) due to risk of
serious injuries including:
o Head trauma, burns, fractures, and drowning
• Supervision does not eliminate risk — injuries can happen in seconds
• Walkers do not help babies walk earlier and may even delay motor development
• Stationary activity centers or floor play are safer alternatives
Clinical pearls:
• Counsel parents during well-child visits against walker use
• Promote tummy time and natural milestones for development
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 32
☆ General Pediatrics: Injury Prevention
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 32

stem:
A 2-month-old infant is brought to clinic for a routine well-baby visit. The infant is healthy, has had normal
growth and development, and is up to date on newborn screening. When routine immunizations are offered, the
parents state that they do not believe in vaccines and refuse all vaccinations, expressing concern
about toxins and a link to autism.
What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Report the family to child protective services

options.label: C
options.text: Attempt to educate the family, and force vaccination if still refusing

options.label: D
options.text: Provide education, address concerns empathetically, and document the discussion

answer: D. Provide education, address concerns empathetically, and document the discussion
explanation:
• Vaccine hesitancy should be met with respectful, empathetic dialogue, not confrontation or
coercion.
• Providers must:
o Explore parental concerns calmly
o Offer clear, evidence-based information about vaccine safety
o Debunk myths (e.g., the autism link, which is conclusively disproven)
o Discuss community protection and risks of non-vaccination
o Offer printed Vaccine Information Statements (VIS)
o Document the discussion and offer to revisit the topic regularly
Other options:
• Reporting to CPS is inappropriate unless refusal results in imminent harm (e.g., during an outbreak
or exposure to VPDs)
• Delaying vaccination without addressing misinformation increases risk and does not promote
informed choice
• Forcing vaccination violates informed consent and legal protections—parents have the right to
refuse
Clinical pearls:
• Build trust and continuity—vaccine-hesitant families may agree in future visits
• AAP supports continuing care for vaccine-refusing families unless risk becomes unmanageable
• Use a declination form and continue to offer vaccination each visit
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 179–180
☆ Preventive Pediatrics & Ethics: Managing Vaccine Refusal
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 179–180

stem:
A mother brings her 13-month-old infant to clinic, concerned that the child has not yet developed any
teeth. Growth and developmental milestones are normal, and there is no dysmorphism or family
history of delayed dentition. At what age is primary tooth eruption considered delayed, prompting
further evaluation?
explanation:
• Primary tooth eruption normally begins between 6–10 months, most often with the lower central
incisors.
• There is wide normal variation, and eruption may be delayed up to 16–18 months in otherwise
healthy children.
• Eruption is only considered delayed if no teeth have appeared by 18 months, especially if there
are other concerning signs (e.g., growth delay, facial dysmorphism, nail/hair abnormalities).
• Earlier evaluations (6–13 months) are not necessary unless accompanied by other abnormal
findings.
Clinical pearls:
• Consider underlying causes of delayed eruption if >18 months:
o Congenital hypothyroidism
o Ectodermal dysplasia
o Down syndrome
o Nutritional deficiency (severe rickets)
• Teething has no effect on systemic signs (e.g., fever, diarrhea).
• Dental referral may be appropriate if no teeth by 18 months.
As per Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review 2nd Ed, p. 586:
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 21; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 121
☆ Growth & Development: Delayed Tooth Eruption
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 21; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 121

stem:
Child has difficulty reading and stutters, saying m-m-mmmumy. At school, she has difficulty reading
and understanding what she reads, but she understands immediately if the teacher reads aloud.
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• The child has:
o Difficulty in reading → hallmark of dyslexia (a specific learning disorder)
o Stuttering may be secondary to reading frustration, not a primary fluency disorder
• Fluency disorder refers to stuttering without reading issues
• Expressive language disorder / mixed language disorder affect vocabulary, sentence structure
— not isolated reading
•
Speech delay is unrelated to specific reading problems
📚 Source: Nelson Developmental Pediatrics; Osama Naga 2024 p. 112–114
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: Speech Disorders, Dyslexia
source: Nelson Developmental Pediatrics; Osama Naga 2024 p. 112–114

stem:
Parents had a previous child with SIDS. What is the most important preventive measure to reduce
the risk in their new infant?

options.label: A
options.text: Wrap the baby well in winter

answer: D. No smoking in the house
explanation:
SIDS is defined as the sudden death of an infant younger than one year of age, which
remains unexplained after a thorough case investigation, including performance of a complete autopsy,
examination of the death scene, and review of the clinical history.
• Parental smoking is a modifiable risk factor for SIDS
• Supine sleep (“back to sleep”), firm mattress, and avoiding overheating are protective
• Prone sleeping increases SIDS risk
📚 Source: AAP Red Book 2021–2024; Nelson Ch. 3
☆ General Pediatrics: SIDS
source: AAP Red Book 2021–2024; Nelson Ch. 3

stem:
A patient in the PICU is intubated and develops new-onset oxygen desaturation. On auscultation,
there are crackles in the lungs. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• Desaturation + new crackles while intubated → likely atelectasis or fluid overload. First step is to
improve oxygenation via PEEP, which re-expands alveoli, reduces shunting and helps
reverse collapse of dependent lung regions.
Complications of Mechanical Ventilation:
•
•
•
Furosemide is only appropriate if clinical signs clearly suggest fluid overload
Bronchodilators are used for wheezing/bronchospasm, not crackles or alveolar collapse
CXR may be ordered to support diagnosis, but not the first step in acute desaturation
Clinical pearls:
• Always ensure airway patency, suctioning, and tube position first
• PEEP adjustment is a key ventilatory strategy in hypoxemia from parenchymal lung disease
• Monitor response with SpO₂, ventilator pressures, and ABG
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 280–281
☆ PICU: Ventilator-Associated Desaturation
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 280–281

stem:
A 2-year-old child presents with acute onset of ear pain, fever, and fussiness. On otoscopic exam,
the tympanic membrane is bulging, erythematous, and immobile with pneumatic otoscopy. The child
has had symptoms for 2 days and has no history of recent antibiotic use or drug allergies.
What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
explanation:
• Amoxicillin is the first-line antibiotic for most cases of acute otitis media (AOM) in children,
especially:
o Age <2 years
o Bilateral AOM
o Presence of moderate to severe symptoms (e.g., fever, pain, bulging TM)
• Standard dose: Amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day divided BID for 7–10 days
Other options:
• Azithromycin: Used in penicillin-allergic patients, but has higher resistance rates
• Ceftriaxone IM: Reserved for vomiting, non-compliance, or treatment failure
• Observation only: Appropriate for mild unilateral AOM in children ≥2 years if follow-up is ensured
Clinical pearls:
• Reassess if no improvement in 48–72 hours
• Consider amoxicillin-clavulanate if recent antibiotic use, purulent conjunctivitis, or recurrent AOM
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 349
☆ ENT & Infectious Disease: Acute Otitis Media – Diagnosis and Empiric Therapy
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 349
topic: Acute Otitis Media – Diagnosis and Empiric Therapy

stem:
A 3-year-old child presents to the ED. On assessment:
- Opens eyes to pain
- Grunts
- Withdraws his hand from painful stimulus
What is the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score?
explanation:
Breakdown using Pediatric GCS:
• GCS scoring for young children (<5 years) is
developmentally adjusted.
• Breakdown of GCS in this case:
o Eye Opening (E): To pain → 2
o Verbal Response (V): Grunting → 2
o Motor Response (M): Withdraws from pain → 4
o Total GCS = 2 (E) + 2 (V) + 4 (M) = 8
Clinical pearls:
• GCS 13–15: mild injury
• GCS 9–12: moderate head injury
• GCS ≤8: severe — consider intubation and neuroimaging
• GCS is critical for monitoring neurological status in trauma
• Use pediatric-specific GCS criteria in children under 5 years
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 264; Osama
Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 356
☆ Emergency Medicine: Pediatric Glasgow Coma Scale
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 264; Osama

stem:
A 3-day-old male neonate presents with poor feeding, vomiting, lethargy, and hypotonia. He is born
at term with an unremarkable perinatal history. On exam, he is minimally responsive and
hyperventilating. You suspect a urea cycle disorder, most likely ornithine transcarbamylase (OTC)
deficiency. Which of the following laboratory findings is most characteristic of this condition?

options.label: A
options.text: Elevated ammonia with normal anion gap and respiratory alkalosis

options.label: B
options.text: Elevated ammonia with metabolic acidosis and ketonuria

options.label: D
options.text: Hypoglycemia with lactic acidosis and elevated methylmalonic acid

answer: A. Elevated ammonia with normal anion gap and respiratory alkalosis
explanation:
• The urea cycle is essentially a way in which the body can excrete the ammonia released from amino
acid degradation.
• Patients with urea cycle disorders typically have discrete episodes of vomiting, tachypnea, and
confusion/coma secondary to hyperammonemia (a metabolic emergency).
• Ornithine transcarbamylase deficiency is the most common urea cycle disorder and is
characterized by hyperammonemia (which is neurotoxic) and elevated urinary orotic acid.
• OTC deficiency has X-linked recessive inheritance (MC in males).
• Metabolic decompensation is often triggered by illness (eg, viral upper respiratory infection, acute
otitis media), fasting, or increased protein intake.
• Classic lab findings:
o Very high plasma ammonia (often >200 µmol/L)
o Respiratory alkalosis (due to central hyperventilation → CO2 washout)
o Normal glucose, normal anion gap, no ketosis
• Option B: suggests organic acidemias (e.g., methylmalonic acidemia)
•
•
Option C: fits galactosemia (hypoglycemia, liver dysfunction)
Option D: seen in methylmalonic acidemia or propionic acidemia
Clinical pearls:
• Organic acidemias → metabolic acidosis, ketonuria, ± hyperammonemia
• Urea cycle defects → respiratory alkalosis, no acidosis, ↑ ammonia
• OTC deficiency is X-linked, and the most common urea cycle disorder
• Diagnosis confirmed with elevated ammonia, low BUN, and ↑ urine orotic acid
• Management:
o Protein restriction, IV glucose, ammonia scavengers (sodium benzoate, phenylacetate)
o Dialysis if ammonia is severely elevated
o Liver transplant is curative
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 97–99; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 49
☆ Genetic/Metabolic Disorders: OTC Deficiency and Urea Cycle Disorders
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 97–99; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 49
topic: OTC Deficiency and Urea Cycle Disorders

stem:
A 12-month-old infant is brought to your clinic for routine vaccination. The mother is concerned about
giving live vaccines, stating that the child’s sister has leukemia and is currently undergoing
chemotherapy. She asks whether her child should receive the scheduled live vaccines. What is
the most appropriate response?

options.label: B
options.text: Do not give OPV until the grandfather is immunocompetent

options.label: C
options.text: Live vaccines are not indicated for children under 1 year of age

options.label: D
options.text: Give all scheduled vaccines, including live vaccines

answer: B. Do not give OPV until the grandfather is immunocompetent
explanation:
• OPV (oral polio vaccine) is a live-attenuated vaccine that is shed in the stool for weeks after
administration.
• In households with immunocompromised contacts, such as chemotherapy patients, OPV is
contraindicated due to the risk of vaccine-derived poliovirus transmission. Both OPV and
rotavirus are shed in the stool and thus at high risk of transmission.
• In such settings, IPV (inactivated polio vaccine) is the safe alternative.
• Varicella and MMR vaccines are safe for healthy children even if household members are
immunosuppressed — transmission risk is minimal.
• Option C is incorrect — live vaccines are recommended at 12 months, not before.
If the question in the exam only has an option that mentions “delay live vaccines until grandfather is
immunocompetent” without specific mention of OPV, I believe it will be the best choice in comparison to
other options.
Clinical pearls:
• OPV poses a unique risk via fecal-oral transmission, making it the exception among live vaccines.
• Household immunocompromised status ≠ contraindication to all live vaccines — only those
that shed.
• If OPV is mandated, consider administering IPV instead, with documentation of the
immunocompromised contact.
• Saudi immunization schedule includes OPV in selected age groups and campaigns — use with
caution in such settings.
Saudi Vaccination Schedule (MOH 2024):
• Preterm infants should receive the vaccine at actual age not the corrected age
• Only exception is Hepatitis B vaccine if birth weight < 2kg — given at 1 month or discharge
(whichever is 1st)
• BCG delay to 6 months helps avoid severe complications in patients with undiagnosed SCID
• Preschool (4-6 years) vaccines are: DTaP, OPV, Varicella, MMR
Remember: Birth, 2–4–6 months build the base, 9–12–18 add extras,
school & teen boosters follow.
- Measles monovalent at 9 months (pre-MMR)
- MMR given at 12, 18 months, and again at 4–6 years
- IPV given at 2, 4, 6 mo, OPV given at 6, 12, 18 mo, 4–6 yrs
- PCV: 3-dose primary (2, 4, 6 mo) + booster at 12 mo
- MCV4: at 9 & 12 months, booster at 18 years
- Hep A: 2-dose series at 18 & 24 months
- HPV: for girls only, at age 11-12
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, AAP, p. 40–41; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 414; Saudi
Ministry of Health Immunization Schedule
☆ Infectious Diseases: Vaccination in Immunocompromised Household Settings
Note: The following question is likely poorly collected; I answered with specific mention of “target rash” in
mind.
source: Red Book 2021–2024, AAP, p. 40–41; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 414; Saudi

stem:
A 7-year-old boy was prescribed amoxicillin for pharyngitis and completed a 7-day course. Shortly
afterward, he developed a target rash. What is the most likely cause of his rash?
explanation:
• Erythema multiforme is a delayed-type (Type IV) hypersensitivity reaction → T-cell mediated.
• EM presents with target lesions and is often triggered by infections rather than medications in
children.
•
•
•
Erythema multiforme in children is most commonly infectionassociated, particularly HSV and Mycoplasma pneumoniae.
Mycoplasma pneumoniae is a common infectious trigger in schoolaged children, and can cause mild respiratory illness followed
by erythema multiforme, even without pneumonia.
o Age: 4-15 years
o Tx: Macrolides
If the child is afebrile and well, this supports a post-infectious
phenomenon rather than an ongoing drug hypersensitivity.
Why not amoxicillin allergy?
• EM following amoxicillin is not due to a classical penicillin
allergy (which is usually IgE-mediated with urticaria or anaphylaxis).
• Amoxicillin may act as a trigger in susceptible individuals (especially
if there is underlying viral infection like EBV), but it is not the primary
cause.
• Though amoxicillin can cause a rash, it is usually morbilliform (not
targetoid), acute, associated with urticaria and more commonly
in EBV-related infections.
A better question testing this concept from Canadian Qbank (QID 3520):
A 10-year-old female presents to your office with a 4-day history of non-productive cough, low grade fever of
38.1°C (100.5°F), and now complains of a rash for one day. She has been taking an over-the-counter cough
suppressant and Tylenol. She is otherwise healthy. On physical exam, she has a temperature of 37.7°C
(99.9°F) and a respiratory rate of 20. Her lung exam reveals no rhonchi or wheezes. Her skin has several
“target” lesions scattered on her trunk and upper arms. A chest x-ray reveals a fine interstitial pattern
with normal cardiac silhouette. She most likely is infected with what organism?
A- Streptococcus pneumoniae
B- Chlamydia pneumoniae
C- Mycoplasma pneumoniae
D- Haemophilus influenzae
E- Influenza A
Explanation: This school-age child has an atypical pneumonia with erythema multiforme, which is
consistent with Mycoplasma pneumonia (choice C) infections. Mycoplasma is the most common cause of
atypical pneumonias in school-aged children.
→ Streptococcus pneumoniae (choice A) is the most common cause of typical bacterial pneumonia in
children. Patients usually are tachypneic and febrile with a consolidated lobar infiltrate on chest x-ray.
→ Chlamydia pneumoniae (choice B) may cause interstitial, atypical pneumonias. Patients are usually
afebrile. There is no associated with erythema multiforme. See Answer A.
→ Haemophilus influenzae (choice D) is a cause of bacterial pneumonia in children, again with a
consolidated lobar infiltrate.
→ Influenza A (choice E) may cause a viral pneumonia with interstitial infiltrates, but patients typically have
systemic symptoms such as fever, malaise, and myalgias.
Another case scenario (similar repeated in Part 1 2024):
A 6-year-old boy presents with fever, urticarial rash, and joint pain involving the knees and wrists. The
symptoms began 7 days after completing a 10-day course of amoxicillin for pharyngitis. He is afebrile at
presentation, and physical exam shows non-blanching urticarial rash on the trunk and extremities, no
mucosal involvement, and no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies reveal
normal platelets, normal liver enzymes, and no hematuria or proteinuria.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
A- Erythema multiforme
B- Henoch-Schönlein Purpura
C- Serum Sickness-Like Reaction
D- Stevens-Johnson Syndrome
Explanation: SSLR is a Type III hypersensitivity reaction, often triggered
by beta-lactam antibiotics (e.g., amoxicillin, cefaclor), presenting 1–2
weeks after drug exposure. Classic features include: fever,
polyarthralgia/arthritis, urticarial or morbilliform rash, absence of
mucosal involvement or systemic vasculitis findings
• Labs: normal platelets, normal renal function, no hematuria,
differentiating it from HSP.
Another potential case scenario:
A 7-year-old boy is brought to the clinic with non-blanching palpable
purpura on his lower extremities. His symptoms began a few days after
completing a 10-day course of amoxicillin for otitis media. He now
complains of colicky abdominal pain and joint pain in both knees.
Urinalysis reveals microscopic hematuria and mild proteinuria. There is no fever or lymphadenopathy.
Platelet count and coagulation profile are normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
A. Serum sickness-like reaction
B. Henoch-Schönlein purpura (IgA vasculitis)
C. Immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP)
D. Acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
Explanation: This child presents with the classic tetrad of HSP: Palpable purpura (w/o thrombocytopenia),
Abdominal pain, Arthritis/arthralgia + Renal involvement (hematuria ± proteinuria)
→ Though HSP is often post-infectious, it can rarely be drug-induced (e.g., after β-lactams like amoxicillin).
- The normal platelets and IgA-predominant small vessel vasculitis distinguish it from ITP and SSLR.
Clinical Pearls:
• Target lesions without mucosal involvement → EM → HSV, Mycoplasma pneumoniae
• Urticarial rash + fever + arthralgia after beta-lactams → Serum sickness-like → T3-HSR, cefaclor
• Palpable purpura + GI pain + hematuria → HSP (IgA vasculitis)
• Facial edema + rash + eosinophilia + hepatitis → DRESS → ASMs
• Mucosal ulcers + skin sloughing after drugs → SJS/TEN → Sulfa drugs
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 972; AAP Red Book 2021–2024, p. 568; Canadian
Qbank (QID 3520); Calgary Guide
☆ Infectious Disease: Infectious Exanthems
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 972; AAP Red Book 2021–2024, p. 568; Canadian

stem:
A 6-month-old infant presents with a high fever for 5 days, appears well with good oral intake, and no
identifiable focus of infection on physical exam. What would be the most likely cause?
explanation:
• In infants <24 months, UTI is the most common cause of fever without source, especially
with prolonged fever (>3 days) and no localizing signs.
• Teething does not cause high-grade fever or fever >38.5°C.
• Viral infections typically resolve by day 3–4, and prolonged high fever without other symptoms
warrants evaluation for UTI.
• Occult bacteremia has become exceedingly rare due to widespread pneumococcal and Hib
vaccination.
Why teething is unlikely:
• Fever pattern: Teething may cause a low-grade temperature elevation (usually <38 °C) and is selflimited, whereas this infant has a high-grade fever persisting 5 days.
• Duration: Teething-related discomfort peaks around eruption and lasts 1–3 days at most;
it never causes prolonged fever (>72 hours).
• Lack of other signs: Teething may lead to drooling, gum erythema, irritability—but not sustained
high fevers in an otherwise well-appearing infant.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 823–824; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 526–527.
☆ General Pediatrics: Fever, UTI
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 823–824; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 526–527.

stem:
A 6-year-old child is brought by his mother for evaluation of painless rectal bleeding. She reports
noticing bright red blood on the stool and toilet paper, and occasionally a soft, reddish mass
protruding from the anus during defecation, which spontaneously retracts afterward. The child
denies any pain, itching, or constipation. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Rectal prolapse presents as a painless protrusion of rectal mucosa through the anus, typically
during straining or defecation.
• It is common in children, especially with chronic diarrhea, malnutrition,
cystic fibrosis, or straining.
• It often presents with painless bleeding and a visible mass that retracts
spontaneously.
• Internal hemorrhoids are rare in children and usually not visible externally.
• External hemorrhoids are typically painful, thrombosed, and visible at
rest.
• Anal fissures present with painful defecation and streaks of blood, not
protrusion.
Clinical pearls:
• Evaluate for underlying causes: cystic fibrosis (esp. with recurrent rectal prolapse), constipation,
or diarrhea.
• Rectal prolapse in a previously healthy infant = screen for CF
• Cystic fibrosis = recurrent prolapse + failure to thrive + foul stools
• In toddlers: think toilet training, constipation, or loose stools from excessive juice
• If neuro deficits present → evaluate for spina bifida or sacral anomalies
• Management includes:
o Treating the underlying condition
o Stool softeners, avoiding straining
o Manual reduction if needed
o Rarely requires surgery unless recurrent
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 520; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 126
☆ Gastroenterology: Rectal Prolapse
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 520; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 126

stem:
A young boy presents with right-sided inguinal pain and vomiting. He appears uncomfortable on
examination. An abdominal X-ray is provided.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Inguinal hernias are common in boys and can become incarcerated, leading to bowel
obstruction
• Air-fluid levels on X-ray + inguinal pain = strong indicator of mechanical obstruction from
incarcerated bowel
• Testicular torsion causes scrotal pain but no bowel changes
• Intussusception typically presents with intermittent abdominal pain, “currant jelly” stool, and
may show a target sign on ultrasound, not plain X-ray
• Constipation does not present with acute inguinal pain or multiple air-fluid levels
In a child with signs of acute bowel obstruction (bilious vomiting, abdominal distension, ill appearance)
→ First priority = stabilize
• Decompress the GI tract with NGT
• Prevent aspiration
• ↓ abdominal pressure
• Provide time to arrange imaging is stable (CT/contrast
studies)
Stepwise Management of Suspected Surgical Abdomen:
1. NGT insertion for decompression
2. IV fluids, electrolyte correction
3. Imaging (CT abdomen if volvulus or obstruction suspected)
4. Urgent surgical consultation
Clinical pearls:
• Incarcerated hernia = surgical emergency
• Attempt gentle reduction if recent onset and no peritonitis
•
Immediate surgical referral if unsuccessful or signs of strangulation
📚 Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1241–1242
☆ Emergency Medicine: Incarcerated Inguinal Hernia

stem:
A 10-year-old child with history of previous endocarditis presents to the clinica known penicillin
allergy is scheduled to undergo a dental procedure. The procedure is considered high-risk
for bacterial endocarditis, and antibiotic prophylaxis is indicated.
What is the most appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis for this patient?
explanation:
Antibiotic prophylaxis before procedures like dental work to prevent infective endocarditis.
It is indicated for the following conditions:
• Prosthetic heart valve (mechanical or bioprosthetic)
• Prosthetic material used for heart valve repair, such as annuloplasty rings, chords or clips.
• Previous infective endocarditis
• Congenital heart disease (CHD) if:
o Unrepaired cyanotic CHD (including palliative shunts and conduits)
o Completely repaired CHD with prosthetic material or device, during the first 6 months after
the procedure
o Repaired CHD with residual defects at or adjacent to prosthetic material
• Cardiac transplant recipients who develop valvulopathy
® Except for the conditions listed above, antibiotic prophylaxis before dental procedures is not
recommended for any other types of congenital heart disease.
® In addition, antibiotic prophylaxis is not recommended for patients with valvular heart disease who are
at high risk of lE for nondental procedures (e.g., TEE, esophagogastroduodenoscopy, colonoscopy, or
cystoscopy) in the absence of active infection.
Abx prophylaxis:
• For endocarditis prophylaxis, give Amoxicillin 50 mg/kg (max 2 g) orally 30–60 minutes before the
procedure.
• If penicillin-allergic, give Clindamycin 20 mg/kg (max 600 mg) or Azithromycin/Erythromycin 15
mg/kg (max 500 mg).
Another possible question:
In which of the following cases is antibiotic prophylaxis indicated to prevent infective endocarditis prior to
dental procedures?
A- 4-year-old girl who had a VSD surgical closure at two years of age complicated by cardiogenic shock
B- 12 months old boy with history of congenital pulmonary stenosis post pulmonary valvuloplasty
complicated by severe pulmonary regurgitation
C- 5-year-old boy recently diagnosed with severely dilated cardiomyopathy listed for urgent heart
transplantation
D- 3-year-old healthy girl with history of infective endocarditis that responded to 6 weeks of IV Abx and
completely recovered
→ Answer: D
Clinical pearls:
• Endocarditis prophylaxis IS recommended if the procedure involves:
o Manipulation of gingival tissue
o Manipulation of periapical region of the teeth
Perforation of oral mucosa
Not all patients require prophylaxis; indications are limited to high-risk cardiac lesions.
First line is amoxicillin, if penicillin allergic: clindamycin, azithromycin and erythromycin are
alternatives.
o
•
•
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1047–1049
☆ Cardiology / Infectious Disease: Infective Endocarditis Prophylaxis
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1047–1049

stem:
A 7-year-old boy presents with abdominal pain, arthralgia, and a rash over the buttocks and lower
extremities, shown in the attached image. His parents report a recent upper respiratory tract
infection 1 week ago. On examination, he is afebrile, the rash is non-blanching and limited to the lower
limbs, and there is mild swelling of the knees. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria. There
is no thrombocytopenia.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
★ Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP):
Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP) is an IgA-mediated (type III HSR), leukocytoclastic vasculitis that
presents with palpable purpura, arthralgia/arthritis, abdominal pain, and/or hematuria.
• Renal involvement is due to mesangial deposition of IgA immune complexes with subsequent
mesangial cell proliferation, neutrophilic infiltration, and glomerular damage.
• Most common vasculitis in children
o 90% of affected individuals < 10 years
o Peak incidence: 6 years
• May be precipitated by an upper respiratory tract/ GI infection by 1–3 weeks.
• Diagnosis is clinical:
o Purpura (usually palpable and in clusters) or petechiae, with lower limb predominance and
without thrombocytopenia or coagulopathy.
o PLUS at least one of the following:
Abdominal pain (usually diffuse, with acute onset)
Arthritis or arthralgia (acute onset)
Renal involvement (proteinuria, hematuria)
IgA deposition (leukocytoclastic vasculitis, proliferative glomerulonephritis)
Normal laboratory values; ↓ Hemoglobin suggests GI bleeding.
Complications:
o Intussusception (ileo-ileal, unlike classic ileo-colic); due to bowel wall edema, most
common GI complication
o Renal failure (especially with nephrotic-range proteinuria or elevated creatinine)
 Chronic kidney disease if severe glomerulonephritis
o Scrotal edema in males
o Rare: pulmonary hemorrhage, CNS vasculitis
Treatment:
o Supportive care: hydration, rest, NSAIDs for arthralgia
 Avoid NSAIDs in patients with IgAV nephritis or GI bleeding
o Corticosteroids: for severe abdominal pain, renal involvement, or intussusception
 Rule out intussusception before starting systemic glucocorticoids in patients with
severe abdominal pain.
o ACE inhibitors: if persistent proteinuria
Prognosis:
o Most cases of IgAV are self-limited and only require supportive care.
o Monitor urine and BP for 6–12 months for renal sequelae
 Urinalysis (for hematuria and proteinuria) every 1–2 weeks for the first 1–2 months →
monthly up to 6–12 months
 BP monitoring with every visit




•
•
•
•
Clinical pearls:
• HSP = palpable purpura + abdominal pain ± arthritis ± renal involvement
• Most cases resolve spontaneously
• Monitor urine and BP weekly for 4–6 weeks
📚 Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Rheumatology Chapter (Vasculitis); Osama Naga 2024, Immunology
☆ Rheumatology: Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP), IgA Vasculitis

stem:
A 10-year-old boy with type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) is accompanied by his asymptomatic
younger brother. The parents are concerned about the possibility of diabetes in the sibling and inquire
about screening. What is the most appropriate next step?
explanation:
•
•
•
According to ISPAD 2024 guidelines, the gold standard for predicting T1DM is autoantibody
screening (e.g., GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8), but HbA1c is the most appropriate and accessible screening
test from the choices given.
HbA1c can detect early dysglycemia and may be elevated before overt hyperglycemia appears.
It is a non-fasting test and provides a 3-month average of glucose levels.
Other options:
• RBS: Can be variable and miss early dysglycemia
• Urine ketones: Only positive in decompensated states—not a screening tool
• FPG: May be normal in early stages; HbA1c is more sensitive in asymptomatic screening
Clinical pearls:
• Autoantibody screening remains the definitive early predictive test in first-degree relatives
• HbA1c ≥6.5% on two occasions confirms diagnosis; 5.7–6.4% indicates increased risk
📚 Source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines 2024; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, p. 234
☆ Endocrinology: T1DM Screening in First-Degree Relatives
source: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines 2024; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, p. 234

stem:
A parent gives antipyretics every time their child has a fever, believing it will prevent febrile
seizures. What is the correct response regarding this practice?

options.label: A
options.text: Give paracetamol and alternate with ibuprofen for maximum effect

options.label: B
options.text: Antipyretics have no effect on the risk of febrile seizures

answer: B. Antipyretics have no effect on the risk of febrile seizures
explanation:
• Febrile seizures are not prevented by antipyretic use. Multiple studies, including randomized
controlled trials, have shown no significant reduction in recurrence of febrile seizures with the use
of paracetamol (acetaminophen) or ibuprofen.
• Antipyretics may improve comfort, but they do not alter the risk of febrile convulsions.
• Switching to ibuprofen does not reduce seizure risk.
• Paracetamol may still be used for comfort or high fever.
• Routine antipyretic use every 4 hours for seizure prevention is not evidence-based and can increase
risk of toxicity.
Another question from Canadian QBank (QID 3488):
A 2-year-old boy presents to the emergency department for evaluation following a witnessed seizure. The
seizure was described as generalized, lasting less than 10 minutes with a short postictal period. The
child has no history of seizures, no family history of seizures, and no history of head injury. His exam
currently is normal, except for a red, bulging right tympanic membrane and a temperature of 39°C. What
is the most appropriate management for this patient?
A- He should be sent for an urgent CT scan of the head
B- He should be given antibiotics and antipyretics and observed at home
C- He should be admitted to the hospital and an EEG should be performed
D- He should be started on phenobarbital and sent home
E- A lumbar puncture and blood cultures should be obtained and anticonvulsants started in the hospital
→ Answer: B. He should be given antibiotics and antipyretics and observed at home
• This patient most likely experienced a simple febrile seizure. Most febrile seizures last less than 10
minutes, are generalized and non-focal, and do not recur within 24 hours.
o These simple febrile seizures do not require evaluation beyond determining and treating
the source of the fever and educating parents.
• There is no need for neuroimaging studies in a case of simple febrile seizure.
• There is no need for an EEG in the case of a simple febrile seizure with a negative family history,
normal neurologic exam, and normal development.
• Although anticonvulsant medications are sometimes used for recurrent episodes of febrile seizures,
the initial simple febrile seizure does not warrant treatment with anticonvulsant drugs.
• A lumbar puncture is not indicated in this patient unless the physical exam revealed symptoms
suggestive of meningitis or encephalitis.
• In this case, the examination is normal except for the otitis media, the source of the fever.
★ Febrile Seizures:
• A febrile seizure is a generally benign neurologic condition characterized by seizure and elevated
temperature (≥38 C [100.4 F]) without signs of CNS infection (eg, nuchal rigidity), metabolic
abnormalities, or a history of afebrile seizures.
• Children age 6 months to 5 years are almost
exclusively affected because seizure threshold is
lower during early years of brain development.
o Age between 6 months and 5 years is
the strongest risk factor for febrile
seizures, peaking at 18 months.
• In addition, young children are particularly
susceptible to febrile viral illnesses (eg, roseola,
influenza) associated with high fever (≥39.5 C
[103 F]), a key risk factor for development of
seizures due to hyperthermia-induced neuronal dysfunction.
Classification:
• Simple: Single, <15 min, nonfocal or tonic-clonic and does not recur within 24 hours.
• Complex: Recurrent (>1 in 24 hr), >15 min, focal, occur outside typical age range
Risk factors for recurrence:
Recurrence rate is higher in the first 2 years!
The risk of recurrent, simple febrile seizures is 33%.
•
•
•
•
age < 18 months
•
hyponatremia or hypocalcaemia at time of presentation
fever < 1 hour before seizure
FHx in first degree relative
temp lower than <40ºC
o seizures developed at low grade fever 38
Risk factors for epilepsy in febrile seizures:
1–2% risk of epilepsy (0.5–1% in gen pop)
•
•
•
•
•
complex febrile seizures
FHx of epilepsy
abnormal development history
∼ 1–2% in children who have had a simple febrile seizure
5–10% in children with complex febrile seizures or a family
history of epilepsy
Red flags → may be syndromic, heat sensitive epilepsy
•
•
•
•
hemiclonic seizures → Dravet syndrome; mutation in sodium channel 1A; sensitive to any increase in temperature (hot weather, hot
bath), photic stimulation → worsened with any sodium channel blockers
prolonged seizures
family history of seizures
afebrile seizures
Management:
• Simple → control fever, monitor; no workup needed
• Complex → consider neuro consult; may require imaging + EEG (possibly as OP)
• If seizing 5 minutes or more → abortive therapy = Diazepam
• If < 5 minutes → Paracetamol
• If non seizing in ER → Paracetamol, regardless of the duration seized at home
• Note: Rectal diazepam or nasal midazolam might be prescribed to be used as needed for children
who are prone to long febrile seizures.
o These medications are typically used to treat seizures that last longer than five minutes or if
the child has more than one seizure within 24 hours.
Indications for LP (Pediatrics 2011;127(2):389)
• Meningeal signs or ill-appearing, <1 yo, and unvaccinated or s/p recent antibiotic treatment
• Consider in <6 mo since true febrile seizures are rare at that age
Clinical pearls:
• Febrile seizures are typically benign and self-limited.
• Simple febrile seizure: generalized, <15 minutes, once in 24 hours, and age 6 months–5 years
• No need for EEG, imaging, or long-term therapy after a first simple episode
• Recurrence risk is ~30%, but risk of epilepsy is low (~2–5%)
• Educate caregivers that recurrent seizures may still occur despite antipyretics.
• Antipyretics are for comfort, not seizure prevention.
• Counsel families about first aid during seizures rather than focusing on fever suppression.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 454; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd ed., page 286; Pocket Pediatrics 2024
☆ Neurology: Febrile Seizures
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 454; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
topic: • A febrile seizure is a generally benign neurologic condition characterized by seizure and elevated

stem:
A 12-hour-old newborn presents with a history of bluish discoloration of the body during crying and
cyanosis of the extremities. The infant is otherwise alert, has stable vital signs, and no respiratory
distress. On examination, there is central cyanosis worsened by agitation, but no murmur is noted.
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
answer: A. Transposition of the Great Arteries (TGA)
explanation:
•
•
•
•
•
Despite its overall low prevalence, transposition of the great arteries (TGA) is the most common
etiology for cyanotic congenital heart disease in the newborn.
o TGA is the most common cause of cyanosis within the first 24–48 hours of life in a term
newborn.
In TGA, the aorta arises from the right ventricle and the pulmonary artery from the left ventricle,
creating parallel circulations.
Patients with TGA usually present with cyanosis in the newborn period (with cyanosis apparent
within hours of birth), but clinical manifestations and courses are influenced predominantly by the
degree of intercirculatory mixing.
o Cyanosis becomes more evident when the ductus arteriosus begins to close, as mixing
of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood becomes limited.
Unlike TOF, there is no murmur early in life and no pulmonary outflow obstruction initially.
Cyanosis at rest or during crying in the first day of life strongly suggests TGA, not VSD or truncus.
Another potential scenario, Canadian Qbank (QID 1384):
A neonate develops severe cyanosis that begins within minutes of birth. Blood drawn one hour after birth
shows metabolic acidosis with respiratory acidosis. A chest x-ray film shows a narrow base to the great
vessels and the heart resembles an egg on its side. ECG is normal. Which of the following is the most
likely diagnosis?
A- Aortic valve stenosis
B- Complete atrioventricular canal defect
C- Tetralogy of Fallot
D- Transposition of the great arteries
E- Underdeveloped (hypoplastic) left ventricle syndrome
→ Answer: D. Transposition of the Great Arteries
• This is transposition of the great arteries, in which the aorta arises from the right ventricle and the
pulmonary artery arises from the left ventricle.
• Affected babies present within minutes of birth with severe cyanosis and metabolic acidosis
secondary to inability to oxygenate tissues.
• The only exchange of blood between pulmonic and circulatory systems is typically occurring through
a patent ductus arteriosus.
• The chest x-ray changes illustrated in the question stem are typical, and are due to superposition of
the great vessels (rather than the normal side-to-side position).
• Surgical repair is usually performed within 7 to 10 days of life.
Clinical pearls:
• Always suspect TGA in a cyanotic term newborn with
no murmur and no respiratory distress.
• Approximately 5% of congenital cardiac anomalies have
transposition of the great arteries.
• Chest x-ray shows characteristic “egg-on-a-string”
appearance with narrow base of the great vessels.
• Diagnosis confirmed by hyperoxia test and echocardiography.
• Prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) should be started immediately to maintain ductal patency.
• Surgical repair is usually performed within 7 to 10 days of life.
Normal anatomy
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 397–398; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd Ed., p. 374–375
☆ Cardiology: Cyanotic Congenital Heart Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 397–398; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 10-year-old previously healthy girl presents with recurrent episodes of microscopic, painless
hematuria found on routine urinalysis. She is otherwise asymptomatic. There is no recent illness,
dysuria, or edema. Upon further history, the mother notes a family history of kidney stones. What is
the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Idiopathic hypercalciuria is the most common cause of recurrent painless microscopic
hematuria in children, especially with a family history of nephrolithiasis.
• Idiopathic hypercalciuria is a common cause of isolated microscopic hematuria in otherwise
healthy children.
• It may occur without symptoms, though gross hematuria, dysuria, or renal colic may develop over
•
•
•
•
time.
A positive family history of nephrolithiasis strongly supports the diagnosis.
Benign familial hematuria (thin basement membrane disease) is also painless and familial,
but hypercalciuria is more common and testable via spot urine calcium/creatinine ratio.
Urinary tract infections usually present with dysuria, frequency, or fever, not isolated hematuria.
PSGN presents with gross hematuria, edema, hypertension, and low complement levels following
a recent streptococcal infection.
Isolated microscopic hematuria (>5 RBC/hpf; asymptomatic):
® Most common cause of transient asymptomatic
hematuria in children: fever, exercise or menstruation
® Most common cause of persistent asymptomatic
hematuria: familial benign hematuria, hypercalciuria
® However be careful: if there is FHx of hearing deficit or
eye problems then look for Alport syndrome
® If present, redo urinalysis in a few days
Clinical pearls:
• Benign familial hematuria typically causes isolated microscopic hematuria, with normal renal
function and no proteinuria or hypertension.
• Often diagnosed based on family history and exclusion of other causes.
• Renal biopsy (if done) shows uniformly thinned glomerular basement membranes.
• No treatment is needed, and prognosis is excellent.
• Always consider hypercalciuria in painless hematuria with family history of kidney stones.
o Idiopathic hypercalciuria is more common than familial benign hematuria — even in the
absence of a family history of kidney stones.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 508–509; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 149
☆ Nephrology: Hematuria
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 508–509; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 149

stem:
A 7-year-old child presents with persistent nocturnal enuresis (bed-wetting). What is the most
effective long-term management strategy for enuresis?
explanation:
• Primary nocturnal enuresis is defined as bed-wetting in a child age ≥5 who has never achieved a
prolonged period of nighttime urinary continence.
• Enuresis alarms are the most effective long-term treatment for primary monosymptomatic
nocturnal enuresis
• They have the highest sustained success rate and work by creating a conditioned response to
bladder fullness
• Desmopressin (DDAVP) is effective short term (e.g., for sleepovers or camps) but has high
relapse rates when stopped
• Decreasing fluids and scheduled voiding are helpful adjuncts, but not curative alone
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 551–553
☆ Nephrology: Nocturnal Enuresis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 551–553

stem: A third fontanelle is most commonly associated with which of the following conditions in infants?
explanation:
• A third fontanelle is an accessory fontanelle located along the sagittal
suture, between the anterior and posterior fontanelles.
• It is considered a minor anomaly but is commonly associated
with chromosomal disorders, particularly Down syndrome (Trisomy 21).
• May also occur in hypothyroidism, but much less commonly.
• Hyperthyroidism and Turner syndrome are not associated with abnormal
fontanelle development.
Clinical pearls:
• Other craniofacial signs in Down syndrome: flat facial profile, upslanting palpebral fissures,
epicanthal folds, and hypotonia.
• Third fontanelle is a soft, midline defect — not always clinically significant on its own but should
prompt screening for syndromic features.
• Evaluation includes karyotyping, TSH screening, and echocardiogram for congenital heart
defects.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 182
☆ Genetics & Dysmorphology: Cranial Fontanelles
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 182

stem:
A neonate is intubated in the delivery room due to apnea and poor tone. On auscultation, the
heart rate is 50 beats per minute despite effective ventilation. What is the next appropriate step?
explanation:
• According to Neonatal Resuscitation Program (NRP) guidelines:
o If heart rate <60 bpm after 30 seconds of effective positive pressure ventilation (PPV),
the next step is to initiate chest compressions
o Compressions are performed at a ratio of 3:1 (compressions:breaths)
o Continue for 60 seconds, then reassess heart rate
o If HR remains <60 bpm, administer epinephrine and consider volume expansion
Clinical pearls:
• Always ensure effective ventilation first (visible chest rise)
• Oxygen concentration for initiating PPV in a term neonate 21%, in preterm neonates 21-30%
• Do not delay compressions if HR <60 despite adequate ventilation
• Epinephrine is given after compressions fail to improve HR
📚 Source: Neonatology – Gomella’s Neonatology, 8th ed., p. 64–66; NRP 8th ed.
☆ NICU: NRP
source: Neonatology – Gomella’s Neonatology, 8th ed., p. 64–66; NRP 8th ed.

stem:
A 13-year-old competitive gymnast is brought by her mother for concerns about poor weight gain.
She trains 5 days per week. Her BMI is at the 3rd percentile, down from the 10th percentile one year
ago. She eats regularly, denies any body image concerns or disordered eating. She is in Tanner stage
III, but has not yet had menarche. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure the family and schedule follow-up in 6 months

options.label: B
options.text: Advise reducing exercise and increasing caloric intake

options.label: C
options.text: Refer to dietitian and psychologist for multidisciplinary assessment

options.label: D
options.text: Order DEXA scan and recheck weight in 3 months

answer: B. Advise reducing exercise and increasing caloric intake
explanation:
• This patient does not exhibit features of an eating disorder (no purging, calorie restriction, or
distorted body image).
• However, she is demonstrating early signs of Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S):
o Low BMI with downward trend
o High physical activity
o Delayed menarche despite progression through puberty
•
•
•
•
The most appropriate first step is nutritional intervention and modification of energy
expenditure, which can often restore healthy pubertal progression and prevent amenorrhea or bone
loss.
Referral to psychology is not indicated unless disordered eating behavior or body image
issues are present.
DEXA is not a first-line test in the absence of prolonged amenorrhea or fracture history.
Delaying follow-up by 6 months would risk progression of energy imbalance.
Another potential scenario:
A 13-year-old girl is brought by her mother due to concerns about lack of weight gain. The mother reports
that her daughter appears increasingly thin over the past year. The patient participates in competitive
gymnastics, training 6 days per week. Her BMI is at the 3rd percentile, down from the 10th percentile one
year ago. The patient says she eats enough, but admits to skipping meals, counting calories, and
feels "too fat" despite reassurance. She has not yet had menarche. Physical exam shows bradycardia
and Tanner stage III breast development. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
A- Reassure the family and schedule follow-up in 6 months
B- Advise reducing exercise and increasing caloric intake
C- Refer to dietitian and psychologist for multidisciplinary assessment
D- Order DEXA scan and recheck weight in 3 months
→ Answer: C. Refer to dietitian and psychologist for multidisciplinary assessment.
Why? Here the patient presents with signs of an evolving eating disorder:
• Distorted body image (“too fat”)
• Calorie restriction behaviors (skipping meals, counting calories)
• Weight loss, bradycardia, and delayed menarche
These features strongly indicate anorexia nervosa and require urgent multidisciplinary management,
including psychological evaluation and nutritional rehabilitation.
Clinical pearls:
• Delayed menarche + declining BMI in an athlete = early RED-S until proven otherwise
• Early intervention with increased caloric intake and reduced training can restore normal pubertal
timing
• Monitor for menstrual onset and growth velocity every 1–2 months
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 100, 218
☆ Adolescent Medicine: Female Athlete Triad & Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 100, 218
topic: Female Athlete Triad & Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S)

stem:
A 1-year-old infant is brought by his parents due to persistent nasal discharge and progressive
coarsening of facial features. The child was normal at birth but has since developed a flat nasal bridge,
macroglossia, bulging fontanelle, hepatosplenomegaly, umbilical hernia, developmental delay, and
