mild joint stiffness. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Hurler syndrome (Mucopolysaccharidosis type I) is an autosomal recessive
lysosomal storage disorder due to α-L-iduronidase deficiency
• Classic presentation includes:
o Coarse facial features
o Macroglossia and flat nasal bridge
o Hepatosplenomegaly
o Umbilical hernia
o Progressive developmental delay
Joint stiffness
Corneal clouding (helps distinguish from Hunter syndrome)
Congenital hypothyroidism can mimic early findings but typically lacks organomegaly and joint
findings
Marfan syndrome presents with tall stature, lens dislocation, and aortic root dilation—not coarse
features
Osteogenesis imperfecta is associated with bone fragility and blue sclerae, not coarse facies or
GAG accumulation
o
o
•
•
•
⚠ Hurler Syndrome Key Features:
• Onset within first 2 years
• Developmental regression
• Coarse facies, macroglossia, frontal bossing
• Corneal clouding (distinguishes from Hunter)
• Hepatosplenomegaly
• Joint stiffness
• Recurrent otitis media or airway obstruction
• Cardiomyopathy or valvular disease
• Urinary GAGs: dermatan sulfate, heparan sulfate
• Diagnosis: enzyme assay, confirmed with genetic testing
• Treatment: enzyme replacement therapy, HSCT in severe cases
Suspect Hunter syndrome in boys with
coarse facies + developmental delay +
skin papules + hearing loss, without
corneal clouding.
Mnemonic: “Hunters with good
eyesight will catch the aggressive
chupacabra active in TeXas:”
→ Hunter syndrome (no corneal
clouding, aggressive behavior,
carpal tunnel syndrome, hyperactivity,
and X-linked recessive inheritance).
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 442; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 172
☆ Genetic/Metabolic Disorders: Lysosomal Storage Diseases – Hurler Syndrome
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 442; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 172

stem:
A child presents to the emergency department after accidental ingestion of kerosene. He
developed a cough immediately after ingestion, but is currently well-appearing and normal on
examination. What is the most appropriate next step?
explanation:
• Hydrocarbon (kerosene; fuel oil [‫ )]ﻏﺎز‬ingestion is usually accidental, and occurs most commonly
in children under five
• The target organs from ingestion are the lungs (most commonly), nervous, cardiac, hepatic, renal,
hematologic and dermatologic
• Even if the child appears well, immediate coughing indicates airway exposure,
and pneumonitis can develop within hours
o Aspiration produces a direct toxic effect on lung parenchyma resulting in pneumonitis
• Initially, the chest radiograph may be normal
o Symptomatic patients should have a chest x-ray
o Asymptomatic patients may have a chest x-ray 6 hours after
ingestion
o Findings include airspace disease which may be patchy at the
onset but coalesce later, particularly in the lower lobes medially
o The pattern is similar to, and may represent in some cases,
pulmonary edema
• Pneumatoceles may form after the first week
• Hemorrhagic alveolitis can peak 3 days after ingestion
• Asymptomatic patients without cough or vomiting may be observed and discharged without
imaging
• Emesis is contraindicated in hydrocarbon ingestion due to aspiration risk
• Antibiotics are not routinely needed unless secondary bacterial infection is suspected
Clinical pearls:
• Cough after hydrocarbon ingestion = red flag for aspiration
• Symptoms may be delayed, so imaging and observation for 6–8 hours is recommended
• Avoid gastric decontamination
• Asymptomatic after 6–8 hours → may discharge
• Symptomatic or abnormal CXR → admit for observation
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1154–1155
☆ Emergency / Toxicology: Hydrocarbon Ingestion
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1154–1155

stem:
A 3-week-old infant presents with a rectal temperature of 38.6°C. The infant is previously healthy,
well-appearing, and feeding normally, with no focus on infection on examination. What is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Full septic workup & start empiric antibiotics

answer: A. Full septic workup & start empiric antibiotics
explanation:
• Any febrile infant ≤28 days is considered high risk for serious bacterial infection (SBI),
including sepsis, meningitis, and UTI, regardless of appearance
• Standard of care is:
o Full sepsis evaluation: CBC, CRP, blood culture, urine culture (via catheter or
suprapubic aspiration), and lumbar puncture
o Start empiric IV antibiotics: typically ampicillin + gentamicin or cefotaxime
 prevents delay in management of life-threatening infection
o Admit to hospital for monitoring and culture results
• Discharge without workup is unsafe and not recommended for infants <28 days
• Risk stratification tools (e.g., Rochester, Philadelphia criteria) apply only to older infants (>28
days)
Latest AAP Guidelines (2021):
Evaluation and Management of Well-Appearing Febrile Infants 8 to 60 Days Old
★ Neonatal Sepsis
• Systemic illness associated with infectious source
• Overall incidence of 1-5 per 1,000 births
o In VLBW infants, rate of incidence of early sepsis is
2% and late sepsis is 25%
• Mortality rate is up to 25% (higher with prematurity)
• Types:
o Early Onset: within 1st 7 d (1st 3 d if hospitalized since
birth)
 Risk factors: prematurity, LBW, PPROM, ROM ≥18 hrs, GBS +ve mother, maternal
fever, fetal distress, chorioamnionitis, meconium fluid, maternal or fetal
tachycardia, preterm labor
 Can use early onset sepsis risk score in ≥34 weeks to guide for NICU
admission/empiric Abx
 Most common: GBS (Step agalactiae), E.coli, Listeria, Staph, Strep pneumo,
Enterococcus, Haemophilus
o Late Onset: after 7 d of life
 Risk factors: indwelling catheters, intubation, prematurity, critical illness.
 Most common: CoNS, gram-negative rods, Staph aureus, GBS, fungal
• Clinical presentation: temp instability, irritability, lethargy, poor perfusion, feeding intolerance,
respiratory distress, dyspnea (e.g., expiratory grunting), apnea (more common in preterm infants)
o ↓ temp, perfusion, BG, BP = late signs
o pH (metabolic acidosis)
o ↑ RR/HR
o Specific symptoms:
 Neonatal meningitis
• Often no signs of meningism
• Early phase: general symptoms, vomiting
• Late phase: bulging fontanelles, shrill crying, seizures, stupor
 Neonatal pneumonia
• Tachypnea with intercostal/sternal retractions and nasal flaring
• Reduced oxygen saturation with cyanosis
• Diagnosis:
Laboratory: blood culture ± UA/UCx ± LP
Other studies CBC, CRP, abdomen Xray, CXR may be indicated depending on clinical
presentation.
Management:
o Empiric Abx: Ampicillin + Gentamycin, broaden if severe clinical illness, use local
resistance guidelines.
o Modify/stop Abx based on culture data.
Protective Factors:
o Adequate IAP (≥4 hours IV penicillin or ampicillin)
o Term gestation
o Asymptomatic neonate
o No rupture of membranes or fever
o
o
•
•
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 798–799; Pocket Pediatrics; AAP Guidelines
☆ NICU: Neonatal Sepsis & Fever in Infants
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 798–799; Pocket Pediatrics; AAP Guidelines

stem:
A 15-month-old girl presents to clinic after experiencing a generalized tonic-clonic seizure during
a febrile illness. She recovered within 2 minutes and has returned to baseline. There is no focality, no
prior seizures, and her development is normal. The mother is anxious and asks what to do in the future
when her child has a fever. What is the most appropriate advice to give the mother?

options.label: A
options.text: Start antiepileptic medication if fever recurs

options.label: B
options.text: Give paracetamol at the onset of fever to prevent seizure

options.label: C
options.text: Observe for fever, and seek medical attention only if seizure occurs

options.label: D
options.text: Seek medical care promptly for fever;

answer: C. Observe for fever, and seek medical attention only if seizure occurs
explanation:
• This is a simple febrile seizure: generalized, <15 minutes, one in 24 hours, child returns to baseline,
aged 6 months–5 years.
• Antiepileptic medications are not indicated for simple febrile seizures.
• Paracetamol (acetaminophen) may be given to reduce discomfort but does not prevent seizures.
• Parents should be reassured and advised to observe during febrile illnesses, but ER visits are not
needed for fever alone.
• Medical care is warranted only if seizures recur, are prolonged, or the child appears ill.
Clinical pearls: (For further details about febrile seizures, see explanation under Q71)
• ~30% recurrence risk with future febrile illnesses
• Antipyretics do not reduce seizure recurrence risk
• Emphasize first-aid seizure precautions, not overmedicalization of fever
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 328–329
☆ Neurology: Febrile Seizures – Parental Counseling & Follow-Up
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 328–329

stem:
A young boy presents with gingival bleeding and joint involvement. Laboratory studies show
a prolonged bleeding time and prolonged aPTT. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
•
•
•
•
•
von Willebrand Disease (vWD) affects both primary
hemostasis (platelet adhesion) and secondary
hemostasis (via decreased factor VIII)
o Inheritance is AD, can be seen in both males &
females
Mucosal bleeding (e.g., gums, epistaxis) and occasional
hemarthrosis in severe cases
Labs: ↑ bleeding time + possibly ↑ aPTT
Hemophilia A/B = joint bleeds with isolated aPTT
elevation, normal bleeding time
o Inheritance is X-linked recessive, more
commonly in males
ITP = mucocutaneous bleeding due to low platelets, with normal coagulation studies
Key Board Tips:
• vWD = MC inherited bleeding disorder; ↑ bleeding time, may have ↑aPTT; suspect in menorrhagia
• Hemophilia A/B = Hemarthrosis, male child, prolonged aPTT only
• ITP = Isolated thrombocytopenia, post-viral, well-appearing child
• DIC = Sick child + bleeding + thrombosis, abnormal all labs
• Platelet function disorders = Normal count, abnormal function tests
★ Overview of Bleeding Disorders:
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 236–238
☆ Hematology: Bleeding Disorders
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 236–238
topic: 📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 236–238

stem:
A 3-year-old child presents with irritability, nausea, and intermittent vomiting for the past week. A
recent CBC showed microcytic anemia. What is the most likely cause?
explanation:
Lead inhibits rate-limiting enzymes in heme synthesis, ferrochelatase, delta amino levulinic
acid dehydrogenase → accumulation of precursors and hypochromatic, microcytic anemia.
• Lead poisoning can cause disruption of heme synthesis, leading to microcytic hypochromic
anemia that may resemble IDA but does not respond to iron therapy
•
•
•
•
Iron deficiency may also cause microcytosis, but the combination of GI and neurologic symptoms
favors lead toxicity.
A classic clue is pica or environmental exposure (e.g., old paint, pottery, soil)
Screening in high-risk pediatric populations
o Living in older housing with peeling paint
o Sibling or playmate with lead poisoning
o Recent immigrant, refugee, or foreign adoptee
o History of pica
Treatment of lead toxicity
o Decrease exposure: professional lead paint abatement, dust reduction, minimization of
contact with bare soil
o Nutrition: calcium, iron, zinc, vitamin C, and vitamin D-rich diet
o Chelation therapy:
o Treatment based on level:
 <45 µg/dL → Remove exposure, monitor, nutrition (iron, Ca, vitamin C)
 45–69 µg/dL → Oral chelation with Succimer (DMSA)
 ≥70 µg/dL or symptomatic → Hospitalization + IV EDTA + BAL (dimercaprol)
 If Iron-deficient → Replace iron before chelation begins
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Hematology, p. 394; Nelson Essentials, Environmental Toxins
☆ Hematology: Anemia, Lead Poisoning
source: Osama Naga 2024, Hematology, p. 394; Nelson Essentials, Environmental Toxins

stem:
A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents due to persistent vomiting,
diffuse abdominal pain, and lethargy for the past 2 days. The child has also been refusing food and is
increasingly sleepy. The family lives in an old apartment building undergoing recent renovations. His
development was previously normal. No fever, diarrhea, or recent travel is reported. On exam, the child
is lethargic but arousable. Vital signs: T 37.4°C, HR 125 bpm, RR 28, BP 94/56 mmHg. He has pallor,
a slightly distended abdomen, and is hypoactive neurologically but without focal deficits. No
organomegaly or rash. A CBC is obtained, showing:
- Hemoglobin: 7.8 g/dL
- MCV: 68 fL
- WBC: 8,000/µL
- Platelets: 250,000/µL
A peripheral blood smear is attached below:
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Basophilic stippling is a hallmark finding in lead poisoning, due to impaired RNA degradation in
erythrocytes
• Clinical signs of lead toxicity include:
o Lethargy, vomiting, abdominal pain, constipation
Irritability, developmental delay, anemia
Other labs: microcytic anemia, elevated serum lead, elevated free erythrocyte protoporphyrin
Iron deficiency may cause pica and microcytosis but does not cause stippling
Thalassemia may show target cells and microcytosis but lacks symptoms of toxicity
Hemolytic anemia shows schistocytes or spherocytes, not stippling
o
•
•
•
•
★ Lead Poisoning:
• Classic signs of lead poisoning:
o Lethargy, vomiting, abdominal pain, constipation
o Irritability, developmental delay, anemia
o PICA (craving for non-food items)
• Children with environmental exposure (old homes, pottery, imported toys) are at risk.
o Always screen with venous lead level if PICA + GI + behavior issues.
• Lead inhibits rate-limiting enzymes in heme synthesis, ferrochelatase, delta amino levulinic acid
dehydrogenase → accumulation of precursors and hypochromic microcytic anemia.
o Iron deficiency may also cause microcytosis, but the combination of GI and neurologic
symptoms favors lead toxicity.
• Basophilic stippling is a hallmark finding in lead poisoning, due to
impaired RNA degradation in erythrocytes
• Screening in high-risk pediatric populations
o Living in older housing with peeling paint
o Sibling or playmate with lead poisoning
o Recent immigrant, refugee, or foreign adoptee
o History of pica
• Treatment of lead toxicity
o Decrease exposure: professional lead paint abatement,
dust reduction, minimization of contact with bare soil
o Nutrition: calcium, iron, zinc, vitamin C, and vitamin D-rich diet
o Chelation therapy:
o Treatment based on level:
 <45 µg/dL → Remove exposure, monitor, nutrition (iron, Ca, vitamin C)
 45–69 µg/dL → Oral chelation with Succimer (DMSA)
 ≥70 µg/dL or symptomatic → Hospitalization + IV EDTA + BAL (dimercaprol)
 If Iron-deficient → Replace iron before chelation begins
Purple-blue line on the gums
(lead line or Burton line)
Lead lines on long bone x-ray
(dense bands at the metaphyses)
Lead flecks on abdominal xray
Clinical pearls:
• Lead toxicity is diagnosed by venous blood lead level
• Start chelation therapy if BLL ≥ 45 µg/dL
• Remove environmental exposure (e.g., peeling paint, imported toys)
📚 Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 427
☆ Emergency Medicine / Toxicology: Lead Poisoning

stem:
A 10-year-old girl with a 6-month history of polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) presents for
routine follow-up. She appears well but complains of mild fatigue. Physical examination is
unremarkable. Her labs show:
- Hemoglobin: 10.5 g/dL
- MCV: 80 fL
- Ferritin: 300 ng/mL
- Serum iron: low
- TIBC: low
What is the most likely cause of her anemia?
explanation:
• This patient has normocytic anemia with elevated ferritin and low TIBC, typical of anemia of
chronic disease (ACD), which is common in chronic inflammatory conditions like JIA.
• Ferritin is increased as an acute-phase reactant.
• Iron is sequestered in macrophages due to elevated hepcidin, decreasing availability despite
normal or increased stores.
• TIBC is low because liver reduces transferrin production in inflammation.
• Iron deficiency anemia would show low ferritin and high TIBC
• Hemolytic anemia would show reticulocytosis, elevated LDH, indirect bilirubin
• Vitamin B12 deficiency causes macrocytic anemia, not normocytic
★ Anemia of Chronic Disease:
• Most common anemia in hospitalized and chronically ill children
• Associated with chronic infections, autoimmune diseases, malignancy, CKD
• Pathophysiology
o Inflammation → ↑ IL-6 → ↑ hepcidin → ↓ iron absorption and trapping of iron in
macrophages
o ↓ erythropoietin response, impaired erythropoiesis
•
•
•
•
Lab Findings
o Low serum iron
o Low TIBC
o Normal or ↑ ferritin (due to inflammation)
o Low transferrin saturation
o Mild normocytic or (later stage) microcytic anemia
Diagnosis
o Based on clinical context + characteristic iron panel
o Must exclude iron deficiency (low ferritin confirms IDA)
Treatment
o Treat underlying disease
o Iron therapy not effective unless concurrent iron
deficiency
o Erythropoietin may be considered in CKD
Key distinguishing features from iron deficiency anemia
o Ferritin is low in IDA, high/normal in ACD
o TIBC is high in IDA, low in ACD
o Serum iron is low in both
Clinical pearls:
• In ACD, ferritin is high but iron is unavailable for erythropoiesis
• Always correlate iron studies with inflammatory markers
• Consider combined ACD + iron deficiency if ferritin is borderline or low-normal
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 487–488
☆ Hematology: Anemia of Chronic Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 487–488
topic: • Most common anemia in hospitalized and chronically ill children

stem:
A 2-year-old previously healthy child is brought for evaluation of progressive pallor. He has no
dysmorphic features, normal growth and development, and no hepatosplenomegaly or
lymphadenopathy. There are no signs of infection, or systemic illness. Investigations show:
- Isolated normocytic anemia
- Bone marrow: reduced erythroid precursors, normal myeloid and megakaryocytic lines
- ADA enzyme activity: normal
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• TEC is a self-limited, acquired red cell aplasia that typically occurs in children aged 6 months to
3 years
• It is characterized by:
o Progressive normocytic normochromic anemia
o Reticulocytopenia
o Normal WBCs and platelets
o Bone marrow: selective decrease in erythroid precursors
• It follows viral infections (e.g., parvovirus B19) but often has no clear trigger
• ADA enzyme normal + no dysmorphic features or anomalies → rules out Diamond-Blackfan anemia
• No physical anomalies or pancytopenia → rules out Fanconi anemia and aplastic anemia
Clinical pearls:
• TEC is the most common cause of acquired red cell aplasia in children
• Patients are typically previously healthy, without congenital anomalies
• Spontaneous recovery in weeks to months — treatment is usually supportive
•
•
Differentiate from Diamond-Blackfan anemia:
o DBA presents in infancy
o Associated with craniofacial anomalies, thumb malformations, and elevated ADA
enzyme
Aplastic anemia involves all three cell lines and may be associated with viral triggers, medications,
or autoimmune conditions
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 437–438; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 167
☆ Hematology: Normocytic Anemia – TEC vs. DBA
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 437–438; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 167

stem:
A 10-year-old child presents with a 2-month history of night sweats, fever, weight loss, and
generalized lymphadenopathy. On physical examination, the child is pale but alert. A chest X-ray
reveals a large mediastinal mass. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• The triad of night sweats, fever, and weight loss ("B symptoms") with generalized lymphadenopathy
and a mediastinal mass is classic for lymphoma, particularly Hodgkin lymphoma in older children.
• Mediastinal involvement is common in Hodgkin lymphoma (especially the nodular sclerosing subtype).
Mnemonic: "The 4 Terrible T’s" of Anterior Mediastinal Masses
1. Thymoma → Teen with anterior mass + myasthenia gravis
2. Teratoma (germ cell tumors) → Anterior mass + calcifications +
↑AFP/β-hCG
3. Thyroid mass (substernal goiter) → Mass moves with swallowing ±
thyroid symptoms
4. Terrible lymphoma (Hodgkin & non-Hodgkin) → Mediastinal mass
+ B symptoms (fever, weight loss, night sweats) in adolescent
Clinical Pearls:
• Leukemia can cause lymphadenopathy but usually presents with pallor, petechiae, bone pain, and
diffuse marrow suppression signs.
• Neuroblastoma may cause a mediastinal mass but is more common in younger children (<5 years) and
often originates from the adrenal glands or sympathetic chain, not generalized lymphadenopathy.
• Tuberculosis can cause lymphadenopathy and constitutional symptoms, but mediastinal mass with
widespread nodes is less typical and onset is usually more subacute/chronic.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 210–211; Osama Naga, 3rd Ed., p. 479–481
☆ Oncology: Anterior Mediastinal Mass, Lymphoma
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 210–211; Osama Naga, 3rd Ed., p. 479–481

stem:
A 3-year-old boy with newly diagnosed cystic fibrosis (CF) is admitted with fever, productive cough,
and pleuritic chest pain. Chest X-ray reveals a localized cavitary lesion consistent with a lung
abscess. He has no history of prior hospitalizations. What is the most likely causative organism?
explanation:
• In young children with CF, Staphylococcus aureus (especially MSSA or MRSA) is the most
common early pathogen, often colonizing the airway before Pseudomonas appears.
• S. aureus can cause necrotizing pneumonia and lung abscess, particularly in the early years
before chronic Pseudomonas colonization is established.
• Pseudomonas aeruginosa becomes more common in older CF patients with repeated
hospitalizations and progressive lung disease.
• S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae are less common in CF due to altered mucus clearance and
chronic airway inflammation.
Clinical pearls: (For more details on organisms in CF lung infections, refer to explanation under Q12)
• Always consider S. aureus in young CF patients with severe or focal lung infections
• Empiric antibiotics should include coverage for MRSA until sensitivities return
• If recurrent infections or older age → suspect Pseudomonas
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 384
☆ Pulmonology: CF Respiratory Pathogens – Age-Based Microbiology
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 384

stem:
A 2-week-old boy is brought to the emergency department with poor feeding, vomiting, and lethargy.
On examination, he is dehydrated, has weak pulses, and appears ill. His labs reveal:
- Sodium: 124 mmol/L
- Potassium: 6.8 mmol/L
- Glucose: 45 mg/dL
- Bicarbonate: 18 mmol/L
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• 21-hydroxylase deficiency, the most common form of CAH, leads to salt-wasting crisis in the first
1–3 weeks of life → males will have normal genitalia and thus more likely to present in salt wasting
crisis.
• Aldosterone deficiency → hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypovolemia
• Cortisol deficiency → hypoglycemia
• Metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate) is common due to volume depletion and impaired perfusion.
• Pyloric stenosis presents with hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, not
hyperkalemia or hyponatremia.
Board Clues / Buzzwords for CAH
• “1-week-old with vomiting, dehydration, and ambiguous genitalia”
• “46 XX with clitoromegaly and hyponatremia”
• “↑ 17-OHP on newborn screen”
•
•
•
•
•
•
“Salt-wasting crisis in male neonate”
21⍺ hydroxylase deficiency (MC) — ↑ 17-OHP; ambiguous genitalia in females
o Male children have normal genitalia.
o Laboratory studies in all forms of 21-hydroxylase deficiency reveal elevated 17-OHP and
testosterone levels.
11β hydroxylase deficiency (2nd MC) — ↑ 17-DOC; ambiguous genitalia in females + hypertension +
hypokalemia
17⍺ hydroxylase deficiency — ambiguous genitalia in males + hypertension + hypokalemia, no
puberty in females
5⍺ reductase deficiency — ambiguous genitalia
in males
Side-chain cleavage enzyme deficiency (LETHAL)
Treatment
Acute (Salt-wasting crisis):
• IV fluids (NS 20 mL/kg bolus)
• Hydrocortisone IV: 25 mg (infant), 50 mg (child), 100 mg (adolescent)
• Correct electrolyte abnormalities
Maintenance:
• Hydrocortisone (preferred steroid in children): 10–15 mg/m²/day, divided TID
• Fludrocortisone (if salt-wasting): 0.05–0.2 mg/day
• NaCl supplementation for infants (1–2 g/day)
• Risk of adrenal crisis during illness → stress dosing of steroids essential
Clinical pearls:
• CAH should be suspected in any neonate with dehydration, hyperkalemia, and hyponatremia
• In male infants, genitalia may appear normal, so diagnosis can be delayed
• Treatment: IV fluids, hydrocortisone, and fludrocortisone
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 222–223
☆ Endocrinology: CAH – Salt-Wasting Crisis in Neonates
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 222–223

stem:
A newborn is noted to have signs of respiratory distress shortly after birth. On physical examination,
he is large for gestational age (LGA). A systolic murmur is heard best at the left lower sternal border.
Echocardiogram reveals features of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) with asymmetric septal
hypertrophy. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause?
explanation:
• Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) in the neonatal period, especially with asymmetric septal
hypertrophy, is a classic finding in infants of diabetic mothers (IDMs)
• This occurs due to fetal hyperinsulinemia, which stimulates cardiac muscle growth, especially
the interventricular septum
• Usually presents with:
o LGA infant
o Murmur (due to LVOT obstruction)
o Signs of poor perfusion or respiratory distress
• It is typically transient, improving over weeks as insulin levels normalize
• Noonan syndrome can also present with HCM, but is associated with other dysmorphic features
and is not the most likely cause here
• Mitochondrial disorders and rubella can cause cardiomyopathy, but they are less common and not
associated with LGA or transient forms
Clinical pearls:
• Always screen IDMs for cardiac anomalies, including septal hypertrophy and LVOTO
• HCM due to IDM often resolves within weeks to months, unlike genetic forms
• Monitor for hypoglycemia, hypocalcemia, polycythemia,
and hyperbilirubinemia in IDMs
• EKG and echocardiography help distinguish obstructive vs. nonobstructive forms of HCM
• IDM has hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia, hypoglycemia,
polycythemia and hyperbilirubinemia – think electrolytes are
decreased, cellular contents are increased
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 220; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 33
☆ Cardiology: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy – Infant of Diabetic Mother
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 220; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 33
topic: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy – Infant of Diabetic Mother

stem:
A 2-year-old girl presents with fever and irritability. Urinalysis shows positive leukocyte esterase
and positive nitrites. A urine culture is pending. Which of the following findings is most specific for
urinary tract infection (UTI)?
explanation:
• Nitrites are produced when gram-negative bacteria (especially E. coli) reduce urinary nitrates to
nitrites.
• Nitrite positivity on urinalysis is highly specific (but not sensitive) for UTI.
• Leukocyte esterase suggests pyuria, but may be present in other conditions or contamination.
• WBCs in urine indicate inflammation but are not specific.
• Proteinuria may be seen in fever, UTI, or other kidney conditions and is nonspecific.
★ Pediatric Urinary Tract Infection (UTI):
• Definitions (NICE Guidelines 2022):
o Lower UTI (Cystitis) – localized infection of the bladder.
o Upper UTI (Pyelonephritis) – involving the kidneys,
leading to systemic symptoms.
o Atypical UTI – features that suggest more serious
infection or underlying pathology (see box 1).
o Recurrent UTI – defined as: ≥2 episodes of upper UTI OR
≥1 upper + ≥1 lower UTI OR ≥3 lower UTIs.
• Epidemiology: Most common bacterial infection in infants and
young children
o Peak incidence: infants <12 months (especially
uncircumcised males), toddlers (females)
o Most common organism: Escherichia coli (>80%)
• Risk factors: Female sex, Uncircumcised males, Vesicoureteral reflux (VUR), Constipation,
Infrequent voiding (withholding with toilet training; delayed bladder emptying), History of previous
UTI, Labial adhesions in girls
• Clinical presentation:
o Assume that babies and children who have bacteriuria but no systemic symptoms or signs
have lower UTI (cystitis).
Infants: fever, irritability, poor feeding, vomiting
Toddlers: abdominal pain, vomiting, fever, foul-smelling urine
Older children: dysuria, urgency, frequency, abdominal/flank pain
Diagnosis:
o Urinalysis (UA):
 Leukocyte esterase: indicates pyuria
 Nitrites: specific for gram-negative bacteria
(e.g., E. coli)
 Pyuria: >5 WBCs/hpf
 Bacteriuria on microscopy
o Urine collection method:
 Catheterization (preferred in <2 years)
 Clean catch (in toilet-trained children)
 Suprapubic aspiration (in neonates or when
needed)
o Urine culture:
 ≥50,000 CFU/mL from catheterized specimen with pyuria is diagnostic
 Clean catch: ≥100,000 CFU/mL
Treatment:
o Infants <3 months: Always refer to the hospital for IV antibiotics. UCx before Tx.
o Children 3 months - 3 years: Fever, vomiting, poor feeding, irritability, abdominal pain, or
smelly urine → Consider UTI.
 Urine dipstick (leukocytes & nitrites): Both positive → Treat as UTI. Only nitrites
positive → Treat & send urine for culture. Only leukocytes positive → Send urine for
culture, consider other infections.
o Children >3 years: Classic symptoms: Dysuria, urgency, frequency, suprapubic pain.
 Dipstick test sufficient in most cases. UCx if atypical features or recurrent.
o Empiric antibiotics (adjusted after culture):
 Oral for uncomplicated mild cystitis: amoxicillin-clavulanate, TMP-SMX , cefixime,
cephalexin
 IV for pyelonephritis or severe infection: ceftriaxone (avoid in neonates due to risk of
kernicterus), cefotaxime, gentamicin (if toxic-appearing or <2 months)
o Duration:
 7–14 days (typically 10 days for febrile UTI/pyelonephritis)
 Shorter duration (5–7 days) for lower UTI in older, afebrile children
Imaging:
o Renal and bladder ultrasound (RBUS): Infants <6 months with first UTI, Any child with
atypical or recurrent UTI.
 done within 6 weeks
o Voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG): If RBUS is abnormal (e.g., hydronephrosis, scarring),
Recurrent febrile UTIs
 Done weeks after infection.
o DMSA scan: evaluates for renal scarring (not routine). Done 4-6 months after atypical or
recurrent UTI.
Complications: Renal scarring, Hypertension, Chronic kidney disease, Recurrence
Prevention:
o Address constipation
o Encourage regular voiding
o Maintain hydration
o No routine antibiotic prophylaxis unless high-grade VUR with recurrent febrile UTIs <3yrs
o
o
o
•
•
•
•
•
Clinical Pearls:
• Always suspect UTI in a febrile infant with no localizing source
• Nitrites are highly specific, but not always present (need ~4 hours bladder dwell time)
o Positive nitrite + leukocyte esterase is highly predictive of UTI in febrile infants
• Always confirm with urine culture (≥50,000 CFU/mL for catheterized sample) for organism and Abx
sensitivity
UA + culture both required for diagnosis in non-toilet-trained child
E. coli is the most common pathogen in >80% of pediatric UTIs → any organism other than E.coli is
considered an atypical UTI
Early treatment prevents renal scarring
Boys <1 year with UTI → consider anatomical workup
o
•
•
•
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 558; UWorld; Pediatric UTI NICE Guidelines 2022
☆ Nephrology: Pediatric Urinary Tract Infection – Diagnosis & Urinalysis Findings
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 558; UWorld; Pediatric UTI NICE Guidelines 2022
topic: Pediatric Urinary Tract Infection – Diagnosis & Urinalysis Findings

stem:
A hospitalized child receiving an intravenous medication suddenly develops flushing and redness
of the upper body, especially the face, neck, and chest, during the infusion. There is no hypotension
or urticaria. What is the most likely cause?
explanation:
• This is a classic presentation of Red Man Syndrome, a rate-related infusion reaction caused by
vancomycin
• It is not a true allergy, but a histamine-mediated reaction associated with:
o Flushing, erythema, pruritus, especially of the face and upper torso
o May also cause hypotension if severe
• Occurs more commonly when vancomycin is given too rapidly
• Management:
o Stop or slow the infusion immediately (typically occurs when infused faster than 10
mg/min); VIR can usually be prevented by administering the drug at rates <10
mg/minute.
o Administer antihistamines (e.g., diphenhydramine 1 mg/kg IV, max 50 mg).
o Wait for symptoms to resolve, then restart vancomycin at a slower rate (≤10 mg/min
or over ≥60–90 minutes).
o Pretreat with antihistamines for future doses if needed.
o Administer antihistamines (e.g., diphenhydramine) prior to future doses if needed
Clinical pearls:
• Infuse vancomycin over at least 60 minutes, or 10 mg/min max
• Not an indication to stop the drug unless reaction is severe or recurrent despite precautions
Note: Red Man Syndrome is not a true IgE-mediated allergy, so vancomycin can still be used with
precaution.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1106
☆ Pharmacology / Infectious Disease: Vancomycin – Red Man Syndrome
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1106

stem:
A 5-year-old child presents with chronic cough, recurrent sinus infections, and recurrent otitis
media. Physical exam reveals nasal congestion, and imaging shows bronchiectasis. A chest X-ray
reveals situs inversus. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Kartagener syndrome is a subset of primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD) characterized by:
o Bronchiectasis
o Chronic sinusitis
o Recurrent otitis media
o Situs inversus (seen in ~50% of cases)
• Due to defective dynein arms in cilia, leading to impaired mucociliary clearance
• Cystic fibrosis can also cause bronchiectasis and chronic infections, but is typically associated
with pancreatic insufficiency and positive sweat chloride test, and situs inversus is not a feature
• IgA deficiency predisposes to sinopulmonary infections but not situs inversus or bronchiectasis
• Asthma causes chronic cough but not structural lung changes or recurrent sinus/ear infections
Clinical pearls:
• Suspect PCD/Kartagener syndrome in children with:
o Chronic wet cough + recurrent sinusitis + otitis media
o Laterality defects (e.g., situs inversus)
• Can also initially present as TTN
• Diagnostic workup:
o Nasal nitric oxide test (screening) → low
o Electron microscopy or genetic testing confirms diagnosis
• Management is supportive:
o Airway clearance, antibiotics for infections
o Hearing evaluations
o Fertility counseling in adolescence
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 456–457; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 150
☆ Pulmonology: Primary Ciliary Dyskinesia – Kartagener Syndrome
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 456–457; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 150

stem:
A 6-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to increasing difficulty keeping his eyes
open, especially toward the end of the day. They report that his eyelids droop in the evening, and he
appears to have a "sad" expression despite feeling happy. His appetite and cognitive function are
normal, and there are no signs of recent infection. On examination, he has bilateral ptosis and weak
extraocular movements that worsen with repeated testing.
What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
explanation:
• The clinical picture suggests ocular myasthenia gravis (fluctuating ptosis and fatigable weakness
in the absence of systemic signs)
• Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disease that causes a decreased number of functional
acetylcholine receptors within the neuromuscular junction. This reduces the number of
postsynaptic cation channels that can open in response to acetylcholine, which reduces the
amplitude of motor end plate potential and prevents muscle fiber depolarization.
• Neostigmine, an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, is the first-line diagnostic and therapeutic option
for symptomatic improvement
o It provides temporary enhancement of neuromuscular transmission by increasing ACh at
the neuromuscular junction
• If poor response, diagnosis may be confirmed via:
o Anti-AChR antibody testing
o Repetitive nerve stimulation
o Edrophonium (Tensilon) test
o CT chest (to rule out thymoma)
★ Myasthenia Gravis:
• Definition: Autoimmune disorder caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors at
the neuromuscular junction → impaired neuromuscular transmission.
• Types:
o Transient neonatal MG – due to maternal antibodies, self-limited
o Congenital MG – genetic defect in NMJ transmission
o Juvenile MG – acquired autoimmune, more common in females
• Presentation:
o Fluctuating muscle weakness, worsens with activity, improves with rest
o Ptosis, diplopia, fatigable chewing, nasal voice
o Bulbar symptoms: dysphagia, poor sucking, weak cry (infants)
o Normal reflexes and sensation
o Respiratory crisis may require ventilation
• Diagnosis:
o Edrophonium (Tensilon) test – rapid symptom improvement (used less now)
o Anti-ACh receptor antibody test – positive in most autoimmune MG
o EMG with decremental response to repetitive stimulation
o CT/MRI chest – evaluate for thymoma or thymic hyperplasia
• Management:
o Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors: Pyridostigmine (first-line),
neostigmine – Dose: 0.5–1 mg/kg/dose PO q4–6h
o Corticosteroids if refractory
o IVIG or plasmapheresis for myasthenic crisis
o Thymectomy in generalized MG (if thymoma or no response to
meds)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 831–832; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 281
☆ Neurology: Myasthenia Gravis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 831–832; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 281
topic: • Definition: Autoimmune disorder caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors at

stem:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to the clinic for chronic constipation and soiling. The parents report that he
has never had regular bowel movements, and toilet training has been unsuccessful. On exam, you
note decreased lower limb tone, absent deep tendon reflexes in the ankles, and flattening of the
gluteal cleft. No stool is palpable in the abdomen. What is the most appropriate next step in
evaluation?

options.label: A
options.text: Begin bowel cleanout and stool softeners

options.label: B
options.text: Check anal wink and cremasteric reflexes

answer: B. Check anal wink and cremasteric reflexes
explanation:
• This child has red flag signs for a neurogenic cause of constipation/soiling, including hypotonia,
hyporeflexia, and sacral abnormalities.
• The anal wink (S2–S4 reflex arc) and cremasteric reflex (L1–L2) help assess sacral and lumbar
spinal cord integrity.
• An absent anal wink would prompt further workup, including MRI of the spine for occult spinal
dysraphism, tethered cord, or spina bifida occulta.
• Red flags in constipation (suggest organic cause)
o Onset <1 month of age
o Delayed meconium (>48 hrs) → think Hirschsprung
o Bilious vomiting, failure to thrive
o Neuro signs (weak legs, abnormal reflexes) → think spinal dysraphism
o Severe abdominal distension
o Anal stenosis or ectopic anus
Anal wink reflex (S2-S4): Stroke the
perianal skin (usually with a cotton
swab or fingertip) → normal response
is a brief contraction of the external
anal sphincter
Other options:
• Bowel cleanout is first-line for typical constipation, but inappropriate here with neurologic signs.
•
•
Abdominal X-ray adds little when no fecal mass is palpable and red flags are present.
Behavioral therapy is helpful in functional encopresis but not in suspected neurologic cases.
Clinical pearls:
• Always examine the lower back and check reflexes in children with chronic constipation or soiling
• Neurogenic causes must be excluded before labeling as functional constipation
• Early detection of tethered cord can prevent irreversible neurologic damage
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 191
☆ Gastroenterology: Red Flags in Pediatric Constipation – Neurologic Evaluation
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 191
topic: Red Flags in Pediatric Constipation – Neurologic Evaluation

stem:
During evaluation of a heart murmur in a child, which of the following is considered a red flag indicating a
pathologic murmur?
explanation:
• A thrill is a palpable vibration over the chest wall due to high-velocity turbulent flow, and is a
classic red flag for a pathologic murmur
• Murmurs that are position-dependent (e.g., change with sitting or standing) are typically innocent
• Fever-only murmurs are often functional flow murmurs due to increased cardiac output
• Soft systolic murmurs, especially grade I–II, are commonly benign in children
Clinical pearls:
• ★ Red flags in pediatric murmurs
o Thrill (Grade IV or higher murmur)
o Diastolic murmur
o Cyanosis or failure to thrive
o Murmur radiating to the back, carotids, or lower extremities
o Loud systolic murmur ≥ Grade III
• Always evaluate with ECG + echocardiogram if red flags present
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 362–364
☆ Cardiology: Evaluation of Heart Murmurs
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 362–364

stem:
A 5-year-old boy is brought by his parents due to episodes of screaming during sleep. These occur
about 1–2 hours after bedtime. During the events, he appears terrified, is inconsolable, and does not
recognize his parents. He returns to sleep within minutes and does not remember the episodes the
next morning. He is otherwise healthy and has no daytime behavioral issues. What is the most
appropriate next step in management?
answer: A. Observe and reassure the parents
explanation:
• The child has classic night terrors, a type of non-REM parasomnia that is self-limited and benign.
• No treatment is needed in most cases, only parental reassurance and good sleep hygiene.
• Referral or EEG is only indicated if:
o Events are frequent, prolonged, or violent
o
o
Occur during the day or multiple times a night
Associated with tonic-clonic movements, incontinence, or postictal
confusion (suggestive of seizures)
Clinical pearls:
• Night terrors: non-REM, no recall, early in the night
• Management: ensure consistent bedtime routine, adequate sleep, and minimize stress
• Usually resolves by late childhood
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 136
☆ Neurology: Pediatric Sleep Disorders; Night Terrors
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 136

stem:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to the general pediatric clinic for a routine checkup. His mother mentions a
concern that he is speaking in sentences but seems to have some language delay. She asks what she
can do at home to best promote his language development.
explanation:
• The most effective and evidence-based way to promote language development in preschool-aged
children is interactive reading
• Reading aloud helps with:
o Vocabulary expansion
o Sentence structure
o Listening comprehension
o Phonemic awareness
• Writing is a more advanced skill not developmentally appropriate as primary intervention at this age
• Watching TV does not improve language — in fact, passive screen time is associated with delayed
expressive language
• Drawing supports fine motor skills and creativity but has limited impact on verbal language
development
Clinical pearls:
• Encourage parents to read aloud daily, ask questions, and involve the child in storytelling
• Shared book reading is a key part of early literacy and language programs
• Screen for speech/language delay if milestones are not met:
o
o
o
2 years: 2-word phrases
3 years: 3-word sentences
4 years: mostly understandable speech
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 37–38; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 12
☆ Developmental & Behavioral: Language Delay and Stimulation
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 37–38; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 12

stem:
A 18-month-old boy is brought for routine follow-up. His parents are concerned that he does not
say any words and does not seem to understand simple commands. His social interaction is limited,
and he does not consistently respond to his name. Which of the following is the strongest indication to
perform hearing assessment?
answer: B. Not speaking a single word
explanation:
• By 18 months, a child should say at least 6 meaningful words, and understand simple
commands.
• Failure to say a single word or follow commands is a red flag for language delay, possibly due
to hearing loss, global developmental delay, or autism spectrum disorder.
• Formal hearing evaluation (e.g., ABR or audiology referral) is indicated when no words are
spoken by 15–18 months.
Other options:
• Saying fewer than 10 words may still be within variation if comprehension
and interaction are intact.
• Lack of babbling is a red flag at 6–9 months, not 18 months.
• Gross motor delay does not specifically prompt hearing testing.
Red flags in development:
 9 months: not sitting alone
 12 months: not passing objects hand to hand
 15 months: abnormal pincer grasp
 18 months: not walking alone
 24 months: not saying words









Hearing assessment: Should be done by 3 months.
Neonatal breast hypertrophy (Gynecomastia): Resolves at 1 year.
Teeth eruption: Normal up to 18 months.
Toe walking: Normal up 3 years, after that:
Abnormal speech > ASD
Normal speech > Short Achillis tendon
Thumb sucking: Stop at 4 years.
Handedness: Should start at 2-4 y (before that check nerves and muscles or perinatal stroke)
Head banging: Normal up to 5 years.
Clinical pearls:
• Hearing loss is one of the most common causes of speech delay
• Always assess receptive language and social engagement
• Consider autism screen if poor eye contact or response to name
📚 Source: Osama Naga – Developmental Milestones; Nelson – Growth & Development Tables
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: Developmental Milestones, Red Flags in Language Delay
source: Osama Naga – Developmental Milestones; Nelson – Growth & Development Tables
topic: Developmental Milestones, Red Flags in Language Delay

stem:
A 6-year-old child has had flu-like symptoms for 3 days. Now presents with facial tenderness,
fever, and worsening symptoms. What is the next step?

options.label: C
options.text: Culture aspirate then based on isolated organisms start treatment

explanation:
• Meets criteria for acute bacterial sinusitis (persistent, worsening symptoms)
• First-line: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (90 mg/kg/day divided BID) for 10–14 days
📚 Source: Pocket Pediatrics, ENT infections
☆ Infectious Diseases: ENT infections, Acute bacterial rhinosinusitis
source: Pocket Pediatrics, ENT infections

stem:
A 10-year-old boy in the PICU is intubated and undergoing placement of a right internal jugular
central venous catheter. During the procedure, he suddenly develops desaturation. A stat chest Xray shows a whitish opacity over the entire right lung field. Breath sounds are decreased on the right.
What is the most likely cause of this finding?
explanation:
• The acute desaturation immediately after CVC insertion suggests a procedure-related
complication.
• A completely white lung field on X-ray, especially after line placement, strongly suggests a
hemothorax or chylothorax, i.e. pleural effusion.
• Pneumothorax would appear as a hyperlucent (black) area, not whitened.
• Atelectasis can appear white but is less likely to occur acutely and without obstruction.
• Malposition alone doesn't typically cause full-lung opacification unless associated with secondary
complications.
Clinical pearls:
• CVC insertion can cause pleural injury → effusion
• Confirm diagnosis with ultrasound or chest CT
• Large effusions may require thoracentesis or chest tube
★ Internal Jugular Line Insertion:
• Right internal jugular vein is preferred (straight path to SVC, avoids thoracic duct)
• Indications: fluid resuscitation, vasopressors, TPN, central venous pressure monitoring
• Use ultrasound guidance to reduce
complication risk
• Entry point: between sternal and clavicular
heads of SCM, lateral to carotid artery
• Post-procedure chest X-ray is mandatory to
confirm tip position and rule out
complications
• Ideal catheter tip position: lower
SVC or cavoatrial junction
• Complications:
o Pneumothorax: hyperlucent
Normal anatomy after RIJV line insertion.
hemithorax, absent lung markings
o Hemothorax: complete white-out of lung field, possible mediastinal shift
o Pleural effusion: blunted costophrenic angle, homogeneous opacity
o Malposition: catheter tip in jugular vein, azygos vein, or right atrium, may cause
arrhythmias or poor access
o Arterial puncture: hematoma or pulsatile bright red blood, confirm with pressure
waveform if unsure
Air embolism: sudden cardiovascular collapse, requires
immediate Trendelenburg and 100% oxygen
Always confirm position and rule out complications before using the
line
o
•
Note: The way this question was originally phrased when collected suggested a
“whitish opacity” over the lung field, which fits with pleural effusion (hemo or
chylothorax). However, this is a rare complication of internal jugular line
insertion (doi: 10.12998/wjcc.v10.i17.5776). Previous questions usually test the
concept of iatrogenic pneumothorax secondary to RIJV line insertion. The original x-ray in the MCQ could
have shown features of a pneumothorax with atelectasis (which would appear whitish as shown above).
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 306; King’s College Radiology Lines and Tubes
(https://ehealth.kcl.ac.uk/tel/radiology/lines-tubes/04-intravascular-devices.html)
☆ PICU: Central Line Complications
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 306; King’s College Radiology Lines and Tubes
topic: • Right internal jugular vein is preferred (straight path to SVC, avoids thoracic duct)

stem:
A 6-year-old boy with Down syndrome and a known complete
atrioventricular septal defect (AVSD) presents with acute respiratory
distress following a recent viral upper respiratory infection. On exam, he
has crackles bilaterally, mild hepatomegaly, and no wheezing. Chest Xray is provided. Oxygen saturation is 88% on room air and improves with
supplemental oxygen. There is no fever, and BNP is elevated. What is
the most appropriate initial pharmacologic management?
explanation:
• This patient has classic features of cardiogenic pulmonary edema, likely
from decompensated congestive heart failure in the setting of a known
congenital heart defect (AVSD).
• The chest x-ray shows:
o Marked cardiomegaly.
o Bilateral irregular, partially confluous, diffuse haziness showing a
perihilar and right-sided predominance.
o Partial effacement of the diaphragm contours.
o Presence of air bronchograms.
• This presentation is consistent with cardiogenic pulmonary edema,
likely due to myocarditis or heart failure, as suggested by:
o Hypoxemia with bilateral crackles
o Pulmonary congestion on X-ray
o Elevated BNP
• Furosemide (1 mg/kg IV) is first-line to relieve pulmonary congestion
and improve oxygenation.
• Bronchodilators are not helpful in the absence of bronchospasm.
• Corticosteroids are not indicated unless there’s an underlying
inflammatory cause (e.g., ARDS, asthma).
• Antibiotics are not needed unless there is evidence of infection.
Clinical pearls:
• Always consider cardiac causes in children with hypoxemia and crackles but no wheeze or fever
• BNP and CXR help distinguish pulmonary edema from pneumonia or asthma
• Diuretics improve symptoms rapidly by reducing preload and pulmonary capillary pressure
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 408–409; Radiopedia: Cardiogenic pulmonary edema
(Pediatric)
☆ Cardiology: Cardiogenic Pulmonary Edema – Diagnosis and Management
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 408–409; Radiopedia: Cardiogenic pulmonary edema
topic: Cardiogenic Pulmonary Edema – Diagnosis and Management

stem:
A 5-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of swelling around the
eyes and ankles. You note the patient to have pitting edema up to the knees bilaterally and periorbital edema with no erythema. You astutely send off a urine analysis and find that the patient has 4+
protein in the urine without signs of infection or red blood cells, and urine protein of 60 mg/m2/hr.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Classic: sudden edema + heavy proteinuria + bland urine sediment
• Most common in preschool children (2–6 years)
• Diagnosis often clinical; renal biopsy if atypical
• MCNS is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (80–90%), especially 2–6 yrs.
• Presents with: sudden-onset edema, heavy proteinuria (>40 mg/m²/hr), hypoalbuminemia.
• NO hematuria, HTN, or impaired renal function at onset.
• Typically responds rapidly to corticosteroids.
📚 Source: Nelson, Nephrology Ch. 474; Osama Naga 2024 p. 523–525; UWorld.
☆ Nephrology: Nephrotic Syndrome
source: Nelson, Nephrology Ch. 474; Osama Naga 2024 p. 523–525; UWorld.

stem:
A 2-year-old child with a known ventricular septal defect (VSD) and pulmonary hypertension
presents with severe hypotension and metabolic acidosis following a respiratory infection. On exam,
the child is tachycardic, mottled, and poorly perfused. Arterial blood gas reveals low pH and
elevated lactate. You are considering initiating inotropic support. Which of the following inotropes is
most appropriate in this setting?
explanation:
• In a child with shock physiology (hypotension + acidosis + poor perfusion), the primary need is
rapid restoration of perfusion pressure and inotropic support.
• Epinephrine is the inotrope of choice for cold shock with hypotension. It provides:
o Strong β1 effects (↑ cardiac output)
o Moderate α1 vasoconstriction (↑ SVR, ↑ BP)
o Works well even in acidotic states where other agents lose efficacy
Other options:
• Dopamine: May be used in mild-moderate shock but less effective in severe acidosis, and has
variable effects.
• Dobutamine: Excellent inotrope for pulmonary hypertension, but causes vasodilation → not ideal
in hypotension.
• Isoproterenol: Potent β-agonist with vasodilatory effect; can worsen hypotension.
• Milrinone: Useful for afterload reduction and RV failure, but causes vasodilation → not first-line
in hypotensive shock.
★ Inotropes & Vasopressors:
Clinical pearls:
• In shock + pulmonary hypertension, use:
o Epinephrine for hypotensive cold shock
o Milrinone or Dobutamine for RV failure or PH, but only if BP is stable
• Avoid vasodilators like milrinone or dobutamine in initial hypotensive states → these agents will
lower SVR too much
• Epinephrine is preferred in decompensated shock with acidosis
• Consider transitioning to milrinone or dobutamine once BP stabilizes if pulmonary pressures
remain high
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 408, 761
☆ PICU: Inotropic Selection in Shock
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 408, 761

stem:
A 10-year-old boy presents to the emergency department immediately after being stung on the lip by
a bee. He has significant swelling of his face and diffuse urticaria on his body. He is hypotensive. Of
the following, the optimal management for this boy is:
explanation:
Anaphylaxis = airway + skin + hypotension. Immediate IM epinephrine is first-line.
★ Anaphylaxis:
• Clinical Criteria:
o Acute onset (min to hr) with involvement of skin and/or mucosal tissue
(hives, itching, swollen lips/uvula/tongue) + 1 of the following:
 Respiratory compromise (wheeze/bronchospasm, stridor,
hoarseness, dyspnea, etc.)
 Reduced blood pressure or signs of end-organ dysfunction (i.e.,
syncope, incontinence)
o OR 2 of the following after exposure to likely allergen for patient:
 Skin/mucosal involvement; respiratory sxs; shock; persistent GI
symptoms (crampy abdominal pain, diarrhea)
o OR reduced BP after exposure to known allergen (min to hr):
 (1) for infants and children, low SBP (age specific) or >30%
decrease in SBP, and
 (2) for adolescents and adults, SBP<90mmHg or >30% decrease from baseline
• Management:
o Remove allergen; ABCDs; place on monitor; O2 prn; establish IV access
o IM epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg of 1:1,000 (0.01 mL/kg); max 0.3 mg in prepubertal child and
max 0.5 mg in teens; repeat q5–15 min if needed
o Inhaled β2 agonists (abulterol) PRN; may be useful in bronchospasm refractory to epi
o Pts who remain hypotensive despite epi should have aggressive fluid resuscitation
o Antihistamines are 2nd line: diphenhydramine (H1 blocker) 1 mg/kg/dose (max 50 mg) IV,
ranitidine (H2 blocker) 1 mg/kg (max 50 mg) IV
o Monitor for at least 4 hr after dose of Epi prior to discharge
o No evidence to support steroid use in simple anaphylaxis
o Discharge with EpiPen (0.3 mg for >30 kg, 0.15 mg/Jr dose for 15–30 kg) and outpatient
referral to Peds Allergy
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, Anaphylaxis Management; Osama Naga, Allergy & Immunology, p. 453;
Pocket Pediatrics 2024 (Tintinalli’s Emergency Medicine. 7th ed. 2011;177:180)
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Anaphylaxis
source: Red Book 2021–2024, Anaphylaxis Management; Osama Naga, Allergy & Immunology, p. 453;

stem:
A 9-year-old boy presents with chronic nasal congestion, sneezing, clear rhinorrhea, and
frequent eye rubbing. Symptoms are worse in the spring and after playing outside. On exam, he has
pale, boggy nasal turbinates, allergic shiners, and a transverse nasal crease. He has no fever or signs
of infection. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
explanation:
• This child has classic allergic rhinitis: seasonal pattern, eye rubbing,
pale turbinates, and clear discharge.
• Allergic rhinitis often causes nasal congestion, sneezing, rhinorrhea,
and conjunctivitis due to an IgE-mediated hypersensitivity
response. Patients can often distinguish patterns that suggest reactions to specific allergens.
• Intranasal corticosteroids (e.g., fluticasone, mometasone) are first-line for moderate to
severe allergic rhinitis and are superior to oral antihistamines in relieving nasal congestion.
• Best used daily and continuously during allergy season for maximal benefit.
Other options:
• Oral antihistamines relieve sneezing and itching but are less effective for nasal congestion.
• Montelukast is adjunctive, not first-line → additionally, they are less effective than intranasal
corticosteroids for allergic rhinitis
• Nasal decongestants (e.g., oxymetazoline) risk rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa) and
are not recommended for chronic use.
Clinical pearls:
• Encourage daily use of intranasal steroids, not PRN
• Proper technique and head position are key to avoid nasal irritation or bleeding
• Add antihistamines or leukotriene inhibitors if symptoms persist
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 270
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Allergic Rhinitis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 270

stem:
A 2-month-old infant is brought by his parents due to a persistent colorless discharge from the
right nostril. There is no fever, no foul odor, and no respiratory symptoms. The nasal mucosa
appears normal. Examination reveals no signs of systemic illness, and the eye on the same side is
slightly moist but not red. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• The diagnosis is congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction (NLDO), which can present with:
o Persistent, clear or mucoid discharge
o Typically unilateral
o No conjunctival injection or systemic signs
• Due to incomplete canalization of the nasolacrimal duct, common in infants <6 months
• Crigler massage (lacrimal sac massage) is the first-line treatment
• Oral antibiotics are not needed unless signs of dacryocystitis (redness, swelling, purulent
discharge) are present
• Cultures and ENT referral are unnecessary unless chronic or complicated
Clinical pearls:
• NLDO resolves spontaneously in 90% of cases by 1 year
of age
• Teach parents proper lacrimal massage technique: firm
downward pressure over the lacrimal sac, 2–4 times/day
• Ophthalmology referral is indicated if:
o No improvement by 6–12 months
o Recurrent infections
• Avoid unnecessary antibiotics unless secondary bacterial infection is evident
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 236; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 65
☆ General Pediatrics: Congenital Nasolacrimal Duct Obstruction
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 236; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 65

stem:
A 7-day-old neonate presents with bilateral conjunctival injection and mucopurulent discharge.
The baby was born via home vaginal delivery, with no immediate post-natal care. Examination shows
marked eyelid swelling and thick purulent drainage. There is no fever or systemic illness. What is
the most likely causative organism?
explanation:
• Chlamydial conjunctivitis typically presents between day 5–14 of life, classically around day 7,
with mucopurulent discharge, chemosis, and mild eyelid swelling.
•
It is not prevented by standard topical erythromycin prophylaxis
and is the most common cause of neonatal conjunctivitis in this
time frame.
Other options:
• Gonorrhea → presents earlier (day 2–5) with copious purulent
discharge and severe eyelid edema; rapid progression with risk of
corneal ulceration and perforation.
• HSV → presents day 6–14, often with vesicular lesions, watery
discharge, and risk of keratitis or systemic spread.
• Staph aureus → rare in neonates, more common in older infants/children or hospital-acquired
infections.
Clinical pearls:
• Always assess maternal STI history and delivery method
• Diagnosis: Giemsa stain or NAAT from conjunctival swab
• Treatment: Oral erythromycin 50 mg/kg/day divided QID × 14 days (to prevent progression to
pneumonia)
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 298; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 247
☆ Neonatology & Infectious Disease: Neonatal Conjunctivitis
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 298; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 247

stem:
A 4-month-old baby boy is brought to the emergency department with a swollen, painful right
thigh. He is otherwise healthy with no history of significant trauma. Radiographs
reveal a spiral fracture of the right femur. The mother reports no falls or injuries
and states that he only recently began rolling over.
What is the most likely cause of this fracture?
explanation:
• Spiral fractures in non-ambulatory infants are highly suspicious for non-accidental
trauma (NAT)
• These fractures are caused by torsional force, not typical in normal infant activity
• Infants at 4 months are not developmentally capable of generating enough force through
rolling to cause this injury
• Must trigger a full child protection workup, including skeletal survey and social services
referral
Clinical pearls:
• Always evaluate for other signs of abuse (e.g., bruises, retinal hemorrhage, multiple fractures)
• Document thoroughly and act in the child's best interest — safety is the priority
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 203–204
☆ General Pediatrics: Child Abuse
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 203–204

stem:
A 5-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her teacher, who is concerned about the child’s behavior.
The child has recently exhibited sexualized play, regression in toileting, and has become increasingly
withdrawn in class. She has also verbalized inappropriate comments about body parts that are not
developmentally expected. On further questioning, she reports that “someone touched me down
there,” but is hesitant to provide details. A comprehensive evaluation for possible sexual abuse is
initiated. A genital exam is performed and appears normal. Swabs are sent for nucleic acid
amplification testing (NAAT) for sexually transmitted infections. The team is reviewing which laboratory
findings, if positive, would be diagnostic of sexual abuse in a prepubertal child. Which of the following
organisms, if detected, is considered confirmatory evidence of sexual abuse in this patient?
explanation:
• Organisms Considered Diagnostic (Confirmatory) of Sexual Abuse in Children ≥1 month:
o Neisseria gonorrhoeae
o Chlamydia trachomatis
o Treponema pallidum (syphilis)
o HIV
→ These findings require mandatory reporting and forensic investigation. NAATs are highly
sensitive but often require confirmatory testing (e.g., culture) for legal proceedings.
• Organisms Considered Suggestive But Not Diagnostic:
o HSV-1 or HSV-2 (depending on site, age, and context)
→ HSV-2 raises concern but is not automatically diagnostic unless clearly genital in
prepubertal child without vertical transmission
o Trichomonas vaginalis
o HPV
• NAAT is the preferred diagnostic method, but confirmatory culture may be required in legal cases
depending on local protocols.
Other options:
• Haemophilus influenzae: Can colonize the genital tract but is not an STI
•
•
Group A Streptococcus: Causes vulvovaginitis; not evidence of abuse
Candida albicans: Often related to antibiotic use or diaper rash; not suggestive of abuse
Clinical pearls:
• Sexual abuse involves any sexual activity with a child where consent is not or cannot be given,
including fondling, penetration, exposure to pornography, and exploitation (e.g., trafficking,
prostitution).
• Perpetrators are often known to the child (family members, caregivers, or trusted adults).
• Environmental risk factors for child abuse include economic deprivation, poor housing, illness, and
unemployment.
• Behavioral red flags (e.g., sexualized behavior, regression, fearful behavior) are highly concerning in
young children
• Normal genital exam does not exclude abuse!
• A complete transection of the hymen to the base between the 4 and 8 o’clock positions (i.e.,
absence of hymenal tissue in the posterior rim) is considered diagnostic for trauma.
• Always assess in a multidisciplinary child advocacy setting.
• Confirmatory STI findings mandate reporting to child protective services.
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 284–286; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 696
☆ Child Abuse: Suspected Sexual Abuse
An additional, but important, read: Evaluating Pediatric Sexual Abuse in the Emergency Department
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 284–286; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 696

stem:
A 10-year-old child presents with sore throat for the past 10 days. A rapid antigen test is positive
for Group A Streptococcus (GAS). You consider whether antibiotic therapy can prevent acute
rheumatic fever (ARF). What is the most appropriate next step regarding rheumatic fever prevention?

options.label: A
options.text: Start penicillin, ARF can still be prevented

options.label: B
options.text: Do not treat, as it's been >9 days and ARF cannot be prevented

options.label: D
options.text: Reassure, antibiotics do not affect ARF risk

answer: A. Start penicillin, ARF can still be prevented
explanation:
• Treatment of Group A Streptococcus pharyngitis with antibiotics (usually penicillin or amoxicillin)
can prevent acute rheumatic fever if given within 9–10 days of symptom onset.
• Even at day 10, it is still acceptable to start antibiotics, especially if there is a high risk of ARF or
poor follow-up.
• Delaying treatment beyond 10 days significantly reduces the efficacy in preventing ARF, but if the
patient is within the window, treatment should be given.
• ARF prevention window is within 9–10 days of symptom onset.
• By day 11 or later, the risk of developing ARF is already established if it will occur, and antibiotics
no longer modify that risk.
o Treatment at this stage may still be given for symptomatic relief if the patient is unwell,
but not to prevent ARF.
Other options:
• ARF can still be prevented up to 10 days after symptom onset.
• Amoxicillin is used for 10 days, not 3.
• Antibiotics do reduce the risk of ARF when given appropriately.
Clinical Pearls:
• Penicillin V for 10 days is first-line; amoxicillin is an acceptable alternative.
• ARF is a non-suppurative complication of untreated GAS pharyngitis.
• Antibiotics do not prevent post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis — only ARF.
• Secondary prophylaxis with benzathine penicillin is indicated in children with prior ARF.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1315–1317; UpToDate: “Treatment and prevention of
streptococcal pharyngitis”
☆ Infectious Disease: Acute Rheumatic Fever Prevention
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1315–1317; UpToDate: “Treatment and prevention of

stem:
A 2-month-old infant is brought to the emergency department after a reported episode of sudden
apnea and limpness at home. The event lasted about 30 seconds and resolved spontaneously. The
infant is now alert but tachypneic. There is no fever, and the mother denies recent illness. Vitals show
mild hypoxia with an oxygen saturation of 89% on room air.
What is the most appropriate initial management?
explanation:
• In an infant with a brief resolved unexplained event (BRUE) presenting with hypoxemia or
respiratory distress, the first step is to stabilize the airway and improve oxygenation.
• 100% oxygen via mask or nasal cannula is immediately indicated to correct desaturation while
you assess further.
• Airway, Breathing, Circulation (ABCs) always come first.
Other options:
• Intubation is reserved for persistent apnea or respiratory failure, not indicated if the infant is alert
and breathing
• Antibiotics are considered if infection (e.g., sepsis or pneumonia) is suspected, but not first-line
unless there are clinical signs
• MRI may be needed later if neurologic signs are present, but not in the acute stabilization phase
Clinical pearls:
• Administer oxygen first in any cyanotic or apneic infant
• Monitor for recurrence, bradycardia, or desaturation
• Admit if high-risk features are present (age <60 days, prematurity, abnormal exam)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 289
☆ Emergency Medicine: Acute Apnea
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 289

stem:
A child being treated with isoniazid (INH) for tuberculosis develops peripheral neuropathy. What is
the most appropriate next step?
explanation:
• Isoniazid interferes with vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) metabolism, leading to peripheral neuropathy,
especially in malnourished, breastfeeding infants, or slow acetylators
• Management is not to stop INH, but to supplement with pyridoxine (vitamin B6)
o Dose: typically 1–2 mg/kg/day for prophylaxis, up to 10–50 mg/day if symptomatic
• Folate does not reverse this toxicity
• Stopping INH is unnecessary if neuropathy is mild and pyridoxine is added
Clinical pearls:
Always co-administer pyridoxine with INH in high-risk patients (e.g., malnourished, pregnant,
breastfeeding, HIV+)
Neuropathy typically presents as tingling, numbness, or burning in hands and feet
INH is also known to cause drug-induced lupus (SLE) → anti-histone antibodies
•
•
•
📚 Source: AAP Red Book 2021–2024, p. 804; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1035
☆ Neurology: Isoniazid-Induced Neuropathy
source: AAP Red Book 2021–2024, p. 804; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1035

stem:
A 12-year-old child is identified during a school TB screening. He has a positive PPD (tuberculin
skin test) but is asymptomatic and his chest X-ray is normal. There is no known contact with an
active TB case. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• A positive PPD with negative chest X-ray and no symptoms indicates latent tuberculosis
infection (LTBI).
• Children <5 years are at higher risk of progression to active TB, so treatment is always
indicated even if asymptomatic.
• The recommended treatment is Isoniazid (INH) 10 mg/kg/day (max 300 mg) for 9 months.
• Reassurance alone is inappropriate due to risk of progression.
• Repeating the PPD is not indicated if already positive.
• RIPE therapy is reserved for active TB, which this child does not have.
★ Pediatric Tuberculosis:
• Transmission & Risk Factors
o Spread via airborne droplets, usually from a household contact
o Infants and children <5 yrs are at highest risk for progression to active disease
• TB Testing: PPD vs IGRA
o PPD (Mantoux)
 Positive if ≥10 mm in high-risk children (e.g., close contacts, age <4, foreign-born)
 Positive if ≥5 mm in immunocompromised, close contacts, or abnormal CXR
IGRA (Quantiferon)
 Preferred in BCG-vaccinated children ≥2 years
 Not reliable in <2 years
 Does not distinguish active vs latent TB
Clinical Features of Active TB
o Pulmonary TB
 Chronic cough, weight loss, fever, fatigue
 Hilar lymphadenopathy, pleural effusion on imaging
o
•
Extrapulmonary TB
 CNS (meningitis), miliary TB, lymph nodes, bones (Pott
disease)
 Infants and toddlers are at high risk for disseminated TB
Diagnosis of Active TB
o Start with chest X-ray
o Sputum AFB smear/culture if age ≥10 or able to expectorate
o Gastric aspirates x3 if <10 years old
o Consider CSF or lymph node biopsy if extrapulmonary TB
o Always screen for HIV
Latent TB Infection (LTBI)
o Defined as positive PPD or IGRA, no symptoms, normal CXR
o Treatment options
 INH x 9 months
 Rifampin x 4 months
 INH + Rifapentine weekly x 3 months (if age >2 yrs, HIVnegative)
o
•
•
•
•
Treatment of Active TB
o RIPE for 2 months: Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol
o Then INH + Rifampin for 4 months
o Total treatment: 6 months, extend to 9–12 months for CNS or bone TB
o Use Directly Observed Therapy (DOT) to ensure adherence
Mothers with TB can breastfeed using expressed milk only
Clinical pearls:
• Positive PPD in children ≥ 5 years: ≥ 10 mm
• Positive PPD in children < 5 years or high-risk (e.g., contacts, immunocompromised): ≥ 5 mm
• Always treat LTBI in children <5 years with a positive TST and negative CXR.
• Preferred regimen: INH for 9 months, or Rifampin daily for 4 months as an alternative.
• Monitor for hepatotoxicity, especially in adolescents and with long courses.
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 821–822; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 399; Osama Naga
Pediatric Board Review, 3rd ed., p. 223
☆ Infectious Disease: Latent Tuberculosis Infection (LTBI)

stem:
A 9-year-old child is identified during a school TB screening. He has a positive PPD (tuberculin skin
test) but is asymptomatic and his chest X-ray is normal. There is no known contact with an active TB
case. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• A positive PPD with negative chest X-ray and no symptoms indicates latent tuberculosis
infection (LTBI).
• Children <5 years are at higher risk of progression to active TB, so treatment is always
indicated even if asymptomatic.
• The recommended treatment is Isoniazid (INH) 10 mg/kg/day (max 300 mg) for 9 months.
• Reassurance alone is inappropriate due to risk of progression.
• Repeating the PPD is not indicated if already positive.
• RIPE therapy is reserved for active TB, which this child does not have.
★ Pediatric Tuberculosis:
• Transmission & Risk Factors
o Spread via airborne droplets, usually from a household contact
o Infants and children <5 yrs are at highest risk for progression to active disease
• TB Testing: PPD vs IGRA
o PPD (Mantoux)
 Positive if ≥10 mm in high-risk children (e.g., close contacts, age <4, foreign-born)
 Positive if ≥5 mm in immunocompromised, close contacts, or abnormal CXR
IGRA (Quantiferon)
 Preferred in BCG-vaccinated children ≥2 years
 Not reliable in <2 years
 Does not distinguish active vs latent TB
Clinical Features of Active TB
o Pulmonary TB
 Chronic cough, weight loss, fever, fatigue
 Hilar lymphadenopathy, pleural effusion on imaging
o Extrapulmonary TB
 CNS (meningitis), miliary TB, lymph nodes, bones (Pott
disease)
 Infants and toddlers are at high risk for disseminated TB
Diagnosis of Active TB
o Start with chest X-ray
o Sputum AFB smear/culture if age ≥10 or able to expectorate
o Gastric aspirates x3 if <10 years old
o Consider CSF or lymph node biopsy if extrapulmonary TB
o Always screen for HIV
Latent TB Infection (LTBI)
o Defined as positive PPD or IGRA, no symptoms, normal CXR
o Treatment options
 INH x 9 months
 Rifampin x 4 months
 INH + Rifapentine weekly x 3 months (if age >2 yrs, HIVnegative)
o
•
•
•
•
Treatment of Active TB
o RIPE for 2 months: Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol
o Then INH + Rifampin for 4 months
o Total treatment: 6 months, extend to 9–12 months for CNS or bone TB
o Use Directly Observed Therapy (DOT) to ensure adherence
•
Mothers with TB can breastfeed using expressed milk only
Clinical pearls:
• Positive PPD in children ≥ 5 years: ≥ 10 mm
• Positive PPD in children < 5 years or high-risk (e.g., contacts, immunocompromised): ≥ 5 mm
• Always treat LTBI in children <5 years with a positive TST and negative CXR.
• Preferred regimen: INH for 9 months, or Rifampin daily for 4 months as an alternative.
• Monitor for hepatotoxicity, especially in adolescents and with long courses.
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 821–822; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 399; Osama Naga
Pediatric Board Review, 3rd ed., p. 223
☆ Infectious Diseases: Latent Tuberculosis Infection (LTBI)
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 821–822; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 399; Osama Naga

stem:
A 15-year-old adolescent girl presents with significant weight loss. She reports excessive exercise,
restricts food intake, and has a fear of gaining weight, believing she is overeating. On exam, her BMI
is 16, temperature is 35.8°C, heart rate is 54 bpm, and blood pressure drops from 94/60 (supine) to
73/48 (standing). Which of the following findings is the most immediate indication for hospital
admission?
explanation:
• Orthostatic hypotension, defined as a drop in systolic BP ≥20 mmHg or
diastolic ≥10 mmHg, especially with symptoms, is a sign of
hemodynamic instability in anorexia nervosa, and is a clear admission
criterion.
• Bradycardia (<50 bpm in adolescents) and hypothermia (<36°C) are
also concerning, but hemodynamic compromise (i.e., orthostatic
changes) is more acutely life-threatening.
• Low BMI or weight loss alone, without systemic compromise, does not
mandate hospitalization unless BMI <14 or rapid weight loss occurs.
Clinical pearls:
• Hospitalization in anorexia nervosa is indicated for:
o Orthostatic hypotension (drop in BP or HR with position change)
o Bradycardia (<50 bpm daytime, or <45 bpm at night)
o Hypothermia (<36°C)
o Electrolyte disturbances, arrhythmias
o BMI <14 or acute weight loss (>1 kg/week)
• Adolescents with vital sign instability are at risk of sudden cardiac death due to electrolyte
imbalance and cardiac atrophy.
• Early multidisciplinary intervention improves long-term outcomes.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 87; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 334
☆ Adolescent Medicine: Anorexia Nervosa and Admission Criteria
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 87; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 334

stem:
A 10-month-old infant is brought for a routine visit. The mother is concerned because the child has no
erupted teeth yet. She asks about the normal timing and factors associated with primary tooth
eruption. Which of the following statements is most accurate?

options.label: A
options.text: Primary teething occurs in the first 18 months

options.label: B
