options.text: Eruption is related to bone maturity

options.label: C
options.text: Dental eruption is considered delayed if not present before 6 months

options.label: D
options.text: First permanent molars erupt before primary central incisors

answer: A. Primary teething occurs in the first 18 months
explanation:
• Primary teeth usually begin to erupt between 6 and 10 months, but eruption as late as 16–18
months can still be normal.
• Lower central incisors are typically the first to erupt.
• Delayed eruption is not diagnosed before 18 months, especially if growth and development are
otherwise normal.
• Eruption is not directly related to bone maturity, but rather genetics and nutritional status.
• Permanent molars erupt around 6 years, long after primary incisors.
Clinical pearls:
• Delayed eruption beyond 18 months may indicate conditions like hypothyroidism, ectodermal
dysplasia, or Down syndrome.
• Early eruption (<4 months) is usually benign but can be associated with natal teeth.
o Natal teeth are associated with risk of aspiration (if mobile) → require removal if mobile
• Teething may cause drooling and irritability, but not fever or diarrhea — that's a myth.
• Order of eruption: Central incisors → lateral incisors → first molars → canines → second molars.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 21; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 121
☆ Growth & Development: Dental Eruption Patterns
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 21; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 121

stem:
A 3-year-old child presents after a fall. On assessment:
- Opens eyes to voice
- Cries but does not speak or follow commands
- Withdraws his hand from painful stimulus
What is the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score?
explanation:
Breakdown using Pediatric GCS:
• GCS scoring for young children (<5 years) is developmentally adjusted.
• Breakdown of GCS in this case:
o Eye Opening (E): To voice → 3
o Verbal Response (V): Cries but inappropriate
response → 3
o Motor Response (M): Withdraws from pain → 4
o Total GCS = 3 (E) + 3 (V) + 4 (M) = 10
Clinical pearls:
• GCS 13–15: mild injury
• GCS 9–12: moderate head injury
• GCS ≤8: severe — consider intubation and neuroimaging
• GCS is critical for monitoring neurological status in trauma
• Use pediatric-specific GCS criteria in children under 5 years
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 264; Osama
Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 356
☆ Emergency Medicine: Pediatric Glasgow Coma Scale
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 264; Osama

stem:
A 12-month-old infant is brought to your clinic for routine vaccination. The mother is concerned about
giving live vaccines, stating that the child’s grandfather has cancer and is currently undergoing
chemotherapy. She asks whether her child should receive the scheduled live vaccines. What is
the most appropriate response?

options.label: B
options.text: Do not give OPV until the grandfather is immunocompetent

options.label: C
options.text: Live vaccines are not indicated for children under 1 year of age

options.label: D
options.text: Give all scheduled vaccines, including live vaccines

answer: B. Do not give OPV until the grandfather is immunocompetent
explanation:
• OPV (oral polio vaccine) is a live-attenuated vaccine that is shed in the stool for weeks after
administration.
• In households with immunocompromised contacts, such as chemotherapy patients, OPV is
contraindicated due to the risk of vaccine-derived poliovirus transmission.
• In such settings, IPV (inactivated polio vaccine) is the safe alternative.
• Varicella and MMR vaccines are safe for healthy children even if household members are
immunosuppressed — transmission risk is minimal.
• Option C is incorrect — live vaccines are recommended at 12 months, not before.
If the question in the exam only has an option that mentions “give all live vaccines” without specific mention
of OPV, I believe it will be the best choice in comparison to other options.
Clinical pearls:
• OPV poses a unique risk via fecal-oral transmission, making it the exception among live vaccines.
• Household immunocompromised status ≠ contraindication to all live vaccines — only those
that shed.
• If OPV is mandated, consider administering IPV instead, with documentation of the
immunocompromised contact.
• Saudi immunization schedule includes OPV in selected age groups and campaigns — use with
caution in such settings.
Saudi Vaccination Schedule (MOH 2024)
• Preterm infants should receive the vaccine at actual age not the corrected age
• Only exception is Hepatitis B vaccine if birth weight < 2kg — given at 1 month or discharge
(whichever is 1st)
• BCG delay to 6 months helps avoid severe complications in patients with undiagnosed SCID
• Preschool (4-6 years) vaccines are: DTaP, OPV, Varicella, MMR
Remember: Birth, 2–4–6 months build the base, 9–12–18 add
extras, school & teen boosters follow.
- Measles monovalent at 9 months (pre-MMR)
- MMR given at 12, 18 months, and again at 4–6 years
- IPV/OPV given at 0, 2, 4, 6, 12, 18 months, and 4–6 years
- PCV: 3-dose primary (2, 4, 6 mo) + booster at 12 mo
- MCV4: at 9 & 12 months, booster at 18 years
- Hep A: 2-dose series at 18 & 24 months
- HPV: for girls only, at age 11-12
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, AAP, p. 40–41; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 414; Saudi
Ministry of Health Immunization Schedule
☆ Infectious Diseases: Vaccination in Immunocompromised Household Settings
source: Red Book 2021–2024, AAP, p. 40–41; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 414; Saudi

stem:
A 15-year-old girl is brought to clinic due to lack of breast development, pubic hair, and absence of
menstruation. She has a history of congenital heart surgery in infancy. On exam, she has short
stature and no signs of secondary sexual characteristics (Tanner stage I for both breast and pubic
hair). Her mother is concerned about delayed puberty and amenorrhea. What is the most appropriate
initial test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
explanation:
• Turner syndrome (45,XO) should be suspected in a girl with:
o Short stature
o Primary amenorrhea
o Lack of secondary sexual characteristics
o History of congenital heart disease (e.g., coarctation of the aorta, bicuspid aortic valve)
• Karyotyping is the gold standard for diagnosing Turner syndrome
• Ultrasound may show streak ovaries or small uterus but does not confirm the genetic cause
• Growth hormone testing is not indicated unless isolated short stature is the concern
•
Thyroid function testing may be appropriate later, as hypothyroidism is common in Turner
syndrome, but it is not diagnostic
Clinical pearls:
• Turner syndrome is the most common chromosomal cause of primary amenorrhea
• Always evaluate for associated conditions: cardiac anomalies, renal malformations,
and autoimmune thyroiditis
• Patients require estrogen replacement to initiate puberty and protect bone health
• Consider echocardiogram, renal ultrasound, and audiology screening after diagnosis
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 108–110; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 74
☆ Endocrinology: Primary Amenorrhea and Turner Syndrome
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 108–110; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 74

stem:
A 2-year-old previously healthy child is brought for evaluation of progressive pallor. He has no
dysmorphic features, normal growth and development, and no hepatosplenomegaly or
lymphadenopathy. There are no signs of infection, systemic illness, or dysmorphic features.
Investigations show:
- Isolated normocytic anemia
- Bone marrow: reduced erythroid precursors, normal myeloid and megakaryocytic lines
- ADA enzyme activity: normal
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• TEC is a self-limited, acquired red cell aplasia that typically occurs in children aged 6 months to
3 years
•
•
•
•
It is characterized by:
o Progressive normocytic normochromic anemia
o Reticulocytopenia
o Normal WBCs and platelets
o Bone marrow: selective decrease in erythroid precursors
It follows viral infections (e.g., parvovirus B19) but often has no clear trigger
ADA enzyme normal + no dysmorphic features or anomalies → rules out Diamond-Blackfan anemia
No physical anomalies or pancytopenia → rules out Fanconi anemia and aplastic anemia
Clinical pearls:
• TEC is the most common cause of acquired red cell aplasia in children
• Patients are typically previously healthy, without congenital anomalies
• Spontaneous recovery in weeks to months — treatment is usually supportive
• Differentiate from Diamond-Blackfan anemia:
o DBA presents in infancy
o Associated with craniofacial anomalies, thumb malformations, and elevated ADA
enzyme
• Aplastic anemia involves all three cell lines and may be associated with viral triggers, medications,
or autoimmune conditions
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 437–438; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 167
☆ Hematology: Normocytic Anemia – TEC vs. DBA
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 437–438; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 167

stem:
A school-aged child presents with night sweats, prolonged fever, and significant weight loss. On
physical examination, he has generalized lymphadenopathy. A chest X-ray reveals a mediastinal
mass. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• The triad of night sweats, fever, and weight loss are B symptoms, strongly suggestive of
•
•
•
•
lymphoma, particularly Hodgkin lymphoma
A mediastinal mass in this age group is highly characteristic of T-cell lymphoblastic lymphoma or
Hodgkin lymphoma
Leukemia can present with fever and lymphadenopathy, but is less commonly associated with a
dominant mediastinal mass
Neuroblastoma typically arises from the adrenal or paraspinal sympathetic chain and presents
in younger children, often with abdominal mass and elevated catecholamines
TB can cause fever, weight loss, and lymphadenopathy, but generalized lymphadenopathy with a
large mediastinal mass is more consistent with lymphoma
Review of Leukemia & Lymphoma:
(please note that this is more extensive detail than what you need to know for the exam! I included it for review and completion)
Clinical pearls:
• Always suspect lymphoma in older children with B symptoms and a mediastinal mass
•
•
•
Hodgkin lymphoma commonly presents with a mediastinal mass and Reed-Sternberg cells on
biopsy
Important initial workup:
o CBC with differential
o LDH and uric acid
o Chest X-ray or CT scan
o Excisional lymph node biopsy
Rule out airway compression before sedation or anesthesia if mediastinal mass is large
s
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 735–738; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p.
249; Maryam AlTayeb Med School Notes: HEM/ONC Leukemias and Lymphomas
☆ Hematology–Oncology: Lymphoma in Children
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 735–738; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p.

stem:
A 10-year-old boy presents with abdominal pain for 18 hours, initially periumbilical and now localized
to the right lower quadrant (RLQ). He has associated nausea, low-grade fever, and anorexia. On
examination:
- Tenderness over McBurney’s point
- Positive rebound tenderness
- Mild leukocytosis on CBC
What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: B
options.text: Apply a pediatric appendicitis score (e.g., Alvarado or PAS)

options.label: C
options.text: Reassure and send home with observation

answer: B. Apply a pediatric appendicitis score (e.g., Alvarado or PAS)
explanation:
• In children, the diagnosis of appendicitis should begin with clinical assessment and scoring:
o Alvarado score
o Pediatric Appendicitis Score (PAS)
• These scoring systems help stratify risk:
o Low risk → observe and re-evaluate
o Intermediate risk → imaging (ultrasound preferred first)
o High risk → surgical consult ± imaging
• CT scan is not first-line in children due to radiation exposure; only used if US is inconclusive and
suspicion remains high
• Reassurance or laxatives are inappropriate with localized peritonitis signs
★ Appendicitis:
• Cause: Obstruction of appendix from inflammation, fecolith, or enlarged lymph node
• Epi: Most common indication for emergent abdominal surgery in children; up to 8% of acute
abdominal pain in children
o Peak incidence: 10–19 years of age, Uncommon in children < 3 years
• Pathophysiology of pain
o Initial obstruction → stretching of hollow organ → stimulation of visceral
nerves → initial referred pain of T10 dermatome (periumbilical)
o As inflammation progresses → local peritoneal irritation → somatic nerve
stimulation → RLQ pain and can eventually progress to peritonitis
• Clinical sxs → dull, crampy, diffuse periumbilical pain migrating to RLQ,
anorexia, vomiting (after onset of pain), pain with movement (peritonitis)
• Px findings:
o Peritoneal signs (pain with coughing, shaking, walking, jumping, bumps
during car ride)
o Not validated in peds – Rovsing sign (pain in RLQ with palpation of LLQ),
psoas sign (↑ RLQ pain with extension of R thigh), obturator sign (↑ RLQ
pain with flexion and internal rotation of R hip)
•
•
•
•
Clinical decision rule: Pediatric appendicitis score (Pediatrics 2008; 153:278–282)
o Assign 1 point for: Anorexia, fever, nausea/vomiting, WBC
>10,000, ANC >7,500, migration of pain to RLQ
o Assign 2 points for: RLQ pain to coughing/percussion/hopping,
tenderness over R iliac fossa
Workup:
o Labs: CBC, BMP
o Imaging:
 Abd US: Up to 98% sensitivity IF appendix is visualized – first choice unless urgent
imaging indicated – look for noncompressible, fluid-filled appendix >6 mm
 CT abd w/ contrast (94–100% Se, 93–100% Sp) – if US fails to r/o appendicitis or high
clinical concern
Mgmt: NPO, mIVF, surgical consult for appendectomy, broad-spectrum ABX if evidence of
perforation or sepsis
o Nonoperative mgmt. of nonperforated appendicitis increasing, 20% failure rate within 14 d,
also increased risk of postsurgical complications
Complications:
o Perforation – Consider when signs of peritonitis or evidence on imaging – up to 25% of pts
 Risk: Not as time dependent as previously thought, OK to wait up to 24 hr prior to
procedure if needed ( Journal of Pediatric Surgery 2018;396–405)
 Tx: Start IV pip/tazo immediately, urgent surgical consult for immediate resection
Clinical pearls:
• Ultrasound is the preferred first imaging modality in pediatric appendicitis
• CT should be reserved for inconclusive US in moderate-to-high risk cases
• Common features of appendicitis:
o RLQ pain, anorexia, fever, leukocytosis, rebound tenderness
• Analgesia does not mask appendicitis signs and should be given for comfort
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 399–401; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p.
124; Pocket Pediatrics 2024, Emergency Department: Surgical Presentations – Appendicitis
☆ Emergency Medicine: Acute Appendicitis Evaluation and Scoring
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 399–401; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p.
topic: • Cause: Obstruction of appendix from inflammation, fecolith, or enlarged lymph node

stem:
A 10-year-old boy presents with difficulty opening his eyes and a "sad-looking" facial expression,
although he is otherwise alert and happy. His symptoms worsen with activity and improve after rest.
On exam, he has ptosis, weakness of facial muscles, and normal deep tendon reflexes. What is
the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
explanation:
• This presentation is classic for myasthenia gravis (MG), an autoimmune disorder of the
neuromuscular junction
• Characteristic features:
o Ptosis and facial weakness
o Symptoms worsen with exertion, improve with rest
o Preserved reflexes and sensation
• Edrophonium (Tensilon) test:
Short-acting acetylcholinesterase inhibitor
Temporary improvement of symptoms confirms diagnosis
Creatine kinase (CK) is elevated in muscular dystrophies, not MG
Muscle biopsy is useful in myopathies, not in neuromuscular junction disorders
Brain MRI is not indicated unless central pathology suspected (e.g., cranial nerve palsy without
fatigability)
o
o
•
•
•
★ Myasthenia Gravis:
• Definition: Autoimmune disorder caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors at
the neuromuscular junction → impaired neuromuscular transmission.
• Types:
o Transient neonatal MG – due to maternal antibodies, self-limited
o Congenital MG – genetic defect in NMJ transmission
o Juvenile MG – acquired autoimmune, more common in females
• Presentation:
o Fluctuating muscle weakness, worsens with activity, improves with rest
o Ptosis, diplopia, fatigable chewing, nasal voice
o Bulbar symptoms: dysphagia, poor sucking, weak cry (infants)
o Normal reflexes and sensation
o Respiratory crisis may require ventilation
• Diagnosis:
o Edrophonium (Tensilon) test – rapid symptom improvement (used less now)
o Anti-ACh receptor antibody test – positive in most autoimmune MG
o EMG with decremental response to repetitive stimulation
o CT/MRI chest – evaluate for thymoma or thymic hyperplasia
• Management:
o Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors: Pyridostigmine (first-line),
neostigmine – Dose: 0.5–1 mg/kg/dose PO q4–6h
o Corticosteroids if refractory
o IVIG or plasmapheresis for myasthenic crisis
o Thymectomy in generalized MG (if thymoma or no response to
meds)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 831–832; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 281
☆ Neurology: Myasthenia Gravis and Diagnostic Approach
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 831–832; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 281
topic: • Definition: Autoimmune disorder caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors at

stem:
An 18-month-old boy is brought to clinic for a routine visit. The parents are concerned that he does not
say any words and seems to have difficulty understanding when spoken to. He follows no verbal
instructions and makes limited eye contact. What is the strongest indication for proceeding with
a hearing assessment?
answer: C. Not speaking a single word
explanation:
• By 18 months, most children should:
o Speak at least 3–6 words
o Understand simple commands
o Follow basic gestures
• Not speaking a single word at this age is a red flag for speech and language delay, and warrants
formal hearing evaluation
• Hearing loss is one of the most common causes of delayed speech and should be ruled out early
• Not speaking >10 words may still be within normal limits depending on other milestones
• Two-word combinations are expected by 24 months, not 18
• Poor attention alone is nonspecific and not the primary indication for hearing testing
Clinical pearls:
• Always consider hearing loss in children with speech delay
• Use behavioral audiometry or automated auditory brainstem response (AABR) depending on age
and cooperation
• Early identification of hearing loss allows for interventions like amplification or cochlear implants
• Speech-language therapy should be initiated early when indicated
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 38–39; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 13
☆ Developmental & Behavioral Pediatrics: Speech Delay and Hearing Evaluation
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 38–39; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 13

stem:
A 10-year-old boy presents to the emergency department immediately after being stung by a bee on
the lip. He has developed significant facial swelling, diffuse urticaria, and is found to
be hypotensive on examination. What is the best next step in management?
explanation:
• This presentation is classic for anaphylaxis, which is a life-threatening, systemic allergic reaction
• Diagnostic criteria for anaphylaxis:
o Exposure to allergen + sudden onset of skin symptoms (e.g., urticaria)
o Respiratory compromise or hypotension or involvement of 2+ systems (e.g., skin +
cardiovascular)
• Intramuscular epinephrine is the first and most critical treatment
o Dose: 0.01 mg/kg of 1:1000 (1 mg/mL) solution, max 0.3–0.5 mg IM into the lateral thigh
• Antihistamines (e.g., diphenhydramine) and steroids (e.g., methylprednisolone) are adjuncts,
not first-line
• Albuterol may help bronchospasm but does not treat systemic anaphylaxis
★ Anaphylaxis:
• Clinical Criteria:
o Acute onset (min to hr) with involvement of skin and/or mucosal tissue
(hives, itching, swollen lips/uvula/tongue) + 1 of the following:
 Respiratory compromise (wheeze/bronchospasm, stridor,
hoarseness, dyspnea, etc.)
 Reduced blood pressure or signs of end-organ dysfunction (i.e.,
•
syncope, incontinence)
o OR 2 of the following after exposure to likely allergen for patient:
 Skin/mucosal involvement; respiratory sxs; shock; persistent GI
symptoms (crampy abdominal pain, diarrhea)
o OR reduced BP after exposure to known allergen (min to hr):
 (1) for infants and children, low SBP (age specific) or >30%
decrease in SBP, and
 (2) for adolescents and adults, SBP<90mmHg or >30% decrease from baseline
Management:
o Remove allergen; ABCDs; place on monitor; O2 prn; establish IV access
o IM epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg of 1:1,000 (0.01 mL/kg); max 0.3 mg in prepubertal child and
max 0.5 mg in teens; repeat q5–15 min if needed
o Inhaled β2 agonists (abulterol) PRN; may be useful in bronchospasm refractory to epi
o Pts who remain hypotensive despite epi should have aggressive fluid resuscitation
o Antihistamines are 2nd line: diphenhydramine (H1 blocker) 1 mg/kg/dose (max 50 mg) IV,
ranitidine (H2 blocker) 1 mg/kg (max 50 mg) IV
o Monitor for at least 4 hr after dose of Epi prior to discharge
o No evidence to support steroid use in simple anaphylaxis
o Discharge with EpiPen (0.3 mg for >30 kg, 0.15 mg/Jr dose for 15–30 kg) and outpatient
referral to Peds Allergy
Clinical pearls:
• Epinephrine IM is the only medication proven to reduce mortality in anaphylaxis
• Administer immediately, even before IV access
• Monitor for biphasic reaction — observe patients for 4–6 hours minimum
• Children with a history of anaphylaxis should be discharged with an epinephrine auto-injector and
referred to allergy clinic
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 199–200; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 90;
Pocket Pediatrics 2024 (Tintinalli’s Emergency Medicine. 7th ed. 2011;177:180)
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Anaphylaxis – Emergency Management
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 199–200; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 90;

stem:
A child underwent a minor surgical procedure under sedation with midazolam 15 mcg/kg and fentanyl
15 mcg/kg. Shortly after the procedure, he develops apnea and is unresponsive.
What is the most appropriate immediate action?
explanation:
• The first priority in any patient with apnea is to secure the airway and support ventilation
• Bag-valve-mask (BVM) ventilation with 100% oxygen is the initial step in apnea after sedation
• Fentanyl and midazolam are both respiratory depressants; apnea may occur with combined use
• Intubation is considered if BVM fails to restore breathing or if prolonged ventilation is needed
• Reversal agents (naloxone for fentanyl, flumazenil for midazolam) can be administered after
airway support has been initiated
• Observation without intervention is inappropriate in apnea
Clinical pearls:
• Always assess ABCs first in any post-sedation event
• Naloxone dose: 0.01–0.1 mg/kg IV/IM/SC
• Flumazenil dose: 0.01 mg/kg IV (max 0.2 mg/dose) — use cautiously in patients with chronic
benzodiazepine use
• Bag-mask ventilation is often effective in reversing hypoxia quickly in opioid/BZD-induced apnea
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 360; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 142
☆ PICU: Sedation Complications – Apnea Management
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 360; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 142

stem:
A 5-year-old boy with a known history of ulcerative colitis on azathioprine presents to the emergency
department with abdominal pain and bloody diarrhea. On examination, he appears pale and in
significant pain. Vital signs reveal tachycardia and borderline hypotension. What is the most
appropriate initial management?

options.label: B
options.text: NPO, blood culture, and IV fluids

options.label: C
options.text: Switch to oral corticosteroids at home

answer: B. NPO, blood culture, and IV fluids
explanation:
• This child presents with signs concerning for severe UC flare or complication such as toxic
megacolon or superimposed infection
• Key features of toxic megacolon:
o Fever, abdominal pain, bloody diarrhea, ill appearance, tenderness
o Use of anti-TNF (infliximab) increases infection and complication risk
• Initial management in the acute setting includes:
o NPO status to rest the bowel
o IV fluid resuscitation due to volume loss
o Blood cultures and stool cultures to rule out sepsis or infection (e.g., CMV, C. difficile)
• Increasing azathioprine dose is not appropriate during an acute flare, especially when systemic
toxicity is suspected
• Oral corticosteroids are used in moderate outpatient flares, not in a child who appears
systemically unwell
• Reassurance alone is inappropriate given this child’s ill appearance and ongoing bleeding
IBD High-Yield Board Tips
• Growth failure + perianal disease → Think Crohn
• Bloody diarrhea + toxic megacolon risk → Think UC
• PSC (primary sclerosing cholangitis) → Strongly associated with UC, risk of cholangiocarcinoma
• Do not perform colonoscopy in suspected toxic megacolon — get abdominal X-ray first
• IBD + arthritis or skin lesions → common extraintestinal manifestations
• Screen for TB and hepatitis B before starting anti-TNF biologics
Clinical pearls:
• Always rule out C. difficile or CMV colitis in children on immunosuppressants with bloody diarrhea,
especially before escalating immunosuppression.
• Hospitalization is warranted in any child with: severe pain, pallor or signs of hypovolemia, highoutput bloody stool, signs of systemic toxicity
• Toxic megacolon is a serious complication in UC—look for abdominal distension, systemic toxicity,
and signs of ileus.
• Early GI and infectious disease consultation is important
• Azathioprine is used for maintenance, not for induction or flare management → also can
cause bone marrow suppression and hepatotoxicity—check CBC and LFTs.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 408–410; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 128
☆ Gastroenterology: Ulcerative Colitis Flare – Emergency Management
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 408–410; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 128

stem:
A 7-year-old girl presents with salt craving, muscle weakness, and generalized fatigue. On physical
examination, she is hypotensive and has hyperpigmentation of the palmar creases and buccal
mucosa. She also appears thin with signs of muscle wasting. What is the most likely laboratory
profile in this patient?

options.label: A
options.text: Low sodium, high potassium, high plasma renin

options.label: B
options.text: Hyponatremia, hypochloremia, normal potassium, normal renin

options.label: C
options.text: Low sodium, low potassium, high plasma renin

options.label: D
options.text: Normal sodium, low potassium, low plasma renin

answer: A. Low sodium, high potassium, high plasma renin
explanation:
• This child has classic signs of primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease):
o Salt craving, fatigue, hypotension, and hyperpigmentation
• Aldosterone deficiency leads to:
o ↓ Na⁺ reabsorption → hyponatremia
o ↓ K⁺ excretion → hyperkalemia
• Volume depletion → ↑ renin as compensation
• Cortisol deficiency leads to increased ACTH, causing hyperpigmentation
ACTH is derived from proopiomelanocortin (POMC) → ↑ melanocyte-stimulating hormone
(MSH) secretion → ↑ melanin and thus hyperpigmentation
Option D: normal Na and low renin is more consistent with secondary adrenal insufficiency
o
•
★ Adrenal Insufficiency:
• Types:
o Primary (Addison’s): adrenal gland failure (↑ ACTH, ↓ cortisol, ↓ aldosterone)
o Secondary: pituitary dysfunction (↓ ACTH, ↓ cortisol, normal aldosterone)
o Tertiary: hypothalamic cause or chronic steroid use (most common in peds)
• Common pediatric cause:
o Exogenous steroid withdrawal (suppression of HPA axis)
o Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency)
• Classic lab findings:
o Hyponatremia
o Hyperkalemia in primary AI due to absence of aldosterone
o Hypoglycemia (more pronounced in infants and young
children)
o Low cortisol, high ACTH in primary
o Low cortisol, low ACTH in secondary/tertiary
• Clinical signs:
o Fatigue, hypotension, salt craving, vomiting, abdominal pain
o Hyperpigmentation (only in primary due to high ACTH)
o Shock in adrenal crisis
• Adrenal crisis triggers:
o Infection, trauma, surgery, abrupt steroid withdrawal
• Diagnosis:
o Early morning serum cortisol <3 µg/dL = diagnostic
o ACTH stimulation test (cosyntropin) confirms diagnosis
o Plasma ACTH, renin, aldosterone to differentiate primary
vs secondary
• Acute management:
o Hydrocortisone IV 50–100 mg/m²/day divided q6h
o IV fluids: D5NS boluses (20 mL/kg) for hypotension +
maintenance
o Correct hypoglycemia: D10–D25 2–4 mL/kg bolus if
needed
• Chronic management:
o Hydrocortisone PO 8–12 mg/m²/day divided TID
o Fludrocortisone 0.05–0.2 mg/day for mineralocorticoid replacement (in primary)
• Stress dosing:
o Triple baseline steroid dose during illness, IM hydrocortisone if vomiting
• Board traps:
o SIADH has euvolemic hyponatremia with normal glucose and BP
o No hyperkalemia in secondary adrenal insufficiency
o Persistent hypoglycemia in neonate → think CAH or adrenal insufficiency
Clinical pearls:
• Always suspect Addison disease with:
o Hyperpigmentation + hypotension +
electrolyte disturbance
• Diagnostic confirmation:
o Low cortisol, high ACTH
o Poor response to cosyntropin stimulation test
• Management:
o Hydrocortisone (stress dosing during illness)
o Fludrocortisone for mineralocorticoid
replacement
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 234–235; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 94;
UWorld; AMBOSS.
☆ Endocrinology: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency – Addison Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 234–235; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 94;

stem: In the renal tubule, where does the majority of bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) reabsorption occur?
explanation:
• The proximal tubule is responsible for reabsorbing approximately 85–90% of filtered bicarbonate
• Mechanism:
o Na⁺/H⁺ exchanger facilitates H⁺ secretion into the lumen
o H⁺ combines with filtered HCO₃⁻ → H₂CO₃ → converted to CO₂ and H₂O by carbonic
anhydrase
o CO₂ diffuses back into the cell and is reconverted to HCO₃⁻ and reabsorbed into blood
• The loop of Henle, distal tubule, and collecting duct handle minor fractions of bicarbonate
reabsorption and fine-tuning of acid-base balance
• The collecting duct is more involved in acid secretion (via α-intercalated cells) rather than bulk
HCO₃⁻ reabsorption
Clinical pearls:
• Proximal (type 2) RTA → impaired HCO₃⁻ reabsorption
• Distal (type 1) RTA → impaired acid secretion in distal nephron
• Acid-base disorders in children often require localization of defect based on urine pH, serum
bicarbonate, and anion gap
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 221; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 96; First
Aid for the USMLE, UWorld
☆ Nephrology: Renal Handling of Bicarbonate – Acid-Base Physiology
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 221; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 96; First
topic: Renal Handling of Bicarbonate – Acid-Base Physiology

stem: A CT scan of a newborn is provided below. What is the most likely causative organism?
explanation:
Congenital CMV infection is classically associated with periventricular calcifications, microcephaly,
ventriculomegaly, and sensorineural hearing loss.
• Toxoplasmosis causes diffuse intracranial calcifications, often involving the basal ganglia
and cortex.
• Rubella typically presents with PDA, cataracts, and deafness, but does not cause calcifications.
• HSV involves the temporal lobes with hemorrhagic necrosis, not calcifications.
★ Congenital (TORCH) infections:
Reminder, TORCH stands for:
Toxoplasmosis
Other (Syphilis, Parvovirus B19, Varicella, HIV, Hepatitis B)
Rubella
Cytomegalovirus (CMV)
HSV
Congenital Rubella triad mnemonic:
CCC
Cataracts
Cochlear defect
Cardiac (PDA)
HSV rash
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 297–299; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 343–344; UWorld;
AMBOSS.
☆ Infectious Diseases: TORCH infections
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 297–299; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 343–344; UWorld;

stem:
A child presents with palpable purpuric rash over the lower limbs (photo provided). If a skin
biopsy is performed, what would be the most characteristic finding?

options.label: C
options.text: Leukocytoclastic vasculitis with neutrophils but no immunoglobulin

answer: A. IgA deposition in dermal vessels
explanation:
★ Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP):
Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP) is an IgA-mediated (type III HSR), leukocytoclastic vasculitis that
presents with palpable purpura, arthralgia/arthritis, abdominal pain, and/or hematuria.
• Renal involvement is due to mesangial deposition of IgA immune complexes with subsequent
mesangial cell proliferation, neutrophilic infiltration, and glomerular damage.
• Most common vasculitis in children
o 90% of affected individuals < 10 years
o Peak incidence: 6 years
• May be precipitated by an upper respiratory tract/ GI infection by 1–3 weeks.
• Diagnosis is clinical:
o Purpura (usually palpable and in clusters) or petechiae, with lower limb predominance
and without thrombocytopenia or coagulopathy.
o PLUS at least one of the following:
 Abdominal pain (usually diffuse, with acute onset)
 Arthritis or arthralgia (acute onset)
 Renal involvement (proteinuria, hematuria)
 IgA deposition (leukocytoclastic vasculitis, proliferative glomerulonephritis)
• Normal laboratory values; ↓ Hemoglobin suggests GI bleeding.
• Complications:
o Intussusception (ileo-ileal, unlike classic ileo-colic); due to bowel wall edema, most
common GI complication
o Renal failure (especially with nephrotic-range proteinuria or elevated creatinine)
 Chronic kidney disease if severe glomerulonephritis
o Scrotal edema in males
o Rare: pulmonary hemorrhage, CNS vasculitis
• Treatment:
o Supportive care: hydration, rest, NSAIDs for arthralgia
 Avoid NSAIDs in patients with IgAV nephritis or GI bleeding
o Corticosteroids: for severe abdominal pain, renal involvement, or intussusception
 Rule out intussusception before starting systemic glucocorticoids in patients with
severe abdominal pain.
o ACE inhibitors: if persistent proteinuria
• Prognosis:
o Most cases of IgAV are self-limited and only require supportive care.
o Monitor urine and BP for 6–12 months for renal sequelae
 Urinalysis (for hematuria and proteinuria) every 1–2 weeks for the first 1–2 months
→ monthly up to 6–12 months
 BP monitoring with every visit
Clinical pearls:
• HSP = palpable purpura + abdominal pain ± arthritis ± renal involvement
• Most cases resolve spontaneously
• Monitor urine and BP weekly for 4–6 weeks
📚 Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Rheumatology Chapter (Vasculitis); Osama Naga 2024, Immunology
☆ Rheumatology: Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP), IgA Vasculitis

stem: Which of the following vaccines provides a short-lived immune response and does not induce longterm immunologic memory in young children?
explanation:
• PPSV23 is a polysaccharide vaccine that induces a T-cell–independent immune response
o Poorly immunogenic in children <2 years
o No immunologic memory formed → short-lived protection
• PCV13 is a conjugate vaccine that links polysaccharides to a protein carrier → T-cell–dependent
response with strong, long-lasting immunity (more immunogenic)
• MMR and Varicella are live-attenuated vaccines that induce robust, long-term cellular and
humoral immunity
• PPSV23 is used in older children and adults with certain high-risk conditions (e.g., asplenia,
immunocompromised)
Clinical pearls:
• Conjugate vaccines (like PCV13, Hib) are preferred in young children for inducing memory
responses
• PPSV23 is recommended after PCV13 in specific high-risk groups (e.g., sickle cell,
immunodeficiency)
• T-cell–independent vaccines do not work well in infants due to immature immune system
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 554; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1010
☆ Infectious Diseases: Pneumococcal Vaccines – Immunologic Response
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 554; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1010

stem:
A 7-year-old child is admitted to the PICU with altered mental status and suspected bacterial
meningitis. During examination, he develops a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. He is treated
promptly with benzodiazepines, IV fluids, ceftriaxone, and vancomycin. Despite appropriate
antiepileptics, the seizure persists. Which of the following is the most likely complication of
meningitis causing seizures that are refractory to standard treatment?
explanation:
• Hyponatremia from the syndrome of inappropriate secretion of ADH (SIADH) is a common
complication of bacterial meningitis.
• Hyponatremic seizures are notoriously resistant to anticonvulsants and need to be treated with
appropriate saline solutions.
• Low medication dose is possible but less likely if dosing is per protocol and seizures were
previously controlled
• Noncompliance is unlikely in an inpatient setting
• A brain abscess is a complication that may cause seizures, but usually is not one of the presenting
signs. This complication may need neurosurgical care.
• Subdural empyema is not a presenting sign, but seizures resulting from irritation from an empyema
usually respond well to therapy.
•
•
→ While subdural empyema is a possible complication of meningitis and can cause seizures, the
most immediate and common correctable cause is hyponatremia
Increased ICP (choice D) is a possible complication from meningitis and may cause seizures (but
not as common as hyponatremia).
Medical therapy is usually not sufficient.
Clinical pearls:
• Always check serum electrolytes in children with seizures unresponsive to AEDs
• In meningitis, monitor for:
o SIADH → hyponatremia
o Cerebral edema
o Septic thrombosis, empyema if seizures persist or focal
• Treat hyponatremia cautiously to avoid central pontine myelinolysis
• Targeted sodium correction:
o Mild: fluid restriction
o Severe/symptomatic: 3% hypertonic saline, 2–4 mL/kg bolus
Similar question/concept in Lange Q&A:
38. Subdural fluid collections (effusions) develop in 10%–30% of patients with bacterial meningitis,
particularly in infants. What clinical features raise concern for a subdural empyema rather than a
simple sterile effusion?
A. Ongoing seizures
B. Persistent or recurrent fever
C. New focal neurological signs
D. Worsening mental status
E. All of the above
Correct Answer: E. All of the above
Explanation:
Subdural empyema is a serious complication of bacterial meningitis, especially in infants and young
children. The presence of:
• Refractory seizures
• Poor clinical response to antibiotics
• Focal neurologic deficits
• Altered consciousness
...should prompt urgent neuroimaging to evaluate for subdural empyema or brain abscess.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 963; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 188;
Canadian Pediatric Qbank (Question #603; QID: 3530), LANGE Q&A Pediatrics, Question 38 — Subdural
fluid complications in meningitis
☆ Neurology: Seizures in Meningitis – Electrolyte-Related Causes
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 963; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 188;

stem:
A 12-year-old boy presents with chronic cough producing whitish-greenish sputum, weight loss, and
a history of recent contact with his uncle, who was recently released from prison. The child appears
thin and mildly febrile. There is no hemoptysis or clubbing noted. What is the most likely cause of his
symptoms?
explanation:
• This child’s presentation is highly suggestive of pulmonary TB, particularly due to:
o Prolonged cough with sputum
o Weight loss and constitutional symptoms
Known TB risk exposure: contact with a recently incarcerated relative (prisons are high-risk
settings for TB)
TB is a chronic infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis
Malignancy (e.g., lymphoma) can cause weight loss and respiratory symptoms, but less likely with
sputum and this exposure history
Viral infections are more acute, typically not associated with weight loss or prolonged cough
Fungal pneumonia is less likely in immunocompetent children without specific exposures
o
•
•
•
•
★ Pediatric Tuberculosis:
• Transmission & Risk Factors
o Spread via airborne droplets, usually from a household contact
o Infants and children <5 yrs are at highest risk for progression to active disease
• TB Testing: PPD vs IGRA
o PPD (Mantoux)
 Positive if ≥10 mm in high-risk children (e.g., close contacts, age <4, foreign-born)
 Positive if ≥5 mm in immunocompromised, close contacts, or abnormal CXR
IGRA (Quantiferon)
 Preferred in BCG-vaccinated children ≥2 years
 Not reliable in <2 years
 Does not distinguish active vs latent TB
Clinical Features of Active TB
o Pulmonary TB
 Chronic cough, weight loss, fever, fatigue
 Hilar lymphadenopathy, pleural effusion on imaging
o Extrapulmonary TB
 CNS (meningitis), miliary TB, lymph nodes, bones (Pott
disease)
 Infants and toddlers are at high risk for disseminated TB
Diagnosis of Active TB
o Start with chest X-ray
o Sputum AFB smear/culture if age ≥10 or able to expectorate
o Gastric aspirates x3 if <10 years old
o Consider CSF or lymph node biopsy if extrapulmonary TB
o Always screen for HIV
Latent TB Infection (LTBI)
o Defined as positive PPD or IGRA, no symptoms, normal CXR
o Treatment options
 INH x 9 months
 Rifampin x 4 months
 INH + Rifapentine weekly x 3 months (if age >2 yrs, HIVnegative)
o
•
•
•
•
Treatment of Active TB
•
o RIPE for 2 months: Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol
o Then INH + Rifampin for 4 months
o Total treatment: 6 months, extend to 9–12 months for CNS or bone TB
o Use Directly Observed Therapy (DOT) to ensure adherence
Mothers with treated TB can breastfeed using expressed milk only
Clinical pearls:
• Always assess TB risk in children with:
o Close contact with known or high-risk TB adults
o Prolonged respiratory symptoms, especially with systemic signs
• Diagnostic approach:
o PPD or IGRA, chest X-ray, and sputum/gastric aspirate AFB smear and culture
• Management:
o RIPE therapy (Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) with direct observation (DOT)
o Screen household contacts
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 903–905; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 216
☆ Infectious Diseases: Tuberculosis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 903–905; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 216

stem:
A 13-year-old obese child presents for a routine check-up. His BMI is >30 kg/m², and a random blood
glucose measured during the visit is 172 mg/dL. He is currently asymptomatic with no history of
polyuria or polydipsia. What is the next best step in management?

options.label: C
options.text: Advise weight loss and follow-up in 4 weeks

explanation:
• A random blood glucose of 172 mg/dL is elevated but not diagnostic of diabetes mellitus
• According to ADA diagnostic criteria for diabetes in children:
o Random glucose ≥200 mg/dL (11.11 mmol/L) with symptoms = diagnostic
o Fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL (7 mmol/L) on 2 occasions
HbA1c ≥6.5% (not always reliable in early pediatric screening)
Since this child is asymptomatic, the next step is to obtain a fasting blood glucose to clarify the
diagnosis
Metformin is appropriate only after confirmed diagnosis of type 2 DM
Weight loss advice is important, but without further testing, this would delay necessary evaluation
While HbA1c is useful, fasting glucose remains the most direct and widely used initial test in
asymptomatic high-risk children
o
•
•
•
•
Clinical pearls:
• All obese children (BMI >95th percentile) with risk factors (family history, signs of insulin
resistance, or ethnicity) should be screened for diabetes
• If fasting blood glucose is borderline, consider oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT)
• Lifestyle modification is always part of management but does not replace diagnostic testing
• Metformin is first-line pharmacologic therapy after confirmed diagnosis
• Note: to convert mg/dL to mmol/L divide by 18
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 690–691; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 173
☆ Endocrinology: Type 2 Diabetes Screening – Pediatric Obesity
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 690–691; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 173

stem:
A 2-year-old boy presented with intermittent abdominal pain and currant jelly stool. He was
diagnosed with intussusception, which resolved after air enema reduction. Subsequent imaging
revealed a persistent tubular structure in the right lower quadrant near the ileum. What is the most
likely underlying diagnosis?
explanation:
• Meckel diverticulum is the most common congenital anomaly of the gastrointestinal tract and
a known lead point for recurrent intussusception
• It is a remnant of the vitelline duct, often located within 2 feet of the ileocecal valve
• It may contain ectopic gastric mucosa, which can cause bleeding or act as a lead point in
intussusception
• After reduction of intussusception, further imaging (e.g., Meckel scan, CT) may reveal a tubular
structure in the ileum
• Bowel ischemia would present with persistent pain, peritonitis, or pneumatosis on imaging, not a
discrete structure
• Duplication cyst can mimic Meckel but is less common and usually cystic, not tubular
• Lymphoma is rare at this age and presents more often with mass effect or systemic symptoms
Clinical pearls:
• Always evaluate for pathologic lead points in intussusception >2 years or recurrent cases
o Suspect Meckel diverticulum in recurrent or persistent intussusception
• Meckel diverticulum = most common congenital GI anomaly; it is a remnant of the
omphalomesenteric duct (normally obliterated by the 6th week of intrauterine life)
• Meckel diverticulum often contains heterotopic gastric mucosa. Secretion of hydrochloric acid by
gastric parietal cells causes ulceration and bleeding of local small bowel, resulting in typical
findings of painless lower gastrointestinal (GI) bleeding and anemia.
• Classic triad: painless bleeding, young child, normal exam
• Meckel’s rule of 2s:
o Occurs in ~2% of population
o Within 2 feet of ileocecal valve
o ~2 inches in length
o Presents before age 2
2 types of ectopic tissue (gastric, pancreatic)
Meckel scintigraphy scan (Meckel scan): a non-invasive nuclear medicine imaging technique using
radiolabelled technetium (99mTc), which is preferentially absorbed by the gastric mucosa and can
identify ectopic gastric mucosa
Management: surgical excision if confirmed and bleeding is significant
o
•
•
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 424–425; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 130
☆ Gastroenterology: Intussusception, Meckel’s Diverticulum
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 424–425; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 130

stem:
A 7-year-old girl presents with chronic diarrhea, failure to thrive, and abdominal distension. Celiac
disease is suspected. Serologic tests are pending. What is the gold standard for confirming the
diagnosis of celiac disease?
explanation:
• The gold standard for diagnosis of celiac disease is duodenal biopsy showing: villous atrophy,
crypt hyperplasia, and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes
• Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA (tTG-IgA) is the best screening test (sensitive and specific)
• Anti-endomysial antibodies (EMA-IgA) are also highly specific but used less due to cost and
operator dependency
• Genetic testing (HLA DQ2/DQ8) is supportive; negative HLA effectively rules out celiac, but positive
status is not diagnostic
Clinical pearls:
• Celiac disease = autoimmune reaction to gluten → villous atrophy
• Antibody of choice: tTG-IgA (check total IgA in case of deficiency)
• Definitive diagnosis: duodenal biopsy showing villous atrophy + crypt hyperplasia
o Most common location(s): distal duodenum, proximal jejunum
o Ensure patient is on gluten-containing diet before biopsy and antibody testing
• Most common nutritional deficiencies: iron deficiency, folate deficiency
o Can present as iron deficiency anemia not responsive to Tx
o Note: iron is absorbed in the duodenum. Folate (Vitamin B9) is absorbed in the duodenum
and jejunum. Vitamin B12 and bile salts are absorbed in the terminal ileum.
• Screen for iron deficiency anemia, short stature, and type 1 DM in at-risk populations
•
Treatment: lifelong gluten-free diet
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 408–409; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 128
☆ Gastroenterology: Celiac Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 408–409; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 128

stem:
A 6-year-old child presents to the clinic with a complaint of esotropia (inward deviation) of one eye. The
parents report the eye appears to turn inward especially when the child looks toward distant objects. No
ptosis or pupillary abnormalities are noted on exam. Which cranial nerve palsy is most likely
responsible for this finding?
explanation:
• Esotropia (inward deviation of the eye) suggests a weakness of the lateral
rectus muscle, which is innervated by the 6th cranial nerve
• 6th nerve palsy leads to impaired abduction, resulting in unopposed medial
rectus action → esotropia.
• 2nd CN = optic nerve → affects vision, not eye movement.
• 3rd CN palsy → ptosis, mydriasis, "down and out" eye.
• 4th CN palsy → vertical diplopia, worse on downgaze (e.g., reading, stairs), not esotropia.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 702–703; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 235–236; UWorld.
☆ Neurology: 6th Cranial Nerve Palsy (Abducens Nerve Palsy)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 702–703; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 235–236; UWorld.

stem:
A 9-month-old child is being followed for failure to thrive. Despite adherence to a high-calorie
feeding plan, the child is not gaining weight appropriately. There are no signs of acute illness or
organic disease on prior workup. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Increase calories further and follow up in 2 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Discharge patient home and continue current plan

options.label: C
options.text: Admit the patient for further evaluation and monitoring

options.label: D
options.text: Start nasogastric tube feeds at home

answer: C. Admit the patient for further evaluation and monitoring
explanation:
• This is a case of non-resolving failure to thrive (FTT) despite adequate caloric intake, which
raises concern for:
o Malabsorption
o Chronic illness
o Neglect or feeding difficulties
• Inpatient admission allows for:
o Direct monitoring of intake/output
o Elimination of external variables (e.g., psychosocial/environmental)
o Comprehensive multidisciplinary evaluation
• Admission Red Flags:
o Weight crossing percentiles rapidly downward
o Signs of dehydration or malnutrition
o Developmental delay with growth failure
o Suspicion of abuse/neglect or caregiver incapacity
o Failed outpatient weight gain despite intervention
o Need for rapid refeeding or IV nutritional support
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 98–100; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd Ed., p. 639–640
☆ General Pediatrics: Failure to Thrive
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 98–100; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 1-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department for painless passage of maroon-colored
stool. He is otherwise active and feeding well. There is no vomiting, no abdominal distension, and no
signs of systemic illness. Vitals are stable. Examination reveals a soft, non-tender abdomen.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Meckel diverticulum is the most common congenital GI anomaly and classically presents with:
o Painless lower GI bleeding
o Stool that is maroon or bright red
o Typically in children <2 years of age
• Caused by ectopic gastric mucosa in the ileum secreting acid → ulceration and bleeding
• Biliary atresia causes conjugated hyperbilirubinemia and pale stools, not blood
• Pyloric stenosis presents with projectile non-bilious vomiting, not bleeding
• Anal fissures cause painful defecation and streaks of blood, not maroon-colored stools
Clinical pearls:
• Meckel diverticulum = most common congenital GI anomaly; it is a remnant of the
omphalomesenteric duct (normally obliterated by the 6th week of intrauterine life)
• Meckel diverticulum often contains heterotopic gastric mucosa. Secretion of hydrochloric acid by
gastric parietal cells causes ulceration and bleeding of local small bowel, resulting in typical
findings of painless lower gastrointestinal (GI) bleeding and anemia.
• Classic triad: painless bleeding, young child, normal exam
• Meckel’s rule of 2s:
o Occurs in ~2% of population
o Within 2 feet of ileocecal valve
o ~2 inches in length
o Presents before age 2
o 2 types of ectopic tissue (gastric, pancreatic)
• Meckel scintigraphy scan (Meckel scan): a non-invasive nuclear medicine imaging technique using
radiolabelled technetium (99mTc), which is preferentially absorbed by the gastric mucosa and can
identify ectopic gastric mucosa
• Management: surgical excision if confirmed and bleeding is significant
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 424–425; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 130
☆ Gastroenterology: Painless GI Bleeding – Meckel Diverticulum
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 424–425; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 130

stem:
A 6-week-old boy presents with persistent diarrhea that began at 1 month of age. His mother reports
abdominal distention, eczema, and recent switch to soy-based formula due to presumed colic, but
symptoms persisted. Stool is positive for occult blood. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Common in infants <6 months, especially when switching from breastmilk to cow’s milk formula
• Epidemiology: primarily affects young infants (typically manifests at 2–8 weeks of age)
• Etiology:
o Hypersensitivity reaction to certain foods, most commonly cow's milk, followed by soy
protein, rice, and eggs
 Cow milk should not be introduced in the first year of life! 🚫🐄
o Associated with a personal and family history of atopy
• Presents with vomiting, bloody diarrhea, urticarial rash, irritability, and poor weight gain
• Symptoms occur shortly after feeding, suggesting a triggered immune response
• Diagnostics: mainly a clinical diagnosis based on patient history and clinical features
o IgE mediated: immediate symptoms: rash, vomiting; usually associated with anaphylaxis,
eczema, asthma
o Non-IgE mediated: delayed GI symptoms, blood in stool; associated with food intolerance,
constipation, GERD
• Breastfed infants may also react if the mother consumes dairy
• Treatment
o Removal of offending foods from the infant's diet
o Breastfed infants
 Elimination of all dairy and soy products from the mother's diet
 Continue breastfeeding
o Formula-fed infants: switching to a hydrolyzed formula (e.g., hydrolyzed casein)
o The offending foods should be carefully reintroduced to assess tolerance after 1 year of age.
• Complications: chronic colitis and/or persistent food allergy (rare)
• Prognosis
o Gross rectal bleeding usually improves within 3–4 days of removing the offending food.
o FPIAP usually resolves spontaneously by one year of age.
Other options:
• Switching to soy formula is often not effective, since 30–50% of CMPA infants cross-react to soy
• Lactose intolerance is rare in neonates and typically does not cause blood in stool
• Soy protein intolerance is possible, but symptoms began before soy exposure
• Infectious gastroenteritis is typically acute, with fever and vomiting
Clinical pearls:
• CMPA often presents between 2–6 months
• Exclusively breastfed infants may react to cow’s milk protein in maternal diet
• Best management: switch to extensively hydrolyzed formula (e.g., Nutramigen)
o Avoid both cow’s milk and soy proteins if symptoms persist
• Most infants outgrow CMPA by 1–3 years of age (Resolution occurs by 12–18 months)
• Consider referral to allergy specialist if unclear diagnosis or failure to respond
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 210–211; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guides, p. 123
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Cow’s Milk Protein Allergy (CMPA)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 210–211; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guides, p. 123

stem:
A breastfed infant is feeding normally, but the mother complains of pain during breastfeeding.
The baby is gaining weight adequately and appears well. What is the most appropriate next step?
explanation:
• Maternal breast pain during feeding is most often due to poor latch or positioning, not milk
insufficiency or feeding frequency.
• A lactation consultant can assess and correct technique, which usually resolves the pain.
• Lengthening intervals may worsen engorgement and reduce milk supply.
• Switching to formula is unnecessary unless breastfeeding is contraindicated.
• Pumping may temporarily relieve pain but doesn’t correct the root cause.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 144; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guide, p 160
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Breastfeeding
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 144; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guide, p 160

stem:
A 3-day-old infant who is exclusively breastfed presents with jaundice. The mother reports the baby
has 6 wet diapers/day and 3 bowel movements/day. The baby is feeding well, and examination
is completely normal. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: C
options.text: Stop breastfeeding for 3 days then continue

explanation:
• This is a classic presentation of physiologic jaundice in a healthy, exclusively breastfed newborn
o Appears after 24 hours of life
o Peaks by day 3–5, resolves by day 10–14
• Infant is hydrated, urinating and stooling well, and feeding normally → reassuring signs
• No evidence of breast milk jaundice (which appears after day 5–7)
• No indication to stop or supplement breastfeeding unless poor feeding, weight loss, or rising
bilirubin with risk
• Phototherapy is only indicated if bilirubin is above the treatment threshold per age and risk factors
★ Neonatal Jaundice
Clinical pearls:
• Continue exclusive breastfeeding unless signs of dehydration or poor feeding
• Monitor bilirubin with transcutaneous or total serum bilirubin as per AAP guidelines
• Educate mother on frequent breastfeeding (8–12 times/day) to enhance bilirubin clearance
• Breast milk jaundice → if persistent after 1 week with high bilirubin but baby is otherwise thriving
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 272–273; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 85
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Jaundice – Physiologic and Breastfeeding-Related
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 272–273; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 85

stem:
A preterm infant born at 32 weeks gestation is now stable and ready for discharge from the NICU. He
has been tolerating feeds, gaining weight, and has no ongoing respiratory support. Which of the
following follow-up evaluations is most essential at this stage?
explanation:
• Preterm infants are at risk of retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) which is exclusive to premature
neonates, and is a leading cause of severe visual impairment and blindness.
• Pathophysiology:
o Normal O₂ saturation in utero ≈ 50–70% → ↑VEGF → normal retinal vascularization by ~40 wks
o Premature birth → Supplemental oxygen may increase PaO₂ and O₂ saturation >90%, which
is hyperoxic relative to fetal levels.
o Phase 1: Hyperoxia → ↓VEGF → arrested vessel growth
o Phase 2: Hypoxia → ↑VEGF → pathologic neovascularization → retinal detachment
• The following infants should be screened for ROP:
o Low birthweight (1500 grams or less)
o Gestational age (<30 weeks or less)
o Birthweight between 1500-2000 grams or gestational age > 30 weeks and believed by their
pediatrician or neonatologist to be at risk for ROP (e.g. history of hypotension requiring
inotropic support, received supplemental oxygen for more than a few days or without oxygen
saturation monitoring)
• ROP screening typically starts at 4 weeks chronologic age or 31 weeks postmenstrual age,
whichever is later
• Pneumogram (sleep study) is not routinely indicated unless the infant had apnea or bradycardia
beyond term
• Echocardiogram is typically done earlier in NICU stay (e.g., for PDA); not standard at discharge
unless cardiac issues persist
• Renal ultrasound is indicated only in specific risk groups (e.g., oligohydramnios, abnormal
anatomy)
Clinical pearls:
• ROP screening should be arranged before discharge with documented follow-up
• Other essential discharge planning for preterms:
o Hearing screen
o Immunization catch-up (e.g., RSV prophylaxis if <29 weeks)
o Developmental follow-up (neurodevelopmental assessment)
• Breastfeeding support and monitoring for anemia of prematurity also important
📚 Source: Gomella’s Neonatology, 8th ed., p. 669–671; Fanaroff and Martin’s Neonatal-Perinatal Medicine,
11th ed., p. 1101
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Discharge Planning in Preterm Infants – ROP Screening
source: Gomella’s Neonatology, 8th ed., p. 669–671; Fanaroff and Martin’s Neonatal-Perinatal Medicine,
topic: Discharge Planning in Preterm Infants – ROP Screening

stem:
A 10-year-old boy presents with fatigue, dyspnea, and tachycardia. On examination, he has muffled
heart sounds, jugular venous distention, and a gallop rhythm with distant heart tones. Pulsus
paradoxus is present. There is no fever or rash. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Muffled heart sounds are classic for pericardial effusion, particularly when large.
• Gallop rhythm (S3) may be present due to restricted ventricular filling (tamponade physiology).
• Pericarditis typically presents with chest pain, pericardial friction rub, and diffuse ST elevations,
not muffled sounds.
• Myocarditis can present with S3 and heart failure symptoms, but heart sounds are usually not
muffled.
• Dilated cardiomyopathy leads to global systolic dysfunction, often with displaced apex and
gallop, but not muffled heart sounds.
Clinical pearls:
• Echocardiography is the diagnostic tool of choice for detecting pericardial effusion.
• Look for signs of cardiac tamponade: muffled heart sounds, hypotension, and jugular venous
distention (Beck’s triad).
• Management includes pericardiocentesis if signs of tamponade or hemodynamic instability.
• Common pediatric causes: viral infections (especially enteroviruses), autoimmune
disease, uremia, malignancy, post-surgical.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 437–438; Park’s Pediatric Cardiology, 6th ed., p. 385
☆ Cardiology: Pericardial Effusion
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 437–438; Park’s Pediatric Cardiology, 6th ed., p. 385

stem:
A 2-day-old term neonate is noted to have multiple erythematous macules and papules with central
yellow-white pustules, primarily on the face, trunk, and proximal limbs. The baby is otherwise
healthy, afebrile, and feeding well.
What is the most appropriate management?
explanation:
• This is classic erythema toxicum neonatorum, a benign and self-limiting skin condition of
newborns.
• Erythema toxicum neonatorum appears within the first week of life as erythematous macules and
papules that progress to pustules with surrounding erythema. It is most likely caused by the
immaturity of the sweat glands and follicles.
• The rash appears on the trunk and proximal extremities, but the palms and soles are typically
spared.
• Typically appears within the first 2–3 days of life and resolves within 1–2 weeks without treatment.
• Although erythema toxicum sounds ominous, the condition is benign and requires no treatment.
Clinical pearls:
• Differentiate from neonatal pustular melanosis (present at birth, pustules rupture leaving
pigmented macules).
• Differentiate from herpes, candidiasis, or bacterial infections — those usually have systemic
signs or unusual distribution.
• Always evaluate the general condition of the baby; if febrile or lethargic, consider sepsis workup.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 99; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 60
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Erythema Toxicum Neonatorum
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 99; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 60

stem:
A 14-year-old boy with no prior medical history collapses and dies suddenly while playing
basketball. He had no preceding symptoms. Family history is negative for cardiac disease or sudden
death. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in
young athletes.
• Caused by autosomal dominant mutations affecting sarcomeric proteins → asymmetric septal
hypertrophy → outflow tract obstruction or arrhythmia
• Sudden death typically occurs during or after exertion, often without prior symptoms.
• A systolic ejection murmur that increases with valsalva maneuver and standing and decreases
with hand grip, squatting, or lying down can be an important clue toward the diagnosis.
Other options:
• Prolonged QT can cause syncope or sudden death but is typically associated with auditory triggers,
medications, or known family history.
• ARVC is another cause but is less common and usually affects the right ventricle, with preceding
symptoms like palpitations.
• Myocarditis may cause arrhythmias or heart failure, but less often presents as sudden death in
athletes.
Clinical pearls:
• Screening athletes with ECG and echocardiogram if family history or suspicious symptoms (e.g.,
syncope with exertion)
• Systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva is a clue to HCM on physical exam
• Disqualify from competitive sports if HCM diagnosed
• Genetic counseling is recommended for family members
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 442–443; Park’s Pediatric Cardiology, 6th ed., p. 398–
399; UWorld.
☆ Cardiology: Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 442–443; Park’s Pediatric Cardiology, 6th ed., p. 398–

stem:
A newborn baby is delivered and found to have the following findings at 1 minute of life:
- Heart rate: 90 bpm
- Weak and irregular cry
- Grimacing to stimulation
- Cyanosis of hands and feet
- Weak flexion of extremities
What is the Apgar score for this newborn?
explanation:
Breakdown of Apgar scoring:
• Heart rate = 90 bpm → 1 point (HR < 100)
• Respiratory effort = weak/irregular cry → 1 point
• Grimace = facial response only (no cough/sneeze) → 1 point
• Muscle tone = weak flexion of extremities → 1 point
• Color = peripheral cyanosis (acrocyanosis) → 1 point
Total score = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 5
Clinical pearls:
• An Apgar score of 5 at 1 minute is moderate
depression, requiring close observation and possibly
supportive care (e.g., ventilation, warming)
• A score of 7–10 is reassuring; 4–6 is moderate
depression; 0–3 indicates severe distress
• Apgar score is assessed at 1 and 5 minutes, and at 10
minutes if score remains low
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 260; Gomella’s Neonatology, 8th ed., p. 282
☆ NICU/Neonatology: Apgar Score – Newborn Assessment
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 260; Gomella’s Neonatology, 8th ed., p. 282

stem:
A 12-year-old boy is found to have elevated blood pressure on routine screening. He is asymptomatic
and has no significant past medical history. Which of the following is the most likely cause of
hypertension in this patient?
explanation:
• <10 years old → Most cases are secondary hypertension
o Common causes: renal parenchymal disease, renal artery stenosis, coarctation of the
aorta, endocrine disorders (e.g., pheochromocytoma, hyperthyroidism)
• ≥10 years old (especially adolescents) → Most cases are primary (essential) hypertension
o Strongly associated with obesity, family history, sedentary lifestyle, metabolic syndrome
• Renal parenchymal disease is still a significant cause in children and must be ruled out with urine
analysis and renal ultrasound.
• Pheochromocytoma is rare and typically presents with episodic hypertension, headaches,
sweating, and palpitations.
• Coarctation of the aorta causes upper limb hypertension with diminished femoral pulses and is
usually diagnosed earlier.
★ Pediatric Hypertension:
• Risk factors: Obesity, male, Hispanic or African
American, FH aortic coarctation, some medications
• Screening/diagnosis (Pediatrics 2017;140(3)1–72)
o ≥3 yo: Screen annually or at every visit if RFs
o Screen <3 yo if: Hx of prematurity (<32 wk), SGA
or VLBW, NICU stay, congenital heart disease,
concern for renal disease, FH of congenital renal
disease, hx of transplant (solid organ, BMT),
medications, concern for ↑ ICP
• Any high BP (>90th percentile) should be rechecked via manual BP. Average of two manual
measurements should be used to assign a BP category per definitions.
• Always compare BP reading by gender, age and height to determine if the patient is truly
hypertensive (https://www.uptodate.com/contents/hypertension-in-children-and-adolescentsdefinition-and-diagnosis?source=autocomplete&index=2~4#topicGraphics)
• Diagnosis:
o Primary HTN: Most common – no obvious etiology/idiopathic
o Secondary HTN
 Causes: MANY – renal and
renovascular disease most common
o NOTE: Kids >6 yo do not need extensive workup
if FH, obesity, and no concerning hx or px
o Hx: Perinatal, nutrition, physical activity,
psychosocial, and family history
o Px: Height, weight, BMI – look for findings
consistent with the underlying secondary
cause for HTN.
• Labs:
o Get a UA, BMP, and lipid panel in every
patient with HTN
o In obese patients (BMI >95%), also get HbA1c
•
•
•
and ALT/AST
o Additional labs based on findings suspicious for
secondary HTN
Imaging: (Pediatrics 2017;140(3):1–72)
o Echocardiography recommended at the time of
medication initiation
o Renal US recommended for age <6 yo or abnormal UA or
renal function
o Doppler renal US recommended for evaluation of RAS in
children ≥8 yo who are not obese and have other
suspicion for renovascular HTN (bruit, significant
diastolic HTN, kidney size discrepancy)
NOTE: CTA/MRA are more sensitive and specific for RAS and may also be considered
Management of HTN:
o Goal of therapy is to prevent or ↓ end-organ damage and CV dx in adulthood
o Target SBP and DBP is <90th percentile or <130/80 in adolescents
o 1. Lifestyle modification: DASH diet, exercise 3–5×/wk
o 2. Pharmacologic Tx: If lifestyle mods fail, stage II HTN, symptomatic HTN, or HTN with
DM/CKD
 Start with a single agent: ACEI, ARB, long-acting CCB, or thiazide are first choices
 If CKD, proteinuria, or DM → start with ACEI or ARB as first line
Hypertensive emergency (Pediatrics 2017;140(3):1–72)
o Should be concerned if BP ↑ >30 mmHg above the 95th percentile (although can have sxs at
lower BP)
o Sxs: Encephalopathy, seizure, visual Δ, shortness of breath, peripheral edema, hematuria, ↓
UOP, tremor, nausea, vomiting
o Acute, severe hypertension is secondary HTN until proved otherwise (need full evaluation)
o Tx: Use short- acting anti-HTN agents
 Severe and life-threatening HTN → can use esmolol (β-blocker), hydralazine
(vasodilator), labetalol (α- and β-blocker), nicardipine (CCB), sodium nitroprusside
(vasodilator)
 Severe and not life threatening → clonidine (central α-agonist), fenoldopam
(dopamine receptor agonist), isradipine (CCB), minoxidil (vasodilator)

Goal: Reduce by no more than 25% of planned reduction in first 8 hr, remainder
over next 12–24 hr. Short-term goal is ∼95th percentile
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 195–196; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p.
186; UpToDate; Pocket Pediatrics 2024: Nephrology – Hypertension
☆ Nephrology: Pediatric Hypertension
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 195–196; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p.
topic: • Risk factors: Obesity, male, Hispanic or African

stem:
A 10-year-old boy with known sickle cell disease (HbSS) presents with acute right-sided
hemiparesis. A non-contrast CT of the head is normal. He is hemodynamically stable. What is
the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: D
options.text: MRI brain and spine before intervention

explanation:
• In sickle cell disease, stroke is a known and severe complication, often due to large vessel
occlusion from sickled RBCs.
• A normal CT does not rule out ischemic stroke, especially early; most pediatric strokes are
ischemic and not visible initially on CT.
• Urgent exchange transfusion is the treatment of choice to:
o Rapidly reduce HbS levels <30%
Improve oxygen-carrying capacity
Reduce further sickling and infarction
Simple blood transfusion is used if exchange transfusion is unavailable, but less effective in
lowering HbS%
Hydroxyurea is preventive, not for acute stroke management
MRI/MRA is used to confirm ischemia and assess vasculature, but should not delay treatment
o
o
•
•
•
Clinical pearls:
• Exchange transfusion is first-line in acute stroke in SCD
• Long-term management includes chronic transfusion program or hydroxyurea, and TCD
(transcranial Doppler) screening
• If transfusions are used chronically, monitor for iron overload and consider chelation therapy
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1009–1010; Osama Naga Pediatric Board, p. 215;
Maryam AlTayeb Med School Notes: Anemias & Hemoglobinopathies
☆ Hematology: Sickle Cell Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1009–1010; Osama Naga Pediatric Board, p. 215;

stem: What is the estimated caloric value of standard cow’s milk-based infant formula per 1 mL?
explanation:
• Standard infant formulas (including cow’s milk-based formulas) are designed to provide:
o 20 kcal/oz, which equals 0.67 kcal/mL
• This is equivalent to the caloric density of human breast milk, ensuring comparable growth
support.
• Specialized formulas (e.g., preterm or high-energy formulas) may provide 24–30 kcal/oz (0.8–1.0
kcal/mL), but standard formula remains 0.67 kcal/mL.
Clinical pearls:
• Preterm infants often need higher-calorie formulas (e.g., 22–24 kcal/oz)
• Caloric density can be increased by concentrating formula or adding fortifiers in NICU settings
• Always monitor weight gain, hydration status, and electrolytes when modifying formula
★ High Yield Caloric Facts for Pediatric Boards:
• Breast milk: 0.67 kcal/mL = 20 kcal/oz → Breast milk preferred for immunity; formula has similar
caloric content but lacks immunologic factors
• Standard formula (term): 0.67 kcal/mL = 20 kcal/oz
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
Preterm formula: 0.8–1.0 kcal/mL = 22–30 kcal/oz
Fortified breast milk (HMF): ~22–24 kcal/oz
Carbohydrates (dextrose): 3.4 kcal/g
Protein (amino acids): 4 kcal/g
Fat (lipids): 9 kcal/g
Daily caloric needs:
o Term infant: 100–120 kcal/kg/day
o Preterm infant: 110–130 kcal/kg/day
o VLBW infants: up to 140–150 kcal/kg/day
o Catch-up growth: up to 150 kcal/kg/day
FTT workup: Assess total caloric intake vs. requirement; inadequate intake <80 kcal/kg/day
Weight drops as much as 10% in the first few days of life, probably as a result of loss of excess fluid;
however, birth weight should be regained 7- 14 days after birth in majority of children. If a neonate is
fed appropriately, anticipate weight gain 20-30 grams per day or 150- 200 grams per week.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 65; Gomella’s Neonatology, 8th ed., p. 116
☆ Nutrition & Neonatology: Infant Formula
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 65; Gomella’s Neonatology, 8th ed., p. 116
category: High Yield Caloric Facts for Pediatric Boards
topic: • Breast milk: 0.67 kcal/mL = 20 kcal/oz → Breast milk preferred for immunity; formula has similar

stem: A child can reach the adult vision power of 20/20 at what age?
explanation:
• Newborns have limited vision (about 20/400).
• Vision improves rapidly in the first year, but adult-level visual acuity (20/20) is typically achieved
around 4 to 5 years of age.
• Earlier ages may show visual maturation, but not full acuity.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 232; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 212–213.
☆ Ophthalmology: Vision
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 232; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 212–213.

stem:
A 13-year-old boy presents with concerns of short stature. His physical exam and history are
unremarkable. His mother’s height is 161 cm and father’s height is 170 cm. What is his midparental target height?
explanation:
Midparental height (girl) = (Father’s height + Mother’s height + 13)/2 = [(170+13)+161]/2 = 172 cm ± 8.5 cm
• Mid-parental height is a key reference point in evaluating short stature.
o A child falling within ±8.5 cm of the MPH is generally considered within normal genetic potential.
☆ Endocrinology: Short stature, mid-parental height calculation

stem:
A 10-month-old infant is brought for a routine well-baby visit. The mother expresses concern that her
child has not yet had any dental eruption. Growth parameters and physical examination are normal.
What is the most likely cause of delayed tooth eruption in this case?
explanation:
• Tooth eruption typically begins between 6–10 months of age.
• In an otherwise healthy infant with normal growth, delayed eruption is most often idiopathic.
• Pathologic causes (e.g., hypothyroidism, hypopituitarism, hypoparathyroidism) are
considered only if other signs are present, such as:
o Short stature, constipation, macroglossia, delayed milestones → Hypothyroidism
o Hypoglycemia, growth failure → Hypopituitarism
o Seizures, hypocalcemia → Hypoparathyroidism
Clinical pearls:
• Delayed eruption is defined as no teeth by 18 months of age or
6 months from expected eruption time.
• Most common cause of isolated delayed eruption
= idiopathic/familial
• If delayed eruption occurs with other systemic signs, evaluate
for underlying endocrine or metabolic disorders
• Eruption pattern is not a reliable indicator of underlying pathology unless other red flags exist
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 64, 355
☆ General Pediatrics: Tooth Eruption
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 64, 355

stem:
A 2-year-old boy from India is brought to the clinic for evaluation of failure to thrive. His parents
report that he is developmentally normal and attends daycare. On examination, his weight is
below the 3rd percentile, and his height is at the 5th percentile. He has no edema, no skin or
hair changes, and no facial dysmorphism. His diet is poor in calories and protein. What is the
most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Marasmus is a form of severe protein-calorie malnutrition, commonly seen in resource-limited
settings.
• Characterized by:
o Wasting without edema
o Significant weight loss or weight below 3rd percentile
o No fat stores, no edema, no skin or hair changes
o Preserved alertness and often normal development early on
• Caused by a deficiency in both energy and protein
Clinical Pearls:
• Kwashiorkor presents with edema, dermatosis, hair changes, hepatomegaly, and apathy
• Kwashiorkor is due to decrease in protein intake with normal caloric intake of carbs, leading to
edema. The expected weight is around 60-80%.
• Marasmus is due to decrease caloric intake of all protein, fat and carbs. No edema, and the weight
is usually below 60%.
