• Rickets causes bony deformities, especially in lower limbs, and is linked to vitamin D deficiency,
not failure to thrive alone
• A normal child would not have such poor weight and height percentile without other findings
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 93–94; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd Ed., p. 646–647
☆ General Pediatrics: Failure to Thrive, Malnutrition
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 93–94; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 3-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with 3 days of cough and shortness of
breath. A venous blood gas (VBG) is obtained and shows the following values:
- pH: 7.10
- pCO₂: 61 mmHg
- HCO₃⁻: 28 mEq/L
Which of the following best describes the acid-base disturbance?
explanation:
Mnemonic (acid-base): SMORE: change in PCO2 in the Same direction as pH → Metabolic
disorder; change in PCO2 in the Opposite direction to pH → REspiratory disorder
Interpreting the VBG:
1. pH = 7.10 (↓) → indicates acidemia
2. pCO₂ = 61 mmHg (↑) → elevated → suggests primary respiratory acidosis
3. HCO₃⁻ = 28 mEq/L (↑) → slightly elevated, indicating renal compensation for a chronic process
• This pattern is typical of respiratory acidosis, often seen in children with hypoventilation due
to lower respiratory tract infection, bronchiolitis, asthma exacerbation, or neuromuscular
weakness
• In the acute setting, it is important to note that a tachypneic patient should have respiratory
alkalosis if ventilation is effective (due to washout of CO2). However, if the patient has normal pH or
is acidotic, this can indicate impending respiratory failure!
Clinical pearls:
• Venous pCO₂ is usually slightly higher than arterial, but still useful to recognize trends
• Normal pH: 7.35–7.45
• Normal pCO₂: 35–45 mmHg
• Normal HCO₃⁻: 22–26 mEq/L
• Always interpret pH first, then CO₂ and HCO₃⁻ to determine primary disorder and compensation
• Consider ABG in severe cases for better accuracy
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 218–219; UWorld.
☆ Emergency Medicine: Acid-Base Disorders, Respiratory Acidosis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 218–219; UWorld.

stem:
A 10-year-old boy with recurrent periumbilical abdominal pain for 3 months is diagnosed with
functional abdominal pain. His physical exam, growth parameters, and lab workup are all normal. The
pain often occurs before school or swimming practice, and he has no red flag symptoms.
What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: B
options.text: Keep the child at home and start a food diary

options.label: C
options.text: Order abdominal CT to rule out organic causes

options.label: D
options.text: Encourage continued school attendance and extracurricular activities

answer: D. Encourage continued school attendance and extracurricular activities
explanation:
• Functional abdominal pain (FAP) is best managed with reassurance, normalization of routine,
and maintenance of normal activities
• Avoidance behaviors (e.g., staying home from school or sports) may reinforce symptoms and
worsen functional disability
• Psychosocial support, including return to school and sports, is essential
• Medications are rarely needed and should only be considered in select cases
• Imaging is not indicated when the history and exam are reassuring
Clinical pearls:
• FAP is a diagnosis of exclusion and one of the most common causes of chronic abdominal pain in
children
• Maintaining routine, physical activity, and social engagement improves outcomes
• Consider referral to psychology for CBT if symptoms persist or worsen
• Red flag symptoms (weight loss, GI bleeding, family history of IBD) must be ruled out
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 403
☆ Gastroenterology: Functional Abdominal Pain
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 403

stem:
A 30-year-old pregnant woman with type 1 diabetes mellitus has had well-controlled glucose levels
all throughout her pregnancy. Her most recent and prior HbA1C levels are within normal range. What
is the most likely condition you expect in the newborn immediately after delivery?
answer: C. Weight normal for gestational age
explanation:
• When glucose control is excellent before conception and throughout pregnancy, fetal exposure to
hyperglycemia is minimized.
• This prevents fetal islet cell hyperplasia and hyperinsulinism.
• The newborn is expected to have normal growth parameters and low risk for metabolic
complications.
• Hypoglycemia and macrosomia are primarily associated with poor glycemic control.
• If the question mentions control only during pregnancy/throughout the later trimesters → then the
fetus is still at risk of hypoglycemia.
Clinical pearls:
• Preconception glucose control is key to reducing fetal and neonatal complications in diabetic
pregnancies
• HbA1C should be normal before conception and during gestation to minimize risks for best fetal
outcomes
• Infants of diabetic mothers with tight control can be comparable to healthy term neonates in
outcomes
• Still advisable to monitor glucose after birth, though routine complications are unlikely
(For more details on complication of maternal diabetes, see explanation under Q29)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 263–264; Fanaroff & Martin’s Neonatal-Perinatal
Medicine, 11th ed., p. 1153
☆ Neonatology & Endocrinology: Infant of Diabetic Mother – Outcomes with Optimal Glycemic Control
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 263–264; Fanaroff & Martin’s Neonatal-Perinatal
topic: Infant of Diabetic Mother – Outcomes with Optimal Glycemic Control

stem: What is the most common cardiac defect associated with congenital rubella syndrome?
explanation:
(For more details see Q43)
• PDA is the hallmark cardiac lesion in congenital rubella syndrome (CRS).
• It is often accompanied by peripheral pulmonary artery stenosis.
• CRS is classically associated with the triad of:
o PDA
o Sensorineural hearing loss
o Cataracts
• VSD and ASD may occur but are less specific or frequent.
• Pulmonary stenosis can be associated but usually in combination with PDA, not as a primary
isolated lesion.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 299–300; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 345.
☆ Infectious Diseases: TORCH infections
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 299–300; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 345.

stem:
A 3 days old term, healthy infant is noted to be jaundiced. Physical examination is otherwise
normal. Laboratory values: Hb 19 g/dL; reticulocytes 1.0%; indirect bilirubin 190 umol/l, direct
bilirubin 7 umol/l. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Key Board Clue: Healthy term baby + jaundice after 24h = physiologic
• Onset: >24 h, peaks 3–5 days, resolves <1 week
• Labs: isolated indirect hyperbilirubinemia, no hemolysis or sepsis
• Direct bili <10% of total = physiologic
• If direct bili >20% of total → always investigate for cholestasis
📚 Source: Nelson Neonatology Ch. 99; Osama Naga 2024 p. 237–238.
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Neonatal Jaundice
source: Nelson Neonatology Ch. 99; Osama Naga 2024 p. 237–238.

stem:
A 5-day-old full term neonate delivered at home, normal birth history, exclusively breast feed,
presented to the emergency department with bruises. Investigations showed; normal platelet count,
prolonged PT & normal fibrinogen. The neonatal examinations are unremarkable apart from the
bruises. No hepatosplenomegaly. Vital signs are stable. This neonate most likely has which of the
following?
explanation:
Home birth + breastfeeding + early bruising = Vit K deficiency bleeding (VKDB).
• Classic: early bleeding (<7 days) in exclusive breastfeeding + no Vit K shot at birth.
• Isolated prolonged PT (extrinsic pathway defect).
• Normal platelets + fibrinogen rules out DIC.
• Vitamin K–deficient bleeding usually occurs in the first week of life but can present through age 6
months.
•
•
•
•
Vitamin K is an essential cofactor for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase, the enzyme responsible for
carboxylation of clotting factors II (prothrombin), VII, IX, and X.
o Healthy children and adults typically have adequate vitamin K stores from dietary sources
(eg, green, leafy vegetables) and from endogenous colonic bacteria that generate the
vitamin.
o In contrast, newborns have low vitamin K stores due to poor transplacental
transfer and low content in breast milk.
o Delayed vitamin K production by the gradual colonization of bacterial gut flora as well as
inefficient use by an immature liver also contribute to low levels in early infancy.
Additional risk factors include exclusive breastfeeding, due to the low vitamin K content of breast
milk, and cystic fibrosis (CF), due to malabsorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
Newborns who do not receive routine vitamin K prophylaxis at birth are at greatest risk for vitamin
K–deficient bleeding. Lack of functioning coagulation factors predisposes to life-threatening
bleeding, such as intracranial hemorrhage and profuse bleeding from the gastrointestinal tract,
umbilicus, and surgical sites.
ICH is a potentially fatal complication and presents with signs of increased intracranial pressure (eg,
lethargy, enlarging head circumference, bulging fontanel, downward-driven eyes), seizures, etc.
📚 Source: Nelson Ch. 453; Osama Naga 2024 p. 612–614.
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Routine newborn care, Vit K deficiency bleeding (VKDB)
source: Nelson Ch. 453; Osama Naga 2024 p. 612–614.
topic: Routine newborn care, Vit K deficiency bleeding (VKDB)

stem:
What is the preventive dose of folic acid for a mother who had a previous child with neural tube
defect?

options.label: D
options.text: Depends on the mother’s folate level

explanation:
• For women with a prior pregnancy affected by a neural tube defect (NTD), the recommended
preventive dose is 4 mg/day of folic acid.
• This high-dose supplementation should start at least 1 month before conception and continue
through the first trimester.
• Folic acid doses in pregnancy:
o Normal pregnancy = 0.4-0.8 mg
o High risk pregnancy = 4 mg
 Hx of neural tube defects, seizure disorder
o SCD (sickle cell) = 5 mg
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 107; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 84–85.
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Neural Tube Defects
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 107; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 84–85.

stem:
While you are examining a newborn, you observe a bilateral hazy cornea, but the red reflex is
present. What is the next best step in management?
explanation:
• A hazy cornea in a newborn is an abnormal finding and can indicate serious causes such
as congenital glaucoma, corneal dystrophy, or intrauterine infection.
•
•
•
Even if the red reflex is present, bilateral corneal clouding requires urgent evaluation to
prevent permanent visual impairment.
Congenital glaucoma is the most common cause and must be ruled out urgently by an
ophthalmologist.
Urine for reducing substances is used to screen for galactosemia, but it is not the initial step in this
clinical setting unless systemic signs (e.g. hepatomegaly, vomiting) are also present.
Clinical pearls:
• Red reflex normal + hazy cornea = suspect anterior segment pathology
• Absent red reflex = suspect cataract or retinoblastoma
• Congenital glaucoma may also present with epiphora, photophobia, and enlarged globe
(buphthalmos)
How to examine fundal reflex: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LNXTLLoYNGg
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 176; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 255
☆ Ophthalmology & Newborn Screening: Corneal Opacities – Urgent Evaluation
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 176; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 255

stem:
A 5-month-old boy brought to ER with respiratory symptoms, found to have high ammonia with the
following lab results:
- Sodium: 137 meq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 meq/L
- Lactic acid: 0.5 mmol/L
- Glucose: 1.5 mmol/L
- Ketone bodies: Negative
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• High ammonia + hypoglycemia + no ketones = fatty acid oxidation defect (FAOD).
• These patients cannot generate energy from fat during catabolic states → leading to:
o Hypoketotic hypoglycemia
o Hyperammonemia
o Low/normal lactate
• Urea cycle defects cause hyperammonemia without hypoglycemia, and ketones are often
present.
• Organic acidemias often show elevated lactate, ketones, and metabolic acidosis.
• Mucopolysaccharidoses are chronic progressive disorders—not associated with acute metabolic
crises.
Clinical pearls:
• Classic FAOD: MCAD deficiency (medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase)
• Triggers: fasting, illness, prolonged exercise
• Diagnosis: acylcarnitine profile, urine organic acids
• Management: avoid fasting, frequent feeding, IV glucose during illness
•
Fatty acid oxidation disorders can cause dilated cardiomyopathy with non-ketotic
hypoglycemia — always screen metabolically
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 91–92; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 149
☆ Metabolic Disorders: Fatty Acid Oxidation Defects – Hypoketotic Hypoglycemia Pattern
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 91–92; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 149
topic: Fatty Acid Oxidation Defects – Hypoketotic Hypoglycemia Pattern

stem: Which of the following is true about creatinine?

options.label: B
options.text: It is the most sensitive marker of kidney function

options.label: C
options.text: It rises only when GFR is 30-40%

options.label: D
options.text: It does not depend on muscle mass

answer: C. It rises only when GFR is 30-40%
explanation:
• Serum creatinine levels typically do not rise until the glomerular filtration rate (GFR) drops
below 30–40% of normal, making it an insensitive early marker of renal dysfunction.
• Creatinine production is relatively constant, not variable, and depends heavily on muscle mass.
• It is not the most sensitive marker—cystatin C or creatinine clearance/GFR estimation is more
accurate in early renal impairment.
Clinical pearls:
• Creatinine is a late marker of kidney dysfunction—substantial nephron loss may occur before it
rises
• GFR equations (e.g., Schwartz formula in children) adjust for age, sex, and body size
o Formula: GFR (mL/min/1.73 m²) = (0.413 × height in cm) / serum creatinine (in mg/dL)
o Or alternatively: GFR (mL/min/1.73 m²) = (36 × height in cm) / serum creatinine (in μmol/L)
• Cystatin C is independent of muscle mass and gaining utility in pediatrics
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 544; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 322
☆ Nephrology: Kidney Function Markers – Creatinine
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 544; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 322

stem:
A 5-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a 4-day history of bloody diarrhea
followed by 1 day of red-colored urine. On examination, he appears pale and lethargic. What is
the next best step?
explanation:
• This presentation is highly suggestive of Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS), a common postdiarrheal complication in young children, typically due to Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (STEC).
• The first and most urgent test is a CBC to confirm microangiopathic hemolytic anemia and
thrombocytopenia, which are key diagnostic components.
o Also important to assess for anemia meeting transfusion parameters – due to mention of
pallor and lethargy.
• Once confirmed, proceed with BUN/creatinine to assess renal involvement.
• Stool culture is useful but does not guide acute management.
• Coagulation profile may be normal in HUS and is more relevant for DIC.
★ Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS):
• Most common cause of AKI in children in developed countries
• Classic triad:
•
•
•
•
•
o Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia
o Thrombocytopenia
o Acute kidney injury (↑ BUN, Cr)
Preceded by:
o Shiga-toxin-producing E. coli (STEC) infection (E. coli O157:H7)
o Bloody diarrhea 5–10 days prior to onset
Key lab findings:
o ↓ Hemoglobin, ↓ Platelets
o ↑ LDH, ↑ Bilirubin (hemolysis)
o ↑ BUN, ↑ Creatinine (AKI)
o Schistocytes on blood smear
o Normal PT/PTT (distinguishes from DIC)
o Urinalysis: Hematuria, proteinuria, RBC casts
Management:
o Supportive care is mainstay
o Careful fluid & electrolyte management
o Dialysis if needed (up to 50% require it temporarily)
o Avoid antibiotics and antimotility agents (can worsen toxin release)
o Do NOT give platelet transfusion unless there is life-threatening bleeding
Atypical HUS: not related to infection, may be complement-mediated → needs eculizumab
Prognosis: most recover fully, but some develop chronic kidney disease
Clinical pearls:
• Classic triad of HUS:
o Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia
o Thrombocytopenia
o Acute kidney injury
• Most common cause of AKI in children in developed countries
• Management is supportive: monitor fluid balance, blood pressure, and renal function
• Avoid antibiotics and antidiarrheals in suspected STEC
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 532–533; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 308
☆ Nephrology & Hematology: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 532–533; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 308

stem:
A 7-year-old girl presents to the ER with right knee pain and swelling. She had pharyngitis 1 week ago,
a positive throat culture for Group A Streptococcus, and was non-compliant with Augmentin. What
is the major criterion for this disease?
explanation:
• This case is classic for acute rheumatic fever following untreated streptococcal pharyngitis.
• The Revised Jones Criteria are used to diagnose rheumatic fever:
o Major criteria (J♡NES):
 Joints → Migratory arthritis
 ♡ → Carditis
 Nodules → Subcutaneous nodules
 Erythema marginatum
 Sydenham’s chorea
o Minor criteria:
 Fever, arthralgia, elevated ESR/CRP, prolonged PR interval
• Erythema marginatum is a non-pruritic, pink rash with central clearing – a major criterion.
o Painless and nonpruritic
o Location: The trunk and limbs are affected; the face is spared. May rapidly appear and
disappear at different locations.
•
•
Erythema nodosum is not a feature of rheumatic fever (seen in other conditions like IBD,
sarcoidosis).
Elevated ESR and positive streptococcal evidence are supportive, not major criteria.
★ Acute Rheumatic Fever:
• Autoimmune complication 2–4 weeks after GAS pharyngitis
• Pathophysiology:
o Autoimmune cross-reaction triggered by GAS pharyngitis
o Molecular mimicry: antibodies against GAS M protein crossreact with host tissues (heart, joints, CNS, skin)
o Leads to inflammatory lesions such as Aschoff bodies in the
heart and immune-mediated damage to tissues
• Requires evidence of recent streptococcal infection (positive
culture or elevated ASO)
• Diagnosis: 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor criteria + evidence of recent streptococcal infection
o Major Jones Criteria (JONES):
 Joints – Migratory arthritis (polyarthritis in 60 –75%)
 ♡ Carditis – Often mitral or aortic regurgitation
 Nodules – Subcutaneous over extensor surfaces
 Erythema marginatum – Pink, serpiginous, non-pruritic
evanescent/migratory rash with central clearing
 Sydenham chorea – Involuntary, purposeless movements
(video)
o Minor Criteria:
 Fever
 Arthralgia
 Prolonged PR interval
 Elevated ESR or CRP (↑ acute phase reactants)
o Note: erythema marginatum and subcutaneous nodules
are rare presentations, but are characteristic of ARF
• Treatment:
o Monthly IM benzathine penicillin G or daily oral penicillin
o Aspirin or NSAIDs for arthritis
o Steroids for carditis with CHF
o Valproic acid or carbamazepine for chorea
o
Secondary prophylaxis: 60% of patients with ARF progress to rheumatic heart disease with
the endocardium, pericardium, and myocardium all potentially involved. Thus, all patients
with ARF should receive antibiotics sufficient to treat the precipitating GAS infection.
 Without carditis: 5 years or until age 21 (whichever is longer)
 With carditis: 10 years or until age 21 (whichever is longer)
 With carditis + valve disease: 10 years or until age 40 (whichever is longer)
Clinical pearls:
• Rheumatic fever typically occurs 2–4 weeks after strep pharyngitis
• Migratory arthritis is the most common initial major manifestation
• Prompt treatment of strep pharyngitis with penicillin prevents rheumatic fever
• Long-term secondary prophylaxis is required to prevent recurrence and carditis
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 529–530; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 314
☆ Infectious Disease: Acute Rheumatic Fever
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 529–530; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 314
topic: • Autoimmune complication 2–4 weeks after GAS pharyngitis

stem:
A 5-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of swelling around the
eyes and ankles for the past 3 days. You note the patient to have pitting edema up to the knees
bilaterally and peri-orbital edema with no erythema. Laboratory investigations reveal the following:
- CBC: normal
- Albumin: 16 (low)
- Urine analysis:
o Leukocyte Esterase: -ve
o Nitrite: -ve
o Protein: ++++
What additional vaccine should this patient receive?
explanation:
• This presentation is classic for nephrotic syndrome (sudden edema + heavy proteinuria + bland
urine sediment), most commonly minimal change disease (MCD).
o MCNS is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (80–90%), especially
2–6 yrs.
o Presents with: sudden-onset edema, heavy proteinuria (>40 mg/m²/hr),
hypoalbuminemia.
o NO hematuria, HTN, or impaired renal function at onset.
o Typically responds rapidly to corticosteroids.
o Diagnosis often clinical; renal biopsy if atypical
• Vaccination against encapsulated organisms is important due to decreased immunity (↓
immunoglobulins with protein loss)
o In addition to routine PCV13, high-risk children (like those with nephrotic syndrome) should
receive the PPSV23 vaccine after age 2.
o Meningococcal vaccine is indicated in select immunocompromised states (e.g. asplenia),
but PPSV23 is specifically recommended for nephrotic patients.
Clinical Pearls:
• PPSV23 protects against additional pneumococcal serotypes not covered by PCV13
• Administer PPSV23 at age ≥2, at least 8 weeks after PCV13
• Booster dose of PPSV23 may be given after 5 years in persistently at-risk patients
• Ensure up-to-date influenza and varicella vaccines to prevent relapse triggers
📚 Source: Nelson, Nephrology Ch. 474; Osama Naga 2024 p. 523–525; UWorld.
☆ Nephrology: Nephrotic Syndrome
source: Nelson, Nephrology Ch. 474; Osama Naga 2024 p. 523–525; UWorld.

stem:
A 10-day-old neonate was admitted with fever and found to have a urinary tract infection (UTI) due to
E. coli. He completed 7 days of appropriate IV antibiotics (gentamicin + ampicillin), is now clinically
well, feeding, afebrile, and ready for discharge. What is the most appropriate oral antibiotic for
discharge?
explanation:
• Ceftriaxone is not given orally and is avoided in neonates <28 days due to the risk of bilirubin
displacement and kernicterus.
• Once the neonate has completed IV therapy and is improving, a step-down to oral therapy can be
done using an agent with good gram-negative coverage, such as amoxicillin-clavulanate.
• Azithromycin has poor coverage for gram-negative enteric organisms (like E. coli).
• Ciprofloxacin is generally avoided in neonates unless there are multidrug-resistant organisms.
Note: The initial answer choices mentioned “continue ceftriaxone for 10-14 days” however it is usually not
preferred in neonates <28 days especially if jaundiced or receiving calcium IV products → risk of biliary
sludging and kernicterus. The preferred alternative is usually cefotaxime.
Clinical pearls:
• Neonatal UTI is often due to E. coli.
• Avoid ceftriaxone in neonates <28 days due to risk of kernicterus.
• For UTI in neonates, IV therapy (ampicillin + gentamicin or cefotaxime) is first-line, followed
by oral Augmentin if improved.
• Always investigate for possible urinary tract anomalies with renal ultrasound after UTI in infants <2
months.
★ Neonatal Sepsis:
• Systemic illness associated with infectious source
• Overall incidence of 1-5 per 1,000 births
o In VLBW infants, rate of incidence of early sepsis is
2% and late sepsis is 25%
• Mortality rate is up to 25% (higher with prematurity)
• Types:
o Early Onset: within 1st 7 d (1st 3 d if hospitalized since birth)
 Risk factors: prematurity, LBW, PPROM, ROM ≥18 hrs, GBS +ve mother, maternal
fever, fetal distress, chorioamnionitis, meconium fluid, maternal or fetal
tachycardia, preterm labor
 Can use early onset sepsis risk score in ≥34 weeks to guide for NICU
admission/empiric Abx
 Most common: GBS (Step agalactiae), E.coli, Listeria, Staph, Strep pneumo,
Enterococcus, Haemophilus
o Late Onset: after 7 d of life
 Risk factors: indwelling catheters, intubation, prematurity, critical illness.
 Most common: CoNS, gram-negative rods, Staph aureus, GBS, fungal
• Clinical presentation: temp instability, irritability, lethargy, poor perfusion, feeding intolerance,
respiratory distress, dyspnea (e.g., expiratory grunting), apnea (more common in preterm infants)
o ↓ temp, perfusion, BG, BP = late signs
o pH (metabolic acidosis)
o ↑ RR/HR
o Specific symptoms:
 Neonatal meningitis
• Often no signs of meningism
• Early phase: general symptoms, vomiting

•
•
•
• Late phase: bulging fontanelles, shrill crying, seizures, stupor
Neonatal pneumonia
• Tachypnea with intercostal/sternal retractions and nasal flaring
• Reduced oxygen saturation with cyanosis
• Early: GBS, Late: Chlamydia
Diagnosis:
o Laboratory: blood culture ± UA/UCx ± LP
o Other studies CBC, CRP, abdomen Xray, CXR may be indicated depending on clinical
presentation.
Management:
o Empiric Abx: Ampicillin + Gentamycin, broaden if severe clinical illness, use local
resistance guidelines.
o Modify/stop Abx based on culture data.
Protective Factors:
o Adequate IAP (≥4 hours IV penicillin or ampicillin)
o Term gestation
o Asymptomatic neonate
o No rupture of membranes or fever
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 312–313; Red Book 2021–2024, p. 553
☆ Infectious Disease & Neonatology: Neonatal UTI
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 312–313; Red Book 2021–2024, p. 553
topic: • Systemic illness associated with infectious source

stem:
A 12-hour-old male newborn, otherwise healthy and well-appearing, is noted to have an ECG
showing wide P waves and upright T waves in the right precordial leads. What is the most likely
explanation?
explanation:
• In the first few days of life, it is normal for neonates to have:
o Upright T waves in right precordial leads (V1–V3)
o Right axis deviation
o Dominant R waves in V1
o Wide P waves due to immature atrial conduction
• This reflects the right ventricular dominance present in fetal
circulation, which gradually regresses over weeks as pulmonary vascular resistance falls.
Other choices:
• RBBB would show rsR' in V1 and delayed R peak time.
• Right heart failure would present with clinical signs (hepatomegaly, tachypnea).
• Complete heart block would show AV dissociation, bradycardia — not present here.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 172–173; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 144–145.
☆ Cardiology: Normal neonatal ECG
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 172–173; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 144–145.

stem:
A 7-year-old previously healthy boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents due
to high-grade fever, vomiting, and lethargy for the past 12 hours. His mother reports that he has
been less responsive and had difficulty concentrating before arrival. While in the ER, he develops
non-blanchable petechial rash over his lower limbs and trunk and becomes hypotensive with
delayed capillary refill. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• The scenario is typical for meningococcemia (caused by Neisseria meningitidis), a rapidly
progressing illness featuring:
o Acute fever and altered sensorium
o Petechial or purpuric rash that is non-blanchable
o Hypotension and early signs of shock
• A serious complication of meningococcemia is bilateral hemorrhagic infarction of the adrenal
glands, resulting in acute adrenal insufficiency (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome).
o Manifestations include hypoglycemia due to cortisol deficiency as well as hyponatremia
and hyperkalemia due to aldosterone deficiency.
o Loss of cortisol and aldosterone also contributes to worsening shock and can result in
rapid deterioration and death despite aggressive intervention.
Other options:
• Streptococcus pneumoniae may cause meningitis or bacteremia but rarely causes the
characteristic petechial rash
• Seizure disorder and brain abscess would not account for the acute rash or septic shock features
Clinical pearls:
• Meningococcemia requires urgent empiric antibiotics (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime) and supportive
care for shock
• Often complicated by DIC, adrenal insufficiency (Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome), and
multiorgan failure
• Prophylactic antibiotics (rifampin, ceftriaxone) are required for close contacts
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 838; Red Book 2021–2024, p. 548
☆ Infectious Disease: Meningococcemia
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 838; Red Book 2021–2024, p. 548

stem:
A 2-week-old full-term male neonate, exclusively breastfed, is brought to the emergency department
by his mother due to persistent greenish vomiting for the past 24 hours. The mother denies fever,
cough, or diarrhea. On examination, the infant appears irritable, with abdominal distention and knee
flexion posture. No bowel movements have been noted in the last 24 hours, and the abdomen is
tympanitic to percussion. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Bilious (green) vomiting in a neonate is a red flag for distal intestinal obstruction, such as from
malrotation with midgut volvulus, jejunoileal atresia, or internal hernias.
• The acute onset, abdominal distension, and irritability point toward volvulus, a surgical
emergency.
• Pyloric stenosis typically presents between 2–8 weeks of age with non-bilious projectile vomiting
and no distention.
• Hirschsprung disease is a differential but usually presents with delayed passage of meconium
and chronic constipation.
• Cystic fibrosis may present with meconium ileus, but this typically occurs in the first few days of
life, not at 2 weeks.
Clinical pearls:
• Bilious vomiting in neonates = surgical emergency until proven otherwise
• First step: abdominal X-ray, then upper GI contrast series if malrotation/volvulus is suspected
• Always consult pediatric surgery urgently
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 407–408; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p.
164; AMBOSS; Gomella’s Neonatology.
☆ Gastroenterology: Neonatal Bilious Vomiting
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 407–408; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p.

stem:
A 5-year-old boy with a known history of mild intermittent asthma is brought to the emergency
department with sudden-onset shortness of breath and wheezing that started while playing. His
mother denies recent viral illness or fever. On examination, he is tachypneic, with decreased air
entry and wheezing localized to the right lung. Chest X-ray is attached below.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Unilateral wheeze + asymmetric chest findings = air trapping from a foreign body. X-ray
showing hyperinflation supports this.
• Age: 1–3 years
• Signs: Sudden cough, unilateral wheeze, ↓ breath sounds
• Buzzword: “Right-sided hyperinflation”
• CXR: Unilateral hyperinflation (air trapping), Asymmetric inflation, shift of mediastinum, May
be normal (~30% of cases)
• Dx/Tx: Rigid bronchoscopy (gold standard); MC in right mainstem bronchus due to anatomy
• Emergency: Heimlich (child) / back blows (infant)
• No response to bronchodilators
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Pulmonology – p. 230; Nelson, Respiratory Emergencies
☆ Pulmonology: Airway Obstruction, Foreign Body Aspiration (FBA)
source: Osama Naga 2024, Pulmonology – p. 230; Nelson, Respiratory Emergencies

stem:
A 12-year-old child presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of upper respiratory
tract infection. While in the ER, he develops a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting 15 minutes.
He regains full consciousness afterward and has no focal neurologic deficits. What is the first
investigation to be performed?
explanation:
• In any child presenting with a new-onset seizure, especially one that
is prolonged, the first step is to rule out metabolic causes, including:
o Hypoglycemia
o Hyponatremia
o Hypocalcemia
o Other electrolyte imbalances
• These causes are common, easily reversible, and must be excluded before
proceeding to imaging or LP.
• Lumbar puncture is indicated if there are signs of meningitis or altered mental
status.
• EEG is not emergently indicated in the acute setting.
• CT brain is not first-line unless there are focal deficits, trauma, or signs of raised ICP.
Clinical pearls:
• Always check blood glucose and electrolytes immediately in any new-onset or prolonged
seizure
• Do not delay metabolic testing while preparing for more advanced investigations
• Febrile seizures are unlikely here given the prolonged duration and age >6 years
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1097–1098; Osama Naga Pediatric Review, p. 348
☆ Emergency Medicine: First-Line Workup of Seizures in Children
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1097–1098; Osama Naga Pediatric Review, p. 348
topic: First-Line Workup of Seizures in Children

stem:
A 7-year-old boy with a recent diagnosis of tonsillitis was started on trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
(Septra) by his primary physician. Two days later, he presents with jaundice, dark urine, and fatigue. On
evaluation, his hemoglobin is 8 g/dL, and his reticulocyte count is elevated. There is no history of
bleeding. What is the most likely cause of his symptoms?
explanation:
Sulfa-containing drugs such as Bactrim (TMP-SMX) trigger oxidative hemolysis in G6PDdeficient patients → acute drop in Hb, jaundice, reticulocytosis.
• This is a classic presentation of G6PD deficiency, a X-linked recessive disorder [M>F] that leads
to oxidative hemolysis after triggers like fava beans, infections, or certain drugs (e.g., sulfa,
antimalarials)
• G6PD is an X-linked enzymatic deficiency that renders red blood cells vulnerable to oxidative
stress (from drugs, infections, or fava beans).
• Jaundice, pallor, dark urine, and drop in hemoglobin following a trigger are key features.
• Peripheral smear shows Heinz bodies (denatured hemoglobin precipitates inside RBCs) and bite
cells (from splenic removal of Heinz bodies)
Clinical pearls:
• G6PD deficiency → episodic intravascular hemolysis, jaundice, hemoglobinuria
• Heinz bodies require supravital staining for visualization
• Common G6PD triggers include sulfa drugs, fava beans, antimalarials, and nitrofurantoin
• Avoid oxidant triggers, especially in high-risk populations (Mediterranean, African, Asian males)
• Confirm diagnosis with G6PD enzyme assay (note: may be falsely normal during acute hemolysis)
• Management includes supportive care, removal of trigger, and transfusion if indicated
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Hematology, p. 391; AMBOSS; UWorld
☆ Hematology: G6PD deficiency
source: Osama Naga 2024, Hematology, p. 391; AMBOSS; UWorld

stem:
A 4-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with lethargy, poor oral intake, vomiting,
and increased irritability during a hot summer week. His parents report he has been sweating
profusely, and he has a history of recurrent lower respiratory tract infections and foul-smelling,
greasy stools. On examination, he is dehydrated, tachycardic, and hypotensive with dry mucous
membranes. Laboratory findings:
- Sodium: 128 mmol/L
- Potassium: 2.9 mmol/L
- Chloride: 85 mmol/L
- Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻): 31 mmol/L
- pH: 7.48
What is the most likely diagnosis?
answer: C. Cystic fibrosis with pseudo-Bartter syndrome
explanation:
• The clinical picture suggests undiagnosed CF: recurrent respiratory infections, steatorrhea, failure
to thrive, and now electrolyte loss from sweat during heat → leading to hypochloremic,
hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis
• This pattern mimics Bartter syndrome but is caused by
sodium and chloride losses through sweat in CF →
termed Pseudo-Bartter syndrome
• Primary ciliary dyskinesia and IgA deficiency cause
recurrent infections, but not these electrolyte changes
• Renal tubular acidosis would cause acidosis, not alkalosis
Clinical pearls:
• Think of CF in any child with recurrent infections,
steatorrhea, and electrolyte disturbances in hot weather
• Confirm with sweat chloride test (>60 mmol/L) or CFTR genetic testing
• Acute management: IV fluids, NaCl and KCl replacement
• Long-term: enzyme replacement, airway clearance, and nutritional support
★ Cystic Fibrosis:
• Autosomal recessive, mutation in CFTR gene (Chr 7), most common = ΔF508.
• CFTR defect → impaired chloride transport → thick, dehydrated secretions.
• Newborns: suspect with meconium ileus (pathognomonic – bowel obstruction + ground-glass on
AXR).
• Pulmonary symptoms: chronic cough, recurrent pneumonia, bronchiectasis, digital clubbing.
• Early colonization: Staph aureus (infancy/early childhood).
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
Later colonization: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (midchildhood/adolescence), Burkholderia cepacia (severe prognosis).
GI: pancreatic insufficiency → steatorrhea, failure to thrive, ADEK
vitamin deficiency.
Intestinal: DIOS (Distal Intestinal Obstruction Syndrome) =
recurrent obstruction in older children.
Liver: focal biliary cirrhosis → portal hypertension.
Reproductive: azoospermia/infertility in males (absent vas
deferens), thick cervical mucus in females.
Electrolytes: hypochloremic, hyponatremic metabolic alkalosis
(esp. in hot weather).
Diagnosis:
o Sweat chloride ≥60 mmol/L (gold standard)
o 2 pathogenic CFTR mutations
o Nasal potential difference (if equivocal)
Treatment:
o Airway clearance: chest physiotherapy, hypertonic saline, dornase alfa (DNAse).
o Antibiotics:
 Early: anti-staphylococcal (e.g., cephalexin, TMP-SMX).
 Chronic Pseudomonas: inhaled tobramycin, aztreonam.
o CFTR modulators:
 Ivacaftor (G551D),
 Lumacaftor/Ivacaftor (ΔF508 homozygous),
 Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (Trikafta – ΔF508 hetero/homo).
o PERT + ADEK vitamins.
Complications: hemoptysis, pneumothorax, CF-related diabetes (CFRD), osteopenia.
Surveillance: annual OGTT (CFRD), DEXA (bone health), sputum cultures (track colonization), liver
US.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 551; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 190;
AMBOSS; UWorld.
☆ Pulmonology: Cystic Fibrosis – Electrolyte Loss and Pseudo-Bartter Syndrome
The following was added as a quick review of renal tubular disorders since they can be confusing.
Some helpful videos:
- RTAs (Dirty Medicine): https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RPi5aoGgjMQ
- Bartter, Gitelman, Liddle Syndromes: https://youtu.be/xtO8dPPuRaw?si=kJZUJf86RmYAjLIM
★ Renal Tubular Disorders:
Renal Tubular Acidosis (RTA):
• All types present with normal anion gap metabolic acidosis 2º to impaired renal excretion
• Mnemonic: “RTA = ABC” → Acidosis, Bicarbonate problem, Childhood growth failure
• Type 1 (Distal RTA) → Mnemonic: Type 1 = 1 stone (nephrocalcinosis)
o α-intercalated cells of the distal tubule are unable to
secrete H+ (apical) → ↓ intracellular production
of HCO3- → ↓ HCO3-/Cl- exchanger activity
(basolateral) → ↓ serum HCO3- → metabolic acidosis
o Urine pH > 5.5 despite systemic acidosis
o ↓ serum K⁺, nephrocalcinosis, and kidney stones
o Etiology: primary: sporadic, familial; Drugs:
ifosfamide, amphotericin B, lithium, NSAIDs
(analgesic nephropathy); sickle cell disease;
autoimmune (Sjögren, SLE)
o Treated with oral bicarbonate (1-3 ,Eq/lg/day)
o Refer for audiometry
• Type 2 (Proximal RTA)
→ Mnemonic: Type 2 = 2 losses (bicarb + K⁺)
o Defect in HCO₃⁻ reabsorption in the proximal tubule
o Can be isolated HCO₃⁻ reabsorption defect or
Fanconi syndrome: impaired reabsorption of HCO3and other compounds (potassium, glucose, phosphate, and amino acids)
o Urine pH initially >5.5, later becomes <5.5
o ↓ serum K⁺, may present with rickets
o Etiology: Fanconi syndrome (cystinosis), Wilson disease, Galactosemia, Type 1 GSD (VonGierke), Drugs: ifosfamide, tenofovir, expired tetracyclines, aminoglycosides, cisplatin
o Treated with alkali therapy (Na/K citrate) 5-15 mmol/kg/day
• Type 4 RTA (Hyperkalemic RTA)
→ Mnemonic: Type 4 = 4 letters = HIGH K⁺
o Due to aldosterone deficiency or resistance,
collecting duct
o ↑ serum K⁺, urine pH <5.5, mild metabolic acidosis
o Etiology: diabetic nephropathy, sickle cell disease, obstructive uropathy, adrenal
insufficiency, Drugs: K+ sparing diuretics, ACEi/ARBs, TMP/SMX, pentamidine, NSAIDs
o Treated with fludrocortisone (if deficient) or dietary K⁺ restriction
Other disorders:
• Bartter Syndrome
o Autosomal recessive inheritance
o Defect in Na⁺-K⁺-2Cl⁻ channel in the thick ascending limb (loop of Henle) → acts like a loop
diuretic (Lasix [furosemide])
 Leads to hypercalciuria
o ↓ K⁺, ↑ renin, ↑ aldosterone, normal BP, metabolic alkalosis
o Presents with polyuria, growth failure, nephrocalcinosis + renal rickets, sensorineural
deafness
o Treated with NSAIDs (indomethacin) and K⁺ supplements
• Gitelman Syndrome
o Autosomal recessive inheritance
Defect in Na⁺-Cl⁻ cotransporter in distal convoluted tubule
(DCT) → acts like a thiazide diuretic
o ↓ K⁺, ↓ Mg²⁺ (severe Mg wasting), ↓ urinary Ca²⁺, normal
BP, metabolic alkalosis
o Presents with fatigue, cramps, mild hypotension
o Treated with K⁺ and Mg²⁺ supplementation
Liddle Syndrome
o Autosomal dominant inheritance
o Increased ENaC activity in the collecting duct → ↑ Na⁺
reabsorption, ↓ K⁺, metabolic alkalosis
o ↓ renin, ↓ aldosterone, hypertension
o Treated with amiloride or triamterene (ENaC blockers)
o
•
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 737–740; Osama
Naga Pediatric Board Review, 3rd ed., p. 228–230; Uworld; AMBOSS.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 737–740; Osama
topic: • Autosomal recessive, mutation in CFTR gene (Chr 7), most common = ΔF508.

stem:
A mother of a 6-year-old boy with asthma asks whether her child will eventually outgrow the
condition. Which of the following is the most appropriate response?

options.label: A
options.text: It is difficult to predict the long-term outcome of asthma

options.label: B
options.text: About 20% of children completely outgrow asthma, while others continue to have symptoms

options.label: C
options.text: Most children with asthma will continue to have it into adulthood

options.label: D
options.text: Children with other allergic conditions have a 25–30% risk of developing asthma

answer: A. It is difficult to predict the long-term outcome of asthma
explanation:
• Asthma has a variable course and its resolution is unpredictable
• Some children, especially those with mild, early-onset asthma and no
atopy, may improve or remit during adolescence
• However, children with persistent symptoms, atopic background, or
family history are more likely to continue having asthma
• Studies suggest that up to 30–50% may experience improvement or
resolution during adolescence, but relapse can occur in adulthood
• Statements like “most will or won’t outgrow it” are not evidence-based
counseling
Clinical pearls:
• Early onset, non-atopic asthma has a better prognosis
• Allergic comorbidities (eczema, rhinitis, food allergy) increase persistence
• Educate families that asthma can be controlled, even if not cured
• Long-term monitoring is essential for growth, lung function, and medication needs
• Asthma may go into remission but can recur later, esp with viral or environmental exposures
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 735
☆ Pulmonology: Asthma
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 735

stem:
A 6-month-old infant is brought to the emergency department due to progressive muscle
weakness, poor head control, and feeding difficulties. On examination, the infant has hypotonia,
absent deep tendon reflexes, tongue fasciculations, and paradoxical breathing. He now shows
signs of respiratory failure. Family history is unremarkable. What is the most likely gene affected in
this condition?
answer: A. SMN1 → Spinal muscular atrophy (Type I – Werdnig-Hoffmann disease)
explanation:
Another potential scenario: 3mo infant lays in the "frog-leg" position, <5th% 2/2 feeding difficulties,
hypotonic, fasciculations of the tongue and absent DTRs.
Explanation:
• The clinical picture is classic for Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) type 1 (Werdnig-Hoffmann
disease)
• It presents in early infancy with symmetrical hypotonia, tongue fasciculations, areflexia,
and progressive respiratory failure
• Caused by homozygous deletion or mutation of the SMN1 gene (Survival Motor Neuron 1) on
chromosome 5q
• Dystrophin mutation (DMD gene) causes Duchenne muscular dystrophy, which presents later,
often after walking begins
• MECP2 is associated with Rett syndrome (affects girls, regression after 6 months)
• GAA mutation is linked to Pompe disease, which can present in infancy but with cardiomegaly and
glycogen storage findings
★ Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA):
•
•
•
•
•
Spinal muscular atrophy (Type I – Werdnig-Hoffmann disease) is the most severe and most
common autosomal recessive motor neuron disease in infancy.
Caused by SMN1 gene mutation → degeneration of anterior horn cells (peripheral motor neurons).
Presents before 6 months with:
o Severe hypotonia
o Symmetric proximal muscle weakness
o Tongue fasciculations
o Absent deep tendon reflexes
o Paradoxical breathing (due to diaphragm sparing)
o Progressive respiratory failure
Diagnosis:
o Genetic testing: homozygous deletion/mutation in SMN1 gene
o EMG: shows denervation
o Muscle biopsy: may show grouped atrophy (rarely needed now)
Treatment:
o Nusinersen (Spinraza) – antisense oligonucleotide that increases SMN2 production
o Gene therapy: Onasemnogene abeparvovec (Zolgensma)
o Supportive care: respiratory support, feeding, physical therapy
There are 5 types of SMA: Type I → non-sitters, type II → sitters, type III → walkers
Clinical pearls:
• SMA type 1 is the most severe form, typically fatal by age 2 if untreated
• Nusinersen, Zolgensma, and Risdiplam are disease-modifying therapies
• Early diagnosis via genetic testing is critical for treatment initiation
• Consider newborn screening in high-risk populations
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 544–545; Osama Naga, 3rd Ed., p. 466; AMBOSS
☆ Neurology: Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 544–545; Osama Naga, 3rd Ed., p. 466; AMBOSS

stem: Of the listed items below which is an absolute contraindication to breastfeeding:
explanation:
• Classic galactosemia is an absolute contraindication to breastfeeding due to the presence
of lactose (galactose + glucose) in breast milk
• Infants with galactosemia cannot metabolize galactose due to GALT enzyme deficiency, leading
to liver dysfunction, sepsis (especially E. coli), cataracts, and possible death if not identified and
managed
• These infants should be fed with soy-based formula (lactose-free)
Other options:
• Hepatitis B: Not a contraindication; breastfeeding is allowed if the infant receives HBIG and
hepatitis B vaccine within 12 hours of birth
• Mastitis: Not a contraindication; breastfeeding should continue to help drain the breast
• Breastfeeding jaundice: A benign condition due to insufficient intake in early days; breastfeeding
should be encouraged, not stopped
Clinical pearls:
• Only true absolute contraindications to breastfeeding:
o Classic galactosemia
o Untreated brucellosis
o Maternal HIV regardless of viral load (in resource-rich countries)
o Maternal HTLV-I/II infection
Active tuberculosis (temporarily, until treatment started) → Direct breastfeeding is
temporarily contraindicated in cases of active, untreated pulmonary TB because of the
risk of airborne transmission, not because of TB transmission via breast milk.
 Treated TB mothers can feed through expressed breast milk (EBM)
CMV and Hep C are not contraindications unless the mother is severely ill or has bleeding nipples.
Mastitis and candida infections are not contraindications; however maternal treatment is
necessary.
o
•
•
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 207; AAP Breastfeeding Guidelines
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Breastfeeding Contraindications
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 207; AAP Breastfeeding Guidelines

stem:
An exclusively breastfed infant presents with diarrhea, alopecia, and a periorificial, erythematous,
scaly rash involving the mouth, diaper area, and eyes. Which of the following substances is likely
deficient in this patient?
explanation:
• The presentation is classic for acrodermatitis enteropathica, a condition caused by zinc
deficiency, either inherited or acquired.
• Acrodermatitis enteropathica (inherited, AR – SLC39A4 mutation)
• Acquired deficiency:
o Prematurity
o Chronic diarrhea
o Malabsorption (e.g., IBD, celiac)
o Poor dietary intake or TPN without zinc supplementation
• Key findings:
o Chronic diarrhea
o Periorificial and acral dermatitis (perioral, perineal, and distal limbs)
o Alopecia, irritability, failure to thrive
• Serum zinc level is the definitive diagnostic test; confirmed by clinical response
to zinc supplementation
•
Treatment:
o Zinc supplementation: 1–3 mg/kg/day
elemental zinc
o Lifelong if inherited; temporary if acquired
Clinical pearls:
• Zinc deficiency = diarrhea + alopecia +
rash (especially mouth, anus, eyes)
• Acquired zinc deficiency seen in breastfed
infants, malnourished, or TPN-dependent patients
• Treat with oral zinc supplementation — rapid
resolution of symptoms
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 96
☆ General Pediatrics: Nutritional Deficiencies, Acrodermatitis Enteropathica
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 96

stem:
A 5-month-old infant is brought to the clinic for a routine visit. The mother expresses concern that the
baby’s weight has not changed since birth. On review of the growth chart, you confirm that the infant’s
current weight is equal to the recorded birth weight. What should be the expected weight pattern at
this age?
explanation:
• By 5 months of age, an infant is expected to have doubled their birth weight
• Birth weight triples by 12 months, and quadruples by 2 years
• Failure to gain weight as expected is a red flag for failure to thrive and requires evaluation for
nutritional, metabolic, or systemic causes
• If an infant is fed appropriately, anticipate weight gain 20-30g/day or 150-200g/week for the first
3-4 months → 15-20g/day or 105-140g/week for the rest of the 1st year.
Clinical pearls:
• Weight gain expectations:
o Double birth weight by 4–5 months
o Triple by 12 months
o Quadruple by 2 years
• Investigate for feeding difficulties, chronic illness,
malabsorption, or caregiver neglect in FTT
• Always plot growth parameters on WHO or CDC growth
charts to assess trends
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 49
☆ General Pediatrics: Infant Weight Milestones and Monitoring
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 49

stem:
A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with acute onset of difficulty swallowing,
drooling, a muffled "hot potato" voice, and right-sided neck swelling. He is febrile and irritable. On
examination, there is limited neck movement and mild trismus. No distinct cervical lymphadenopathy
is noted. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• The clinical features of dysphagia, drooling, muffled voice, unilateral neck swelling, fever, and
limited neck mobility strongly suggest retropharyngeal abscess, a deep neck space infection
common in young children
• Peak incidence: ages 2–4 years, due to abundance of retropharyngeal lymph nodes
• Often follows a recent upper respiratory infection
• May rapidly progress to airway obstruction or mediastinitis if not promptly treated
Other options:
• Parotitis presents with swelling near the jaw angle, typically more lateral and not associated with
drooling or dysphagia
• Cervical lymphadenitis causes tender nodes but usually doesn’t involve drooling, trismus, or
voice change
• SCM tumor (fibromatosis colli) is a congenital, non-infectious mass; not associated with fever or
acute symptoms
Clinical pearls:
• Order lateral neck X-ray (look for prevertebral space widening) or contrast-enhanced CT for
confirmation
• Start IV antibiotics (ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin ± vancomycin), and consider ENT
consultation for drainage if airway is compromised or abscess is large
• Infection within the retropharyngeal space drains inferiorly to the superior mediastinum.
o Spread to the carotid sheath can cause thrombosis of the internal jugular vein and deficits in
cranial nerves IX, X, XI, and XII.
o Extension through the alar fascia into the "danger space" (between the alar and prevertebral
fasciae) can rapidly transmit infection into the posterior mediastinum to the level of the
diaphragm.
o Acute necrotizing mediastinitis is a life-threatening complication characterized by fever,
chest pain, dyspnea, and odynophagia, and requires urgent surgical intervention.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 282
☆ Infectious Disease / ENT: Retropharyngeal Abscess
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 282

stem:
A 6-month-old infant is brought to the clinic by his mother due to a recent episode of apnea. She
reports that the baby had a runny nose, low-grade fever, and mild cough for a few days. This was
followed by severe bouts of paroxysmal coughing, during which the baby turns red, and occasionally
vomits or struggles to catch his breath. There is no fever currently, and examination is otherwise
unremarkable. What is the most appropriate treatment?
explanation:
• The presentation is classic for pertussis (whooping cough), particularly in young infants, who
may also present with apnea in the catarrhal or early paroxysmal phase
• Pertussis toxin is responsible for most of the systemic manifestations associated with whooping
cough (e.g., hypoglycemia, lymphocytosis, modulation of host immune response).
• Pertussis classically has three stages: catarrhal, paroxysmal, and convalescent.
o Symptoms may vary, however, based on age and immunization status; vaccinated
individuals tending to have a milder illness without characteristic whooping.
• The treatment of choice is a macrolide antibiotic, and azithromycin is preferred in infants <1
month to reduce the risk of pyloric stenosis seen with erythromycin
• Antibiotic treatment does not shorten the duration of symptoms once paroxysms begin but helps
prevent transmission
Coughing attack with tongue out in Pertussis:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S3oZrMGDMM
w&t=15s
Other options:
• Amoxicillin, ceftriaxone, cefixime are not effective against Bordetella pertussis and are not used
to treat pertussis
• They may be used in bacterial pneumonia or otitis media, but not in this clinical context
Clinical pearls:
• Suspect pertussis in infants <6 months with apnea and prolonged cough
• Cough increases gradually in the catarrhal stage → highly contagious
• PCR or culture of nasopharyngeal swab confirms diagnosis
• Ensure child is up to date with DTaP vaccine — pertussis is most severe before full immunization
• Post-exposure prophylaxis with azithromycin is indicated for household contacts
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 589–592; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 364
☆ Infectious Disease: Pertussis
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 589–592; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 364

stem:
A 7-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department due to recent-onset hyperactivity, fear of
water, and difficulty swallowing. His parents mention that he had sustained a superficial wound to
his left cheek from a dog bite one month ago while visiting a rural farm. The wound has healed
completely, but over the past few days he has become anxious, agitated, and refuses to eat or drink.
On exam, there is a healed scar on the left cheek, and the child becomes distressed when offered
water. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• This child presents with classic signs of encephalitic (furious) rabies, including hyperactivity,
hydrophobia, aerophobia, and neurologic deterioration following a bite wound from an animal
(especially in a rural setting)
• Rabies has an incubation period of 1–3 months, but it can be shorter in facial or highly
innervated wounds
• Rabies virus travels retrograde via peripheral nerves to the brainstem and limbic system,
causing neuronal dysfunction without inflammation. This leads to:
o Dysfunction of cranial nerves IX and X (glossopharyngeal and vagus nerves) → pharyngeal
and laryngeal spasm
o Exaggerated reflex excitability of the brainstem
• Hydrophobia and aerophobia are hallmark signs caused by pharyngeal spasms triggered by the
attempt to swallow.
o Hydrophobia: panic or spasms when attempting to drink water
o Hypersalivation, aerophobia: sensitivity to air currents
Other options:
• Brucellosis causes undulant fever, arthralgia, hepatosplenomegaly—not neurologic or
behavioral symptoms
• Malaria can cause altered mental status in severe cases, but is usually febrile with anemia or
thrombocytopenia
• Leishmaniasis can present with skin lesions (cutaneous) or fever, hepatosplenomegaly (visceral),
but not rabies-like symptoms
Clinical pearls:
• Rabies is almost always fatal once symptoms appear
• Ask about minor bite or scratch from dog/bat even weeks/months earlier → Animal bites to the
face require urgent evaluation for rabies prophylaxis due to shorter incubation
• Rabies virus travels retrograde via peripheral nerves to the CNS → encephalitis
• Hydrophobia = pathognomonic in rabies
• Infants and toddlers may present with feeding refusal, hyperactivity, or apnea
• Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) with rabies vaccine + rabies immunoglobulin must be
started before symptoms—once symptomatic, rabies is almost universally fatal
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 590; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1061
☆ Infectious Disease: Rabies
For any of you Dr. House fans, this was actually featured on an episode: https://youtu.be/OIrYioXX1xA?si=H6J36KHRaUMcsJ1J
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 590; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1061

stem:
A full-term 15-hour-old newborn is noted to be pale on routine exam. Lab results show hemoglobin 10
g/dL and serum bilirubin 42 mmol/L. The infant is clinically stable. Family history reveals the father
had a splenectomy in childhood for an unknown cause. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Hereditary spherocytosis is an autosomal dominant red cell membrane disorder caused by
defective binding of the red cell cytoskeleton to the plasma membrane due to mutations involving
ankyrin, band 3, or spectrin proteins
• Commonly presents in early neonatal period with anemia and indirect hyperbilirubinemia due
•
•
•
to hemolysis
Family history of splenectomy and cholelithiasis at a young age are key diagnostic clues
Neonates often develop jaundice (indirect hyperbilirubinemia)
within the 1st 24 hours of life
Diagnosis: peripheral smear (spherocytes), osmotic fragility
test, EMA-binding test
o Compared to normal red blood cells, spherocytes are smaller
and have a more intensely-staining cytoplasm due to membrane
loss and red cell dehydration.
o The red cell index most specific for spherocytosis is an elevation
in mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC).
Other options:
• ABO incompatibility: Causes early jaundice, not severe anemia; no link to family history of
splenectomy.
• G6PD deficiency: Hemolysis triggered by oxidative stress, not typically severe at birth unless
exposed in utero.
• Alpha-thalassemia trait: Mild microcytic anemia without significant jaundice or neonatal
symptoms; splenectomy not typical.
Clinical Pearls:
• These infants are at risk of gallstones and aplastic crisis later in life
• Splenectomy is often delayed until after age 5 to reduce sepsis risk
• Folic acid supplementation is recommended as patients are at risk for megaloblastic anemia.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 646; UWorld.
☆ Hematology: Hereditary Spherocytosis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 646; UWorld.

stem:
A 2-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department after she was found next to her mother’s
open bottle of prenatal vitamins. The bottle, originally containing 25 tablets, was empty. Four hours
post-ingestion, her serum iron level is 550 mcg/dL. She is currently vomiting and appears lethargic.
Which of the following should be administered?
explanation:
• Deferoxamine is the antidote of choice for iron poisoning, especially when serum iron levels >500
mcg/dL or if signs of systemic toxicity are present (e.g., vomiting, lethargy, metabolic acidosis).
• Iron overdose causes GI toxicity, shock, hepatic failure, and metabolic acidosis.
• Chelation is warranted at this level (550 mcg/dL).
Other choices:
• A. Dimercaprol (BAL): Used for arsenic, lead, and mercury poisoning, not iron.
• C. Edetate calcium disodium (EDTA): Indicated for lead poisoning.
• D. Ipecac: No longer recommended due to risk of aspiration and limited efficacy.
• E. Activated charcoal: Ineffective for iron, which is not bound by charcoal.
★ Iron Poisoning
• Toxic Dose: >60 mg/kg of elemental iron is potentially toxic
• Stages of Toxicity:
1. Stage I (0–6 hrs): GI symptoms – vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, hematemesis
•
•
2. Stage II (6–24 hrs): Apparent recovery
3. Stage III (12–48 hrs): Systemic toxicity – shock, metabolic acidosis, hepatic failure
4. Stage IV (2–5 days): Liver failure
5. Stage V (2–6 weeks): GI scarring, obstruction
Key Investigations:
o Serum iron level (peak at 4–6 hrs post-ingestion)
>500 mcg/dL = severe toxicity
o Abdominal X-ray for radiopaque tablets
o LFTs, ABG, glucose, coagulation panel
Management:
o Activated charcoal: Ineffective (iron not bound)
o Whole bowel irrigation if large pill burden
o IV Deferoxamine if:
 Serum iron >500 mcg/dL
 OR patient is symptomatic (shock, acidosis, lethargy) regardless of level
 Deferoxamine clue: Urine turns pink ("vin rose") = due to ferrioxamine
Clinical Pearls:
• Activated charcoal does not bind iron, and is ineffective
• First symptom: nausea and vomiting
• Tx; whole bowel irrigation and deferoxamine
• Toddler + prenatal vitamins → Suspect toxicity
• >60 mg/kg ingested → Toxic dose
• <1 hr post-ingestion → Consider whole bowel irrigation
• 4–6 hrs post-ingestion → Check serum iron level
• Symptomatic + no level yet → Start deferoxamine
• Shock + normal iron level → Still give deferoxamine
• >500 mcg/dL serum iron → Chelation indicated
• GI symptoms resolve (stage II), then shock → Stage III toxicity
• Abdominal X-ray: radiopaque tablets → Confirm ingestion
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 156
☆ Toxicology: Iron Poisoning
Note: The following question is likely poorly collected; I answered with specific mention of “target rash” in mind.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 156

stem:
A young couple bring their 30-month-old infant son to their pediatrician because he has not learned
to speak, does not interact or play with other children, does not display eye contact and repeatedly flaps
his hands. Physical examination is normal. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Autism spectrum disorder is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by impaired
social interaction and communication, repetitive and stereotyped patterns of behavior, and uneven
intellectual development often with mental retardation.
• Symptoms begin in early childhood.
• The cause in most children is unknown, although
evidence supports a genetic component; in some,
autism may be caused by a medical condition.
• Diagnosis is based on developmental history and
observation.
• Autism feeding difficulties are common;
involve sensory sensitivity
• Treatment consists of behavioral management and
sometimes drug therapy.
• Key Developmental Clues:
Board Buzzwords for Autism:
o No babbling by 12 months
- No response to name
- Limited joint attention
o No pointing or single words by 16 months
- Echoing speech (echolalia)
o No 2-word phrases by 24 months
- Sensory sensitivity
o Regression in speech or social skills at any age
(e.g., avoids textures/sounds)
``
o Lack of pretend play
• Screening & Diagnosis:
o Screen all children at 18 & 24 months (AAP guidelines)
o Tool: M-CHAT-R/F (Modified Checklist for Autism in Toddlers)
o Gold standard Dx: ADIR / ADOS (by developmental pediatrician or psychologist)
• Management:
o Early behavioral therapy (ABA) = most effective
o Speech & occupational therapy
o Risperidone / aripiprazole → for severe aggression/irritability (USFDA approved)
📚 Source: AAP PREP 2024; Canadian Qbank (QID 804); UWorld
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: Autism
source: AAP PREP 2024; Canadian Qbank (QID 804); UWorld

stem:
A newborn is born to a mother with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) and is noted to have
bradycardia. ECG confirms complete heart block. Which of the following statements is true regarding
this condition?

options.label: C
options.text: It occurs because of maternal anti dsDNA autoantibodies

explanation:
• Congenital complete heart block in neonates of mothers with SLE is due to transplacental
passage of maternal anti-Ro (SSA) and anti-La (SSB) antibodies, not anti-dsDNA.
• These autoantibodies damage the fetal conduction system, causing irreversible fibrosis of the AV
node, leading to complete heart block.
• It is not transient — the block is permanent.
• Many affected neonates require a pacemaker, especially if symptomatic or with very low heart
rates (<55 bpm).
• The condition carries significant morbidity and mortality, especially with hydrops or associated
structural heart disease.
★ Neonatal Lupus:
• Cause: Transplacental passage of maternal anti-Ro (SSA) or anti-La (SSB) antibodies
• Most common serious complication: Congenital complete heart block (CCHB)
• ECG: Complete AV block (bradycardia, AV dissociation)
• CCHB is irreversible → majority require permanent pacemaker if HR <55 bpm or symptomatic
• Cutaneous form: Annular erythematous rash (face, scalp), appears in first few weeks → resolves by
6 months
•
•
•
•
•
Other findings: Transient thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, anemia, elevated LFTs
Not caused by anti-dsDNA (common board trap)
Detected antenatally via fetal ECHO starting at 18–20 weeks if mother is Ab-positive
Management:
o CCHB: Monitor HR, pace if symptomatic
o Skin and blood findings: Supportive care only
Recurrence risk: ~15–20% in future pregnancies if mother is anti-Ro/La positive
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 496–497; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 391.
☆ Cardiology: Congenital heart block; Neonatal SLE
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 496–497; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 391.
topic: • Cause: Transplacental passage of maternal anti-Ro (SSA) or anti-La (SSB) antibodies

stem:
A 3 days old term infant is noted to be jaundiced. Physical examination is otherwise normal.
Laboratory values: Hb 17 g/dL; reticulocytes 1.0%; total serum bilirubin 14 mmol/l, direct bilirubin 3
mmol/l. The baby’s blood group is O+ and the mother is A+. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Physiologic jaundice typically peaks between days 2–4 of life in healthy term neonates.
While the direct bilirubin appears to be slightly >20% of the total, the absolute level is low (0.18 mg/dL
out of 0.82 mg/dL) and such mild elevations are common, especially early on.
•
•
•
•
Onset: >24 h, peaks 3–5 days, resolves <1 week.
Labs: isolated indirect hyperbilirubinemia, no hemolysis or sepsis.
Direct bili <10% of total = physiologic.
If direct bili >20% of total → always investigate for cholestasis.
Clinical pearls:
• Physiologic jaundice is due to increased bilirubin production and immature hepatic conjugation.
• Direct (conjugated) hyperbilirubinemia is defined as:
o >1 mg/dL when total bilirubin >5 mg/dL
o >20% of total if TB ≤5 mg/dL
• ABO hemolytic disease can increase indirect bilirubin even without anemia.
• Mild transient elevation of the direct fraction can be seen early in life.
• Jaundice in the first 24 hours is always pathologic and needs urgent evaluation.
📚 Source: Nelson Neonatology Ch. 99; Osama Naga 2024 p. 237–238.
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Neonatal Jaundice
source: Nelson Neonatology Ch. 99; Osama Naga 2024 p. 237–238.

stem:
A 10-day-old term infant is brought to the emergency department for petechiae. He is wellappearing, feeding well, with an unremarkable physical examination (no dysmorphic features;
thumbs and radii are present). Laboratory findings show:
- WBC = 9 × 10⁹/L
- Hemoglobin = 18 g/dL
- Platelets = 10,000/μL (confirmed by manual smear, showing large platelets)
What is the most appropriate next step?
explanation:
This infant presents with isolated severe thrombocytopenia and large platelets,
indicating peripheral destruction rather than marrow failure.
• The most likely diagnosis is Neonatal Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia (NAIT) — analogous to Rh
disease but affecting platelet antigens. Maternal antibodies (most commonly against HPA-1a)
cross the placenta and destroy fetal platelets.
• First-line step: Check the mother’s platelet count. If the mother has a normal count, it strongly
supports NAIT.
• PT/PTT are not helpful in isolated thrombocytopenia unless coagulopathy is suspected.
• IVIG is initiated after diagnosis or if there's active bleeding or critical platelet drop.
• Bone marrow biopsy is not warranted unless marrow failure syndromes are suspected (e.g.,
Fanconi, TAR).
Clinical pearls:
• NAIT typically presents in the first week of life with petechiae, purpura, or intracranial hemorrhage.
•
•
•
•
Platelets are large on smear, differentiating from marrow failure.
TAR syndrome presents with thrombocytopenia + bilateral radial aplasia (thumbs present in NAIT).
Treatment: IVIG ± antigen-negative platelet transfusion.
In future pregnancies, antenatal management is required to prevent recurrence.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2063–2065; UpToDate
☆ Hematology/Neonatology: Neonatal Thrombocytopenia and NAIT
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2063–2065; UpToDate

stem:
A 5-year-old child presents with progressive pallor and hepatosplenomegaly since birth. There is
no history of acute illness or bleeding. Physical exam confirms pallor and significant
hepatosplenomegaly. What is the best diagnostic test?
explanation:
The presentation of chronic pallor and
hepatosplenomegaly since infancy strongly suggests a chronic
hemolytic anemia, most notably a hemoglobinopathy such as βthalassemia major or HbE disease.
• Hb electrophoresis is the definitive diagnostic test to
detect abnormal hemoglobin variants and quantify HbA,
HbF, and HbA2.
• Peripheral smear may show target cells or nucleated RBCs
but is not diagnostic.
• Bone marrow biopsy is invasive and not first-line unless
malignancy or marrow failure is suspected.
• Serum ferritin is helpful for iron overload monitoring, especially in transfused patients, but not
diagnostic.
Clinical pearls:
• Hemoglobin chains → 4 types in different combinations: ⍺, β, ɣ, δ → all Hb chains have ⍺ globin, the
other chain determines the type
o alpha thalassemia = ⍺-globin, beta thalassemia = β-globin
o HbF (Fetal) → ⍺⍺-ɣɣ → ɣ chain synthesis is only predominant in utero
o HbA (Adult, >95%) → ⍺⍺-ββ → β chain synthesis ↑↑ at 3-6 months (switch to HbA)
o HbA2 (Adult, <5%) → ⍺⍺-δδ
• Diagnosis via Hb electrophoresis → done for microcytic anemia + no iron deficiency
• In β-thalassemia major, Hb electrophoresis typically shows absent HbA with elevated HbF and
HbA2
• Splenomegaly in these children is due to extramedullary
hematopoiesis and increased red cell destruction.
• Diagnosis is typically established in infancy or early childhood.
• Early transfusion dependency is a hallmark of major thalassemias.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2436–2440; UWorld; UpToDate; Maryam AlTayeb Med
School Notes: Anemias & Hemoglobinopathies
☆ Hematology: Hemoglobinopathies and Thalassemia
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2436–2440; UWorld; UpToDate; Maryam AlTayeb Med

stem:
What is the key difference between the pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13) and the
pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine (PPSV23)?

options.label: C
options.text: Given with MMR as one injection

options.label: D
options.text: Available in oral form which is less costly

explanation:
• Pneumococcal conjugate vaccine (PCV13) is conjugated to a protein carrier, which induces a Tcell dependent immune response, making it more immunogenic, especially in infants and young
children under 2 years of age.
• PPSV23 is a polysaccharide vaccine that does not induce memory and is less effective in young
children because it elicits a T-cell independent response.
• PCV13 is given in multiple doses in infancy (not a single dose).
• It is not combined with MMR.
• Neither vaccine is oral; both are injectable.
Clinical pearls:
• PCV13 is part of the routine immunization schedule in infancy.
o Age: 2-4-6-12 months of age.
• PPSV23 is given to older children and adults with certain risk factors (e.g., asplenia,
immunocompromised).
o Starts at age of 2 years.
o Repeat dose after 3-5 years.
o Indications:
 Sickle cell disease.
 Asplenia.
 Splenic dysfunction.
 Cochlear implant.
 HIV.
 Immunodeficiency.
 Chronic diseases (Lung - Kidney - Cardiac).
 Diabetes mellitus.
• Conjugate vaccines are essential for effective vaccination in children <2 years due to their
immature immune systems.
• PCV13 protects against 13 serotypes; PPSV23 covers 23 serotypes, but with less durable
immunity in young children.
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 553–561; Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1456
☆ Infectious Diseases: Immunizations and Vaccines
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 553–561; Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1456

stem:
A 28-week preterm infant, now 3 weeks old, is receiving 100 kcal/kg/day from preterm formula
(24 kcal/30 mL) and is on diuretics and caffeine for PDA. His weight gain is only 5 g/day.
What is the most likely cause of this poor weight gain?

options.label: B
options.text: Preterm has twice metabolic rate compared to term

options.label: C
options.text: Diuretic losing glucose and protein in the urine

explanation:
• Preterm infants typically require 110–130 kcal/kg/day to support adequate growth (15–20
g/kg/day).
• This infant is only receiving 100 kcal/kg/day, which is suboptimal, especially in the setting
of increased energy expenditure (due to caffeine, diuretics, and cardiorespiratory demands).
• Option B is incorrect: Preterms have higher energy needs, but not exactly twice the metabolic
rate of term infants.
•
•
Option C is incorrect: Diuretics primarily cause electrolyte loss, not significant glucose or
proteinuria unless associated with renal disease.
Option D is a contributing factor but not the primary cause here. Even with increased caloric need
due to cardiorespiratory stress, the caloric intake remains insufficient.
Clinical pearls:
• Expected weight gain for preterm infants is 15–20 g/kg/day.
• Caloric requirement:
o Preterm: 110–130 kcal/kg/day
o Term: 90–110 kcal/kg/day
• 24 kcal/oz preterm formula provides 0.8-1.0 kcal/mL. Ensure total fluid volume supports the
necessary caloric intake.
• Medications like diuretics and caffeine may increase energy expenditure.
• Always reassess calorie intake, fluid status, and comorbidities in poor weight gain.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 837–839
☆ Neonatology: Nutrition in Preterm Infants
The following was repeated in this exam twice (Q53)
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 837–839

stem:
A baby presents to the ER with irritability, scattered bruises, and bluish discoloration noted
behind the left ear and on the right side of the chest.
What is the most appropriate first step in management?

options.label: B
options.text: Inform child protective services and discharge

explanation:
This presentation raises strong suspicion of non-accidental trauma (NAT). Key findings
like postauricular ecchymosis (Battle sign) and chest wall bruising are concerning.
• A skeletal survey is the initial diagnostic tool for children under 2 years of age in suspected
abuse to detect occult fractures.
• In suspected child abuse, always begin with:
o Skeletal survey (if <2 years)
o Head CT if neurologic symptoms or scalp bruising
o Dilated eye exam for retinal hemorrhages
o Labs: CBC, coagulation profile, liver enzymes, amylase/lipase
• HOWEVER, if the options included CT → due to postauricular ecchymosis (Battle sign)
indicating basilar skull fracture → urgent CT brain to rule out intracranial hemorrhage or injury.
o In this case, skeletal survey is still essential, but after ruling out life-threatening CNS
injury.
• In infants under 1 year of age, any skull fracture, particularly without an adequate trauma
history, is highly concerning for non-accidental trauma (NAT)
• Linear skull fractures may occur accidentally, but in a non-ambulatory infant with no history
of trauma, this mandates a full workup for abuse:
o Skeletal survey, fundoscopic exam, coagulation profile, report to child protective
services (CPS)
o Admitting the child for further evaluation is common in such cases
• Simply educating the parent or discharging is inappropriate and unsafe at this stage
Clinical pearls:
• NAT red flags: young age, inconsistent history, skull fractures, retinal hemorrhages, multiple
fractures of different ages
• In all infants with suspected non-accidental trauma, once stabilized, also do:
o Skeletal survey (if <2 years)
o Head CT if neurologic symptoms or scalp bruising → Battle sign
o Fundoscopy
o Coagulation profile to rule out bleeding diathesis
• Early recognition and reporting are essential to protect the child

stem:
A 2-year-old boy is brought to the ER with barking cough, inspiratory stridor, and hoarseness. His
symptoms started with a mild URTI 2 days ago. He is afebrile, has no drooling, and is in mild respiratory
distress with oxygen saturation of 96% on room air. What is the most appropriate initial
management?
answer: C. Oral dexamethasone and nebulized epinephrine
explanation:
• This is a classic case of viral croup (laryngotracheitis), usually caused by parainfluenza virus.
• Mild to moderate croup (no hypoxia, no marked retractions) is managed with:
o Oral dexamethasone: 0.6 mg/kg once
o Nebulized epinephrine (Racemic or L-epinephrine) for moderate to severe cases:
 0.5 mL of racemic epinephrine 2.25% diluted in 2.5 mL NS via nebulizer
 Or: L-epinephrine 1:1000 at 0.5 mL/kg (max 5 mL) via nebulizer
• Amoxicillin or ceftriaxone are not indicated unless bacterial superinfection is suspected.
• Salbutamol is not helpful in croup (upper airway obstruction).
• IV dexamethasone reserved only if oral route is not possible.
Clinical Pearls:
• Croup peak age: 6 months to 3 years
• Classic triad: barking cough, inspiratory stridor, hoarseness
• X-ray may reveal subglottic narrowing (ie, steeple sign).
• Steroids reduce airway edema and hospital stay
• Epinephrine improves symptoms within 30 minutes, but effect lasts ~2 hours → observe for 2–
3 hours post-nebulization
• Always assess for signs of impending respiratory failure: fatigue, cyanosis, decreased air entry
