📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2085–2086; UpToDate
☆ General Pediatrics: Respiratory Infections and Airway Management
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2085–2086; UpToDate

stem:
A boy can hop on one foot, dress himself, draw a cross, and tell stories to strangers.
What is his developmental age?
explanation:
• Hops on one foot → ~4 years
• Dresses self, cross drawing, tells stories all point to 4-yearold milestones
• 5 years = triangle drawing, skips, knows colors and alphabet
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Development Table p. 138; Nelson
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: Developmental Milestones
source: Osama Naga 2024, Development Table p. 138; Nelson

stem:
A full-term male neonate, born via vaginal delivery after prolonged second stage of labor, is noted
on day 2 of life to have a firm, well-circumscribed swelling over the right parietal region of the scalp.
The swelling is non-tender, does not cross suture lines, and there are no skin changes or
discoloration. The infant is feeding well and has stable vitals. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Cephalohematoma is a subperiosteal hemorrhage, limited by cranial sutures, most
commonly over the parietal bones. It presents as a firm, non-fluctuant swelling that does not
cross suture lines, and no overlying skin changes are present.
•
•
•
Caput succedaneum is a diffuse, edematous swelling of the
scalp that crosses suture lines, is softer, and often associated
with skin ecchymosis.
Subgaleal hemorrhage is more extensive, crosses sutures and
fontanelles, and is fluctuant. It is a medical emergency due to
risk of massive blood loss.
Epidural hematoma is intracranial, typically following trauma. It
does not present as a visible scalp swelling.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 831–833
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Birth trauma, Cranial hematomas
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 831–833

stem:
A 10-year-old child presents with a 3-month history of pulsating headaches, localized mainly in
the frontal region, occurring approximately every 3–4 weeks. The headaches are sometimes
associated with nausea and photophobia, and improve after rest. Neurological examination is normal.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• The described features — recurrent, pulsating, frontal headaches with nausea and
photophobia, and normal neuro exam — are classic for migraine without aura in children.
o Migraines are often bilateral in children, unlike adults.
o The frequency (~every 3–4 weeks), severity, and associated symptoms support the
diagnosis.
Other options:
• Tension-type headaches are typically dull, bilateral, non-pulsating, and lack associated
symptoms like nausea or photophobia.
• Cluster headaches are rare in children and involve severe unilateral periorbital pain, often
with autonomic symptoms (tearing, rhinorrhea).
• Sinusitis causes daily dull frontal pain, worsens with leaning forward, and is typically associated
with nasal congestion/discharge.
Clinical pearls:
• Pediatric migraines may be bilateral and shorter than adult migraines
• Diagnosis is clinical, based on ICHD criteria
• Rule out secondary causes with imaging only if red flags are present
📚 Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 798–800
☆ Neurology / Adolescent Medicine: Migraine Headache

stem:
A 6-month-old infant is brought to clinic for evaluation of poor feeding. On further questioning, the
mother reports the baby has no fever, but often sweats during feeding and seems to tire quickly,
although he eats normally otherwise. Which of the following historical features is most suggestive
of congenital heart disease (CHD)?
explanation:
• Diaphoresis with feeding is a classic symptom of CHF in infants, which may result
from congenital heart disease(especially left-to-right shunts like VSD, PDA, AV canal).
• Sweating occurs due to increased sympathetic stimulation caused by high metabolic demand and
poor cardiac output during feeding.
• Fever suggests an infectious process, not CHD.
• Cough may suggest respiratory infection or aspiration, but not specific to CHD.
• Sneezing is nonspecific and more often a benign symptom in infants.
Clinical Pearls:
• CHD in infants may present with:
o Tachypnea, poor weight gain, diaphoresis with feeds
o Recurrent respiratory infections
o Cyanosis (for cyanotic CHDs)
• Always investigate feeding patterns and growth in suspected CHD.
• Further workup: CXR, ECG, and Echocardiogram.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2224–2226
☆ Cardiology: Congenital Heart Disease in Infants
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2224–2226

stem:
A 14-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with acute lower abdominal pain,
described as 8/10 in severity, associated with nausea and vomiting. She had participated in vigorous
physical activity the day prior but reports no trauma. On examination, her temperature is 38.3°C,
heart rate is 120 bpm, and blood pressure is normal. An urgent pelvic ultrasound reveals an
abnormally positioned ovary with decreased Doppler blood flow. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Ovarian torsion typically presents with sudden, severe, unilateral lower abdominal pain, often
associated with nausea, vomiting, and sometimes fever or leukocytosis.
• The key diagnostic feature is decreased or absent blood flow on Doppler ultrasound, along with
an abnormally located ovary.
• Torsion occurs due to twisting of the ovary on its ligamentous supports, often in the presence of an
ovarian mass or cyst.
• Ruptured ovarian cyst: Can cause acute pain but Doppler flow is usually normal; typically occurs
mid-cycle.
• PID: Usually has bilateral lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, and vaginal
discharge; not associated with abnormal ovary location or Doppler findings.
• Appendicitis: Presents with RLQ pain, often with rebound tenderness, but does not involve ovarian
position or Doppler flow.
Clinical pearls:
• Ovarian torsion is a gynecologic emergency and requires prompt surgical intervention to preserve
ovarian function.
• It is most common in adolescent girls and women of reproductive age.
• Intermittent torsion-detorsion may cause waxing and waning symptoms, making diagnosis
difficult.
• Doppler ultrasound may sometimes be normal early in torsion, so clinical suspicion should guide
decisions.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2122–2123
☆ Emergency Medicine: Ovarian Emergencies, Ovarian torsion
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2122–2123

stem:
A 6-year-old child is found to have a previously undiagnosed atrial septal defect (ASD) during
evaluation for a murmur. The child is currently asymptomatic and thriving well. What is the most likely
long-term complication if the defect is left unrepaired into adulthood?
explanation:
• ASD (most commonly secundum type) leads to left-to-right shunt due to higher pressure in the left
atrium.
• Over decades, this results in chronic increased pulmonary blood flow, which can lead
•
•
•
•
to pulmonary hypertension.
As pulmonary pressures exceed systemic pressures, the shunt reverses to
right-to-left, resulting in Eisenmenger syndrome, with cyanosis and clubbing.
Shunting in adulthood is typically left-to-right, but Eisenmenger physiology is
the feared long-term consequence.
Cyanosis during infancy is rare with isolated ASD unless there's another cyanotic
defect.
ASD volume overload mainly affects the right heart, leading to right atrial and
ventricular enlargement, not LVH.
Clinical Pearls:
• Wide fixed splitting of S2 is the hallmark auscultatory finding in ASD.
• Closure is generally recommended for moderate to large ASDs even in asymptomatic children to
prevent pulmonary vascular disease.
• Repair is usually done between ages 2–5 years to prevent long-term complications.
• Paradoxical embolism is a rare but serious complication in adults with unrepaired ASD.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2314–2316; UWorld.
☆ Cardiology: Atrial Septal Defect (ASD), Eisenmenger syndrome
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2314–2316; UWorld.

stem:
A 6-year-old boy presents for a routine check-up. He is asymptomatic, active, and growing well. On
examination, a grade II mid-systolic murmur is heard best at the left lower sternal border. The
murmur is vibratory in quality, does not radiate, and decreases when the child stands up. There are
no clicks or thrills, and the child tolerates exercise normally. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Still’s murmur is the most common innocent murmur of childhood.
• It is mid-systolic, low-grade (I–II/VI), vibratory or musical, and best heard at the left lower sternal
border.
• It is position dependent (decreases with standing or Valsalva).
• Child is otherwise asymptomatic, with normal growth, exercise tolerance, and no cardiac
findings.
• Aortic stenosis: Typically heard at the right upper sternal border, often radiates to the neck,
louder, and can be associated with clicks or reduced exercise tolerance.
• Mitral valve prolapse: Classically has a mid-systolic click, best heard at the apex, and may be
associated with mitral regurgitation murmur. Can be seen in Marfan syndrome, anorexia nervosa.
• Pulmonary stenosis: Murmur is best heard at the left upper sternal border and may have an
ejection click. Associated with noonan syndrome.
Clinical Pearls:
• Innocent murmurs (like Still’s) are soft, systolic, position-dependent, and heard in an otherwise
well child. Video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1vWk7SCERmE
• Most pathologic murmurs are loud (grade ≥ III), diastolic, harsh, associated with clicks/thrills,
or associated with symptoms (e.g., poor feeding, cyanosis, fatigue).
• No further cardiac workup is needed for classic Still’s murmur in a well child.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2300–2301; UWorld.
☆ Cardiology: Innocent Murmurs
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2300–2301; UWorld.

stem:
A 2-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department due to a generalized tonic-clonic
seizure. Her parents report a history of delayed developmental milestones and increased irritability
over the past few months. Her physical examination finding is shown below. Ophthalmologic
examination reveals increased intraocular pressure in the ipsilateral eye. A CT scan of the brain
shows cortical calcifications in the parieto-occipital region.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Sturge-Weber syndrome is a neurocutaneous disorder characterized by:
o Facial port-wine stain (nevus flammeus) in the V1 distribution
o Leptomeningeal angiomas causing seizures, stroke-like episodes, developmental delay
o Tram-track intracranial calcifications seen on CT scan
o May have glaucoma in the ipsilateral eye
•
•
Port-wine stain in the V1 distribution mandates neuroimaging to rule out Sturge-Weber
Klippel-Trénaunay syndrome: Port-wine stains + limb hypertrophy + venous malformations (no
CNS involvement)
★ Neurocutaneous Disorders:
Sturge-Weber Syndrome:
• Sporadic, somatic GNAQ mutation ≠ familial/inherited
• Findings: Facial port-wine stain (CN V1 distribution), leptomeningeal angiomas, seizures
o Complications include glaucoma, stroke-like episodes, and hemiparesis
o Seizures and developmental delay often develop within the first 2 years of life
• Imaging: Contrast-enhanced MRI to detect leptomeningeal angiomas; CT may show classic tramtrack calcifications
• Management: Antiepileptic drugs, monitor for glaucoma, physical therapy, laser therapy for skin
lesions
Neurofibromatosis:
Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1)
- Gene: NF1, chromosome 17
- Key findings: ≥6 café-au-lait spots,
axillary/inguinal freckling, Lisch nodules
(iris hamartomas), neurofibromas
- Associations: Optic gliomas, learning
disabilities, bony dysplasia
- Inheritance: Autosomal dominant
- Mnemonic: Café SPOTLITE — Spots,
Plexiform, Optic glioma, Tibial dysplasia,
Lisch, Inheritance (AD), Tumors, Eye
Neurofibromatosis type 2
(NF2)
- Gene: NF2, chromosome
- Key findings: Bilateral
vestibular schwannomas,
hearing loss, meningiomas
- Associations: Cataracts
- Mnemonic: 2 ears, 2 eyes,
2 tumors (bilateral)
Tuberous Sclerosis:
• Genes: TSC1 (hamartin), TSC2 (tuberin)
• Skin: Hypopigmented “ash-leaf” spots, shagreen patches, facial angiofibromas
• Neuro: Seizures, intellectual disability, cortical tubers
• Renal: Angiomyolipomas
• Cardiac: Rhabdomyomas
• Mnemonic: HAMARTOMAS – Hamartomas, Angiofibromas, Mitral regurgitation,
Ash-leaf, Rhabdomyoma, Tuberous sclerosis, autosomal dOminant, Mental retardation,
Angiomyolipoma, Seizures/Shagreen
Ataxia Telangiectasia:
• Gene: ATM, defect in DNA repair
• Findings: Cerebellar ataxia, oculocutaneous telangiectasia, recurrent sinopulmonary infections
• Labs: ↑AFP, IgA deficiency
• Risk: Lymphoid malignancies
• Mnemonic: A-T: Ataxia + Telangiectasia + immunodeficiency + ↑AFP
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2912–2913; UWorld; Pediatric On Squares
☆ Neurology: Neurocutaneous Syndromes, Sturge-Weber
121-
A 6-year-old boy is brought to the clinic with 3 days of sore throat, fever, and rash. His mother
notes that he’s been irritable, refusing to eat, and that his skin feels rough. On exam, his temperature
is 39°C, and he has tonsillar erythema with white exudates, an erythematous rash with a sandpaper
texture over the trunk and extremities, circumoral pallor, and a “strawberry tongue”. There is no
conjunctivitis, and his palms and soles are not involved. What is the most likely diagnosis?
A- Kawasaki disease
B- Scarlet fever
C- Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
D- Infectious mononucleosis
Explanation: Scarlet fever is caused by Group A Streptococcus (GAS) producing erythrogenic exotoxins.
It typically occurs in children with streptococcal pharyngitis and is characterized by:
• High fever, sore throat, tonsillar exudates
• Fine, sandpaper-like rash beginning on the neck/trunk → spreading to extremities
• Circumoral pallor, “strawberry tongue”, and Pastia’s lines (rash accentuated in skin folds)
• Rash appears within 1–2 days of onset of fever and desquamates after resolution
•
•
•
Kawasaki disease: More common <5 years old, includes conjunctivitis, mucositis, extremity
changes, and lasts >5 days
SSSS: Often in neonates or infants, diffuse erythema, skin sloughing, tender skin, no pharyngitis
Mononucleosis: Caused by EBV, exudative pharyngitis possible, but rash is maculopapular and
follows ampicillin/amoxicillin use
Clinical Pearls:
• Diagnosis: Clinical, but confirmed with throat culture or rapid antigen test
• Treatment: First-line is oral penicillin V or amoxicillin for 10 days
• Complications: Rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis (not prevented by antibiotics), otitis media,
abscess
• Desquamation occurs as the rash resolves
• GAS strains causing scarlet fever
are toxigenic, producing superantigens
📚 Source: Red Book 2024, p. 701–704; Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1351–1354; AMBOSS
☆ Infectious Disease: Scarlet Fever, Pediatric Exanthema
source: Red Book 2024, p. 701–704; Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1351–1354; AMBOSS

stem:
A 6-year-old boy presents with a palpable purpuric rash on the buttocks and lower extremities,
abdominal pain, and swollen knees and ankles. His parents report a recent upper respiratory tract
infection 1 week ago. On examination, he is afebrile, the rash is non-blanching and limited to the lower
limbs, and there is mild swelling of the knees. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
★ Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP):
Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP) is an IgA-mediated (type III HSR), leukocytoclastic vasculitis that
presents with palpable purpura, arthralgia/arthritis, abdominal pain, and/or hematuria.
• Renal involvement is due to mesangial deposition of IgA immune complexes with subsequent
mesangial cell proliferation, neutrophilic infiltration, and glomerular damage.
• Most common vasculitis in children
o 90% of affected individuals < 10 years
o Peak incidence: 6 years
• May be precipitated by an upper respiratory tract/ GI infection by 1–3 weeks.
• Diagnosis is clinical:
o Purpura (usually palpable and in clusters) or petechiae, with lower limb predominance and
without thrombocytopenia or coagulopathy.
o PLUS at least one of the following:
 Abdominal pain (usually diffuse, with acute onset)
 Arthritis or arthralgia (acute onset)
 Renal involvement (proteinuria, hematuria)
 IgA deposition (leukocytoclastic vasculitis, proliferative glomerulonephritis)
• Normal laboratory values; ↓ Hemoglobin suggests GI bleeding.
• Complications:
o Intussusception (ileo-ileal, unlike classic ileo-colic); due to bowel wall edema, most
common GI complication
o Renal failure (especially with nephrotic-range proteinuria or elevated creatinine)
 Chronic kidney disease if severe glomerulonephritis
o Scrotal edema in males
o Rare: pulmonary hemorrhage, CNS vasculitis
• Treatment:
o Supportive care: hydration, rest, NSAIDs for arthralgia
 Avoid NSAIDs in patients with IgAV nephritis or GI bleeding
o Corticosteroids: for severe abdominal pain, renal involvement, or intussusception
 Rule out intussusception before starting systemic glucocorticoids in patients with
severe abdominal pain.
o ACE inhibitors: if persistent proteinuria
• Prognosis:
o Most cases of IgAV are self-limited and only require supportive care.
o Monitor urine and BP for 6–12 months for renal sequelae
 Urinalysis (for hematuria and proteinuria) every 1–2 weeks for the first 1–2 months →
monthly up to 6–12 months
 BP monitoring with every visit
Clinical Pearls:
• Most common systemic vasculitis in children
• Skin biopsy shows IgA deposition in dermal vessels
• Renal involvement can be delayed—monitor urine for 6–12 months
• Steroids help only in severe GI or renal cases
• Can be associated with intussusception in toddlers
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1167–1169, UpToDate, "IgA vasculitis (HenochSchönlein purpura): Clinical manifestations and diagnosis"
☆ Rheumatology: Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP; IgA Vasculitis)
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1167–1169, UpToDate, "IgA vasculitis (HenochSchönlein purpura): Clinical manifestations and diagnosis"

stem:
A hospitalized child suddenly collapses. Cardiac monitor shows pulseless electrical activity
(PEA). You confirm no pulse, and high-quality CPR has already been initiated. What is the next best
step in management?
explanation:
Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA) is the presence of an organized rhythm without a palpable
pulse. It is not a shockable rhythm, so defibrillation is not indicated. The correct immediate action is:
• Continue high-quality CPR
• Administer epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg IV/IO (0.1 mL/kg of
1:10,000 solution) every 3–5 minutes
• Search and treat reversible causes (H’s and T’s)
Why other options are incorrect:
• Defibrillation: Only used in shockable rhythms
(VF/pulseless VT), not PEA
• Synchronized cardioversion: For tachyarrhythmias
with a pulse
• Amiodarone: Used in refractory VF/pulseless VT, not
indicated in PEA
Clinical pearls:
• PEA = organized rhythm + no pulse
• Always evaluate and correct reversible causes:
• H's: Hypovolemia, Hypoxia, Hydrogen ion (acidosis),
Hypo/hyperkalemia, Hypoglycemia, Hypothermia
• T's: Tension pneumothorax, Tamponade, Toxins,
Thrombosis (PE/MI), Trauma
• Epinephrine is first-line drug, no role for defibrillation
📚 Source: Pediatric Advanced Life Support (PALS) Guidelines; American Heart Association 2020
☆ PICU: Cardiac Arrest, Pulseless Electrical Activity
source: Pediatric Advanced Life Support (PALS) Guidelines; American Heart Association 2020

stem:
A child presents with pain after foreign body ingestion. X-ray is provided below. What is the most
appropriate action?
explanation:
• A coin or button battery in the esophagus is considered
an urgent foreign body due to the risk of mucosal
injury, perforation, and airway compression.
• Endoscopic removal is the standard of care for symptomatic
esophageal foreign bodies or those not passing spontaneously
within 24 hours
• Button batteries are always removed due to risk of leakage and perforation.
• Discharge home is inappropriate for symptomatic or impacted FBs.
• Barium studies are contraindicated as they interfere with endoscopic visibility + aspiration risk.
• Inducing vomiting is dangerous due to risk of aspiration or esophageal damage.
Foreign Body Ingestion:
X-ray findings:
• Use neck, chest, and abdominal films
• Radiopaque FB (e.g., coins, batteries, metal) are
usually visible
• Location clues:
o Esophageal FB: appears in the coronal
(face-on) view on AP film
o Tracheal FB (if any): seen in sagittal
(side-view) orientation on lateral film
Orientation:
• Coin in esophagus → flat on AP view
• Coin in trachea → edge visible on AP, flat on lateral
Clinical clues:
• Dysphagia, drooling, chest discomfort, vomiting
• Usually no respiratory distress unless
compression or aspiration
Foreign Body Aspiration (FBA):
X-ray findings:
• Chest X-ray (CXR) is key
• May show:
o Unilateral hyperinflation (air trapping, especially
on expiratory view)
o Mediastinal shift (away from the affected side)
o Atelectasis if airway is completely obstructed
o Visible FB only if radiopaque (rare — e.g., metal)
• Common site: Right main bronchus (more vertical in
children)
Orientation:
• FB may not be seen; rely on indirect signs like air trapping
Clinical clues:
• Sudden coughing, choking, wheezing, unilateral
decreased breath sounds
• No GI symptoms
Coin ingestion:
• If stable, asymptomatic → may wait 24 hours before removing the coin (may pass spontaneously)
• If drooling or cannot swallow → requires removal within 2 hours
• If respiratory symptoms are present → immediate removal
Button battery: if in the esophagus → remove immediately, if beyond esophagus: >5 yrs + <20 mm diameter
(repeat x ray in 2 days)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 622; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guide, p 416
☆ Gastroenterology: Foreign body ingestion
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 622; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guide, p 416

stem:
A 2-year-old child is brought to the emergency department after being witnessed swallowing a
foreign body. The child is asymptomatic. X-ray is shown below.
What is the next best step?

options.label: C
options.text: Admit for observation and serial imaging

answer: D. Discharge home with outpatient follow-up
explanation:
If the ingested foreign body has passed into the stomach and the child is asymptomatic,
most smooth, non-sharp objects like coins will pass spontaneously within a few days. The correct
approach is conservative:
• Reassure and discharge
• Instruct parents to monitor stools
• Follow-up in 1–2 weeks to confirm passage if not seen in stool
• Urgent intervention only if symptoms (vomiting, pain, fever, refusal to eat) develop
• Endoscopic removal: indicated for sharp, large, multiple magnets, batteries, or symptomatic pts
• Ipecac: Contraindicated due to risk of aspiration and ineffective in foreign body ingestion
• Admission: Not necessary in an asymptomatic child with object already in stomach
Clinical pearls:
• Esophageal foreign bodies require removal if symptomatic or if not passed within 24 hrs
• Stomach or beyond: Most pass without intervention
• Objects >2.5 cm or retained >1 week in stomach may require removal
• Button batteries = emergency if in esophagus, urgent if not passed in 48–72 hrs from stomach
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2785–2787; UpToDate: Foreign body ingestion in
children
☆ Gastroenterology: Foreign body ingestion
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2785–2787; UpToDate: Foreign body ingestion in

stem:
A preschool-aged child presents for a routine check-up. On auscultation, a soft systolic murmur
is heard that does not radiate, and decreases with position change (e.g., from supine to sitting). The
child is otherwise asymptomatic with a normal exam. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This is a classic presentation of an innocent murmur (also called a functional or physiologic
murmur), common in preschool-aged children. These murmurs:
• Are soft, systolic, non-radiating
• Vary with position, typically louder when supine and softer or absent when sitting or standing
• Do not indicate structural heart disease
• Are not associated with symptoms like cyanosis, failure to thrive, or syncope
• Aortic stenosis: Typically radiates to the neck and may be associated with a thrill
• VSD: Murmur is usually harsh, holosystolic + may be associated with symptoms depending on size
• HCM: Murmur increases with standing or Valsalva; often associated with family history and risk of
sudden cardiac death
Clinical pearls:
• Innocent murmurs are found in up to 50–70% of children
• Still’s murmur is the most common type in preschoolers: musical or vibratory, best heard at LLSB
• No workup needed if child is asymptomatic with normal growth and exam
• Refer to cardiology if murmur is diastolic, harsh, radiating, or associated with abnormal findings
• Always evaluate with ECG + echocardiogram if red flags present
★ Red flags in pediatric murmurs
o Thrill (Grade IV or higher murmur)
o Diastolic murmur
o Cyanosis or failure to thrive
o Murmur radiating to the back, carotids, or lower extremities
o Loud systolic murmur ≥ Grade III
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2415–2416; UpToDate: Approach to the child with a
cardiac murmur
☆ Pediatric Cardiology: Innocent Murmurs
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2415–2416; UpToDate: Approach to the child with a

stem:
A 14-year-old girl presents with significant weight loss, amenorrhea, and intense fear of gaining
weight. She adheres to a restrictive diet, denies binge eating or purging, and has low BMI. Labs show
bradycardia, low LH/FSH, and borderline electrolyte abnormalities. Which of the following is TRUE
regarding anorexia nervosa?

options.label: A
options.text: It is equally prevalent in both sexes

options.label: B
options.text: Emotional eating is more common in females

options.label: C
options.text: Male adolescents are most commonly affected

answer: B. Emotional eating is more common in females
explanation:
While anorexia nervosa is marked by voluntary restrictive eating, emotional eating—defined
as eating in response to negative emotions—is significantly more common in females, particularly during
adolescence. This may contribute to the development or perpetuation of various eating disorders.
•
•
•
Anorexia nervosa has a strong female predominance, with a 10:1 female-to-male ratio.
Female adolescents are the most commonly affected group.
Weight loss in anorexia nervosa is intentional and driven by distorted body image.
Clinical pearls:
• Common physical signs: bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia,
lanugo, amenorrhea, delayed puberty
• Laboratory abnormalities: low LH/FSH, hypokalemia,
hypophosphatemia, elevated liver enzymes
• Complications: osteoporosis, refeeding syndrome, cardiac
arrhythmia
o refeeding syndrome: reintroducing nutrition → ↑ insulin →
shifts K+, P, Mg2+ intracellular
o risk for prolonged QT → can progress to torsades de
pointes and sudden death
• Treatment: multidisciplinary (nutrition, psychotherapy, medical
monitoring); family-based therapy is first-line in adolescents
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 84–89; UpToDate, "Anorexia nervosa in adolescents:
Clinical features, diagnosis, and treatment"
☆ Adolescent Medicine / Psychiatry: Anorexia Nervosa

As We Approach Our First Exam…
As we stand at the threshold of our first exam in pediatric residency, let’s take a
moment to reflect—not just on what we’ve studied, but on how far we’ve come. In
the face of long nights, steep learning curves, and heavy responsibilities, we
showed up with resilience, with heart, and with purpose. Whatever the outcome,
we owe ourselves a moment of pride. We’ve grown not only in knowledge, but in
courage and commitment. Let this be a reminder that we are not alone, and that
every step we take brings us closer to becoming the physicians we once dreamed
of becoming.
May we continue to support one another and grow—together—in pursuit of
child health, wellbeing, and knowledge.

stem:
A term newborn develops cyanosis after birth that improves with crying. The infant is stable
between feeds but becomes dusky when quiet or attempting to feed. There are no major anomalies
noted on physical examination. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Cyanosis that improves with crying is classic for bilateral choanal atresia, because
crying forces the infant to breathe through the mouth.
• Newborns are obligate nasal breathers for the first 4–6 weeks of life.
• Unilateral choanal atresia is often asymptomatic in the newborn period and usually
discovered later due to persistent nasal obstruction or discharge.
• GERD does not cause isolated cyanosis relieved
by crying.
• TTN presents with tachypnea and grunting, not
episodic cyanosis relieved by crying.
Clinical pearls:
Unilateral choanal atresia
• Bilateral choanal atresia should be suspected in
any neonate with cyclic cyanosis relieved by crying
• Diagnosis confirmed by inability to pass a nasogastric tube and
nasal endoscopy or CT
• Emergent airway stabilization may be needed, often using
an oral airway initially
• Associated with CHARGE syndrome
Bilateral choanal atresia
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 187
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Congenital Airway Malformations
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 187

stem:
An 11-month-old presents with a history of poor stamina with feeding and play, shortness
of breath, and fatigue over several weeks. Family history is notable for sudden infant death.
On exam, he is afebrile with a gallop rhythm. Laboratory studies reveal non-ketotic
hypoglycemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) in infants may present with feeding difficulties, easy fatigability,
tachypnea, and reduced interaction or play
• A gallop rhythm suggests ventricular dysfunction
• Family history of sudden death suggests a possible genetic/metabolic cause
• Non-ketotic hypoglycemia is a hallmark of fatty acid oxidation defects (e.g., VLCAD), which are
associated with DCM
• Myocarditis is more acute and often febrile
• Endocarditis is rare without risk factors and typically presents with fever
• Restrictive cardiomyopathy is rare in infancy and leads to signs of diastolic dysfunction without
dilation
★ Dilated Cardiomyopathy:
• Most common pediatric cardiomyopathy; involves ventricular dilation and ↓ systolic
function (EF)
• Causes: idiopathic, viral (Coxsackie B), genetic (AD), metabolic
(VLCAD), toxic (doxorubicin), nutritional (thiamine)
o Familial DCM is often due to autosomal dominant
mutations in sarcomere or structural proteins
 Most common genetic cause: truncating
mutations in the TTN gene (encodes titin)
 Titin anchors myosin to Z-discs and contributes
to passive myocardial tension
 Incomplete penetrance → symptoms may
be delayed or absent in some carriers
• Signs: poor feeding, tachypnea, fatigue, hepatomegaly, S3
gallop
o Neonatal: diaphoresis/fatigue during feeding
• Red flags: family history of sudden death, non-ketotic hypoglycemia
• Workup: CXR (cardiomegaly), ECG (tachycardia, low voltage), echo (dilated LV, ↓ EF)
• Management: ACE inhibitors, diuretics, beta-blockers, milrinone if decompensated
• Screen for metabolic/genetic causes in infants
• Risk of arrhythmias, heart failure, thromboembolism
• Transplant for refractory cases
Clinical pearls:
• In infants, look for feeding fatigue, diaphoresis, poor weight gain, tachypnea, and decreased
activity as signs of heart failure.
•
•
•
Fatty acid oxidation disorders can cause DCM with non-ketotic hypoglycemia — always screen
metabolically
Consider echocardiography + metabolic/genetic testing in unexplained cardiomyopathy
Gallop rhythm = S3 → reflects poor ventricular function
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 430
☆ Cardiology: Dilated Cardiomyopathy
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 430
topic: • Most common pediatric cardiomyopathy; involves ventricular dilation and ↓ systolic

stem:
An 11-year-old child presents for routine immunization. According to the Saudi Ministry of Health
vaccination schedule, which of the following vaccines is recommended at this age?
explanation:
• At 11 years of age, the Saudi MOH schedule recommends a booster dose of Tdap
• Tdap contains tetanus, reduced diphtheria, and acellular pertussis antigens — appropriate
for older children, adolescents, and adults
• DTaP is used for children <7 years; contains higher diphtheria and pertussis antigen doses
o should never be used in individuals >7 years due to high reactogenicity
• Td is given every 10 years as a booster after Tdap
• IPV is not routinely given alone or with Td at 11 years in the Saudi schedule
Clinical pearls:
• Tdap is required once at age 11–12 years regardless of previous DTaP history
• Provides pertussis protection, important for preventing transmission to infants
• Ensure Td boosters every 10 years after Tdap
• DTaP should never be used in individuals >7 years due to high reactogenicity
Saudi Vaccination Schedule (MOH 2024)
• Preterm infants should receive the vaccine at actual age not the corrected age
• Only exception is Hepatitis B vaccine if birth weight < 2kg — given at 1 month or discharge
(whichever is 1st)
• Preschool (4-6 years) vaccines are: DTaP, OPV, Varicella, MMR
Remember: Birth, 2–4–6 months build the base,
9–12–18 add extras, school & teen boosters follow.
- Measles monovalent at 9 months (pre-MMR)
- MMR given at 12, 18 months, and again at 4–6 years
- IPV/OPV given at 0, 2, 4, 6, 12, 18 months, and 4–6 years
- PCV: 3-dose primary (2, 4, 6 mo) + booster at 12 mo
- MCV4: at 9 & 12 months, booster at 18 years
- Hep A: 2-dose series at 18 & 24 months
- HPV: for girls only, at age 12
📚 Source: AAP & CDC Vaccination Guidelines, Saudi MoH Vaccination schedule.
☆ General Pediatrics: Vaccines
source: AAP & CDC Vaccination Guidelines, Saudi MoH Vaccination schedule.

stem:
A girl presents with painless swelling inside her mouth that has a rapid onset and tends to fluctuate
in size. There is no associated fever or pain. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Mucocele is a benign, painless, bluish cystic lesion of the oral cavity caused by rupture or
blockage of a minor salivary gland duct
o Common in children and young adults due to lip biting or trauma
o May spontaneously resolve; persistent lesions require excision of the gland
o Rapid recurrence or atypical features warrant referral to ENT or oral surgery
• Commonly appears on the lower lip or floor of the mouth (ranula)
• Characterized by sudden onset, fluctuating size, and translucent bluish hue
• Oral lymphangiomas are congenital, tend to be pebbly or vesicular, and don’t fluctuate as
dramatically
• Branchial cleft cysts present as neck masses, not intraoral
• Pyogenic granulomas are red, friable, vascular lesions that bleed easily — not fluctuant or cystic

Notes

bluish, fluctuates in size

post-trauma

floor of mouth

(plunging ranula)

White plaques, do not
scrape off easily
Tongue, buccal mucosa
Common in infants,
immunosuppressed

drooling, decreased PO
intake

buccal

with acyclovir

base with red rim

may interfere with

stem:
You saw a patient with acute otitis media who was prescribed amoxicillin. He came for a follow up &
you noticed that the pharmacy gave him double the dose. Patient is doing well otherwise. What do
you do next?

options.label: A
options.text: You discharge the patient on correct dose

options.label: C
options.text: Tell the family this is the pharmacy mistake

answer: D. Disclose the issue and apologize
explanation:
According to the principles of medical ethics and professionalism endorsed by the American
Academy of Pediatrics and emphasized in pediatric literature like Nelson's and Harriet Lane, full disclosure
is ethically mandatory when a medication error is identified—even if the patient is asymptomatic or harmed.
This is a matter of transparency and maintaining trust with the patient and their caregivers.
Key Concepts:
• The error was a medication overdose, even if harmless in this case.
• Ethical practice dictates honest communication about the mistake.
• Disclosure includes explaining the mistake, apologizing, and correcting it.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Edition – Professionalism & Ethics; Harriet Lane Handbook
– Medication Safety Principles (implied in drug administration guidelines).
☆ Ethics: Medication Safety
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Edition – Professionalism & Ethics; Harriet Lane Handbook

stem:
An exclusively breastfed infant presents with diarrhea, alopecia, and a periorificial, erythematous,
scaly rash involving the mouth, diaper area, and eyes. What is the most appropriate investigation to
confirm the diagnosis?
explanation:
• The presentation is classic for acrodermatitis enteropathica, a condition caused
by zinc deficiency, either inherited or acquired.
• Acrodermatitis enteropathica (inherited, AR – SLC39A4 mutation)
• Acquired deficiency:
o Prematurity
o Chronic diarrhea
o Malabsorption (e.g., IBD, celiac)
o Poor dietary intake or TPN without zinc supplementation
• Key findings:
o Chronic diarrhea
o Periorificial and acral dermatitis (perioral, perineal, and distal limbs)
o Alopecia, irritability, failure to thrive
• Serum zinc level is the definitive diagnostic test; confirmed by clinical response
to zinc supplementation
•
Treatment:
o Zinc supplementation: 1–3 mg/kg/day elemental zinc
o Lifelong if inherited; temporary if acquired
Clinical pearls:
• Zinc deficiency = diarrhea + alopecia +
rash (especially mouth, anus, eyes)
• Acquired zinc deficiency seen in breastfed
infants, malnourished, or TPN-dependent patients
• Treat with oral zinc supplementation — rapid
resolution of symptoms
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 96
☆ General Pediatrics: Nutritional Deficiencies
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 96

stem:
A 6-month-old infant presents with fever, tachypnea, and basilar rales on auscultation. The
infant appears nontoxic and is feeding adequately. What is the most appropriate next step?
explanation:
• Follow your ABCs! In any infant with respiratory symptoms and tachypnea, the first
priority is to assess oxygenation
• Pulse oximetry is a non-invasive, immediate, and critical triage tool
• Helps determine the need for supplemental oxygen, further imaging, or hospitalization
• Chest X-ray, CBC, and CRP may be considered after initial stability is confirmed, especially
if pneumonia is suspected — but none precede oxygen assessment in triage
• The child is nontoxic → no immediate indication for invasive testing before vitals and
saturation are checked
Clinical pearls:
• Always check oxygen saturation first in febrile infants with respiratory signs
• Basilar rales may suggest early pneumonia, bronchiolitis, or viral LRTI
• Stable infants with normal sats may be managed outpatient, depending on clinical course
• CBC and CRP have limited diagnostic utility in viral LRTIs and are not first-line
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 146
☆ General Pediatrics: Acute Respiratory Illness in Infants
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 146

stem:
An adolescent with anorexia nervosa is admitted for nutritional rehabilitation. As feeding is initiated,
what is the most likely early complication to develop?
explanation:
• This patient is at high risk for refeeding syndrome, a serious complication that
•
•
•
occurs when feeding is restarted after prolonged starvation
During starvation, the body shifts to fat metabolism and preserves electrolytes
intracellularly
Refeeding (especially carbohydrates) causes a sudden insulin surge,
driving phosphate, potassium, and magnesium into cells
Hypophosphatemia is the hallmark of refeeding syndrome and can lead
to cardiac failure (prolonged QT interval → torsades [see Q7]), respiratory
muscle weakness, and seizures
★ Refeeding Syndrome:
• Classic refeeding syndrome: reintroducing nutrition → ↑ insulin → shifts K+, P, Mg2+
intracellularly.
• Pathophysiology: very rapid increase in daily food intake in severely malnourished patients can
cause massive insulin release → increased displacement of magnesium, potassium,
and phosphate (shift from extracellular to intracellular) → ↓↓↓ phosphate (most critical marker,
MC), ↓ potassium, ↓ magnesium
o Electrolytes: hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia,
and hypomagnesemia + hypoglycemia
• Risk in:
o Severely malnourished (e.g., anorexia nervosa,
NICU, short bowel syndrome)
o Prolonged fasting / minimal intake (>7 days)
o Chronic TPN dependence
• Clinical features:
o Weakness, lethargy
o Arrhythmias
o Seizures
o Respiratory failure (↓ ATP → diaphragm weakness)
o Fluid overload, edema
• Prevention & Management:
o Start feeds slowly (25–50% of needs)
 low-calorie feeds (~20 kcal/kg/day)
o Aggressive monitoring & repletion:
 Phosphate, potassium, magnesium
 Thiamine supplementation before/with feeding
 Monitor electrolytes daily for 1st week
 Watch for signs of heart failure, arrhythmia, and edema
o Note: these patients also likely have some degree of thiamine deficiency, and can benefit
from initial supplementation (before glucose administration).
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 396
☆ Endocrinology: Eating Disorders & Refeeding Syndrome
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 396
topic: • Classic refeeding syndrome: reintroducing nutrition → ↑ insulin → shifts K+, P, Mg2+

stem:
A female adolescent is admitted due to heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia) severe enough to
cause anemia and hemodynamic instability. She has regular cycles since menarche, no pelvic pain,
and no sexual activity. What is the most likely cause of her heavy bleeding?
explanation:
• In adolescents within the first 2–3 years post-menarche, heavy menstrual bleeding is most often
due to an underlying bleeding disorder, especially if bleeding is severe enough to require
hospitalization
• The most common bleeding disorder in this population is von Willebrand disease
• PCOS causes oligomenorrhea or amenorrhea, not heavy regular bleeding
• Cervical polyps and hemangiomas are rare and not typical causes of diffuse heavy uterine
bleeding
• Always consider bleeding disorders in young teens with menorrhagia, especially with family
history, easy bruising, or epistaxis
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 410
☆ Hematology: Bleeding Disorders
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 410

stem:
A 2-year-old boy is brought by his mother due to a crusted skin lesion around the mouth. The
lesion began as small red papules and progressed to honey-colored crusts. He is otherwise well,
afebrile, and has no systemic symptoms.
What is the most appropriate treatment?
explanation:
• Classic presentation of non-bullous impetigo, most common in toddlers
• Caused by Staphylococcus aureus and/or Group A Streptococcus
• Localized lesions without systemic symptoms are treated with topical mupirocin
• Oral antibiotics reserved for extensive disease or systemic involvement
• Hydrocortisone is contraindicated — may worsen bacterial infections
Clinical pearls:
• Honey-colored crust around mouth/nose = impetigo
• Topical Antibiotics (e.g., mupirocin 2% ointment or retapamulin) → Few, localized lesions, nonbullous impetigo, no systemic signs
• Oral Antibiotics (e.g., Cephalexin, Clindamycin) → Extensive or multiple
lesions, bullous impetigo, systemic symptoms (fever, LAD); failed topical
treatment
• Keep child home for 24 hours after starting antibiotics (Topical
mupirocin)
o Children can return to school or daycare 24 hours after starting appropriate antibiotic
therapy, if lesions are covered and show signs of improvement.
• If lesions worsen or spread rapidly, escalate to oral therapy
• Prevent transmission via good hygiene and lesion coverage
• Localized impetigo is treated with topical antibiotics and most patients have no long-term sequelae.
However, a small number of patients with impetigo due to GAS develop poststreptococcal
glomerulonephritis (PSGN) several weeks after the initial infection.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 846; UWorld
☆ Dermatology: Bacterial Skin Infections
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 846; UWorld

stem:
A teenage girl presents for treatment of acne and expresses feeling upset because her mother
won’t allow her to go out with friends. She says she won’t be popular, believes her mother is doing
this to avoid her being tired for school, and feels less attractive compared to her peers. What is the
most appropriate classification of her developmental stage?
explanation:
• Mid adolescence (ages ~14–16) is marked by:
o Heightened concern about peer acceptance, popularity, and body image
o Ongoing development of abstract thinking and beginning to interpret parental motives
o Increased conflict with parents as autonomy grows
• Early adolescence (10–13) focuses more on concrete thinking and pubertal changes
• Late adolescence (17–19) is characterized by more stable identity, future orientation, and
improved reasoning
• Pre-adolescents (<10–11 years) typically don’t express peer-based identity concerns at this level
Note: According to the way previous questions were solved, many chose the answer as early adolescent,
however, according to Nelson adolescent development (22nd ed 2024; p 1158), the description (social
pressure, peer acceptance, conflicts over control and independence) fits more with mid-adolescent.
Clinical pearls:
• Mid adolescents often struggle with identity, peer status, and parental
limits
• Important time for psychosocial screening (e.g., HEADSS assessment)
• Acne treatment can significantly affect self-esteem and social
functioning
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, p. 99–101
☆ Adolescent Medicine: Developmental Stages
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, p. 99–101

stem:
A child presents with severe diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA): history of polydipsia, weight loss,
abdominal pain, and vomiting. Labs show blood glucose >300 mg/dL, pH 6.9, and HCO₃⁻ 6 mEq/L.
What is the most appropriate initial management step?

options.label: A
options.text: IVF normal saline bolus of 10 mL/kg over 1 hr

answer: A. IVF normal saline bolus of 10 mL/kg over 1 hr
explanation:
• This patient has severe DKA: pH <7.0, HCO₃ <10, and clinical signs of dehydration
• Initial management in DKA is fluid resuscitation to restore perfusion
• 10 mL/kg normal saline over 1 hour is preferred in children with severe acidosis to avoid
cerebral edema (avoid rapid correction)
• Bicarbonate is contraindicated in pediatric DKA — increases risk of cerebral edema and
paradoxical CNS acidosis
• Maintenance + 9% is not a standard fluid plan in DKA
• 1.5× maintenance may be used after bolus, for ongoing fluid and deficit replacement — not first
step
★ Diabetic Ketoacidosis: (very high yield for wards & boards)
• Approximately 25% of children with T1DM present in diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA).
• Pathophysiology: Triggered by inadequate insulin/increased
insulin need (stress from infection, steroids, etc.)
o ↓ insulin → glycogenolysis + gluconeogenesis + lipolysis
o ↑ glucose → polyuria → dehydration
o ↑ lipolysis leads to creation of ketone bodies, if ↑↑ → ↓
pH → electrolyte abnormalities
• Patients with DKA present initially with polyuria, polydipsia,
nausea, and vomiting.
o Abdominal pain occurs frequently and can mimic an
acute abdomen.
o The abdomen may be tender from vomiting or
distended secondary to a paralytic ileus.
•
Diagnosis: Glucose >200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L); Moderate–large ketones in urine,
betahydroxybutyrate in blood; AGAP metabolic acidosis
General principles of DKA Management:
• Initial labs: glucose, VBG, Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, HCO₃⁻, BUN, Cr, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, phosphate, urinalysis (ketones)
• Monitor: neuro status, vitals hourly, glucose q1hr & electrolytes q2–4h
• Start with fluids — insulin comes after volume resuscitation begins
o 1st start w/ a NS bolus 10 mg/kg
o 2nd continuous IV fluids at 1.5–2× maintenance (to rehydrate and avoid dehydration)
 DO NOT USE HYPOTONIC FLUIDS – cerebral edema risk
 ↑ dextrose-containing fluids once blood glucose <300 and ↓ nondextrose fluids
• Insulin infusion – Regular insulin (0.1 u/kg/hr) – start 1 hr after initial NS bolus
o Goal to ↓ glucose by 50–100 mg/dL/hr (2.8–5.6 mmol/L/hr)
• At risk of initial hypokalemia and hypophosphatemia 2º to urine losses, hypernatremia 2º to dehydration
(may present as pseudohyponatremia)
o insulin shifts K⁺ and PO4 shift → hypokalemia & hypophosphatemia risk [total body depletion]
o K added to IV fluids [typically 20 mmol/L potassium phosphate + 20 mmol/L potassium chloride or
acetate]
o PO4 replaced only if symptomatic (e.g., muscle weakness, arrhythmia, hemolysis)
• Avoid bicarbonate unless pH <6.9 with life-threatening instability – risk of cerebral edema
Mild DKA: pH 7.2 – <7.3, HCO3 10 – <15
Usually well-appearing, alert
Fluids:
10–20 mL/kg NS over 1–2 hr
Maintenance + deficit (5%) over 36–
48 hrs
Insulin:
Start 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr IV
regular insulin 1 hour after fluids
Potassium: Add once urine output
confirmed
Glucose: Add D5% when BG < 250–300
mg/dL (13.9–16.7 mmol/L)
Moderate DKA: pH 7.1 – <7.2, HCO3 5 – <10
More dehydration, possible lethargy
Fluids:
10–20 mL/kg NS over 1–2 hr
Maintenance + 7–8% deficit over 48 hrs
Insulin:
Start 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr IV regular
insulin 1 hour after fluids
Potassium: Monitor K⁺ closely — total body
K⁺ is low even if serum is normal
Glucose: Add D5% when BG < 250–300
mg/dL (13.9–16.7 mmol/L)
Severe DKA: pH <7.1, HCO3 <5
High risk for cerebral edema, may be
obtunded or confused
Fluids:
10–20 mL/kg NS over 1–2 hr
Replace 10% deficit over 48 hrs,
using 0.9% or 0.45% NaCl
depending on corrected Na⁺
Insulin:
Start 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr IV
regular insulin 1 hour after fluids
Potassium: Monitor K⁺ closely — total
body K⁺ is low even if serum is normal
Glucose: Add D5% when BG < 250–
300 mg/dL (13.9–16.7 mmol/L)
Avoid bicarbonate unless pH <6.9
with cardiac instability or coma
Complications:
• Cerebral edema (0.5% to 1%)
o Signs/Sx: Headache, incontinence, altered mental status, emesis, hypertension, bradycardia, irregular
respirations
o Risk factors: age <5 yo, severe acidosis (pH <7.1, bicarb <5), severe dehydration (elevated BUN), severe
hyperosmolarity (corrected Na >155, glucose >1,000mg/dL [55.5 mmol/L]), longer duration of sx, new-onset
diabetes, aggressive initial fluid resuscitation, treatment with bicarbonate
o Management: Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg intravenously over 20 min or hypertonic saline (3% NS) 5–10 mL/kg over 30
min
 repeated if there is no response in 30 minutes
o Respiratory compromise – Rare respiratory failure
• Electrolyte abnormalities
o Cardiac dysrhythmias
o Rapid correction has risk for CNS fluid shifts
When is the patient considered out of DKA?
• Venous pH > 7.3
• Bicarbonate ≥ 15 mEq/L
• No ketonuria or ketonemia
• Alert mental status
• Hemodynamically stable and well hydrated
• Tolerating oral intake
Next steps:
• Start subcutaneous insulin once DKA has
resolved and the child is eating
• Give SC insulin 30–60 minutes before stopping
the insulin drip to avoid rebound hyperglycemia
Clinical pearls:
• Always start DKA management with isotonic fluid bolus (10–20 mL/kg) based on severity
•
•
•
Do not give bicarbonate unless pH <6.9 with life-threatening symptoms (e.g., arrhythmia)
Replace deficit + maintenance over 36–48 hours; add insulin only after 1–2 hours of fluids
Monitor glucose, electrolytes, pH, and mental status closely for cerebral edema
DKA mnemonic: The most important findings of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) are: Delirium/psychosis,
Dehydration, Kussmaul respirations, Abdominal pain/nausea/vomiting, fruity (Acetone) breath odor.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 264–265; Osama Naga 3rd ed., p. 173; Oncall
Pediatrics; Pocket Pediatrics.
☆ Endocrinology: Diabetic Ketoacidosis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 264–265; Osama Naga 3rd ed., p. 173; Oncall

stem:
A family brings their young son to the clinic with complaints of increased irritability and perianal
itching, especially at night. They report seeing small white worms emerging from his anus. An image
of the organism is provided.
What is the most appropriate diagnostic test?
explanation:
• This presentation is typical of Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm) infection
• Classic signs: perianal itching, nighttime irritability, and visible white worms near anus
• The tape test is the diagnostic gold standard: apply clear adhesive tape to the perianal region in the
early morning before bathing or defecation
• Ova & parasite stool tests are not useful — pinworm eggs are deposited perianally, not passed in
stool
• Stool culture and rectal swab culture are irrelevant — used for bacterial pathogens, not
helminths
★ Pinworm infection (Enterobius vermicularis)
• Epidemiology
o Most common helminthic infection in the U.S.
o Prevalence in the US: ∼ 12%
o Primarily affects children 5–10 years of age
Mode of transmission
o Initial infection: fecal-oral
o Reinfection: digital-oral after scratching anal region
• Clinical features
o Anal pruritus (especially at night)
o Vulvovaginitis, especially in children
o Occasionally, symptoms of intestinal infection (i.e., nausea,
vomiting, and abdominal pain which may become severe
enough to mimic appendicitis)
• Diagnosis
o Tape test: microscopic detection of oval eggs (ova) and/or
pinworms on tape that has been pressed against the perianal
region
o Can be an incidental finding on endoscopy
• Treatment: bendazoles OR pyrantel pamoate
o Treat with mebendazole 100 mg PO, repeat after 2 weeks
 Alternatives: albendazole 400 mg, or pyrantel
pamoate
o Treat all close contacts to prevent reinfection
o Emphasize strict hand hygiene, daily underwear
changes, and washing of linens and bedding
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 145
☆ Infectious Diseases: Helminthic Infections
•
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 145

stem:
A young patient develops jaundice and dark urine shortly after eating fava beans. What is the most
likely finding on peripheral blood smear?
explanation:
• This is a classic presentation of G6PD deficiency, a X-linked recessive disorder [M>F] that leads
to oxidative hemolysis after triggers like fava beans, infections, or certain drugs (e.g., sulfa,
antimalarials)
• Peripheral smear shows Heinz bodies (denatured hemoglobin precipitates inside RBCs) and bite
cells (from splenic removal of Heinz bodies)
• Stomatocytes are seen in hereditary stomatocytosis or liver disease
• Howell-Jolly bodies appear in asplenic or hyposplenic states (nuclear remnants)
• Target cells are seen in thalassemia, liver disease, or postsplenectomy
Clinical pearls:
• G6PD deficiency → episodic intravascular hemolysis, jaundice, hemoglobinuria
• Heinz bodies require supravital staining for visualization
• Avoid oxidant triggers, especially in high-risk populations (e.g., Mediterranean, African, Asian
males)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 963
☆ Hematology: Hemolytic Anemias
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 963

stem:
A patient with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is started on regular ibuprofen for worsening
arthritis. She later develops a severe headache and vomiting, prompting referral. What is the most
likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Ibuprofen and other NSAIDs can trigger drug-induced aseptic meningitis, especially
in autoimmune conditions like SLE
• Presents with headache, photophobia, vomiting, and neck stiffness, typically without fever or
infection
• CSF findings: pleocytosis, ↑ protein, normal glucose, negative cultures
• CNS lupus can present with seizures or psychosis but not classically with isolated meningeal
symptoms
• PRES typically occurs in hypertensive or nephritic states and includes visual symptoms +
seizures
• Condition resolves after NSAID withdrawal
Clinical pearls:
• Always suspect NSAID-induced aseptic meningitis in SLE + new neurologic symptoms
• Discontinuation of the drug leads to rapid improvement
• Important to distinguish from CNS lupus, which requires immunosuppression
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 764–765
☆ Rheumatology: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 764–765

stem:
A child presents with recurrent episodes of fever, oral ulcers, exudative tonsillitis, and cervical
lymphadenopathy every 6 weeks for the past 9 months. Between episodes, the child is completely
well. Cultures are consistently negative. Labs during episodes show elevated ESR and CRP. What is the
most definitive treatment?
explanation:
• This is classic PFAPA syndrome (Periodic Fever, Aphthous stomatitis, Pharyngitis, and Adenitis)
• PFAPA typically affects preschool and school-age children with clockwork-like periodic febrile
episodes
• Diagnosis is clinical: regular intervals of high fever + at least one of the 3 (aphthous ulcers,
pharyngitis, adenitis), and exclusion of infections
•
•
•
•
Cultures are negative, and child is well between episodes
Tonsillectomy is the only curative option proven to eliminate recurrences
Steroids (e.g., prednisone single dose) may abort episodes but do not prevent recurrence
Naproxen and cimetidine have been tried, but are not curative
Clinical pearls:
• PFAPA is the most common periodic fever syndrome in children
• Age <5 years, clockwork regular fevers, normal growth, and well appearance between attacks
strongly favor PFAPA
• Labs: ↑ ESR/CRP during episodes; normal between
• Fever episodes are predictable, with return to baseline health between attacks
• Tonsillectomy can result in permanent resolution in most cases
• Do not confuse with cyclic neutropenia or monogenic periodic fever syndromes
Differentials of Periodic Fever in Pediatrics:
- PFAPA: Clockwork fevers every 3–6 weeks, aphthous ulcers,
pharyngitis, cervical adenitis, well between episodes, steroidresponsive
- Cyclic neutropenia: Fever every 21 days, aphthous ulcers,
pharyngitis, ANC <500 during episodes, serial CBC diagnostic
- FMF (Familial Mediterranean Fever): Fever + serositis
(abdomen/chest), arthritis, Middle Eastern descent, risk of
amyloidosis
- HIDS (Hyper-IgD Syndrome): Early infancy, fever + rash + diarrhea +
lymphadenopathy, ↑ serum IgD
- TRAPS: Prolonged fever episodes, migratory muscle pain, rash, TNF
receptor mutation, poor steroid response
- Recurrent infections: Irregular fever, positive cultures, not periodic,
usually due to common viral or bacterial causes
- CVID/Immunodeficiency: Recurrent sinopulmonary infections,
chronic diarrhea, poor growth, low immunoglobulins
- Leukemia/Lymphoma: Prolonged fevers, weight loss, night sweats,
pallor, hepatosplenomegaly, abnormal CBC
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics,
7th ed., p. 282–283; Pocket Pediatrics 2024
☆ Rheumatology: Periodic Fever Syndromes
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics,

stem:
A young girl with previously normal development presents with a history of progressive
neurodevelopmental decline. She developed speech delay, followed by loss of purposeful hand
use, and began to show repetitive hand-wringing movements. Genetic testing is pending. What is
typically the earliest sign in this disorder?
explanation:
• This clinical picture is consistent with Rett syndrome, an X-linked dominant neurodevelopmental
disorder affecting mostly girls, caused by MECP2 gene mutations
• Occurs due to due to arrested brain development ≠ neurodegeneration
• Head circumference deceleration is often the earliest objective sign, typically between 3–6
months of age, even before clear regression of motor or speech milestones
• Speech delay, loss of purposeful hand skills, and hand-wringing (stereotypies) appear later (618 months)
• Motor regression follows the early stagnation phase
• Diagnosis is clinical and confirmed with MECP2 genetic testing
• Prognosis: There is not enough data regarding life expectancy beyond the age of 40, as long-term
studies are not available and the disease is fairly rare
Clinical pearls:
• Always suspect Rett syndrome in girls with normal early
development who show neuroregression + stereotypic
hand movements
• Key early marker: slowing head growth trajectory
• Later features: seizures, gait abnormalities, autistic
features, and breathing irregularities
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 135
☆ Neurology: Neurodevelopmental Disorders
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 135

stem:
A child is able to stack 4 blocks, run away from parents, and throw a ball without falling
backward. Growth records show a height velocity of 12 cm/year. What is the most likely age
of this child?
explanation:
• At 18 months, a child typically:
o Builds a 3–4 block tower
o Runs steadily
o Throws a ball while standing
o Demonstrates growing independence (e.g., running away from caregiver)
• 12–15 months: running is just beginning, stacking is 1–2 blocks
• 24 months: child stacks 6 blocks, copies a line, and walks up/down stairs holding rail
• Growth velocity of 12 cm/year is appropriate for 1st – 2nd year of life
Clinical pearls:
• 18 months = key transition point in both gross motor and fine motor skills
• Also expected: points to body parts, scribbles, follows 1-step commands
• Use milestones + growth pattern for age estimation in board questions
📚 Source: Osama Naga – Developmental Milestones; Nelson – Growth & Development Tables
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: Developmental Milestones
source: Osama Naga – Developmental Milestones; Nelson – Growth & Development Tables

stem:
A young child presents with pendular nystagmus, head bobbing, and torticollis. What is the most
likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Spasmus nutans is a benign neurological condition typically seen in infants 4–12 months old
• Triad includes:
o Pendular (low-amplitude, high-frequency) nystagmus, often asymmetric or disconjugate
o Head bobbing (nodding or jerking)
o Torticollis (head tilt)
• It is self-limited, resolving by age 2–3 years
• Requires observation and reassurance if classic presentation
• Examples of Spasmus nutans triad: https://youtu.be/ljnwpW1Ul10?si=jJ19XP0S1IX-6fPJ,
https://youtu.be/J77RgASz3yk?si=NgAJ7iFle4LHUih7
• Cerebellar ataxia presents with gait imbalance, wide-based stance, and not typically with head
bobbing or isolated nystagmus
• Myoclonic seizures cause sudden jerks, not sustained head movements or nystagmus
• Infantile spasms involve symmetric flexor/extensor spasms, EEG shows hypsarrhythmia
Clinical pearls:
• Always consider chiasmal/hypothalamic tumors in differential — order MRI brain if atypical
features, asymmetric nystagmus, vision loss, poor development, age > 2 yrs, worsening symptoms
• Ensure normal development and eye exam to rule out other causes!
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 259
☆ Neurology: Movement Disorders
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 259

stem:
A toddler has episodes of temper tantrums followed by breath-holding spells, during which
he turns blue and loses muscle tone for about 1 minute before recovering. His mother is
concerned and requests pharmacological treatment. The physician recommends behavioral
strategies but also wants to reduce episode frequency. What pharmacologic agent should be
added?
explanation:
• This is a classic case of cyanotic breath-holding spells, commonly triggered by crying or
•
•
•
•
•
frustration in toddlers
The child briefly stops breathing, turns blue, may have loss of tone or consciousness, and
then recovers spontaneously
Iron deficiency is strongly associated with increased frequency and severity of breathholding spells
Iron supplementation has been shown to reduce the number and intensity of episodes,
even in children without anemia
Risperidone and diazepam are not appropriate for this benign, self-limiting condition
Vitamin D has no role in breath-holding spell management
Clinical pearls:
• Breath-holding spells are benign, peak at 1–2 years, and resolve by age 5
• Two types: cyanotic (most common) and pallid (vagal reflex-mediated)
• Always check CBC and ferritin; treat iron deficiency if found
• Reassure parents — no long-term neurologic consequences
• Avoid overreaction and use positive behavioral reinforcement
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 100, 153
☆ Neurology: Paroxysmal Events in Childhood
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 100, 153

stem:
A 2-week-old infant presents with a rectal temperature of 38.3°C but is otherwise well-appearing,
feeding well, and with no focal findings on exam. What is the most appropriate next step in
management?

options.label: A
options.text: Full septic workup & start empiric antibiotics

answer: A. Full septic workup & start empiric antibiotics
explanation:
• Any febrile infant ≤28 days is considered high risk for serious bacterial infection (SBI),
including sepsis, meningitis, and UTI, regardless of appearance
• Standard of care is:
o Full sepsis evaluation: CBC, CRP, blood culture, urine culture (via catheter or
suprapubic aspiration), and lumbar puncture
o Start empiric IV antibiotics: typically ampicillin + gentamicin or cefotaxime
 prevents delay in management of life-threatening infection
o Admit to hospital for monitoring and culture results
• Discharge without workup is unsafe and not recommended for infants <28 days
• Risk stratification tools (e.g., Rochester, Philadelphia criteria) apply only to older infants (>28
days)
Latest AAP Guidelines (2021):
Evaluation and Management of Well-Appearing Febrile Infants 8 to 60 Days Old
★ Neonatal Sepsis
• Systemic illness associated with infectious source
• Overall incidence of 1-5 per 1,000 births
o In VLBW infants, rate of incidence of early sepsis is
2% and late sepsis is 25%
• Mortality rate is up to 25% (higher with prematurity)
• Types:
o Early Onset: within 1st 7 d (1st 3 d if hospitalized since
birth)
 Risk factors: prematurity, LBW, PPROM, ROM ≥18 hrs, GBS +ve mother, maternal
fever, fetal distress, chorioamnionitis, meconium fluid, maternal or fetal
tachycardia, preterm labor
 Can use early onset sepsis risk score in ≥34 weeks to guide for NICU
admission/empiric Abx
 Most common: GBS (Step agalactiae), E.coli, Listeria, Staph, Strep pneumo,
Enterococcus, Haemophilus
o Late Onset: after 7 d of life
 Risk factors: indwelling catheters, intubation, prematurity, critical illness.
 Most common: CoNS, gram-negative rods, Staph aureus, GBS, fungal
• Clinical presentation: temp instability, irritability, lethargy, poor perfusion, feeding intolerance,
respiratory distress, dyspnea (e.g., expiratory grunting), apnea (more common in preterm infants)
o ↓ temp, perfusion, BG, BP = late signs
o pH (metabolic acidosis)
o ↑ RR/HR
o Specific symptoms:
 Neonatal meningitis
• Often no signs of meningism
• Early phase: general symptoms, vomiting
• Late phase: bulging fontanelles, shrill crying, seizures, stupor
 Neonatal pneumonia
• Tachypnea with intercostal/sternal retractions and nasal flaring
• Reduced oxygen saturation with cyanosis
• Diagnosis:
o Laboratory: blood culture ± UA/UCx ± LP
o Other studies CBC, CRP, abdomen Xray, CXR may be indicated depending on clinical
presentation.
•
•
Management:
o Empiric Abx: Ampicillin + Gentamycin, broaden if severe clinical illness, use local
resistance guidelines.
o Modify/stop Abx based on culture data.
Protective Factors:
o Adequate IAP (≥4 hours IV penicillin or ampicillin)
o Term gestation
o Asymptomatic neonate
o No rupture of membranes or fever
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 798–799; Pocket Pediatrics; AAP Guidelines
☆ NICU: Neonatal Sepsis & Fever in Infants
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 798–799; Pocket Pediatrics; AAP Guidelines

stem:
A child presents with a scaly, pruritic plaque on the palm of his hand. His parent reports that
he recently began playing frequently with homemade slime without wearing gloves. An image of
the lesion is provided.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Irritant contact dermatitis is common in children exposed to chemicals or allergens in
substances like slime, which often contains borax, fragrances, or preservatives
• Characteristically presents with localized, scaly, itchy plaques — often on hands, wrists, or
areas of contact
• Atopic dermatitis typically involves flexural surfaces and is chronic, relapsing, and often
associated with personal/family history of atopy
• Scabies presents with intense nocturnal pruritus, usually with burrows, vesicles, or papules
in interdigital spaces, not a single scaly plaque
• Seborrheic dermatitis affects scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, and behind ears — not usually
isolated to the palm
Condition
Appearance
Contact
Dermatitis
Location
Key Features
Clues for Dx
Hands, wrists, or
area of contact
Exposure to slime, chemicals, soaps, nickel,
plants
Localized, often with a known
irritant or allergen
→ Tx removal of trigger, topical
steroids (low-potency for face,
medium for body), emollients
Infants: Cheeks,
scalp, extensor
surfaces
Children: Flexural
areas
• Irritant type: Non-immune, occurs
immediately after exposure; common
with saliva, soaps, diapers; ill-defined
erythema and scaling.
• Allergic type: Type IV hypersensitivity,
appears 24–72 hrs after exposure; caused
by nickel, fragrances, rubber; welldemarcated, vesicular or lichenified rash.
• Pruritus must be present for diagnosis,
chronic or relapsing course
• Family history of atopy
• Xerosis (dry skin), may worsen in winter
• Denni-Morgan lines, allergic
shiners, hyperlinear palms
Erythematous, scaly
plaques, may vesiculate
Atopic
Dermatitis
(Eczema)
Pruritic, chronic relapsing rash
with age-specific distribution,
dry skin, and family history of
atopy.
→ Tx emollients + topical
Dry, scaly, excoriated
patches, lichenification
Scabies
(antecubital,
popliteal fossae)
• Secondary infections (Staph aureus →
impetiginization)
• Food triggers: more common <2 years
corticosteroids; calcineurin
inhibitors, phototherapy if
severe/refractory
Interdigital
spaces, wrists,
waist, axillae,
genitals
• Severe nocturnal pruritus
• Family members affected
• Maculopapular rash with linear tracks
affecting the interdigital spaces
Burrows, intense itch, treat all
contacts
→ Tx topical permethrin
Small vesicles,
papules, burrows
Seborrheic
Dermatitis
Scalp, eyebrows,
nasolabial folds,
ears, diaper area
Greasy, yellowish scales
over erythematous base
Infancy: “cradle cap”
Non-itchy, greasy appearance,
well-appearing infant
Older children: mild facial rash
• Onset typically in first weeks of life
• Self-limited; often resolves by 12 mo
→ Tx Mild: Baby shampoo,
emollients, gentle brushing.
→ Tx Moderate-severe: Topical
antifungals (ketoconazole), lowpotency steroids (hydrocort 1%)
Clinical pearls:
• Homemade slime is a rising trigger for irritant and allergic contact dermatitis in school-aged
children
• Management includes avoiding the trigger, using topical corticosteroids, and applying
emollients
• Always take a detailed exposure history in new-onset localized rashes
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 848–849
☆ Dermatology: Contact and Irritant Dermatitis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 848–849

stem:
A neonate develops direct hyperbilirubinemia at 10 hours of life. No family history or ABO
incompatibility. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Direct (conjugated) hyperbilirubinemia in the first day of life is always pathologic and requires
urgent investigation.
• Breast milk jaundice causes indirect hyperbilirubinemia and begins after day 3–5, never in
the first 10 hours.
• Hypothyroidism can cause:
o Prolonged jaundice
o Both unconjugated and conjugated
hyperbilirubinemia
o Often subtle signs: hypotonia, constipation,
macroglossia
• Hereditary spherocytosis and ABO incompatibility would
cause hemolytic, indirect hyperbilirubinemia.
★ Neonatal Jaundice
📚 Source: Nelson Neonatology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 65–68.
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Neonatal Jaundice
source: Nelson Neonatology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 65–68.

stem:
An unvaccinated girl living on a farm is found unresponsive. She has a recent history of upper
respiratory infection and diarrhea, and on examination, she is icteric. Her family declined vaccination.
What is the most likely positive test result?
explanation:
• Hepatitis A virus (HAV) is fecal-oral transmitted, often associated with poor sanitation, farm
settings, and unvaccinated individuals
• It is the most common subtype of viral hepatitis in children
o In children age <6, HAV infection is most often (>80%) silent or subclinical ("anicteric,” with
no jaundice observed).
Patients typically present acutely with a prodrome of fever, malaise, anorexia, nausea/vomiting,
diarrhea and right upper quadrant abdominal pain.
o Symptoms can include an aversion to smoking (for unclear reasons).
o After several days (up to 1 week), prodromal symptoms lessen as patients develop signs of
cholestasis such as jaundice, pruritus, dark-colored urine (due to increased conjugated
bilirubin levels), and acholic stool (lacks bilirubin pigment).
o Hepatomegaly is commonly seen.
• Serum aminotransferases spike early in the illness, followed by increases in bilirubin and alkaline
phosphatase.
• IgM anti-HAV confirms acute infection – remember “A is for Acute”
• Liver failure and death due to HAV are extremely rare (<0.5% of cases)
• Self-limiting disease and does not progress to chronic hepatitis, cirrhosis, or hepatocellular
carcinoma.
• Treatment is largely supportive, with complete recovery expected in 3-6 weeks.
•
Serology of hepatitis A virus (HAV) infection:
-
-
Anti-HAV IgM antibodies are detectable ∼ 5–10 days after infection (5–10 days
before clinical symptoms develop), with peak levels ∼ 1 month after infection.
Levels typically become undetectable within 3–6 months post infection.
Assessing for the presence of anti-HAV IgM antibodies is the standard diagnostic
test for active hepatitis A.
During HAV infection, anti-HAV IgG antibodies are typically detectable when
clinical symptoms develop and subsequently persist indefinitely. They are also
produced in response to vaccination and are a marker of immunity to reinfection.
HAV RNA can be detected in stool and serum samples in patients with hepatitis
A. This test is not routinely used but can be helpful during outbreaks, as HAV RNA
can be detected as early as the first week after infection.
Clinical pearls:
• HAV is a leading cause of acute hepatitis in children, especially in low-resource or
unvaccinated populations
• Hepatitis A IgM is the diagnostic marker for acute infection
• Children often recover, but can present with acute liver failure (encephalopathy, coagulopathy)
• Hepatitis B is bloodborne and vertical; unlikely to cause sudden encephalopathy in unvaccinated
child without perinatal risk
• Hepatitis C is also bloodborne, and rarely causes acute liver failure in children
• Hepatitis E is more common in pregnant women, adults, or endemic areas — not typical in
children in most regions
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 386–388
☆ Infectious Diseases: Viral Hepatitis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 386–388

stem:
A 10-year-old boy presents with fever, cough, and weight loss. He has a history of close contact
with his grandmother, who has had chronic cough, hemoptysis, and weight loss for 3 months.
His PPD test is 12 mm. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic test?
answer: D. Sputum AFB stain and culture
explanation:
• This child has clinical signs and epidemiologic risk for active pulmonary tuberculosis
• Diagnosis is by Sputum AFB smear/culture if age ≥10 or able to expectorate
• Gastric aspiration for AFB is the diagnostic test of choice in children <10 years who cannot
expectorate sputum
• Quantiferon-TB is an interferon gamma release assay (IGRA) useful for latent TB, not for active
disease diagnosis
• CT chest is not first-line; chest X-ray precedes advanced imaging
• Sputum AFB would be ideal if child can produce sputum, but most under 10 cannot
★ Pediatric Tuberculosis:
• Transmission & Risk Factors
o Spread via airborne droplets, usually from a household contact
o Infants and children <5 yrs are at highest risk for progression to active disease
• TB Testing: PPD vs IGRA
o PPD (Mantoux)
 Positive if ≥10 mm in high-risk children (e.g., close contacts, age <4, foreign-born)
 Positive if ≥5 mm in immunocompromised, close contacts, or abnormal CXR
o
IGRA (Quantiferon)
•
•
•
•
•
 Preferred in BCG-vaccinated children ≥2 years
 Not reliable in <2 years
 Does not distinguish active vs latent TB
Clinical Features of Active TB
o Pulmonary TB
 Chronic cough, weight loss, fever, fatigue
 Hilar lymphadenopathy, pleural effusion on imaging
o Extrapulmonary TB
 CNS (meningitis), miliary TB, lymph nodes, bones (Pott
disease)
 Infants and toddlers are at high risk for disseminated TB
Diagnosis of Active TB
o Start with chest X-ray
o Sputum AFB smear/culture if age ≥10 or able to expectorate
o Gastric aspirates x3 if <10 years old
o Consider CSF or lymph node biopsy if extrapulmonary TB
o Always screen for HIV
Latent TB Infection (LTBI)
o Defined as positive PPD or IGRA, no symptoms, normal CXR
o Treatment options
 INH x 9 months
 Rifampin x 4 months
 INH + Rifapentine weekly x 3 months (if age >2 yrs, HIVnegative)
Treatment of Active TB
o RIPE for 2 months: Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol
o Then INH + Rifampin for 4 months
o Total treatment: 6 months, extend to 9–12 months for CNS or bone TB
o Use Directly Observed Therapy (DOT) to ensure adherence
Mothers with TB can breastfeed using expressed milk only
Clinical pearls:
• PPD ≥5 mm is positive in children with TB contacts
• In suspected active TB in young children, perform:
o Chest X-ray
•
o Gastric aspirates x 3 for AFB stain and culture
o Start empiric treatment if high suspicion
Quantiferon has limited use in active disease workup in young children <2 years
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 863–865; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 414; AMBOSS.
☆ Infectious Diseases: Tuberculosis
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 863–865; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 414; AMBOSS.

stem:
A 3-year-old girl is brought to clinic by her mother, who is not concerned about development. The
child said her first word at 11 months and now speaks in sentences. However, she often says “m-mm-mummy, mummy, mummy” and uses filler sounds like "uh" between sentences. She also
has motor tics. What is the next best step?
explanation:
• This is a classic case of developmental (physiologic) stuttering, which is common between 2–5
years of age, especially during language explosion
• Features such as repetition of initial syllables, interjections ("uh"), and lack of awareness or
frustration are typical
• No red flags (e.g., word avoidance, facial grimacing, lasting >6 months, worsening, FHx of
speech disorders)
• The concurrent presence of transient motor tics does not mandate immediate referral if:
o The tics are not persistent beyond 1 year
o There is no evidence of impairment, distress, or progression
o There is no family history of Tourette syndrome or complex neuropsychiatric symptoms
• Transient motor tics may co-occur in this age group and are often benign
