• Reassurance and monitoring are appropriate if the child is otherwise developing normally
• No immediate need for audiology, developmental testing, or psychiatry unless concerns arise
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 113; Osama Naga 3rd ed., p. 52
☆ Behavioural & Developmental Pediatrics: Speech and Language
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 113; Osama Naga 3rd ed., p. 52

stem:
A child who had previously been dry at night for several months has now started wetting the bed
again, only during sleep. What is the correct classification of this condition?
explanation:
• Secondary enuresis is defined as resumption of bedwetting after ≥6 months of dryness
• Because it occurs only at night, it is classified as secondary nocturnal enuresis
• Primary nocturnal enuresis = child has never been dry for ≥6 months
• Diurnal enuresis involves daytime wetting, not seen here
Clinical pearls:
• Always consider psychosocial stress, constipation, UTI, or sleep disorders in secondary enuresis
• Initial workup: urinalysis, assess for constipation, review history
• Management = address underlying cause + standard enuresis therapy
★ Nocturnal Enuresis:
• Definition
o Involuntary urination during sleep in child ≥5
years old
o Occurs ≥2 times/week for ≥3 months
• Types
o Primary: never dry for >6 months
o Secondary: recurrence after 6 months of
dryness
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
Causes
o Delayed bladder maturation (most common)
o Nocturnal polyuria (↓ ADH at night)
o Small functional bladder capacity
o Constipation
o Psychological stress (especially in secondary)
o Sleep arousal disorder
More common in boys (ratio 2:1).
Family history plays a significant role:
o One enuretic parent → 44% chance in child.
o Two enuretic parents → 77% chance in child.
Spontaneous remission occurs at a rate of 15% per year.
Red Flags
o Daytime incontinence, urgency, frequency
o Poor urinary stream, dribbling
o Constipation, UTIs
o Abnormal gait or lumbosacral findings
Workup
o History + physical exam
o Urinalysis (rule out UTI, diabetes)
o Assess for constipation
o No imaging/labs unless red flags
First-Line Management
o Reassure parents — usually self-limited
o Voiding before bed
o Fluid restriction in evening
o Positive reinforcement
o Treat constipation if present
o Most Effective Treatment
 Enuresis alarm
• Long-term cure, behaviorally conditioned
• Use nightly for 3–6 months
o Pharmacologic Therapy
 Desmopressin (DDAVP)
• Quick response, high relapse if stopped
• Use for >6 yrs or special events (sleepovers)
 Monitor for hyponatremia
 Oxybutynin: only if daytime symptoms or detrusor overactivity
Referral
o Failure of therapy after 6 months
o Non-monosymptomatic enuresis
o Neurologic or anatomical concern
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 551
☆ Nephrology: Nocturnal Enuresis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 551

stem:
A child with a known diagnosis of Tourette syndrome and comorbid ADHD is being started
on methylphenidate. What potential side effect must be discussed before initiating treatment?
explanation:
•
•
•
•
•
Methylphenidate, a CNS stimulant, is commonly used for ADHD
In children with Tourette syndrome, stimulants may exacerbate motor or vocal tics, although
this risk is less than previously thought
If tics worsen, alternatives include non-stimulants (e.g., atomoxetine, guanfacine, or clonidine)
Other side effects: appetite suppression, insomnia, emotional lability
Depression and psychosis are rare but possible at high doses
Clinical pearls:
• Mild tics are not a contraindication to stimulant use
• Start with low dose, monitor for tic exacerbation
• Consider behavioral therapy or alpha-agonists if tics are prominent
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 118–119
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: ADHD and Tic Disorders
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 118–119

stem:
A school-aged child is suspected of having attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) due to
poor concentration and behavior issues. What is the most appropriate method to confirm the
diagnosis?

options.label: B
options.text: Asking teacher and parents about symptoms

options.label: C
options.text: Educational assessment by a different teacher who has never taught the child

answer: B. Asking teacher and parents about symptoms
explanation:
• ADHD is a clinical diagnosis based on history and behavioral observation in ≥2 settings (home
and school)
• Input from teachers and parents using validated questionnaires (e.g., Vanderbilt or Conners
scales) is essential
• A trial of medication is never diagnostic
• An assessment from a teacher who has not worked with the child lacks relevance
• IQ testing and neurologic exams may be supportive but are not required for diagnosis
Clinical pearls:
• ADHD diagnosis = persistent inattention, hyperactivity/impulsivity >6 months
• Symptoms must cause functional impairment in ≥2 settings
• Use Vanderbilt scales or Conners rating scales for standardized assessment
• Rule out hearing/vision problems, learning disorders, or emotional causes
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 116–118
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: ADHD Diagnosis and Management
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 116–118

stem:
A patient with a history of recurrent Staphylococcus aureus abscesses now presents with severe
pneumonia and empyema. Blood cultures are negative, but pleural fluid grows Serratia. What is the
most appropriate initial immunologic test?
explanation:
• This presentation is classic for Chronic Granulomatous Disease (CGD)
o Recurrent abscesses, especially with catalase-positive organisms (e.g., Staph aureus,
Serratia, Burkholderia, Nocardia, Aspergillus)
o Red flag = Unusual organisms like Serratia in deep-seated infections (e.g., empyema)
• Oxidative burst test (e.g., dihydrorhodamine 123 assay, DHR) evaluates NADPH oxidase
function in neutrophils
• Nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) test is an older version of this assessment
• Immunoglobulin levels are used to assess humoral deficiencies (e.g., CVID)
• Lymphocyte subset analysis evaluates T and B cell counts, more useful in suspected
combined immunodeficiency
★ Chronic Granulomatous Disease:
• Pathophysiology
o Defect in NADPH oxidase → impaired respiratory
burst in neutrophils
o Can’t kill catalase-positive organisms →
recurrent granulomatous infections
• Inheritance: X-linked recessive (most common, M>F) or
autosomal recessive
• Classic Infections:
o Recurrent Staph aureus skin abscesses
o Serratia, Burkholderia, Nocardia, Aspergillus
o Pneumonia, empyema, osteomyelitis, hepatic
abscesses
o Perirectal and GI granulomas
• Red Flags
o Infections with catalase-positive organisms
o Negative blood cultures with unusual organisms in
abscesses (e.g., Serratia)
o Recurrent deep-tissue infections despite treatment
• Diagnosis
o Dihydrorhodamine (DHR) test → measures oxidative
burst (preferred test)
o Nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) test = older method
o Confirm with genetic testing
• Treatment
Mnemonic for catalase positive
organisms: “Cats need PLACESS to sit”
Pseudomonas
Listeria
Aspergillus
Candida
E. coli
Staphylococcus aureus
Serratia
o
o
o
Prophylaxis:
 TMP-SMX + itraconazole
 ± Interferon-γ to boost macrophage killing
Aggressive treatment of infections
Hematopoietic stem cell transplant = curative
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 421; AMBOSS; UWorld
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Primary Immunodeficiency Disorders, Chronic Granulomatous Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 421; AMBOSS; UWorld

stem: In a patient with anemia of chronic disease, what lab value is most likely to be elevated?
explanation:
• In anemia of chronic disease (ACD) or chronic inflammation, iron is sequestered in storage
sites
• Ferritin (an acute phase reactant) is typically elevated or normal due to inflammation, even if
iron is functionally unavailable
• TIBC and transferrin are usually low (liver decreases production during inflammation)
• Serum iron is low due to reduced absorption and trapping in macrophages
★ Anemia of Chronic Disease:
• Most common anemia in hospitalized and chronically ill children
• Associated with chronic infections, autoimmune diseases, malignancy, CKD
• Pathophysiology
o Inflammation → ↑ IL-6 → ↑ hepcidin → ↓ iron absorption and trapping of iron in
macrophages
o ↓ erythropoietin response, impaired erythropoiesis
• Lab Findings
o Low serum iron
o Low TIBC
o Normal or ↑ ferritin (due to inflammation)
o Low transferrin saturation
o Mild normocytic or (later stage) microcytic anemia
• Diagnosis
o Based on clinical context + characteristic iron panel
o Must exclude iron deficiency (low ferritin confirms IDA)
• Treatment
o Treat underlying disease
o Iron therapy not effective unless concurrent iron
deficiency
o Erythropoietin may be considered in CKD
•
Key distinguishing features from iron deficiency anemia
o Ferritin is low in IDA, high/normal in ACD
o TIBC is high in IDA, low in ACD
o Serum iron is low in both
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 474
☆ Hematology: Anemia of Chronic Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 474
topic: • Most common anemia in hospitalized and chronically ill children

stem:
A child presents with microcytic hypochromic anemia. You suspect iron deficiency. What is the
best single test to confirm the diagnosis?
explanation:
• Serum ferritin reflects total body iron stores and is the most reliable test to confirm iron
deficiency anemia (IDA)
• Low ferritin is diagnostic of iron deficiency, and it is the first laboratory parameter to drop
o Ferritin <12–15 ng/mL confirms IDA (cutoff may be higher in inflammatory states)
• Serum iron has high variability and low sensitivity
• TIBC and transferrin rise in IDA but are less specific
As iron deficiency develops, the following sequence can be seen:
1. Decreased bone marrow iron stores (ferritin and hemosiderin)
2. Decreased serum ferritin
3. Increased serum total iron-binding
capacity, reflecting increased transferrin
4. Decreased serum iron concentration
5. Decreased hemoglobin
6. Appearance of microcytic,
hypochromic RBCs (low mean
corpuscular volume [MCV])
★ Iron Deficiency Anemia (IDA):
• Most common nutritional deficiency in children
• Typically due to inadequate intake, rapid growth, blood loss, or low iron stores at birth (e.g.,
prematurity)
• Risk Factors
o Exclusive breastfeeding >6 months without iron supplementation
o Cow's milk before 12 months or >24 oz/day
o Prematurity, low birth weight
o Poor dietary intake in toddlers/adolescents
o GI blood loss (e.g., Meckel, IBD, parasitic infections)
• Clinical Features
o Often asymptomatic
o Pallor, fatigue, irritability, poor concentration
o Pica, systolic murmur if moderate-to-severe
• Lab Findings
o Microcytic hypochromic anemia
o ↑ RDW
o Low ferritin (best test to confirm)
o Low serum iron
o ↑ TIBC
•
•
•
o Low transferrin saturation
Screening Guidelines (AAP)
o Universal screening at 12 months with hemoglobin and risk assessment
o Earlier if high risk (e.g., prematurity, low birth weight, dietary)
Prevention
o Full-term infants: 1 mg/kg/day of iron starting at 4–6 months if breastfed
o Preterm infants: 2 mg/kg/day of iron by 1 month of age until 12 months
o Limit cow's milk <24 oz/day after 1 year
Treatment
o Elemental iron 3–6 mg/kg/day divided BID for 3 months
 Note that iron supplements can cause constipation and black tarry stools
o Recheck reticulocyte count in 1 week, Hb in 4 weeks
o Continue treatment for 2–3 months after normalization to replete stores
Clinical pearls:
• Always interpret ferritin in context of inflammation — it is an acute phase reactant
• If ferritin is normal or high but suspicion is strong, check CRP/ESR and consider trial of iron
therapy
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 468–470; UWorld; AMBOSS.
☆ Hematology: Iron Deficiency Anemia
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 468–470; UWorld; AMBOSS.

stem:
A teenage girl is brought to clinic after an episode of hematemesis with bright red blood. She has a
history of preoccupation with body image, and her mother reports that she frequently goes to the
bathroom immediately after meals. On exam, she has dental enamel erosion and is pale. Labs
show microcytic anemia with low ferritin. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• This is a classic case of bulimia nervosa with recurrent self-induced vomiting
• Dental enamel erosion and iron deficiency anemia support chronic vomiting
• Mallory-Weiss syndrome is a mucosal tear at the gastroesophageal junction caused by
repeated retching or vomiting, leading to hematemesis with fresh blood
• Peptic ulcer can cause GI bleeding, but less common in this context and associated
with melena or occult blood, not fresh hematemesis
• Aspiration causes respiratory symptoms, not GI bleeding
• Esophagitis (e.g., reflux-related) may cause bleeding but usually not sudden hematemesis after
vomiting
Clinical pearls:
• In adolescent girls with hematemesis + enamel erosion, always consider eating disorder with
self-induced vomiting
• Mallory-Weiss tears present with sudden, painless hematemesis after retching
• IDA suggests chronic blood loss or nutritional deficiency
• EGD, within 24 hours of bleeding, shows one or more linear bleeding lesions at or just above GE
junction.
• Lesions heal quickly after vomiting and retching stops and may not be visualized if EGD is
delayed by 96 hours.
• Medical management is supportive.
• Interventional endoscopy is rarely necessary (e.g., hemoclips).
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 182–183
☆ Gastroenterology / Adolescent Medicine: Eating Disorders & Upper GI Bleeding
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 182–183

stem:
A 10-year-old boy presents with acute onset of joint pain, swelling, fever, and refusal to bear weight.
Arthrocentesis is performed. Which synovial fluid result is most consistent with a diagnosis of septic
arthritis?
explanation:
• Septic arthritis is confirmed by purulent synovial fluid with WBC >50,000/mm³ and PMN
predominance >75–90%
• WBC counts can be as high as 100,000–250,000/mm³ in bacterial arthritis
• PMN >90% suggests acute bacterial infection
• Lower WBC and PMN percentages are seen in viral arthritis, transient synovitis,
or inflammatory arthritis
★ Septic Arthritis in Pediatrics:
• Acute bacterial infection of the joint space, most common in young children and infants
o typically via hematogenous spread
• Common Pathogens
o <2 months: Group B Strep, E. coli, S. aureus
o 2 months–5 years: S. aureus (MSSA/MRSA), Kingella kingae, Strep pneumo
o >5 years: S. aureus, GAS
• Sexually active adolescents: Neisseria gonorrhoeae
• Clinical Presentation
o Acute onset of fever + joint pain/swelling + refusal to bear weight or move joint
o Most commonly affects hip, knee, or ankle
•
•
•
•
o May appear toxic or systemically ill
o In infants: pseudoparalysis, irritability, decreased movement of limb
Diagnosis
o Joint aspiration is key:
 WBC >50,000/mm³, PMN >75–90%
 Low glucose, high protein
 Gram stain and culture
o Elevated CRP, ESR, and WBC
o Ultrasound to detect joint effusion
o Blood cultures positive in ~50%
Management
o Urgent joint drainage (arthrocentesis or surgical)
o Empiric IV antibiotics
 <2 months: vancomycin + cefotaxime
 >2 months: vancomycin ± ceftriaxone (for Kingella/Strep)
o Adjust based on culture results
o Duration: 3–4 weeks total, start IV then switch to oral when improving
Complications
o Joint destruction, growth plate damage, chronic arthritis, osteomyelitis
o Early treatment prevents long-term sequelae
Differential Diagnosis
o Transient synovitis, JIA, trauma, reactive arthritis, Lyme arthritis (if endemic)
Clinical pearls:
• Septic arthritis requires urgent drainage + IV antibiotics
• Always consider in child with hot, swollen joint + fever + refusal to move limb
• Order gram stain, culture, glucose, protein, and cell count on joint fluid
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 824
☆ Infectious Diseases: Septic Arthritis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 824
topic: • Acute bacterial infection of the joint space, most common in young children and infants

stem:
A child is diagnosed with septic arthritis. He had a history of bloody diarrhea 5 days prior to
symptom onset. What is the most likely causative organism?
explanation:
• Salmonella can cause bacteremia following gastroenteritis, particularly bloody diarrhea
(exudative/inflammatory diarrhea), and can seed joints, leading to septic arthritis
• This is especially seen in infants, young children, and immunocompromised hosts (e.g., sickle
cell disease)
• E. coli causes GI infections but is not a common cause of septic arthritis
• Rotavirus causes watery diarrhea, not invasive disease or arthritis
•
Giardia causes non-bloody diarrhea and does not lead to bacteremia or joint involvement
★ Salmonella:
• Nontyphoidal Salmonella = most common in pediatrics
o Source: undercooked eggs/poultry, reptiles
o Causes bloody diarrhea, fever, abdominal pain
o Complications: bacteremia, osteomyelitis (sickle cell), septic arthritis (sickle cell,
immunocompromised pts, recent GE), meningitis in infants
• Typhoidal Salmonella (S. typhi/paratyphi)
o Rare in U.S.; endemic in developing countries
o Features: prolonged fever, bradycardia (Faget sign), rose spots, hepatosplenomegaly
• Diagnosis
o Stool culture for diarrhea
o Blood/bone/joint culture if invasive
• Treatment
o No antibiotics for mild
gastroenteritis in healthy child
o Give antibiotics if <3 months,
immunocompromised, or invasive
disease
o Drugs: ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin
• Buzzwords:
o Sickle cell + bone/joint infection →
Salmonella
o Bloody diarrhea + later arthritis → post-Salmonella septic arthritis
★ Overview of Acute Infectious Diarrhea:
• Definition: Sudden onset of ≥3 loose or watery stools/day, lasting <14 days
• Most Common Cause
o Viral > bacterial > parasitic
o Most common: Rotavirus, Norovirus
• Red Flags (Suggest Bacterial/Parasitic)
o Bloody stools, high fever, toxic appearance
o Severe abdominal pain, dehydration
o History of travel, animal contact, or antibiotic use
• Common Etiologies
o Rotavirus: watery diarrhea, vomiting, fever (infants
<2 yrs)
o Norovirus: outbreaks, vomiting + diarrhea (older
children, schools)
o Salmonella: bloody or watery diarrhea, fever,
reptile exposure
o Shigella: high fever, seizures, bloody stools, risk of HUS
o Campylobacter: bloody diarrhea, abdominal cramps, undercooked poultry
o EHEC (O157:H7): bloody diarrhea, NO fever, risk of HUS
o Giardia: foul-smelling, greasy stools, chronic course, daycare/travel
• Workup Indicated If
o Bloody diarrhea
o High fever
o Severe dehydration
o Prolonged symptoms (>7 days)
o Very young age or immunocompromised
• Tests: stool culture, fecal leukocytes, ova/parasite,
C. diff if recent antibiotics
• Management
o Mainstay = oral rehydration therapy (ORS)
Continue age-appropriate diet
Avoid antidiarrheals
Antibiotics only if:
 Proven/probable Shigella, Campylobacter, severe Salmonella (young,
immunocompromised)
 Avoid in EHEC → ↑ risk of HUS
Prevention
o Rotavirus vaccine (oral, given at 2, 4, 6 months)
o Hand hygiene, safe food/water
o
o
o
•
Clinical pearls:
• Always consider Salmonella septic arthritis in children with recent bloody diarrhea and joint
pain/swelling
• In sickle cell patients, Salmonella is a key cause of both osteomyelitis and septic arthritis
• Blood and stool cultures may both be needed
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 824–826
☆ Infectious Diseases: Septic Arthritis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 824–826
topic: • Nontyphoidal Salmonella = most common in pediatrics

stem:
A child with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is now experiencing increased hyperactivity,
aggression, and sleep disturbance. The family requests medical treatment to help manage these
behaviors. What is the most appropriate pharmacologic option?
explanation:
• Risperidone and aripiprazole are FDA-approved for irritability, aggression, and self-injurious
behavior in children with ASD (age 5–16)
• Shown to reduce agitation, tantrums, mood lability, and aggressive outbursts
• Monitor for side effects: weight gain, sedation, hyperprolactinemia, extrapyramidal symptoms
• Lamotrigine is an anticonvulsant/mood stabilizer, not first-line for aggression in autism
• Phenobarbital is used for seizures; inappropriate for behavioral management
• Fluoxetine may help repetitive behaviors/anxiety, not acute aggression
★ Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD):
• Key Developmental Clues:
o No babbling by 12 months
o No pointing or single words by 16 months
o No 2-word phrases by 24 months
o Regression in speech or social skills
at any age
o Lack of pretend play
• Screening & Diagnosis:
o Screen all children at 18 & 24
months (AAP guidelines)
o Tool: M-CHAT-R/F (Modified Checklist
for Autism in Toddlers)
o Gold standard Dx: ADIR / ADOS (by
developmental pediatrician or
psychologist)
• Management:
o Early behavioral therapy (ABA) = most
Board Buzzwords for Autism:
effective
• No response to name
• Limited joint attention
o Speech & occupational therapy
• Echoing speech (echolalia)
o Risperidone / aripiprazole → for severe
• Sensory sensitivity (e.g., avoids textures/sounds)
aggression/irritability (USFDA approved)
•
Clinical pearls:
o First-line behavioral treatment for ASD = structured therapies (ABA, behavioral therapy)
o Consider medication only if behavioral strategies are insufficient and symptoms impair
function
o Aripiprazole is another FDA-approved option for similar indications
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 114–115
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: Autism
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 114–115

stem:
A child is able to undress independently, use a spoon without spilling, and speak in a 3-word
sentence that includes a noun, adjective, and verb. What is the most likely developmental age?
explanation:
• At 30 months, children typically:
o Undress independently
o Feed self well with a spoon
o Use 3-word sentences with grammatical structure (noun + verb + adjective/adverb)
• 18–24 months: start combining 2–3 words but less complex structure
• 48 months (4 years): use complete sentences, understand colors, tell stories
• Developmental milestones are cumulative — language + motor function support 30-month level
Clinical pearls:
• Sentence complexity and fine motor self-help skills (e.g., undressing, using utensils) are key
clues to age
• 2-word combinations = 24 months
• 3+ words in structured sentence + independent dressing = 30 months
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 21–22; Osama Naga 3rd ed., Developmental
Milestones
☆ Developmental Pediatrics: Milestones
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 21–22; Osama Naga 3rd ed., Developmental

stem:
An 8-year-old girl presents with brown spotting and foul-smelling vaginal discharge for several
weeks. Exam reveals labial erythema, annular hymen, and no signs of trauma or abuse. Tanner
stage I–II, with no pubic or axillary hair. Underwear is noted to have discharge. What is the most likely
diagnosis?
explanation:
• Vaginal foreign body (often toilet paper) is a common cause of foul-smelling, brown or bloodstained discharge in prepubertal girls
• Typically presents with chronic spotting, no systemic symptoms, and normal external genital
exam with annular hymen intact
• Discharge may persist despite hygiene efforts
• Diagnosis often made by careful inspection or irrigation, sometimes requires exam under
anesthesia if symptoms persist
• Physiologic leukorrhea is clear or white, odorless, and occurs in Tanner stage II–III girls as part
of puberty
•
•
Precocious menarche is rare in isolation and would be associated with more advanced
secondary sexual characteristics
Group A Strep vaginitis typically causes acute erythema, itching, pain, and mucoid or purulent
discharge, not brown spotting for weeks
Clinical pearls:
• Always consider foreign body in prepubertal girl with chronic, foul-smelling vaginal discharge
• Evaluate in a non-threatening, reassuring manner
• If not visualized externally, may need referral to pediatric gynecology
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 733–734
☆ General Pediatrics: Vaginal Discharge in Prepubertal Girls
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 733–734

stem:
A girl with known depressive symptoms presents to the ER within 45 minutes of ingesting a large
quantity of amitriptyline. She is currently well-appearing and hemodynamically stable. What is the
most appropriate next step?

options.label: C
options.text: Observe and repeat ECG in 6 hours

explanation:
• Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose is potentially life-threatening, even in asymptomatic
patients
• Activated charcoal is effective within 1–2 hours of ingestion, especially if no
contraindications (alert, intact airway)
• TCAs cause cardiac toxicity (arrhythmias, QRS widening) and CNS effects (seizures, coma)
• Psychiatric consult is essential but not the first step — medical stabilization comes first
• Inducing emesis is contraindicated
•
TCAs have an inhibitory effect on multiple receptor types, thereby causing a variety of
symptoms in the case of overdose:
o Inhibition of CNS receptors can cause sedation (histamine [H1] receptors), seizures
(GABA A receptors), and agitation or delirium (central muscarinic acetylcholine receptors).
o Antagonism of central and peripheral muscarinic acetylcholine receptors results
in anticholinergic symptoms, including hyperthermia, dry and flushed skin, dilated
pupils, tachycardia, decreased bowel sounds, and urinary retention.
o Cardiovascular toxicity may lead to QRS widening with increased risk for ventricular
arrhythmia due to inhibition of cardiac fast sodium channels, and is the major cause of
mortality in TCA overdose.
o Hypotension can also occur due to blockade of alpha-1 adrenergic receptors.
Clinical pearls:
• After initial stabilization and charcoal: monitor EKG, look for QRS >100 ms, terminal R in aVR
• Treat TCA toxicity with sodium bicarbonate if QRS widening or hypotension
• Always admit for observation if any concerning features or significant ingestion
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1153–1154
☆ Emergency / Toxicology: Tricyclic Antidepressant Overdose
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1153–1154

stem:
A child with status epilepticus received IV diazepam followed by lorazepam to abort seizures
lasting 10 minutes. Seizures have stopped, but the patient now develops labored breathing and
oxygen desaturation to 70%. What is the next best step in management?
answer: C. Intubate and initiate mechanical ventilation
explanation:
• Benzodiazepines (especially multiple doses) can cause respiratory depression,
hypoventilation, and hypoxia
• This child has signs of acute respiratory failure: labored breathing, O₂ sat 70% → needs airway
protection and mechanical ventilation
• Flumazenil (Benzodiazepine antagonist) is contraindicated in seizure patients — may
precipitate refractory seizures
o Acts by competitively inhibiting the GABA-A receptor at the benzodiazepine binding site
o Used to reverse benzodiazepine sedation in controlled settings (e.g., procedural
sedation, overdose with known BZD use only in otherwise healthy child, no seizures)
• Naloxone is for opioid toxicity, not benzodiazepines
• Supplemental oxygen is not sufficient in setting of poor ventilation and falling O₂ sat
Clinical pearls:
• Always monitor for respiratory depression after IV benzodiazepines
• Flumazenil is never given in patients with known or suspected seizures
• Airway management takes priority after seizure control
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 272–273
☆ Neurology: Status Epilepticus – Post-treatment Complications
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 272–273

stem:
A patient post-lobectomy presents with a wound that appears infected, described as nasty and
possibly purulent. A clinical image is provided showing signs of deep infection. What is the most
appropriate next step?

options.label: C
options.text: Open wound, take cultures & start IV antibiotics

answer: C. Open wound, take cultures & start IV antibiotics
explanation:
• In a post-surgical wound infection, especially after a major procedure like lobectomy, it's
essential to:
o Open the wound to assess for abscess, necrosis, or dehiscence
o Obtain wound cultures to guide targeted therapy
o Start empiric IV antibiotics for broad coverage while
awaiting culture results
• Simply starting oral or even IV antibiotics without opening
the wound may miss abscess formation and delay healing
Clinical pearls:
• Surgical site infections (SSIs) post-thoracic surgery must
be treated aggressively
• Always ensure source control in wound infections
•
Use broad-spectrum IV antibiotics initially (e.g., vancomycin + cefepime), then narrow based on
cultures
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 848; Pediatric Surgery guidelines
☆ Infectious Diseases / Surgery: Wound Infections Post-Operative
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 848; Pediatric Surgery guidelines

stem:
A 7-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of fever, swelling, and
redness around the left eye. His mother reports he had a recent upper respiratory infection. On
examination, he has eyelid edema, erythema, proptosis, and restricted extraocular movements. His
temperature is 39°C. Visual acuity is decreased in the left eye. What is the most appropriate next step
in management?

options.label: B
options.text: Discharge with oral antibiotic and follow-up within 24 hours

explanation:
• Orbital cellulitis is a sight-threatening emergency involving the tissues posterior to the orbital
septum
• Classic signs: proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, pain with eye movement, decreased vision, fever
• Emergency due to risk of vision loss, abscess, and intracranial spread!
• Requires hospital admission and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics targeting Staph aureus
(including MRSA), Strep species, and anaerobes
• Imaging (contrast CT orbit) is often done to assess for abscess or sinus involvement
• Oral antibiotics or topical therapy are not appropriate and risk progression to vision loss or
intracranial spread
Clinical pearls:
• Differentiate from preseptal cellulitis, which has no proptosis, ophthalmoplegia or extraocular
movement limitation
o Oral Abx are appropriate for preseptal cellulitis only
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
MCC is complicated bacterial rhinosinusitis (ethmoid and maxillary)
Organisms: Streptococcus followed by staph aureus
Clinical diagnosis based on signs of post-septal involvement
Confirm with contrast-enhanced CT or MRI orbit/sinuses: orbital fat stranding, abscess, or sinus
involvement
Labs: CBC, blood cultures
Empiric IV therapy: vancomycin (MRSA) + ceftriaxone or cefotaxime (Strep, H. flu) ±
metronidazole (anaerobes if dental/sinus source) for 2-3 weeks
Consult ophthalmology and ENT
Surgical drainage if: abscess on imaging, worsening vision, no improvement on antibiotics within
24–48 hrs
Complications include subperiosteal abscess, orbital abscess, cavernous sinus thrombosis,
meningitis, and intracranial extension.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 264–265
☆ Infectious Diseases: Orbital Cellulitis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 264–265

stem:
A 2-year-old unvaccinated child presents with inspiratory stridor, barking cough, and is non-toxic
in appearance. What is the most likely causative organism?
explanation:
• This presentation is classic for viral croup (laryngotracheitis):
o Inspiratory stridor, barking cough, hoarseness, worse at night
o Non-toxic appearance is key
• Parainfluenza virus is the most common cause of croup, especially in ages 6 months to 3 years
• Hib causes epiglottitis, which presents with sudden onset, high fever, drooling, and toxic
appearance
• RSV typically causes bronchiolitis, not stridor
• Adenovirus can cause URI or pharyngitis but not classic croup
Clinical pearls:
• Croup = parainfluenza + stridor + barking cough + non-toxic
• Treat mild cases with oral dexamethasone; moderate/severe may need nebulized epinephrine
• Toxic + stridor + drooling → think epiglottitis (Hib) in unvaccinated
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 247–248
☆ Pulmonology: Croup vs Epiglottitis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 247–248

stem:
A child presents with recurrent episodes of staring, sometimes associated with lip
smacking and squinting. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Absence seizures present with brief staring spells, lasting <30 seconds, often multiple times per
day
• Duration is <15 seconds
• No post ictal phase
• Can have simple automatisms → lip smacking, eye fluttering, head nodding
• Can be induced by hyperventilation
• Absence seizures are characterized by momentary lapses in consciousness (~10 seconds)
associated with a classic 3-Hz spike-wave pattern on electroencephalogram.
•
Tx:
o
o
o
1st line = ethosuximide (Zarontin)
 Mechanism: blocks T-type calcium
channels in thalamic neurons
 Highly effective with fewer side
effects compared to alternatives.
2nd line or if associated with generalized
seizures = sodium valproate (Depakene)
3rd line = lamotrigine
Other options:
• Daydreaming is not associated with automatisms or interruption of activity when called
• Pseudoseizures (non-epileptic events) often involve dramatic movements and occur in older
children
• Myoclonic seizures involve sudden, brief muscle jerks — not staring or automatisms
Clinical pearls:
• Triggered by hyperventilation in clinic
• First-line treatment: ethosuximide
• Typical onset: age 4–10 years
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 271; UWorld.
☆ Neurology: Seizure Disorders – Absence Seizures
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 271; UWorld.

stem:
A child presents with chronic constipation, soiling of underwear, and a palpable abdominal
mass. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• This is classic for functional constipation with overflow fecal incontinence (encopresis)
o retentive encopresis is associated with functional constipation (80%)!
• The palpable mass is likely a large stool burden in the rectum or colon
• Soiling occurs due to liquid stool leaking around impacted feces
• Imaging is not needed when the diagnosis is clinically obvious
• Immediate treatment is disimpaction (e.g., high-dose PEG or enemas), followed by maintenance
laxatives
• Reassurance alone is inappropriate without addressing the underlying constipation
★ Pediatric Constipation:
• Definition
o <2 bowel movements per week or hard, painful stools
o ± fecal incontinence, large-diameter stools, or stool withholding
• Most common type
o Functional constipation (>95% of cases)
o Onset: during toilet training, starting school, or diet change
o No underlying organic disease
• Red flags (suggest organic cause)
o Onset <1 month of age
o Delayed meconium (>48 hrs) →
think Hirschsprung
o Bilious vomiting, failure to thrive
o Neuro signs (weak legs, abnormal reflexes) →
think spinal dysraphism
o Severe abdominal distension
o Anal stenosis or ectopic anus
• Common Causes
o Functional withholding (fear of pain → retention cycle)
o Low fiber, high dairy diet
o Inadequate water intake
o Hypothyroidism, Hirschsprung, spinal abnormalities (less common)
• Clinical Features
o Infrequent, large, hard stools
o Stool withholding behavior (e.g., leg crossing, stiff posture)
o Soiling (encopresis)
o Abdominal pain, decreased appetite
o Palpable fecal mass
• Diagnosis
o Clinical in most cases
o No imaging or labs unless red flags
o Abdominal X-ray only if uncertain or severe fecal load suspected
• Treatment Approach
o Step 1: Disimpaction
 Oral PEG 3350 (1–1.5 g/kg/day x 3–6 days)
 Or rectal enemas (if oral fails)
o Step 2: Maintenance therapy
 Oral PEG 3350 (0.4–0.8 g/kg/day) for months
 Add fiber, fluids, toilet routine (sit after meals)
o Step 3: Wean slowly after 6 months of symptom-free period
• Follow-up
o Monitor stool frequency, abdominal symptoms, and adherence
o
Relapse common if treatment stopped early
Clinical pearls:
• Most common cause of encopresis is chronic functional constipation
• Treatment requires:
o Disimpaction → maintenance → behavioral/dietary changes
o Use PEG 3350 (1–1.5 g/kg/day) for disimpaction
• Stool softeners must be continued for months to prevent recurrence
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 398–400
☆ Gastroenterology: Functional Constipation and Encopresis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 398–400

stem:
A 12-month-old infant is found to have iron deficiency anemia with a hemoglobin level of 7 g/dL,
but is cardiorespiratory stable. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Start oral iron & follow in 2 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Add meat to diet & follow in 2 weeks

answer: A. Start oral iron & follow in 2 weeks
explanation:
• In a hemodynamically stable infant with IDA and Hgb ≥7 g/dL, the appropriate management
is oral iron therapy
• Elemental iron dose: 3–6 mg/kg/day divided BID
• Recheck reticulocyte count in 1 week and Hgb in 4 weeks
• Dietary changes (e.g., adding meat) are supportive but insufficient as sole treatment for moderate
anemia
• Reassurance alone is inappropriate with Hgb of 7
• Transfusion reserved for Hgb <7, hemodynamic instability, or failure of oral therapy
Clinical pearls:
• Check for excessive milk intake (>24 oz/day) and low-iron diet as causes
• Continue iron for 2–3 months after Hgb normalizes to replete stores
• Screen at 12 months per AAP guidelines
[Further explanation about IDA under Q40]
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 468–470
☆ Hematology: Iron Deficiency Anemia
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 468–470

stem:
A male adolescent presents with a tender, rubbery 2 cm mass under the right areola. He is
in Tanner stage 2–3 and has a normal genital exam. What is the most appropriate next step?
explanation:
• This is a classic case of physiologic pubertal gynecomastia, which occurs in up to 65% of
adolescent boys, especially during Tanner stages 2–3
• Typically presents as a unilateral or bilateral, tender, subareolar rubbery mass
• It is self-limited, often resolving within 6 months to 2 years
• No further testing is needed if:
o Size <4 cm
o No nipple discharge
No signs of virilization or feminization
Imaging or endocrine referral is indicated only if the mass is large, firm, fixed, rapidly growing, or
associated with systemic signs
o
•
Clinical pearls:
• Physiologic gynecomastia = reassurance and follow-up
• Avoid unnecessary imaging or intervention
• Always check medication history (e.g., steroids, antipsychotics) in persistent cases
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 738
☆ Endocrinology: Pubertal Gynecomastia
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 738

stem:
A mother concerned about her 10-year-old child’s weight calls your office to find out how to calculate
her child’s BMI. Your office nurse tells her the BMI can be calculated only if one knows which of the
following values?
explanation:
• BMI (Body Mass Index) is calculated using the formula:
o BMI = weight (kg) / [height (m)]²
• Therefore, only weight and height are required for the actual calculation
• Age and sex are necessary after calculation to plot BMI on CDC growth charts to
determine percentile and classify as underweight, healthy, overweight, or obese
Clinical pearls:
• In children aged 2–20 years, BMI must be interpreted using age- and sex-specific percentiles
• BMI ≥95th percentile = obesity
• BMI 85–94th percentile = overweight
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 75–76
☆ General Pediatrics: Growth and BMI Assessment
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 75–76

stem:
A patient presents after a motor vehicle accident with left upper quadrant pain, left shoulder
pain, hypotension, and low hemoglobin. What is the most likely cause?
explanation:
• Splenic injury is the most common intra-abdominal injury following blunt trauma,
especially motor vehicle collisions
• Classic presentation includes:
o Left upper quadrant pain
o Referred left shoulder pain (Kehr sign)
o Hemodynamic instability (e.g., hypotension, tachycardia)
o Falling hemoglobin due to intra-abdominal bleeding
•
•
•
Liver laceration usually presents with right upper quadrant pain
Pancreatic injuries are rare and cause epigastric pain, often delayed
Renal injuries present with flank pain and hematuria, not LUQ pain or shoulder referral
Clinical pearls:
• Kehr sign = LUQ trauma + left shoulder pain → suspect splenic rupture
• Management depends on hemodynamic stability:
o Stable → imaging and observation
o Unstable → urgent surgical intervention
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1440
☆ Emergency / Surgery: Abdominal Trauma – Splenic Injury
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1440

stem:
A 7-year-old child presents with palpable purpura on the lower limbs, mild abdominal pain, and
no joint or renal symptoms. Vital signs are stable and the child is otherwise well-appearing. What is
the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: C
options.text: Urine dipstick, hydration, bed rest, and analgesia

answer: C. Urine dipstick, hydration, bed rest, and analgesia
explanation:
• This is classic for Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP), also called IgA vasculitis
• Mild abdominal pain is common and self-limited
• Initial management includes:
o Urinalysis to screen for renal involvement (hematuria/proteinuria)
o Hydration and supportive care
o Analgesics (e.g., acetaminophen or NSAIDs) for pain
• Skin biopsy is not needed unless the diagnosis is uncertain
• Steroids are reserved for severe GI, renal, or joint symptoms
★ Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP):
Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP) is an IgA-mediated (type III HSR), leukocytoclastic vasculitis that
presents with palpable purpura, arthralgia/arthritis, abdominal pain, and/or hematuria.
• Renal involvement is due to mesangial deposition of IgA immune complexes with subsequent
mesangial cell proliferation, neutrophilic infiltration, and glomerular damage.
• Most common vasculitis in children
o 90% of affected individuals < 10 years
o Peak incidence: 6 years
• May be precipitated by an upper respiratory tract/ GI infection by 1–3 weeks.
• Diagnosis is clinical:
o Purpura (usually palpable and in clusters) or petechiae, with lower limb predominance
and without thrombocytopenia or coagulopathy.
o PLUS at least one of the following:
 Abdominal pain (usually diffuse, with acute onset)
 Arthritis or arthralgia (acute onset)
 Renal involvement (proteinuria, hematuria)
 IgA deposition (leukocytoclastic vasculitis, proliferative glomerulonephritis)
• Normal laboratory values; ↓ Hemoglobin suggests GI bleeding.
• Complications:
o Intussusception (ileo-ileal, unlike classic ileo-colic); due to bowel wall edema, most
common GI complication
o Renal failure (especially with nephrotic-range proteinuria or elevated creatinine)
 Chronic kidney disease if severe glomerulonephritis
o Scrotal edema in males
o Rare: pulmonary hemorrhage, CNS vasculitis
• Treatment:
o Supportive care: hydration, rest, NSAIDs for arthralgia
 Avoid NSAIDs in patients with IgAV nephritis or GI bleeding
o Corticosteroids: for severe abdominal pain, renal involvement, or intussusception
 Rule out intussusception before starting systemic glucocorticoids in patients with
severe abdominal pain.
o ACE inhibitors: if persistent proteinuria
• Prognosis:
o Most cases of IgAV are self-limited and only require supportive care.
o Monitor urine and BP for 6–12 months for renal sequelae
 Urinalysis (for hematuria and proteinuria) every 1–2 weeks for the first 1–2 months
→ monthly up to 6–12 months
 BP monitoring with every visit
Clinical pearls:
• HSP = palpable purpura + abdominal pain ± arthritis ± renal involvement
• Most cases resolve spontaneously
• Monitor urine and BP weekly for 4–6 weeks
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 922–923
☆ Rheumatology: Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (IgA Vasculitis)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 922–923

stem:
A 4-year-old child presented to ER with status
asthmatics, received full asthma management
then shifted to PICU and received continuous
salbutamol nebulization, he started to have
weakness and fatigability. ECG was provided.
The likely cause is?
explanation:
Beta-agonists like salbutamol shift K⁺ intracellularly → causes hypokalemia, especially with prolonged
use.
ECG: flat T waves, U waves, muscle weakness = classic signs.
Hypokalemia ECG:
- T-wave flattening in the limb leads
- Prominent U waves in the
precordial leads
📚 Source: Nelson – Respiratory/ECG Complications, Osama Naga 2024, Pulmonology, p. 233
☆ PICU: Electrolyte Disorders, ECG Interpretation
source: Nelson – Respiratory/ECG Complications, Osama Naga 2024, Pulmonology, p. 233

stem:
A 10-day-old male neonate presents with lethargy and vomiting. Initial labs reveal
hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and metabolic acidosis. No glucose has been administered
yet. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• This presentation is classic for salt-wasting CAH, most commonly due to 21hydroxylase deficiency (confirm with 17-hydroxyprogesterone)
• Seen in the second week of life, especially in male infants, as females are often
diagnosed earlier due to genital ambiguity
• Key findings:
o
o
o
Vomiting, lethargy, poor feeding
Hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, metabolic
acidosis
Risk of adrenal crisis if untreated
 Cortisol deficiency causes
hypoglycemia and further impairs
the ability to maintain BP.
Other options:
• Neonatal sepsis may present similarly but usually
has fever, irritability, and hemodynamic
instability
• Inborn errors of metabolism can cause lethargy
and acidosis but typically not hyperkalemia
• Pyloric stenosis causes hypochloremic,
hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, not acidosis
Board Clues / Buzzwords for CAH
• “1-week-old with vomiting, dehydration, and
ambiguous genitalia”
• “46 XX with clitoromegaly and hyponatremia”
• “↑ 17-OHP on newborn screen”
• “Salt-wasting crisis in male neonate”
•
•
•
21⍺ hydroxylase deficiency (MC) — ↑ 17-OHP; ambiguous genitalia in females
o Male children have normal genitalia.
o Laboratory studies in all forms of 21-hydroxylase deficiency reveal elevated 17-OHP and
testosterone levels.
11β hydroxylase deficiency (2nd MC) — ↑ 17DOC; ambiguous genitalia in females +
hypertension + hypokalemia
17⍺ hydroxylase deficiency — ambiguous
genitalia in males + hypertension + hypokalemia,
no puberty in females
•
•
5⍺ reductase deficiency — ambiguous genitalia in males
Side-chain cleavage enzyme deficiency (LETHAL)
Treatment
Acute (Salt-wasting crisis):
• IV fluids (NS 20 mL/kg bolus)
• Hydrocortisone IV: 25 mg (infant), 50 mg (child), 100 mg (adolescent)
• Correct electrolyte abnormalities
Maintenance:
• Hydrocortisone (preferred steroid in children): 10–15 mg/m²/day, divided TID
• Fludrocortisone (if salt-wasting): 0.05–0.2 mg/day
• NaCl supplementation for infants (1–2 g/day)
• Risk of adrenal crisis during illness → stress dosing of steroids essential
Clinical pearls:
• In any neonate with electrolyte derangements + vomiting, rule out CAH
• Start IV fluids with dextrose, hydrocortisone, and correct electrolytes immediately
• Confirm diagnosis with 17-hydroxyprogesterone level
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 840–842
☆ Endocrinology: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 840–842

stem:
A 3-year-old child presents to the ophthalmology clinic for a routine check-up. What is the most
appropriate method to assess visual acuity in this age group?
explanation:
• The Tumbling E chart is ideal for assessing visual acuity in preschool-aged
children (typically age 3–5 years)
• It requires the child to indicate the direction the "E" is facing, which is easier than
recognizing or naming letters
• Snellen chart is used in older, literate children, generally ≥6 years
• Visual evoked response is used in nonverbal, very young, or neurologically
impaired children
• Indirect ophthalmoscope is for fundus examination, not visual acuity
Pediatric Vision Screening:
® Newborn: Red reflex, external eye exam, blink to light
® 6 months–3 years: Fixation, ocular alignment, cover test, strabismus signs
® 3–5 years: Visual acuity with LEA, HOTV, or Tumbling E; refer if VA <20/40 or >1 line asymmetry
® ≥6 years: Snellen chart; refer if VA <20/30 or ≥2-line difference
® Every age: Red reflex, external inspection at all well visits
® Refer: Abnormal red reflex, strabismus, ptosis, or failed vision screening
® Nonverbal/disabled: Visual evoked potentials, ERG if needed
Clinical pearls:
• At age 3, visual acuity screening becomes part of routine well-child care
• Other suitable tools for this age: LEA symbols, HOTV charts
• Early detection of amblyopia or refractive errors improves visual outcomes
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 142, AAP Policy Statement (Pediatrics 2016); AAO
Guidelines
☆ Ophthalmology / General Pediatrics: Vision Screening
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 142, AAP Policy Statement (Pediatrics 2016); AAO

stem:
A 12-year-old boy presents after a motor vehicle accident with abdominal pain, pallor,
hypotension (BP 90/65), RR 60, and low hemoglobin. Chest X-ray shows free air under the
diaphragm. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: B
options.text: Emergency laparotomy with stabilization and delayed repair

answer: B. Emergency laparotomy with stabilization and delayed repair
explanation:
• The presence of pneumoperitoneum (air under the diaphragm) strongly suggests hollow viscus
perforation, typically of the bowel
• With hemodynamic instability and peritonitis signs, this is a surgical emergency
• Emergency laparotomy should not be delayed by imaging
• CT abdomen and FAST are appropriate only in stable trauma patients
• Serial exams are inappropriate in the context of instability and confirmed perforation
Clinical pearls:
• Blunt abdominal trauma + free air + unstable vitals = OR immediately
• Resuscitate en route to or during surgery: fluids, blood products
• Delay in surgery increases risk of sepsis and death
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1440
☆ Emergency Medicine / Pediatric Surgery: Trauma – Bowel Perforation
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1440

stem:
A 9-month-old infant presents with persistent head lag, poor feeding, and respiratory difficulty.
There is a history of sibling death in infancy. On exam, the child has hepatomegaly, and chest Xray shows cardiomegaly. What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?

options.label: D
options.text: Enzyme assay from dried blood spot

answer: D. Enzyme assay from dried blood spot
explanation:
• This clinical picture is classic for Pompe disease (infantile glycogen storage disease type II)
o Features include: hypotonia, cardiomegaly, hepatomegaly, macroglossia, feeding
difficulty, and progressive respiratory failure
o Often presents before 12 months and may be fatal without early diagnosis
• Dry blood spot enzyme assay (for acid alpha-glucosidase [GAA] deficiency) is the first-line
test for Pompe disease
• Muscle biopsy is more invasive and now less commonly needed due to molecular and enzyme
testing
•
•
Nerve conduction studies are not diagnostic
Liver biopsy is not useful for diagnosing Pompe and is unnecessarily invasive
Clinical pearls:
• Pompe disease = infantile hypotonia + cardiomegaly + hepatomegaly + respiratory failure
• Confirm with GAA enzyme assay from dried blood spot
• Early enzyme replacement therapy improves outcomes
★ Glycogen Storage Diseases (GSD):
• Inherited enzyme defects affecting glycogen
metabolism → abnormal storage/use of glycogen in
liver, muscle, or both
• Liver GSDs: Fasting hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly
• Muscle GSDs: Exercise-induced symptoms,
myoglobinuria
• Liver + muscle (Type III): Combination of both
features
•
•
•
•
Type I – Von Gierke Disease (Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency)
o Liver involvement
o Severe fasting hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis,
hyperuricemia, hyperlipidemia
o Hepatomegaly, doll-like face, short stature, seizures
o No rise in glucose with glucagon
o Tx: Frequent feeds, cornstarch, avoid fructose/galactose
Type II – Pompe Disease (Acid alpha-glucosidase deficiency)
o Muscle + heart (lysosomal glycogen buildup)
o Cardiomegaly, macroglossia, hypotonia, feeding
difficulty, respiratory failure in infancy
o Enzyme assay = diagnostic
o Tx: Enzyme replacement therapy (ERT)
Type III – Cori Disease (Debranching enzyme deficiency)
o Mild hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, hypotonia, muscle
weakness
o Labs: Elevated liver enzymes, normal lactate
o Glucose partially rises with glucagon
o Tx: High-protein diet, frequent feeds
Type V – McArdle Disease (Muscle phosphorylase deficiency)
o Muscle-only involvement
o Teen with exercise intolerance, muscle cramps, myoglobinuria
o Second-wind phenomenon
o Elevated creatine kinase
o Tx: Avoid intense exercise, carbohydrate intake before activity
GSD 1:
Doll-like facies
and distended
abdomen with
hepatomegaly.
GSD 2 (Infantile):
Massive
cardiomegaly
GSD 3:
Hepatomegaly
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 241–242
☆ Genetic/Metabolic: Metabolic Myopathies – Pompe Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 241–242

stem:
A newborn infant delivered at home presents to the emergency department with marked
pallor but no jaundice. The mother’s blood type is A positive, and the baby’s is O positive.
What is the most likely cause of the infant’s anemia?
explanation:
• Fetomaternal hemorrhage (also known as transplacental bleeding) is a non-hemolytic cause of
neonatal anemia, often associated with pallor without jaundice
o Definition: Fetal blood enters maternal circulation across placenta
o Clue: Pallor without jaundice in newborn
o Causes: Spontaneous, trauma, home delivery, abruption, procedures
o Diagnosis: Kleihauer-Betke test, low neonatal hemoglobin
o Management: PRBC transfusion, supportive care
o Complication: Hypovolemic shock, hydrops fetalis
• ABO incompatibility typically causes jaundice due to hemolysis, often with only mild anemia. The
fetus’s blood type is O which would not cause ABO incompatibility.
• Rh incompatibility is ruled out since both mother and infant are Rh positive
• Physiologic anemia develops later in infancy and is not present at birth
Clinical pearls:
• Newborn with anemia + no jaundice → fetomaternal hemorrhage
• Diagnosis: Kleihauer-Betke test to detect fetal red cells in maternal blood
• Management may require transfusion if severe
• In a home delivery, always keep in mind: Vitamin K deficiency (bleeding), transplacental bleeding,
and neonatal tetanus.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 837
☆ Neonatology / Hematology: Neonatal Anemia – Fetomaternal Hemorrhage
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 837

stem:
A 10-day-old, well-appearing infant presents with a total serum bilirubin of 150 µmol/L.
He is exclusively breastfed and has no signs of illness. What is the most appropriate next
step?
explanation:
• In a well-appearing 10-day-old exclusively breastfed infant, breast milk jaundice is a common
benign cause of prolonged unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia
• Bilirubin of 150 µmol/L (~8.8 mg/dL) at day 10 is within normal range for age and does not require
phototherapy
• Breastfeeding should be continued without interruption
• Formula is not necessary unless weight loss, dehydration, or severe jaundice are present
• Phototherapy threshold is typically much higher (>250 µmol/L in term infants at this age)
★ Neonatal Jaundice
Clinical pearls:
• Breast milk jaundice can persist for 3–6 weeks; baby remains well
• Always rule out pathologic causes if baby is sick, has conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, or rising levels
after 2 weeks
• Monitor feeding, hydration, and weight gain
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 237
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Breast Milk Jaundice
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 237

stem:
A 30-day-old infant presents with poor feeding and lethargy, with a history of a sibling who
died with similar symptoms. The only investigation available is a normal ammonia level (99
µmol/L). What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
•
•
•
•
Galactosemia typically presents in the first weeks of life with:
o Lethargy, poor feeding, vomiting
o Jaundice, hepatomegaly, coagulopathy, sepsis (especially E. coli)
A family history of sibling death raises suspicion for an inborn error of metabolism (autosomal
recessive)
Organic acidemias and urea cycle disorders often present with severe metabolic acidosis and
high ammonia
Aminoacidopathies (e.g., maple syrup urine disease) may also present early, but ammonia is often
elevated
★ Type 1: Classic Galactosemia:
•
•
•
•
•
Definition: Autosomal recessive disorder caused by galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase
(GALT) deficiency
® Result: Accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate → toxic to liver, kidneys, brain
Onset: First few days to weeks of life after lactose ingestion (breast milk or
formula)
o Poor feeding, vomiting, lethargy, jaundice, hepatomegaly
o E. coli sepsis is a classic and life-threatening complication
o Hypoglycemia, coagulopathy, failure to thrive, cataracts
Diagnosis:
o Newborn screen
o Confirm with GALT enzyme assay or galactose-1-phosphate
o Positive urine reducing substances (non-glucose)
Management:
o Stop breast milk/formula (lactose-free/soy formula)
o Lifelong galactose restriction
Complications:
o Intellectual disability, speech delay
o Ovarian failure (in females)
o Liver dysfunction
o Sepsis (E. coli) – early and deadly
Type II – Galactokinase deficiency:
• Causes isolated cataracts in infancy without liver or kidney involvement
• Managed with dietary galactose restriction
Type III – UDP-galactose-4-epimerase deficiency:
• Benign form is asymptomatic; severe form mimics classic galactosemia
• Diagnosis via enzyme assay; treat severe form with galactose restriction
Mnemonic: “Fruits from the
Galapagos are kind” (galactokinase
and fructokinase deficiencies
present with mild symptoms).
Clinical pearls:
• Galactosemia is diagnosed by measuring GALT enzyme activity or galactose-1-phosphate levels
• Treatment: immediate elimination of lactose/galactose from diet
• Early diagnosis prevents liver failure, sepsis, and death
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 241–243
☆ Metabolic / Inborn Errors: Galactosemia
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 241–243

stem:
A child is admitted with status epilepticus, requiring midazolam infusion for seizure control. A few
hours later, the child develops red-colored urine. Urinalysis reveals positive blood (++++). What is the
most likely cause?
explanation:
• Seizures, especially prolonged or status epilepticus, can lead to muscle breakdown →
rhabdomyolysis
• Urine dipstick detects heme (in myoglobin or hemoglobin), not just RBCs
o Positive dipstick + no RBCs on microscopy = myoglobinuria → strongly suggests
rhabdomyolysis
• The classic presenting triad of myalgia, generalized weakness, and tea-colored urine is seen in a
minority of patients.
• Diagnosis is confirmed in patients with an elevated serum CPK, typically > 5× ULN
• Renal injury in rhabdomyolysis 2º to myoglobin filtration and degradation within the glomeruli.
o Heme pigment is released, which causes acute tubular necrosis by direct cytotoxicity and
renal vasoconstriction.
o In ATN, injured tubular epithelial cells slough off into the tubular lumen, forming granular,
muddy brown casts.
o Hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, & hyperuricemia also occur due to myocyte lysis.
• Patients with rhabdomyolysis require careful resuscitation with IV fluids and close monitoring of
their urine output and electrolytes. Hemodialysis may be indicated in cases of severe AKI.
Other options:
• UTI would show leukocytes/nitrites ± hematuria, not isolated heme positivity without RBCs
• Glomerulonephritis and catheter trauma would show RBCs
Clinical pearls:
• Always suspect rhabdomyolysis after prolonged seizures
• Confirm with serum creatine kinase
• Manage with aggressive IV fluids to prevent acute kidney injury
📚 Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 273, 805
☆ Neurology: Rhabdomyolysis After Seizure

stem:
A child presents after foreign body ingestion. X-ray is provided below. The child is asymptomatic.
What is the most appropriate action?

options.label: B
options.text: Discharge home with follow up in 2 weeks

explanation:
• Esophageal foreign bodies, especially in the proximal esophagus, require urgent endoscopic
removal even if the patient is asymptomatic.
•
•
•
•
•
•
Endoscopic removal is the standard of care for symptomatic esophageal foreign bodies or those
not passing spontaneously within 24 hours.
If passed within 24 hours, once in the stomach, 90% of coins pass within 4–6 days
Serial X-rays are not routinely needed unless the coin fails
to pass in 2–4 weeks
Discharge home is inappropriate for symptomatic or
impacted FBs.
Barium studies are contraindicated as they interfere with
endoscopic visibility and pose aspiration risk.
Inducing vomiting is dangerous due to risk of aspiration or
esophageal damage.
Foreign Body Ingestion:
X-ray findings:
• Use neck, chest, and abdominal films
• Radiopaque FB (e.g., coins, batteries, metal) are
usually visible
• Location clues:
o Esophageal FB: appears in the coronal
(face-on) view on AP film
o Tracheal FB (if any): seen in sagittal
(side-view) orientation on lateral film
Orientation:
• Coin in esophagus → flat on AP view
• Coin in trachea → edge visible on AP, flat on lateral
Clinical clues:
• Dysphagia, drooling, chest discomfort, vomiting
• Usually no respiratory distress unless
compression or aspiration
Foreign Body Aspiration (FBA):
X-ray findings:
• Chest X-ray (CXR) is key
• May show:
o Unilateral hyperinflation (air trapping, especially
on expiratory view)
o Mediastinal shift (away from the affected side)
o Atelectasis if airway is completely obstructed
o Visible FB only if radiopaque (rare — e.g., metal)
• Common site: Right main bronchus (more vertical in
children)
Orientation:
• FB may not be seen; rely on indirect signs like air trapping
Clinical clues:
• Sudden coughing, choking, wheezing, unilateral
decreased breath sounds
• No GI symptoms
Coin ingestion:
• If stable, asymptomatic → may wait 24 hours
before removing the coin (may pass
spontaneously)
• If drooling or cannot swallow → requires
removal within 2 hours
• If respiratory symptoms are present →
immediate removal
Button battery: if in the esophagus → remove
immediately, if beyond esophagus: >5 yrs + <20 mm
diameter (repeat x ray in 2 days)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th
ed., page 622; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study
Guide, 3rd ed., page 416. UptoDate: Foreign body
ingestion in children
☆ Emergency Medicine: Foreign body ingestion
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th

stem: What is the most likely cause of non-obstructive hydrocephalus?

options.label: B
options.text: Obstruction of cerebral aqueduct of Sylvius

options.label: D
options.text: Obliteration of cisterns and arachnoid villi

answer: D. Obliteration of cisterns and arachnoid villi
explanation:
• Non-obstructive (communicating) hydrocephalus occurs when cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is
produced normally but there is impaired absorption → usually at arachnoid villi or cisterns
• Common causes include:
o Post-meningitis fibrosis
o Subarachnoid hemorrhage
o Congenital absence or dysfunction of arachnoid granulations
• Posterior fossa tumors, aqueductal stenosis, and fourth ventricle
obstruction cause obstructive (non-communicating) hydrocephalus by physically blocking CSF
flow
★ Hydrocephalus:
• Physiologic CSF flow: lateral ventricles → interventricular foramina of
Monro → third ventricle → cerebral aqueduct of Sylvius → fourth
ventricle → lateral foramina of Luschka or midline foramen of
Magendie → subarachnoid space
Remember MuSLiM (MSLM) for the different foramina for CSF to flow
through → Monro → Sylvius → Luschka → Magendie
• Hydrocephalus: Abnormal accumulation of CSF within the
ventricular system → leads to ventricular dilation and increased
intracranial pressure
• Types:
o Non-communicating (obstructive): Blocked CSF flow within ventricles
 Causes: Aqueductal stenosis, posterior fossa tumor, Chiari
malformation, Dandy-Walker malformation
o Communicating (non-obstructive): Impaired CSF absorption
at arachnoid villi
 Causes: Post-meningitis, intraventricular hemorrhage
(IVH), subarachnoid hemorrhage
• Clinical Features:
o Infants: Rapid head circumference growth, bulging
fontanelle, sunsetting eyes, vomiting, irritability
o Older children: Headache, vomiting, papilledema, lethargy, gait
disturbance
• Diagnosis:
o Head ultrasound (if fontanelle open)
o CT or MRI brain: Enlarged ventricles, assess for obstruction or mass
o Measure head circumference and plot on growth chart
• Treatment:
o Surgical:
 Ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt: most common
 Endoscopic third ventriculostomy (ETV): for obstructive
hydrocephalus
 Treat underlying cause (e.g., Abx for meningitis, resect tumor)
• Complications:
o Shunt infection or blockage
o Developmental delay, seizures, vision loss
o Requires long-term follow-up
Hydrocephalus in a 3 month old;
obvious macrocephaly with
sunset eyes.
Clinical pearls:
• Rapid head growth + sunsetting eyes in infant → suspect hydrocephalus. Always distinguish
communicating vs obstructive to guide treatment.
• Communicating hydrocephalus = impaired absorption
• Non-communicating hydrocephalus = structural blockage within ventricles
• Imaging (MRI or CT) shows ventricular enlargement without visible obstruction
📚 Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 798
☆ Neurology: Hydrocephalus – Communicating vs Non-Communicating
topic: • Physiologic CSF flow: lateral ventricles → interventricular foramina of

stem:
A 4-month-old boy is brought to the clinic with a right undescended testis. The left testis is
normally descended, and the child is otherwise healthy. What is the most appropriate next
step?
explanation:
• Cryptorchidism (undescended testis) is common in neonates but may descend spontaneously
by 6 months
• At 4 months, the appropriate next step is re-examination at 5–6 months
• If still undescended by 6 months, refer for orchiopexy, ideally completed by 12 months to reduce
risks of infertility and malignancy
• Ultrasound is not routinely recommended, as it often does not change management or improve
localization
• Immediate referral at 4 months is too early unless there are atypical features (e.g., nonpalpable
testis, associated anomalies)
Clinical pearls:
• Testes should be palpated bilaterally at each well visit in the first year
• Orchiopexy before 1 year improves fertility outcomes and allows early cancer surveillance
• Imaging is low yield in routine cryptorchidism
📚 Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 752
☆ Urology / General Pediatrics: Cryptorchidism

stem:
A 13-year-old girl presents with recurrent episodes of headache for the past 6 months. The
headaches are described as unilateral, throbbing, and often associated with nausea, photophobia,
and phonophobia. They typically last for several hours and cause her to miss school. There is no fever,
vomiting, or abnormal neurologic findings on examination. Her mother has a similar history of
headaches. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• This is a classic presentation of migraine without aura:
o Unilateral, throbbing pain
o Photophobia, phonophobia, nausea
o Disabling, often causes school absenteeism
o Normal neuro exam and family history support diagnosis
• No aura (e.g., visual changes, sensory symptoms), so this excludes migraine with aura
• Tension headache is bilateral, pressure-like, and lacks associated features
• Pseudotumor cerebri would show signs of increased intracranial pressure, such as papilledema
or diplopia
Clinical pearls:
• Pediatric migraines may be bilateral and shorter than adult migraines
• Diagnosis is clinical, based on ICHD criteria
• Rule out secondary causes with imaging only if red flags are present
📚 Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 798–800
☆ Neurology / Adolescent Medicine: Migraine Headache

stem:
A child with no family history of asthma presents with no respiratory distress, but has flaring of
the alae nasi and a localized wheeze heard over the right lower lung zone. Chest X-ray shows
mild hyperinflation of the right lower lobe without other abnormalities. What is the most
appropriate next step?
explanation:
• A localized wheeze in a child, especially with unilateral hyperinflation, strongly suggests a foreign
body aspiration (FBA).
• Even in the absence of a choking history, FBA should be suspected in non-asthmatic
children with focal signs.
• Bronchoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic in suspected FBA.
• Salbutamol is not indicated unless there’s evidence of reactive airway disease, which this case
lacks.
• CT scan is not first-line; bronchoscopy is more direct.
• Antibiotics are unnecessary without evidence of infection.
★ Foreign Body Aspiration (FBA):
• Age: 1–3 years
• Signs: Sudden cough, unilateral wheeze, ↓ breath sounds
•
•
•
•
•
Buzzword: “Right-sided hyperinflation”
CXR: Unilateral hyperinflation (air trapping), Asymmetric inflation, shift of mediastinum, May
be normal (~30% of cases)
Dx/Tx: Rigid bronchoscopy (gold standard); MC in right mainstem bronchus due to anatomy
Emergency: Heimlich (child) / back blows (infant)
No response to bronchodilators
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 622; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd ed., page 415
☆ Emergency Medicine: Foreign Body Aspiration (FBA)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 622; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 12-year-old presents with hypertension, occasional headache, leg cramps, weak and delayed
femoral pulses, and higher systolic BP in the upper limbs than in the lower limbs. A chest radiograph
is provided.
Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?
explanation:
• Classic features of coarctation of the aorta include:
o Upper limb hypertension, lower limb hypotension
o Weak/delayed femoral pulses
o Rib notching on chest X-ray due to collateral circulation
o Associated symptoms: headache, leg fatigue or cramps,
epistaxis
• Chest x-ray findings:
o Rib notching (see red arrows on xray) → Best sign in older patients
Figure “3” sign (see blue line) → in ~50% of patients with coarct. Abnormal aortic knob
shadow from coarct and dilation of the left subclavian artery.
Atrioventricular septal defect presents earlier with signs of heart
failure
Mitral valve prolapse may cause a murmur but not BP differential or
rib notching
Supravalvular aortic stenosis causes systemic hypertension but not
pulse differential or rib notching
o
•
•
•
Clinical pearls:
• Always check four-limb BPs in hypertensive children
• Most common associated anomaly: bicuspid aortic valve
• Confirm with echocardiogram or CT/MRI angiography
• Definitive management is surgical repair or balloon angioplasty
📚 Source: LANGE Q&A Pediatrics, Question 145
☆ Cardiology: Coarctation of the Aorta
source: LANGE Q&A Pediatrics, Question 145

stem:
A term neonate presents with poor feeding, lethargy, and vomiting. An inborn error of metabolism is
suspected. What is the most appropriate initial management step?
answer: C. Start intravenous high dextrose infusion
explanation:
• In suspected metabolic crisis, the priority is to halt catabolism and promote anabolism
• This is achieved by giving high-concentration dextrose (e.g., D10–D12.5 at 1.5× maintenance) to
provide calories and suppress endogenous protein and fat breakdown
• Protein restriction may be necessary later depending on the type of disorder (e.g., urea cycle
defect, organic acidemia), but not before stabilizing the child
• Fluid resuscitation is needed only if the patient is in shock or dehydrated
• Empiric antibiotics may be given if sepsis is a differential, but do not directly address the metabolic
crisis
Clinical pearls:
• Initial labs: blood gas, ammonia, lactate, glucose, electrolytes, urine ketones
• Early metabolic management = high dextrose, ± lipid, stop protein, and correct
acidosis/hyperammonemia
Note: This answer was validated by the pediatric EM team at KFSHRC; since the question specifically
mentioned “you suspect metabolic disease,” there is a priority given to IV high dextrose infusion to halt the
underlying metabolic processes. Starting a bolus of plain IV fluids will not correct the underlying issue.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 241–243
☆ Emergency Medicine: Acute Management of Inborn Errors of Metabolism
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 241–243
topic: Acute Management of Inborn Errors of Metabolism

stem:
A 4-month-old infant presents with bloody diarrhea shortly after starting cow’s milk-based formula.
The infant is otherwise growing well and is afebrile. What is the most appropriate next step in
management?
explanation:
• This is a classic presentation of food protein–induced proctocolitis, most commonly due to
cow’s milk protein allergy (CMPA)
• Bloody stools in a well-appearing infant shortly after formula introduction is highly suggestive
• Management:
o Switch to extensively hydrolyzed formula
o If symptoms persist, consider amino acid–based formula
• No need to stop all feeds or start antibiotics in a well infant
• Abdominal X-ray is not needed unless toxic appearance or severe distention
Clinical pearls:
• CMPA often presents between 2–6 months
• Exclusively breastfed infants may react to cow’s milk protein in maternal diet
• Resolution occurs by 12–18 months in most infants
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 495
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Cow’s Milk Protein Allergy – Proctocolitis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 495

stem:
A 5-day-old term male infant is brought to the emergency department for poor feeding, irritability, and
lethargy. He was previously well but began to feed poorly and became progressively more drowsy over
the past 2 days. He has had episodes of vomiting and now appears floppy and minimally responsive.
On exam, he is hypotonic with decreased spontaneous movements and intermittent tremors. His urine
has a distinct sweet, “maple syrup” odor. Laboratory tests show hypoglycemia, and metabolic
acidosis. Which of the following amino acids would be elevated in this condition?
explanation:
• MSUD (Maple Syrup Urine Disease) is an autosomal recessive branched-chain ketoacid
dehydrogenase complex deficiency
• Leads to accumulation of branched-chain amino acids
(BCAAs): leucine, isoleucine, and valine
• Presents in the first days of life with poor feeding,
lethargy, vomiting, hypoglycemia, and encephalopathy
• Hallmark: maple syrup odor (sweet-smelling urine)
• Confirmed by plasma amino acid analysis
Clinical pearls:
• Elevated leucine is neurotoxic and correlates with
symptom severity
• Requires urgent metabolic management: high dextrose infusion, protein restriction, possible
dialysis in crisis
• Long-term: BCAA-restricted diet and metabolic follow-up
Mnemonic: Grab the Maple BRANCH if you want to LIVe! In Maple syrup urine disease, the breakdown of
BRANCHED amino acids (Leucine, Isoleucine, and Valine) is impaired.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 243
☆ Metabolic / Genetics: Maple Syrup Urine Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 243

stem:
A 4-year-old child with known sickle cell disease presents with acute drop in hemoglobin to 6 g/dL
(baseline 8–9 g/dL) and splenomegaly (now 8 cm below costal margin, previously 6 cm). He is
hemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
Acute drop in Hb + splenomegaly = acute splenic sequestration crisis. First-line: transfuse
PRBCs (10 mL/kg over 2–4 hours; raises hemoglobin by ~2–3 g/dL). Avoid rapid correction to prevent
volume overload. Splenectomy considered after recurrence.
• Splenic sequestration = young child + big spleen +
rapid Hb drop
• Aplastic crisis = low retic, think Parvovirus B19
• Hyperhemolysis = post-transfusion Hb drops lower
than baseline
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Hematology – SCD Management, p. 395; Nelson, Sickle Cell Disease
Management; AMBOSS
☆ Hematology: Hemoglobinopathies; Sickle Cell Disease
source: Osama Naga 2024, Hematology – SCD Management, p. 395; Nelson, Sickle Cell Disease

stem:
An infant is able to say "baba" and sit with support. Which of the following developmental milestones is
also expected at this age?

options.label: B
options.text: Transfer object from hand to hand

answer: B. Transfer object from hand to hand
explanation:
• At around 6 months, infants typically:
o Sit with support
o Babble using consonants (e.g., "baba", "dada")
o Transfer objects from one hand to the other
o Show early stranger anxiety
• Pull to stand usually develops by 9–10 months
• Walking with one hand held is around 12 months
• Building a tower of 2 blocks is a 15-month milestone
Clinical pearls:
• 6-month milestones:
o Gross motor: sits with support
o Fine motor: transfers hand to hand
o Language: babbles consonants
o Social: recognizes familiar faces, begins stranger anxiety
📚 Source: Osama Naga – Developmental Milestones; Nelson – Growth & Development Tables
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: Developmental Milestones
source: Osama Naga – Developmental Milestones; Nelson – Growth & Development Tables

stem:
A 3-week-old male infant is brought to the emergency department with seizures. He was born at
term without complications. On physical examination, he has low-set ears, micrognathia, and a cleft
palate. Cardiac auscultation reveals a harsh systolic murmur, and echocardiogram shows a truncus
arteriosus. Labs reveal hypocalcemia. Chest X-ray shows an absent thymic shadow. What type of
immunodeficiency is typically seen in this condition?
explanation:
• DiGeorge syndrome results from a 22q11.2 deletion causing failure of development of the 3rd
and 4th pharyngeal pouches
• Key Clinical Features (mnemonic: CATCH-22):
o Cardiac defects → conotruncal anomalies (e.g., tetralogy of Fallot, truncus arteriosus,
VSD)
o Abnormal facies → low-set ears, micrognathia
o Thymic hypoplasia → ↓ T-cells, recurrent viral/fungal infections
o Cleft palate
o Hypocalcemia → due to parathyroid hypoplasia → seizures
• Immunologic findings:
o ↓ or absent T cells, normal or reduced B cells
 Thymus is essential for T-cell maturation; absence leads to increased risk of viral
and fungal infections
 B-cell numbers are typically normal, but antibody production may be reduced due
to lack of T-cell help
o Recurrent infections begin in infancy (esp. viral, fungal, Pneumocystis)
• Other associated features:
o Speech delay, learning disabilities, psychiatric disorders (e.g., schizophrenia risk later in life)
• Diagnosis:
o FISH or microarray to detect 22q11.2 deletion
o CXR may show absent thymic shadow
• Management:
o Calcium and vitamin D for hypocalcemia
o Avoid live vaccines if severe T-cell deficiency
o Severe immunodeficiency → consider thymic transplant or HSCT
o Early developmental & speech therapy
Clinical pearls:
• Infections begin early and are mostly viral, fungal, or Pneumocystis jirovecii
