• Diagnosis confirmed via FISH for 22q11.2 deletion
• Live vaccines are contraindicated if T-cell deficiency is severe
• Severe cases may require thymic transplant or bone marrow transplant
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 542–543
☆ Allergy & Immunology: DiGeorge Syndrome (22q11.2 Deletion)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 542–543

stem:
A 7-year-old boy presents to the clinic with facial swelling and dark-colored urine. His mother reports
that his urine has looked like “tea” for the past 2 days. He had a sore throat and fever about 10 days
ago that resolved without antibiotics. On examination, he has periorbital edema and a blood pressure
of 130/85 mmHg. Urinalysis reveals hematuria with red blood cell casts and mild proteinuria. What is
the most appropriate test to support the diagnosis?
explanation:
• This presentation is classic for post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN)
• Low serum complement levels indicative of an antigen-antibody interaction are a universal finding
in the acute phase of PSGN
• In the context of diagnosing PSGN, a low C3 level is the best single diagnostic test among the
options provided, as it is more specific to the pathophysiology of PSGN and is a key component of
the diagnostic criteria.
• Note: this presentation is also fitting with post-infectious glomerulonephritis (broader term than
PSGN), where it is not necessarily preceded by Streptococcal infection.
• The streptozyme test is useful for confirming antecedent streptococcal infection but is less specific
for PSGN itself.
o While this is supportive in the diagnosis of PSGN, it is not specific for glomerulonephritis and
can be positive in any recent streptococcal infection, regardless of renal involvement.
• Urine culture is unnecessary unless there's a concern for concurrent infection
• ANA is used in autoimmune workup, not post-infectious glomerulonephritis
Clinical pearls:
• Edema + hematuria + HTN = nephritic pattern
• PSGN typically occurs 1–2 weeks after strep pharyngitis or 3-6 weeks after skin infection
• Hallmarks: hematuria (cola), RBC casts, HTN, edema.
• Diagnosis = evidence of recent strep infection + nephritic syndrome signs
o Labs: streptozyme confirms recent infection, low complement (C3) in acute phase
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 664–666; MedScape, Hypocomplementemia (C3) as
an Independent Predictor for Children With Acute Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis: A Long-Term
Observation.
☆ Nephrology: Nephritic Syndrome, Post-Streptococcal Glomerulonephritis (PSGN)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 664–666; MedScape, Hypocomplementemia (C3) as

stem:
A previously healthy child presents with sudden onset of painless rectal bleeding. He is
hemodynamically stable and has no history of abdominal pain or diarrhea. What is the most appropriate
next step in evaluation?
explanation:
• In a child with sudden, painless rectal bleeding, the most likely cause is Meckel diverticulum,
especially in toddlers and young children
• Meckel scan (technetium-99m pertechnetate scan) detects ectopic gastric mucosa, which
causes ulceration and bleeding
• Upper endoscopy is useful for hematemesis or upper GI bleed
• Colonoscopy is more useful for older children or chronic bleeding
• X-ray has limited utility in painless rectal bleeding unless obstruction or perforation is suspected
Clinical pearls:
• Meckel diverticulum = most common congenital GI anomaly; it is a remnant of the
omphalomesenteric duct (normally obliterated by the 6th week of intrauterine life)
• Meckel diverticulum often contains heterotopic gastric mucosa. Secretion of hydrochloric acid by
gastric parietal cells causes ulceration and bleeding of local small bowel, resulting in typical
findings of painless lower gastrointestinal (GI) bleeding and anemia.
• Classic triad: painless bleeding, young child, normal exam
• Meckel scintigraphy scan (Meckel scan): a non-invasive nuclear medicine imaging technique using
radiolabelled technetium (99mTc), which is preferentially absorbed by the gastric mucosa and can
identify ectopic gastric mucosa
• Management: surgical excision if confirmed and bleeding is significant
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 579–580
☆ Gastroenterology: Meckel Diverticulum
Note: The following question is poorly collected, I believe the intention was to be a case of shaken baby
syndrome → fundoscopy would reveal retinal hemorrhages (may be the only verifiable sign of child abuse).
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 579–580

stem:
A child presents with fever and acute vomiting, and is found to be lethargic. An urgent CT scan
(image provided in exam) reveals intracranial hemorrhage with midline shift. Which of the following
would best help confirm the diagnosis or evaluate for possible cause?
explanation:
• In a child with intracerebral hemorrhage, it's critical to evaluate for underlying coagulopathy,
especially if there is no trauma history.
• A coagulation profile (PT, aPTT, INR) helps identify bleeding disorders (e.g., hemophilia, vitamin K
deficiency, DIC)
• Coagulation profile helps detect:
o Sepsis-induced DIC
o Inherited bleeding disorder
o Vitamin K deficiency
• Even if meningitis is present, a coagulation defect may be the mechanism by which CNS bleeding
occurred — and that’s what alters immediate management.
Other options:
• Complete blood count may show anemia or thrombocytopenia but does not assess clotting factor
deficiencies. It is supportive but not diagnostic in this setting.
• Lumbar puncture is contraindicated in the presence of midline shift due to the high risk of brain
herniation.
• Fundoscopy may show signs of increased ICP (e.g., papilledema), but CT has already confirmed
raised ICP. It does not help determine the etiology of hemorrhage. (See note above the question)
Note: There was no image provided when this question was collected but the following are examples of ICH
in pediatric patients. On CT, blood appears hyperdense (white), and a midline shift is evident.
Clinical pearls:
• In acute neurologic decline with hemorrhage: secure airway, neuroimaging, and assess for
coagulopathy
• Avoid LP when there is evidence of increased ICP or midline shift
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 841–842
☆ Emergency Medicine: Acute Intracerebral Hemorrhage in Children
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 841–842

stem:
A well-appearing infant is brought to the ER with sudden onset of gray-blue skin discoloration.
The parents are farmers and report that the family drinks from well water, and there are goats on the
property. The infant is feeding well and has no respiratory distress. What is the most appropriate
next step in management?
explanation:
• This presentation is classic for methemoglobinemia, particularly due to nitrate-contaminated
well water, which oxidizes hemoglobin to methemoglobin
• Infants are especially vulnerable due to fetal hemoglobin and immature enzyme systems
• Key clues:
o Cyanosis unresponsive to oxygen
 SpO2 stays at around 85%
o Well-appearing infant with sudden cyanosis
o Rural/well water exposure
•
•
Diagnosis: co-oximetry (but treat empirically if suspected), may be measured on VBG
Treatment:
o Supportive:
 High-flow oxygen
 Cardiac support
 Treatment of hypoglycemia (reduction of methemoglobin requires glucose)
o If methemoglobin level >30%: Reducing agents
 Methylene blue 1–2 mg/kg IV as a 1% solution in saline over 3-5 minutes
• May be repeated at 1 mg/kg Q30mins as needed (may 7mg/kg)
• Contraindicated in G6PD
 Alternatives: ascorbic acid (vitamin C), riboflavin
o Hyperbaric oxygen therapy
o Exchange transfusion
Pathophysiology:
• Methemoglobinemia occurs when heme iron atoms are oxidized from the ferrous (Fe2+) to the
ferric (Fe3+) state. Fe3+ heme is unable to bind to oxygen; furthermore, the oxygen affinity of the
remaining heme moieties in the hemoglobin is increased.
o This results in a functional anemia (due to reduced oxygen binding) and a left shift of the
oxygen dissociation curve (due to increased oxygen affinity), which leads to impairment in
oxygen delivery despite adequate partial pressure of oxygen in arterial blood.
• Blood appears chocolate brown and does not change color when exposed to air.
• Due to the wavelength of light absorbed by Fe3+ heme, pulse oximetry converges on 85% as the
proportion of methemoglobin increases, regardless of the true oxygen saturation status.
• Treatment with methylene blue or ascorbic acid reduces iron back to the Fe2+ state.
Clinical pearls:
• Methemoglobinemia = cyanosis + normal PaO₂ + chocolate-colored blood
• Avoid methylene blue in G6PD deficiency
• Acquired methemoglobinemia from drugs [oxidizing agents]
o Local anesthetics (benzocaine) — throat spray prior to endoscopy
o Nitric oxide (inhaled pulmonary vasodilator) — pHTN in neonates
o Dapsone (tuberculosis) — rarely used
o Can also occur in the initial phase after accidental naphthalene ball Ingestion (mothballs)
• Nitrate poisoning from drinking water is the most common cause of methemoglobinemia in
infants and is especially common in high-altitude areas (e.g., the Appalachian Mountains).
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 395–396
☆ Emergency Medicine / Toxicology: Methemoglobinemia
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 395–396

stem:
A 2-year-old girl is brought to clinic by her mother due to chronic diarrhea, abdominal bloating, and
poor weight gain over the past several months. She has foul-smelling, bulky stools and appears pale
and fatigued. Her growth chart shows a drop from the 50th to the 10th percentile for weight over 6
months. On examination, she has a distended abdomen and muscle wasting. Laboratory studies reveal
iron deficiency anemia and elevated anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTG) IgA antibodies. Total serum
IgA is normal.
What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: B
options.text: Refer for endoscopic small bowel biopsy

options.label: D
options.text: Reassure and repeat testing in 6 months

answer: B. Refer for endoscopic small bowel biopsy
explanation:
• Classic celiac disease presents after introduction of gluten into the diet (between age 6-24
months) with symptoms of malabsorption (eg, diarrhea, steatorrhea, flatulence, nutrient
deficiencies, weight loss).
o Other manifestations include delayed puberty and short stature in children.
• The gold standard for diagnosis is confirmation by small bowel biopsy before starting a glutenfree diet.
• Starting treatment before biopsy may normalize histology and interfere with diagnosis.
• Repeating tests or just ordering total IgA is inappropriate here — diagnosis can proceed to biopsy.
• If the diagnosis is already confirmed, the next immediate management is elimination of glutencontaining grains:
o Wheat, barley, rye (note: oats may be introduced later if certified gluten-free)
• With strict adherence to a gluten-free diet, symptom resolution occurs within weeks and is
followed by normalization of histology and antibody levels.
Note: Some international guidelines recommend starting gluten free diet without biopsy in a child with
classic symptoms + strongly positive tTG-IgA if antibody levels are >10× the upper limit of normal and
positive EMA. However locally and on most board exams, the correct answer is to proceed with biopsy as it
is the gold standard of diagnosing Celiac disease.
Clinical pearls:
• Celiac disease = autoimmune reaction to gluten → villous atrophy
• Antibody of choice: tTG-IgA (check total IgA in case of deficiency)
• Definitive diagnosis: duodenal biopsy showing villous atrophy + crypt hyperplasia
o Most common location(s): distal duodenum, proximal jejunum
• Most common nutritional deficiencies: iron deficiency, folate deficiency
o Can present as iron deficiency anemia not responsive to Tx
o Note: iron is absorbed in the duodenum. Folate (Vitamin B9) is absorbed in the duodenum
and jejunum. Vitamin B12 and bile salts are absorbed in the terminal ileum.
• Treatment: lifelong gluten-free diet
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 489–490; AMBOSS; UWorld.
☆ Gastroenterology: Celiac Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 489–490; AMBOSS; UWorld.

stem:
A 6-year-old boy presents with difficulty walking following a febrile upper respiratory tract
infection one week ago. He prefers not to walk and complains of pain in his calves. On exam, he is
afebrile, alert, and has tenderness of the calf muscles without joint swelling or neurologic deficits.
What is the next best step in management?
explanation:
• This is a classic presentation of benign acute viral myositis, most often caused by influenza or
other viral illness
• Key findings:
o Recent viral illness, bilateral calf pain, tenderness, refusal to walk
o Normal neurologic exam and afebrile child
• Serum CK is elevated due to muscle inflammation and helps confirm diagnosis
• Condition is self-limiting, resolves in a few days with supportive care
• No need for imaging or invasive testing unless atypical signs present
★ Viral Myositis:
• Definition: Acute inflammation of skeletal muscle caused by viral infection, often after viral
prodrome (especially influenza)
o Etiology: Influenza A/B (most common), Coxsackievirus, Adenovirus, Parainfluenza,
Enterovirus
• Typical Age: 3–9 years (school-age children), boys > girls
• Clinical Features:
o Fever, malaise after URI
o Sudden calf pain, reluctance/refusal to walk
o Tender gastrocnemius, normal neuro exam
o Often bilateral but may be asymmetric
•
•
•
•
o No significant joint involvement
Labs:
o Elevated CK (can be >10,000 IU/L)
o Mild leukocytosis
o Normal inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP)
o Urine myoglobin may be positive (without renal failure)
Diagnosis: Clinical, supported by elevated CPK
o Rule out: rhabdomyolysis, Guillain-Barré, septic arthritis, osteomyelitis
Management:
o Supportive: hydration, rest, NSAIDs
o Full recovery in 3–7 days
o No antibiotics or antivirals needed unless other complications
Red Flags (Not typical):
o Focal neurological signs
o Persistent or worsening symptoms
o Very high CK with dark urine → evaluate for rhabdomyolysis
o Tachycardia out of proportion, hypotension, or chest discomfort → think viral
myocarditis
 Always consider ECG and cardiac markers if systemic symptoms suggest
myocardial involvement
Clinical pearls:
• Acute viral myositis = flu + calf pain + refusal to walk
• Normal neuro exam, resolves spontaneously
• CK elevation is often 1000–5000 U/L
• Rule out rhabdomyolysis if severe or if dark urine
• Differentiate from GBS (which causes weakness, not pain)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1042–1043
☆ Neurology: Viral Myositis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1042–1043
topic: • Definition: Acute inflammation of skeletal muscle caused by viral infection, often after viral

stem:
A teenager with known Marfan syndrome presents with sudden onset of sharp chest pain and
shortness of breath. On examination, breath sounds are decreased on one side. What is the most
likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Marfan syndrome is caused by an inherited defect in the fibrillin-1 gene. They have
a characteristic body habitus, with long thin extremities, loose joints, and long fingers
(arachnodactyly).
• Marfan syndrome is associated with apical blebs and a high risk of spontaneous
pneumothorax, especially in tall, thin adolescents
• Sudden chest pain and dyspnea with decreased breath sounds =
classic for pneumothorax
• Marfan patients are also at risk for aortic dissection, this presents
with tearing chest pain, hypotension, and often radiates to the back.
• The cause of death in Marfan syndrome is most often due to
cardiovascular complications (eg, aortic root dilation, dissection,
and rupture).
Clinical pearls:
• Always consider pneumothorax first in Marfan patients with sudden dyspnea
• Confirm with chest X-ray
• Management depends on severity: observation, needle decompression, or chest tube
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 142, 275; UWorld; AMBOSS.
☆ Genetics: Marfan Syndrome & Spontaneous Pneumothorax
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 142, 275; UWorld; AMBOSS.

stem:
A child received an antiemetic for nausea and subsequently developed abnormal involuntary
movements and muscle stiffness, including oculogyric crisis and dystonia. What is the most
likely causative agent?
explanation:
• Metoclopramide, a dopamine D₂ receptor antagonist, is used as an antiemetic and promotility
agent
• It commonly causes extrapyramidal side effects (EPS), especially in children and adolescents
o Acute dystonia, oculogyric crisis, akathisia, Parkinsonism-like symptoms
• Ondansetron (5-HT₃ antagonist) does not cause EPS
• Diphenhydramine is actually used to treat EPS
• Ranitidine does not cause extrapyramidal reactions
Acute Dystonic Reaction:
Clinical pearls:
• Treat metoclopramide-induced EPS with IV or IM diphenhydramine or benztropine
• Avoid metoclopramide in young children unless absolutely necessary
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 843; Harriet Lane Handbook, Drug Section –
Metoclopramide
☆ Neurology: Drug-Induced Extrapyramidal Symptoms
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 843; Harriet Lane Handbook, Drug Section –

stem:
A 6-month-old infant presents to the ER with fever and decreased level of consciousness. You
strongly suspect meningitis, and an LP (lumbar puncture) is indicated for diagnosis and management.
However, the parents refuse consent for the LP. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Perform LP regardless of parents’ will

options.label: B
options.text: Don't do LP and treat empirically with antibiotics

options.label: D
options.text: Explore parents fears and address their concerns and the importance of the procedure in the management

answer: D. Explore parents’ fears and address their concerns and the importance of the procedure in
explanation:
the management
Explanation:
• Parents have legal authority to consent or refuse medical procedures.
• But physicians have a duty to act in the best interest of the child.
• Initial step must always be to:
o Communicate clearly
o Address concerns respectfully
o Educate about risks of not performing the LP
o Build trust to obtain informed consent
📚 Source: Nelson Ethics.
☆ Ethics: Informed consent
source: Nelson Ethics.

stem:
A 9-month-old infant is brought to the clinic due to developmental delay. She is unable to sit
without support and has an exaggerated startle response to sound. On examination, she has
hypotonia and a cherry-red spot on the macula. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Tay-Sachs disease is a GM2 gangliosidosis caused by hexosaminidase A deficiency
• Seen in Ashkenazi Jewish population
• Key features include:
o Normal development for first few months
o Followed by loss of motor milestones, hypotonia, exaggerated startle reflex,
macrocephaly
o Cherry-red macula due to retinal ganglion cell degeneration
• No hepatosplenomegaly (which distinguishes it from Niemann-Pick)
• Rett syndrome affects girls and presents with regression and hand-wringing
• Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) causes hypotonia but not cherry-red spots or startle response
★ Sphingolipidoses:
• Definition: a group of inherited lysosomal storage disorders caused by enzyme
deficiencies that impair the degradation of sphingolipids, leading to their accumulation in cells,
especially in the brain, liver, and spleen, causing progressive dysfunction.
• Tay-Sachs: Hexosaminidase A deficiency, cherry-red spot, no hepatosplenomegaly,
neurodegeneration, Ashkenazi Jews.
• Niemann-Pick: Sphingomyelinase deficiency, cherry-red spot with hepatosplenomegaly, foamy
macrophages.
• Gaucher: Glucocerebrosidase deficiency, hepatosplenomegaly, bone pain, crumpled tissue
paper macrophages.
• Krabbe: Galactocerebrosidase deficiency, globoid cells, peripheral neuropathy, optic atrophy,
developmental delay.
• Metachromatic Leukodystrophy: Arylsulfatase A deficiency, central & peripheral
demyelination, ataxia, dementia.
• Fabry (X-linked): α-galactosidase A deficiency, angiokeratomas, burning pain, renal & cardiac
disease, adolescent males.
Clinical pearls:
• Tay-Sachs = neurodegeneration + cherry-red spot + startle reflex
• Confirm diagnosis via enzyme assay or genetic testing
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 244–245
☆ Genetic/Metabolic Diseases: Tay-Sachs Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 244–245
topic: • Definition: a group of inherited lysosomal storage disorders caused by enzyme

stem:
A 6-year-old child presents with a history of recurrent chest infections, chronic sinusitis, and
nasal polyps. What is the most appropriate diagnostic test to confirm the underlying condition?
explanation:
• This clinical picture is classic for cystic fibrosis (CF):
o Recurrent lower respiratory infections
o Chronic sinusitis and nasal polyps
o May also present with failure to thrive, greasy stools, or meconium ileus
• Sweat chloride test is the gold standard for diagnosis of CF:
o Values ≥60 mmol/L on two separate tests are diagnostic
• Immunoglobulin levels are useful if suspecting immunodeficiency
• CXR may show bronchiectasis but is not diagnostic
• Nasal endoscopy may visualize polyps but won’t confirm CF
★ Cystic Fibrosis:
• Autosomal recessive, mutation in CFTR gene (Chr 7), most common = ΔF508.
• CFTR defect → impaired chloride transport → thick, dehydrated secretions.
• Newborns: suspect with meconium ileus (pathognomonic – bowel obstruction + ground-glass on
AXR).
• Pulmonary symptoms: chronic cough, recurrent pneumonia, bronchiectasis, digital clubbing.
• Early colonization: Staph aureus (infancy/early childhood).
• Later colonization: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (midchildhood/adolescence), Burkholderia cepacia (severe prognosis).
• GI: pancreatic insufficiency → steatorrhea, failure to thrive, ADEK
vitamin deficiency.
• Intestinal: DIOS (Distal Intestinal Obstruction Syndrome) =
recurrent obstruction in older children.
• Liver: focal biliary cirrhosis → portal hypertension.
• Reproductive: azoospermia/infertility in males (absent vas
deferens), thick cervical mucus in females.
• Electrolytes: hypochloremic, hyponatremic metabolic alkalosis (esp. in hot weather).
• Diagnosis:
o Sweat chloride ≥60 mmol/L (gold standard)
o 2 pathogenic CFTR mutations
o Nasal potential difference (if equivocal)
• Treatment:
o Airway clearance: chest physiotherapy, hypertonic saline, dornase alfa (DNAse).
o Antibiotics:
 Early: anti-staphylococcal (e.g., cephalexin, TMP-SMX).
 Chronic Pseudomonas: inhaled tobramycin, aztreonam.
o CFTR modulators:
•
•
 Ivacaftor (G551D),
 Lumacaftor/Ivacaftor (ΔF508 homozygous),
 Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (Trikafta – ΔF508 hetero/homo).
o PERT + ADEK vitamins.
Complications: hemoptysis, pneumothorax, CF-related diabetes (CFRD), osteopenia.
Surveillance: annual OGTT (CFRD), DEXA (bone health), sputum cultures (track colonization), liver
US.
Clinical pearls:
• Always suspect CF in children with chronic wet cough + nasal polyps + poor growth
• Confirm with sweat test, or CFTR genetic testing if borderline
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 482–484; Osama Naga 2024 p. 421–423; AMBOSS;
UWorld.
☆ Pulmonology: Cystic Fibrosis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 482–484; Osama Naga 2024 p. 421–423; AMBOSS;
topic: • Autosomal recessive, mutation in CFTR gene (Chr 7), most common = ΔF508.

stem:
A 4-month-old infant previously healthy presented to emergency room with history of swelling
over the right side of his head. The child is irritable and crying. On examination he looks happy and
well. The mother denies any history of head trauma. Radiological head CT scan was done which
showed linear fracture of the right parietal bone. What is your next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Admitted to PICU for close observation

options.label: B
options.text: Inform the mother the dangerous of the linear fracture

options.label: C
options.text: Report the case to child protect service and discharge the patient home

options.label: D
options.text: Start working him up as a case of non-accidental injury

answer: D. Start working him up as a case of non-accidental injury
explanation:
• In infants under 1 year of age, any skull fracture, particularly without an adequate trauma
history, is highly concerning for non-accidental trauma (NAT)
•
•
Linear skull fractures may occur accidentally, but in a non-ambulatory infant with no history
of trauma, this mandates a full workup for abuse
o Skeletal survey, fundoscopic exam, coagulation profile, report to child protective
services (CPS)
o Admitting the child for further evaluation is common in such cases
Simply educating the parent or discharging is inappropriate and unsafe at this stage
Clinical pearls:
• NAT red flags: young age, inconsistent history, skull fractures, retinal hemorrhages, multiple
fractures of different ages
• Early recognition and reporting are essential to protect the child
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 94–95
☆ Child Abuse: Non-Accidental Trauma in Infants
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 94–95

stem:
A 6-month-old infant is brought to clinic for evaluation of poor weight gain and increased
irritability. The parents report that the child has had two arm fractures in the past 3 months, both
attributed to routine handling. On examination, the infant has decreased muscle tone, a healing
fracture of the left humerus, and blue sclerae. There is no history of trauma or suspicion of abuse.
Family history reveals that the father had frequent fractures during childhood. What is the most likely
diagnosis?
explanation:
• Osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) is a group of genetic disorders characterized by defective type I
collagen synthesis
•
•
•
•
•
Classic features include:
o Multiple fractures with minimal trauma
o Blue sclera (due to thinning of connective tissue over choroid)
o May also have dentinogenesis imperfecta, hearing loss, joint laxity
Diagnosis is clinical and may be confirmed with COL1A1/COL1A2 gene testing or skin biopsy
Rickets presents with bowing and growth plate abnormalities, not blue sclera
Osteopetrosis involves brittle bones but is associated with anemia and cranial nerve
compression
NAT must be ruled out but is less likely with classical systemic signs like blue sclera
Clinical pearls:
• OI = blue sclera + fractures + positive family history or bone fragility
• ~50% of adults with OI will have some degree of hearing loss
• Management includes:
o Bisphosphonates, fracture prevention, and rehabilitation
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 259–260
☆ Genetics: Osteogenesis Imperfecta
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 259–260

stem:
The parents of a 5-year-old child bring him to clinic due to episodes where he stops talking midsentence, stares blankly for a few seconds, then resumes talking. These episodes occur multiple
times per day. Neurologic examination is normal. EEG shows 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Absence seizures are brief episodes (usually <20 seconds) of impaired awareness, often
with staring, speech arrest, or automatisms
• Classic EEG finding: 3-Hz generalized spike-and-wave discharges
• Onset is typically between 4–10 years of age
• Distinct from daydreaming by sudden onset/offset, lack of awareness, and inability to be
interrupted
• No post ictal phase
• Can have simple automatisms → lip smacking, eye fluttering, head nodding
• Can be induced by hyperventilation
• EEG show classic 3-Hz spike-wave pattern.
•
Tx:
o
o
o
1st line = ethosuximide (Zarontin)
 Mechanism: blocks T-type calcium channels in thalamic neurons.
 Highly effective with fewer side effects compared to alternatives.
2nd line or if associated with generalized seizures = sodium valproate (Depakene)
3rd line = lamotrigine
Clinical pearls:
• Differentiate from focal or complex partial seizures which last longer and may have postictal
confusion
• Normal development between episodes

stem:
A tired-appearing mother of a 2-month-old boy states that he is feeding and sleeping well, but
cries for a total of about 3 hours each day. The baby’s physical examination is normal and growth is
appropriate. She is concerned something might be wrong with him.
Which of the following statements about crying in infants is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Crying increases throughout the entire first year of life

options.label: C
options.text: Infant crying is associated with a higher mortality rate

options.label: D
options.text: This amount of crying is normal for this age

answer: D. This amount of crying is normal for this age
explanation:
• Crying peaks around 6–8 weeks of age, often up to 3 hours/day, then gradually decreases
• This is developmentally normal and often part of “period of PURPLE crying” (Peak of crying,
Unexpected, Resists soothing, Pain-like face, Long lasting, Evening)
• Not necessarily related to hunger or discomfort
• Reassurance, soothing techniques, and parental support are key
• Rule out red flags: fever, feeding difficulty, abnormal exam
Clinical pearls:
• Educate parents on normal crying patterns
• Screen for postpartum depression when parental stress is high
• Avoid unnecessary interventions in a well child
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 31
☆ General Pediatrics: The Crying Infant
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 31

stem:
An 8-year-old girl is brought to clinic by her mother. She expresses intense fear about being away
from her mother, especially when holidays end and school resumes. However, she enjoys playing
with her friends when they visit her home. She does not have difficulties in social interactions at
home. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Separation anxiety disorder is characterized by excessive fear of being separated from
attachment figures, especially when returning to school or after holidays.
• Separation anxiety disorder consists of excessive and distressing anxiety (≥4 weeks in children,
≥6 months in adults) due to separation from attachment figures. Children with this disorder often
experience physical symptoms and nightmares.
• These children are socially normal at home, differentiating it from social anxiety (phobia)
• Common around ages 7–9; may present with somatic complaints (e.g., stomachaches)
Clinical pearls:
• Management includes reassurance, CBT, and
family support
• Pharmacotherapy (SSRIs) only for severe, persistent
cases
• Always rule out school refusal due to bullying or
academic struggles
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 97; UWorld
☆ Developmental & Behavioral / Child Psychiatry: Separation Anxiety
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 97; UWorld

stem:
A full-term infant of a diabetic mother is clinically well. Routine laboratory tests show a hemoglobin
of 24 g/dL and a hematocrit of 76%. He is not tachypneic and feeds well. What is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Observe and repeat CBC in 6 hours

explanation:
• A hematocrit >65% in neonates = neonatal polycythemia
• Common in infants of diabetic mothers, due to fetal hypoxia → erythropoiesis
• Hct >70–75% even in asymptomatic neonates → risk of hyperviscosity syndrome →
requires partial exchange transfusion to reduce hematocrit
• Symptoms (if present) include irritability, poor feeding, cyanosis,
seizures, NEC, or thrombosis
• If asymptomatic with Hct <70%, monitor closely
Clinical pearls:
• Target hematocrit after partial exchange = ~55%
• Use isotonic saline or plasma for exchange
• Early feeding and hydration help reduce viscosity
📚 Source: Fanaroff and Martin's Neonatal-Perinatal Medicine, 11th ed., p. 719
☆ Hematology: Neonatal polycythemia
source: Fanaroff and Martin's Neonatal-Perinatal Medicine, 11th ed., p. 719

stem:
A 6-month-old infant is brought to the clinic for routine immunization. Which of the following
includes the vaccines due at this visit?

options.label: A
options.text: Hepatitis B, DTaP, Hib, PCV, IPV, OPV, BCG

options.label: C
options.text: Hepatitis B, DTaP, Hib, PCV, IPV, OPV

options.label: D
options.text: Hepatitis B, DTaP, Hib, OPV, BCG

answer: A. Hepatitis B, DTaP, Hib, PCV, IPV, OPV, BCG
explanation:
• At 6 months, the following vaccines are due per Saudi MOH 2024 schedule:
o 3rd dose of: Hepatitis B, DTaP, Hib, IPV, PCV, OPV
o BCG (only dose, now deferred to 6 months)
 BCG timing change allows time to rule out primary immunodeficiencies
• Rotavirus (3rd dose) is also typically included but was not in the answer options
o First dose: Must be given before 15 weeks of age
o Last dose: Must be completed by 8 months (32 weeks)
Saudi Vaccination Schedule (MOH 2024)
• Preterm infants should receive the vaccine at actual age not the corrected age
• Only exception is Hepatitis B vaccine if birth weight < 2kg — given at 1 month or discharge
(whichever is 1st)
• BCG delay to 6 months helps avoid severe complications in patients with undiagnosed SCID
• Preschool (4-6 years) vaccines are: DTaP, OPV, Varicella, MMR
Remember: Birth, 2–4–6 months build the base, 9–12–18 add extras,
school & teen boosters follow.
- Measles monovalent at 9 months (pre-MMR)
- MMR given at 12, 18 months, and again at 4–6 years
- IPV/OPV given at 0, 2, 4, 6, 12, 18 months, and 4–6 years
- PCV: 3-dose primary (2, 4, 6 mo) + booster at 12 mo
- MCV4: at 9 & 12 months, booster at 18 years
- Hep A: 2-dose series at 18 & 24 months
- HPV: for girls only, at age 11-12
📚 Source: AAP & CDC Vaccination Guidelines, Saudi MoH Vaccination schedule.
☆ General Pediatrics: Vaccines
source: AAP & CDC Vaccination Guidelines, Saudi MoH Vaccination schedule.

stem:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his parents due to difficulty walking and frequent falls. He
needs to use his hands to push himself up from the floor. On physical examination, you note
waddling gait, lumbar lordosis, and firm enlargement of both calves. The boy is alert and cognitively
normal. Laboratory tests show elevated creatine kinase. What is the most likely mode of inheritance
for his condition?
explanation:
• The presentation is classic for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD): progressive proximal
muscle weakness, Gowers sign, and calf pseudohypertrophy
• DMD is caused by mutations in the dystrophin gene on Xp21
• It is inherited in an X-linked recessive pattern → predominantly affects boys, females are usually
carriers
• Diagnosis is confirmed by genetic testing or muscle biopsy
• Elevated CK is a sensitive early lab finding
★ Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD):
• Etiology: X-linked recessive mutation in dystrophin gene (FHx)
• Disease onset is age 2-5. Muscles of the proximal lower extremities, back, and pelvic and shoulder
girdles are affected first.
• Symptoms: Proximal muscle weakness, delayed milestones, Gower sign, calf pseudohypertrophy
• CK is very high in DMD (often >10,000 IU/L).
o It is the most sensitive and least invasive initial test.
o Elevated CK precedes clinical symptoms and confirms muscle breakdown.
o Guides need for definitive DMD gene testing (e.g., MLPA or PCR).
• Death usually from cardiomyopathy or respiratory failure (2nd decade)
• Treatment: Steroids (prednisone, deflazacort), cardiac monitoring, physical therapy
Clinical pearls:
• DMD presents before age 5 with delayed motor milestones, falls, and gait abnormalities
• Cardiomyopathy is a major cause of morbidity
• Glucocorticoids are used to slow progression
• Carrier females should be offered genetic counseling
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 595
☆ Neurology: Muscular Dystrophies
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 595

stem:
An 18-month-old boy was recently hospitalized and diagnosed with immune thrombocytopenic
purpura (ITP). He received a dose of intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) during his admission. He
presents to your clinic for routine vaccination. Which of the following vaccines should be deferred
at this time?
explanation:
• IVIG interferes with immune response to live vaccines like MMR.
• Per Saudi MOH guidelines, MMR is scheduled at 12 and 18 months. Other live vaccines
scheduled at 18 months include Varicella, and Oral Polio Virus (OPV).
• If IVIG was given, live vaccines should be deferred for up to 11 months depending on dose.
• Inactivated vaccines (DTaP, Hib, Hep A) can be given as scheduled.
★ General vaccine precautions: A precaution is a condition in the
recipient that might increase the risk for serious adverse reactions or
interfere with a vaccine's ability to produce immunity. Generally,
vaccination should be deferred when a precaution is present, except
when the benefits of protection outweigh the risks.
• 4 weeks after stopping steroids
o Oral steroid for less than 2 weeks, inhaled steroid, and
topical steroid are not contraindications for live vaccines
o Oral steroid cause immunosuppression only if given more
than 2 weeks with a dose of 2 mg/kg per day
• 3 months (12 weeks) after stopping other types of chemotherapy
• No live or killed vaccines after rituximab treatment for 6 months
• Acute moderate or severe illness (with or without fever)
• Delayed for 3-11 months after IVIG, 6 months after PRBC, 7 months after FFP or platelets,
immediately after packed RBCs
Special precautions:
• Rotavirus vaccine: uncorrected GI tract malformation (e.g., Meckel's diverticulum)
• Egg protein-containing vaccines: allergic reactions other than urticaria (e.g., angioedema,
bronchospasm) to egg products — yellow fever vaccine
• Pertussis vaccine: progressive or unstable neurologic disorders (e.g., uncontrolled epilepsy,
infantile spasms, progressive encephalopathy)
• Tetanus toxoid-containing vaccines
o Development of Guillain-Barré syndrome within 6 weeks of a previous dose
o Arthus reaction after a previous dose
Clinical pearls:
• Live vaccines (MMR, varicella, oral polio) must be postponed after IVIG therapy.
• Always check timing of IVIG vs. vaccine before administration.
• Inactivated vaccines are safe post-IVIG and should not be delayed.
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 643; Saudi MOH Immunization Schedule
☆ Infectious Diseases: Immunization
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 643; Saudi MOH Immunization Schedule
topic: A precaution is a condition in the

stem:
A 3-year-old previously healthy child was playing on a farm and was later found lethargic with
wheezing, excessive salivation, and lacrimation. He was brought to the emergency department. There
is no mention of pupil size or vital signs. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Organophosphates irreversibly inhibit acetylcholinesterase (AChE) → ↑↑ ACh levels → ↑↑
cholinergic activity
o amplify parasympathetic NS — remember rest & digest; everything will be
contracting/secreting
 diarrhea, drooling, incontinence
 pinpoint pupils
 wheezing & respiratory distress + BRADYCARDIA
• Clinical features mnemonic “DUMBBBELLSS” Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bronchospasm,
Bradycardia, Bronchorrhea, Emesis, Lacrimation, Lethargy, Sweating, Salivation.
• Lethargy can occur due to CNS cholinergic excess.
• Common in farm exposures (pesticides).
•
•
•
Never use succinylcholine to intubate these patients (even for RSI) → succinylcholine is
metabolized by AChE → this leads to further inhibition of AChE and will prolong the effects of the
poison
Atropine will only improve muscarinic symptoms (muscarinic antagonist) → improves
bronchoconstriction & respiratory distress, but no effect on neuromuscular symptoms
Pralidoxime is a cholinesterase reactivating agent → must be given AFTER ATROPINE → causes
transient inactivation of AChE before it begins to work again
o if given alone, it could cause respiratory muscle paralysis
o improves both muscarinic and nicotinic symptoms
Clinical pearls:
• Key signs: wheezing + lacrimation + salivation in farm setting → suspect organophosphate.
• Initial management: airway support, then atropine (for muscarinic symptoms) + pralidoxime (2PAM) to regenerate enzyme.
• Decontaminate skin and clothing.
• The greatest danger in organophosphate poisoning is respiratory failure → patient may require
intubation & mechanical ventilation until toxidrome wears off (remember your ABCs!) →
symptoms last until the body is able to synthesize new AChE enzymes
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 404
☆ Emergency Medicine: Toxicology
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 404

stem:
A 4-year-old boy from a low-income rural area is brought to the clinic by his mother due to
progressive difficulty seeing at night. She also reports that he often rubs his eyes and has been
having dry, irritated eyes for several weeks. On examination, the child appears undernourished with
dry conjunctivae and triangular, foamy patches on the temporal sclera. What is the most likely
diagnosis?
explanation:
• Nyctalopia (night blindness) is the earliest symptom of vitamin A deficiency.
• Xerophthalmia: dryness of conjunctiva/cornea
• Bitot spots: foamy accumulations on conjunctiva
• Risk factors: malnutrition, measles, fat malabsorption, low socioeconomic
status
• Can progress to corneal ulceration and blindness if untreated
Clinical pearls:
• Vitamin A is essential for retinal phototransduction and epithelial integrity.
• Treat with high-dose vitamin A supplementation (per WHO/UNICEF protocols).
• Preventive supplementation recommended in at-risk children under 5 years.
•
In resource-rich regions, most individuals have adequate dietary intake of vitamin A, but patients
may develop deficiency due to fat malabsorption related to biliary obstruction, exocrine
pancreatic insufficiency (eg, cystic fibrosis), or small-bowel resection (eg, Crohn disease,
bariatric surgery).
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 158
☆ General Pediatrics / Nutrition: Vitamin Deficiencies
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 158

stem:
A child with known cerebral palsy and gastroesophageal reflux disease presents with respiratory
distress shortly after feeding. Chest examination reveals bilateral basal crepitations. A chest X-ray is
provided below.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Children with CP and GERD are at high risk of aspiration during or after feeds.
• The x-ray provided (provided by Learning Radiology) shows bibasilar airspace disease (white
arrows) in this patient with a tracheostomy tube (red arrow) who had chronically aspirated.
o Ground-glass opacities + hyperinflation = suggestive of aspiration-related
pneumonia, especially when bilateral and in dependent areas.
• Bilateral basal crepitations and timing after feeding point strongly to aspiration.
• ARDS may appear similar radiologically but is associated with more severe hypoxia and systemic
illness.
• Due to gravity, aspiration pneumonia typically develops in the most dependent portions of the
lung. Patients who aspirate while lying supine typically have involvement of the posterior
segments of the upper lobes and the superior segments of the lower lobes.
• The right lung is more prone t o aspiration than the left lung as the right main bronchus has a
larger diameter, is shorter, and is more vertically oriented than the left main bronchus
(mnemonic: "Inhale a bite, goes down the right").
Clinical pearls:
• Consider aspiration in any neurologically impaired child with feeding difficulties and recurrent
pneumonia.
• Modified feeding strategies and anti-reflux measures are essential in management.
• May require swallowing assessment and/or gastrostomy for long-term prevention.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 508
☆ Pulmonology: Aspiration Syndromes
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 508

stem:
A 3-month-old infant presents with failure to thrive and frequent emesis after feeding. The
baby is otherwise healthy. What is the best diagnostic method to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
explanation:
• Classic presentation of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) in infants
includes emesis and poor weight gain.
• pH probe monitoring is the gold standard for confirming acid reflux in infants.
• Barium swallow may help rule out anatomical abnormalities but is less sensitive for reflux.
• Endoscopy is reserved for complicated or severe cases.
Note: In infants with persistent symptoms of GERD despite normal pH probe results, further evaluation
can be done by esophageal impedance study (Multichannel Intraluminal Impedance (MII)), which
detects both acid and non-acid reflux. This is especially useful in infants, who feed frequently (buffering
acid), making reflux often non-acidic.
Clinical pearls:
• GERD is common in infancy but concerning when associated with failure to thrive, apnea, or
respiratory symptoms.
• Consider thickened feeds, positional therapy, or pharmacologic intervention if conservative
measures fail.
• Rule out other causes like pyloric stenosis if projectile vomiting or electrolyte abnormalities are
present.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 366
☆ Gastroenterology: Gastroesophageal Reflux
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 366

stem:
A 6-month-old male infant presents with a history of recurrent ear infections and pneumonia.
His parents are concerned about persistent bleeding from minor injuries and frequent bruising.
They also report that he has a persistent itchy, red rash over his face and trunk that started at 2
months of age. On examination, he has petechiae and eczema-like lesions on his cheeks and
flexural areas. Laboratory studies show thrombocytopenia with small platelets. Family history is
notable for two maternal uncles who died of severe infections in infancy. What is the best initial
laboratory test to support the suspected diagnosis?
explanation:
•
•
•
•
The clinical presentation is classic for Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome (WAS):
o Wiskott (eczema)
o Autoimmunity/infections (recurrent)
o Thrombocytopenia with small platelets
o X-linked recessive inheritance pattern with affected maternal male relatives
Quantitative immunoglobulin levels are the best initial test.
o IgM is usually low, while IgA and IgE are elevated → reflect the dysregulated immune
function seen early in WAS
Lymphocyte subsets may follow but are not the first test.
o T and B cell numbers may be normal or only mildly reduced early on
o The functional defect is more important than the absolute count
o Thus, subset analysis may miss the diagnosis initially
Other differentials:
o SCID would present earlier with lymphopenia and more severe opportunistic infections.
o CGD (diagnosed by oxidative burst) has normal platelets and no eczema.
o Complement disorders (diagnosed by low CH50)
Clinical pearls:
• Immunoglobulin levels are the best first-line screening test when
suspecting combined immunodeficiency with bleeding diathesis
• WAS is an X-linked recessive primary immunodeficiency.
• Triad: eczema, recurrent infections, thrombocytopenia with small
platelets.
• Labs: ↓ IgM, ↑ IgA and IgE, thrombocytopenia with small platelets
• Gene defect: WAS gene on Xp11.22
• Severe recurrent infections develop after transplacental maternal
IgG and maternal mucosal IgA are degraded around 6 months.
• Treatment: hematopoietic stem cell transplantation is curative.
• Avoid platelet transfusion unless bleeding is severe.
• Avoid live vaccines.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 427
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Primary Immunodeficiency Disorders
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 427

stem:
A 3-year-old boy presents with a 3-day history of fever and vomiting. Examination reveals neck
stiffness but is otherwise unremarkable. He is diagnosed with Haemophilus influenzae type b
(Hib) meningitis and treated appropriately. He is now stable and ready for discharge.
What is the most important test to perform before discharge?
explanation:
• Sensorineural hearing loss is a well-known complication of bacterial meningitis, particularly
Hib.
• Audiologic assessment is recommended before discharge or shortly after recovery in all children
with bacterial meningitis.
o Repeat evaluation should be done at 4-8 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months after
discharge.
• MRI is only indicated for neurologic complications (e.g., seizures, focal deficits).
• Developmental surveillance is important but not urgent before discharge.
• In Pneumococcal meningitis specifically, treatment with dexamethasone prior to antibiotic
therapy has been shown to reduce inflammation and decrease the risk of adverse outcomes
(eg, seizures, focal neurologic deficits) and death.
Clinical pearls:
• Always assess hearing after Hib, pneumococcal, or meningococcal meningitis.
• Permanent hearing loss may occur early and is often bilateral.
• Early identification allows for timely audiologic and speech interventions.
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 505; First Aid for the USMLE
☆ Infectious Diseases: Bacterial Meningitis
source: Red Book 2021–2024, p. 505; First Aid for the USMLE

stem:
An unvaccinated child is brought to the Emergency Department with a sudden onset of high-grade
fever (39.5°C), severe sore throat, difficulty swallowing, and drooling. His mother reports that he
is refusing to speak or lie down and prefers to sit leaning forward with his mouth open and neck
extended. On examination, he appears toxic, anxious, and has inspiratory stridor. He is
tachypneic, and his voice is muffled (“hot potato voice”). Attempts to visualize the oropharynx were
avoided due to concern for airway compromise. Lateral neck X-ray is provided.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Classic epiglottitis:
o Abrupt onset with high fever, sore throat, drooling, and dysphagia
o Tripod position, muffled voice, stridor without cough
o Toxic appearance, often in unvaccinated children
o Thumb sign on lateral neck X-ray
o Most common cause: Haemophilus influenzae type B (Hib)
• "Thumb sign" on lateral neck X-ray represents enlarged, swollen epiglottis.
• This is an airway emergency — do not examine throat unless intubation is ready.
• Steroids/antibiotics come after securing airway.
o Secure airway immediately in OR or ICU setting
IV antibiotics: ceftriaxone or cefotaxime
Monitor in PICU
Narrowing of the larynx causes signs of respiratory distress (eg, retractions) with inspiratory
stridor, and patients classically assume tripod positioning (ie, leaning forward with the neck
extended) to maximize airflow.
o
o
•
•
•
•
Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) was historically the most common pathogen that caused
epiglottitis, but routine immunization in infancy has greatly decreased its incidence. Individuals
with incomplete vaccination against Hib remain at greatest risk of developing
epiglottitis. Pathogens implicated in immunized children more commonly include nontypeable H
influenzae and Streptococcus pneumoniae, in addition to rare cases of Hib.
All close contacts (including daycare center staff and children) who are exposed to a child with
epiglottitis should receive a 4-day prophylactic course of treatment with rifampin at 20 mg/kg
(not to exceed 600 mg/d).
Children older than 2 years with epiglottitis do not need vaccination, because the disease
provides immune protection.
📚 Source: Nelson Infectious Diseases & Emergency; Osama Naga 2024 p. 476–478.
☆ Emergency Medicine: Respiratory Emergencies, Airway Obstruction/Stridor, Epiglottitis
source: Nelson Infectious Diseases & Emergency; Osama Naga 2024 p. 476–478.

stem:
A 12-year-old male athlete presents with anterior knee pain that worsens with activity such as
running and jumping. On examination, the knee joint is stable and nontender with full range of
motion. There is localized swelling and tenderness over the tibial tuberosity. X-ray is provided.
What is the most appropriate next step in management?
answer: D. Rest, activity modification, and reassurance
explanation:
• This is classic for Osgood-Schlatter disease, an overuse apophysitis of the tibial tuberosity due
to repetitive traction from the patellar tendon
• Common in physically active adolescents during growth spurts
• Diagnosis is clinical, supported by typical history and X-ray.
• Self-limited; managed conservatively with:
o Rest from aggravating activities
o Ice, NSAIDs
o Stretching and strengthening exercises
Clinical pearls:
• Do not inject steroids near the growth plate
• MRI and aspiration are not indicated unless red flags (effusion, instability, fever, systemic
symptoms)
• If avulsion fracture is present → management shifts from conservative OSD treatment to
immobilization or surgery, depending on displacement.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 878
☆ Orthopedics: Osgood-Schlatter Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 878

stem:
A 2-year-old child is brought to the emergency department after ingestion of an unknown amount
of alkaline detergent. He has no vomiting or respiratory symptoms at presentation. Physical exam is
unremarkable. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• Alkali ingestion (e.g., detergents, drain cleaners) causes liquefactive necrosis, which can lead
to deep tissue injury in the esophagus and stomach
• Endoscopy within 12–24 hours is recommended in all symptomatic patients or if there is
uncertainty regarding the extent of injury
• CXR is not the initial test unless there are respiratory symptoms or concern for aspiration/
perforation
• Charcoal is contraindicated in caustic ingestions
• Oral fluids or milk may be offered to cooperative patients with no signs of perforation but is not a
substitute for endoscopic evaluation
Clinical pearls:
• Monitor for signs of perforation: fever, chest pain, subcutaneous emphysema, mediastinitis
• Do not induce vomiting or perform blind NG insertion
• Long-term risks: strictures, esophageal carcinoma
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 404–405
☆ Emergency Medicine / Toxicology: Caustic Ingestion
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 404–405

stem:
A 6-year-old boy is brought to the pediatric hematology clinic for evaluation of pallor and increasing
fatigue. He has a history of frequent nosebleeds and easy bruising. On examination, he has short
stature, hyperpigmented café-au-lait spots, hypoplastic thumbs, and microcephaly. A CBC shows
pancytopenia. His parents are first cousins. Bone marrow biopsy reveals hypocellularity. Which of
the following diagnostic tests is most appropriate to confirm the suspected diagnosis?

options.label: D
options.text: Serum vitamin B12 and folate levels

explanation:
• Fanconi anemia is an autosomal recessive inherited bone marrow failure syndrome.
• Characterized by:
o Pancytopenia (progressive aplastic anemia)
o Congenital anomalies: short stature, abnormal thumbs, skin pigmentation, microcephaly
o Increased risk of AML and solid tumors
• Diagnosis
o Chromosomal breakage study using diepoxybutane (DEB) or mitomycin C (MMC)
o Genetic testing (e.g. FANCA gene most common)
o CBC + Bone marrow biopsy (shows hypocellularity)
• Management
o Supportive care: transfusions for anemia/thrombocytopenia
o Androgens: stimulate erythropoiesis (limited effect)
o HSCT (hematopoietic stem cell transplant) is curative for marrow failure
o Cancer surveillance: due to high malignancy risk
Key Board Buzzwords for Fanconi Anemia:
• "Radial ray defects + pancytopenia"
• "Chromosomal breakage with DEB/MMC"
• "Bone marrow failure + congenital anomalies"
• "AML in a child with short stature and thumb anomalies"
Clinical pearls:
• Genetic counseling is essential in consanguineous families
• Avoid DNA-damaging agents (e.g., chemo, radiation) unless in a transplant setting
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 820
☆ Hematology: Bone Marrow Failure Syndromes, Fanconi Anemia
127/127 questions

https://neonatalsepsiscalculator.kaiserpermanente.org/,
https://www.mdcalc.com/calc/10528/neonatal-early-onset-sepsis-calculator

stem:
A child presents with 3 weeks of fever, migratory rash over the thigh that coincides with febrile
episodes, and abdominal pain. What lab abnormality is expected?
explanation:
Classic systemic-onset Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis (sJIA): spiking fevers, salmon-colored
rash, arthritis, hepatosplenomegaly. Thrombocytosis is a marker of systemic inflammation.
•
Treatment of JIA:
o 1st line agent (NSAID)
 Nephrotoxic, GIT bleeding
o 2nd line agent: methotrexate
 Mucositis, nephrotoxicity, hepatotoxicity, megaloblastic anemia
 Folic acid and leucovorin can help to decrease methotrexate toxicity
o Intraarticular steroids can used temporally in patients with severe joint pain
o 3rd line: Biological agents — Anti –TNF (infliximab)
📚 Osama Naga, Pediatric Board Study Guide 2024, Rheumatology Chapter, p. 527
☆ Rheumatology: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis

stem: A child has corneal clouding noted on physical exam. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Hurler syndrome (MPS I): developmental delay, coarse facies, hepatosplenomegaly, corneal
clouding. Hunter syndrome (MPS II) lacks corneal clouding.
Full Clinical Vignette:
A 14-month-old boy is brought to your clinic for evaluation of developmental delay and progressive
coarsening of facial features. His parents report that he was “normal” at birth, but over the past several
months, they’ve noticed that his forehead has become prominent, and he now has stiff joints and
frequent upper respiratory infections. He also seems to have trouble seeing and hearing. On exam, you
note macrocephaly, frontal bossing, coarse facies, a flattened nasal bridge, hepatosplenomegaly,
corneal clouding, and limited shoulder range of motion. A cardiac murmur is appreciated. Urine shows
elevated dermatan sulfate and heparan sulfate.
What is the most likely diagnosis? Hurler syndrome
⚠ Hurler Syndrome Key Features:
• Onset within first 2 years
• Developmental regression
• Coarse facies, macroglossia, frontal bossing
• Corneal clouding (distinguishes from Hunter syndrome)
• Hepatosplenomegaly
• Joint stiffness
• Recurrent otitis media or airway obstruction
• Cardiomyopathy or valvular disease
• Urinary GAGs: dermatan sulfate, heparan sulfate
• Diagnosis: enzyme assay, confirmed with genetic testing
• Treatment: enzyme replacement therapy, HSCT in severe cases
Suspect Hunter syndrome in boys with coarse facies + developmental delay + skin papules + hearing
loss, without corneal clouding.
Mnemonic: “Hunters with good eyesight will catch the aggressive chupacabra active in TeXas:”
→ Hunter syndrome (no corneal clouding, aggressive behavior, carpal tunnel syndrome, hyperactivity,
and X-linked recessive inheritance).
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Genetics Chapter; AMBOSS
☆ Genetics: Mucopolysaccharidosis (Inborn Errors of Metabolism)
source: Osama Naga 2024, Genetics Chapter; AMBOSS

stem:
A full-term neonate is noted at birth to have microcephaly, overlapping fingers, and clenched hands
that are difficult to extend. The infant is small for gestational age and has a weak cry. Based on these
findings, what other clinical feature is most likely to be present?
explanation:
Classic features of Trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome): microcephaly, clenched fists with
overlapping fingers, rocker bottom feet, cardiac defects.
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Genetics Chapter, p. 108; Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, Chromosomal
Anomalies
☆ Genetics: Trisomies
Mnemonic: PRINCE Edward turned 18: Prominent occiput, Rocker-bottom feet, Intellectual
disability, Nondisjunction (in meiosis), Clenched fists/Cardiac (VSD), low-set Ears, and chromosome 18.
Trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome):
Mnemonic: 7 Ps of Patau syndrome: 1. holoProsencephaly — most common etiology, 2 & 3. cleft
liP and Palate, 4. Polydactyly, 5. Pump disease (congenital heart disease: VSD), 6. Polycystic
kidney disease, 7. cutis aPlasia
source: Osama Naga 2024, Genetics Chapter, p. 108; Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, Chromosomal

stem:
A 14-year-old boy on isoniazid (INH) therapy for latent tuberculosis presents with morning stiffness,
fatigue, and a new rash. Laboratory findings reveal a positive ANA titer of 1:1280, negative antidsDNA, and elevated ESR (50 mm/hr). What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Drug-induced lupus (especially from INH, hydralazine) typically presents with +ANA
and negative anti-dsDNA. SLE usually has both ANA and dsDNA positivity.
• Drug-induced lupus (DIL) is a known complication of isoniazid (INH)
• DIL typically presents with arthralgia or arthritis, fatigue, rash, and positive ANA
• Anti-dsDNA is usually negative, which helps differentiate from SLE, where dsDNA is typically
positive
• JIA would not explain the ANA pattern in this scenario and would not be associated with a drug
exposure
• Active TB would not produce positive ANA or mimic lupus serology
Mnemonic: “He needs to lower his tone! I think he’s on drugs…” — Anti-histone antibodies are present
in drug-induced lupus
• ANA + anti-histone antibody = drug induced lupus
• Abrupt onset of symptoms, sparing CNS, oral mucosa and kidneys
• Most common medication known to cause drug-induced SLE is hydralazine (HTN Tx)
• Hx of TB = think of isoniazid exposure
• Hx of malignant hypertension/CKD = think of hydralazine exposure
• Hx of epilepsy = think of phenytoin exposure
• Reversible upon discontinuation of offending agent
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Rheumatology, p. 527
☆ Rheumatology: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
source: Osama Naga 2024, Rheumatology, p. 527

stem:
A neonate was born with abnormal looking genitalia (exact photo). Chromosomal analysis revealed 46
XX. Which one of the following is most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Virilization of 46 XX infants = CAH (most commonly 21-hydroxylase deficiency). Salt-wasting,
elevated 17-OHP.
Board Clues / Buzzwords for CAH
• “1-week-old with vomiting, dehydration, and ambiguous genitalia”
• “46 XX with clitoromegaly and hyponatremia”
• “↑ 17-OHP on newborn screen”
• “Salt-wasting crisis in male neonate”
•
•
21⍺ hydroxylase deficiency (MC) — ↑ 17-OHP; ambiguous genitalia in females
o Male children have normal genitalia.
o Laboratory studies in all forms of 21-hydroxylase deficiency reveal elevated 17-OHP and
testosterone levels.
11β hydroxylase deficiency (2nd MC) — ↑ 17-DOC; ambiguous genitalia in females + hypertension +
hypokalemia
•
•
•
17⍺ hydroxylase deficiency — ambiguous genitalia in males + hypertension + hypokalemia, no
puberty in females
5⍺ reductase deficiency — ambiguous genitalia
in males
Side-chain cleavage enzyme deficiency (LETHAL)
Treatment
Acute (Salt-wasting crisis):
• IV fluids (NS 20 mL/kg bolus)
• Hydrocortisone IV: 25 mg (infant), 50 mg (child), 100 mg (adolescent)
• Correct electrolyte abnormalities
Maintenance:
• Hydrocortisone (preferred steroid in children): 10–15 mg/m²/day, divided TID
• Fludrocortisone (if salt-wasting): 0.05–0.2 mg/day
• NaCl supplementation for infants (1–2 g/day)
• Risk of adrenal crisis during illness → stress dosing of steroids essential
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials, Endocrinology Chapter; Osama Naga 2024, Endocrine, p. 183
☆ Endocrinology: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia, Ambiguous Genitalia
source: Nelson Essentials, Endocrinology Chapter; Osama Naga 2024, Endocrine, p. 183

stem:
A 6-year-old child presents with fatigue, macrocytic anemia, mouth sores (stomatitis), and a recent
history of diarrhea. There is no known chronic illness. On physical exam, the child appears pale and
mildly irritable. What is the most appropriate initial lab test to order?
explanation:
Diarrhea + glossitis + macrocytic anemia → think of B12 deficiency (e.g., from malabsorption
or dietary deficiency).
B12 Deficiency
“A 5-month-old breastfed infant of a vegan mother presents with hypotonia, lethargy, poor weight gain, and
macrocytic anemia. Neuro exam shows decreased tone and delayed milestones.”
🧠 Think: B12 deficiency → neuro signs, delayed development, vegan maternal diet
🔍 Confirm with: ↓ B12, ↑ methylmalonic acid, ↑ homocysteine
Vitamin B12 Deficiency caused by malabsorption (eg, sprue, enteritis, Diphyllobothrium latum,
achlorhydria [chronic PPI use], bacterial overgrowth), lack of intrinsic factor (eg, pernicious anemia, gastric
bypass surgery), absence of terminal ileum (surgical resection, short bowel syndrome, Crohn disease),
certain drugs (eg, metformin), or insufficient intake (eg, veganism).
Folate Deficiency
“A 2-year-old on a restricted diet of goat milk and crackers presents with fatigue, pale skin, and macrocytic
anemia. No neurologic findings.”
🧠 Think: Folate deficiency → macrocytosis without neuro signs
🔍 Confirm with: ↓ folate, normal methylmalonic acid
📚 Source: Nelson, Hematology Chapter; Osama Naga 2024, Hematology, p. 392
☆ Hematology: Macrocytic Anemia; Vitamin Deficiencies
source: Nelson, Hematology Chapter; Osama Naga 2024, Hematology, p. 392

stem:
A 4-year-old child presents with a 2-week history of bruising, lower limb swelling, petechiae, and gum
hypertrophy with bleeding. The mother reports the child has also developed a high-grade fever over
the past 2 days. On exam, there are perifollicular hemorrhages, ecchymoses, and swollen, tender
limbs. What is the most important lab test to order to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
explanation:
This is classic scurvy (vitamin C deficiency): perifollicular hemorrhages, bleeding gums,
pseudoparalysis, ecchymoses in dependent areas. Occurs in malnourished children or those with
restrictive diets (e.g., autism). Fever may reflect inflammation or infection superimposed on hemorrhagic
sites.
• Vitamin C is a cofactor for the hydroxylation of proline and lysine during collagen
synthesis. Hydroxyproline and hydroxylysine are essential for the spontaneous cross-linking of
collagen molecules. Vitamin C deficiency impairs collagen cross-linking, compromising tensile
strength, particularly in the skin and vascular walls. Increased vascular wall fragility leads to:
o Perifollicular hemorrhages
o Petechiae/ecchymoses
o Periodontal disease (gingival erythema/edema/bleeding)
o Intramuscular or subperiosteal hemorrhages, especially in the lower extremities due to
increased hydrostatic pressure. This results in pain and difficulty with ambulation, which
may be the presenting symptoms in children.
• Other features associated with defective collagen production in vitamin C deficiency include poor
wound healing, follicular hyperkeratosis, and corkscrew hairs.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Nutrition & Vitamin Deficiency, Ch. 31; Osama Naga 2024,
Nutritional Deficiencies, p. 287
☆ General Pediatrics: Scurvy/Vitamin C Deficiency; Vitamin Deficiencies
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Nutrition & Vitamin Deficiency, Ch. 31; Osama Naga 2024,

stem:
A child presents with irritability, nausea, vomiting, and PICA (craving for non-food items).
What is the next best investigation?
explanation:
Classic signs of lead poisoning, especially with PICA and GI symptoms. Children with
environmental exposure (old homes, pottery, imported toys) are at risk. Always screen with venous lead
level if PICA + GI + behavior issues.
Purple-blue line on the gums
(lead line or Burton line)
Lead lines on long bone xray
(dense bands at the metaphyses)
Lead flecks on abdominal xray
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Toxicology Chapter; PREP 2024, Environmental Exposure
☆ General Pediatrics: Lead Poisoning
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Toxicology Chapter; PREP 2024, Environmental Exposure

stem:
A 7-year-old boy presents with abdominal pain, arthralgia, and a purpuric rash over the buttocks
and lower extremities, shown in the attached image. There is no thrombocytopenia. If a skin biopsy is
performed, what is the most characteristic histopathologic finding?

options.label: C
options.text: Leukocytoclastic vasculitis with neutrophils but no immunoglobulin

explanation:
★ Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP):
Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP) is an IgA-mediated (type III HSR), leukocytoclastic vasculitis that
presents with palpable purpura, arthralgia/arthritis, abdominal pain, and/or hematuria.
• Renal involvement is due to mesangial deposition of IgA immune complexes with subsequent
mesangial cell proliferation, neutrophilic infiltration, and glomerular damage.
• Most common vasculitis in children
o 90% of affected individuals < 10 years
o Peak incidence: 6 years
• May be precipitated by an upper respiratory tract/ GI infection by 1–3 weeks.
• Diagnosis is clinical:
o Purpura (usually palpable and in clusters) or petechiae, with lower limb predominance and
without thrombocytopenia or coagulopathy.
o PLUS at least one of the following:
 Abdominal pain (usually diffuse, with acute onset)
 Arthritis or arthralgia (acute onset)
 Renal involvement (proteinuria, hematuria)
 IgA deposition (leukocytoclastic vasculitis, proliferative glomerulonephritis)
• Normal laboratory values; ↓ Hemoglobin suggests GI bleeding.
• Complications:
o Intussusception (ileo-ileal, unlike classic ileo-colic); due to bowel wall edema, most
common GI complication
o Renal failure (especially with nephrotic-range proteinuria or elevated creatinine)
 Chronic kidney disease if severe glomerulonephritis
o Scrotal edema in males
o Rare: pulmonary hemorrhage, CNS vasculitis
• Treatment:
o Supportive care: hydration, rest, NSAIDs for arthralgia
 Avoid NSAIDs in patients with IgAV nephritis or GI bleeding
Corticosteroids: for severe abdominal pain, renal involvement, or intussusception
 Rule out intussusception before starting systemic glucocorticoids in patients with
severe abdominal pain.
o ACE inhibitors: if persistent proteinuria
Prognosis:
o Most cases of IgAV are self-limited and only require supportive care.
o Monitor urine and BP for 6–12 months for renal sequelae
 Urinalysis (for hematuria and proteinuria) every 1–2 weeks for the first 1–2 months →
monthly up to 6–12 months
 BP monitoring with every visit
o
•
Clinical pearls:
• HSP = palpable purpura + abdominal pain ± arthritis ± renal involvement
• Most cases resolve spontaneously
• Monitor urine and BP weekly for 4–6 weeks
📚 Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Rheumatology Chapter (Vasculitis); Osama Naga 2024, Immunology
☆ Rheumatology: Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP), IgA Vasculitis

stem: A child in the PICU, intubated for respiratory failure, develops desaturation. Chest exam reveals newonset crackles. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
Desaturation + new crackles while intubated → likely atelectasis or fluid overload. First step
is to improve oxygenation via PEEP, which re-expands alveoli and reduces shunting.
• Increase RR → used for respiratory acidosis (↑CO₂), not hypoxia
• Increase PIP → may help if there's poor tidal volume or lung compliance, but crackles and
desaturation = oxygenation issue, not ventilation
• Decrease FiO₂ → would worsen hypoxia
Complications of Mechanical Ventilation:
📚Source: Nelson, Mechanical Ventilation & Respiratory Failure; Osama Naga 2024, PICU/Respiratory
☆ PICU: Mechanical Ventilation
source: Nelson, Mechanical Ventilation & Respiratory Failure; Osama Naga 2024, PICU/Respiratory

stem:
A girl in PICU on MV has pulmonary edema and heart failure receiving anti-heart failure medications
and Abx (no names mentioned). Blood gas showed ↑ pH (7.54), ↑ HCO₃ (44), and ↑ pCO₂ (53). What is
the most likely cause?
answer: A. Metabolic alkalosis due to medications
explanation:
HF medications including diuretics (e.g., furosemide) → contraction alkalosis (loss of H⁺,
Cl⁻, and K⁺). Kidneys retain HCO₃. Elevated pCO₂ is compensatory hypoventilation.
Mnemonic (acid-base): SMORE: change in PCO2 in the Same direction as pH → Metabolic disorder; change
in PCO2 in the Opposite direction to pH → REspiratory disorder
Interpreting the VBG:
1. pH = 7.54 (↑) → Alkalemia (so we rule out acidosis)
2. HCO₃⁻ = 44 (↑) → Elevated bicarbonate → metabolic origin
3. pCO₂ = 53 (↑) → This is compensatory hypoventilation (the body is holding onto CO₂ to balance the
alkalosis)
→ Therefore, this is a primary metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation.
Diuretics cause:
• Loss of Cl⁻, Na⁺, and H⁺ → contraction alkalosis
• ↑ bicarbonate reabsorption in proximal tubule
• Hypokalemia, which drives H⁺ into cells → alkalemia
Other options:
• Choice B. Ventilation cannot directly cause a
metabolic disturbance — it affects CO₂ (a respiratory component), not HCO₃⁻.
• Choice C. Respiratory alkalosis would show low pCO₂, not elevated. And meds like diuretics don’t
cause respiratory alkalosis.
• Choice D. This would show ↓ pCO₂ with or without ↓ HCO₃. But here, pCO₂ is high, not low.
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Fluids & Electrolytes, p. 353; Nelson, Acid-Base Balance
☆ PICU: Acid-Base Disturbances, Cardiology: Heart Failure
source: Osama Naga 2024, Fluids & Electrolytes, p. 353; Nelson, Acid-Base Balance

stem:
A 2-week-old male neonate is brought to the emergency department with a history of progressive nonbilious projectile vomiting for the past 3 days. On physical exam, a palpable olive-shaped mass is
felt in the epigastric region, and the infant appears dehydrated. Which of the following laboratory
abnormalities is most likely present?
explanation:
This is classic for hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. Vomiting leads to loss of gastric HCl,
causing metabolic alkalosis, and secondary renal K⁺ loss.
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
Age: 2–8 weeks
Sex: Firstborn male
Vomiting: Non-bilious, projectile
Exam: Olive-shaped mass, visible peristalsis
Labs:
o Hypochloremic, hypokalemic
o Metabolic alkalosis
Dx:
o Ultrasound: >3 mm thick, >15 mm length
o “String sign” on contrast if unclear
Tx:
o IV fluids, correct electrolytes
o Ramstedt pyloromyotomy
📚 Source: Nelson, GI Disorders – Pyloric Stenosis; Osama Naga 2024, Surgery/GI, p. 588
☆ Gastroenterology: Pyloric Stenosis
source: Nelson, GI Disorders – Pyloric Stenosis; Osama Naga 2024, Surgery/GI, p. 588

stem:
A 5-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with severe abdominal pain that woke her
from sleep, green bilious vomiting, and appears toxic and ill. Abdominal X-ray is attached below.
What is the most appropriate next step?
explanation:
Sudden pain + bilious/green vomit + ill appearance + multiple air fluid levels on xray =
possible volvulus or bowel obstruction.
• Aggressive fluid resuscitation is also important to manage hypovolemia and prevent shock →
HOWEVER, in this question stem there is no mention of hemodynamic instability, making
decompression the best answer choice
•
•
CT scan (C) is not first-line in unstable children; ultrasound or upper GI series may be used for
diagnosis after stabilization
Barium enema is contraindicated in suspected high obstruction and if volvulus is suspected
→ First priority = stabilize
• ABCDE approach
• Decompress the GI tract with NGT
• Prevent aspiration
• ↓ abdominal pressure
• Provide time to arrange imaging (CT or
contrast studies)
Stepwise Management of Suspected Surgical
Abdomen:
1. NGT insertion for decompression
2. IV fluids, electrolyte correction
3. Imaging (CT abdomen if volvulus or obstruction
suspected)
4. Urgent surgical consultation
📚 Source: Pocket Pediatrics, Bowel Obstruction
☆ Gastroenterology: Bowel Obstruction
source: Pocket Pediatrics, Bowel Obstruction

stem:
14 years old boy presented with agitation and angry shifted to hospital as he fought with his
schoolmate and hit his teacher. In the ER patient appears agitated, sweating with tachycardia, and
mydriasis. What is the cause of this symptoms?
explanation:
Sympathomimetic toxidrome: dilated pupils, agitation, hyperthermia, tachycardia → classic
of stimulants (amphetamines, cocaine).
• Clues: Sudden agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, dilated pupils
(mydriasis), sweating, seizures
• Toxidrome: Sympathomimetic
• Buzzwords: “Fighting at school,” “sweaty,” “hyperthermic,” “tachycardic,” “mydriasis”
• Common sources: ADHD meds (e.g. methylphenidate, dextroamphetamine), illicit drugs
• Complications: Rhabdomyolysis, seizures, hyperthermia, arrhythmias
• Management:
o Benzodiazepines (first-line) → sedate the patient,
control hypertension, and prevent seizures
o Cooling for hyperthermia
o IV fluids for rhabdo prevention
o Avoid β-blockers → risk of unopposed αvasoconstriction
Key point: Differentiate sympathomimetic from anticholinergic by:
• Sympathomimetics = sweaty
• Anticholinergics = dry skin
• + opioid overdose PEx will show miosis/constricted pupils ≠ mydriasis/dilated pupils
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Toxicology, p. 397; Nelson, Toxicology Chapter
☆ Emergency Medicine: Toxidromes
source: Osama Naga 2024, Toxicology, p. 397; Nelson, Toxicology Chapter

stem:
A previously healthy 12-year-old girl was watching television when she suddenly collapsed.
Bystanders report that she lost consciousness without preceding symptoms and was noted to be
pulseless. CPR was immediately initiated by family. There was no recent illness, trauma, or signs of
seizure activity. What is the most likely cause of her cardiac arrest?
explanation:
Sudden cardiac arrest in a child at rest is most often due to arrhythmia, especially Long
QT → ventricular tachycardia or torsades.
• QTc > 460 ms
• Syncope: during exercise (LQT1), emotion/startle (LQT2), sleep (LQT3)
• Risk: torsades → sudden cardiac death
•
•
•
•
Triggers: macrolides, fluoroquinolones, antihistamines, antipsychotics
Tx:
o β-blockers (propranolol, nadolol)
o ICD if high risk
o Avoid QT-prolonging drugs
Family screening required
2 types; both present with syncopal attacks and sudden death
o Romano-Ward syndrome (autosomal dominant) — no deafness
o Jervell & Lange-Nielsen syndrome (autosomal recessive) — deafness (sensorineural hearing
loss)
Remember in an ECG:
1 small box = 0.04 sec (40 ms)
1 large box = 0.20 sec (200
ms) = 5 small boxes
5 large boxes = 1 sec
ECG in a patient with anorexia showing severe prolonged QTc
- Sinus rhythm, HR 59 bpm, QT interval 0.7s (700 ms)
How to read QT interval on ECG:
- Identify lead II or V5/V6 — use the one
with a clearly defined T-wave
- Measure QT (in ms): From start of QRS
to end of T wave (not the U wave)
- Measure RR interval (peak of one R to next
R), in seconds
- Apply Bazett’s formula (corrected QT):
QTc = QT / √RR
→ QTc < 440 ms (<2.2 large boxes) = normal
→ QTc 440–460 ms = borderline
→ QTc > 460 ms = prolonged (in M&F)
→ QTc ≥ 500 ms = high risk for torsades
Clinical pearls:
• Always consider ECG screening in unexplained syncope or sudden cardiac arrest
• QTc >460–480 ms in children should prompt evaluation
• Management: β-blockers (e.g., propranolol), avoid QT-prolonging drugs, and ICD in high-risk
patients
📚 Source: Nelson, Cardiology Chapter – Arrhythmias; Osama Naga 2024, Cardiology, p. 408
☆ Cardiology: Arrhythmias, Congenital Long QT syndrome
source: Nelson, Cardiology Chapter – Arrhythmias; Osama Naga 2024, Cardiology, p. 408

stem:
A 3-week-old infant is brought for a routine newborn check-up. Physical
examination finding is attached. There is erythematous or drainage. The
infant is feeding well and has passed the newborn hearing screen. There
is no family history of hearing loss or congenital anomalies. What is the
most appropriate next step?

options.label: B
options.text: MRI to rule out branchial cleft

explanation:
If the vignette only says “preauricular pit, no other findings” → Answer = A. Observation
If it says “+ hearing loss” or “+ dysmorphism/family history” → Answer = C. Genetic referral
When to choose "Observation" (Answer A):
• Isolated, asymptomatic pit
• No hearing loss
• No family history or dysmorphic features
→ This is the default answer in general pediatrics and on boards when there are no red flags
Choose "Genetic referral" only if any of the following red flags apply:
• Associated hearing loss
