• Family history of deafness or renal anomalies
• Other facial anomalies/dysmorphism
• Suspicion for syndromes:
o Branchio-Oto-Renal (BOR) syndrome
o CHARGE syndrome
o Townes-Brocks syndrome
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Congenital Anomalies
☆ General Pediatrics: Congenital Anomalies, Ear pit / Preauricular sinus
source: Osama Naga 2024, Congenital Anomalies
topic: Congenital Anomalies, Ear pit / Preauricular sinus

stem:
A 5-year-old child who was diagnosed with acute otitis media two days ago now presents to the
emergency department with fever, worsening ear pain, and swelling behind the right ear. On
physical examination, there is erythema, tenderness, and fluctuance over the mastoid process,
with posterior displacement of the auricle. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Postauricular swelling + history of otitis = mastoiditis. Confirm with CT mastoid if diagnosis
uncertain. Treat with IV antibiotics ± surgical drainage.
• Mastoiditis is a complication of acute otitis media caused by extension of infection into the
mastoid air cells
• Posterior auricular swelling, erythema, tenderness, and protrusion of the pinna are classic
signs
• Lymphadenitis would typically present with discrete nodes and no auricular displacement
• Cellulitis may cause redness and swelling, but not deep tenderness or ear displacement
• Otitis externa involves the external auditory canal and does not cause mastoid tenderness
• Mastoiditis is a complication of AOM and presents with fever, ear pain, protrusion of the outer ear,
and postauricular erythema and tenderness.
• Temporal CT scan may be indicated to confirm the diagnosis for atypical cases.
•
•
•
•
Mastoiditis is a complication of AOM and presents with fever, ear pain, protrusion of the outer ear,
and postauricular erythema and tenderness.
Temporal CT scan may be indicated to confirm the diagnosis for atypical cases.
Board Clues
o “Otitis media + swelling/redness behind ear + protruding pinna”
o “CT shows opacified mastoid air cells”
o “No response to oral abx” = switch to IV and admit
Tx:
o Hospital admission
o IV antibiotics:
 First-line empiric: Ceftriaxone or Cefotaxime + Vancomycin
• Covers: Strep pneumoniae, Strep pyogenes, H. influenzae, MRSA
 If complications or chronic OM: add metronidazole for anaerobes
o Duration: 7–10 days → may switch to oral for a total of 14–21 days based on response
o Surgical Indications:
 No improvement within 24–48 hrs of IV antibiotics
 Fluctuant abscess
 Subperiosteal collection, facial nerve palsy, or intracranial complications
 Options:
• Myringotomy ± tympanostomy
• Mastoidectomy (if severe or chronic)
📚 Source: Nelson, Infectious Diseases; Osama Naga 2024, ENT Infections, p. 298
☆ Infectious Disease: ENT infections, Otitis media complications
source: Nelson, Infectious Diseases; Osama Naga 2024, ENT Infections, p. 298

stem:
A 7-year-old boy is being treated with nitrofurantoin for a recent urinary tract infection. Two days into
treatment, his parents notice he appears unusually tired and has yellowing of the eyes. On
examination, he is pale and mildly jaundiced. Lab results show a hemoglobin of 8 g/dL, elevated
reticulocyte count, and indirect hyperbilirubinemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Nitrofurantoin triggers oxidative hemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients → acute drop in Hb,
jaundice, reticulocytosis.
• This is a classic presentation of G6PD deficiency, a X-linked recessive disorder [M>F] that leads to
oxidative hemolysis after triggers like fava beans, infections, or certain drugs (e.g., sulfa,
antimalarials, nitrofurantoin)
• Typical findings include acute anemia (drop in Hb), jaundice, dark urine, and elevated retics and
LDH with low haptoglobin following exposure to trigger.
• Peripheral smear shows Heinz bodies (denatured hemoglobin precipitates inside RBCs) and bite
cells (from splenic removal of Heinz bodies).
Clinical pearls:
• G6PD deficiency → episodic intravascular hemolysis, jaundice, hemoglobinuria
• Heinz bodies require supravital staining for visualization
• Common G6PD triggers include sulfa drugs, fava beans, antimalarials, and nitrofurantoin
• Avoid oxidant triggers, especially in high-risk populations (Mediterranean, African, Asian males)
• Confirm diagnosis with G6PD enzyme assay (note: may be falsely normal during acute hemolysis)
• Management includes supportive care, removal of trigger, and transfusion if indicated
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Hematology, p. 391; Red Book 2021–2024, G6PD-related hemolysis;
AMBOSS; UWorld.
☆ Hematology: G6PD deficiency
source: Osama Naga 2024, Hematology, p. 391; Red Book 2021–2024, G6PD-related hemolysis;

stem:
A 9-year-old child with sickle cell disease is being followed in the hematology clinic. His mother asks
if there is a permanent cure for her son's condition. The child is currently on hydroxyurea and has
had multiple pain crises in the past. What is the most appropriate response?
explanation:
• The only curative therapy for sickle cell disease is allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell
transplantation (HSCT), typically from an HLA-matched sibling donor
• Hydroxyurea reduces the frequency of vaso-occlusive crises and improves quality of life but is not
curative
• Saying “no cure” is misleading, as transplant is an established cure in select patients
• Chronic transfusions are used for prevention of stroke or severe complications but are also not
curative
Clinical pearls:
•
•
•
HSCT is most effective when performed early, before end-organ damage
Gene therapy is under development but not yet standard of care
Discuss risks of HSCT (e.g., graft-vs-host disease, rejection) when counseling families
📚 Source: Nelson, Hematology Chapter, Osama Naga 2024, p. 395
☆ Hematology: Hemoglobinopathies; Sickle Cell Disease
source: Nelson, Hematology Chapter, Osama Naga 2024, p. 395

stem:
A three weeks old infant, was born at home, brought by his parents because of abnormal looking
genitalia (exact picture). Mother reported that one of her brothers is infertile. Chromosomal analysis
revealed 46 XY. Serum testosterone and dihydrotestosterone levels were high. Which one of the
following is most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
46,XY DSD with ambiguous genitalia, ↑ T and DHT, and infertility in male relatives → classic
for partial androgen insensitivity.
• The patient has a 46,XY DSD → undervirilized male
• ↑ Testosterone AND ↑ DHT = indicates normal 5-alpha-reductase
activity
o So 5-alpha-reductase deficiency is ruled out because in
that disorder DHT would be ↓ despite high testosterone
• Ambiguous genitalia, with both androgens elevated, suggests:
o Normal androgen production
o Abnormal androgen receptor function
Other choices:
• CAIS → 46,XY + normal T + female external genitalia, no uterus, no
ambiguity.
• 5⍺ reductase deficiency → Would show ↑ testosterone and ↓ DHT, not ↑ DHT
• Testicular biosynthesis defect → ↓ testosterone and ↓ DHT due to defects in steroidogenesis
Board Buzzwords:
• “Female phenotype, no uterus, intra-abdominal testes” → CAIS
• “Ambiguous genitalia + ↑ testosterone, ↓ DHT” → 5α-reductase deficiency
• “46,XX + virilized female genitalia + salt wasting” → CAH
• “Pubertal virilization in phenotypic female” → 5α-reductase deficiency
• “Ambiguous genitalia + family history of infertility” → PAIS
📚 Source: Nelson, Endocrinology – DSD, Osama Naga 2024, Endocrine
☆ Endocrinology: Disorders of Sexual Development (DSDs), Ambiguous genitalia
source: Nelson, Endocrinology – DSD, Osama Naga 2024, Endocrine
topic: Disorders of Sexual Development (DSDs), Ambiguous genitalia

stem:
A 1-week-old full-term male neonate is noted to be macrosomic, has hepatomegaly and umbilical
hernia, and is requiring high concentration dextrose infusion to maintain euglycemia. Labs show
hypoglycemia with absent ketone bodies. Which of the following tests is most diagnostic for the
underlying condition?
explanation:
• This is a classic presentation of congenital hyperinsulinism (persistent hyperinsulinemic
hypoglycemia of infancy)
• Key features:
o Macrosomia (possibly due to maternal
diabetes or syndromic cause like BeckwithWiedemann)
o Hypoglycemia without ketones (insulin
suppresses lipolysis and ketogenesis)
o Hepatomegaly due to glycogen accumulation
• Elevated insulin in the setting of hypoglycemia
confirms diagnosis → both insulin and C-peptide are
elevated
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Endocrine/Metabolic p. 384; Nelson, Hypoglycemia in Neonates
☆ Endocrinology: Neonatal Hypoglycemia, Hyperinsulinism
source: Osama Naga 2024, Endocrine/Metabolic p. 384; Nelson, Hypoglycemia in Neonates

stem:
A newborn boy from a diabetic mother was macrocosmic weighting at 4.3kg. Upon examination you
noticed the baby having an umbilical hernia, large tongue and hypoglycemia. Most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Classic triad: macroglossia, organomegaly, neonatal hypoglycemia, abdominal wall defects (e.g.,
umbilical hernia). Also at risk of Wilms tumor, hepatoblastoma → screen with AFP, abdominal US.
Other options:
• B. IDA → Does explain macrosomia & hypoglycemia, but NOT macroglossia or umbilical hernia (not
full syndrome)
• C. Congenital hypothyroidism → May cause macroglossia, umbilical hernia, and hypoglycemia, but
baby would not be macrosomic
• D. Congenital hyperinsulinism → Explains hypoglycemia, not hernia, macroglossia, or macrosomia
Board Buzzwords:
• Macroglossia + hypoglycemia + abdominal defect = Beckwith-Wiedemann
• Frontal bossing + advanced bone age + tall child = Sotos syndrome
• Overgrowth + polydactyly + organomegaly (X-linked) = Simpson-Golabi-Behmel
• Macroorchidism + long face = Fragile X
• Tall teen boy + small testes, gynecomastia = Klinefelter
📚 Source: Nelson, Genetics/Metabolism; Osama Naga 2024, Genetics p. 328
☆ Genetics: Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, Endocrinology: Pediatric Overgrowth Syndromes
source: Nelson, Genetics/Metabolism; Osama Naga 2024, Genetics p. 328
topic: Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, Endocrinology: Pediatric Overgrowth Syndromes

stem:
A 3-day-old newborn presented to ER with history of vomiting and mild dehydration.
Labs showed:
- Normal CBC.
-
Na: 125
K: 7
Bicarbonate 18
Cl 98
BUN: 8 mg/dL [high]
Creatinine: 30 μmol/L [normal]
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Hyponatremia + hyperkalemia + dehydration + metabolic
acidosis → classic for salt-wasting CAH (21-hydroxylase
deficiency) in neonates.
Confirm with 17-hydroxyprogesterone.
• Although affected girls are usually identified at birth
due to ambiguous genitalia (virilization) from excess
androgens, boys have normal genitalia and typically
are identified at age 1-2 weeks with vomiting,
hypotension, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia due
to lack of sufficient mineralocorticoids (salt
wasting). Cortisol deficiency causes hypoglycemia
and further impairs the ability to maintain blood
pressure.
[Further explanation & Tx under Q6]
📚 Source: Nelson, Endocrinology – CAH; Osama Naga
2024, Endocrine p. 373
☆ Endocrinology: Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH)
source: Nelson, Endocrinology – CAH; Osama Naga

stem:
A 7-year-old boy is brought to the clinic with a 2-week history of a scaly, erythematous patch with hair
loss on the scalp. The lesion has raised borders, some broken hairs, and black dots. He was
previously prescribed a topical antibiotic without improvement. There is no fever or lymphadenopathy.
What is the most appropriate next step in treatment?
explanation:
• This is tinea capitis, a dermatophyte infection.
• Topicals don’t penetrate the hair shaft → requires systemic antifungal
• Griseofulvin (20–25 mg/kg/day for 6–8 weeks) is 1st-line.
Full clinical vignette:
A 5-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his mother for evaluation of a patch of hair loss on his scalp that
has been worsening over the past 2 weeks. She notes the area began as a small scaly patch and has now
expanded, with more noticeable hair loss and crusting. The child
sometimes scratches the area, and she reports no improvement despite using
over-the-counter antibacterial ointment. On exam, you note a well-demarcated,
erythematous, scaly alopecic plaque on the parietal scalp with black dot
appearance (broken hairs at follicular openings). There is no fever, and
lymphadenopathy is absent.
Other options:
• Topical antifungals: Ineffective for scalp involvement
• Topical steroids: May worsen fungal infections
• Oral fluconazole: Alternative but not first-line (less effective than griseofulvin for Microsporum spp)
📚 Osama Naga 2024 – Dermatology; Nelson Textbook – Fungal Infections of the Scalp
☆ Dermatology: Fungal Infections

stem: What is true about this lesion? (exact picture):
answer: B. Appears 2 days before the rash
explanation:
• The described lesion is a Koplik spot — a small, bluish-white papule on an erythematous base,
pathognomonic for measles
• Appears 1–2 days before the classic maculopapular rash
• Found on buccal mucosa, typically opposite the molars
Key High-Yield Differentiators:
• Measles: 3 Cs + Koplik + very sick
• Roseola: High fever → rash
• Fifth: Slapped cheek + lacy rash
• Varicella: Vesicles in different stages
• Scarlet: Sandpaper rash + pharyngitis
• HFMD: Vesicles + mouth + palms/soles
📚 Osama Naga 2024 – Infectious Exanthems, p. 498; Red Book 2021–2024 – Measles Diagnosis
☆ Infectious Diseases: Viral Exanthems

stem:
A 6-year-old previously healthy girl is brought to the emergency department with a 2-week history
of increased thirst, frequent urination, and daytime fatigue. Her mother also reports noticeable
weight loss over the last month. Today, the child has been more lethargic, and her breathing appears
deep and rapid. On exam, she is tachycardic, and dehydrated. What is the most appropriate next
step in management?
explanation:
• Suspected new-onset type 1 diabetes mellitus
• Best rapid test = check for glucosuria + ketonuria
• If positive, move to full labs, electrolytes, and ABG
★ Diabetic Ketoacidosis: (very high yield for wards & boards)
• Approximately 25% of children with T1DM present in diabetic
ketoacidosis (DKA).
• Pathophysiology: Triggered by inadequate insulin/increased insulin
need (stress from infection, steroids, etc.)
o ↓ insulin → glycogenolysis + gluconeogenesis + lipolysis
o ↑ glucose → polyuria → dehydration
o ↑ lipolysis leads to creation of ketone bodies, if ↑↑ → ↓ pH → electrolyte abnormalities
•
•
Patients with DKA present initially with polyuria, polydipsia, nausea, and vomiting.
o Abdominal pain occurs frequently and can mimic an acute abdomen.
o The abdomen may be tender from vomiting or distended secondary to a paralytic ileus.
Diagnosis: Glucose >200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L); Moderate–large ketones in urine,
betahydroxybutyrate in blood; AGAP metabolic acidosis
General principles of DKA Management:
• Initial labs: glucose, VBG, Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, HCO₃⁻, BUN, Cr, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, phosphate, urinalysis (ketones)
• Monitor: neuro status, vitals hourly, glucose q1hr & electrolytes q2–4h
• Start with fluids — insulin comes after volume resuscitation begins
o 1st start w/ a NS bolus 10 mg/kg
o 2nd continuous IV fluids at 1.5–2× maintenance (to rehydrate and avoid dehydration)
 DO NOT USE HYPOTONIC FLUIDS – cerebral edema risk
 ↑ dextrose-containing fluids once blood glucose <300 and ↓ nondextrose fluids
• Insulin infusion – Regular insulin (0.1 u/kg/hr) – start 1 hr after initial NS bolus
o Goal to ↓ glucose by 50–100 mg/dL/hr (2.8–5.6 mmol/L/hr)
• At risk of initial hypokalemia and hypophosphatemia 2º to urine losses, hypernatremia 2º to dehydration
(may present as pseudohyponatremia due to hyperglycemia)
o insulin shifts K⁺ and PO4 shift → hypokalemia & hypophosphatemia risk [total body depletion]
o K added to IV fluids [typically 20 mmol/L potassium phosphate + 20 mmol/L potassium chloride or
acetate]
o PO4 replaced only if symptomatic (e.g., muscle weakness, arrhythmia, hemolysis)
• Avoid bicarbonate unless pH <6.9 with life-threatening instability – risk of cerebral edema
Mild DKA: pH 7.2 – <7.3, HCO3 10 – <15
Usually well-appearing, alert
Fluids:
10–20 mL/kg NS over 1–2 hr
Maintenance + deficit (5%) over 36–
48 hrs
Insulin:
Start 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr IV
regular insulin 1 hour after fluids
Potassium: Add once urine output
confirmed
Glucose: Add D5% when BG < 250–300
mg/dL (13.9–16.7 mmol/L)
Moderate DKA: pH 7.1 – <7.2, HCO3 5 – <10
More dehydration, possible lethargy
Fluids:
10–20 mL/kg NS over 1–2 hr
Maintenance + 7–8% deficit over 48 hrs
Insulin:
Start 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr IV regular
insulin 1 hour after fluids
Potassium: Monitor K⁺ closely — total body
K⁺ is low even if serum is normal
Glucose: Add D5% when BG < 250–300
mg/dL (13.9–16.7 mmol/L)
Severe DKA: pH <7.1, HCO3 <5
High risk for cerebral edema, may be
obtunded or confused
Fluids:
10–20 mL/kg NS over 1–2 hr
Replace 10% deficit over 48 hrs,
using 0.9% or 0.45% NaCl
depending on corrected Na⁺
Insulin:
Start 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr IV
regular insulin 1 hour after fluids
Potassium: Monitor K⁺ closely — total
body K⁺ is low even if serum is normal
Glucose: Add D5% when BG < 250–
300 mg/dL (13.9–16.7 mmol/L)
Avoid bicarbonate unless pH <6.9
with cardiac instability or coma
Complications:
• Cerebral edema (0.5% to 1%)
o Signs/Sx: Headache, incontinence, altered mental status, emesis, hypertension, bradycardia, irregular
respirations
o Risk factors: age <5 yo, severe acidosis (pH <7.1, bicarb <5), severe dehydration (elevated BUN), severe
hyperosmolarity (corrected Na >155, glucose >1,000mg/dL [55.5 mmol/L]), longer duration of sx, new-onset
diabetes, aggressive initial fluid resuscitation, treatment with bicarbonate
o Management: Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg intravenously over 20 min or hypertonic saline (3% NS) 5–10 mL/kg over 30
min
 repeated if there is no response in 30 minutes
o Respiratory compromise – Rare respiratory failure
• Electrolyte abnormalities
o Cardiac dysrhythmias
o Rapid correction has risk for CNS fluid shifts
When is the patient considered out of DKA?
• Venous pH > 7.3
• Bicarbonate ≥ 15 mEq/L
• No ketonuria or ketonemia
• Alert mental status
• Hemodynamically stable and well hydrated
• Tolerating oral intake
Next steps:
• Start subcutaneous insulin once DKA has
resolved and the child is eating
• Give SC insulin 30–60 minutes before stopping
the insulin drip to avoid rebound hyperglycemia
Clinical pearls:
• Always start DKA management with isotonic fluid bolus (10–20 mL/kg) based on severity
• Do not give bicarbonate unless pH <6.9 with life-threatening symptoms (e.g., arrhythmia)
• Replace deficit + maintenance over 36–48 hours; add insulin only after 1–2 hours of fluids
• Monitor glucose, electrolytes, pH, and mental status closely for cerebral edema
DKA mnemonic: The most important findings of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) are: Delirium/psychosis,
Dehydration, Kussmaul respirations, Abdominal pain/nausea/vomiting, fruity (Acetone) breath odor.
📚 Source: Nelson – Diabetes Mellitus; Osama Naga 2024 – Endocrinology
☆ Endocrinology: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus, Diabetic Ketoacidosis
source: Nelson – Diabetes Mellitus; Osama Naga 2024 – Endocrinology

stem: (Exact picture in exam), How old is the baby?
explanation:
• If the image clearly shows stable sitting without support, then the baby is at least 6 months old
• A 4-month-old may sit with support, but not independently
• A 10-month-old would show more gross motor advancement (pulling to stand, crawling, cruising)
Red flags in development:
 9 months: not sitting alone
 12 months: not passing objects hand to hand
 15 months: abnormal pincer grasp
 18 months: not walking alone
 24 months: not saying words







Hearing assessment: Should be done by 3 months.
Neonatal breast hypertrophy (Gynecomastia): Resolves at 1 year.
Teeth eruption: Normal up to 18 months.
Toe walking: Normal up 3 years, after that:
o Abnormal speech > ASD
o Normal speech > Short Achillis tendon
Thumb sucking: Stop at 4 years.
Handedness: Should start at 2-4 y (before that check nerves and muscles or perinatal stroke)
Head banging: Normal up to 5 years.
📚 Source: Osama Naga – Developmental Milestones; Nelson – Growth & Development Tables
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: Developmental Milestones
source: Osama Naga – Developmental Milestones; Nelson – Growth & Development Tables

stem:
A 5-year-old child with a documented egg allergy is scheduled to receive the annual influenza
vaccine. His previous reaction to egg ingestion included hives and mild wheezing, but no
hospitalizations. What is the appropriate management?

options.label: D
options.text: Give either type of influenza active and inactive

answer: D. Give either type of influenza active and inactive
explanation:
Current Recommendation (AAP & CDC):
• Children with egg allergy of any severity (including anaphylaxis) can receive any age-appropriate
inactivated influenza vaccine (IIV) without special precautions.
• Live-attenuated vaccine (LAIV) is also considered safe in
most egg-allergic individuals but IIV is the preferred and
safest option.
• Thus, both inactivated influenza vaccine (IIV) and live
attenuated influenza vaccine (LAIV) are safe to
administer to individuals with any severity of egg
allergy.
• No special precautions or allergy testing are required
prior to vaccination.
Indications for influenza vaccine:
1- Health care practitioners
2- Migrant & refugees
3- High risk groups: <5 years old, especially <2 years
Highest risk: <6 months (can’t be vaccinated!)
General vaccine precautions:
A precaution is a condition in the recipient that
might increase the risk for serious adverse
reactions or interfere with a vaccine's ability to
produce immunity. Generally, vaccination should be deferred when a precaution is present, except when
the benefits of protection outweigh the risks.
• 4 weeks after stopping steroids
o Oral steroid for less than 2 weeks, inhaled steroid, and topical steroid are not
contraindications for live vaccines
o Oral steroid cause immunosuppression only if given more than 2 weeks with a dose of 2
mg/kg per day
• 3 months (12 weeks) after stopping other types of chemotherapy
• No live or killed vaccines after rituximab treatment for 6 months
• Acute moderate or severe illness (with or without fever)
• Delayed for 3-11 months after IVIG, 6 months after PRBC, 7 months after FFP or platelets,
immediately after packed RBCs
Special precautions:
Rotavirus vaccine: uncorrected GI tract malformation (e.g., Meckel's diverticulum)
Egg protein-containing vaccines: allergic reactions other than urticaria (e.g., angioedema,
bronchospasm) to egg products — yellow fever vaccine
•
•
•
•
Pertussis vaccine: progressive or unstable neurologic disorders (e.g., uncontrolled epilepsy,
infantile spasms, progressive encephalopathy)
Tetanus toxoid-containing vaccines
o Development of Guillain-Barré syndrome within 6 weeks of a previous dose
o Arthus reaction after a previous dose
📚 Source: AAP & CDC Vaccination Guidelines, UWorld.
☆ General Pediatrics: Vaccine contraindications
source: AAP & CDC Vaccination Guidelines, UWorld.

stem:
A 9-year-old girl with chronic immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) underwent a splenectomy 1
month ago. She presents for her routine immunizations, including the seasonal influenza vaccine.
Which of the following is the most appropriate statement regarding influenza vaccination in this
patient?

options.label: A
options.text: She can receive all types of influenza vaccine

options.label: D
options.text: Live attenuated vaccine is preferred post-splenectomy

explanation:
• Asplenic patients are at higher risk for infections by encapsulated organisms (e.g., Strep pneumo, H.
influenzae, Neisseria) and viral respiratory pathogens
• Live attenuated influenza vaccine (LAIV) is contraindicated in asplenic or immunocompromised
patients due to the risk of disseminated viral infection.
• Use inactivated influenza vaccine annually
Note:
•
•
Inactivated (killed or component) viral vaccines primarily
generate a humoral immune response against extracellular viral
antigens, preventing viral entry into the cell.
In contrast, live attenuated viral vaccines can generate a strong
cell-mediated immune response that can kill virally-infected
cells, in addition to providing humoral immunity.
Clinical pearls:
• Asplenic children should receive:
o PPSV23 + PCV13 (as per schedule)
o Hib, MenACWY + MenB, and annual influenza IIV
• Avoid LAIV in asplenia, immunodeficiency, or close contacts of immunocompromised individuals
📚 Source: AAP & CDC Vaccination Guidelines, UWorld.
☆ General Pediatrics: Vaccine contraindications
source: AAP & CDC Vaccination Guidelines, UWorld.

stem: Which of the following vaccines are given to preschool age children (4–6 years)?
explanation:
• These are the booster doses for school entry in Saudi Arabia and globally
• Hep A and MCV4 are given earlier (18–24 months)
• PCV13 is completed by 12 months unless catch-up is needed
Saudi Vaccination Schedule (MOH 2024)
• Preterm infants should receive the vaccine at actual age not the corrected age
•
•
Only exception is Hepatitis B vaccine if birth weight < 2kg — given at 1 month or discharge
(whichever comes first)
Preschool (4-6 years) vaccines are: DTaP, OPV, Varicella, MMR
Remember: Birth, 2–4–6 months build the base, 9–12–18 add extras, school & teen boosters follow.
- Measles monovalent at 9 months (pre-MMR)
- MMR given at 12, 18 months, and again at 4–6 years
- IPV/OPV given at 0, 2, 4, 6, 12, 18 months, and 4–6 years
- PCV: 3-dose primary (2, 4, 6 mo) + booster at 12 mo
- MCV4: at 9 & 12 months, booster at 18 years
- Hep A: 2-dose series at 18 & 24 months
- HPV: for girls only, at age 12
Special Considerations:
• BCG vaccine: delayed to 6 months to help avoid
disseminated BCGitis in undiagnosed SCID
• Influenza vaccine: annually from 6 months onward
• HPV: now part of the MOH schedule for girls ≥12 years
• Catch-up schedules available per CDC/AAP if delayed
📚 Source: AAP & CDC Vaccination Guidelines, Saudi MoH Vaccination schedule.
☆ General Pediatrics: Vaccines
source: AAP & CDC Vaccination Guidelines, Saudi MoH Vaccination schedule.

stem:
A 6-year-old boy with diagnosed autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is brought by his parents due
to picky eating behaviors. He consumes a limited variety of foods, often refusing vegetables and
proteins, but has no signs of dysphagia, choking, vomiting, or gastrointestinal symptoms. His
weight and height are within the normal range for age. The parents are concerned about his rigid eating
habits and want to know how to improve mealtime behaviors. What is the best initial management
approach?

options.label: A
options.text: Model preferred eating behaviors as family group

options.label: C
options.text: Allow eating between meals throughout the day

answer: A. Model preferred eating behaviors as family group
explanation:
• Autism feeding difficulties are common;
involve sensory sensitivity
• Best approach = structured mealtimes, family
modeling, avoid pressure
• Long sessions or snacks between meals worsen
behavior
• Key Developmental Clues:
o No babbling by 12 months
o No pointing or single words by 16 months
o No 2-word phrases by 24 months
Board Buzzwords for Autism:
o Regression in speech or social skills at any age
- No response to name
o Lack of pretend play
- Limited joint attention
- Echoing speech (echolalia)
• Screening & Diagnosis:
- Sensory sensitivity
o Screen all children at 18 & 24 months (AAP guidelines)
(e.g., avoids textures/sounds)
o Tool: M-CHAT-R/F (Modified Checklist for Autism in Toddlers)
``
o Gold standard Dx: ADIR / ADOS (by developmental pediatrician or psychologist)
• Management:
o Early behavioral therapy (ABA) = most effective
o Speech & occupational therapy
o Risperidone / aripiprazole → for severe aggression/irritability (USFDA approved)
📚 Source: Nelson Symptom-Based Diagnosis – Feeding; AAP PREP 2024
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: Autism
source: Nelson Symptom-Based Diagnosis – Feeding; AAP PREP 2024

stem:
A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic for evaluation of a chronic skin rash. His mother reports
thick, red, scaly plaques that have been present for months, mostly on the elbows and knees, and
recently a similar patch developed on the scalp. The lesions are well-demarcated, symmetrical, and
mildly pruritic. There is no known contact with irritants or pets. He has a history of mild nail pitting on
examination. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Plaque psoriasis is the most common form in children and presents with chronic, welldemarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery-white scale, often on extensor surfaces and
the scalp
o Psoriasis = plaques, silvery scale on extensor surfaces
• Nail changes like pitting or onycholysis support the diagnosis
• Tinea corporis tends to be annular with central clearing and often unilateral
• Contact dermatitis is usually acute, confined to the site of exposure, and may show vesicles
• Atopic dermatitis tends to involve flexural areas and presents with intense pruritus and oozing in
acute phases
Clinical pearls:
• Triggers include infections, stress, and trauma (Koebner phenomenon)
• First-line treatment: topical corticosteroids
• Consider vitamin D analogs or phototherapy for extensive or refractory cases
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024, Dermatology; Red Book – Fungal Skin
Conditions, Pocket Pediatrics 2016 Dermatologic Images
☆ Dermatology: Fungal Infections
source: Osama Naga 2024, Dermatology; Red Book – Fungal Skin

stem:
A 14-year-old boy is brought to clinic due to concerns about short stature. His height is below the 5th
percentile, and he has no signs of pubertal development (Tanner stage l). His growth velocity over
the past year has been appropriate. The father mentions that he himself was also a "late bloomer" and
didn't reach his adult height until late adolescence. Examination is otherwise normal. Bone age is
consistent with a 12-year-old. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Family history + delayed puberty + delayed bone age = constitutional delay, not pathological
• Height typically catches up by late adolescence
• No treatment required unless psychosocial concern
Board Clues:
• Bone age is the key to narrow the cause:
o Normal bone age = likely familial
o Delayed bone age = constitutional, endocrine, systemic
• Normal growth velocity = variant of normal
• ↓ growth velocity = always pathologic
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Endocrinology, Short stature
☆ Endocrinology: Short Stature
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Endocrinology, Short stature

stem:
A 14 years old boy presented to the clinic with his parents with concerns about their child being short,
his current bone age is 11 years, and he is tanner stage 1. Testicles measured 3 ml bilaterally,
otherwise he is well with no apparent dysmorphic features. Father’s height is 170 cm he reached his
full maturation in college. His mother’s height is 160cm, and her menarche was at age 12. (Picture of
growth chart showed both the height and weight were constantly on the 3rd centile). Laboratory
findings:
- FSH 4.9 (normal)
- Testosterone 45 (slightly elevated)
- LH 17 (slightly elevated)
What is most appropriate plan?
explanation:
• This picture is consistent with constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP); delayed bone age +
family Hx of late puberty + normal growth
• Delayed puberty with psychological impact → consider low-dose testosterone for induction
• GH not indicated as child is growing normally
• No red flags to warrant MRI
Why testosterone is the right choice:
1. A short 3–6-month course of low-dose testosterone (e.g. testosterone enanthate or cypionate 50–
100 mg IM monthly) can jump-start puberty and improve growth velocity and psychological wellbeing.
2. It does not compromise final height when bone age is significantly delayed.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Endocrinology, Short stature
☆ Endocrinology: Short Stature
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Endocrinology, Short stature

stem:
A 6-year-old child presents to the clinic with a 3-day history of flu-like symptoms, including
rhinorrhea, cough, and low-grade fever. Over the past 24 hours, his symptoms have worsened, and
he now complains of facial pain and tenderness over the maxillary sinuses, along with persistent
fever. Examination reveals purulent nasal discharge and tenderness over the cheeks. What is the
most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: C
options.text: Culture aspirate then based on isolated organisms start treatment

explanation:
• Meets criteria for acute bacterial sinusitis (persistent, worsening symptoms)
• First-line: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (90 mg/kg/day divided BID) for 10–14 days
• The child has acute bacterial sinusitis, which typically follows viral upper respiratory infections
• Worsening of symptoms after initial improvement, or persistent symptoms >10 days, or
severe symptoms (fever, facial pain) indicate a bacterial cause
• First-line treatment is high-dose amoxicillin-clavulanate to cover Streptococcus pneumoniae,
H. influenzae, and Moraxella catarrhalis
• Sinus aspirates are invasive and rarely needed in uncomplicated pediatric sinusitis
• Symptomatic treatment alone is insufficient for bacterial etiology
Clinical Pearls:
• Use high-dose amoxicillin-clavulanate (90 mg/kg/day of amoxicillin component divided BID) in
moderate to severe cases or high-resistance areas
• Consider alternative antibiotics in penicillin allergy (e.g., levofloxacin, clindamycin + cefixime)
• Nasal corticosteroids may help with symptoms but are not a substitute for antibiotics
📚 Source: Pocket Pediatrics, ENT infections
☆ Infectious Diseases: ENT infections
source: Pocket Pediatrics, ENT infections

stem:
A 4-year-old child is brought to the clinic with a 3-day history of foul-smelling, unilateral nasal discharge and
low-grade fever. There is no history of trauma, allergy, or recent upper respiratory infection. The child is
otherwise active and playful. Examination reveals mucopurulent discharge from the right nostril without
signs of periorbital edema or sinus tenderness. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure and discharge the patient home

options.label: B
options.text: Prescribe oral antibiotics and follow up in 3 days

options.label: C
options.text: Refer for urgent nasal endoscopy and removal of foreign body

options.label: D
options.text: Send nasal swab for culture and start broad-spectrum antibiotics

answer: C. Refer for urgent nasal endoscopy and removal of foreign body
explanation:
• Most likely cause = retained nasal foreign body or viral rhinitis
• No signs of systemic illness → no antibiotics initially
• Advise return if persistent or worsening
• Foul-smelling, unilateral nasal discharge in a young child with no other systemic signs is highly
suggestive of a nasal foreign body, especially if persistent
• Antibiotics alone are not curative and may delay diagnosis or cause complications like sinusitis or
aspiration
• Culture is unnecessary as removal of the foreign body is curative
• Reassurance alone may miss a potentially dangerous retained object
Clinical pearls:
• Common in toddlers and preschoolers who may not report inserting objects into their nose
• Objects like beads, paper, food, or foam can cause secondary bacterial infection
• Foreign bodies should be removed via instrumentation or suction, preferably by ENT if not visible
📚 Source: UWorld; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 260
☆ ENT Emergency: Nasal Foreign Body - Unilateral Foul-Smelling Discharge in a Child
source: UWorld; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 260
topic: Nasal Foreign Body - Unilateral Foul-Smelling Discharge in a Child

stem:
A previously healthy 4-month-old boy presents with HR 240 bpm, poor feeding, now unresponsive.
ECG is provided. What is the most appropriate next step?
explanation:
• Absent P wave + regular rhythm + narrow QRS complex = SVT
• Unstable SVT = shockable rhythm → immediate synchronized cardioversion
o Dose: 0.5–1 J/kg, escalate to 2 J/kg if needed
• Vagal maneuvers are first-line in stable SVT
• Defibrillation is used for VF/pulseless VT
Electrical cardioversion:
o Unsynchronized (defibrillation)
 High energy shock delivered immediately
 Used for V.fib and pulseless V.tach
o Synchronized
 Low energy shock synchronized to occur during QRS complex
 Avoids shock during T wave which can lead to ventricular fibrillation
 Used for stable or unstable SVT (afib, aflutter)
📚 Source: ACLS, PALS
☆ Cardiology: Arrhythmias
source: ACLS, PALS

stem:
12 -year-old girl presented to the emergency department for palpitations. She reports that her
symptoms began approximately 10 minutes ago and have not subsided. Vital signs are significant for
pulse of 250/min otherwise normal. An electrocardiogram is performed that shows a narrow QRScomplex tachyarrhythmia (Photo provided). What is the best initial step?
explanation:
• Absent P wave + regular rhythm + narrow QRS complex = SVT
• Patient is stable, thus the next best step is vagal maneuvers (valsalva, breath holding, coughing,
deep respirations, gagging, swallowing), carotid massage (via ↑ vagal tone).
• If persistent, can proceed with adenosine.
• If patient remains stable with no response, then synchronized cardioversion can be considered.
📚 Source: ACLS, PALS
☆ Cardiology: Arrhythmias
source: ACLS, PALS

stem:
A teenage girl is diagnosed with complete androgen insensitivity syndrome (CAIS) after evaluation
for primary amenorrhea. The patient identifies as female and was raised as such. Her mother requests
that you not inform the patient about the diagnosis, fearing psychological harm. What is the most
appropriate next step?

options.label: A
options.text: Encourage parents to come with patient to discuss the diagnosis

options.label: B
options.text: Respect mother wish, not to tell the girls about diagnosis

options.label: C
options.text: In the next visit tell the daughter

options.label: D
options.text: Ask father for help how to tell the daughter

answer: A. Encourage the parents to come with the patient to discuss the diagnosis
explanation:
• This is a classic ethics + adolescent medicine scenario.
• The diagnosis of CAIS (46,XY with female phenotype) has major implications for identity, fertility,
and autonomy.
• Adolescents have the right to know their diagnosis, especially one affecting medical and
psychological care.
• The preferred approach is family-centered, involving supportive disclosure with the family and
patient together.
• Delaying or hiding the diagnosis violates the patient’s right to autonomy and truth.
☆ Ethics: Adolescent Medicine, Patient Autonomy

stem:
A 12-year-old girl presented to ER with her parents complaint of one day history of cola colored urine.
On examination, her systolic blood pressure was 137 over 85 mmHg. She has generalized edema.
Urine analysis showed: +3 protein +4 RBC with casts. CBC and albumin were unremarkable. What is
the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Edema + hematuria + HTN = nephritic pattern.
• Hallmarks: hematuria (cola), RBC casts, HTN, edema.
• Often post-strep GN (PSGN): follows skin/throat infection by 1–3 weeks.
• Labs: low complement (C3) confirms PSGN.
📚 Source: Nelson Nephrology Ch. 474; Osama Naga 2024 p. 530–532.
☆ Nephrology: Nephritic Syndrome
source: Nelson Nephrology Ch. 474; Osama Naga 2024 p. 530–532.

stem:
A child presents with history of:
- Fever, weight loss, leg pain
- Periorbital ecchymosis + proptosis
- Abdominal mass
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Abdominal mass + periorbital ecchymosis = neuroblastoma.
• Most common extracranial solid tumor in children <5 years.
• Patients typically present at age <2 with a firm abdominal mass, most often involving the adrenal
gland, and constitutional symptoms (eg, weight loss, fatigue).
o Mass may cross midline → important differentiating point from nephroblastoma (Wilm’s)
• Hallmark: "raccoon eyes" (periorbital ecchymosis), proptosis, abdominal mass.
• Although rare, opsoclonus-myoclonus is a paraneoplastic syndrome highly
associated with neuroblastoma and is believed to be an autoantibody
response to central nervous system antigens.
• Labs: ↑ urinary catecholamine metabolites (VMA/HVA).
• The most common tumours in children are:
o Leukemia (28%)
o Brain and spinal tumours (26%)
o Abdominal tumours
 Neuroblastoma (30% of abdominal tumours, 8% overall) — MC abdominal tumor
 Nephroblastoma; Wilm’s Tumour (25% of abdominal tumours, 5% overall)
 Lymphoma (15% of abdominal tumours, 8% overall)
 Best initial investigation: ultrasound (US)
 Best diagnostic test: CT or MRI
📚 Source: Nelson Oncology Ch. 479; Osama Naga 2024 p. 591–593.
☆ Oncology: Pediatric Solid Tumours; Neuroblastoma
source: Nelson Oncology Ch. 479; Osama Naga 2024 p. 591–593.

stem:
A child was brought to ER with history of fever for 3 months. On examination the patient is pale, and
there are bruises of different ages, there is no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. CBC: WBC
1.6, Hb 8, Plt 60, Retics 0.1%. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Key Board Clue: Pancytopenia + no organomegaly = aplastic anemia
• Pancytopenia + hypocellular marrow + no HSM/LAD = classic for aplastic anemia.
• Leukemia: usually blasts, HSM, LAD.
• Aplastic anemia is characterized by pancytopenia with inappropriately low reticulocyte count,
morphologically normal cell lines on peripheral smear, and no splenomegaly on physical
examination
•
•
•
•
Although aplastic anemia is usually idiopathic (no clear cause), it can be triggered by viral infection
(eg, hepatitis, HIV), chemical or radiation exposure, or medications (eg, carbamazepine).
Patients typically have manifestations of pancytopenia such as severe fatigue and pale mucous
membranes (anemia), bleeding/bruising (thrombocytopenia), or infection (eg, leukopenia).
No significant extramedullary hematopoiesis occurs (eg, no splenomegaly) because few
hematopoietic stem cells continue to function.
Drug-induced AA is typically categorized as follows:
o Dose-dependent reactions are caused by medications (eg, chemotherapy,
immunosuppressives) that are toxic to the bone marrow when drug levels exceed a certain
concentration for a certain period.
 They tend to be predictable in onset.
o Idiosyncratic reactions are often caused by antiseizure medications (eg, carbamazepine,
valproic acid), sulfonamides, or nifedipine.
 They are unpredictable, unrelated to the dose of the drug, and may occur at any point
during or after therapy.
 Although the mechanism is unclear, patients often have genetic mutations in drugmetabolizing enzymes or efflux pumps, which may promote toxicity.
📚 Source: Nelson Hematology Ch. 439; Osama Naga 2024 p. 480–481; UWorld.
☆ Hematology: Aplastic Anemia
source: Nelson Hematology Ch. 439; Osama Naga 2024 p. 480–481; UWorld.

stem:
A 5-day-old full-term neonate, born at home with an unremarkable birth history, is brought to the
emergency department due to multiple bruises. The baby is exclusively breastfed and appears well
with stable vital signs. On examination, no hepatosplenomegaly or signs of sepsis are noted. Lab
investigations show: normal platelet count, prolonged PT, normal fibrinogen, and normal aPTT.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Board clue: Home birth + breastfeeding + early bruising = Vit K deficiency bleeding (VKDB).
• Classic: early bleeding (<7 days) in exclusive breastfeeding + no Vit K shot at birth.
• Isolated prolonged PT (extrinsic pathway defect).
• Normal platelets + fibrinogen rules out DIC.
• Vitamin K–deficient bleeding usually occurs in the first week of life but can present through age 6
months.
•
•
•
•
•
Vitamin K is an essential cofactor for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase, the enzyme responsible for
carboxylation of clotting factors II (prothrombin), VII, IX, and X.
Healthy children and adults typically have adequate vitamin K stores from dietary sources (eg,
green, leafy vegetables) and from endogenous colonic bacteria that generate the vitamin.
o In contrast, newborns have low vitamin K stores due to poor transplacental transfer
and low content in breast milk.
o Delayed vitamin K production by the gradual colonization of bacterial gut flora as well as
inefficient use by an immature liver also contribute to low levels in early infancy.
Additional risk factors include exclusive breastfeeding, due to the low vitamin K content of breast
milk, and cystic fibrosis (CF), due to malabsorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
Newborns who do not receive routine vitamin K prophylaxis at birth are at greatest risk for vitamin
K–deficient bleeding. Lack of functioning coagulation factors predisposes to life-threatening
bleeding, such as intracranial hemorrhage and profuse bleeding from the gastrointestinal tract,
umbilicus, and surgical sites.
ICH is a potentially fatal complication and presents with signs of increased intracranial pressure (eg,
lethargy, enlarging head circumference, bulging fontanel, downward-driven eyes), seizures, etc.
Clinical pearls:
• Early VKDB (<24 hrs): associated with maternal medications (e.g., anticonvulsants, rifampin).
• Classic VKDB (2–7 days): presents with bruising, bleeding from umbilicus or GI tract.
• Late VKDB (>2 weeks–6 months): may cause life-threatening intracranial hemorrhage.
• Immediate IM/IV vitamin K should be administered if VKDB is suspected.
📚 Source: Nelson Ch. 453; Osama Naga 2024 p. 612–614.
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Routine newborn care, Vit K deficiency bleeding (VKDB)
source: Nelson Ch. 453; Osama Naga 2024 p. 612–614.
topic: Routine newborn care, Vit K deficiency bleeding (VKDB)

stem:
A preterm neonate with a history of necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC) underwent bowel resection and
developed short bowel syndrome. He has been on total parenteral nutrition (TPN) and recently had
his TPN calories increased due to poor weight gain. Within 48 hours, he developed
hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, and mild edema, despite no signs of sepsis or renal dysfunction.
What is the most likely cause of his electrolyte abnormalities?

options.label: B
options.text: Increased renal losses of phosphate and potassium

options.label: C
options.text: Electrolytes were not added to the TPN

options.label: D
options.text: Ongoing GI loss due to short bowel syndrome

answer: A. Active anabolism shifts electrolytes intracellularly
explanation:
• Classic refeeding syndrome: reintroducing nutrition → ↑ insulin → shifts K+, P, Mg2+
intracellularly.
• Pathophysiology: very rapid increase in daily food intake in severely malnourished patients can
cause massive insulin release → increased displacement of magnesium, potassium,
and phosphate (shift from extracellular to intracellular) → ↓↓↓ phosphate (most critical marker,
MC), ↓ potassium, ↓ magnesium
o Electrolytes: hypokalemia, hypophosphatemia, and hypomagnesemia + hypoglycemia
• Risk in:
o Severely malnourished (e.g., anorexia
nervosa, NICU, short bowel syndrome)
o Prolonged fasting / minimal intake (>7 days)
o Chronic TPN dependence
• Clinical features:
o Weakness, lethargy
o Arrhythmias
o Seizures
o Respiratory failure (↓ ATP → diaphragm
weakness)
o Fluid overload, edema
• Prevention & Management:
o Start feeds slowly (25–50% of needs)
o Aggressive monitoring & repletion:
o Phosphate, potassium, magnesium
o Thiamine supplementation before/with feeding
o Monitor: electrolytes daily for 1st week
o Note: these patients also likely have some degree of thiamine deficiency, and can benefit
from initial supplementation (before glucose administration).
📚 Source: Nelson Nutrition Ch. 47; Osama Naga 2024 p. 127.
☆ Endocrinology: Refeeding syndrome
source: Nelson Nutrition Ch. 47; Osama Naga 2024 p. 127.

stem:
A 3-week-old, exclusively breastfed baby is not gaining weight, has yellow diarrhea (non-bloody).
What is the next best step?
explanation:
• At 3 weeks, in an exclusively breastfed infant, yellow non-bloody stools are often normal.
• Breastfed babies typically have loose, yellow, seedy stools, which can be misinterpreted as
diarrhea.
• The main concern here is not gaining weight, but before jumping to formula changes, observe stool
pattern and assess feeding adequacy.
• Weight monitoring essential, but most cases self-resolve.
•
•
Choice A: Lactose free formula may be considered only in confirmed lactose intolerance (which
presents with explosive watery stools, gas, irritability), which is NOT suggested here.
Choice B: Switching to formula is not first-line based only on stool appearance unless there’s
confirmed cow’s milk protein allergy (would expect bloody stools, eczema, fussiness).
📚 Source: Nelson Neonatology Ch. 99; Osama Naga 2024 p. 240.
☆ Gastroenterology: Diarrhea
source: Nelson Neonatology Ch. 99; Osama Naga 2024 p. 240.

stem:
A 14-year-old collapses at school with tunnel vision, diaphoresis, and 2 min loss of consciousness.
There is immediate recovery, a normal exam, and no history of epilepsy. What is the most likely
diagnosis?
explanation:
• Classic prodrome (sweating, pallor, tunnel vision) + transient LOC + quick recovery.
• No post-ictal phase, no confusion = NOT seizure.
• Vasovagal syncope typically involves a trigger (eg, prolonged standing), a prodrome (eg, warmth,
light-headedness), and rapid recovery (eg, within 1-2 min). It results from a transient, autonomic
nervous system–mediated cardioinhibitory and vasodepressor response.
📚 Source: Nelson Cardiology Ch. 449; Osama Naga 2024 p. 372.
☆ Cardiology: Vasovagal syncope
source: Nelson Cardiology Ch. 449; Osama Naga 2024 p. 372.

stem: A 9-year-old girl has positive PPD and normal chest X-ray. What is the best management?
explanation:
• Classic latent TB infection (LTBI): +PPD, no active disease (normal CXR).
• Isoniazid (INH) 9 months = gold standard for LTBI.
• INH side effects:
o Hepatotoxicity (monitor LFTs)
o Peripheral neuropathy (prevented with pyridoxine/vitamin B6)
★ Pediatric Tuberculosis:
• Transmission & Risk Factors
o Spread via airborne droplets, usually from a household contact
o Infants and children <5 yrs are at highest risk for progression to active disease
• TB Testing: PPD vs IGRA
o PPD (Mantoux)
 Positive if ≥10 mm in high-risk children (e.g., close contacts, age <4, foreign-born)
 Positive if ≥5 mm in immunocompromised, close contacts, or abnormal CXR
IGRA (Quantiferon)
 Preferred in BCG-vaccinated children ≥2 years
 Not reliable in <2 years
 Does not distinguish active vs latent TB
Clinical Features of Active TB
o Pulmonary TB
 Chronic cough, weight loss, fever, fatigue
 Hilar lymphadenopathy, pleural effusion on imaging
o Extrapulmonary TB
 CNS (meningitis), miliary TB, lymph nodes, bones (Pott
disease)
 Infants and toddlers are at high risk for disseminated TB
Diagnosis of Active TB
o Start with chest X-ray
o Sputum AFB smear/culture if age ≥10 or able to expectorate
o Gastric aspirates x3 if <10 years old
o Consider CSF or lymph node biopsy if extrapulmonary TB
o Always screen for HIV
Latent TB Infection (LTBI)
o Defined as positive PPD or IGRA, no symptoms, normal CXR
o Treatment options
 INH x 9 months
 Rifampin x 4 months
 INH + Rifapentine weekly x 3 months (if age >2 yrs, HIVo
•
•
•
•
•
negative)
Treatment of Active TB
o RIPE for 2 months: Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol
o Then INH + Rifampin for 4 months
o Total treatment: 6 months, extend to 9–12 months for CNS or bone TB
o Use Directly Observed Therapy (DOT) to ensure adherence
Mothers with treated TB can breastfeed using expressed milk only
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024; Nelson Infectious Diseases Ch. 315. AMBOSS; UWorld.
☆ Infectious Diseases: Tuberculosis
source: Red Book 2021–2024; Nelson Infectious Diseases Ch. 315. AMBOSS; UWorld.

stem: What type of infection is a patient with C8 deficiency most prone to?
explanation:
• C5–C9 deficiencies → risk of Neisseria infections → due to inability to form the membrane attack
complex (MAC)
• Classical finding: recurrent meningococcal meningitis despite treatment.
Why Neisseria specifically?
• Gram-negative bacteria have an outer membrane (rich in LPS)
that requires MAC insertion for direct killing. Neisseria depends
heavily on MAC for clearance because its natural defenses + location
(mucosal invasion) make it vulnerable.
• Many Gram-negative organisms (e.g., E. coli, Klebsiella) are primarily
cleared by opsonization + phagocytosis, even if MAC is impaired.
• Gram-positive bacteria lack an outer membrane → they are resistant
to MAC-mediated lysis (they're killed via opsonization instead).
Differentiating between types of complement deficiencies:
• Recurrent Neisseria + normal other immunity → C5–C9 deficiency.
• Recurrent bacterial + early-onset SLE → classical pathway (esp. C2).
• Swelling (angioedema) + no urticaria → C1-INH deficiency (not an infection issue!); Autosomal
dominant inheritance → Positive FHx
• Central C3 defect → most severe: recurrent infections + immune complex disease.
• Low CH50 + low AH50 → C3 or terminal (C5–C9) defect
• Low CH50 + normal AH50 → Classical pathway defect
• Note: Most complement deficiencies → Autosomal recessive.
📚 Source: Nelson Immunology Ch. 317; Osama Naga 2024 p. 308–310.
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Primary Immunodeficiency, Terminal Complement Deficiency
source: Nelson Immunology Ch. 317; Osama Naga 2024 p. 308–310.

stem:
A 6-year-old boy is brought to the clinic with a history of chronic cough and poor weight gain. He has
had multiple episodes of lower respiratory tract infections requiring antibiotics. His parents report
ongoing nasal congestion, recurrent sinus infections, and foul-smelling, bulky stools. On
examination, he has digital clubbing and nasal polyps. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• CF = exocrine dysfunction: thick mucus → recurrent sinopulmonary infections + nasal polyps +
bronchiectasis.
• Look for failure to thrive + steatorrhea.
★ Cystic Fibrosis:
• Autosomal recessive, mutation in CFTR gene (Chr 7), most common = ΔF508.
• CFTR defect → impaired chloride transport → thick, dehydrated secretions.
• Newborns: suspect with meconium ileus (pathognomonic – bowel obstruction + ground-glass on
AXR).
• Pulmonary symptoms: chronic cough, recurrent pneumonia, bronchiectasis, digital clubbing.
• Early colonization: Staph aureus (infancy/early childhood).
• Later colonization: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (midchildhood/adolescence), Burkholderia cepacia (severe
prognosis).
• GI: pancreatic insufficiency → steatorrhea, failure to thrive, ADEK
vitamin deficiency.
• Intestinal: DIOS (Distal Intestinal Obstruction Syndrome) =
recurrent obstruction in older children.
• Liver: focal biliary cirrhosis → portal hypertension.
• Reproductive: azoospermia/infertility in males (absent vas deferens), thick cervical mucus in
females.
• Electrolytes: hypochloremic, hyponatremic metabolic alkalosis (esp. in hot weather).
• Diagnosis:
o Sweat chloride ≥60 mmol/L (gold standard);
o 2 pathogenic CFTR mutations;
o Nasal potential difference (if equivocal).
• Treatment:
Airway clearance: chest physiotherapy, hypertonic saline, dornase alfa (DNAse).
Antibiotics:
 Early: anti-staphylococcal (e.g., cephalexin, TMP-SMX).
 Chronic Pseudomonas: inhaled tobramycin, aztreonam.
o CFTR modulators:
 Ivacaftor (G551D),
 Lumacaftor/Ivacaftor (ΔF508 homozygous),
 Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (Trikafta – ΔF508 hetero/homo).
o PERT + ADEK vitamins.
Complications: hemoptysis, pneumothorax, CF-related diabetes (CFRD), osteopenia.
Surveillance: annual OGTT (CFRD), DEXA (bone health), sputum cultures (track colonization), liver
US.
o
o
•
•
📚 Source: Nelson Pulmonology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 421–423; AMBOSS; UWorld.
☆ Pulmonology: Cystic Fibrosis
source: Nelson Pulmonology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 421–423; AMBOSS; UWorld.
topic: • Autosomal recessive, mutation in CFTR gene (Chr 7), most common = ΔF508.

stem:
A 6-year-old boy is brought to the clinic due to a history of chronic productive cough and persistent
nasal discharge. He has experienced multiple episodes of otitis media and chronic sinusitis since
infancy. His parents report that he tires easily and has a daily wet cough. On examination, he
has digital clubbing, and chest auscultation reveals bilateral crackles. A recent chest X-ray
showed bronchiectasis and dextrocardia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Kartagener syndrome is a subset of primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD) characterized by:
o Bronchiectasis
Chronic sinusitis
Recurrent otitis media
Situs inversus (seen in ~50% of cases)
Due to defective dynein arms in cilia, leading to impaired mucociliary clearance
Cystic fibrosis can also cause bronchiectasis and chronic infections, but is typically associated
with pancreatic insufficiency and positive sweat chloride test, and situs inversus is not a feature
IgA deficiency predisposes to sinopulmonary infections but not situs inversus or bronchiectasis
o
o
o
•
•
•
📚 Source: Nelson Immunology & Respiratory; Osama Naga 2024 p. 426–428.
☆ Pulmonology: Kartagener syndrome
source: Nelson Immunology & Respiratory; Osama Naga 2024 p. 426–428.

stem:
An asthmatic patient undergoes a PFT >80%; and symptoms of night cough 1–2x/month; and is
symptomatic with exercise but can play. What is the patient’s asthma classification?
explanation:
• Exercise-induced asthma is a trigger pattern, not a separate severity class.
• Asthma is classified by the worst category across all domains.
• PFT improvement ≥12% after bronchodilator = diagnostic of asthma
★ Board Clues:
• Nighttime symptoms are often the best clue to
differentiate mild vs. moderate vs. severe persistent asthma.
• Watch for clues like:
o “Symptoms with exercise, can play” = Mild persistent.
o “Daily inhaler + frequent night cough” = Moderate persistent.
📚 Source: Nelson Pulmonology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 421–425; GINA Guidelines.
☆ Pulmonology: Asthma
source: Nelson Pulmonology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 421–425; GINA Guidelines.
topic: • Nighttime symptoms are often the best clue to

stem:
A patient presents with history of chronic cough and exercise intolerance. Which of the following has
the most diagnostic value?
explanation:
• Spirometry = gold standard for diagnosing asthma in patients ≥ 6 years of age
o Findings of an obstructive lung pattern (↓ FEV1, ↓ FEV1/FVC ratio) that reverses with
bronchodilation would confirm a diagnosis of asthma.
o In children < 6 years of age diagnosis is largely dependent on a detailed history and the
response to a trial of asthma medication.
• If the diagnosis is still nonconclusive, a methacholine challenge test may be attempted or
watchful waiting initiated.
• Support x-ray is not always indicated and, if done, can be normal in mild cases of asthma or may
show evidence of hyperinflation (e.g., flattened diaphragm, wide intercostal spaces).
• Bronchoscopy is for anatomy concerns.
• Sweat chloride: CF-specific.
📚 Source: Nelson Pulmonology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 421–422; AMBOSS; UWorld.
☆ Pulmonology: Asthma
source: Nelson Pulmonology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 421–422; AMBOSS; UWorld.

stem:
A 10-year-old child presents with a 3-week history of fever, pharyngitis, generalized rash, and
tonsillitis. Examination reveals cervical lymphadenopathy and hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory
findings show atypical lymphocytosis on peripheral smear. What is the most appropriate
management?
explanation:
This is classic Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infectious mononucleosis:
• Features: fever, sore throat, lymphadenopathy, rash (especially after antibiotics).
•
•
•
•
•
Treatment: supportive only.
Most cases are self-limited and require supportive care only
Amoxicillin or ampicillin often causes a maculopapular rash in EBV patients, so it should be
avoided
Acyclovir is not routinely recommended; no clear benefit in uncomplicated EBV
Steroids are reserved for airway obstruction, thrombocytopenia, or severe hemolytic anemia
📚 Source: Nelson Infectious Diseases; Osama Naga 2024 p. 478–480.
☆ Infectious Diseases: Ebstein-Barr virus (EBV), Infectious Mononucleosis
source: Nelson Infectious Diseases; Osama Naga 2024 p. 478–480.

stem:
A 10-year-old child presents to the clinic with high-grade fever, sore throat, and
fatigue for the past 4 days. On examination, the child has bilateral exudative
tonsillitis, tender cervical lymphadenopathy, and mild hepatomegaly. A
maculopapular rash appeared after receiving amoxicillin. What is the most
likely laboratory finding?
explanation:
In EBV infectious mononucleosis:
• Blood smear → atypical lymphocytosis (>10% atypical lymphocytes).
• Other findings: mild ↑ transaminases, ± thrombocytopenia.
•
After entering the bloodstream through the pharyngeal mucosa and tonsillar crypts, the EpsteinBarr virus (EBV) preferentially infects B lymphocytes by binding to CD21 cell surface receptors.
EBV-infected B lymphocytes then activate cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CD8+) through the
presentation of viral antigens on major histocompatibility complex class I molecules.
•
•
These reactive (atypical) CD8+ T lymphocytes are the primary immune response to EBV and
clonally expand to destroy virus-infected cells.
Atypical lymphocytes make up more than 10% of the cells in a peripheral blood smear in IM, and
most (>95%) of those cells are CD8+ T lymphocytes.
o They classically appear much larger than quiescent lymphocytes, with abundant basophilic
cytoplasm and a cell membrane that conforms to the borders of neighboring cells.
📚 Source: Nelson Infectious Diseases; Osama Naga 2024 p. 478–480.
☆ Infectious Diseases: Ebstein-Barr virus (EBV), Infectious Mononucleosis
source: Nelson Infectious Diseases; Osama Naga 2024 p. 478–480.

stem:
A 9-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department with a 2-week history of persistent
headaches, early morning vomiting, excessive urination, and recent difficulty seeing the board at
school. On examination, she has bilateral visual field deficits. Brain imaging (CT) reveals a
suprasellar mass consistent with a craniopharyngioma. Which of the following hormones is most
likely deficient in this patient?
explanation:
• Craniopharyngiomas are benign tumors arising from remnants of Rathke’s pouch and commonly
occur in the suprasellar region, compressing the pituitary stalk + hypothalamus → central
diabetes insipidus (↓ ADH)
• Polyuria + polydipsia = hallmark.
• Prolactin may rise (stalk effect) but ADH deficiency is primary.
• Other anterior pituitary hormones (GH, TSH) may also be affected, but DI from ADH loss is often
the earliest and most symptomatic
• Visual disturbances occur due to compression of the optic chiasm, often presenting
as bitemporal hemianopia
📚 Source: Nelson Endocrinology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 342–344; UWorld.
☆ Endocrinology: Diabetes Insipidus
source: Nelson Endocrinology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 342–344; UWorld.

stem:
A 14-year-old girl presents with a 1-week history of headache, transient visual obscuration, and
nausea. She is currently taking oral isotretinoin for severe acne. On examination, her visual acuity is
normal, but bilateral papilledema is noted on fundoscopy. A non-contrast head CT is normal. What
is the most appropriate next investigation to determine the underlying cause?
explanation:
• Pseudotumor cerebri (idiopathic intracranial HTN):
o Papilledema + normal imaging.
o Diagnostic LP → elevated opening pressure (>250mmH2O) + normal CSF composition
• Isotretinoin is a well-known trigger.
• MRI/MRV is useful prior to LP to rule out venous sinus thrombosis or subtle structural lesions if
not yet done, but LP is diagnostic
Clinical pearls:
• Common IIH symptoms: headache, papilledema, vision changes, tinnitus
• Risk factors: obesity, female sex, tetracyclines, isotretinoin, vitamin A toxicity
• Management includes acetazolamide ± serial LPs, and weight loss if applicable
Mnemonic: “A female TOAD”: female sex, Tetracyclines, Obesity, excessive intake of vitamin A, and
Danazol are the major risk factors for pseudotumor cerebri.
📚 Source: Nelson Neurology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 415–417.
☆ Neurology: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH)
source: Nelson Neurology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 415–417.

stem:
A 10-year-old boy presents with multiple episodes of gross hematuria that consistently occur a few
days after upper respiratory tract infections. Each episode resolves spontaneously within a few
days, and he remains well in between. He has no dysuria, fever, or edema. Family history is
unremarkable, and physical exam is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
New onset gross hematuria:
• IgA nephropathy = Gross hematuria + 3-5 days after URTI
• PSGN = Gross hematuria + 1-2 weeks after URTI or 2-4 weeks after skin infection
• Alport syndrome = Gross or microscopic hematuria + Hearing loss
o mnemonic: “can't pee, can't see, can't hear a bee”
o glomerulonephritis → isolated hematuria, progressive renal failure
o nerve deafness → sensorineural hearing loss
o ocular disturbances → lens dislocation, cataract
Clinical pearls:
• IgA nephropathy is the most common glomerulonephritis in children and young adults worldwide
• Diagnosis is confirmed by renal biopsy showing mesangial IgA deposits
• Management includes monitoring, ACE inhibitors for proteinuria, and steroids in selected cases
📚 Source: Nelson Nephrology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 510–512; UWorld.
☆ Nephrology: IgA Nephropathy
source: Nelson Nephrology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 510–512; UWorld.

stem:
A 6-year-old boy is seen for a routine checkup. You detect a mid-systolic click with a late systolic
murmur. What is the best test to confirm the diagnosis?
explanation:
• This is classic for Mitral Valve Prolapse (MVP).
• Diagnosis is best confirmed with echocardiography showing valve leaflet prolapse.
• CXR and ECG are typically normal.
• Standing/Valsalva increases murmur/click.
• Squatting decreases murmur.
• Associated with Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Osteogenesis Imperfecta (OI).
o Tall, thin adolescent girl + scoliosis + murmur → Marfan
o Hypermobile joints or fragile skin → Ehlers-Danlos
o Bone fractures + blue sclerae → Osteogenesis Imperfecta
📚 Source: Nelson Cardiology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 225–227.
☆ Cardiology: Murmurs, Mitral Valve Prolapse
source: Nelson Cardiology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 225–227.

stem:
A boy at a preschool checkup has a short, musical, medium-pitched grade 2 systolic murmur. What
could be an additional feature?
answer: D. Changes with respiration and position
explanation:
• Innocent murmurs:
o Short, low-grade, musical, change with position/respiration.
• Pathologic murmurs radiate or are fixed.
Clinical pearls:
• Always evaluate with ECG + echocardiogram if red flags present
★ Red flags in pediatric murmurs
o Thrill (Grade IV or higher murmur)
o Diastolic murmur
o Cyanosis or failure to thrive
o Murmur radiating to the back, carotids, or lower extremities
o Loud systolic murmur ≥ Grade III
📚 Source: Nelson Cardiology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 226–228.
☆ Cardiology: Murmurs, Innocent murmur
source: Nelson Cardiology; Osama Naga 2024 p. 226–228.

stem:
A 6-year-old child with known thalassemia major is on a regular blood transfusion schedule and
currently receiving iron chelation therapy with deferoxamine. He presents to the emergency
department with a 3-day history of watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, and low-grade fever. On
examination, he is alert and mildly dehydrated. Stool is non-bloody. No recent antibiotic use.
What is the most likely pathogen?
explanation:
• Yersinia loves iron-rich environments → risk in thalassemics on deferoxamine.
• Classic: GI symptoms, pseudo-appendicitis, iron overload link.
• It causes gastroenteritis, often with abdominal pain mimicking appendicitis, diarrhea (which may
be watery or bloody), and sometimes mesenteric adenitis.
• Deferoxamine acts as a siderophore (iron carrier) → directly delivers iron to pathogens like
Yersinia, Mucor, and Vibrio.
• Yersinia enterocolitica is most classically tested: Thalassemia + iron chelation → abdominal pain,
diarrhea, sepsis
• In thalassemia or sickle cell, always ask about recent transfusions, iron chelation, and Hx of raw
food exposure (especially seafood).
📚 Source: Nelson Infectious Diseases; Osama Naga 2024 p. 495–497.
☆ Infectious Diseases: Siderophilic Organisms
source: Nelson Infectious Diseases; Osama Naga 2024 p. 495–497.

stem:
An unvaccinated child is brought to the Emergency Department with a sudden onset of high-grade
fever (39.5°C), severe sore throat, difficulty swallowing, and drooling. His mother reports that he is
refusing to speak or lie down and prefers to sit leaning forward with his
mouth open and neck extended. On examination, he appears toxic,
anxious, and has inspiratory stridor. He is tachypneic, and his voice is
muffled (“hot potato voice”). Attempts to visualize the oropharynx were
avoided due to concern for airway compromise. Lateral neck X-ray is
provided. What is the first and most important step?
explanation:
• Classic epiglottitis (Hib):
o Never attempt airway alone.
o Always secure airway with expert help first.
• Steroids/antibiotics come after securing airway.
• Narrowing of the larynx causes signs of respiratory distress (eg, retractions) with inspiratory stridor,
and patients classically assume tripod positioning (ie, leaning forward with the neck extended) to
maximize airflow.
•
•
•
Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) was historically the most common pathogen that caused
epiglottitis, but routine immunization in infancy has greatly decreased its incidence. Individuals
with incomplete vaccination against Hib remain at greatest risk of developing
epiglottitis. Pathogens implicated in immunized children more commonly include nontypeable H
influenzae and Streptococcus pneumoniae, in addition to rare cases of Hib.
All close contacts (including daycare center staff and children) who are exposed to a child with
epiglottitis should receive a 4-day prophylactic course of treatment with rifampin at 20 mg/kg (not
to exceed 600 mg/d).
Children older than 2 years with epiglottitis do not need vaccination, because the disease provides
immune protection.
📚 Source: Nelson Infectious Diseases & Emergency; Osama Naga 2024 p. 476–478.
☆ Emergency Medicine: Respiratory Emergencies, Airway Obstruction/Stridor, Epiglottitis
source: Nelson Infectious Diseases & Emergency; Osama Naga 2024 p. 476–478.

stem:
A patient presents to the emergency department after ingesting detergent at home. The patient
deteriorated and was intubated. What is the most appropriate investigation to assess
for esophageal perforation (not next)?
explanation:
• In stable patients, the gold standard for evaluating caustic esophageal injury — including
identifying perforation — is upper GI endoscopy within 12–24 hours.
• While chest X-ray can be used for screening, it is not sensitive enough to rule out small
perforations or grade mucosal injury.
• Flexible endoscopy allows:
o Direct visualization of mucosal injury
o Identification of full-thickness necrosis or perforation
o Grading injury severity for prognosis and management
o Important caveat: It should be done only in controlled settings, ideally with surgical backup
if perforation is suspected.
• NG tube insertion is contraindicated in this setting due to risk of worsening mucosal damage.
Pneumomediastinum:
★ Board pearl: In a deteriorating patient with suspected esophageal injury from caustic ingestion,
flexible endoscopy is the most appropriate definitive test — provided perforation is not already
obvious clinically or radiographically.
📚 Source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, GI Emergencies; Osama Naga 2024 – GI & Toxicology, p. 198–
200; Red Book 2021–2024 – Caustic Ingestion Protocols; AMBOSS
☆ Gastroenterology: Esophageal perforation
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, GI Emergencies; Osama Naga 2024 – GI & Toxicology, p. 198–
topic: In a deteriorating patient with suspected esophageal injury from caustic ingestion,

stem:
A 7-month-old infant presents with vomiting, inspiratory and expiratory stridor that increases with
cold or when upset. Examination reveals suprasternal and mild subcostal retractions. What is the
most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Stridor is indicative of upper airway obstruction ≠ lower airway
• Stridor: high-pitched, over trachea which may occur on:
o Inspiratory stridor: supraglottic/glottic obstruction (e.g., laryngomalacia, epiglottitis).
o Expiratory stridor: intrathoracic obstruction (e.g., tracheomalacia).
o Biphasic stridor: fixed central airway lesion (e.g., subglottic stenosis, vascular ring, vocal
cord paralysis).
• Videos: https://youtu.be/JSdEK79J4dw?si=mG9kFtIgs1yrz8jX,
https://youtu.be/Qbn1Zw5CTbA?si=jAR7oxT325vgqv47
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024 p. 442–444; Nelson Pediatrics Ch. 399
☆ Cardiology: Vascular Ring, Stridor
source: Osama Naga 2024 p. 442–444; Nelson Pediatrics Ch. 399

stem:
A 4-year-old child with a history of biliary atresia who underwent a Kasai portoenterostomy in infancy
presents with progressive clumsiness, unsteady gait, and difficulty with coordination. On
examination, she has areflexia, ataxia, and reduced proprioception. There are no signs of acute
illness. What is the most likely underlying cause of her symptoms?
explanation:
• Biliary atresia is a progressive fibro-obliterative disease of the extrahepatic bile ducts.
• Treated initially with Kasai portoenterostomy.
• Many patients develop fat malabsorption due to impaired bile secretion → deficiency in fatsoluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
• Neurologic dysfunction in vitamin E deficiency occurs due to free radical damage to neuronal cell
membranes.
o Ataxia due to degeneration of spinocerebellar tracts
o Loss of position and vibration sense due to degeneration of the dorsal columns
o Weakness and loss of deep tendon reflexes due to peripheral nerve degeneration
o Ophthalmoplegia
• Muscle-related symptoms: Muscle weakness, Hypotonia
• Hematologic findings (especially in premature infants): Hemolytic anemia, Thrombocytosis
o due to free radical damage of erythrocyte membranes
• Ophthalmologic: Pigmentary retinopathy
• In neonates and preterm infants: Increased risk of retinopathy of prematurity, Intraventricular
hemorrhage
• Treat with high-dose oral or parenteral vitamin E supplementation.
📚 Source: Osama Naga 2024 p. 129–130; Nelson Nutrition Ch. 55
☆ Gastroenterology: Biliary Atresia, Vitamin Deficiencies
source: Osama Naga 2024 p. 129–130; Nelson Nutrition Ch. 55

stem:
The mother of a 4-year-old boy is concerned that her child has clumsy gait. She told you that he started
to roll over at 6 months of age. He was able to sit at 8 months, started to stand without support at 18
months of age. Fine motor development was appropriate for age. Examination shows calf hypertrophy
and proximal muscle weakness. The child has a maternal uncle who had inability to walk as well
and died at the age of 15. Which of the following is the next best investigation in this patient?
explanation:
The clinical picture is classic for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD):
• Etiology: X-linked recessive mutation in dystrophin gene (FHx)
• Disease onset is age 2-5. Muscles of the proximal lower extremities, back, and pelvic and shoulder
girdles are affected first.
• Symptoms: Proximal muscle weakness, delayed milestones, Gower sign, calf pseudohypertrophy
• CK is very high in DMD (often >10,000 IU/L).
o It is the most sensitive and least invasive initial test.
o Elevated CK precedes clinical symptoms and confirms muscle breakdown.
o Guides need for definitive DMD gene testing (e.g., MLPA or PCR).
• Death usually from cardiomyopathy or respiratory failure (2nd decade)
• Treatment: Steroids (prednisone, deflazacort), cardiac monitoring, physical therapy
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 543; Osama Naga p. 465
☆ Neurology: Muscular Dystrophies
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 543; Osama Naga p. 465

stem:
A 6-month-old infant presents with muscle weakness, tongue fasciculations, and respiratory
failure. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Another potential scenario: 3mo infant lays in the "frog-leg" position, <5th% 2/2 feeding difficulties,
hypotonic, fasciculations of the tongue and absent DTRs.
Explanation:
• Spinal muscular atrophy (Type I – Werdnig-Hoffmann disease) is the most severe and most
common autosomal recessive motor neuron disease in infancy.
• Caused by SMN1 gene mutation → degeneration of anterior horn cells (peripheral motor neurons).
• Presents before 6 months with:
o Severe hypotonia
o Symmetric proximal muscle weakness
o Tongue fasciculations
o Absent deep tendon reflexes
o Paradoxical breathing (due to diaphragm sparing)
o Progressive respiratory failure
• Diagnosis:
o Genetic testing: homozygous deletion/mutation in SMN1 gene
o EMG: shows denervation
o Muscle biopsy: may show grouped atrophy (rarely needed now)
• Treatment:
o Nusinersen (Spinraza) – antisense oligonucleotide that increases SMN2 production
o Gene therapy: Onasemnogene abeparvovec (Zolgensma)
o Supportive care: respiratory support, feeding, physical therapy
There are 5 types of SMA: Type I → non-sitters, type II → sitters, type III → walkers
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 544–545; Osama Naga, 3rd Ed., p. 466; AMBOSS
☆ Neurology: Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 544–545; Osama Naga, 3rd Ed., p. 466; AMBOSS

stem:
A young girl presents after a road traffic accident (RTA) with loss of consciousness for few seconds,
vomiting, and a positive CT scan for intracranial hemorrhage. Physical examination was
unremarkable. Her family is concerned and asks: Which of the following features increases her longterm risk of post-traumatic epilepsy?
explanation:
• Post-traumatic epilepsy (PTE) can occur weeks to months after traumatic brain injury (TBI).
o Early (within 7 days) → often provoked, lower long-term epilepsy risk
o Late (after 7 days) → higher risk of becoming epilepsy
• Risk factors are related to the severity and type of injury—not just the presence of transient
symptoms.
• Intracranial hemorrhage (e.g., subdural, epidural, intraparenchymal) disrupts brain tissue,
increasing excitability and scarring.
o It is a major risk factor for both early (<7 days) and late (>7 days) post-traumatic seizures.
o Hemorrhage, especially if involving the cortex, creates an epileptogenic focus.
• Other major risk factors for post-traumatic epilepsy:
• Penetrating brain injury
• Depressed skull fracture involving brain tissue
• Prolonged loss of consciousness or coma
• Severe TBI with structural damage
• Prevention: Antiepileptics (e.g., phenytoin or levetiracetam) used short term in severe TBI with highrisk features
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 554–556; Osama Naga, 3rd Ed., p. 494–495
☆ Emergency Medicine: Head Trauma, RTA, Post-traumatic epilepsy
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 554–556; Osama Naga, 3rd Ed., p. 494–495

stem:
The parents of a 5-year-old child bring him to clinic due to episodes where he stops talking midsentence, stares blankly for a few seconds, then resumes talking. These episodes occur multiple
times per day. Neurologic examination is normal. EEG shows 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges. What
is the most appropriate treatment?
explanation:
• Patient with frequent staring spells (ie, absence seizures) and an unprovoked tonic-clonic seizure
most likely has juvenile absence epilepsy.
• Duration is <15 seconds
• No post ictal phase
• Can have simple automatisms → lip smacking, eye fluttering, head nodding
• Can be induced by hyperventilation
• Absence seizures are characterized by momentary lapses in consciousness (~10 seconds)
associated with a classic 3-Hz spike-wave pattern on electroencephalogram.
•
Tx:
o
o
o
1st line = ethosuximide (Zarontin)
 Mechanism: blocks T-type calcium channels in thalamic neurons.
 Highly effective with fewer side effects compared to alternatives.
2nd line or if associated with generalized seizures = sodium valproate (Depakene)
3rd line = lamotrigine
📚 Source: UWorld.
