☆ Neurology: Absence seizures
source: UWorld.

stem:
A 9-month-old boy in the PICU, already intubated and mechanically ventilated,
suddenly desaturates to 60%, becomes tachycardic and hypotensive.
Chest X-ray is provided. What is the most appropriate next urgent step?
explanation:
Classic scenario for tension pneumothorax:
• Sudden hypoxia on a ventilator
• Hypotension and tachycardia (due to impaired venous return)
• Mediastinal shift on CXR
• Absent lung markings on affected side
• First and immediate step: Needle decompression
o Location: 2nd intercostal space,
midclavicular line
o Purpose: Rapidly relieve
intrathoracic pressure and restore
venous return
• Follow with: Chest tube thoracostomy
for definitive management
Simple pneumothorax is most common, but tension pneumothorax can develop if the rupture creates a
one-way tissue valve that opens during inspiration to allow air into the pleural space and then closes during
expiration to trap that air.
→ As a result, intrapleural pressure increases with each breath and leads to contralateral mediastinal shift
with vena cava compression and decreased venous return to the heart.
→ The reduced cardiac output causes hypotension and tachycardia (ie, obstructive shock) and can rapidly
lead to cardiac arrest; therefore, emergent decompression of the pleural space is needed.
Tension pneumothorax in a ventilated child is a
must-not-miss emergency.
Treat before confirmation with imaging if the clinical
picture is clear!
📚 Source: UWorld.
☆ PICU: Complications of Mechanical Ventilation,
Tension pneumothorax
source: UWorld.

stem:
A 2-month-old full-term infant, exclusively breastfed until recently, is now on regular milk formula.
The patient presented with an urticarial rash, irritability, vomiting and diarrhea which is sometimes
bloody shortly after each time he is fed. He is also noted to have failure to gain weight. What is the most
likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Common in infants <6 months, especially when switching from breastmilk to cow’s milk formula
• Epidemiology: primarily affects young infants (typically manifests at 2–8 weeks of age)
• Etiology:
o Hypersensitivity reaction to certain foods, most commonly cow's milk, followed by soy
protein, rice, and eggs
 Cow milk should not be introduced in the first year of life! 🚫🐄
o Associated with a personal and family history of atopy
• Presents with vomiting, bloody diarrhea, urticarial rash, irritability, and poor weight gain
• Symptoms occur shortly after feeding, suggesting a triggered immune response
• Can be IgE-mediated (immediate symptoms: rash, vomiting) or non-IgE mediated (delayed GI
symptoms, blood in stool)
o IgE mediated: usually associated with anaphylaxis, eczema, asthma
o Non-IgE mediated: associated with food intolerance, constipation, GERD
• Diagnostics: mainly a clinical diagnosis based on patient history and clinical features
• Breastfed infants may also react if the mother consumes dairy
• Treatment
o Removal of offending foods from the infant's diet
o Breastfed infants
 Elimination of all dairy and soy products from the mother's diet
 Continue breastfeeding
o Formula-fed infants: switching to a hydrolyzed formula (e.g., hydrolyzed casein)
o The offending foods should be carefully reintroduced to assess tolerance after one year of
age.
• Complications: chronic colitis and/or persistent food allergy (rare)
• Prognosis
o Gross rectal bleeding usually improves within 3–4 days of removing the offending food.
o FPIAP usually resolves spontaneously by one year of age.
Clinical Pearls:
• Non-IgE CMPA (e.g., FPIAP) often presents with bloody stools without systemic anaphylaxis
• Lactose intolerance is rare in infants and does not cause bloody stools or rash
• Acute gastroenteritis usually presents acutely with fever, non-bloody diarrhea, and is not linked
to feeding
• Celiac disease does not present before gluten exposure, typically after 6 months
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 91–92; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd Ed., p. 608
☆ Allergy & Immunology: Cow’s Milk Protein Allergy (CMPA)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 91–92; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A cyanotic 2-day-old neonate is suspected of having congenital heart disease. He has an increased
left ventricular impulse and a 3/6 holosystolic murmur along the left sternal border. The ECG
shows left-axis deviation and left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH). He is noted to have decrease
pulmonary vascular marking on chest X-Ray. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Cyanosis in the first days of life with structural heart disease features points to a duct-dependent
congenital heart defect.
• Left-axis deviation and LVH on ECG are hallmark findings
for tricuspid atresia.
• Tricuspid atresia leads to absent right ventricular
development, causing a dominant left ventricle → LVH
• Holosystolic murmur is due to an associated VSD (almost
always present in tricuspid atresia).
• ↓ pulmonary vascular markings = ↓ pulmonary blood flow
• Increased left ventricular impulse indicates that the LV is
doing the work of both ventricles.
• VSD alone: Causes increased pulmonary markings,
usually not cyanotic, and ECG shows biventricular
hypertrophy, ≠ isolated LVH.
Normal anatomy
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 397–398; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd Ed., p. 374–375
☆ Cardiology: Cyanotic Congenital Heart Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 397–398; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 12-month-old boy is brought to the clinic due to persistent, copious nasal discharge and poor
developmental progress. He was reportedly normal at birth. On examination, he has coarse facial
features, a flat nasal bridge, a bulging anterior fontanelle, a large protruding tongue, umbilical
hernia, and hepatosplenomegaly. What is the most likely diagnosis?
answer: A. Hurler syndrome (For more details, refer to Q3)
explanation:
• Hurler syndrome (Mucopolysaccharidosis type I) typically presents in infancy (6–12 months)
after a normal perinatal period.
• Classic findings include:
o Persistent nasal discharge (from GAG accumulation in nasal mucosa)
o Coarse facial features, flat nasal bridge
o Macroglossia, umbilical hernia
o Bulging fontanelle, due to increased intracranial pressure
o Hepatosplenomegaly
• Caused by α-L-iduronidase deficiency, leading to GAG accumulation (heparan sulfate and
dermatan sulfate)
Clinical Pearls:
• Hypothyroidism may have some overlapping features (macroglossia, umbilical hernia) but usually
includes constipation, hypotonia, and bradycardia
• Cystic fibrosis causes nasal discharge but not coarse facies or organomegaly
• Down syndrome includes hypotonia, flat facies, and macroglossia but not hepatosplenomegaly
or bulging fontanelle
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 196–197; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd Ed., p. 264–265
☆ Genetics: Mucopolysaccharidosis (Inborn Errors of Metabolism)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 196–197; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 10-month-old girl is brought to the clinic with a history of fever for 6 days. She is otherwise wellappearing, with no localizing signs or symptoms on examination. Her vaccination status is up to
date. Which of the following is the best diagnostic test to identify a potential source of fever in this
patient?
explanation:
• Fever without a source (FWS) in a well-appearing 10-month-old infant requires consideration
of urinary tract infection (UTI), which is one of the most common occult bacterial infections in
this age group.
• Urinalysis is the best test to identify or rule out UTI, particularly in infants 6–24 months with
unexplained fever.
•
It provides rapid evidence of infection (leukocyte esterase, nitrites, WBCs), guiding whether to
initiate antibiotics and obtain urine culture.
Clinical Pearls:
• UTI may be the only sign of serious bacterial illness in infants with prolonged unexplained fever.
• CBC and CRP are nonspecific and not diagnostic for occult infection.
• Blood culture may be obtained if the child is toxic or not improving, but urinalysis is more
diagnostic in well-appearing infants.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 1043–1044; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study
Guide, 3rd Ed., p. 834–835
☆ General Pediatrics: Fever without a source (FWS), UTI
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 1043–1044; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study

stem:
A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with 6 days of irritability, red lips, and
bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis. His mother also reports a persistent fever. On physical
examination, he has scattered erythematous rash, subtle cervical lymphadenopathy, and dry,
cracked lips. What is the next best step in management?
explanation:
Kawasaki disease is an acute, self-limited medium-vessel vasculitis primarily affecting children <5
years. It is the leading cause of acquired heart disease in children in developed countries.
•
•
•
Diagnostic Criteria (Classic Kawasaki Disease): “CRASH & Burn”
o Fever ≥5 days PLUS ≥4 of the following 5 (no other explanation):
o Conjunctivitis – bilateral, non-purulent
o Rash (polymorphous) – non-vesicular, widespread
o Adenopathy (cervical lymphadenopathy) – usually unilateral, >1.5 cm
o Strawberry tongue – oropharyngeal mucositis + red/cracked lips
o Hand and foot swelling – edema, erythema, periungual desquamation
Incomplete Kawasaki:
o Fever ≥5 days plus 2–3 criteria
o Support with labs (↑CRP/ESR, leukocytosis, sterile pyuria,
thrombocytosis) and echocardiographic findings
Phases
•
•
1. Acute Phase (Days 1–10): High fever, diagnostic features present
2. Subacute Phase (Days 11–25): Desquamation, thrombocytosis,
coronary aneurysm risk highest
3. Convalescent Phase (Weeks 4–8): Resolution of clinical features,
normalization of labs
Cardiac Complications:
o Coronary artery aneurysms (most feared)
o Myocarditis, pericardial effusion, valvular disease
o Risk highest in untreated children or those treated late
Management:
o IVIG 2 g/kg IV over 10–12 hours – early administration of IVIG within the first 10 days of
illness significantly reduces risk of coronary artery aneurysms from ~25% to <5%.
o Aspirin:
 High dose: 80–100 mg/kg/day (anti-inflammatory during acute phase)
 Low dose: 3–5 mg/kg/day (antiplatelet after fever resolves)
o Echocardiography:
 At diagnosis, 2–3 weeks, and 6–8 weeks
o Steroids:
 Consider in high-risk patients or IVIG-resistant cases
Clinical Pearls:
• Desquamation (hands/feet) usually starts in subacute phase (10–14 days)
• Labs: ↑CRP/ESR, thrombocytosis (subacute), leukocytosis, sterile pyuria
• Early recognition and IVIG administration within 10 days (ideally <7 days) of fever onset is critical
to reduce coronary artery aneurysm risk from ~25% to <5%.
• Steroids are not first-line unless high-risk or IVIG-resistant cases.
o Children with persistent fever >36 hrs post-IVIG → suspect resistant Kawasaki
• Empiric antibiotics are not indicated without evidence of bacterial infection.
• Always follow with baseline echocardiography and aspirin after IVIG.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 535–537; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd Ed., p. 593–595
☆ Rheumatology: Kawasaki Disease
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 535–537; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 12-month-old boy is seen in the well-child screening clinic. He is asymptomatic. Routine laboratory
results reveal the following:
- Low hemoglobin
- Low mean corpuscular volume (MCV)
- Low mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH)
- Elevated red cell distribution width (RDW)
There is no known family history of anemia or hemoglobinopathy. Which of the following is the most
appropriate next diagnostic test?
explanation:
• This pattern is classic for iron deficiency anemia:
o Microcytic, hypochromic anemia (low MCV, low MCH)
o High RDW: variation in RBC size is a hallmark of nutritional iron deficiency
o Most common in toddlers due to poor dietary iron intake or excessive cow’s milk (>24
oz/day)
• Iron profile (serum iron, ferritin, TIBC, transferrin saturation) confirms diagnosis
•
Early treatment prevents neurodevelopmental delay
Clinical Pearls:
• Thalassemia trait can also cause microcytosis but typically has normal RDW and often higher RBC
count
• Hemoglobin electrophoresis is useful if thalassemia is suspected or if anemia is unresponsive to
iron
• Sickling test is indicated if there’s a family history or signs of hemolytic anemia, not for isolated
microcytosis with high RDW
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 959–960; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd Ed., p. 785–786
☆ Hematology: Microcytic anemia
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 959–960; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 6-year-old child is brought to the clinic by his parents with mild unilateral eyelid swelling that began 2
days ago. The swelling is painless, there is no redness, no fever, and no discharge or conjunctivitis.
There is no tenderness, no proptosis, and vision is unaffected. The child is otherwise well and active.
What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• The image and clinical description are consistent with a chalazion, a chronic granulomatous
inflammation of the Meibomian gland.
• Characteristic features include:
o Painless, non-tender, localized eyelid swelling
o No erythema, no fever, no conjunctival injection, no proptosis
o Often appears as a firm, immobile nodule away from the lid margin
•
•
First-line treatment is conservative management: Hordeola typically resolve within 2
weeks; chalazia typically resolve in a month or more.
o Gentle massage with a warm compress for 10–15 minutes, 3–5 times daily.
o Regular eyelid hygiene
If symptoms persist or recur, refer to ophthalmology for additional management. This may include:
o Topical antibiotics (e.g., bacitracin, erythromycin) +/- topical corticosteroids
o Oral antibiotics (e.g., doxycycline)
o Procedural intervention
Clinical Pearls:
• Hordeolum (stye) is painful, more acute, and may require topical antibiotics.
• Preseptal cellulitis presents with tender, red eyelid swelling—requires antibiotics.
• Orbital cellulitis has systemic signs and ocular involvement—needs emergent imaging and IV
antibiotics.
• Ophthalmology referral is only needed if there is no resolution after several weeks or for incision
and curettage in persistent cases.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 195–196; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd Ed., p. 302–303
☆ Ophthalmology: Chalazion vs Stye
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 195–196; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 5-year-old boy presented to ER with history of yellowish eye discharge. On examination, the eyelid is
red and swollen; the cornea is clear; the eyelashes have a dry and crusted discharge. What is the
diagnosis for his condition?
explanation:
• Blepharitis is a chronic inflammation of the eyelid margins, often
associated with bacterial colonization (Staph)or seborrheic
dermatitis.
• Blepharitis presents with burning or itching of the lids, discharge,
and often a foreign body sensation in the eye.
• Examination shows redness, swelling, and scaling at the lid
margin, and the adjacent conjunctiva may also be inflamed.
o The cornea is typically clear, and symptoms are localized
to the eyelid margin rather than the conjunctiva.
Clinical Pearls:
• Bacterial conjunctivitis usually presents with diffuse conjunctival injection, purulent discharge,
and not typically crusting of lashes.
• Viral conjunctivitis presents with watery discharge, often bilateral, and associated
with preauricular lymphadenopathy.
•
Keratitis involves the cornea, presents with pain, photophobia, and requires slit-lamp exam for
diagnosis.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 196; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide, 3rd
Ed., p. 303–304
☆ Ophthalmology: Blepharitis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 196; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide, 3rd

stem:
A 15-year-old girl weighing 60 kg presents to the emergency department 1 hour after ingesting 20
tablets of 500 mg paracetamol in a suspected intentional overdose. She is currently asymptomatic
and hemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• The patient ingested a potentially toxic dose (10 g > 150
mg/kg) of paracetamol.
• She presented within 1 hour, which is within the
window where activated charcoal can significantly reduce
drug absorption.
• N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is the antidote, but its use
depends on serum acetaminophen levels plotted on
the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, which becomes valid
only 4 hours post-ingestion.
• Administering activated charcoal now does not interfere
with later NAC therapy or lab assessment.
Clinical Pearls:
• NAC is indicated if level above treatment line at 4 hours post-ingestion or if
ingestion >150 mg/kg and serum level not available.
• Activated charcoal should be given within 1–2 hours of ingestion if airway is
protected and patient is alert.
• Discharging or delaying treatment is inappropriate given the toxic dose.
• Most useful indicator factor for paracetamol toxicity: PT+ pH
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 1113–1114; Osama Naga, p. 909–910
☆ Emergency Medicine/Toxicology: Acetaminophen overdose
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 1113–1114; Osama Naga, p. 909–910

stem:
A 3-year-old child is brought to the emergency department 4 hours after ingesting an unknown
quantity of iron tablets. The ingestion was unwitnessed, and no serum iron level is yet available. The
child is starting to show signs of abdominal discomfort and vomiting. What is the most appropriate
next step in management?

options.label: C
options.text: Obtain serum iron level and abdominal X-ray

explanation:
• In cases of suspected toxic iron ingestion where the exact amount is unknown, and the patient is
symptomatic, empiric chelation therapy with deferoxamine is indicated if more than 4 hours have
passed and serum levels are not available.
• Serum iron level is most useful between 4–6 hours, but in symptomatic patients or severe
ingestion, do not delay therapy
Iron Poisoning
Toxic Dose: >60 mg/kg of elemental iron is potentially toxic
Stages of Toxicity:
1. Stage I (0–6 hrs): GI symptoms – vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, hematemesis
2. Stage II (6–24 hrs): Apparent recovery
3. Stage III (12–48 hrs): Systemic toxicity – shock, metabolic acidosis, hepatic failure
4. Stage IV (2–5 days): Liver failure
5. Stage V (2–6 weeks): GI scarring, obstruction
Key Investigations:
• Serum iron level (peak at 4–6 hrs post-ingestion)
>500 mcg/dL = severe toxicity
• Abdominal X-ray for radiopaque tablets
• LFTs, ABG, glucose, coagulation panel
Management:
• Activated charcoal: Ineffective (iron not bound)
• Whole bowel irrigation if large pill burden
• IV Deferoxamine if:
o Serum iron >500 mcg/dL
o OR patient is symptomatic (shock, acidosis, lethargy)
regardless of level
Deferoxamine clue: Urine turns pink ("vin rose") = due to
ferrioxamine
Clinical Pearls:
• Activated charcoal does not bind iron, and is ineffective
• First symptom: nausea and vomiting
• Tx; whole bowel irrigation and deferoxamine
• Toddler + prenatal vitamins → Suspect toxicity
• >60 mg/kg ingested → Toxic dose
• <1 hr post-ingestion → Consider whole bowel irrigation
• 4–6 hrs post-ingestion → Check serum iron level
• Symptomatic + no level yet → Start deferoxamine
• Shock + normal iron level → Still give deferoxamine
• >500 mcg/dL serum iron → Chelation indicated
• GI symptoms resolve (stage II), then shock → Stage III toxicity
• Abdominal X-ray: radiopaque tablets → Confirm ingestion
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 1111–1112; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study
Guide, 3rd Ed., p. 907–908
☆ Emergency Medicine/Toxicology: Iron Poisoning
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 1111–1112; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study

stem:
While you were oncall covering neonatal intensive care unit in a tertiary center, you received a
consultation from your colleague in a primary hospital. He just had a delivery of a newborn who
developed respiratory distress immediately after birth. His abdomen is scaphoid. No breath
sounds are heard on the left side of his chest, but they are audible on the right. What is your advice to
do right now?

options.label: D
options.text: Surgical repair as soon as possible

explanation:
• The newborn has classic signs of congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH):
o Immediate respiratory distress at birth
o Scaphoid abdomen (abdominal contents herniated into chest)
o Absent breath sounds on left, shifted to the right
• The first and most critical step is to intubate the infant immediately to
provide controlled ventilation and prevent gastric insufflation, which
worsens respiratory compromise.
• Bag-mask ventilation is contraindicated in CDH because it can insufflate
the stomach and bowel, further compressing the lungs and heart.
Clinical Pearls:
• Avoid bag-mask ventilation in suspected CDH
• OG/NG tube should be placed to decompress the stomach
• ECMO is reserved for severe pulmonary hypertension or refractory hypoxemia, not first step
• Surgical repair is delayed until the infant is hemodynamically stable and adequately oxygenated
Scaphoid abdomen
📚 Source: UWorld; AMBOSS.
☆ NICU: Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia (CDH)
source: UWorld; AMBOSS.

stem:
A 6-year-old asthmatic boy, with a strong family history of atopy, presented to your clinic with a 3
months history of burning sensation, epigastric tenderness, and difficulty swallowing solid food.
On examination, there is mild epigastric pain otherwise unremarkable examination. He is allergic to
multiple foods. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
•
•
•
•
•
•
The child has a chronic history (3 months) of epigastric burning, dysphagia to solids,
and epigastric tenderness, in the context of:
o Asthma and strong family history of atopy
o Multiple food allergies
Can be triggered by multiple foods, possibly aeroallergens
This clinical pattern is highly suggestive of eosinophilic esophagitis
(EoE), a chronic immune-mediated disorder characterized by
eosinophilic infiltration of the esophageal mucosa.
Diagnosis: EGD (w/ biopsy) showing >15 eosinophils/HPF at more
than one location
Symptoms often include:
o Dysphagia, especially to solids
o Food impaction
o Heartburn or abdominal pain not responding to PPIs
Treatment
o Goal of therapy: Relieve symptoms and prevent disease
progression
o Allergy testing for trigger foods, elimination diets (empiric or skin
testing–based)
o Fluticasone (sprayed and swallowed) or oral viscous budesonide
Clinical Pearls:
• PPI trial failure is often a clue for EoE
• Endoscopy with esophageal biopsy is diagnostic (≥15 eosinophils/HPF)
• Hiatal hernia is rare in children and doesn’t explain atopy or dysphagia
• Peptic ulcer disease causes epigastric pain but not dysphagia or food
impaction
• Gastric tumors are extremely rare in this age group and don’t match the
allergic context
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 575–576; Osama Naga, 3rd Ed., p. 696–697
☆ Gastroenterology: Eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 575–576; Osama Naga, 3rd Ed., p. 696–697

stem:
A 6-month-old infant who is exclusively breastfed is brought for evaluation of poor weight gain.
Parents report small, non-bilious emesis after some feeds, but the infant is otherwise alert, feeding
well, and meeting developmental milestones. On review of the growth chart, weight consistently
tracks at the 3rd percentile, while length tracks at the 50th percentile. What is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.label: B
options.text: Admit and count calories with metabolic screening

answer: C. Begin supplemental formula after each feed
explanation:
• This infant shows a classic pattern of failure to thrive due to inadequate caloric intake:
o Weight < length percentile suggests calorie deficiency, not systemic illness
o Normal length growth indicates preserved linear growth and rules against chronic illness or
endocrine disorder
• The child is otherwise well, with mild physiologic reflux, and no red flags.
• The most appropriate step is to increase caloric intake—supplementing breast milk with
formula is safe and effective at this age.
Admission Red Flags:
• Weight crossing percentiles rapidly downward
• Signs of dehydration or malnutrition
• Developmental delay with growth failure
• Suspicion of abuse/neglect or caregiver incapacity
• Failed outpatient weight gain despite intervention
• Need for rapid refeeding or IV nutritional support
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 98–100; Osama
Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide, 3rd Ed., p. 639–640
☆ General Pediatrics: Failure to Thrive
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 98–100; Osama

stem:
A 10-year-old girl presents with recurrent episodes of abdominal pain and vomiting. She has a
known history of recurrent pancreatitis, and current symptoms are similar to her previous episodes.
You suspect an underlying cause for the recurrent inflammation. Which of the following is the best
investigation to aid in determining the etiology?
explanation:
• Recurrent pancreatitis is defined as ≥2 episodes of documented acute pancreatitis with complete
resolution in between.
• In a child with recurrent pancreatitis, the best test to identify anatomic or obstructive
causes (such as biliary ductal anomalies, choledochal cysts, or pancreas divisum) is ERCP.
• ERCP provides detailed visualization of the pancreatic and biliary ducts and also allows
for therapeutic intervention if needed (e.g., sphincterotomy, stenting).
• While non-invasive tests (e.g., lipids, ultrasound) are useful screening tools, ERCP is the most
definitive to detect structural causes.
• UpToDate: MRCP is the imaging study of choice in the evaluation for chronic pancreatitis
Etiology of recurrent pancreatitis in pediatrics:
• Anatomic → Pancreas divisum, choledochal cyst, biliary
stones/sludge
• Genetic → PRSS1, SPINK1, CFTR mutations
• Metabolic → Hypertriglyceridemia, hypercalcemia
• Drug-induced → Valproic acid, azathioprine, corticosteroids
• Infectious → Mumps, Coxsackievirus, EBV, CMV
• Autoimmune → Autoimmune pancreatitis (IgG4-related)
• Trauma → Blunt abdominal trauma, non-accidental injury
• Idiopathic → Consider genetic panel if workup is negative
Clinical Pearls:
• Serum lipid profile is essential for detecting hypertriglyceridemia, but does not visualize ductal
anatomy
• Ultrasound is limited in detecting pancreatic duct anomalies
• CT scan helps assess complications, not underlying etiology
• In many centers, MRCP (non-invasive) may be preferred over ERCP for diagnosis, but ERCP remains
gold standard when anatomy needs confirmation and intervention
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 600–601; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 679–681
☆ Gastroenterology: Recurrent pancreatitis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 600–601; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 679–681

stem:
A 3-year-old girl is brought to the clinic for evaluation of chronic watery diarrhea and persistent rash.
On examination, she has erythematous, scaly dermatitis involving the perioral area, diaper region,
and extremities. Her growth is suboptimal, and she appears irritable. Which of the following is the
most appropriate test to order?

options.label: D
options.text: Total IgA and tissue transglutaminase IgA

explanation:
• The presentation is classic for acrodermatitis enteropathica, a condition caused by zinc
deficiency, either inherited or acquired.
• Acrodermatitis enteropathica (inherited, AR – SLC39A4 mutation)
• Acquired deficiency:
o Prematurity
o Chronic diarrhea
o Malabsorption (e.g., IBD, celiac)
o Poor dietary intake or TPN without zinc supplementation
• Key findings:
o Chronic diarrhea
o Periorificial and acral dermatitis (perioral, perineal, and distal limbs)
o Alopecia, irritability, failure to thrive
• Serum zinc level is the definitive diagnostic test; confirmed by clinical response to zinc
supplementation
•
Treatment:
o Zinc supplementation: 1–3 mg/kg/day
elemental zinc
o Lifelong if inherited; temporary if acquired
Clinical pearls:
• Zinc deficiency = diarrhea + alopecia +
rash (especially mouth, anus, eyes)
• Acquired zinc deficiency seen in breastfed
infants, malnourished, or TPN-dependent patients
• Treat with oral zinc supplementation — rapid
resolution of symptoms
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 242–243; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 336–337
☆ Gastroenterology: Zinc Deficiency, Acrodermatitis enteropathica
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 242–243; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 336–337

stem:
A term neonate presents with recurrent episodes of cyanosis during feeding that improve when crying.
Physical examination is otherwise unremarkable. What is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?

options.label: C
options.text: Pass a nasogastric tube through each nostril to assess patency

answer: C. Pass a nasogastric tube through each nostril to assess patency
explanation:
Cyanosis during feeding that improves with crying is classic for bilateral choanal atresia.
• Choanal atresia is characterized by congenital obstruction of the posterior nasal passages.
• When crying, the baby breathes through the mouth;
during feeding, nasal obstruction leads to hypoxia.
• Infants with bilateral choanal atresia can present with
upper airway obstruction and cyanosis with feeding, but
not excessive drooling and coughing.
• Inability to pass a nasogastric tube through the nares
is suggestive of the diagnosis.
• Further evaluation includes CT scan to define the
anatomy and surgical planning.
Other options:
• Chest X-ray may be useful later, but not the first step.
• ABG does not localize the cause.
• Sepsis is less likely without systemic signs (e.g., temperature instability, poor feeding, irritability).
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 233–234; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 332–333.
☆ General Pediatrics: Choanal Atresia
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 233–234; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 332–333.

stem:
An 18 month old infant brought by parents for loss of consciousness. The parents report that the
episodes occur when the child is angry or frustrated, followed by intense crying, and then a brief
period of unresponsiveness lasting a few seconds. The child recovers spontaneously. Physical and
neurological examinations are normal. What is the most important action?
explanation:
This is a classic presentation of a breath-holding spell — a benign, self-limited event commonly seen in
children between 6 months and 6 years. The cyanotic type is triggered by emotional stimuli (anger,
frustration), followed by crying and transient loss of consciousness due to vagal overactivity and apnea.
• No seizure activity, no prolonged LOC, and full recovery are key diagnostic clues.
• No investigations are needed if the history is classic and the exam is normal.
• Important: Always rule out iron-deficiency anemia, which can worsen frequency/severity.
• EEG or neuro referral is not indicated unless episodes are atypical (e.g., postictal phase, no clear
trigger, abnormal exam).
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 78–79; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 168.
☆ General Pediatrics: Breath-holding spell
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 78–79; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 168.

stem:
A newborn term infant after birth develops cyanosis that improves with crying. What’s the most likely
diagnosis?
explanation:
This is a classic presentation of bilateral choanal atresia. Newborns are obligate nasal breathers;
obstruction of the posterior nasal passages causes cyanosis, particularly when quiet or feeding. Crying
opens the mouth and relieves the obstruction — hence cyanosis improves.
• Transposition of great arteries and Tetralogy of Fallot cause persistent central cyanosis, not
intermittent.
• Laryngomalacia presents with inspiratory stridor, not cyanosis improved by crying.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 233–234; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 332–333.
☆ General Pediatrics: Choanal Atresia
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 233–234; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 332–333.

stem:
A 10-year-old child presents with sudden-onset unilateral facial droop, inability to close the eye on
the affected side, and loss of taste sensation. There is no history of trauma, fever, or systemic
symptoms. Symptoms have been present for 2 days. What is the most likely cause?
explanation:
This presentation is classic for Bell’s palsy (idiopathic facial nerve palsy), the most common cause of
acute-onset unilateral facial nerve (CN VII) paralysis in children.
• Bell’s = idiopathic = MCC of facial nerve palsy
• Sudden facial asymmetry, inability to close the eye, and loss of taste (chorda tympani
involvement) are hallmark features.
• Usually self-limited, often post-viral, and occurs without systemic findings.
• Trauma or AOM may cause facial palsy, but they are far less common.
• Cerebral thrombosis typically presents with additional neurological signs and does not affect taste
or cause isolated LMN-type facial weakness.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 663–664; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 244.
☆ Neurology: Bell’s Palsy, Cranial Nerve Palsies
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 663–664; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 244.

stem:
A 3-year-old unvaccinated girl comes to urgent care because since she woke up that morning she has
been “clumsy” and “has been falling much more than usual.” She has slurred speech. She has not
vomited. On examination, she is afebrile and well appearing. There is horizontal nystagmus on endgaze bilaterally. When she reaches for a toy, her accuracy is poor bilaterally. Her gait is wide-based
and she tends to veer to the side and prefers to walk along the hallway wall to help her balance. Her
mother notes that about 10 days earlier she had a febrile illness with vomiting and diarrhea. What is
the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This is a prototypical case of acute post-infectious cerebellar ataxia, the most common cause of acute
ataxia in children aged 2–5 years.
• It often follows a viral illness by 1–3 weeks.
• Symptoms (cerebellar signs): acute ataxia, dysmetria, nystagmus, and slurred speech in a wellappearing child.
• Varicella zoster virus (VZV) is the most common cause, especially in unvaccinated children.
• HOWEVER, the prior prodrome of GI symptoms is more consistent with enterovirus (echovirus,
coxsackievirus) which can also acute acute postinfectious cerebellar ataxia.
• Causes in children: EBV, VZV, coxsackievirus,
COVID-19, parvovirus B19, HHV-6, hepatitis A,
enteroviruses, measles, mumps, etc.
Other choices:
• Measles is associated more with SSPE (subacute
sclerosing panencephalitis) years after infection.
• Enterovirus and HSV typically
cause encephalitis or meningoencephalitis, not
isolated cerebellar signs.
In post-infectious cerebellar ataxia:
Classic board answer = Varicella
Clinically precise answer in this scenario =
Enterovirus (because of preceding GI illness and
no rash)
•
•
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p.
731–733; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 249–250.
☆ Neurology: Acute Post-Infectious Cerebellar Ataxia
(Post-Infectious Cerebellitis)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p.

stem:
A 2-year-old child undergoing toilet training is noted to have a painless, non-itchy mass that
protrudes with defecation and spontaneously reduces. There is no bleeding or discomfort reported.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Rectal mucosal prolapse is common in toddlers, especially during toilet training, due to straining
and increased intra-abdominal pressure.
• The prolapsed mucosa appears as a painless, soft, concentric mass that
protrudes with defecation and spontaneously reduces.
• It is common in children, especially with chronic diarrhea, malnutrition,
cystic fibrosis, or straining.
• It often presents with painless bleeding and a visible mass that retracts
spontaneously.
• Internal hemorrhoids are rare in children and usually not visible externally.
• External hemorrhoids are typically painful, thrombosed, and visible at
rest.
• Anal fissures present with painful defecation and streaks of blood, not
protrusion.
Clinical pearls:
• Evaluate for underlying causes: cystic fibrosis (esp. with recurrent rectal prolapse), constipation,
or diarrhea.
• Rectal prolapse in a previously healthy infant = screen for CF
• Cystic fibrosis = recurrent prolapse + failure to thrive + foul stools
• In toddlers: think toilet training, constipation, or loose stools from excessive juice
• If neuro deficits present → evaluate for spina bifida or sacral anomalies
• Management includes:
o Treating the underlying condition
o Stool softeners, avoiding straining
o Manual reduction if needed
o Rarely requires surgery unless recurrent
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 520; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 126
☆ Gastroenterology: Rectal Bleeding and Rectal Prolapse
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 520; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Review, p. 126

stem:
A young boy presents with difficulty opening his eyes and a facial expression that appears sad
despite being happy. His exam suggests facial muscle weakness and possible ptosis, without
altered mental status or seizures. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
explanation:
This clinical presentation is classic for juvenile myasthenia gravis, characterized by:
• Ptosis (difficulty opening the eyes)
• Facial weakness leading to a "sad or expressionless face"
• Fluctuating weakness, often worse with activity and improving with rest
The Edrophonium test is a short-acting acetylcholinesterase inhibitor used to transiently improve
neuromuscular transmission, confirming myasthenia gravis.
• CK is elevated in muscular dystrophies, not MG
• MRI and EEG are not first-line in suspected neuromuscular junction disorders
★ Myasthenia Gravis:
• Definition: Autoimmune disorder caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors at
the neuromuscular junction → impaired neuromuscular transmission.
• Types:
o Transient neonatal MG – due to maternal antibodies, self-limited
o Congenital MG – genetic defect in NMJ transmission
o Juvenile MG – acquired autoimmune, more common in females
• Presentation:
o Fluctuating muscle weakness, worsens with activity, improves with rest
o Ptosis, diplopia, fatigable chewing, nasal voice
o Bulbar symptoms: dysphagia, poor sucking, weak cry (infants)
o Normal reflexes and sensation
o Respiratory crisis may require ventilation
• Diagnosis:
o Edrophonium (Tensilon) test – rapid symptom improvement (used less now)
o Anti-ACh receptor antibody test – positive in most autoimmune MG
o EMG with decremental response to repetitive stimulation
o CT/MRI chest – evaluate for thymoma or thymic hyperplasia
• Management:
o Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors: Pyridostigmine (first-line),
neostigmine – Dose: 0.5–1 mg/kg/dose PO q4–6h
o Corticosteroids if refractory
o IVIG or plasmapheresis for myasthenic crisis
o Thymectomy in generalized MG (if thymoma or no response to
meds)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 740–741; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 259–260.
☆ Neurology: Myasthenia gravis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 740–741; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 259–260.
topic: • Definition: Autoimmune disorder caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors at

stem:
A 14-year-old boy with a known history of ulcerative colitis, currently maintained on infliximab,
presents to the emergency department with acute worsening of bloody diarrhea, severe abdominal
pain, and fever for 1 day. On examination, he appears toxic, with diffuse abdominal tenderness and
guarding. Bowel sounds are decreased. There are currently no signs of peritonitis. What is the most
appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
This patient is showing signs concerning for toxic megacolon, a life-threatening complication of UC.
Key features:
• Fever, abdominal pain, bloody diarrhea, ill appearance, tenderness
• Use of anti-TNF (infliximab) increases infection and complication risk
• The first step is to rule out perforation or colonic dilation:
o Abdominal X-ray is essential to assess for toxic megacolon, perforation, or dilated bowel.
o Colonoscopy is contraindicated in this acute setting due to risk of perforation.
o Stool PCR for C. difficile is important later, but ruling out surgical emergency takes priority.
IBD High-Yield Board Tips
• Growth failure + perianal disease → Think Crohn
• Bloody diarrhea + toxic megacolon risk → Think UC
• PSC (primary sclerosing cholangitis) → Strongly associated with UC, risk of cholangiocarcinoma
• Do not perform colonoscopy in suspected toxic megacolon — get abdominal X-ray first
• IBD + arthritis or skin lesions → common extraintestinal manifestations
• Screen for TB and hepatitis B before starting anti-TNF biologics
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 688–690; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 460–461.
☆ Gastroenterology: Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD), Toxic Megacolon
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 688–690; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 460–461.

stem: What is the maintenance IV fluid requirement over 24 hours for a 34 kg boy?
explanation:
100-50-20 rule (OR 4-2-1 x24)
For weight = 34 kg:
• First 10 kg → 10 × 100 mL = 1000 mL
• Second 10 kg → 10 × 50 mL = 500 mL
• Remaining 14 kg → 14 × 20 mL = 280 mL
Total = 1000 + 500 + 280 = 1780 mL per 24 hours
OR alternatively, calculate the hourly rate and multiply by 24:
• First 10 kg → 10 × 4 mL = 40 mL
• Second 10 kg → 10 × 2 mL = 20 mL
• Remaining 14 kg → 14 × 1 mL = 14 mL
Total = 40 + 20 + 14 = 74 mL per hour x 24 = 1776ml per 24 hrs
☆ General Pediatrics: Fluids

stem:
A 13-year-old girl is brought by her mother to the clinic due to concern about short stature. She is
otherwise healthy with no significant symptoms. Growth chart review shows her height is below the 5th
percentile. Her physical examination is normal, with no dysmorphic features or signs of chronic
disease. Her mother's height is 155 cm and her father's height is 178 cm. What is the most
appropriate estimate for this patient's mid-parental target height?
explanation:
Midparental height (girl) = (Father’s height + Mother’s height – 13)/2 = [(178-13)+155]/2 = 160 cm ± 8.5 cm
• Mid-parental height is a key reference point in evaluating short stature.
o A child falling within ±8.5 cm of the MPH is generally considered within normal genetic potential.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Endocrinology, Short stature
☆ Endocrinology: Short stature, mid-parental height calculation
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Endocrinology, Short stature

stem:
A child presents with a history of recent viral illness and now complains of shortness of breath on
exertion. On examination, she has fever, tachycardia, muffled heart sounds, and a gallop rhythm.
There is no history of chest pain. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Myocarditis often follows a viral illness and presents with:
o Tachycardia disproportionate to fever
o Exertional dyspnea and signs of early heart failure
o Gallop rhythm (S3/S4), possibly muffled heart sounds
o Usually no chest pain
o Patients die weeks after due to arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death
• Pericarditis is more likely to present with chest pain and a pericardial rub, not a gallop.
• Endocarditis presents with fever, new murmur, and embolic phenomena, not gallop/SOB.
Myocarditis: Viral prodrome + gallop + SOB = myocarditis
•
•
•
•
•
Most common cause: Viral (Coxsackie B, adenovirus, parvovirus B19, COVID-19)
Typical history: Recent viral illness followed by fatigue, dyspnea, poor feeding
Key findings:
o Tachycardia out of proportion to fever
o Gallop rhythm (S3)
o Hepatomegaly, signs of CHF
o Muffled heart sounds (if associated pericardial involvement)
Diagnostics:
o ECG: low voltage, ST-T changes
o CXR: cardiomegaly
o Echo: ↓ ejection fraction
o ↑ Troponin, ↑ BNP
Management:
o Supportive care: inotropes (milrinone, dobutamine)
o Consider IVIG for viral myocarditis
Acute pericarditis: Chest pain + rub + positional relief = pericarditis
•
•
•
•
•
•
Common causes: Viral (Coxsackie), TB, post-surgical (pericardiotomy)
Presentation:
o Pleuritic chest pain, worse lying down, improves leaning forward
o Fever, malaise
Key signs:
o Pericardial friction rub
o Tachycardia
o Muffled heart sounds (if effusion)
Diagnostics:
o ECG: diffuse ST elevation, PR depression
o Echo: pericardial effusion (look for tamponade)
Complications:
o Cardiac tamponade (Beck’s triad: hypotension, JVD, muffled sounds)
Treatment:
o NSAIDs + colchicine
o Pericardiocentesis if tamponade
Infective Endocarditis: Fever + murmur + embolic signs = endocarditis
•
•
•
•
•
•
•
Risk factors: Congenital heart disease, prosthetic valves, central lines
Organisms: Strep viridans, Staph aureus, HACEK
Clinical features:
o Fever + new murmur
o Splenomegaly, petechiae, fatigue
Classic signs:
o Osler nodes (tender finger/toe pads)
o Janeway lesions (non-tender palms/soles)
o Roth spots (retinal hemorrhages)
o Splinter hemorrhages
Diagnosis:
o 3 blood cultures
o Echo: look for vegetations (TTE/TEE)
o Use Modified Duke Criteria
Treatment:
o IV antibiotics for 4–6 weeks
o Surgery if complications (valve destruction, emboli)
Infective endocarditis prophylaxis: oral amoxicillin
o Indications:
 Previous Hx of endocarditis
 Unrepaired cyanotic congenital heart disease
 Prosthetic heart valves
o Procedures: Dental work, Respiratory procedure involving incision of the respiratory mucosa
(tonsillectomy, adenoidectomy), Skin procedure (infected skin or musculoskeletal)
 other surgical procedures (ex: hernia repair) do not warrant prophylaxis!
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 492–494; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 387–388. 10
Pediatric ECGs not to be missed: https://dontforgetthebubbles.com/ten-not-to-be-missed-paediatric-ecgs/
☆ Cardiology: Myocarditis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 492–494; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 387–388. 10

stem:
An infant presents with a history of diaphoresis during feeding. On examination, basal crackles are
heard. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Diaphoresis during feeding is a classic sign of congestive heart failure in infants, due to the high
metabolic demand of feeding and limited cardiac reserve.
• Basal crackles indicate pulmonary congestion, further supporting the diagnosis.
• Other signs may include tachypnea, hepatomegaly, poor weight gain, and cardiomegaly on imaging.
★ Dilated Cardiomyopathy:
• Most common pediatric cardiomyopathy; involves ventricular dilation + ↓ systolic function (EF)
• Causes: idiopathic, viral (Coxsackie B), genetic (AD), metabolic
(VLCAD), toxic (doxorubicin), nutritional (thiamine)
o Familial DCM is often due to autosomal dominant
mutations in sarcomere or structural proteins
 Most common genetic cause: truncating mutations
in the TTN gene (encodes titin)
 Titin anchors myosin to Z-discs and contributes
to passive myocardial tension
 Incomplete penetrance → symptoms may be delayed
or absent in some carriers
• Signs: poor feeding, tachypnea, fatigue, hepatomegaly, S3 gallop
o Neonatal: diaphoresis/fatigue during feeding
• Red flags: family history of sudden death, non-ketotic hypoglycemia
• Workup: CXR (cardiomegaly), ECG (tachycardia, low voltage), echo (dilated LV, ↓ EF)
•
•
•
•
Management: ACE inhibitors, diuretics, beta-blockers, milrinone if decompensated
Screen for metabolic/genetic causes in infants
Risk of arrhythmias, heart failure, thromboembolism
Transplant for refractory cases
Clinical pearls:
• In infants, look for feeding fatigue, diaphoresis, poor weight gain, tachypnea, and decreased
activity as signs of heart failure.
• Fatty acid oxidation disorders can cause DCM with non-ketotic hypoglycemia — always screen
metabolically
• Consider echocardiography + metabolic/genetic testing in unexplained cardiomyopathy
• Gallop rhythm = S3 → reflects poor ventricular function
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 430
☆ Cardiology: Heart failure
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 430
topic: • Most common pediatric cardiomyopathy; involves ventricular dilation + ↓ systolic function (EF)

stem:
A child presented with vomiting and abdominal pain localized to the right lower quadrant. On
examination he had positive rebound tenderness on the right iliac fossa. What is the best to confirm
his diagnosis?
explanation:
• In the absence of ultrasound as an option, CT abdomen is the most accurate test to confirm
appendicitis.
• It has high sensitivity and specificity, especially when clinical findings are suggestive.
• Pediatric appendicitis score is used for clinical risk assessment, not for confirmation.
• Abdominal X-ray is not helpful in appendicitis diagnosis.
• Empiric antibiotics are not started until diagnosis is confirmed or strongly suspected.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 829–830; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 533.
☆ Emergency Medicine: Appendicitis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 829–830; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 533.

stem:
A child presents with recurrent episodes of hemarthrosis. What is the most important initial
laboratory test?
explanation:
• Recurrent hemarthrosis is highly suggestive of a coagulation factor
deficiency, especially Hemophilia A (Factor VIII deficiency) or
Hemophilia B (Factor IX deficiency).
• Both are X-linked recessive and affect the intrinsic pathway, which is
best assessed by aPTT.
o PT and platelet count are typically normal in such patients.
• PT (extrinsic pathway) and platelet count are usually normal in hemophilia
• Platelet disorders cause mucocutaneous bleeding, not deep joint
bleeding.
How to memorize the coagulation cascade: https://youtu.be/kGkjB8UtfMg?si=FyeIu9gQwBQU12l8
★ Hemophilia:
• Definition:
o Hemophilia A = ↓ Factor VIII
o Hemophilia B = ↓ Factor IX
o X-linked recessive, males affected, ~30% new mutations
• Severity (by factor activity):
o Severe (<1%) → spontaneous joint/muscle bleeds
o Moderate (1–5%) → bleeding with minor trauma
o Mild (>5%) → bleeding with major trauma/surgery
• Clinical Clues:
o Excessive bruising from minor trauma
o Hemarthrosis (knee > elbow > ankle > shoulder > wrists
> hips)
o Muscle hematomas, post-op bleeding (e.g.,
circumcision)
o Life-threatening: intracranial hemorrhage
• Diagnosis:
o ↑ aPTT, normal PT, normal platelets/BT
o Mixing study corrects → factor assay confirms diagnosis
• Treatment:
o Factor VIII dose = desired % × wt (kg) × 0.5
o Factor IX dose = desired % × wt (kg) × 1.3
o Target:
 Severe bleed: 80–100%
 Minor bleed: 30–50%
o Desmopressin (DDAVP): mild Hemophilia A only
o Antifibrinolytics (e.g., tranexamic acid): mucosal bleeds
• Prophylaxis:
o Early regular recombinant factor (2–3×/week)
o Prevents arthropathy & spontaneous bleeds
• Inhibitors:
o Seen in ~30% Hemophilia A
o Use bypass agents: rFVIIa, aPCC
• Avoid:
o IM injections, aspirin/NSAIDs
o Use acetaminophen
o Vaccines: SC with firm pressure
• Differentials:
o vWD, ITP, leukemia, Vit K deficiency, liver disease, NAT
★ Overview of Bleeding Disorders:
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 928–930; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 638–640; Pocket
Pediatrics, Hemophilia.
☆ Hematology: Hemophilia
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 928–930; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 638–640; Pocket

stem:
An infant presents with a history of repeated brief abnormal movements of the entire body lasting 2–
3 seconds. The child also has developmental delay. EEG is shown below.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• The history of brief, sudden bilateral movements in an infant lasting 2–3 seconds, especially
with developmental delay, is classic for infantile spasms (West syndrome).
• The EEG in the image shows hypsarrhythmia: chaotic, high-amplitude, asynchronous slow waves
and spikes — pathognomonic for infantile spasms.
• Lennox-Gastaut syndrome occurs in older children (1–8 years), not infancy, and EEG shows slow
spike-and-wave discharges, not hypsarrhythmia.
Infantile Spasms:
• Head nodding or drop with flexor spasms; brief symmetric contraction of both arms and neck
stiffening
o Sudden extension of the arms, flexion of the knees and crying
• Age: 3-7 months
• EEG showing background disorganization, chaotic wave activity, multiple slow waves, spikes, and
polyspike → hypsarrhythmia
•
•
•
•
West syndrome → triad of infantile spasms, psychomotor retardation, and hypsarrhythmia
Tuberous sclerosis (found in 2-25% of pts)
Skin examination for neurocutaneous findings of tuberous sclerosis → ash-leaf spots,
angiofibromas, shagreen patches, subungual fibromas
Tx:
o 1st line: ACTH
o Prednisone
o Vigabatrin (1st line for infantile spasms in tuberous sclerosis pts)
o Vitamin B6
o
o
Benzodiazepines
Surgery → done in cortical dysplasia

stem:
A 12-year-old girl with known persistent asthma presents with persistent nausea and vomiting. She
denies fever and diarrhea. Her medications include daily fluticasone and as-needed salbutamol. She
had recently received a 14-day course of oral prednisolone for worsening asthma symptoms with no
clinical improvement. Current laboratory studies reveal hyponatremia, hypoglycemia, and elevated
creatinine. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Prolonged corticosteroid use leads to HPA axis suppression, causing secondary adrenal
insufficiency.
• Hyponatremia and hypoglycemia are key biochemical findings.
• Elevated creatinine indicates volume depletion, common in adrenal crisis.
• No response to steroids and absence of fever or diarrhea rules out asthma exacerbation or
gastroenteritis.
• SIADH does not cause hypoglycemia or volume depletion.
★ Adrenal Insufficiency:
• Types:
o Primary (Addison’s): adrenal gland failure (↑ ACTH, ↓ cortisol, ↓ aldosterone)
o Secondary: pituitary dysfunction (↓ ACTH, ↓ cortisol, normal aldosterone)
o Tertiary: hypothalamic cause or chronic steroid use (most common in peds)
• Common pediatric cause:
o Exogenous steroid withdrawal (suppression of HPA axis)
o Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency)
• Classic lab findings:
o Hyponatremia
o Hyperkalemia in primary AI due to absence of aldosterone
o Hypoglycemia (more pronounced in infants and young
children)
o Low cortisol, high ACTH in primary
o Low cortisol, low ACTH in secondary/tertiary
• Clinical signs:
o Fatigue, hypotension, salt craving, vomiting, abdominal pain
o Hyperpigmentation (only in primary due to high ACTH)
o Shock in adrenal crisis
• Adrenal crisis triggers:
o Infection, trauma, surgery, abrupt steroid withdrawal
•
•
•
•
•
Diagnosis:
o Early morning serum cortisol <3 µg/dL = diagnostic
o ACTH stimulation test (cosyntropin) confirms diagnosis
o Plasma ACTH, renin, aldosterone to differentiate primary
vs secondary
Acute management:
o Hydrocortisone IV 50–100 mg/m²/day divided q6h
o IV fluids: D5NS boluses (20 mL/kg) for hypotension +
maintenance
o Correct hypoglycemia: D10–D25 2–4 mL/kg bolus if
needed
Chronic management:
o Hydrocortisone PO 8–12 mg/m²/day divided TID
o Fludrocortisone 0.05–0.2 mg/day for mineralocorticoid replacement (in primary)
Stress dosing:
o Triple baseline steroid dose during illness, IM hydrocortisone if vomiting
Board traps:
o SIADH has euvolemic hyponatremia with normal glucose and BP
o No hyperkalemia in secondary adrenal insufficiency
o Persistent hypoglycemia in neonate → think CAH or adrenal insufficiency
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 347; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guide, p. 379
☆ Endocrinology: Adrenal Insufficiency
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 347; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guide, p. 379

stem:
A 12-year-old boy presents with purulent discharge from a chest wound sustained 1 week ago
following a road traffic accident (RTA) and blunt chest trauma. There is no history of fever or systemic
signs of sepsis. On exam, the wound shows signs of local infection with surrounding erythema and
pus. What is the most likely pathogen?
explanation:
• Staphylococcus aureus is the most common cause of wound infections, especially in posttraumatic or surgical wounds.
• It causes purulent infections, often with localized abscesses, and is common in skin and soft
tissue infections.
• Pseudomonas aeruginosa is typically seen in burn wounds or in immunocompromised hosts and
produces green-blue discharge with characteristic odor.
• Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A Strep) causes rapidly progressive soft tissue infections, but
less purulent and more likely with systemic signs.
• Streptococcus pneumoniae is primarily associated with respiratory tract infections, not skin or
wound infections.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 903; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd ed., page 546
☆ Infectious Diseases: Localized wound infection after trauma
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 903; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 4-year-old boy with a history of ventricular septal defect (VSD) repair two months ago is brought to the
emergency department with persistent fever for two weeks, fatigue, poor appetite, and a new holosystolic
murmur. On examination, he appears pale with mild hepatomegaly but no rash or petechiae. There is no
history of recent dental work or invasive procedures. What is the most likely causative organism?
explanation:
• Staphylococcus aureus is the most common cause of early-onset infective endocarditis
especially within 2 months of cardiac surgery, including prosthetic material or suture
involvement.
• It causes acute, aggressive disease, often with systemic symptoms, emboli, and valve
destruction.
• Streptococcus pyogenes rarely causes endocarditis.
• Klebsiella is not a common organism in native or prosthetic valve endocarditis.
• Streptococcus pneumoniae can rarely cause endocarditis but is more associated with pneumonia
or meningitis in this age group.
Timeline of Most Common Organisms in Infective Endocarditis (IE):
Buzz phrase: Early = Staph, Late = Strep
<60 Days Post-Surgery (Early-Onset IE):
• Staphylococcus aureus – Most common,
aggressive, rapid onset
• Coagulase-negative Staph (CoNS) – Common
with prosthetic material
• Gram-negative rods – Nosocomial (e.g.,
Klebsiella, Pseudomonas)
• Candida – Consider in neonates or
immunocompromised patients
>60 Days Post-Surgery or Native Valve IE:
• Viridans streptococci – Oral source (dental),
subacute presentation
• Staphylococcus aureus – Still common,
especially with skin breaches
• Enterococcus spp. – Associated with GI/GU
procedures
• HACEK organisms – Cause culture-negative
IE, slow-growing
Special Situations:
• Congenital heart disease (late onset): Viridans streptococci
• IV drug use: Staphylococcus aureus (often right-sided IE)
• Prosthetic valve IE:
o Early: CoNS, Staphylococcus aureus, Candida
o Late: Viridans streptococci, Enterococcus
(For more details about infective endocarditis, see explanation under Q116)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 881; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd ed., page 514
☆ Infectious Diseases: Infective endocarditis (IE)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 881; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A patient underwent surgical resection of the ileum and cecum. On follow-up, labs are being
evaluated for possible anemia. What is the most likely type of anemia?
explanation:
• Ileum is the site of vitamin B12 absorption (via intrinsic factor-B12 complex).
• Resection of the terminal ileum leads to vitamin B12 deficiency.
• B12 deficiency results in macrocytic (megaloblastic) anemia with elevated MCV.
• Cecum resection does not significantly contribute to nutrient deficiency but often accompanies
ileal surgery in Crohn’s or tumors.
• Microcytic anemia is seen with iron deficiency or chronic blood loss.
• Normocytic anemia is more common in acute blood loss or chronic disease.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 303; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd ed., page 452
☆ Hematology: Macrocytic anemia
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 303; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A child presents with a skin infection followed by signs of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis
(PSGN). Labs are ordered to support the diagnosis. What is the most likely finding?
explanation:
• ASO (antistreptolysin O) titers are typically low or absent after skin infections, unlike pharyngitis.
o Skin strains of Group A Strep don’t produce much streptolysin O, so ASO titers are low.
o Instead, you should test for anti-DNase B antibodies, which are reliably elevated in postskin infection PSGN.
• Anti-DNase B titers are more reliable for skin-associated group A Streptococcus infections.
• PSGN is characterized by low C3 (not high) due to complement consumption.
• The question incorrectly mentions DNase antigen — the test used is anti-DNase B antibody, not
the antigen.
• Therefore, low/normal ASO is the expected finding in PSGN following impetigo or skin infection.
Osama Naga, 3rd ed., page 487: “In PSGN after skin infection, ASO may be negative; anti-DNase B is
more useful.” → If the question options include high anti-DNase antibody, that is the most correct answer.
•
•
•
•
PSGN commonly presents 2 weeks after pharyngitis and 4 weeks after skin infection.
Age: 5-12 years
Next best step: labs → urinalysis
Start furosemide if HTN is present
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 699; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guide, p 487
☆ Nephrology: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 699; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guide, p 487

stem:
A 4-hour-old newborn born at 36 weeks of gestation in a birthing center, to a 26-year-old mother who
had positive screening tests for GBS and received IV ampicillin for 6 hours before delivery. Mother
has no history of membrane rupture and the delivery was NSVD without complications. The
newborn examination was normal and he is clinically and vitally stable. In regard to his mother's GBS
status, what is your next step in management?

options.label: B
options.text: Limited evaluation with observation for 48 hours

options.label: C
options.text: Full septic work up with observation

explanation:
• GBS prophylaxis is considered adequate if IV penicillin/ampicillin is given ≥4 hours before
delivery.
• The neonate is ≥35 weeks, clinically well, and has no risk factors (e.g., prolonged rupture,
chorioamnionitis).
• In such a case, no lab evaluation or antibiotics are indicated.
• CDC and AAP guidelines recommend routine observation for 48 hours in asymptomatic term or
near-term neonates with adequately treated
maternal GBS.
• Limited or full sepsis workup is reserved for
neonates with symptoms or high-risk delivery
parameters.
★ Neonatal Sepsis
• Systemic illness associated with infectious source
• Overall incidence of 1-5 per 1,000 births
o In VLBW infants, rate of incidence of early sepsis is 2% and late sepsis is 25%
• Mortality rate is up to 25% (higher with prematurity)
• Types:
o Early Onset: within 1st 7 d (1st 3 d if hospitalized since birth)
 Risk factors: prematurity, LBW, PPROM, ROM ≥18 hrs, GBS +ve mother, maternal
fever, fetal distress, chorioamnionitis, meconium fluid, maternal or fetal
tachycardia, preterm labor
 Can use early onset sepsis risk score in ≥34 weeks to guide for NICU
admission/empiric Abx
 Most common: GBS (Step agalactiae), E.coli, Listeria, Staph, Strep pneumo,
Enterococcus, Haemophilus
o Late Onset: after 7 d of life
 Risk factors: indwelling catheters, intubation, prematurity, critical illness.
 Most common: CoNS, gram-negative rods (E.coli), Staph aureus, GBS, fungal
• Clinical presentation: temp instability, irritability, lethargy, poor perfusion, feeding intolerance,
respiratory distress, dyspnea (e.g., expiratory grunting), apnea (more common in preterm infants)
o ↓ temp, perfusion, BG, BP = late signs
o pH (metabolic acidosis)
o ↑ RR/HR
o Specific symptoms:
 Neonatal meningitis
• Often no signs of meningism
• Early phase: general symptoms, vomiting
• Late phase: bulging fontanelles, shrill crying, seizures, stupor
 Neonatal pneumonia
• Tachypnea with intercostal/sternal retractions and nasal flaring
• Reduced oxygen saturation with cyanosis
• Diagnosis:
o Laboratory: blood culture ± UA/UCx ± LP
o Other studies CBC, CRP, abdomen Xray, CXR may be indicated depending on clinical
presentation.
• Management:
o Empiric Abx: Ampicillin + Gentamycin, broaden if severe clinical illness, use local
resistance guidelines.
o Modify/stop Abx based on culture data.
• Protective Factors:
o Adequate IAP (≥4 hours IV penicillin or ampicillin)
o
o
o
Term gestation
Asymptomatic neonate
No rupture of membranes or fever
CDC/AAP Key Cutoffs:
• ≥37 wks + adequate IAP + well-appearing → Observe 48 hrs
• ≥35–36 wks + adequate IAP + well-appearing → Observe, but lower threshold to evaluate if
additional risk present
• <35 wks GA or symptomatic at any time → Full workup
EOS risk assessment among infants born ≥35 weeks’ gestation (AAP)
EOS risk assessment among infants born ≤34 weeks’ gestation (AAP)
Board Tip:
• Asymptomatic term or near-term neonate with adequately treated mother → Observe 48 hrs, no labs
or antibiotics
• Symptomatic neonate or inadequate IAP + risk factors → Evaluate and consider empiric antibiotics
📚 Source: Red Book 2021–2024, page 653; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 369; Pocket
Pediatrics, NICU: Sepsis. AAP Article on Management of Infants at Risk for Group B Streptococcal Disease
☆ NICU: GBS, Neonatal sepsis
source: Red Book 2021–2024, page 653; Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 369; Pocket

stem: What suggests an additional congenital anomaly?
explanation:
•
•
•
•
Normally: 2 arteries + 1 vein in the umbilical cord
o SUA = only 1 artery + 1 vein
o Occurs in 0.5–1% of live births
Associated with a higher risk of renal, cardiac, and chromosomal
anomalies, especially when found with other dysmorphic features.
o Most commonly associated anomaly: renal (e.g., agenesis,
hydronephrosis, VUR)
o Other associations: cardiac defects, GI
anomalies, IUGR, VACTERL, trisomy 13,
18, sometimes 21
Isolated SUA (normal anatomy on prenatal US) →
no further workup needed
Non-isolated SUA (other findings or dysmorphism)
→ order renal ultrasound, consider
echocardiogram and genetic testing (esp. if
dysmorphic or IUGR)
Other options:
• Hypospadias can be isolated but may be associated with disorders of sexual development
(DSD) if severe.
• Skin tags are typically benign and isolated.
• Umbilical hernia is common, especially in Black infants, and rarely indicates other anomalies
unless part of a syndrome (e.g., Down syndrome).
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 130; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd ed., page 112
☆ General Pediatrics: Congenital anomalies
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 130; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 2-week-old female infant is brought in for evaluation of poor feeding and lethargy. On examination,
she has a webbed neck, low posterior hairline, and widely spaced nipples. You also note
diminished femoral pulses compared to brachial pulses, and a harsh systolic murmur is heard over
the left upper sternal border. The mother reports a normal vaginal delivery with no complications. A
clinical image is shown on the exam, demonstrating characteristic facial features. What is the most
likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Turner syndrome (45,XO) occurs in phenotypic females and classically presents with:
o Webbed neck
o Lymphedema of hands/feet in neonates
o Low hairline, widely spaced nipples
o Weak or diminished femoral pulses due to coarctation of the aorta
Other options:
• Noonan syndrome can have a webbed neck, but typically in males and without weak pulses
•
•
Trisomy 13 (Patau) presents with midline defects (e.g., cleft lip/palate, holoprosencephaly,
polydactyly) [For more details see explanation under Q4]
Prader-Willi shows hypotonia, poor feeding, and later hyperphagia and obesity, not webbed neck
or weak pulses
o Mnemonic: “H4O” = Hypotonia , Hypogonadism, Hyperphagia, Hands are small, Obesity
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 129; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd ed., page 133
☆ Genetics: Chromosomal anomalies, Turner syndrome
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 129; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem: What is the commonly used standard confidence interval (CI) in medical research and statistics?
explanation:
• A 95% confidence interval is the standard in clinical research and epidemiology.
• It means there is a 95% probability that the interval contains the true population parameter.
• A narrow CI indicates high precision; a wide CI suggests more variability or smaller sample size.
• 5% typically refers to the alpha level (type I error), not the CI.
• 100% CI would be meaningless in practical research as it implies total certainty, which is
unrealistic.
📚 Source: Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide, 3rd ed., page 63
☆ Research & Epidemiology
source: Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide, 3rd ed., page 63

stem:
A school-aged child presents with a bright red rash on the cheeks followed by a reticular, lacy rash
on the trunk and extremities. There is no fever or systemic illness at presentation. What is the most
likely causative organism?
explanation:
• Parvovirus B19 causes erythema infectiosum (fifth disease), characterized by:
o Slapped cheek facial rash
o Followed by a reticular/lacy rash on the trunk and limbs
o Often afebrile by the time rash appears
Other options:
• Coxsackie A virus causes hand-foot-and-mouth disease, with vesicular rash on palms, soles,
and oral ulcers
•
Measles (Rubeola) presents with fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, and a confluent
maculopapular rash starting on the face
•
Rubella causes a milder maculopapular rash with posterior auricular and occipital LAD
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 1035; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guide, p 518
☆ Infectious Diseases: Viral exanthems
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 1035; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guide, p 518

stem:
A 12-hour-old newborn, delivered via vacuum (ventouse) assistance, is noted to be pale with scalp
swelling that extends to the orbital margin. Labs show low hemoglobin. What is the most likely
diagnosis?
explanation:
• Subgaleal hemorrhage is a life-threatening accumulation
of blood between the scalp aponeurosis and periosteum.
• Seen commonly after vacuum-assisted deliveries.
• Key findings:
o Fluctuant scalp swelling that crosses suture lines
o May extend to orbits, neck
o Hypovolemia, pallor, anemia, and shock can result
from blood loss
• Cephalohematoma is subperiosteal → does not cross
sutures, appears hours after birth, no acute anemia
• Caput succedaneum is benign, edematous, crosses sutures, and resolves in hours
• Congenital anomaly is incorrect — this is a birth trauma complication, not a malformation
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 331; Osama Naga Pediatric Study Guide, page 96
☆ NICU/Neonatology: Birth trauma, Cranial hematomas
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 331; Osama Naga Pediatric Study Guide, page 96

stem:
What is a common clinical finding that may be seen in both rheumatic fever and infective
endocarditis?
explanation:
• Gallop rhythm (S3 or S4) indicates myocardial dysfunction and can occur in both conditions due
to carditis or valvular involvement.
• Petechiae and Osler nodes are classic for infective endocarditis, not rheumatic fever.
• Erythema marginatum is specific to rheumatic fever, not seen in IE.
• Therefore, gallop rhythm is the shared finding indicating myocardial stress or inflammation.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., pages 878–881; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study
Guide, 3rd ed., pages 510–514
☆ Cardiology: Auscultation findings
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., pages 878–881; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study

stem:
An 18-month-old girl presents with a history of febrile seizure. You are counseling the parents about
risk factors for recurrence and febrile seizure susceptibility. Which of the following is a known risk
factor for febrile seizures?
explanation:
• A febrile seizure is a generally benign neurologic condition characterized by seizure and elevated
temperature (≥38 C [100.4 F]) without signs of CNS infection (eg, nuchal rigidity), metabolic
abnormalities, or a history of afebrile seizures.
• Children age 6 months to 5 years are almost exclusively affected because seizure threshold is
lower during early years of brain development.
o Age between 6 months and 5 years is the strongest risk factor for febrile seizures, peaking
at 18 months.
• In addition, young children are particularly susceptible to febrile viral illnesses (eg, roseola,
influenza) associated with high fever (≥39.5 C
[103 F]), a key risk factor for development of
seizures due to hyperthermia-induced
neuronal dysfunction.
Classification:
• Simple: Single, <15 min, nonfocal or tonicclonic and does not recur within 24 hours.
• Complex: Recurrent (>1 in 24 hr), >15 min,
focal, occur outside typical age range
Risk factors for recurrence:
Recurrence rate is higher in the first 2 years!
The risk of recurrent, simple febrile seizures is 33%.
• age < 18 months
• fever < 1 hour before seizure
• FHx in first degree relative
• temp lower than <40ºC
o seizures developed at low grade fever 38
• hyponatremia or hypocalcaemia at time of presentation
Risk factors for epilepsy in febrile seizures:
1–2% risk of epilepsy (0.5–1% in gen pop)
• complex febrile seizures
• FHx of epilepsy
• abnormal development history
• ∼ 1–2% in children who have had a simple febrile
seizure
• 5–10% in children with complex febrile seizures or
a family history of epilepsy
Red flags → may be syndromic, heat sensitive epilepsy
• hemiclonic seizures → Dravet syndrome; mutation in sodium channel 1A; sensitive to any increase in temperature (hot
weather, hot bath), photic stimulation → worsened with any sodium channel blockers
• prolonged seizures
• family history of seizures
• afebrile seizures
Management:
• Simple → control fever, monitor; no workup needed
• Complex → consider neuro consult; may require imaging + EEG (possibly as OP)
•
•
•
•
If seizing 5 minutes or more → abortive therapy = Diazepam
If < 5 minutes → Paracetamol
If non seizing in ER → Paracetamol, regardless of the duration seized at home
Note: Rectal diazepam or nasal midazolam might be prescribed to be used as needed for children
who are prone to long febrile seizures.
o These medications are typically used to treat seizures that last longer than five minutes or if
the child has more than one seizure within 24 hours.
Indications for LP (Pediatrics 2011;127(2):389)
• Meningeal signs or ill-appearing, <1 yo, and unvaccinated or s/p recent antibiotic treatment
• Consider in <6 mo since true febrile seizures are rare at that age
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 454; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd ed., page 286; Pocket Pediatrics
☆ Emergency Medicine: Neurologic emergencies, Febrile seizure
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 454; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A patient was treated with Cefaclor, and after 5–7 days developed allergic reaction symptoms such
as rash, fever, and arthralgia. What is the most likely type of hypersensitivity reaction?
explanation:
• Type III hypersensitivity involves immune complex deposition, leading to serum sickness-like
reaction (SSLR).
• Cefaclor is a classic trigger, especially in children, and symptoms typically occur 5–7 days after
drug initiation.
• Symptoms: fever, urticaria or morbilliform rash, lymphadenopathy, arthralgia/arthritis
Other options:
• Type I is immediate (minutes to hours) → IgEmediated anaphylaxis, urticaria
• Type II is antibody-mediated cytotoxicity (e.g.,
hemolytic anemia)
• Type IV is delayed T-cell mediated (e.g., contact
dermatitis, DRESS)
Type III HSR
mechanism:
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed.,
page 558; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd ed., page 543
