stem:
A presents with a persistent rash over the extensor surfaces of the elbows and knees. He was
initially diagnosed with atopic dermatitis and has been treated with high-potency topical
corticosteroids for 2 weeks, but there has been minimal improvement. The lesions appear welldemarcated, thickened, and scaly, and the child reports no significant itching. What is the most
likely reason for treatment failure?
explanation:
• Atopic dermatitis typically affects the flexor surfaces (e.g., antecubital and popliteal fossae) in
older children.
• Extensor distribution may suggest a misdiagnosis — consider psoriasis, scabies, or contact
dermatitis.
• Infected eczema (e.g., with S. aureus) may cause flares but usually shows oozing, crusting, or
pustules.
• Non-compliance is possible, but with proper high-potency steroid use and no improvement,
misdiagnosis is more likely.
• Steroid resistance is rare in clinical practice.
Sample scenario (A): A 5-year-old child presents for follow-up of atopic dermatitis that has worsened over
the past 2 weeks. The rash initially involved the extensor surfaces of the elbows and knees and was being
treated with appropriate use of high-potency topical corticosteroids. Despite this, the lesions remain
erythematous, crusted, and oozing, and the child is frequently scratching. The mother is concerned that
the treatment is not working. What is the most likely reason for treatment failure? → secondary infection,
MC Staphylococcus aureus, leading to impetiginization
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 1001; Osama Naga Pediatric Study Guide, p 537
☆ Dermatology: Atopic dermatitis (eczema)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 1001; Osama Naga Pediatric Study Guide, p 537

stem:
A newborn delivered via cesarean section due to abruptio placentae has Apgar scores of 2 and 5 at
1 and 5 minutes. No additional data (e.g., aEEG or MRI) are provided. Which of the following is the least
reliable predictor of long-term neurological outcome?
explanation:
• Apgar score is a poor predictor of long-term neurological outcomes. It reflects immediate
postnatal adaptation, not brain injury severity.
• aEEG (amplitude-integrated EEG) is an early and reliable predictor of hypoxic-ischemic
encephalopathy (HIE) severity and outcomes.
• Neurological examination in the first 24–48 hours helps stratify HIE and correlates with prognosis.
• MRI of the brain (especially after 5–7 days) is a strong predictor of long-term neurologic disability.
📚 Source: Fanaroff and Martin’s Neonatal-Perinatal Medicine, 11th ed., page 1197; Osama Naga Pediatric
Board Study Guide, 3rd ed., page 99
☆ NICU/Neonatology: Predictors of neonatal neurologic outcome
source: Fanaroff and Martin’s Neonatal-Perinatal Medicine, 11th ed., page 1197; Osama Naga Pediatric

stem:
A known case of ventricular septal defect (VSD) presents with heart failure and pulmonary edema.
On exam, the patient is pale, has splenomegaly, clubbing, and non-tender lesions on the palms and
soles. There is no cyanosis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Bacterial endocarditis (infective endocarditis) is a known complication in children with congenital
heart disease, especially VSD.
• Non-tender lesions on palms/soles = Janeway lesions, classic for infective endocarditis.
•
•
•
Splenomegaly, pallor (anemia), and clubbing suggest chronic infection and embolic
phenomena.
Eisenmenger syndrome would present with cyanosis due to reversal of shunt (left to right → right to
left), which is not present here.
Rheumatic fever presents with migratory arthritis, carditis, erythema marginatum, and nodules,
but not Janeway lesions or clubbing.
(For more details about infective endocarditis, see explanation under Q116)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 881; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd ed., page 514
☆ Infectious Diseases: Infective endocarditis (IE)
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 881; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A 7-year-old child presents with tachycardia, capillary refill of 4 seconds, blood pressure of 94/60
mmHg, and is febrile on examination. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Minimum SBP in this case is 70 + age x2 = 70 + 14 = 84 mmHg
• Compensated shock:
o Signs: tachycardia, prolonged capillary refill (>2 sec), cool extremities, but normal BP
o This child has a low-normal BP, suggesting the body is maintaining perfusion by
compensatory mechanisms
• Severe sepsis requires organ dysfunction (e.g., altered mental status, renal failure, coagulopathy),
which is not described here
• Bacterial sepsis is a broader term and possible, but shock physiology with normal BP makes
compensated shock the more precise choice
• Key in board questions: evidence of shock (poor perfusion) with normal BP → compensated
★ Minimum Systolic BP Formula (Hypotension cutoff):
Minimum SBP = 70 + (2 × age in years)
(for ages 1–10 years)
• Example:
o 6-year-old → 70 + (2 × 6) = 82 mmHg
o SBP <82 mmHg is hypotensive
In patients >10 yrs hypotension is SBP <90 mmHg.
PALS High-Yield Tips:
• Always correlate BP with capillary refill, mental status, and peripheral perfusion.
• Normal BP does not exclude shock — especially compensated shock, where perfusion is
impaired but BP is maintained.
• MAP (Mean Arterial Pressure) targets:
o Neonates: ≥45 mmHg
o Older children: ≥55–60 mmHg
Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP):
MAP reflects average arterial pressure during a single cardiac cycle; best indicator of organ
perfusion
• Formulas:
o MAP = Diastolic BP + 1/3 (Systolic BP - Diastolic BP), Equivalent: MAP = (SBP + 2 × DBP) / 3
• Clinical Targets:
o Neonates: MAP ≥ gestational age in weeks (e.g., 30 weeks → MAP ≥ 30 mmHg)
o Infants/Children: MAP ≥ 55–60 mmHg
• MAP is superior to systolic BP for assessing perfusion of brain, kidneys, and heart
•
Guides resuscitation, vasopressors, and fluid management in shock and critical illness
Example: BP 90/60 → MAP = 60 + 1/3(30) = 70 mmHg
o
•
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 129; Osama Naga Pediatric Study Guide, p 480
☆ PICU: Shock
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 129; Osama Naga Pediatric Study Guide, p 480

stem:
A 5 years old boy with speech delay, but other developmental domains were normal, and
unremarkable family history, this boy most likely has?
explanation:
• Isolated speech delay with otherwise normal development is most commonly due to hearing
impairment.
• Children with deafness may appear to develop normally in motor, cognitive, and social areas but
struggle with language acquisition.
• ADHD presents with inattention, hyperactivity, not isolated language delay.
• Cerebral palsy affects motor function and would show abnormal milestones or tone, not isolated
speech delay.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 75; Osama Naga Pediatric Study Guide, p 245
☆ Developmental & Behavioural: Isolated speech delay
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 75; Osama Naga Pediatric Study Guide, p 245

stem:
A 9-year-old child presents with vomiting, crampy abdominal pain, fever, and palpable purpura
localized to the lower extremities. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• HSP (IgA vasculitis) is the most common vasculitis in children and presents with:
o Palpable purpura (typically on lower extremities)
o Crampy abdominal pain, vomiting
o May have fever, arthritis, and renal involvement
• Meningococcemia presents with toxic appearance, high fever, shock, and diffuse
petechial/purpuric rash, not isolated to the legs
• ITP presents with isolated thrombocytopenia, petechiae/purpura without fever or systemic
symptoms
(For more details see explanation under Q11)
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 735; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,
3rd ed., page 471
☆ Rheumatology: Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP), IgA Vasculitis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 735; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study Guide,

stem:
A young child presents with a 2–3 month history of recurrent head tilting episodes. The child is
asymptomatic between episodes. On examination, there is a head tilt to the left, but no asymmetry
of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, and neurologic exam is normal.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Paroxysmal torticollis of infancy is a benign, self-limited
condition presenting with recurrent episodes of head tilting in infants
or toddlers.
• Episodes last hours to days, and the child is well between attacks.
• There is no fixed muscular shortening or SCM asymmetry, unlike
congenital muscular torticollis, which shows persistent tilt and
palpable SCM tightness.
• Posterior fossa tumors can cause torticollis but are usually associated with neurologic signs (e.g.,
vomiting, ataxia).
• Benign paroxysmal vertigo causes brief vertigo episodes but not persistent head tilt.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 248; Osama Naga Pediatric Study Guide, p 438
☆ Neurology: Paroxysmal torticollis of infancy
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 248; Osama Naga Pediatric Study Guide, p 438

stem: At what age should we stop adjusting for prematurity?
explanation:
• In preterm infants, developmental milestones are adjusted using corrected age (chronologic age
minus weeks of prematurity).
• Correction is applied until 24 months of age for most domains (motor, language, cognitive).
• After 2 years, assessments are based on chronological age alone, as most children have caught up
developmentally.
• Growth percentiles may be adjusted slightly longer in some cases, but not milestone evaluation.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., page 68; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Guide, p 232
☆ General Pediatrics/Neonatology: Age correction for prematurity

Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome is a clinical entity of dyspnea, cyanosis
resistant to supplemental oxygen, and bilateral chest infiltrates on chest
radiography.
Pediatric ARDS, and its severity, is defined by (1) timing: onset within 7 days of
known clinical insult; (2) respiratory failure not fully explained by cardiac failure or
fluid overload; (3) chest imaging findings of new infiltrate(s) consistent with acute
pulmonary parenchymal disease; and (4) impairment in oxygenation

stem: Vaccine with Short live induced immunity :
explanation:
PPSV23 (Polysaccharide Vaccine):
Contains pure polysaccharides
T-cell independent response
No immune memory
Immunity is short-lived
Less effective in children <2 years
PCV13 (Conjugate Vaccine):
• Contains pneumococcal polysaccharides
linked to a protein carrier
• T-cell dependent immune response
• Produces immune memory
• Effective in infants and young children
• Leads to longer-lasting immunity

Acute cerebellar ataxia:
• Relatively common in children, often in second decade of life
• Most commonly associated with viral illness (varicella, EBV...)
• Thought to be due to molecular mimicry
- Clinical features
• Rapid onset of ataxia, over hours to days

Prognosis (self-limiting ) May take several weeks or months for complete resolution of symptoms

stem: Which plasma amino acids are elevated in maple syrup urine disease (MSUD)
answer: A. High valine, isoleucine and leucine.
explanation:
Maple syrup urine disease (MSUD) is a rare disease and
an autosomal recessive
It is characterized by deficiency of an enzyme
(branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase) which
is required to metabolize the three branched-chain
amino acids (leucine, isoleucine and valine)
MSUD presentation (brain damage):
1. non-specific neurological dysfunction (include
lethargy, irritability and poor feeding) followed by focal
neurological (seizure, spasticity) then sever (coma)
2. characteristic odor of maple syrup is detected in the
urine (usually within 1-2 day after birth)
- Management: dietary restriction

stem:
6weeks old boy with dirrhea start at age of 1 month Mother changed formula to
soya milk because of colic. He still having diarrhea, abdominal distention and skin
rash in form of Eezema. Positive occult blood in stool
What is the dx:
What is the most common site of osteomylites in neonate?
explanation:
●
Osteomyelitis is a pyogenic infection of the bone.
The pathogenesis of the disease is similar to
septic arthritis, with the origin of infection
occurring from hematogenous spread, direct
extension of a local infection, or direct inoculation
of bone either from trauma (e. g. , puncture
wound or open fracture) or surgical manipulation.
In children, the most frequent presentation is
acute hematogenous spread. The most
common location of osteomyelitis is the
metaphysis of the distal femur. The most
prevalent pathogens are the same as those seen
in septic arthritis. Staphylococcus aureus is the
most common pathogen, with group A betahemolytic streptococci a distant second.
Neonates are at risk for group B beta-hemolytic
streptococci.

stem:
You are evaluating a 3-year-old non-verbal boy with autism spectrum disorder who was
brought to the emergency department after a fall. His mother reports that he sometimes
expresses pain through gestures, but his facial expressions and crying are not reliable
indicators of pain. What is the most appropriate pain assessment tool to use in this situation?

options.label: C
options.text: Ask the mother to rate pain severity

explanation:
• The FLACC scale (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability) is validated for:
o Non-verbal or developmentally delayed children
o Age 2 months–7 years
o Situations where facial expression and crying are unreliable
• It uses observable behaviors (including gestures and activity) to estimate pain severity in
a structured way.
• Other options:
o FACES scale: Requires child participation to select a face, which is not reliable here.
o Parent input alone is not an objective assessment tool.
o Numeric scale: Only for verbal children ≥7 years.

Clinical pearls:
• FLACC scale is the preferred validated tool for assessing pain in nonverbal or cognitively
impaired children.




Note: You are not required to memorize the scoring exactly, just know which is used for different
patients.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 82–83
topic: Pain Assessment in Nonverbal Pediatric Patients

stem:
A 6-year-old child presents to the emergency department in severe status asthmaticus that
persists despite initial management with high-dose nebulized salbutamol, ipratropium
bromide, systemic corticosteroids, and supplemental oxygen. The child continues to
have severe respiratory distress with marked accessory muscle use. An arterial blood gas
shows pH 7.28, pCO2 35 mm Hg, HCO3 26 mEq/L. The PICU has been contacted for further
management. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

4

options.label: B
options.text: Proceed to intubation and mechanical ventilation

explanation:
• Interpretation of the ABG:
o pH is acidemic (7.28).
o Normal CO2 (35) is concerning in severe asthma—children are usually hypocapnic
from hyperventilation. A normalizing CO2 suggests impending fatigue and
respiratory failure.
• Escalation of pharmacologic therapy before intubation includes:
o IV magnesium sulfate (if available), or IV aminophylline infusion.
o Among these options, aminophylline is an appropriate next adjunct.
• Asthma indications for intubation:
o Hypoxemia despite provision of high concentrations of oxygen or non-invasive
positive pressure ventilation (NPPV; partial pressure of oxygen [pO2] <60 on 100
percent oxygen or NPPV)
o Severe and unremitting increased work of breathing (e.g., inability to speak)
o Altered mental status
o Respiratory or cardiac arrest
o Note: Hypercarbia alone is not an indication for intubation.
§ However, intubation is warranted if a patient demonstrates a progressively
rising arterial partial pressure of carbon dioxide (PaCO2) despite maximal
medical therapy and/or NPPV and if hypercarbia is causing significant
respiratory acidosis or altered mental status.
Other options:
• Further increasing salbutamol frequency can help but often is insufficient alone at this
stage.
• Intubation is reserved for deterioration, altered consciousness, rising CO2 >45–50, or
apnea.
• Hypertonic saline is not used in asthma management.

Clinical pearls:
• IV aminophylline is a second-line bronchodilator when inhaled therapy fails.
• Watch for toxicity: vomiting, arrhythmias, seizures.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 730–732; UpToDate

stem:
A 6-year-old boy with severe status asthmaticus presents to the emergency department with
progressive respiratory distress, decreased air entry, and worsening hypoxemia despite
maximal medical therapy (inhaled beta-agonists, intravenous corticosteroids, magnesium
sulfate). The team is preparing for rapid sequence intubation (RSI). Which of the following
medication regimens is most appropriate this scenario?
explanation:
• Ketamine is preferred in severe asthma because:
o Provides sedation and dissociation
o Is a potent bronchodilator
o Preserves airway reflexes and spontaneous respiration (at sub-dissociative doses)
• Rocuronium provides rapid paralysis without histamine release (unlike succinylcholine or
atracurium).
• Midazolam can be added for additional sedation/amnesia.

Other options:
• Fentanyl is often avoided in severe asthma due to risk of chest wall rigidity and
respiratory depression.
• Atropine is not routinely needed unless there is high vagal tone or risk of bradycardia (more
relevant in neonates).
• Etomidate: Neutral hemodynamics but no bronchodilation benefit; succinylcholine can
cause hyperkalemia and histamine release.
• Propofol: Can cause profound hypotension and has no bronchodilation effect.

Clinical pearls:
• Ketamine: Best induction agent in severe asthma because it bronchodilates.
→ Remember: Ketamine ‫ﻟﻠﻤﻜﺘﻮﻣﯿﻦ‬
• Rocuronium: Best paralytic because it does NOT release histamine (unlike
succinylcholine or atracurium).
• Midazolam: Adds sedation/amnesia if needed.
• Fentanyl: Avoid if possible in status asthmaticus because of chest wall rigidity.
• Atropine: Generally unnecessary in this age group, used in neonates due to bradycardia
risk.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1033–1034; Pocket Pediatrics 2024; PALS

stem:
A full-term newborn is evaluated in the nursery. On examination, the baby appears healthy, with
no dysmorphic features or systemic abnormalities. Growth charts show that both weight and
length are below the 3rd percentile. A growth chart (likely representing the father) is attached.
No other abnormalities are noted on exam.




What is the most likely inheritance pattern of this condition?
explanation:
• Familial short stature is:
o Normal growth velocity along a low percentile
o No dysmorphism or chronic disease
o Predictable adult height based on parental mid-parental height
o Autosomal dominant inheritance is the most common pattern (tends to run in
families)
• Other options:
o X-linked inheritance is not typical for familial short stature.
o Multifactorial patterns more commonly describe conditions like obesity or mild
developmental delays.
o Autosomal recessive inheritance is seen in many syndromic or metabolic disorders,
not simple familial short stature.

Clinical pearls:
• Differentiate familial short stature (normal bone age) vs. constitutional growth
delay (delayed bone age, delayed puberty).
• Consistently low percentile + normal development = normal variant.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 65–66

stem:
A 6-week-old infant is brought to clinic for evaluation of a persistent facial rash. The mother
has Sjögren syndrome and is known to be positive for anti-Ro (SSA) antibodies. Physical
exam finding is shown below. There is no fever or systemic illness, and the remainder of the
exam is unremarkable.



8
In regards to the rash, what is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• Neonatal lupus results from transplacental passage of maternal anti-Ro/SSA
antibodies.
• The rash is self-limited, resolving spontaneously by 6 months as maternal antibodies
disappear.
• Mild transaminitis also resolves without intervention.
• Treatment: Supportive care, sun protection, reassurance.
o Topical steroids can be used for cosmetic improvement if desired but are not
essential.
o Systemic steroids are reserved for cardiac involvement (e.g., congenital heart
block).
• IVIG is not indicated.

★ Neonatal Lupus:
• Cause: Transplacental passage of maternal anti-Ro (SSA) or anti-La (SSB) antibodies
• Most common serious complication: Congenital complete heart block (CCHB)
• ECG: Complete AV block (bradycardia, AV dissociation)
• CCHB is irreversible → majority require permanent pacemaker if HR <55 bpm or
symptomatic
• Cutaneous form: Annular erythematous rash (face, scalp), appears in first few weeks
→ resolves by 6 months




•   Other findings: Transient thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, anemia, elevated LFTs
•   Not caused by anti-dsDNA (common board trap)
•   Detected antenatally via fetal ECHO starting at 18–20 weeks if mother is Ab-positive
•   Management:
o CCHB: Monitor HR, pace if symptomatic
o Skin and blood findings: Supportive care only
•   Recurrence risk: ~15–20% in future pregnancies if mother is anti-Ro/La positive

9
Clinical pearls:
• Always screen for congenital heart block (EKG).
• Rash itself does not require systemic therapy.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 725–726

stem:
A full-term newborn is evaluated shortly after birth for diffuse petechiae and bruising.
Laboratory studies show:
- Platelet count: 2,000/mm³
- Maternal platelet count: 370,000/mm³
The infant appears well otherwise, with no dysmorphic features.
What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Transfusion of washed, irradiated maternal platelets

answer: A. Transfusion of washed, irradiated maternal platelets
explanation:
• This is classic neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia (NAIT):
o It is analogous to Rh disease but affecting platelet antigens. Maternal antibodies
(most commonly against HPA-1a) cross the placenta and destroy fetal platelets.
o Maternal platelets are normal because the antigens are paternally derived.
• Severe thrombocytopenia (<30,000) requires urgent platelet transfusion.
o If <30k, transfuse regardless of symptoms because of high risk of spontaneous
intracranial hemorrhage.
o This is especially urgent if <10,000.
• Washed, irradiated maternal platelets are preferred because they lack the antigen
targeted by maternal antibodies.
• Other options:
o IVIG can be used as adjunctive therapy but is not the first step in severe bleeding
risk.
o Steroids have no role acutely.
o Observation is inappropriate with platelets at 2,000 and risk of intracranial
hemorrhage.

Clinical pearls:
• NAIT resembles Rh hemolytic disease but affects platelets.
• Always consider urgent head ultrasound to assess for bleeding.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 771–772

stem:
A 3-year-old boy presents with 5 days of high fever, bilateral conjunctival injection,
erythematous cracked lips, diffuse polymorphous rash, and swelling of hands and feet.
Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and CRP. Echocardiogram shows no coronary
aneurysms. What is the most appropriate initial treatment for this condition?
explanation:
Acute Kawasaki Disease Treatment:
• IVIG single dose 2 g/kg over 10–12 hours.
• High-dose aspirin (80–100 mg/kg/day divided QID) in the acute phase for anti-
inflammatory effect.
• After fever resolves, aspirin is reduced to low-dose (3–5 mg/kg/day) for 6–8 weeks for
antiplatelet effect.

★ Kawasaki Disease:
• Kawasaki disease is an acute, self-limited medium-vessel vasculitis primarily
affecting children <5 years. It is the leading cause of acquired heart disease in children in
developed countries.




•   Diagnostic Criteria (Classic Kawasaki Disease): “CRASH & Burn”
o Fever ≥5 days PLUS ≥4 of the following 5 (no other explanation):
o Conjunctivitis – bilateral, non-purulent
o Rash (polymorphous) – non-vesicular, widespread
o Adenopathy (cervical lymphadenopathy) – usually unilateral, >1.5 cm
o Strawberry tongue – oropharyngeal mucositis + red/cracked lips
o Hand and foot swelling – edema, erythema, periungual desquamation
•   Incomplete Kawasaki:
o Fever ≥5 days plus 2–3 criteria
o Support with labs (↑CRP/ESR, leukocytosis, sterile pyuria,
thrombocytosis) and echocardiographic findings
•   Phases
1. Acute Phase (Days 1–10): High fever, diagnostic features
present
2. Subacute Phase (Days 11–25): Desquamation,
thrombocytosis, coronary aneurysm risk highest
3. Convalescent Phase (Weeks 4–8): Resolution of clinical
features, normalization of labs
•   Cardiac Complications:
o Coronary artery aneurysms (most feared)
o Myocarditis, pericardial effusion, valvular disease
o Risk highest in untreated children or those treated late
•   Management:
11
o IVIG 2 g/kg IV over 10–12 hours – early administration of IVIG within the first 10 days
of illness significantly reduces risk of coronary artery aneurysms from ~25% to
<5%.
o Aspirin:
§ High dose: 80–100 mg/kg/day (anti-inflammatory during acute phase)
§ Low dose: 3–5 mg/kg/day (antiplatelet after fever resolves)
o Echocardiography:
§ At diagnosis, 2–3 weeks, and 6–8 weeks
o Steroids:
§ Consider in high-risk patients or IVIG-resistant cases

Clinical Pearls:
• Desquamation (hands/feet) usually starts in subacute phase (10–14 days)
• Labs: ↑CRP/ESR, thrombocytosis (subacute), leukocytosis, sterile pyuria
• Early recognition and IVIG administration within 10 days (ideally <7 days) of fever onset is
critical to reduce coronary artery aneurysm risk from ~25% to <5%.
• Steroids are not first-line unless high-risk or IVIG-resistant cases.
o Children with persistent fever >36 hrs post-IVIG → suspect resistant Kawasaki
• Empiric antibiotics are not indicated without evidence of bacterial infection.
• Always follow with baseline echocardiography and aspirin after IVIG.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 572–573; Osama Naga Pediatric Board Study

stem:
A full-term neonate is born to a mother who received no prenatal care. On examination at
delivery, the infant has the finding shown below. The bowel appears matted and edematous.




What is the most typical anatomical location of this defect?

options.label: C
options.text: To the right of the umbilicus

options.label: D
options.text: To the left of the umbilicus

answer: C. To the right of the umbilicus
explanation:
• This is a case of gastroschisis:
o Right paraumbilical abdominal wall defect.
o Herniation of bowel without peritoneal sac.
o Often associated with bowel inflammation from amniotic fluid exposure.
• Omphalocele is different:
o Herniation through the umbilicus and covered by a peritoneal sac.




12
Clinical pearls:
• Gastroschisis = right of cord, no sac.
• Omphalocele = through cord, covered sac, associated anomalies.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 679–680

stem:
A 7-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with severe right ear pain and purulent
discharge for 2 days. His mother reports that he cries when she touches his ear. On
examination, there is marked tenderness when the tragus is manipulated, swelling and
erythema of the external auditory canal with visible purulent debris, and tender
preauricular lymphadenopathy. The tympanic membrane could not be visualized due to canal
edema. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Otitis externa (swimmer’s ear):
o Severe pain with tragal or pinna manipulation (classic sign).
o Edematous, erythematous canal with debris.
o Purulent otorrhea.
o TM often not visible due to canal swelling.
• Other options:
o A. Acute otitis media: TM bulging visualized, no tragal tenderness, pain more deep.
o B. Chronic otitis media: Chronic drainage >6 weeks, perforation, less acute pain.
o D. Mastoiditis: Postauricular swelling, ear pushed forward, systemic toxicity.

Clinical pearls:
• Tragus tenderness = otitis externa.
• If canal completely obstructed, consider wick placement and topical antibiotics.




13
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1070–1071

stem:
A term newborn is evaluated in the nursery. The mother had varicella (chickenpox) infection
approximately 1 month before delivery. On examination, the infant appears well, with no skin
lesions or systemic symptoms. Which of the following eye findings is most likely to be present
in this neonate?
explanation:
• Congenital varicella syndrome results from maternal infection in the first or early second
trimester (<20 weeks). Classic features:
o Cicatricial skin scarring (dermatomal)
o Limb hypoplasia
o Chorioretinitis and cataracts
o Neurologic defects (e.g., cortical
atrophy, microcephaly)
• Infection in late pregnancy (~1 month before
delivery) rarely causes congenital syndrome
but is the period most often linked
to chorioretinitis as the ocular finding.

Other options:
• Aniridia: Associated with Wilms tumor, not varicella.
• Conjunctivitis: Non-specific, not a classic congenital varicella manifestation.
• Horner syndrome: Sympathetic chain injury, unrelated to varicella.

Clinical pearls:
• Infection within 5 days before or 2 days after delivery risks neonatal varicella with
severe disseminated disease, not congenital varicella syndrome.
• Chorioretinitis is a classic eye finding in congenital varicella.

stem:
A 10-year-old girl with known type 1 diabetes mellitus was admitted to the PICU with diabetic
ketoacidosis (DKA). She received intravenous fluids and insulin infusion. Twelve hours after
admission, her nurse reports that she is complaining of a severe headache and is becoming
progressively drowsy. Her capillary blood glucose is currently 20 mmol/L. What is the most
likely cause of her deterioration?
explanation:
• Cerebral edema is the most serious complication of DKA treatment.
o Occurs in 0.5–1% of DKA episodes.
o Typically presents within 4–12 hours of initiating treatment.
o Early signs: headache, lethargy, irritability.
o Progresses to altered consciousness, seizures, Cushing reflex.
• Other options:
o Hypoglycemia unlikely with glucose still >200 mg/dL.
o Hypokalemia can cause arrhythmias and weakness but does not explain headache
and altered mental status.
o Acidosis improves with treatment and does not typically worsen neurologic function
in this way.

Clinical pearls:
• Most cases of DKA-related cerebral edema occur 4–12 hours after therapy begins.
• Early signs include headache, irritability, vomiting, and altered mental status.
• Risk factors: Severe acidosis at presentation, Substantially elevated BUN at presentation,
Severe hypocapnia
o Young child (<5 years) and/or new onset of diabetes are not independent risk factors
but are markers for more severe DKA because they are associated with delayed
diagnosis
• Rapid fluid administration or bicarbonate use may increase risk.
• Suspected cerebral edema should be treated empirically with hypertonic saline
(3%) or mannitol, without delay.
• Immediate management: Reduce IV fluids, give mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV, elevate head of
bed, consider hypertonic saline.
• Monitor for early neurologic signs in all children on DKA protocols.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 711–712

stem:
A 6-year-old boy is brought to clinic. His mother reports that he has been irritable, particularly
at night. On examination, there is mild erythema around the anus but no significant excoriation.
A microscopic image of the organism found is shown below:




Which of the following symptoms is most likely to be present in this child?
explanation:
This is a case of Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm) infection:
• Most common symptom: intense nighttime perianal itching due to gravid female worms
migrating to lay eggs.
• May also have disrupted sleep and irritability.

Other options:
• Iron deficiency anemia is associated with hookworm infection (Ancylostoma, Necator).
• Diarrhea with mucus is more characteristic of Giardia or Entamoeba.
• Hepatosplenomegaly does not occur with Enterobius.

stem:
A family brings their young son to the clinic with complaints of increased irritability
and perianal itching, especially at night. They report seeing small white worms emerging
from his anus. What is the most appropriate diagnostic test?
explanation:
• This presentation is typical of Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm) infection
• Classic signs: perianal itching, nighttime irritability, and visible white worms near anus
• The tape test is the diagnostic gold standard: apply clear adhesive tape to the perianal
region in the early morning before bathing or defecation
• Ova & parasite stool tests are not useful — pinworm eggs are deposited perianally, not
passed in stool
• Stool culture and rectal swab culture are irrelevant — used for bacterial pathogens, not
helminths

★ Pinworm infection (Enterobius vermicularis)
• Epidemiology
o Most common helminthic infection in the U.S.
o Prevalence in the US: ∼ 12%
o Primarily affects children 5–10 years of age
• Mode of transmission
o Initial infection: fecal-oral
o Reinfection: digital-oral after scratching anal region
• Clinical features
o Anal pruritus (especially at night)
o Vulvovaginitis, especially in children
o Occasionally, symptoms of intestinal infection (i.e.,
nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain which may
become severe enough to mimic appendicitis)
• Diagnosis
o Tape test: microscopic detection of oval eggs (ova)
and/or pinworms on tape that has been pressed
against the perianal region
o Can be an incidental finding on endoscopy
21
•   Treatment: bendazoles OR pyrantel pamoate
o Treat with mebendazole 100 mg PO, repeat after 2 weeks
§ Alternatives: albendazole 400 mg, or pyrantel pamoate
o Treat all close contacts to prevent reinfection
o Emphasize strict hand hygiene, daily underwear changes, and washing of linens
and bedding
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 145

stem:
A preterm neonate born at 29 weeks gestation is on day 3 of life. Cranial ultrasound shows
intraventricular hemorrhage (IVH) with mild ventricular dilatation. The baby is clinically
stable, tolerating feeds, and has no signs of increased intracranial pressure.
What is the preferred next step in imaging to monitor this condition?
explanation:
• IVH grading and evolution are monitored serially by cranial ultrasound.
o Safe, portable, no radiation.
o Allows assessment of ventricular size progression or resolution.
o Typically repeated 1–2 weeks later to monitor for post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus.
• Cranial Ultrasound is the gold standard for screening and diagnosis of IVH.




25
•   Routine screening for all neonates <30 weeks gestation or <1500g birth weight, and
neonates with perinatal risk factors, including severe respiratory distress, hypotension,
metabolic acidosis, coagulopathy, or signs of neurological instability.
o First CUS between 7–14 days of life → detects early IVH, which typically occurs
within the first 72 hours of life.
§ A single scan at the end of the first week shows 90% of all hemorrhages as
well as their maximum extent.
o Follow-up at 4–6 weeks → to detect periventricular leukomalacia.
o Additional weekly scans if IVH is present to monitor progression.
§ For mild IVH (Grade I or II): Repeat ultrasound at 36–40 weeks
postmenstrual age to monitor resolution.
§ For severe IVH (Grade III or IV):
§ Weekly or biweekly ultrasound to assess for progressive
hydrocephalus.
§ If ventricular dilation progresses, consider MRI at term-equivalent
age.
o Further ultrasound examinations (up to two to three times weekly) are required in
infants with any degree of hemorrhage to detect the occurrence of posthemorrhagic
ventricular dilation (PHVD), which occurs in approximately 30%, and the
occurrence of associated PVL.
•   Other options:
o CT scan is not routinely needed; higher radiation and requires transport.
o Lumbar puncture is not indicated unless infection or high-pressure hydrocephalus is
suspected.
o EEG is used for seizure evaluation, not IVH monitoring.




Clinical pearls:
• Most IVH occurs within first 72 hours in preterm infants.
• Serial head ultrasounds are the standard of care for follow-up.
• Antenatal steroids (betamethasone or dexamethasone) significantly reduce IVH risk by
enhancing vascular stability.
source: Fanaroff and Martin’s Neonatal-Perinatal Medicine, 11th ed., p. 1742–1744;

stem:
2-week-old preterm infant with a history of necrotizing enterocolitis requiring extensive
bowel resection now has short bowel syndrome. The baby is currently receiving partial
parenteral nutrition. On examination, he appears wasted, with dry, flaky skin and a poorly
healing abdominal incision. Laboratory tests show thrombocytopenia. Which of the following
deficiencies most likely explains these findings?
explanation:
• Essential fatty acid deficiency (EFAD) can develop within 1–2
weeks in infants on parenteral nutrition without adequate lipid
supplementation.
• Characteristic findings:
o Dry, scaly dermatitis (similar to “crazy-paving” skin)
o Poor wound healing
o Thrombocytopenia
o Growth failure (wasted appearance)

Other options:
• Calorie deficiency: Leads to weight loss but not characteristic skin
findings.
• Essential amino acid deficiency: Causes poor growth not dermatitis.
• Vitamin E deficiency: Causes hemolytic anemia and neurologic dysfunction, not dermatitis
or thrombocytopenia.

Clinical pearls:
• EFAD develops quickly in preterm infants if lipids are not provided.
• Diagnosis is confirmed by low linoleic acid or triene/tetraene ratio >0.2.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 269–270

stem:
A 9-month-old girl recently recovered from episodes of vomiting and diarrhea. When mother
restarted the formula, the girl developed severe watery diarrhea, flatulence and
abdominal distention. Of the following, the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms is:
explanation:
• This is secondary (transient) lactose intolerance due to brush border enzyme deficiency
after acute viral gastroenteritis (commonly rotavirus).
o The damaged villi temporarily lose lactase activity.
o Reintroducing lactose-containing feeds leads to:
§ Watery diarrhea
§ Flatulence
§ Abdominal distention
Other options:
• Celiac disease presents later (after prolonged gluten exposure) with chronic symptoms and
poor growth.
• Bacterial enteritis typically causes fever, blood or mucus in stool.
27
•    Persistent viral shedding rarely causes severe osmotic diarrhea after recovery.
•    Cow’s milk protein allergy is more likely to cause bloody stools and colitis symptoms in
infants.

Clinical pearls:
• Secondary lactose intolerance is common after gastroenteritis.
• Usually resolves within 1–2 weeks as enterocytes regenerate.
• Use lactose-free formula or lactose-reduced feeds until symptoms resolve (usually 1–2
weeks). Once diarrhea improves, gradually reintroduce lactose-containing feeds.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1059–1060; UWorld.

stem:
A 7-year-old boy presents with a 2-week history of progressive neurologic symptoms. His
parents report that he developed right-sided weakness, left facial weakness, and difficulty
moving his left eye laterally. Examination confirms right hemiplegia, left facial palsy, and
left abducens palsy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Key features pointing to brainstem glioma:
o Cranial nerve VI palsy (abducens) + facial weakness (VII) + contralateral
hemiplegia: classic “crossed findings” localizing to the pons.
§ Cranial nerves → lower motor neuron; flaccid
§ Body → upper motor neuron; weakness + ↑ DTRs
§ + opposite sides
o Gradual progression over weeks.


Major nuclei in the pons include the following:
•    CN V: trigeminal motor nucleus (innervation to the
muscles of mastication) and the main trigeminal
nucleus (sensory innervation to the face and jaw)
•    CN VI: abducens nucleus (motor innervation to the
lateral rectus muscle)
•    CN VII: facial nucleus (motor innervation to the
muscles of facial expression)
•    CN VIII: dorsal and ventral cochlear nuclei (hearing)
and the vestibular nucleus (head position and
movement)



Other options:
• AVM hemorrhage would present acutely with sudden onset deficits, seizures, not progressive
findings.
• A rapidly growing supratentorial tumor could cause mass effect, but would not typically cause
crossed brainstem signs.
• Cerebellar tumors cause ataxia, dysmetria, and increased intracranial pressure signs but not
cranial nerve VI palsy with contralateral weakness.

Clinical pearls:
• Pontine glioma (diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma) is classic for this presentation.
28
•   Usually in children aged 5–10 years.
•   Prognosis is poor; biopsy often avoided because imaging is diagnostic.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 205–206

stem:
A 14-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother because she has not started
menstruation and has remained shorter than her peers. The family reports that her growth
has been consistently below the 5th percentile. She has no significant past medical
history. On examination, she has Tanner stage 1 breast and pubic hair development, a
height below the 3rd percentile, and no dysmorphic facial features. Laboratory studies
reveal markedly elevated FSH and LH concentrations.
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• Turner syndrome (45,XO) should be suspected in a girl with:
o Short stature
o Primary amenorrhea
o Lack of secondary sexual characteristics
o History of congenital heart disease (e.g., coarctation of the aorta, bicuspid aortic
valve)
• Karyotyping is the gold standard for diagnosing Turner syndrome
• Ultrasound may show streak ovaries or small uterus but does not confirm the genetic
cause

Clinical pearls:
• Turner syndrome is the most common chromosomal cause of primary amenorrhea
• Always evaluate for associated conditions: cardiac anomalies, renal malformations,
and autoimmune thyroiditis
• Patients require estrogen replacement to initiate puberty and protect bone health
• Consider echocardiogram, renal ultrasound, and audiology screening after diagnosis




29
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 108–s110; Osama Naga Pediatric Board

stem:
A preterm infant born at 35 weeks gestation is noted to have recurrent apnea episodes in
the neonatal unit. During these episodes, he becomes pale and has a brief drop in oxygen
saturation to as low as 84%, but recovers rapidly when stimulated by the nurses. Outside of
these events, his oxygen saturation and examination are normal. What is the most appropriate
initial management?

options.label: B
options.text: Begin empiric antibiotics and perform a full sepsis workup

options.label: C
options.text: Provide 30% supplemental oxygen by nasal cannula

answer: B. Begin empiric antibiotics and perform a full sepsis workup
explanation:
• Apnea of prematurity is less common and less likely to be the primary cause of apnea in
late preterm infants (>34 weeks).
• Apnea of prematurity:
o Typically occurs in infants <34 weeks gestation
o Related to immaturity of central respiratory control
o Peaks at 2–3 days of life, resolves by 37 weeks postmenstrual age
• After 34 weeks gestation:
o Less likely to be due to primary apnea of prematurity
o More likely to be secondary apnea (infection, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, anemia,
seizures, reflux)
• First priority: rule out infection before labeling as physiologic apnea.

Other options:
• Caffeine is appropriate for apnea of prematurity but only after excluding sepsis.
• Supplemental oxygen may help but does not address possible infection.
• Intubation is not indicated unless apnea is prolonged or unresponsive to stimulation.




30
★ Neonatal Sepsis:
• Systemic illness associated with infectious source
• Overall incidence of 1-5 per 1,000 births
o In VLBW infants, rate of incidence of early sepsis
is 2% and late sepsis is 25%
• Mortality rate is up to 25% (higher with prematurity)
• Types:
o Early Onset: within 1st 7 d (1st 3 d if hospitalized since birth)
§ Risk factors: prematurity, LBW, PPROM, ROM ≥18 hrs, GBS +ve mother,
maternal fever, fetal distress, chorioamnionitis, meconium fluid, maternal or
fetal tachycardia, preterm labor
§ Can use early onset sepsis risk score in ≥34 weeks to guide for NICU
admission/empiric Abx
§ Most common: GBS (Step agalactiae), E.coli, Listeria, Staph, Strep pneumo,
Enterococcus, Haemophilus
o Late Onset: after 7 d of life
§ Risk factors: indwelling catheters, intubation, prematurity, critical illness.
§ Most common: CoNS, gram-negative rods, Staph aureus, GBS, fungal
• Clinical presentation: temp instability, irritability, lethargy, poor perfusion, feeding
intolerance, respiratory distress, dyspnea (e.g., expiratory grunting), apnea (more
common in preterm infants)
o ↓ temp, perfusion, BG, BP = late signs
o pH (metabolic acidosis)
o ↑ RR/HR
o Specific symptoms:
§ Neonatal meningitis
• Often no signs of meningism
• Early phase: general symptoms, vomiting
• Late phase: bulging fontanelles, shrill crying, seizures, stupor
§ Neonatal pneumonia
• Tachypnea with intercostal/sternal retractions and nasal flaring
• Reduced oxygen saturation with cyanosis
• Early: GBS, Late: Chlamydia
• Diagnosis:
o Laboratory: blood culture ± UA/UCx ± LP
o Other studies CBC, CRP, abdomen Xray, CXR may be indicated depending on clinical
presentation.
• Management:
o Empiric Abx: Ampicillin + Gentamycin, broaden if severe clinical illness, use local
resistance guidelines.
o Modify/stop Abx based on culture data.
• Protective Factors:
o Adequate IAP (≥4 hours IV penicillin or ampicillin)
o Term gestation
o Asymptomatic neonate
o No rupture of membranes or fever

Clinical pearls:
• Any apnea in a 35-week infant requires evaluation for sepsis first.
• Blood cultures, CBC, CRP, glucose, and empiric antibiotics (ampicillin + gentamicin) are
standard.

31
•   Once infection and other causes are excluded, caffeine is appropriate.
o Loading: 20 mg/kg IV or oral caffeine citrate
o Maintenance: 5–10 mg/kg daily
source: Fanaroff and Martin's Neonatal-Perinatal Medicine, 11th ed., p. 1286–1288

stem:
A 2-month-old infant is brought to the clinic because the parents noticed a swelling on the
left side of the neck that becomes more visible when the baby’s head is turned to the
right. The baby is otherwise well-appearing, with no respiratory symptoms, skin discoloration,
or systemic findings.
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Mass that becomes more prominent when the neck is rotated away strongly suggests
SCM contracture.
• Even without specifying anatomic position, the key clue is prominence of the mass when
turning the head to the opposite side.
o This is due to tightness and thickening of the
sternocleidomastoid muscle, which stands out when
stretched.
• Early physiotherapy is first-line management.

Other options:
• Thyroglossal duct cysts are midline and do not change with neck
movement.
• Cystic hygroma (lymphatic malformation) is soft, fluctuant, transilluminates, and often
always visible.
• Branchial cleft cysts are fluctuant lateral neck masses, but do not typically become more
prominent with neck motion.

Neck Mass        Location              Age              Board Clues       Image      Physical Exam


>1 year,           Moves with                    Soft, cystic midline
Thyroglossal      Midline (below
sometimes late     swallowing and                mass elevates with
duct cyst         hyoid)
infancy            tongue protrusion             tongue movement



Infancy to early   Firm, does not move           Firm, non-tender,
Dermoid cyst      Midline
childhood          with swallowing               midline




Any age            Enlarged thyroid,             Symmetrical neck
Midline thyroid
Goiter                              (newborn if        moves with                    swelling, moves with
area
congenital)        swallowing                    swallowing




Later infancy      Fluctuant, lateral
Branchial cleft   Anterior to                                                        Soft cystic swelling
or childhood,      mass, may become
cyst              SCM
often after URI    infected and drain

Cystic hygroma    Posterior         At birth or
transilluminates,             enlarges with crying,
(lymphangioma)    triangle          early infancy

33
Congenital                                        Firm “pseudotumor,”
Tight SCM, head tilt
First few       more prominent when
muscular          Within SCM                                                     toward mass, chin
weeks of life   head rotated opposite
torticollis                                                                      rotated away
side


Any age,
Reactive          Any cervical                    Tender mobile nodes,           Enlarged tender lymph
common in
lymphadenopathy   area
toddlers
recent infection               nodes, possibly warm



Soft, compressible
Anywhere on     At or soon      Bluish or reddish
Hemangioma                                                                       mass, increases in size
neck            after birth     compressible lesion
early months

Thymic cyst
sheath          childhood       lower neck                     swelling




At birth or     Rapidly enlarging              Firm mass, sometimes
Teratoma          Anywhere        prenatal        mass, may cause                calcified, may obstruct
detection       airway compression             airway




Clinical Pearls (Neck Masses in Infancy/Childhood):
• Moves with swallowing and tongue protrusion? → Thyroglossal duct cyst
• Transilluminates and soft in posterior triangle? → Cystic hygroma
• Firm mass in SCM, prominent with head rotation? → Congenital torticollis
• Anterior to SCM, fluctuant, post-URI? → Branchial cleft cyst
• Rapid growth, airway compression? → Teratoma
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 224–225
topic: Neck Masses in Infancy – Congenital Muscular Torticollis

stem:
A 3-week-old neonate is brought to the clinic because of persistent watering and mild
redness of the right eye since birth. Physical examination finding is shown below.




Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• Key clues:
o Excessive tearing since birth
o Enlarged eye and hazy/white cornea
o Corneal cloudiness

34
•   This presentation is classic for congenital glaucoma, which is an emergency because it
can lead to permanent vision loss if not treated promptly.

Other options:
• Topical antibiotics are for bacterial conjunctivitis (no discharge or purulence here).
• Nasolacrimal duct obstruction presents with tearing and crusting but normal cornea and
eye size.
• Routine referral delays diagnosis—urgent evaluation is required.

Clinical pearls:
• Triad of congenital glaucoma:
o Epiphora (tearing)
o Photophobia
o Blepharospasm
• Other findings: buphthalmos (enlarged eye), corneal
haze
• Definitive treatment: surgical intervention
(goniotomy/trabeculotomy).
• Red reflex normal + hazy cornea = suspect anterior
segment pathology
• Absent red reflex = suspect cataract or retinoblastoma
• Congenital glaucoma may also present with epiphora, photophobia, and enlarged globe
(buphthalmos)




How to examine fundal reflex: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LNXTLLoYNGg
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 211–212

stem:
A 4-month-old infant is brought for evaluation of chronic diarrhea present since birth and
progressive clumsiness, especially noticeable at night. On examination, the child appears
underweight but otherwise well-appearing. A peripheral blood smear is shown below.




What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Key features of abetalipoproteinemia:
• Autosomal recessive disorder of microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP)
• Absent ApoB-containing lipoproteins (chylomicrons, VLDL, LDL)
• Can’t form chylomicrons → severe fat malabsorption: chronic diarrhea, steatorrhea since
birth
• Can’t form VLDL/LDL → defective lipid delivery to tissues
• Acanthocytosis on peripheral smear (spur cells)
• Progressive neurologic dysfunction (ataxia, loss of
proprioception, worse in dark) due to fat-soluble vitamin
deficiencies (especially vitamin E deficiency)

Other options:
• Immunodeficiency may cause diarrhea but not
acanthocytes or neurologic features.
• CCLD causes metabolic alkalosis and electrolyte loss—not
acanthocytosis.
•   Isolated vitamin E deficiency causes neurologic dysfunction
but not diarrhea or acanthocytes.
o Peripheral blood smear would show Burr cells (echinocytes), but not acanthocytes.

Clinical pearls:
• Abetalipoproteinemia diagnosis confirmed by absent ApoB and low triglycerides.
• Peripheral blood smear shows burr cells
• Management: high-calorie, low-fat diet + fat-soluble vitamin supplementation.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 365–366

stem:
A 10-month-old infant was hospitalized 6 weeks ago with severe bronchiolitis requiring
supplemental oxygen. Since discharge, the baby has persistent tachypnea, wheezing, and
hypoxemia. On examination, there are diffuse crackles and hyperinflation. You are concerned
about post-infectious bronchiolitis obliterans. Which of the following is the most
appropriate test to assess this diagnosis?
explanation:
• Bronchiolitis obliterans (constrictive):
o Chronic fibrotic obstruction of small airways after severe viral infection (e.g.,
adenovirus)
o Persistent tachypnea, hypoxemia, wheeze despite treatment

36
o   High-resolution chest CT is the best test because it shows:
§ Mosaic attenuation (air-trapping)
§ Bronchiectasis
§ Small airway narrowing
•   Other options:
o Lung biopsy is definitive but invasive and not first-line.
o Chest X-ray is nonspecific and insensitive.
o V/Q scan is not useful for diagnosis.

Clinical pearls:
• Most common after severe viral lower respiratory infection in infancy.
• CT is the gold standard imaging for suspected bronchiolitis obliterans.
• No effective reversal—management is supportive +/- steroids/trials of immunosuppression.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 587–588

stem:
A 6-year-old girl with cystic fibrosis is being followed in clinic. She has been
receiving pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy at a dose of lipase 2,500 units/day. At
her last visit 6 months ago, her BMI was at the 60th percentile, but today it has declined to
the 40th percentile. She has no fever, cough, or other signs of acute illness. Examination is
otherwise unremarkable.
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Surgical insertion of a gastrostomy tube for supplemental feeding

options.label: C
options.text: Comprehensive nutritional and behavioural assessment with increased caloric intake

options.label: D
options.text: Initiate empiric antibiotics for possible chronic respiratory infection

answer: C. Comprehensive nutritional and behavioural assessment with increased caloric
explanation:
• Key points:
o Declining BMI percentile is an early sign of malabsorption or inadequate caloric
intake.
o The current enzyme dose (2,500 units/day) is significantly below typical
recommended dosing, which is usually 500–2,500 units/kg per meal (not per day).
o Management includes:
§ Dietitian review to optimize calories and fat intake
§ Reassess enzyme dosing (often increased substantially)
§ Check for fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies
Other options:
• GT tube feeding is reserved for persistent failure to thrive despite maximal oral
strategies.
• Increasing lipase slightly without assessing caloric needs is insufficient.
• No evidence of acute infection requiring antibiotics.




37
★ Cystic Fibrosis:                                     Cystic fibrosis presenting signs mnemonic: CF PANCREAS
• Autosomal recessive, mutation in CFTR             Chronic cough and wheezing
Failure to thrive
gene (Chr 7), most common = ΔF508.               Pancreatic insufficiency (malabsorption, steatorrhea)
• CFTR defect → impaired chloride transport         Alkalosis and hypotonic dehydration
Neonatal intestinal obstruction (meconium ileus)/ Nasal polyps
→ thick, dehydrated secretions.                  Clubbing of fingers/ Chest radiograph with characteristic changes
Rectal prolapse
• Newborns: suspect with meconium                   Electrolyte elevation in sweat, salty skin
ileus (pathognomonic – bowel obstruction         Absence or congenital atresia of vas deferens
Sputum with Staph or Pseudomonas (mucoid)
+ ground-glass on AXR).
• Pulmonary symptoms: chronic cough, recurrent
pneumonia, bronchiectasis, digital clubbing.
• Early colonization: Staph aureus (infancy/early
childhood).
• Later colonization: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (mid-
childhood/adolescence), Burkholderia cepacia and
Stenotrophomonas maltophilia (severe prognosis).
• GI: pancreatic insufficiency → steatorrhea, failure to
thrive, ADEK vitamin deficiency.
• Intestinal: DIOS (Distal Intestinal Obstruction Syndrome)
= recurrent obstruction in older children.
• Liver: focal biliary cirrhosis → portal hypertension.
• Reproductive: azoospermia/infertility in males (absent vas deferens), thick cervical
mucus in females.
• Electrolytes: hypochloremic, hyponatremic metabolic alkalosis (esp. in hot weather).
• Diagnosis:
o Sweat chloride ≥60 mmol/L (gold standard)
o 2 pathogenic CFTR mutations
o Nasal potential difference (if equivocal)
• Treatment:
o Airway clearance: chest physiotherapy, hypertonic saline, dornase alfa (DNAse).
o Antibiotics:
§ Early: anti-staphylococcal (e.g., cephalexin, TMP-SMX).
§ Chronic Pseudomonas: inhaled tobramycin, aztreonam.
o CFTR modulators:
§ Ivacaftor (G551D),
§ Lumacaftor/Ivacaftor (ΔF508 homozygous),
§ Elexacaftor/Tezacaftor/Ivacaftor (Trikafta – ΔF508 hetero/homo).
o Pancreatic Enzyme Replacement Therapy (PERT) + ADEK vitamins.
§ Indication: All CF patients with exocrine pancreatic insufficiency (≈85–
90% of cases) → Prevents malabsorption, steatorrhea, fat-soluble vitamin
deficiency, and growth failure.
§ Dosing Principles:
• 500–2,500 units lipase/kg per meal → max 10,000 units
lipase/kg/day
• Half-dose with snacks.
• Capsules must be swallowed whole or opened and mixed in acidic
soft food.
§ Monitoring:
• Assess stool frequency, consistency, weight gain, BMI.
• Check fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
§ Treatment Response:


38
•   Improved growth and decreased stool fat.
•   If poor response, consider dosing errors,
acid suppression (PPI), or adherence.
§ Possible Complications:
• Perianal irritation (enzymes in stool).
• Fibrosing colonopathy (dose-dependent):
o Associated with high doses
(>10,000 units/kg/day).
o Leads to colonic strictures,
obstruction.
• Allergic reactions are rare.
•   Complications: hemoptysis, pneumothorax, CF-related diabetes (CFRD), osteopenia.
•   Surveillance: annual OGTT (CFRD), DEXA (bone health), sputum cultures (track
colonization), liver US.




Clinical pearls:
• Weight faltering in CF often reflects combination of inadequate enzyme replacement and
insufficient caloric intake.
• PERT is always titrated dose based on weight and symptoms.
o Never exceed 10,000 units/kg/day to avoid fibrosing colonopathy.
o Enzymes must be taken with all meals and snacks.
• Nutritional status is a strong predictor of lung function and survival.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 704–706

stem:
A 5-year-old boy is brought to the clinic with 3 days of fever, vomiting, and watery
diarrhea. His parents report that he also had sore throat and redness of the eyes. On
39
examination, he appears mildly dehydrated. You note conjunctival injection, an
erythematous throat with small petechial spots on the soft palate, and no rash. Which of
the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Pharyngoconjunctival fever 2º to adenovirus infection:
o Fever
o Sore throat
o Conjunctivitis (non-purulent)
• May have associated gastrointestinal symptoms (diarrhea, vomiting)
• Often affects school-aged children
• Risk factors
o Daycare centers and households with young children
o Closed or crowded settings (e.g., public swimming pools, military barracks, and
medical facilities)
• Mostly self-limited and treatment is supportive
• Tx if immunocompromised patients or severe disease: Cidofovir → dose-limiting
nephrotoxicity




Other options:
• Coxsackievirus: herpangina (vesicles on posterior oropharynx) or hand-foot-mouth disease
(vesicles on hands, feet, mouth).
• Measles: cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, Koplik spots (buccal mucosa), maculopapular rash.
• EBV: exudative pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly.

Clinical pearls:
• Pharyngoconjunctival fever is classic adenovirus.
• Adenovirus can also cause pneumonia and hemorrhagic cystitis.
• Adenovirus is also the most common cause of tonsillitis in young children.




40
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1057–1058; MedBullets Step 2/3 –

stem:
A 14-year-old adolescent presents with 7 days of fever, severe sore throat, and fatigue. On
examination, he has bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and a diffuse
maculopapular rash. Laboratory studies reveal lymphocytosis with >10% atypical
lymphocytes. A rapid heterophile antibody (Monospot) test is positive. What is the most
appropriate management?
explanation:
• Classic infectious mononucleosis (EBV):
o Fever, pharyngitis, cervical lymphadenopathy
o Splenomegaly
o Rash (especially if ampicillin given)
o Atypical lymphocytosis




41
o  Positive Monospot test
•   Treatment is supportive:
o Rest, hydration, analgesia
o Avoid contact sports (splenic rupture risk)




Other options:
• Antibiotics are not indicated unless secondary bacterial infection develops.
• Acyclovir reduces viral shedding but does not improve symptoms.
• Corticosteroids are reserved for airway obstruction, massive tonsillar hypertrophy, or
severe cytopenias.

Clinical pearls:
• Avoid ampicillin/amoxicillin: rash in >90% of EBV patients.
• No routine antivirals or steroids.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1073–1075

stem:
A 7-year-old boy initially presented with fever and cough and was prescribed amoxicillin for
presumed pharyngitis by a private clinic. He completed a 7-day course without improvement
and now returns with a target-shaped rash on his trunk and extremities. He is otherwise well-
appearing.
What is the most likely cause of his rash?
explanation:
42
•   Erythema multiforme is a delayed-type (Type IV) hypersensitivity reaction → T-cell
mediated.
•   EM presents with target lesions and is often triggered by
infections rather than medications in children.
•   Erythema multiforme in children is most commonly infection-
associated, particularly HSV and Mycoplasma pneumoniae.
•   Mycoplasma pneumoniae is a common infectious trigger
in school-aged children, and can cause mild respiratory
illness followed by erythema multiforme, even without
pneumonia.
o Age: 4-15 years
o Tx: Macrolides
•   If the child is afebrile and well, this supports a post-infectious phenomenon rather than
an ongoing drug hypersensitivity.

•   Other options:
o Amoxicillin allergy typically causes morbilliform rash or urticaria, not target
lesions.
o Pneumococcus rarely causes rashes.
o Henoch-Schönlein purpura presents with palpable purpura, arthritis, abdominal
pain, not target lesions.

Why not amoxicillin allergy?
• EM following amoxicillin is not due to a classical penicillin
allergy (which is usually IgE-mediated with urticaria or
anaphylaxis).
• Amoxicillin may act as a trigger in susceptible individuals
(especially if there is underlying viral infection like EBV), but it is
not the primary cause.
• Though amoxicillin can cause a rash, it is
usually morbilliform (not targetoid), acute, associated with urticaria and more commonly
in EBV-related infections.




Another Question from Canadian Qbank (QID 3520):
A 10-year-old female presents to your office with a 4-day history of non-productive cough, low
grade fever of 38.1°C (100.5°F), and now complains of a rash for one day. She has been taking an
over-the-counter cough suppressant and Tylenol. She is otherwise healthy. On physical exam, she
43
has a temperature of 37.7°C (99.9°F) and a respiratory rate of 20. Her lung exam reveals no rhonchi
or wheezes. Her skin has several “target” lesions scattered on her trunk and upper arms. A
chest x-ray reveals a fine interstitial pattern with normal cardiac silhouette. She most likely is
infected with what organism?
A- Streptococcus pneumoniae
B- Chlamydia pneumoniae
C- Mycoplasma pneumoniae
D- Haemophilus influenzae
E- Influenza A


Answer: C. Mycoplasma pneumoniae
Explanation: This school-age child has an atypical pneumonia with erythema multiforme, which
is consistent with Mycoplasma pneumonia (choice C) infections. Mycoplasma is the most
common cause of atypical pneumonias in school-aged children.
→ Streptococcus pneumoniae (choice A) is the most common cause of typical bacterial
pneumonia in children. Patients usually are tachypneic and febrile with a consolidated lobar
infiltrate on chest x-ray.
→ Chlamydia pneumoniae (choice B) may cause interstitial, atypical pneumonias. Patients are
usually afebrile. There is no associated with erythema multiforme. See Answer A.
→ Haemophilus influenzae (choice D) is a cause of bacterial pneumonia in children, again with a
consolidated lobar infiltrate.
→ Influenza A (choice E) may cause a viral pneumonia with interstitial infiltrates, but patients
typically have systemic symptoms such as fever, malaise, and myalgias.

stem:
A 6-year-old boy presents with fever, urticarial rash, and joint pain involving the knees and
wrists. The symptoms began 7 days after completing a 10-day course of cefaclor. He is
afebrile at presentation, and physical exam shows non-blanching urticarial rash on the trunk
and extremities, no mucosal involvement, and no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly.
Laboratory studies reveal normal platelets, normal liver enzymes, and no hematuria or
proteinuria.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Type III hypersensitivity involves immune complex deposition, leading to serum
sickness-like reaction (SSLR).
• Often triggered by beta-lactam antibiotics (especially cefaclor, penicillin, TMP-SMX).
• Presents ~1–2 weeks after drug exposure.
• Classic features include: fever, polyarthralgia/arthritis, urticarial or morbilliform rash,
absence of mucosal involvement or systemic vasculitis findings
• Labs: normal platelets, normal renal function, no hematuria, differentiating it from HSP.




44
Type III HSR
mechanism:




Other options:
• Erythema multiforme: target lesions, often
following herpes, usually no arthritis.
• HSP: palpable purpura, abdominal pain,
hematuria.
• SJS: severe mucosal involvement, skin
detachment, systemic toxicity.

Clinical pearls:
• Treatment is supportive ± antihistamines or short course of steroids.
• Avoid re-exposure to the offending drug.




Clinical Pearls:
• Target lesions without mucosal involvement → EM → HSV, Mycoplasma pneumoniae
• Urticarial rash + fever + arthralgia after beta-lactams → Serum sickness-like → T3-HSR,
cefaclor
• Palpable purpura + GI pain + hematuria → HSP (IgA vasculitis)
• Facial edema + rash + eosinophilia + hepatitis → DRESS → ASMs
• Mucosal ulcers + skin sloughing after drugs → SJS/TEN → Sulfa drugs
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 972; AAP Red Book 2021–2024, p. 568;
topic: Infectious Exanthems & Delayed Onset Drug Reactions

stem:
A 5-year-old boy was found to have a serum potassium level of 7.4
mEq/L. Intravenous calcium gluconate was started immediately. What is the purpose of
giving calcium gluconate?

options.label: B
options.text: To protect and stabilize the cardiac muscle

options.label: D
options.text: To enhance renal excretion of potassium

answer: B. To protect and stabilize the cardiac muscle
explanation:
• Calcium gluconate does not lower serum potassium.
• It raises the threshold potential of cardiac myocytes,
stabilizing cell membranes, and reduces the risk of fatal
arrhythmias (eg, ventricular fibrillation).
• Other therapies must be given to shift or remove potassium.


Clinical pearls:
• Effect begins within minutes, duration ~30–60 min.
• Should be administered slowly while monitoring ECG.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 292–293

stem:
A 4-year-old boy fell from a 1-meter-high playground structure. The fall was witnessed, and
he had no loss of consciousness, no vomiting, and has remained alert and interactive since
the injury. His vital signs are stable. On examination, you note a localized parietal
hematoma without neurologic deficits. What is the most appropriate next step in
management?
explanation:
• This scenario meets low-risk criteria for clinically important traumatic brain injury,
per PECARN guidelines (>2 years):
o No loss of consciousness
o No vomiting
o Normal mental status and behavior
o No signs of basilar skull fracture
o Isolated, small, non-frontal scalp hematoma
o No severe mechanism (fall <3 feet in >2-year-old)
• In this setting, observation without imaging is appropriate.
• Other options:
o Plain X-ray does not reliably detect intracranial injury.
o CT is reserved for higher-risk features (e.g., altered mental status, severe
mechanism, large scalp hematoma, signs of skull fracture).

Clinical pearls:

46
•   For low-risk children, observation is preferred over CT to avoid unnecessary radiation
exposure.
•   Caregivers should be given clear return precautions for worsening symptoms.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 391–392
topic: Minor Head Trauma – PECARN Decision Rules

stem:
A full-term infant is exclusively breastfed. The mother expresses concern about the risk
of iron deficiency. The infant appears well and is growing appropriately. What is the most
appropriate recommendation regarding iron supplementation?

options.label: A
options.text: Reassure the mother—no iron supplementation is needed if she consumes an iron-rich diet

options.label: B
options.text: Start iron supplementation at birth in all breastfed infants

options.label: C
options.text: Start iron supplementation at 4 months of age if the infant remains exclusively breastfed

options.label: D
options.text: Delay iron supplementation until 12 months of age

answer: C. Start iron supplementation at 4 months of age if the infant remains exclusively
explanation:
• Full-term infants are born with enough iron stores to last about 4 months.
• After 4 months, breast milk alone does not provide sufficient iron, and supplementation
is recommended:
o 1 mg/kg/day elemental iron starting at 4 months until iron-rich complementary
foods are established.
• Other options:
o Maternal diet does not significantly increase iron content in breast milk.
o Preterm infants need supplementation earlier, but full-term infants do not need iron
at birth.
o Waiting until 12 months increases the risk of iron deficiency anemia.

Clinical pearls:
• Iron-fortified cereal and meats can replace supplementation once consistently consumed.
• Preterm infants (<37 weeks): start iron by 2 weeks of age.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 71–72

stem:
A 3-year-old child presents with microcytic hypochromic anemia. You suspect iron
deficiency anemia. What is the best single test to confirm the diagnosis?
explanation:
• Serum ferritin is the most sensitive and specific single test for iron deficiency:
o Reflects iron stores in the bone marrow and liver.
o Low ferritin = diagnostic of iron deficiency.
• Important note: Ferritin is an acute phase reactant and can be falsely normal or elevated in
infection or inflammation.
47
•   Other options:
o A. Serum iron is low in iron deficiency but fluctuates throughout the day and with
diet.
o C. TIBC is elevated in iron deficiency but is less specific alone.
o D. Transferrin is similar to TIBC—an indirect measure, not preferred as a single test.

Clinical pearls:
• Best approach: low ferritin + low serum iron + high TIBC confirms iron deficiency.
• If inflammation suspected, use soluble transferrin receptor as a more reliable test.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 686–688

stem:
A 12-month-old boy is on cow’s milk and consumes very little solid food. Laboratory results
reveal hemoglobin 7 g/dL. He is otherwise clinically stable and well-appearing. What is
the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Encourage increased meat intake and follow up in 2 weeks

options.label: B
options.text: Start oral iron supplementation and follow up in 2 weeks

options.label: C
options.text: Transfuse packed red blood cells (PRBCs) immediately

answer: B. Start oral iron supplementation and follow up in 2 weeks
explanation:
• Cow’s milk overconsumption (>24 oz/day) is the most common cause of iron deficiency
anemia in toddlers.
• Despite a low hemoglobin (7 g/dL), if the child is hemodynamically stable and
asymptomatic, transfusion is not required.
• Management:
o Oral iron therapy (3–6 mg/kg/day elemental iron).
o Dietary counseling to limit cow’s milk and increase iron-rich foods.
o Recheck hemoglobin in 2–4 weeks (a rise by ≥1 g/dL confirms the diagnosis).
• Other options:
o Diet changes alone are inadequate for moderate to severe anemia.
o Transfusion is reserved for symptomatic anemia (tachycardia, dyspnea, heart
failure).
o IV iron is rarely needed in first-line management.

Clinical pearls:
• Children with iron deficiency anemia often tolerate low hemoglobin levels if chronic.
• Always provide dietary guidance along with iron therapy.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 686–688

stem:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to the clinic because of recent irritability, difficulty
concentrating, and episodes of abdominal pain. His parents report he has been
taking traditional herbal remedies regularly. On exam, he appears pale and slightly
ataxic. Laboratory workup shows:
- Hemoglobin: 8.2 g/dL
- MCV: 72 fL
- Peripheral smear: basophilic stippling
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Key features suggesting lead poisoning:
o Behavioral changes (irritability, poor concentration)
o GI symptoms (abdominal pain, constipation)
o Microcytic anemia with basophilic stippling
o Exposure risk: traditional remedies, old paint, contaminated environments
• Other options:
o Iron deficiency anemia can cause microcytosis but does not cause basophilic
stippling or neurologic signs.
o Thalassemia minor has microcytosis with target cells and normal iron studies, but
usually no neuro symptoms.
o Sideroblastic anemia shows ring sideroblasts in bone marrow, not peripheral
basophilic stippling.

★ Lead Poisoning:
• Classic signs of lead poisoning:
o Lethargy, vomiting, abdominal pain, constipation
o Irritability, developmental delay, anemia
o PICA (craving for non-food items)
• Children with environmental exposure (old homes, pottery, imported toys) are at risk.
o Always screen with venous lead level if PICA + GI + behavior issues.
• Lead inhibits rate-limiting enzymes in heme synthesis, ferrochelatase, delta amino
levulinic acid dehydrogenase → accumulation of precursors and hypochromic microcytic
anemia.
o Iron deficiency may also cause microcytosis, but the combination of GI and
neurologic symptoms favors lead toxicity.
• Basophilic stippling is a hallmark finding in lead poisoning,
due to impaired RNA degradation in erythrocytes
• Screening in high-risk pediatric populations
o Living in older housing with peeling paint
o Sibling or playmate with lead poisoning
o Recent immigrant, refugee, or foreign adoptee
o History of pica
• Treatment of lead toxicity
o Decrease exposure: professional lead paint abatement, dust reduction,
minimization of contact with bare soil
49
o Nutrition: calcium, iron, zinc, vitamin C, and vitamin D-rich diet
o Chelation therapy:
o Treatment based on level:
§ <45 µg/dL → Remove exposure, monitor, nutrition (iron, Ca, vitamin C)
§ 45–69 µg/dL → Oral chelation with Succimer (DMSA)
§ ≥70 µg/dL or symptomatic → Hospitalization + IV EDTA + BAL
(dimercaprol)
§ If Iron-deficient → Replace iron before chelation begins




Purple-blue line on the gums
(lead line or Burton line)




Lead lines on long bone x-ray    Lead flecks on abdominal xray
(dense bands at the metaphyses)

Clinical pearls:
• Lead toxicity is diagnosed by venous blood lead level
• Start chelation therapy if BLL ≥ 45 µg/dL
• Remove environmental exposure (e.g., peeling paint, imported toys)

stem:
A patient with acute heart failure presents with pulmonary edema and volume overload. You
want to administer a diuretic with the fastest onset of action. Which of the following
medications is most appropriate?
explanation:
• Furosemide (IV loop diuretic) is the fastest-acting diuretic:
o Onset of action IV: 5–15 minutes
o Reduces preload rapidly via venodilation and diuresis
• Other options:
o Digoxin improves contractility but does not provide acute diuresis.
o Hydrochlorothiazide is slow-acting and used orally.
o Spironolactone is potassium-sparing, slow onset.

Clinical pearls:
• For acute pulmonary edema: IV furosemide + supplemental oxygen + nitrates if needed.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 258–259

stem:
A 6-month-old infant is brought for a routine check-up. On examination, you find
a continuous murmur best heard below the left clavicle and a bounding peripheral pulse. The
infant is feeding and growing well without signs of distress. What is the most appropriate
management?
explanation:
• Continuous murmur + bounding pulses = Patent Ductus Arteriosus (PDA).
• In infancy (>1 month) when the ductus persists, spontaneous closure is unlikely.
• If the child is stable and asymptomatic:
o Plan for elective closure by catheter device or surgery between 6–12 months.
• Other options:
o Prostaglandin keeps the duct open—not indicated here.
o Waiting further is discouraged because of risk of pulmonary overcirculation and
endarteritis.
o Emergency repair is not needed unless in severe heart failure.

Clinical pearls:
• In premature infants, NSAIDs (indomethacin/ibuprofen) can close PDA.
• In older infants, device closure is preferred.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 385–387

stem: What is the most likely complication after acute rheumatic fever in a child?
explanation:
• Acute rheumatic fever most commonly affects the mitral valve.
o Early complication: mitral regurgitation (pansystolic murmur at apex)
o Chronic complication: mitral stenosis (years later)
• Other options:
o Aortic regurgitation occurs less frequently.
o Heart failure can occur in severe carditis but is less common than isolated valvular
regurgitation.
o Tricuspid involvement is rare.

stem:
A child has a history of recurrent chest infections and otitis media. On exam, he is noted to
have dextrocardia. Kartagener syndrome is suspected. What is the most appropriate
diagnostic test?
answer: C. Nasal biopsy with electron microscopy
explanation:
• Kartagener syndrome = primary ciliary dyskinesia with:
o Chronic respiratory infections (impaired mucociliary clearance)
o Chronic otitis media
o Situs inversus (dextrocardia)
• Diagnosis:
o Nasal or bronchial biopsy showing absent or defective dynein arms on electron
microscopy.
• Other options:
o Immunoglobulin deficiency causes infections but not situs inversus.
o Oxidative burst test diagnoses chronic granulomatous disease.
o Sweat chloride test for cystic fibrosis.



54
Clinical pearls:
• Kartagener syndrome is a subset of primary ciliary dyskinesia (PCD) characterized by:
o Bronchiectasis
o Chronic sinusitis
o Recurrent otitis media
o Situs inversus (seen in ~50% of cases)
• Due to defective dynein arms in cilia, leading to impaired mucociliary clearance
