• Infertility is common in both sexes due to impaired ciliary motility in sperm and fallopian
tubes.
• Neonates can present with respiratory distress due to impaired clearance.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 589–590

stem:
A 15-month-old boy with a history of GERD presents with failure to thrive and new onset
difficulty tolerating solids. A plain X-ray was performed. What is the most appropriate next
step in management?
answer: C. Contrast study of the esophagus
explanation:
In a child with chronic GERD + progressive dysphagia, esophageal stricture is a
likely cause.
• Contrast esophagram (barium swallow) is the first step to evaluate for:
o Strictures
o Anatomic abnormalities (eg, vascular rings)
o Achalasia

55
•   Other options:
o Endoscopy is useful after imaging to confirm and dilate stricture if needed.
o CT is not first-line.
o pH monitoring assesses acid exposure, not obstruction.

Clinical pearls:
• Symptoms suggesting stricture: progressive dysphagia, FTT, food impaction.
• Always start with imaging before endoscopy.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 573–575

stem:
A 2-year-old child is brought to the clinic with ear pain, fever, and decreased appetite. On
examination, the tympanic membrane is erythematous and bulging. What is the most
common causative organism?
explanation:
• Most common pathogens in acute otitis media:
o Streptococcus pneumoniae (~30–50%)
o Non-typeable Haemophilus influenzae (~20–30%)
o Moraxella catarrhalis (~10–20%)
• Other options:
o Hib is mainly invasive disease (meningitis, epiglottitis), not common in AOM.
o Staph aureus is rare in uncomplicated otitis media.
o Group B strep is neonatal pathogen, not a cause in this age.




56
Clinical pearls:
• First-line treatment: high-dose amoxicillin.
• Consider amoxicillin-clavulanate if recurrent or resistant.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1182–1183; Osama Naga 2024 p. 298

stem:
A 21-day-old baby girl presents with vomiting, lethargy, and seizures unresponsive to
antiepileptics, calcium, vitamin B6 or glucose. There is a history of 2 sibling deaths in the
neonatal period. Sepsis workup is negative, and CBC and coagulation profile are normal. No
other labs were provided. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This presentation is consistent with a metabolic decompensation: vomiting,
lethargy, refractory seizures. Neonatal seizures unresponsive to pyridoxine (vitamin B6) are
classically seen in organic acidemias, due to toxic metabolite accumulation causing severe
metabolic encephalopathy.
• Urea cycle defects (UCDs) are possible, but:
o OTC deficiency (most common) is X-linked recessive and less likely in a female.
o Other urea cycle defects are rarer.
• Galactosemia would show liver failure, coagulopathy, hypoglycemia—not normal.
• PKU causes gradual developmental delay and irritability over months, not neonatal
seizures.

★ Inborn Errors of Metabolism:
A. Metabolic Crisis
• Suspect in neonate/infant with: poor feeding, vomiting, lethargy, seizures, apnea,
hypotonia, unexplained shock, developmental regression, HSM, liver failure,
cardiomyopathy
• Specific triggers:
o Vomiting, fasting, infection, fever, vaccinations, surgery, accident/injury,
changes in diet → protein/carbohydrate metabolism disorders
o High-protein diet and/or catabolic state → aminoacidopathies, organic
acidemia, UCDs
o Fruit, sugar (sucrose), liquid medicines → fructose intolerance
o Lactose (milk) → galactosemia
o High fat → fatty acid oxidation disorders
o Drugs → porphyria, glucose-6-phosphate-dehydrogenase deficiency
o Extensive exercise → disorders of fatty acid oxidation, glycolysis, respiratory chain
• Initial emergency labs: glucose, ammonia, ABG/VBG, lactate, ketones, electrolytes,
LFTs, CBC
o Hyperammonemia = metabolic emergency
o Metabolic acidosis + ketosis → organic acidemia
o Hyperammonemia without acidosis → urea cycle defect
o Hypoglycemia without ketones → fatty acid oxidation defect, hyperinsulinism
• Initial management:
o Stop protein temporarily

57
o   High dextrose IV fluids
o   Correct acidosis/electrolytes
o   Treat hyperammonemia urgently
o   Avoid fasting
o   Metabolic/genetics consult

B. Organic Acidemias
• Findings: poor feeding, hypotonia, refractory seizures
• Autosomal recessive inheritance
• Typically manifest in the first 1–3 weeks of life after protein feeding
• Can present with severe illness before labs document metabolic acidosis if not checked
• Labs: high anion gap metabolic acidosis, ketosis, hyperammonemia
• Treatment: dextrose, stop protein, carnitine ± dialysis

C. Urea Cycle Defects
• Severe defects typically present in term newborns who appear well for the first 24 to 48
hours after birth
• Findings: vomiting, lethargy, seizures, encephalopathy
• Labs: severe hyperammonemia, respiratory alkalosis, normal glucose
• Key clue: hyperammonemia WITHOUT metabolic acidosis
• Treatment: stop protein, dextrose, ammonia scavengers, dialysis if severe

D. MSUD (Maple Syrup Urine Disease)
• Findings: poor feeding, dystonia, encephalopathy
• Labs: ketosis, elevated branched-chain amino acids
• Key clue: maple syrup/burnt sugar urine odor
• Treatment: restrict leucine/isoleucine/valine

E. Galactosemia
• Findings: jaundice, hepatomegaly, cataracts, E. coli sepsis
• Labs: positive urine reducing substances
• Key clue: neonatal cataracts + sepsis
• Treatment: eliminate lactose/galactose

F. Phenylketonuria (PKU)
• Findings: developmental delay, eczema, seizures
• Labs: elevated phenylalanine
• Key clue: musty/mousy odor
• Treatment: low phenylalanine diet

G. MCAD Deficiency
• Findings: lethargy/seizures after fasting
• Labs: hypoketotic hypoglycemia
• Key clue: low ketones despite hypoglycemia
• Treatment: avoid fasting, dextrose

H. Glycogen Storage Disease Type I
• Findings: hepatomegaly, doll facies, growth failure
• Labs: hypoglycemia, lactic acidosis, hyperuricemia
• Key clue: severe fasting hypoglycemia
• Treatment: frequent cornstarch feeds


58
I. Biotinidase Deficiency
• Findings: seizures, alopecia, dermatitis
• Labs: metabolic acidosis
• Key clue: rash + alopecia
• Treatment: biotin

J. Pyridoxine-Dependent Epilepsy
• Findings: refractory neonatal seizures
• Labs: often normal
• Key clue: responds to vitamin B6
• Treatment: pyridoxine lifelong

Differential Diagnosis of IEM Based on Lab Findings
Primary Finding              Secondary Finding                       Potential Disorder
Acidosis                     ↓ BG, ↑ lactate, ketosis, NH3, ↑ AG     Various organic acid disorders
Significantly ↑ lactate, normal BG      - Mitochondrial disorders
- Pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency
- ⍺-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase deficiency
- pyruvate carboxylase deficiency
Significantly ↑ lactate, ↓ BG           - Glycogen storage disease type I (Von Gierke)
- Fructose 1,6-bisphosphatase deficiency
Normal AG, ↑ lactate, no ketosis        RTA
Hyperammonemia               Alkalosis or normal pH, normal          Urea cycle disorders
lactate
↓ BG, ↑ LFT, normal ketones             Fatty acid oxidation defects
↓ pH w/ ↑ AG, ketosis, ↑ lactate, ↑     Various organic acid disorders
NH3
Hypoglycemia                 Hepatomegaly, ↑ lactate                 Various organic acid disorders
No ketosis, no acidosis, normal         - Hyperinsulinemia
lactate                                 - Fatty acid oxidation defects
↓ Na, ↓ BP                              Adrenal insufficiency
Hepatic failure                         - Tyrosinemia
- Glycogen storage disease type IV
- Galactosemia
- Niemann-Pick type C
(Pediatrics in Review 2009; 31:131)


Clinical pearls:
• Although inborn errors of metabolism (IEM) are a rare cause of seizures in children, seizures
may occur in virtually all IEM
• Neonatal seizures unresponsive to pyridoxine (vitamin B6) are classically seen in organic
acidemias, due to toxic metabolite accumulation causing severe encephalopathy.
• In a neonate with:
o Neonatal encephalopathy + sibling deaths + unresponsive seizures
o No acid-base labs reported
o Female infant in a consanguineous setting (KSA)
→ Organic acidemia is statistically most likely.
• Next step: send blood gas, ammonia, lactate, urine organic acids.
• Early management: stop protein feeds, start glucose infusion, correct acidosis.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 176–178; Pediatric Secrets 2024
topic: Inborn Errors of Metabolism – Organic Acidemias

stem:
A 6-month-old infant presents with recurrent otitis media and sinusitis, chronic cough and
episodes of bacterial pneumonia. Which finding would be most suggestive of the underlying
diagnosis?
answer: D. Absent IgG, very low IgM
explanation:
• This presentation is classic for X-linked agammaglobulinemia (Bruton disease):
o Recurrent sinopulmonary infections with encapsulated bacteria starting
after maternal IgG wanes (~6 months).
o Profound deficiency of all immunoglobulins (IgG, IgM, IgA).
o Absent B cells on flow cytometry.
• Other options:
o Lymphopenia suggests T-cell or combined immunodeficiency (e.g., SCID).
o Absent CD40 ligand = Hyper IgM syndrome (high IgM, low IgG).
o Abnormal oxidative burst = chronic granulomatous disease (abscesses, catalase-
positive organisms).




★ How to Differentiate Types of Immunodeficiency:
Humoral (B-cell) Deficiencies:
• Onset: After 4–6 months of age (loss of maternal IgG)
• Infections: Recurrent sinopulmonary bacterial infections (S. pneumoniae, H.
influenzae)
• Key Types:
o X-linked Agammaglobulinemia (Bruton): Absent B cells, all immunoglobulins
low
o Selective IgA Deficiency: Mild, recurrent mucosal infections, anaphylaxis with
blood products
o Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID): Low IgG/IgA,
adolescence/adulthood onset
T-cell or Combined T/B-cell Deficiencies:
• Onset: Neonatal period or early infancy
• Infections: Severe viral, fungal, opportunistic infections (PCP, CMV, Candida)
• Key Types:

60
o Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID): Absent T cells, severe infections,
failure to thrive
o DiGeorge Syndrome (22q11 deletion): Thymic aplasia, hypocalcemia,
conotruncal cardiac anomalies
Phagocytic Defects (Neutrophil Dysfunction):
• Infections: Recurrent skin and lung abscesses, poor wound healing
• Key Types:
o Chronic Granulomatous Disease (CGD):
Defective oxidative burst, catalase-
positive organisms (S. aureus, Serratia)
o Leukocyte Adhesion Deficiency (LAD):
Delayed umbilical cord separation, no pus
Complement Deficiencies:
• Infections: Encapsulated bacteria and Neisseria
(depending on component)
• Key Types:
o C3 Deficiency: Severe bacterial infections
early in life
o C5–C9 Deficiency (MAC): Recurrent
Neisseria infections
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 474–476

stem:
A child with newly diagnosed acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) develops laboratory
abnormalities consistent with tumor lysis syndrome. Which of the following laboratory
patterns is most characteristic of tumor lysis syndrome?
explanation:
• Tumor lysis syndrome labs:
o Hyperkalemia (cell lysis releases intracellular K)
o Hyperphosphatemia (from nucleic acids and phosphates)
o Hypocalcemia (phosphate binds calcium)
o Hyperuricemia (purine breakdown)

★ Tumor Lysis Syndrome (TLS):




61
•   Definition: TLS is a potentially life-threatening oncologic emergency caused by rapid
lysis of tumor cells, leading to release of intracellular contents into the bloodstream.
•   Etiology (common triggers):
o High-grade lymphomas (especially Burkitt lymphoma)
o Acute leukemias (ALL, AML with high WBC)
o Large tumor burden or rapid cell turnover
o After chemotherapy, corticosteroids, or spontaneously → may be the initial
presentation of cancer
•   Core Electrolyte Abnormalities (mnemonic: PUKED):
o Phosphatemia (↑ PO₄)
o Uric acid (↑ UA)
o Kalemia (↑ K⁺)
o sEcondary hypoCalcemia (↓ Ca²⁺)
o Dehydration & renal failure risk
•   Clinical Features:
o Vomiting, lethargy, muscle cramps, seizures, tetany
o Arrhythmias from hyperkalemia or hypocalcemia
o Acute kidney injury due to uric acid or calcium phosphate nephropathy
•   Diagnostic Criteria (Cairo-Bishop):
o Laboratory TLS = ≥2 of the following within 3 days before or 7 days after
chemotherapy:
§ Uric acid > 8 mg/dL (or 25% increase)
§ Potassium > 6 mEq/L (or 25% increase)
§ Phosphate > 6.5 mg/dL (children)
§ Calcium < 7 mg/dL (or 25% decrease)
o Clinical TLS = Lab TLS + one of the following:
§ Renal insufficiency
§ Cardiac arrhythmia/sudden death
§ Seizure
•   Prevention (most high-yield concept):
o IV hydration 2–3x maintenance (start before chemo)
o Allopurinol to prevent uric acid buildup
§ Prevents formation of uric acid, does not lower existing uric acid.
§ Start: 1–2 days before chemotherapy in moderate-risk TLS patients.
o Rasburicase in high-risk patients to lower uric acid quickly
§ Treatment and prevention: rapidly degrades uric acid into allantoin,
which is more water-soluble and easily excreted.
62
§  Start: at first sign of hyperuricemia or for high-risk TLS even before
hyperuricemia appears.
§ Contraindicated in G6PD deficiency → risk of hemolysis and
methemoglobinemia
o Note: Avoid simultaneous use of allopurinol and rasburicase: allopurinol inhibits
xanthine oxidase → co-administration reduces efficacy and increases risk of
nephropathy
o Close monitoring: electrolytes, renal function, urine output
•   Management:
o Aggressive hydration
o Correct electrolyte abnormalities:
§ Hyperkalemia: calcium gluconate, insulin/glucose, albuterol
§ Hyperphosphatemia: phosphate binders, avoid phosphate intake
§ Hypocalcemia: treat only if symptomatic (seizure, tetany)
o Dialysis if refractory hyperkalemia, severe AKI, or fluid overload
•   High-Risk Features for TLS (prophylaxis needed):
o WBC >100,000/mm³ in leukemia
o LDH >2x normal
o Preexisting renal dysfunction
o Burkitt lymphoma or T-cell ALL
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1874–1876; Pocket Pediatrics 2024

stem:
A school-aged child has had a persistent barking, brassy cough for 6 months. The cough
occurs throughout the day, disappears during sleep, and stops when the child is distracted.
There are no seasonal changes or identifiable triggers. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Key features of habit (psychogenic) cough:
o Persistent, loud, repetitive cough (“honking” or “barking”)
o Absent during sleep
o Improves with distraction
o No associated wheeze, dyspnea, or trigger
• Other options:
o B. Asthma: typically worse at night, associated wheeze, dyspnea, and identifiable
triggers.
o C. Spasmodic croup: episodic stridor and cough, not chronic for 6 months.
o D. Psychogenic dyspnea is episodic breathlessness, not chronic cough.

Clinical pearls:
• Treatment: reassurance and behavioral techniques; avoid unnecessary medications.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 630–631

stem:
A 6-year-old boy is brought to clinic because of concerns about early development of
secondary sexual characteristics. His parents report that his penis has increased in length,
but the testicle size appears normal for his age. On examination, he has pubic hair Tanner
stage I, his height is above the 98th percentile, and his blood pressure is 130/90
mmHg. His newborn metabolic screen was unremarkable. What is the most likely cause of
this presentation?
explanation:
• Key features pointing to 11β-hydroxylase deficiency:
o Peripheral androgen excess: increased penile length with prepubertal testicular
volume
o Hypertension due to excess 11-deoxycorticosterone
o Accelerated growth without testicular enlargement
o Normal newborn screen (screening typically detects 21-hydroxylase deficiency)
• Other options:
o Sertoli cell tumors do not produce androgens.
o True (central) precocious puberty shows testicular enlargement.
o Hypothalamic tumor also causes true precocious puberty with large testes.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 677–678

stem:
A 6-year-old boy from Jazan region presents with 3 days of high fever, chills, and
vomiting. On examination, he is febrile to 39.8 °C, pale, and mildly jaundiced. He has
splenomegaly. Laboratory studies show:
- Hemoglobin: 7.8 g/dL
- Platelets: 55,000/mm³
- Peripheral blood smear:




Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?
explanation:
• P. falciparum distinctive features on smear:
o Multiple ring forms in a single RBC
o Delicate appliqué (“headphone”) forms attached to cell
membrane
• Epidemiology:
o Jazan region (Southwest Saudi Arabia) is endemic
for falciparum malaria, not vivax predominance.
• Other species:
o P. vivax/ovale: single ring per RBC, Schüffner stippling,
enlarged RBCs
o P. malariae: band forms

★ Malaria:
• Etiology:
o Protozoan parasites of the genus Plasmodium
o Major species:
§ P. falciparum: most severe, cerebral malaria risk
§ P. vivax: common cause of relapse
(hypnozoites)
§ P. ovale: less common, also has
hypnozoites
§ P. malariae: chronic low-grade infection
§ P. knowlesi: zoonotic, Southeast Asia
• Transmission:
o Anopheles mosquito (bite at night)
o Less commonly via transfusion, congenital, shared
needles
• Pathophysiology/Life Cycle:
o A bite by a female Anopheles mosquito transmits
sporozoites into the host bloodstream; sporozoites
then spread to the liver, infect hepatocytes, and replicate.
o Lysis of infected hepatocytes releases merozoites into the blood, which rapidly
invade circulating RBCs.
§ P falciparum infects erythrocytes of all ages, leading to a very high degree of
parasitemia
§ P vivax and Plasmodium ovale prefer young, larger erythrocytes
o Following erythrocyte invasion, the merozoites feed on hemoglobin and mature from
ring forms → trophozoites → schizonts.
§ Schizont maturation occurs synchronously within erythrocytes, resulting in
simultaneous cell lysis and release of proinflammatory substances, which
causes paroxysms of fever.
o Unlike other species, P vivax and P ovale form hypnozoites in the liver that
are dormant and reactivate weeks or months later. Therefore, these species must
be treated with a medication that targets the intraerythrocytic phase (eg,
chloroquine) as well as the dormant hepatic phase (eg, primaquine) to ensure
eradication and prevent relapse.
§ P.vivax is most commonly associated with relapse.
• Epidemiology (Saudi Arabia):
o Endemic areas: Southwestern regions (e.g., Jazan, Asir), near Yemen border
o P. falciparum is predominant species (≈70–80%)
o P. vivax also present, especially among immigrants and travelers

65
o Seasonal transmission peaks after rainy periods
•   Clinical Presentation:
o Uncomplicated malaria:
§ Fever (often cyclical), chills, sweats
§ Malaise, myalgias, headache
§ Anemia, splenomegaly
o Severe malaria (especially falciparum):
§ Altered consciousness, seizures (cerebral malaria)
§ Severe anemia
§ Hypoglycemia
§ Respiratory distress/acidosis
§ Renal failure
•   Diagnosis:
o Gold standard: Peripheral blood smear
§ Thick smear: sensitivity
§ Thin smear: species identification, parasitemia quantification
o Rapid antigen tests: Useful if microscopy unavailable
o Additional labs: CBC (anemia, thrombocytopenia), glucose
•   Treatment Principles:
o If sensitive → chloroquine; if resistant → mefloquine, doxycycline or
atovaquone/proguanil
o If life threatening, use intravenous quinine or artesunate (test for G6PD deficiency)
o Uncomplicated falciparum malaria: Artemisinin-based combination therapy (e.g.,
artemether-lumefantrine)
o Severe falciparum malaria: IV artesunate
(preferred) or quinine/quinidine if artesunate
unavailable
o P. vivax and P. ovale:
§ Treat blood stage with chloroquine (where
sensitive) or ACT
§ Add primaquine to eradicate liver
hypnozoites (prevent relapse)—must
check G6PD status
o Supportive care: Manage hypoglycemia, anemia, fluids
•   Prevention:
o In endemic regions of Saudi Arabia:
§ Vector control (spraying, bed nets)
§ Chemoprophylaxis for travelers (e.g., atovaquone-proguanil, doxycycline)
§ Soldier going to the south in KSA: atovaquone/proguanil
§ Prophylaxis in KSA in general for non-military personnel: mefloquine
§ Prophylaxis in patients with psychiatric illness: atovaquone/ proguanil
because mefloquine is contraindicated
§ Prompt diagnosis and treatment




66
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 1474–1475; UWorld; First Aid for the USMLE.

stem:
A 14-year-old girl is brought to clinic because of weight loss despite increased appetite,
excessive sweating, and difficulty sleeping over the past 2 months. Her mother has noticed that
her neck appears enlarged. On examination, she has a diffuse, non-tender
goiter, tachycardia, and a fine tremor. Her blood pressure is elevated for age. Initial thyroid
function tests confirm hyperthyroidism with suppressed TSH and elevated free T₄. In addition
to thyroid function tests, what is the most appropriate investigation to confirm the
diagnosis?
explanation:
• Classic presentation of Graves disease (autoimmune hyperthyroidism):
o Diffuse goiter
o Symptomatic thyrotoxicosis (polyphagia, weight loss, tremor, tachycardia)
o Suppressed TSH
• Thyroid stimulating immunoglobulin (TSI) is the specific test to confirm Graves disease.

Other options:
• Antithyroglobulin antibody: More commonly seen in Hashimoto thyroiditis.
• Thyroid ultrasound: Useful for nodules, not needed in classic Graves presentation.
• Thyroglobulin: Nonspecific marker of thyroid activity, not diagnostic.
67
Clinical Pearls:
• Radioactive iodine uptake scan can also help confirm Graves if diagnostic uncertainty
remains.
• First-line treatment is methimazole; beta-blockers help control symptoms.
• Eye involvement (exophthalmos) is common in Graves but absent in this case.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 702–703

stem:
A 10-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his mother because of 2 weeks of worsening fatigue,
excessive thirst, and frequent urination. His mother reports he has been waking up multiple
times at night to drink water and urinate. Over the past month, he has lost approximately 3 kg
despite having a good appetite. Today, he also complains of frontal headache and mild blurry
vision. There is no recent illness. On examination, he appears tired but alert. Vital signs are
normal. Which of the following results would confirm the diagnosis of diabetes?

options.label: D
options.text: Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) at 2 hours 136 mg/dL

explanation:
• Diagnostic criteria for diabetes mellitus (per ADA and ISPAD):
o Fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL on two separate occasions OR
o Random glucose ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms OR
o 2-hour OGTT ≥200 mg/dL OR
o Hemoglobin A1c ≥6.5%
• If any of the results were ≥200 mg/dL (random or 2-hour OGTT), that would confirm diabetes
if symptomatic.

Clinical Pearls:
• Two abnormal fasting glucose measurements ≥126 mg/dL confirm diagnosis.
• Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, and glucose ≥200 = diagnosis.
• Early testing for ketones is recommended if concern for DKA.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 697–699; ISPAD Guidelines 2022

stem:
A 3-week-old infant is brought to the emergency department with bilious vomiting, irritability,
and decreased feeding. On examination, the infant is ill-appearing, with abdominal
distension and erythema of the abdominal wall. After fluid resuscitation and stabilization,
what is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
• Bilious vomiting in a neonate is an emergency until proven otherwise.
• This scenario suggests midgut volvulus with possible bowel ischemia/perforation
(signaled by abdominal wall erythema).
• Upper GI contrast study is the gold standard diagnostic test only if the infant is stable
without peritonitis.
• In any case with signs of ischemia, perforation, or peritonitis (abdominal wall erythema,
severe tenderness, or shock), immediate surgical exploration is indicated → do not delay for
imaging.

Other options:
• Upper GI series: Used in stable patients to diagnose malrotation/volvulus.
• Upper barium study: Same as above (upper GI).
• Gastrotomy: Not indicated here.

Clinical Pearls:
• Bilious vomiting + peritonitis = emergency laparotomy.
• Delay in surgery can result in extensive bowel necrosis.
• Always stabilize first with fluids, NG decompression, and broad-spectrum antibiotics.
• Malrotation is characterized by incomplete physiologic rotation of the bowel in utero.
o Duodenal obstruction can occur due to abnormally positioned peritoneal bands;
however, this occurs more commonly in infancy (vs adulthood).
o Imaging typically shows predominantly left-sided large bowel and right-sided small
bowel.
• Volvulus occurs when an intestinal loop twists around itself and its mesentery.
o It can result in SBO, but CT scan classically reveals mesenteric twisting ("whirl sign")
as the transition point.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 496–498

stem:
A young child has had 3 days of vomiting and diarrhea. The mother has been giving apple
juice and water, which the child drinks eagerly, but he has been unable to tolerate solids or
formula. In the past 8 hours, he has become lethargic—opening his eyes spontaneously but
requiring assistance to sit upright. What is the most likely cause of his current condition?
explanation:
• Prolonged vomiting and diarrhea with free water intake (apple juice and water) and
minimal solids increase risk of dilutional hyponatremia.
• Hyponatremia causes neurologic symptoms:
o Lethargy
o Weakness
o Seizures (if severe)
• Hypoglycemia could cause lethargy but would likely have more abrupt neurologic
deterioration.
• Hypokalemia causes muscle weakness but less commonly this degree of lethargy acutely.
• Dehydration alone typically presents with tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, delayed
capillary refill—not isolated lethargy with preserved drinking.

Clinical Pearls:
• Juice and water worsen hyponatremia in diarrheal illness.
• Always consider serum sodium in lethargic infants with gastroenteritis.
• Rapid correction of hyponatremia can cause central pontine myelinolysis—correct
slowly.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 330–332

stem:
A 15-month-old child is brought for evaluation of 4 recent episodes of abnormal movements.
During the episodes, the child develops a limping posture while walking, body stiffening, and
up-rolling of the eyes. Some episodes occur during sleep or upon awakening. The mother
also reports recent vomiting. Between episodes, the child is playful and the neurologic
examination is normal.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This child most likely has Sandifer syndrome, a rare manifestation of GERD characterized by:
• Episodic dystonic posturing
• Body stiffening/opisthotonic movements
• Eye deviation
• Abnormal neck or trunk positioning
• Associated vomiting/reflux symptoms
Episodes may mimic seizures, but neurologic examination and development are usually normal.
The movements are thought to be a response to esophageal discomfort from reflux.

Other options:

77
•   B. Myoclonic seizure – Typically brief shock-like jerks, not associated with vomiting or
dystonic posturing.
•   C. Infantile spasm – Usually occurs between 3–12 months with developmental regression
and clustered flexor/extensor spasms.
•   D. Breath-holding spell – Triggered by crying or frustration, often associated with cyanosis or
pallor.

Clinical Pearls:
• Sandifer syndrome is a classic board mimic of epilepsy.
• Key clue: abnormal movements + vomiting/reflux + normal neuro exam.
• EEG is usually normal.
• Treatment of GERD leads to resolution of movements.
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1944–1945; UpToDate

stem:
An 8-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department because of spotting of blood on
her underwear and mild genital discomfort. She denies trauma or sexual activity. On
examination, there is a circumferential (“donut-shaped”) erythematous mass protruding
from the urethral opening, while the hymen and surrounding genital structures are intact.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Urethral prolapse is a classic cause of genital bleeding in prepubertal girls and
presents as a donut-shaped circumferential mass protruding from the urethral meatus. It is often
mistaken for trauma or abuse.
86
Note that an image
was provided in the
exam, but I could not
find a similar one, so
please refer to these
images.

Other options:
• B. Sexual abuse – May cause bruising, lacerations, hymenal injury, or behavioral changes,
but not a circumferential urethral lesion.
• C. Imperforate hymen – Presents with bulging bluish hymenal membrane and primary
amenorrhea in adolescents.
• D. Vaginal foreign body – Usually causes foul-smelling discharge or bleeding, not a urethral
donut-shaped mass.

Clinical Pearls:
• Typical patient: prepubertal girl with painless vaginal bleeding
• Exam finding: circular pink/red mass around urethral meatus
• Treatment: sitz baths, topical estrogen, barrier cream; surgery if persistent/severe
• Important board clue: “donut-shaped lesion” = urethral prolapse
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2788–2789; UpToDate

stem:
A 7-year-old boy is brought to clinic because of recurrent bone pain and difficulty walking. Two
months ago, he sustained a fracture after a minor fall. His parents report progressive fatigue
and decreased activity over the past year. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild
hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies show anemia, low serum bicarbonate,
hyperchloremia, and mild hypokalemia. Renal imaging demonstrates bilateral medullary
calcifications. The patient’s X-ray is shown below:




What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Osteopetrosis is caused by defective osteoclast function leading to failure of normal
bone resorption. Bones become abnormally dense but fragile, predisposing to recurrent fractures.
Marrow cavity narrowing causes bone marrow failure, resulting in anemia and
hepatosplenomegaly from extramedullary hematopoiesis.




The associated metabolic findings (hyperchloremia, low bicarbonate, hypokalemia) and
nephrocalcinosis strongly suggest an associated renal tubular acidification defect due to carbonic
anhydrase II deficiency.

Other options:
• B. Vitamin D deficiency – Causes rickets with osteopenia and bone deformities, not dense
brittle bones.
• C. Lead poisoning – May cause abdominal pain, neurocognitive symptoms, and
metaphyseal lead lines, but not diffuse bony sclerosis with fractures.
89
•   D. Hypervitaminosis D – Causes hypercalcemia and nephrocalcinosis, not generalized
skeletal sclerosis.

Clinical Pearls:
• Osteopetrosis = “marble bone disease”
• Features: recurrent fractures, anemia, hepatosplenomegaly, cranial nerve compression
• X-ray clues: diffuse sclerosis, bone-within-bone appearance, Erlenmeyer flask deformity
• Osteopetrosis + RTA + cerebral calcifications = Carbonic anhydrase II deficiency
syndrome
• Severe infantile form may require stem cell transplant
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 3567–3569; UpToDate; Orthobullets

stem:
A 7-year-old boy is brought to clinic because of loud nightly snoring, restless sleep, and daytime
irritability for the past several months. His teacher reports poor concentration in school. The parents
also noticed episodes where he seems to “stop breathing” briefly during sleep. Physical examination is
otherwise unremarkable. What is the best next step in management?
explanation:
This child has a classic presentation of pediatric obstructive sleep apnea (OSA):
• Loud snoring
• Witnessed apneas
• Restless sleep
• Daytime behavioral or school problems
• The gold standard diagnostic test for pediatric OSA is overnight polysomnography (sleep study).

Other options:
• B. Adenotonsillectomy – First-line treatment only when adenotonsillar hypertrophy is clearly present
(not mentioned in question stem).
• C. Inhaled corticosteroids – May help mild OSA associated with allergic rhinitis, but not first-line.
• D. Reassurance – Inappropriate because untreated OSA can affect behavior, growth, and
cardiovascular health.

Clinical Pearls:
• Pediatric OSA symptoms: snoring, witnessed apnea, hyperactivity, poor school performance
• Gold standard diagnosis = polysomnography
• Most common cause = adenotonsillar hypertrophy
• First-line treatment with adenotonsillar hypertrophy = adenotonsillectomy
• Obesity is an important risk factor in older children
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2070–2073;

stem:
A child presents with chronic watery rhinorrhea and frequent sneezing. Symptoms occur year-round
without clear triggers. A trial of oral antihistamines produced minimal improvement. Skin-prick allergy
testing and serum IgE levels are negative.
What is the most effective management?
explanation:
This child most likely has nonallergic rhinitis, characterized by:
• Sneezing and rhinorrhea
• Negative allergy testing
• Poor response to antihistamines
• Intranasal corticosteroids are the most effective treatment for persistent rhinitis symptoms, even
in many nonallergic cases.

112
Other options:
• B. Strict environmental avoidance – More effective in confirmed allergic rhinitis with identified
triggers.
• C. Saline irrigation – Helpful adjunct, but less effective than intranasal steroids.
• D. Oral antibiotics – No evidence of bacterial infection.

Clinical Pearls:
• Allergic rhinitis:
o Positive IgE/skin testing
o Often responds to antihistamines
• Nonallergic rhinitis:
o Negative testing
o Triggered by irritants/weather changes
• Intranasal steroids are first-line for persistent rhinitis symptoms
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1138–1140

stem:
A 6-year-old child presents with recurrent chest infections, chronic sinusitis, chronic productive
cough, and nasal polyps. What is the most appropriate diagnostic test to confirm the underlying
condition?
explanation:
This child has classic features of cystic fibrosis (CF):
• Recurrent sinopulmonary infections
• Chronic sinusitis
• Nasal polyps
• Failure to thrive/clubbing
• The diagnostic test of choice is the sweat chloride test: Chloride ≥60 mmol/L = diagnostic
• CF is caused by mutations in the CFTR gene, leading to abnormal chloride transport and thick
secretions.

Other options:
• A. Immunoglobulin levels – Evaluate humoral immunodeficiency, but nasal polyps suggest CF.
• B. Nasal endoscopy – Can identify polyps but does not confirm diagnosis.
• C. Chest X-ray – May show bronchiectasis/hyperinflation but is nonspecific.

Clinical Pearls:
• Nasal polyps in a child = think CF until proven otherwise
• Common organisms:
o Early: Staph aureus
o Older children/adolescents: Pseudomonas aeruginosa
• Other features:
o Pancreatic insufficiency
o Meconium ileus
o Male infertility
• Newborn screen: elevated immunoreactive trypsinogen (IRT)
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2465–2472; AMBOSS

stem:
The parents of a 20-month-old female bring her to your office because she has lost consciousness
twice recently. They describe two episodes where the child was crying vigorously then “turned purple
and passed out”. During some episodes, the parents notice stiffening and occasional brief seizure-like
movements. The child rapidly returns to baseline afterward and has normal development. The episodes
are not associated with fever or other symptoms. Physical examination of the child is normal.
What is the most likely diagnosis?

123
explanation:
Breath-holding spells are common benign events in toddlers, typically triggered by anger, fear,
frustration, or minor injury. The child cries → holds breath → becomes cyanotic or pale → may briefly lose
consciousness. Brief stiffening or anoxic seizure-like activity can occur due to transient cerebral hypoxia.
• These are a form of autonomic syncope frequently misdiagnosed as seizures.
• Occur in early childhood and infancy.
• They can be of two forms: cyanotic, and pallid.
o Cyanotic form usually occurs after vigorous crying
o Pallid form commonly occurs after a sudden fright or minor injury
• The history of a prodrome of injury, vigorous crying, or sudden fright is key to distinguishing a breath-
holding spell from a seizure.
• Parents can be reassured that no brain damage occurs.
• There is no diagnostic test that confirms the diagnosis of breath-holding spells but because iron
deficiency anemia is highly associated with breath-holding spells, evaluation with complete
blood count and serum ferritin is warranted in these children.

Other options:
• A. Temper tantrum – Does not cause cyanosis or LOC.
• C. Seizure – Usually not triggered by crying/emotion and lacks stereotypical sequence.
• D. Cyanotic heart disease – Cyanosis is persistent/recurrent without emotional trigger.

Clinical Pearls:
• Typical age: 6 months–6 years
• Cyanotic type = most common
• Pallid type triggered by pain/fear
• Iron deficiency anemia association is high yield
• Reassurance is main management
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 3070–3071; UpToDate: “Breath-holding spells in

stem:
A child presents with a recurrent pruritic rash for the past 2 weeks. Examination finding is shown:




What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
The image shows classic urticarial wheals: transient, raised, erythematous plaques with
central pallor. Since symptoms have been present for less than 6 weeks, this is classified as acute
urticaria.

Other options:
• Chronic idiopathic urticaria requires symptoms for >6 weeks.
• Urticarial vasculitis lesions are painful/burning, persist >24 hours, and may leave
hyperpigmentation.
• Pityriasis rosea causes oval scaly lesions along skin cleavage lines (“Christmas tree pattern”), not
transient wheals.

Clinical Pearls:
• Acute urticaria is commonly triggered by viral infections, foods, or medications.
• Individual lesions usually last <24 hours.
• First-line treatment: non-sedating antihistamines.

stem:
A 6-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to increasing difficulty keeping his eyes
open, especially toward the end of the day. They report that his eyelids droop in the evening, and he
appears to have a "sad" expression despite feeling happy. His appetite and cognitive function are
normal, and there are no signs of recent infection. On examination, he has bilateral ptosis and weak
extraocular movements that worsen with repeated testing.
What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
136
explanation:
• The clinical picture suggests ocular myasthenia gravis (fluctuating ptosis and fatigable weakness
in the absence of systemic signs)
• Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disease that causes a decreased number of functional
acetylcholine receptors within the neuromuscular junction. This reduces the number of
postsynaptic cation channels that can open in response to acetylcholine, which reduces the
amplitude of motor end plate potential and prevents muscle fiber depolarization.
• Neostigmine, an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, is the first-line diagnostic and therapeutic option
for symptomatic improvement
o It provides temporary enhancement of neuromuscular transmission by increasing ACh at
the neuromuscular junction
• If poor response, diagnosis may be confirmed via:
o Anti-AChR antibody testing
o Repetitive nerve stimulation
o Edrophonium (Tensilon) test
o CT chest (to rule out thymoma)




★ Myasthenia Gravis:
• Definition: Autoimmune disorder caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors at
the neuromuscular junction → impaired neuromuscular transmission.
• Types:
o Transient neonatal MG – due to maternal antibodies, self-limited
o Congenital MG – genetic defect in NMJ transmission
o Juvenile MG – acquired autoimmune, more common in females
• Presentation:
o Fluctuating muscle weakness, worsens with activity, improves with rest
o Ptosis, diplopia, fatigable chewing, nasal voice
o Bulbar symptoms: dysphagia, poor sucking, weak cry (infants)
o Normal reflexes and sensation
o Respiratory crisis may require ventilation
• Diagnosis:
o Edrophonium (Tensilon) test – rapid symptom improvement (used less now)
o Anti-ACh receptor antibody test – positive in most autoimmune MG




137
o EMG with decremental response to repetitive stimulation
o CT/MRI chest – evaluate for thymoma or thymic hyperplasia
• Management:
o Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors: Pyridostigmine (first-line),
neostigmine – Dose: 0.5–1 mg/kg/dose PO q4–6h
o Corticosteroids if refractory
o IVIG or plasmapheresis for myasthenic crisis
o Thymectomy in generalized MG (if thymoma or no response to
meds)

Clinical pearls:
• Infant with poor suck, weak cry, ptosis, hypotonia, worse when feeding → consider neonatal MG
• Older child with ptosis that worsens by end of day → Juvenile MG
• Symptoms often worsen with fatigue and improve with rest
• Always screen for thymic pathology (CT chest) in confirmed cases
o Up to 75% of MG patients have thymic hyperplasia.
o Around 10–15% have a thymoma (thymic tumor), even in young patients.




• In generalized MG: immunotherapy (e.g., corticosteroids, IVIG, plasmapheresis) is considered
• Avoid aminoglycosides or magnesium which may worsen symptoms
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 592; Red Book 2021–2024

stem:
A 4-year-old child presented to ER with status
asthmaticus, received full asthma management
then shifted to PICU and received continuous
salbutamol nebulization, he started to have
weakness and fatigability. ECG was provided.
The likely cause is?
explanation:
Beta-agonists like salbutamol shift K⁺ intracellularly → causes hypokalemia, especially with prolonged
use.
ECG: flat T waves, U waves, muscle weakness = classic signs.




Hypokalemia ECG:
- T-wave flattening in the limb leads
- Prominent U waves in the
precordial leads
source: Nelson – Respiratory/ECG Complications, Osama Naga 2024, Pulmonology, p. 233

stem:
A 7-year-old boy is brought to the clinic with non-blanching palpable purpura on his lower
extremities. His symptoms began a few days after completing a 10-day course of amoxicillin for otitis
media. He now complains of colicky abdominal pain and joint pain in both knees. Urinalysis
reveals microscopic hematuria and mild proteinuria. There is no fever or lymphadenopathy. Platelet
count and coagulation profile are normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This child presents with the classic tetrad of HSP: palpable purpura (w/o
thrombocytopenia), abdominal pain, arthritis/arthralgia + renal involvement (hematuria ± proteinuria)
→ Though HSP is often post-infectious, it can rarely be drug-induced (e.g., after β-lactams like amoxicillin).
- The normal platelets and IgA-predominant small vessel vasculitis distinguish it from ITP and SSLR.




Clinical Pearls:
• Target lesions without mucosal involvement → EM → HSV, Mycoplasma pneumoniae
148
•   Urticarial rash + fever + arthralgia after beta-lactams → Serum sickness-like → T3-HSR, cefaclor
•   Palpable purpura + GI pain + hematuria → HSP (IgA vasculitis)
•   Facial edema + rash + eosinophilia + hepatitis → DRESS → ASMs
•   Mucosal ulcers + skin sloughing after drugs → SJS/TEN → Sulfa drugs
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 972; AAP Red Book 2021–2024, p. 568; Canadian

stem:
A 21-day-old baby girl presents with vomiting, lethargy, and seizures unresponsive to antiepileptics,
calcium, or glucose. There is a history of 2 sibling deaths in the neonatal period. Sepsis workup is
negative, and CBC and coagulation profile are normal. No other labs were provided. What is the most
likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This presentation is consistent with a metabolic decompensation: vomiting, lethargy,
refractory seizures.
• Organic acidemias (eg, methylmalonic acidemia, propionic acidemia):
o Autosomal recessive inheritance—common in consanguineous populations (Saudi Arabia).
o Typically manifest in the first 1–3 weeks of life after protein feeding.
o Can present with severe illness before labs document metabolic acidosis if not checked.
• Urea cycle defects are possible, but:
o OTC deficiency (most common) is X-linked recessive and less likely in a female.
o Other urea cycle defects are rarer.
• Galactosemia would show liver failure, coagulopathy, hypoglycemia—labs would not be normal.
• PKU causes gradual developmental delay and irritability over months, not neonatal seizures.
Clinical pearls:
• In a neonate with:
o Neonatal encephalopathy + sibling deaths + unresponsive seizures
o No acid-base labs reported
o Female infant in a consanguineous setting (KSA)
→ Organic acidemia is statistically most likely.
• Next step: send blood gas, ammonia, lactate, urine organic acids.
• Early management: stop protein feeds, start glucose infusion, correct acidosis.
📚 Source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 176–178
☆ Inborn Errors of Metabolism – Organic Acidemias
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 176–178

stem:
Mother of a 4-year-old boy is concerned that her child clumsy gait. she told
you that he started to roll over at 6 months of age. He was able to sit at 8
months, started to stand without support at 18 months of age. fine motor
development was appropriate for age. examination shows calf hypertrophy
and proximal muscle weakness. The child has a maternal uncle who had
inability to walk as well and died at the age of 15. What is the most likely
diagnosis?
explanation:
Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) is
an X-linked recessive disorder caused by
mutations in the dystrophin gene.
Affected boys typically develop proximal
muscle weakness around age 2–3, with
the lower extremities involved first.
Classic findings include Gower’s sign and
calf pseudohypertrophy. Creatine kinase
levels are usually elevated to more than
10 times normal. Diagnosis is confirmed
with genetic testing, though muscle
biopsy may be needed in rare case

stem:
A child presented with polyuria, polydipsia, and headache. Which of
the following would confirm the diagnosis:

Overweight adolescent BMI =30 which is above 97 centile her with RBS
=170. Next step:

options.label: B
options.text: Advise to lose weight and follow up in one month

options.label: C
options.text: Start metformin and see him after 2weeks

answer: A. Chick for fasting blood sugar
explanation:
Patient should have further testing
for diabetes mellitus. Current
recommendations for diagnosing
diabetes mellitus are based on either
fasting glucose level or a 2-hour 75-g
oral glucose tolerance test. A
random blood glucose level > 11.1
mmol/L is also diagnostic of diabetes
mellitus in patients with symptoms
of hyperglycemia. If unequivocal
hyperglycemia is not present, the
diagnosis must be confirmed by
testing on another .

stem:
DKA admitted to PICU already, currently has CNS symptoms, high blood
glucose [20mg/dl], currently complains of headache and (?? altered
mental status), most likely cause?
explanation:
• what is the risk factors to develop cerebral edema?
Initial very low Pco2 (most risk)Treatment in NaHCO3 (most risk)Elevate BUN.
(most risk)Severe DKA Young age group Newly diagnosis DM Aggressive fast
initial therapy Elevated creatinine Na not rising by 1.6 mmol/L for every 100
mg drop in RBS
• What is the earliest and latest sign of brain edema
Lethargy - headache consider early sign
Cushin triad consider late sign

stem:
When reviewing the chemistry panel of a newly diagnosed patient with
acute lymphoblastic leukemia who is lethargic, complaining of flank
pain, and experiencing nausea and vomiting, which of the following
you expect to see? (Tumor lysis syndrome )labs in newly diagnosed ALL.
What do you expect?
explanation:
The classic triad of tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) includes:
• Hyperuricemia
• Hyperkalemia
• Hyperphosphatemia (which leads to secondary hypocalcemia)
• These metabolic abnormalities result from rapid tumor cell
breakdown and release of intracellular

stem: Infant of diabetic mother with controlled A1c. Most likely finding?
explanation:
• Women with type I diabetes who have tight glucose control during
pregnancy (average daily glucose levels <95 mg/dL.) deliver infants
with birthweights and anthropomorphic features similar to those of
infants of nondiabetic mothers.

stem:
Infant of mother with Sjögren
syndrome. Skin manifestation shown
(pic attached, dsicoid lupus) , labs of
high LFT and positive Anti Ro
antibody (anti SS) Picture .. what is
the most appropriate next step in
management?
explanation:
• The clinical manifestations of NSLE are
wide ranging . The classic NSLE rash is
characterized by elliptical
erythematous papulosquamous
lesions with central clearing, annular
erythema or dyspigmentation, and a
fine scale, which worsens with
exposure to ultraviolet light. The rash
becomes apparent during the first 3
months after birth an commonly
spontaneously resolves after maternal
antibodies disappear at 6 to 8 months
of age.

stem:
Newborn with multiple petechiae and his PLT 2000, mother PLT 370K.
What to give?

options.label: A
options.text: transfusion of washed irradiated maternal platelets

options.label: D
options.text: Observe as no need for medication

answer: A. transfusion of washed irradiated maternal platelets
explanation:
Pathophysiology: Caused by maternal IgG antibodies against fetal
platelet antigens (most commonly HPA-1a) inherited from the father.
Leads to peripheral platelet destruction in the newborn.
Presentation: Unlike Rh disease, can occur in the first pregnancy.
Newborn typically has petechiae, purpura, or mucosal bleeding at
birth.
ICH risk: 10–20%, so prompt treatment is essential.
Treatment:
First choice: transfuse maternal HPA-1a–negative platelets.

stem: Kawasaki Disease (KD) – treatment?
explanation:
• IVIG
• 2 g/kg IV once (standard
treatment).
• Delay live vaccines (MMR,
varicella) for 11 months after this
dose.
• Aspirin:
• High-dose during acute
inflammation.
• Low-dose afterward for
antiplatelet effect until coronaries
normalize.

A child presented with pain while touching targus, ottorhea,
edematous canal. Tympanic membrane bulging (was not visualised)
tender LN. Most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Infection of the external auditory canal causing severe ear pain
worsened by tragus/pinna traction, otorrhea, and canal edema, often
after water exposure due to loss of the protective cerumen barrier.
Usual pathogens are Pseudomonas and Staph aureus; Aspergillus less
commonly. Risk factors include swimming, humidity, sweating, trauma
(cotton swabs), and skin disease. Unlike otitis media, OE has no URI
symptoms and shows localized canal tenderness without middle-ear
findings. Treatment is canal cleaning plus topical antibiotic–steroid
drops for up to 14 days; refer to ENT if symptoms persist or canal
visualization is limited.

stem:
A 28-week preterm infant, now 3 weeks old, is receiving 100
kcal/kg/day from preterm formula(24 kcal/30 mL) and is on diuretics
and caffeine for PDA. His weight gain is only 5 g/day. What is the most
likely cause of this poor weight gain?

options.label: B
options.text: Preterm has twice metabolic rate compared to term

options.label: C
options.text: Diuretic losing glucose and protein in the urine

explanation:
Preterm infants have significantly higher nutritional requirements
than term infants. A caloric
intake of 100 kcal/kg/day is inadequate for a growing 28-week
premature infant, who typically requires:
• 110–130 kcal/kg/day
• Sometimes higher if chronic lung disease or PDA is present
• Expected weight gain for preterm infants is approximately: 15–
20 g/kg/day
• This infant’s gain of only 5 g/day strongly suggests insufficient
caloric intake.

A 6-year-old child with cystic fibrosis is receiving pancreatic enzyme
replacement therapy at a dose of lipase 2500 units/day. During followup, Her BMI percentile has decreased from the 60th to the 40th
percentile. She has no signs of acute illness. What is the most
appropriate next step in management?

options.label: A
options.text: Surgical insertion of a gastrostomy tube (GT)

options.label: C
options.text: Comprehensive nutritional review with increased calorie intake

answer: C. Comprehensive nutritional review with increased calorie intake
explanation:
• A fall in BMI percentile—without acute illness—almost always reflects
insufficient calories, not enzyme failure.
• Before escalating therapy, CF guidelines recommend a full nutritional
assessment, including:
o Daily caloric intake
oFat-soluble vitamin intake
o Feeding behavior
o Stool pattern
oEnzyme adherence and timing

A 5-year-old child is brought to the clinic with 3 days of fever,
vomiting, and watery diarrhea. The parents also report that he had
sore throat and eye redness. On examination, you find conjunctival
injection, erythematous throat with small spots on the soft palate, and
mild dehydration. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Common presentation: Febrile or afebrile exudative pharyngitis, often in warm
months.
Classic triad:Exudative pharyngitis, Nonpurulent conjunctivitis,
Preauricular/anterior cervical lymphadenopathy
Systemic symptoms: Headache, malaise, abdominal pain, anorexia.
Other organ involvement (less common): Pneumonia, bronchitis, Myocarditis
,Diarrhea ,Acute hemorrhagic cystitis(serotype 2,11,21), Keratoconjunctivitis
,Meningitis/encephalitis
Course: Usually self-limited; severe disease in immunocompromised children.
Transmission: Respiratory droplets, fecal–oral, fomites.
Treatment: Supportive; acyclovir only for severe disease or immunocompromised
hosts.
