stem:
Asthmatic patient, distressed, ER wants to perform intubation . Best
medication to use?

A child presents with fever, migratory joint pain, and a recent history of untreated streptococcal
pharyngitis. You are evaluating the patient using the Jones criteria for acute rheumatic fever
(RF). Which of the following is a major criterion of rheumatic fever?
explanation:
The major Jones criteria for acute rheumatic fever are:
• Migratory polyarthritis
• Carditis
• Sydenham chorea
• Erythema marginatum
• Subcutaneous nodules
Minor criteria include:
• Fever
• Arthralgia
• Elevated ESR/CRP
• Prolonged PR interval

★ Acute Rheumatic Fever:
• Autoimmune complication 2–4 weeks after GAS
pharyngitis
• Pathophysiology:
o Autoimmune cross-reaction triggered
by GAS pharyngitis
o Molecular mimicry: antibodies against GAS M
protein cross-react with host tissues (heart, joints, CNS, skin)
o Leads to inflammatory lesions such as Aschoff bodies in the heart and immune-
mediated damage to tissues
• Requires evidence of recent streptococcal infection (positive culture or elevated ASO)
• Diagnosis: 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor criteria + evidence of recent streptococcal
infection




52
o Major Jones Criteria (JONES):
§ Joints – Migratory arthritis (polyarthritis in 60 –75%)
§ ♡ Carditis – Often mitral or aortic regurgitation
§ Nodules – Subcutaneous over extensor surfaces
§ Erythema marginatum – Pink, serpiginous, non-pruritic
evanescent/migratory rash with central clearing
§ Sydenham chorea – Involuntary, purposeless movements
(video)
o Minor Criteria:
§ Fever
§ Arthralgia
§ Prolonged PR interval
§ Elevated ESR or CRP (↑ acute phase
reactants)
o Note: erythema marginatum and subcutaneous
nodules are rare presentations, but are characteristic
of ARF
•   Treatment:
o Monthly IM benzathine penicillin G or daily oral
penicillin
o Aspirin or NSAIDs for arthritis
o Steroids for carditis with CHF
o Valproic acid or carbamazepine for chorea
o Secondary prophylaxis: 60% of patients with ARF progress to rheumatic heart
disease with the endocardium, pericardium, and myocardium all potentially
involved. Thus, all patients with ARF should receive antibiotics sufficient to treat the
precipitating GAS infection.
§ Without carditis: 5 years or until age 21 (whichever is longer)
§ With carditis: 10 years or until age 21 (whichever is longer)
§ With carditis + valve disease: 10 years or until age 40 (whichever is longer)




53
Clinical pearls:
• Rheumatic fever typically occurs 2–4 weeks after strep pharyngitis
• Migratory arthritis is the most common initial major manifestation
• Carditis most commonly affects the mitral valve, leading to mitral regurgitation in the
early stage, followed by mitral stenosis in the late stage.
• Prompt treatment of strep pharyngitis with penicillin prevents rheumatic fever
• Long-term secondary prophylaxis is required to prevent recurrence and carditis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 529–530; Osama Naga Pediatric Board

stem:
A 4-year-old boy is brought to clinic because of a clumsy gait and frequent falls. His mother
reports delayed gross motor milestones:
• Rolled over at 6 months
• Sat unsupported at 8 months
• Stood without support at 18 months

His fine motor and language development are normal. On examination, he has calf
hypertrophy, waddling gait, and proximal muscle weakness. Gowers sign is positive.
Family history reveals that his maternal uncle lost the ability to walk and died at age 15.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This child has classic features of Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD):
• X-linked recessive inheritance → affected maternal uncle
• Onset before age 5
• Delayed motor milestones
• Proximal muscle weakness
• Calf pseudohypertrophy
• Progressive loss of ambulation, usually by age 12
• Death in teenage years from cardiomyopathy or respiratory failure if untreated
DMD is caused by mutations in the dystrophin gene leading to absent dystrophin.

Other options:
• B. Guillain-Barré syndrome – Acute ascending weakness with areflexia, not chronic
progressive weakness or calf hypertrophy.
• C. Viral myositis – Acute muscle pain/tenderness after viral illness, not progressive
inherited weakness.
• D. Becker muscular dystrophy – Milder dystrophinopathy with later onset and slower
progression.

Clinical Pearls:
• DMD: markedly elevated CK levels early in disease
• Confirm with genetic testing ± muscle biopsy
• Common findings: Gowers sign, lordosis, toe walking
• Cardiomyopathy and restrictive lung disease are major complications
• Steroids (prednisone/deflazacort) slow progression
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 3197–3201;

stem:
An overweight adolescent girl with a BMI of 30 kg/m² (>97th percentile) is found to have a
random blood glucose (RBS) of 170 mg/dL during a clinic visit. She is otherwise
asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.label: B
options.text: Advise weight loss and follow up in one month

options.label: C
options.text: Start metformin and review after 2 weeks

explanation:
A random blood glucose of 170 mg/dL in an asymptomatic adolescent is abnormal but not
diagnostic of diabetes mellitus. The next step is to perform a confirmatory diagnostic test, most
commonly:
• Fasting plasma glucose (FPG)
• HbA1c
• Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT)
Diagnostic criteria for diabetes:
• FPG ≥126 mg/dL
• HbA1c ≥6.5%
• Random glucose ≥200 mg/dL with symptoms
• 2-hour OGTT glucose ≥200 mg/dL

71
Other options:
• B. Advise weight loss only – Important, but diagnosis must first be clarified.
• C. Start metformin – Treatment should not begin before confirming diabetes.
• D. Diagnose T2DM immediately – RBS 170 alone in an asymptomatic child is insufficient
for diagnosis.

Clinical Pearls:
• Obese adolescents should be screened for type 2 diabetes starting at age 10 or puberty if
risk factors are present.
• Common associated findings: acanthosis nigricans, hypertension, dyslipidemia.
• Type 2 DM is increasingly common in adolescents with obesity.
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2895–2898;

stem:
A father of a 5-year-old child is planning to install a swimming pool at home. He asks about
the most effective measure to prevent drowning accidents in children. What is the best
recommendation?

options.label: A
options.text: Install a fence with self-latching gates around the pool

options.label: B
options.text: Enroll the child in professional swimming lessons

options.label: C
options.text: Tell the child not to swim without adult supervision

options.label: D
options.text: Use flotation devices and pool floats

answer: A. Install a fence with self-latching gates around the pool
explanation:
The most effective intervention to prevent pediatric drowning is four-sided fencing with self-
closing and self-latching gates surrounding the pool. This significantly reduces unsupervised
access and lowers drowning risk by more than 50%.

Other options:
• B. Swimming lessons – Helpful but do not replace physical barriers or supervision.
• C. Verbal instructions – Young children cannot reliably appreciate danger.
• D. Pool floats/flotation devices – Not reliable safety devices and may create false
reassurance.

Clinical Pearls:
• Drowning is a leading cause of accidental death in children ages 1–4 years.
• Recommended pool safety measures:
o Four-sided fencing
o Constant adult supervision
o CPR-trained caregivers
o Life jackets for open water
• Inflatable arm bands and floats are not substitutes for approved life jackets.
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 389–390;

stem:
A 8-year-old boy is brought to clinic because of repetitive barking sounds, lip smacking, and
intermittent motor tics. His parents report that he occasionally interrupts conversations with
these behaviors, but he can briefly suppress them voluntarily. He is doing well academically,
has good peer relationships, and participates normally in sports and social activities.
What is the most appropriate management?
explanation:
This child has mild tic disorder/Tourette syndrome with minimal functional impairment. The
best initial management is education and reassurance of parents, teachers, and the child,
along with monitoring. Treatment is only indicated if tics cause:
• Social impairment
• Pain/injury
• Emotional distress
• Academic dysfunction

Other options:
• B. Methylphenidate – Used for ADHD; stimulants may worsen tics in some children.
• C. Fluoxetine (SSRI) – Used for comorbid OCD/anxiety, not isolated mild tics.
• D. Reassure – Helpful but incomplete; education of family and school is essential.

Clinical Pearls:
• Tourette syndrome = multiple motor tics + ≥1 vocal tic for >1 year.
• Tics are often suppressible and worsen with stress/excitement.
• Most children improve during adolescence.
• First-line for bothersome tics: behavioral therapy (CBIT).
• Medications (guanfacine, clonidine, antipsychotics) reserved for significant impairment.
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 3096–3099;

stem:
A 6-month-old infant presents with recurrent otitis media, sinusitis, chronic productive
cough, and multiple episodes of bacterial pneumonia requiring hospitalization. Physical
examination is otherwise unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely underlying
immunodeficiency?
answer: D. Absent IgG with low immunoglobulins
explanation:
This infant has recurrent sinopulmonary bacterial infections, which are classic for
a humoral (B-cell/antibody) immunodeficiency, such as:
• X-linked agammaglobulinemia (Bruton disease)
• Common variable immunodeficiency (later onset)

73
Maternal IgG wanes around 6 months, which is why symptoms often begin at this age.

Other options:
• A. Lymphopenia – Suggests T-cell deficiency (e.g., SCID), which presents with viral, fungal,
and opportunistic infections.
• B. Absent CD18 – Leukocyte adhesion deficiency; causes delayed cord separation, poor
wound healing, absent pus formation.
• C. Defective oxidative burst – Chronic granulomatous disease (CGD); presents with
recurrent deep abscesses and catalase-positive organisms.

Clinical Pearls:
• Recurrent encapsulated bacterial infections → think antibody deficiency.
• XLA presents after 6 months with:
o Low/absent B cells
o Very low immunoglobulins
o Small/absent tonsils
• Common organisms:
o Streptococcus pneumoniae
o Haemophilus influenzae
• Initial workup:
o Quantitative IgG, IgA, IgM
o Vaccine antibody titers
o Flow cytometry for B cells (CD19/CD20)
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1095–1099;

stem:
A 10-month-old infant is brought to the emergency department with 3 days of vomiting and
diarrhea. The mother reports giving him mostly apple juice and water, which he drinks eagerly,
but he refuses baby food and solids. Over the past 8 hours, he has become increasingly
lethargic. On examination, he opens his eyes spontaneously but requires assistance to sit.
Mucous membranes are dry and capillary refill is mildly prolonged. Which of the following is
most likely to be present?
explanation:
This child most likely has hyponatremic dehydration, which occurs when sodium losses from
vomiting and diarrhea are replaced with hypotonic fluids such as water and apple juice. During
gastroenteritis, children lose both water and electrolytes, especially sodium. If these losses are
replaced with fluids that contain little sodium, excess free water accumulates and dilutes the
serum sodium concentration, leading to hyponatremia. Apple juice is particularly inappropriate
because it is low in sodium and high in carbohydrates, which may also worsen osmotic diarrhea.

The progression to lethargy and inability to sit independently suggests neurologic involvement from
cerebral edema, which occurs when low serum sodium causes water to shift into brain cells. This
neurologic deterioration is a classic clue pointing toward hyponatremia rather than simple
74
dehydration alone. While dehydration is present, the history of excessive hypotonic fluid intake
makes hyponatremia the most likely associated abnormality. Hypokalemia can occur with diarrhea
but more commonly causes muscle weakness or ileus, and hypoglycemia is less likely given the
intake of carbohydrate-containing juice.

Other options:
• A. Hypokalemia – Common in diarrhea, but usually causes muscle weakness, ileus, or
arrhythmias rather than acute neurologic decline.
• B. Hypoglycemia – Possible in prolonged poor intake, but less likely given the excessive
carbohydrate intake from juice.
• C. Dehydration – Present, but does not fully explain the neurologic deterioration; the fluid
type strongly suggests sodium imbalance.

★ Hyponatremia
• Definition:
o Serum sodium <135 mEq/L
o Mild: 130-134 mEq/L | Moderate: 120-129 mEq/L | Severe: <125 mEq/L
• Sodium reflects water balance more than total body sodium
• Serum osmolality classification:
o Hypotonic hyponatremia = true
hyponatremia (most common)
o Isotonic hyponatremia =
pseudohyponatremia
o Hypertonic hyponatremia = hyperglycemia,
mannitol
• Volume status classification:
o Hypovolemic:
§ GI losses
§ Diuretics
§ Adrenal insufficiency
o Euvolemic:
§ SIADH
§ CNS infections
§ Hypothyroidism
§ Psychogenic polydipsia
o Hypervolemic:
§ CHF
§ Nephrotic syndrome
§ Liver failure
• Pathophysiology: low Na → low plasma osmolality → water shifts intracellularly → brain
swelling → cerebral edema
• Symptoms depend on severity & rapidity of onset
o Mild symptoms: nausea, headache, weakness, irritability
o Severe symptoms: vomiting, altered mental status, lethargy, seizures, coma,
respiratory arrest
• Acute hyponatremia (<48 hrs) is more dangerous with higher risk of cerebral
edema/herniation
• Chronic hyponatremia: brain adapts → symptoms may be milder
• SIADH clues: euvolemia + low serum osmolality + concentrated urine + high urine Na
o Causes: meningitis, pneumonia, CNS disease, Carbamazepine
• Acute hyponatremia (<48 hr): higher risk of cerebral edema/herniation
• Pseudohyponatremia: hyperglycemia (DKA), hyperlipidemia, hyperproteinemia

75
o   Hyperglycemia effect: Na ↓ ~1.6 mEq/L for every 100 mg/dL (5.6 mmol/L) ↑ in
glucose
•   Investigations: serum Na, serum/urine osmolality, urine sodium, glucose, volume status
•   Treatment:
o Treat underlying cause
o Hypovolemic → normal saline
o SIADH → fluid restriction
o Severe/symptomatic: 3% hypertonic saline 3–5 mL/kg over 10–20 min
•   Goal of correction:
o Raise Na enough to stop seizures/symptoms (4 to 6 mEq/L is usually sufficient)
o Avoid rapid overcorrection! Maximum correction: 8–10 mEq/L/day
o Every 1 mL/kg of NaCl will raise Na level by almost 1 mEq/L
o The dose can be repeated every 10 to 20 minutes.
o Increasing the serum sodium concentration by only 4 to 6 mEq/L is usually sufficient
to stop hyponatremic seizures.
•   Major complications: cerebral edema (under-correction concern), osmotic demyelination
syndrome
•   Rapid correction → osmotic demyelination syndrome:
o Usually with chronic hyponatremia (>48 hours)
o Comorbid factors; severe liver disease, hypoxemia, hypokalemia, chronic thiazide
use.
o Symptoms: mutism, dysarthria, spastic quadriplegia, pseudobulbar palsy, and
ataxia → “Locked-in syndrome”
o Can be diagnosed by brain MRI x14 days post correction.




76
Clinical Pearls:
• Most dangerous complication of hyponatremia = cerebral edema
• Most common pediatric cause = GI losses replaced with hypotonic fluids
• SIADH = hyponatremia + euvolemia + concentrated urine
• Hyperglycemia causes pseudohyponatremia
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 399–401;

stem:
A 5-year-old child presents with progressive pallor and hepatosplenomegaly since infancy.
There is no history of acute illness, jaundice, or bleeding. Physical examination confirms
marked pallor and significant enlargement of the liver and spleen. Growth is mildly affected.
What is the best diagnostic test?
explanation:
This presentation is classic for thalassemia major, which presents with:
• Chronic severe anemia from infancy
• Hepatosplenomegaly due to extramedullary hematopoiesis
• Progressive pallor and growth delay
• The best diagnostic test is hemoglobin electrophoresis, showing increased HbF and
HbA1c, and absent/markedly reduced HbA

Other options:
• A. Peripheral blood smear – May show target cells and microcytosis, but is not diagnostic.
• B. Bone marrow biopsy – Not first-line; invasive and unnecessary initially.
• D. Serum ferritin – Evaluates iron stores; helps differentiate iron deficiency but does not
diagnose hemoglobinopathy.

★ Thalassemia
◦ Definition: inherited disorder with ↓ globin chain synthesis → ineffective erythropoiesis +
hemolysis
◦ alpha thalassemia = ⍺-globin, beta thalassemia = β-globin
◦ hemoglobin chains → 4 types in different combinations: ⍺, β, ɣ, δ → all Hb chains have ⍺
globin, the other chain determines the type
◦ HbF (Fetal) → ⍺⍺-ɣɣ → ɣ chain synthesis is only predominant in utero
◦ HbA (Adult, >95%) → ⍺⍺-ββ → β chain synthesis ↑↑ at 3-6 months (switch to HbA)
◦ HbA2 (Adult, <5%) → ⍺⍺-δδ
◦ diagnosis via Hb electrophoresis → done for microcytic anemia + no iron deficiency



78
79
Clinical Pearls:
• β-thalassemia major usually presents after 6 months when HbF declines.
• Smear findings: target cells, anisopoikilocytosis, nucleated RBCs.
• Complications:
o Iron overload
o Bone deformities (“crew-cut skull”)
o Growth failure
• Management:
o Regular transfusions
o Iron chelation
o Possible stem cell transplant
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2350–2354;

stem:
A 6-year-old child presents with ear pain, fever, and irritability for 2 days. Otoscopic
examination reveals a bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane with decreased mobility.
What is the most common causative organism of acute otitis media?
explanation:
Among the listed options, Moraxella catarrhalis is the most common cause of acute otitis media.
Although Streptococcus pneumoniae is overall the most common organism in AOM, it is not
listed here.

Other options:
• A. Haemophilus influenzae type b – Hib is now uncommon due to vaccination; non-typeable
strains are more relevant.
• C. Staphylococcus aureus – Rare cause of uncomplicated AOM.
• D. Group B Streptococcus – Primarily neonatal pathogen.
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 3395–3397;

stem:
A child presents with decreased urine output, hematuria, petechiae, and purpuric rash one
week after an episode of gastroenteritis with bloody diarrhea. Laboratory evaluation shows
hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury. Peripheral blood smear is
attached.




80
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This is the classic triad of Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS):
• Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (schistocytes)
• Thrombocytopenia
• Acute kidney injury
Typical HUS occurs after infection with Shiga toxin–producing E. coli (EHEC O157:H7), usually
following bloody diarrhea.

Other options:
• B. ITP – Causes isolated thrombocytopenia without AKI or hemolytic anemia.
• C. HSP – Can cause hematuria and purpura, but thrombocytopenia is absent and
schistocytes are not seen.
• D. DIC – Causes schistocytes and thrombocytopenia but usually occurs with severe
sepsis/shock and abnormal coagulation profile.

★ Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS):
• Most common cause of AKI in children in developed countries
• Classic triad:
o Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia
o Thrombocytopenia
o Acute kidney injury (↑ BUN, Cr)
• Preceded by:
o Shiga-toxin-producing E. coli (STEC) infection (E. coli O157:H7)
o Bloody diarrhea 5–10 days prior to onset
• Key lab findings:
o ↓ Hemoglobin, ↓ Platelets
o ↑ LDH, ↑ Bilirubin (hemolysis)
o ↑ BUN, ↑ Creatinine (AKI)
o Schistocytes on blood smear
o Normal PT/PTT (distinguishes from DIC)
o Urinalysis: Hematuria, proteinuria, RBC casts
• Management:
o Supportive care is mainstay
o Careful fluid & electrolyte management
o Dialysis if needed (up to 50% require it temporarily)
81
o Avoid antibiotics and antimotility agents (can worsen toxin release)
o Do NOT give platelet transfusion unless there is life-threatening bleeding
•   Atypical HUS: not related to infection, may be complement-mediated → needs eculizumab
•   Prognosis: most recover fully, but some develop chronic kidney disease




Clinical pearls:
• Classic triad of HUS:
o Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (Smear: schistocytes/helmet cells)
o Thrombocytopenia
o Acute kidney injury
• Most common cause of AKI in children in developed countries
• Labs: ↑LDH, ↑bilirubin, ↓haptoglobin, Platelets low, coagulation studies usually normal
• Management is supportive: monitor fluid balance, blood pressure, and renal function
•   Avoid antibiotics and antimotility agents in EHEC infection
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2520–2524; UWorld for the USMLE, AMBOSS

stem:
A mother brings her 2-month-old infant for a well-baby visit. The infant is exclusively
breastfed. She reports that she recently:
•   Stopped using a pacifier
•   Uses a wedge in the crib to elevate the baby
•   Places padded bumpers/blankets around the crib railing
She asks which practice is known to reduce the risk of Sudden Infant Death Syndrome
(SIDS).

options.label: A
options.text: Using a wedge in the bed

explanation:
Exclusive or partial breastfeeding reduces the risk of SIDS and is strongly recommended by the
AAP. The protective effect is thought to be related to improved arousal mechanisms and reduced
infections.

Other options:
• A. Wedge in the bed – Not recommended; increases risk of suffocation and positional
asphyxia.
• B. Padded crib bumpers – Contraindicated; associated with suffocation and entrapment.

82
•   D. Avoiding pacifier use – Incorrect; pacifier use during sleep is associated with reduced
SIDS risk.

Clinical Pearls:
• Safe sleep recommendations:
o Supine sleeping position (“Back to sleep”)
o Firm mattress
o No loose blankets, pillows, wedges, or bumpers
o Room-sharing without bed-sharing
o Pacifier at sleep time after breastfeeding established
• Highest SIDS risk age: 2–4 months
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 822–823; AAP Safe Sleep Guidelines 2022

stem:
A 3-year-old partially vaccinated child is brought to the emergency department with high
fever, drooling, muffled voice, inspiratory stridor, and respiratory distress. The child is
sitting leaning forward and refuses to lie down. Which type of vaccine could have prevented
this presentation?
explanation:
This presentation is classic for acute epiglottic inflammation. Epiglottitis in children
is classically caused by Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib), historically prevented by the Hib
vaccine, which is a conjugate vaccine. Conjugate vaccines link polysaccharide antigens to a
protein carrier, allowing infants and young children to mount an effective T-cell–dependent
immune response.

Other options:
• A. Killed – Examples: IPV, hepatitis A.
• B. Live attenuated – Examples: MMR, varicella, rotavirus.
• C. Toxoid – Examples: tetanus and diphtheria vaccines.

Clinical Pearls:
• Epiglottitis: drooling + tripod position + muffled voice
• Avoid agitating the child; secure airway first.
• Hib also causes:
o Meningitis
o Cellulitis
o Septic arthritis
• Hib vaccine dramatically reduced pediatric epiglottitis




83
Vaccine Types:
• Live attenuated: MMR, varicella, rotavirus, OPV →
contraindicated in immunocompromised patients
• Killed/Inactivated: IPV, hepatitis A, rabies, IM
influenza → safer in immunocompromised
• Toxoid: diphtheria, tetanus → inactivated toxins
• Conjugate: Hib, pneumococcal (PCV),
meningococcal → best for encapsulated organisms in
infants
• Subunit/Recombinant: HPV, hepatitis B, acellular
pertussis
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1432–

stem:
A child presents with a history of a chronic dry scaly rash involving the face and extensor
surfaces presents with a new rash over the right wrist. Examination shows a scaly, crusted,
oozing plaque with surrounding erythema. The rash has not responded to emollients and
shows only minimal improvement with moderate-potency topical steroids.




What is the best next step in management?
explanation:
This child likely has atopic dermatitis with secondary bacterial infection (impetiginization),
most commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus. Clues include:

84
•  Chronic eczema history
•  Oozing/crusting lesions
•  Poor response to topical steroids and moisturizers
Superinfected eczema commonly requires anti-staphylococcal therapy (e.g., cephalexin).

Other options:
• A. High-potency topical steroids – May worsen untreated bacterial infection.
• C. Observation – Inappropriate due to signs of secondary infection.
• D. Topical permethrin – Used for scabies; would expect intense nocturnal pruritus and
household spread.

Clinical Pearls:
• Atopic dermatitis commonly affects:
o Infants: face/extensors
o Older children: flexural areas
• Signs of bacterial superinfection: honey-colored crusting, oozing, rapid worsening
• Most common organism: Staphylococcus aureus
• Treatment:
o Emollients
o Topical steroids for inflammation
o Antibiotics if infected

★ Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis):
• Associated with asthma, allergic rhinitis, atopy (“atopic march”)
• Pathophysiology:
o Skin barrier dysfunction (filaggrin defect)
o Immune dysregulation
o Increased transepidermal water loss
• Distribution by age:
o Infants: face, scalp, extensor surfaces
o Children: flexural surfaces (antecubital/popliteal fossae)
o Adolescents/adults: flexural + hands
• Hallmark features: pruritus, xerosis, erythematous scaly plaques, lichenification
• Common triggers: soaps, wool, heat, sweating, allergens, infections
• Secondary bacterial infection clues: oozing, crusting, pustules, sudden worsening,
honey-colored crusts
o Most common infecting organism: Staphylococcus aureus
• Treatment: emollients (mainstay), topical steroids, tacrolimus/pimecrolimus for
face/flexures, antihistamines for itch, antibiotics if infected
• Eczema herpeticum: HSV infection, fever, punched-out vesicles → treat with acyclovir
• Board pearls: pruritus precedes rash, flexural eczema in children is classic, moisturizers are
first-line, avoid high-potency steroids on face/genitals




85
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1164–1168; UpToDate: “Treatment of atopic

stem:
A 15-year-old adolescent girl presents with significant weight loss over several months. She
reports excessive exercise, severe restriction of food intake, and intense fear of gaining weight
despite appearing underweight. On examination, her BMI is 16 kg/m², temperature is 35.8°C,
heart rate is 54 bpm, and blood pressure drops from 94/60 mmHg supine to 73/48 mmHg
standing. Which of the following findings is the most immediate indication for hospital
admission?
explanation:
• Anorexia nervosa is characterized by low body weight (BMI<18.5 kg/m2), fear of becoming
fat, and distorted body image. Unlike other patterns of restrictive eating (eg, bulimia
nervosa), patients with anorexia nervosa experience complications of progressive clinical
starvation, such as bradycardia, osteoporosis, and amenorrhea.
• This is a classic presentation of anorexia nervosa: weight loss, bradycardia, hypothermia,
lanugo hair, and amenorrhea
• Bradycardia (HR 40) is common due to increased vagal tone and electrolyte disturbances
• Patients are at risk for prolonged QT due to hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and
malnutrition → Risk of torsades de pointes and sudden death



91
ECG in a patient with                                                                                    How to read QT interval on ECG:
- Identify lead II or V5/V6 — use the one
anorexia showing severe                                                                                    with a clearly defined T-wave
- Measure QT (in ms): From start of QRS
to end of T wave (not the U wave)
- Measure RR interval (peak of one R to
next R), in seconds
- Apply Bazett’s formula (corrected QT):
QTc = QT / √RR
→ QTc < 440 ms (<2.2 large boxes) = normal
Remember in an ECG:                                                                                       → QTc 440–460 ms = borderline
1 small box = 0.04 sec (40 ms), 1 large box = 0.20 sec (200 ms) = 5 small boxes, 5 large boxes = 1 sec    → QTc > 460 ms = prolonged (in M&F)
→ QTc ≥ 500 ms = high risk for torsades


Clinical pearls:
• Anorexia nervosa can lead to cardiac complications,
including bradycardia, hypotension, QT prolongation, and pericardial effusion
• EKG is essential in evaluation — check for QTc >460–
470 ms (>2.3 big boxes)
• Cardiac instability (bradycardia, prolonged QT,
orthostasis) = indication for inpatient admission
• Monitor electrolytes and initiate nutritional
rehabilitation cautiously to avoid refeeding syndrome
• Right axis deviation is not a typical feature in anorexia
and would be more suggestive of congenital or
pulmonary cardiac pathology
• Short PR interval – more commonly seen in pre-
excitation syndromes (e.g., WPW)
• Left ventricular hypertrophy – rare in malnourished patients, more in athletes or HTN
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 395

stem:
A 30-month-old boy is brought for a well-child visit. His parents are concerned about his
language development because a cousin was recently diagnosed with autism spectrum
disorder. The child follows commands, plays appropriately, and interacts with family members.
Which of the following findings is abnormal for his age and warrants referral for further speech
and language evaluation?

options.label: A
options.text: The child cannot converse using two back-and-forth exchanges

options.label: B
options.text: The child is unable to answer simple questions

options.label: C
options.text: The child is unable to make two-word sentences

options.label: D
options.text: The child is unable to talk about his day

answer: C. The child is unable to make two-word sentences
explanation:
By age 2 years (24 months), children should be able to:
92
•   Use 2-word phrases (“want milk,” “mommy go”)
•   Have at least ~50 words
•   Follow 2-step commands
Failure to form spontaneous two-word phrases by 24 months is a major speech-language red flag
and warrants further evaluation.

Other options:
• A. Two back-and-forth conversational exchanges – Expected later, around 3–4 years.
• B. Answering simple questions – Develops more consistently after age 3.
• D. Talking about their day – More appropriate for preschool/school-age children.

Clinical Pearls:
• Always assess:
o Hearing
o Social interaction
o Autism features
• Autism clues:
o Poor eye contact
o Lack of joint attention
o Restricted/repetitive behaviors

Red flags in development:
Board Buzzwords for Autism:
§ 9 months: not sitting alone
-    No response to name
§ 12 months: not passing objects hand to hand, no babbling                      -    Limited joint attention
§ 15 months: abnormal pincer grasp, no single words                             -    Echoing speech (echolalia)

§ 18 months: not walking alone                                                  -    Sensory sensitivity
(e.g., avoids textures/sounds)
§ 24 months: not saying words/ two-word phrases                                 ``


§   Hearing assessment: Should be done by 3 months.
§   Neonatal breast hypertrophy (Gynecomastia): Resolves at 1 year.
§   Teeth eruption: Normal up to 18 months.
§   Toe walking: Normal up 3 years, after that:
o Abnormal speech > ASD
o Normal speech > Short Achillis tendon
§   Thumb sucking: Stop at 4 years.
§   Handedness: Should start at 2-4 y (before that check nerves and muscles or perinatal stroke)
§   Head banging: Normal up to 5 years.
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 44–47; UpToDate

stem:
A child is able to run well and build a tower of 4 blocks during developmental assessment.
What is the most likely age corresponding to these developmental milestones?
explanation:
By 2 years (24 months), children typically:
• Run well

93
•   Build a tower of 4–6 blocks
•   Use 2-word phrases
•   Follow 2-step commands

stem:
A child is able to hop on one foot, tell simple stories, dress and undress almost independently,
and identify several colors correctly. What is the most likely age corresponding to these
developmental milestones?
explanation:
By 4 years (48 months), children typically:
• Hop on one foot
• Tell stories
• Dress/undress with minimal assistance
• Know colors
• Speak in full understandable sentences

stem:
A child is able to pull to stand, crawl, wave bye-bye, and use an immature pincer grasp during
developmental assessment. What is the most likely age corresponding to these developmental
milestones?
explanation:
By 10 months, infants typically:
• Pull to stand
• Crawl well
• Wave bye-bye
• Develop an immature pincer grasp

stem:
A 12-month-old infant is brought for a well-child visit. The parents ask about expected
developmental milestones at this age. Which of the following milestones is most appropriate
for a 12-month-old child?
explanation:
By 12 months, infants develop social and communicative gestures such as waving
bye-bye, clapping, and pointing. They may also say 1–2 words and cruise or take first steps.
Building block towers and dressing independently occur later in toddlerhood and preschool years.



94

stem:
An 8-month-old infant is evaluated during a routine health visit. Which of the following
developmental milestones is most appropriate for this age?

options.label: A
options.text: Rolls over and sits without support

answer: A. Rolls over and sits without support
explanation:
By 8 months, most infants can roll over both ways and sit without support. They may
also transfer objects between hands and begin babbling. Running, two-word phrases, and hopping
are milestones achieved much later.
source: Osama Naga 2024, Development Table p. 138; Nelson

stem:
A 12-year-old boy is brought to clinic for a routine health visit. His BMI is above the 95th
percentile for age and sex. Physical examination reveals velvety, hyperpigmented thickening
of the skin in the axillae and neck folds. He has no other symptoms. What is this child most at
risk of developing?
explanation:
This child has acanthosis nigricans, a classic marker of insulin resistance.
Hyperinsulinemia stimulates keratinocyte and dermal fibroblast proliferation, causing the
characteristic velvety hyperpigmented plaques. Obese children with acanthosis nigricans are at
high risk for:
• Type 2 diabetes mellitus
• Metabolic syndrome
• Dyslipidemia
• Hypertension

Other options:
• A. Addison disease – Causes diffuse hyperpigmentation with hypotension and weight loss,
not velvety plaques.
• B. Cushing syndrome – Associated with obesity, striae, moon facies, hypertension.
• D. Hypothyroidism – May cause weight gain and dry skin, but not classic acanthosis
nigricans.

Clinical Pearls:
• Acanthosis nigricans = strong clue for insulin resistance
• Common locations: neck, axillae, groin
• Screen obese children for:
o Type 2 DM
o Dyslipidemia
o NAFLD
• Lifestyle modification is first-line treatment
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2895–2898; UpToDate

stem:
A 3-week-old male infant is brought to clinic because of poor urinary stream, recurrent febrile
urinary tract infections, and failure to thrive. Renal function tests show mildly elevated
creatinine. A voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) is shown below.




What is the most likely diagnosis?
96
explanation:
Posterior urethral valves (PUV) are congenital
obstructing membranous folds in the posterior urethra
occurring only in males. Although commonly diagnosed in
infancy, milder cases may present later in childhood with:
• Weak urinary stream
• Recurrent UTIs
• Enuresis
• Bladder dysfunction
• Chronic kidney disease

VCUG classically shows:
• Dilated posterior urethra
• Bladder trabeculation
• Possible vesicoureteral reflux




Other options:
• B. Vesicoureteral reflux – May coexist with PUV but does not cause posterior urethral
obstruction.
• C. UPJ obstruction – Causes hydronephrosis without lower urinary tract obstructive
symptoms.
• D. Neurogenic bladder – May cause retention/incontinence but usually associated with
neurologic abnormalities.




97
Clinical Pearls:
• PUVs are the most common cause of lower urinary tract obstruction in male
infants/children
• Complications: hydronephrosis, reflux, CKD, recurrent UTIs
• Diagnosis: VCUG
• Definitive treatment: endoscopic valve ablation
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2558–2560; Radiopedia; AMBOSS

stem:
A child with known ulcerative colitis is admitted to the hospital because of bloody diarrhea
and started on IV corticosteroids. The following day, he develops high fever, severe abdominal
pain, and progressive abdominal distension. Examination shows diffuse tenderness and
tachycardia. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
This child most likely developed toxic megacolon, a life-threatening complication of
ulcerative colitis characterized by:
• Fever
• Severe abdominal pain/distension
• Systemic toxicity
• Colonic dilation
• Patients are at high risk of perforation, sepsis and shock.
• As per UTD: “Emergency colectomy is required for patients with acute severe colitis and
uncontrolled hemorrhage or patients with complications such as toxic megacolon or
bowel perforation.”
• Colonoscopy is contraindicated because it increases perforation risk.
• While supportive measures (NG decompression, bowel rest, IV steroids/antibiotics) may be
initiated, worsening toxicity requires urgent surgical evaluation/colectomy.

UpToDate Algorithm for management of a hospitalized child or adolescent with acute severe
ulcerative colitis:



98
Other options:
• A. NGT decompression – Supportive only, not definitive in severe toxic megacolon.
• B. Colonoscopy – Contraindicated due to perforation risk.
• C. Rectal tube – Not definitive management and may worsen condition.

Clinical Pearls:
• Toxic megacolon = UC + fever + abdominal distension + systemic toxicity
• Diagnosis: abdominal X-ray showing colonic dilation (>6 cm)
• Avoid colonoscopy/barium enema
• Initial treatment:
o NPO
o IV steroids
o Broad-spectrum antibiotics
o Surgical consultation early




99
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1921–1923; UpToDate “Management of the

stem:
A 6-week-old infant is brought to the emergency department because of progressive vomiting
after every feed for the past 2 weeks. The vomiting is non-bilious and projectile. Despite
vomiting, the infant appears hungry immediately after feeds and remains active. Physical
examination shows mild dehydration and weight loss. His abdominal X-ray is shown below.




What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This is classic for hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. X-ray shows a distended stomach with
minimal distal bowel gas, reflecting gastric outlet obstruction from hypertrophied pyloric muscle.

•   Age: 2–8 weeks
•   Sex: Firstborn male
•   Vomiting: Non-bilious, projectile
o   “Hungry vomiter” / “happy spitter”
•   Exam: Olive-shaped mass, visible peristalsis
•   Labs:
o Hypochloremic, hypokalemic
o Metabolic alkalosis
•   Dx:
o Ultrasound: >3 mm thick, >15 mm length
o “String sign” on contrast if unclear
•   Tx:
o IV fluids, correct electrolytes
o Ramstedt pyloromyotomy

Other options:
• B. Intussusception – Episodic colicky pain, currant jelly stool, lethargy.
• C. Biliary atresia – Jaundice, acholic stools, hepatomegaly.
• D. GERD – Usually effortless regurgitation, not projectile vomiting with dehydration.
101
Clinical Pearls:
• Classic electrolyte abnormality: hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis
• Initial management: correct fluids/electrolytes first
• Definitive treatment: Ramstedt pyloromyotomy
• Ultrasound = diagnostic test of choice
source: Nelson, GI Disorders – Pyloric Stenosis; Osama Naga 2024, Surgery/GI, p. 588

stem:
A12-year-old presents with hypertension, occasional headache, leg cramps, weak and
delayed femoral pulses, and higher systolic BP in the upper limbs than in the lower limbs. A
chest radiograph is provided.




Which of the following is the most likely cause of these findings?
explanation:
• Classic features of coarctation of the aorta include:
o Upper limb hypertension, lower limb hypotension
o Weak/delayed femoral pulses
o Rib notching on chest X-ray due to collateral circulation
o Associated symptoms: headache, leg fatigue or cramps,
epistaxis
• Chest x-ray findings:
o Rib notching (see red arrows on xray) → Best sign in older patients
o Figure “3” sign (see blue line) → in ~50% of patients with coarct. Abnormal aortic knob
shadow from coarct and dilation of the left subclavian artery.
• Atrioventricular septal defect presents earlier with signs of heart
failure
• Mitral valve prolapse may cause a murmur but not BP differential or
rib notching
• Supravalvular aortic stenosis causes systemic hypertension but not
pulse differential or rib notching

Clinical pearls:
• Always check four-limb BPs in hypertensive children
• Most common associated anomaly: bicuspid aortic valve
• Confirm with echocardiogram or CT/MRI angiography
• Definitive management is surgical repair or balloon angioplasty
102
source: LANGE Q&A Pediatrics, Question 145

stem:
A child presents with chronic greasy stools, poor growth, recurrent infections, and fatigue. Examination
reveals short stature and pallor. Laboratory studies show pancytopenia. X-ray of the long bones
demonstrates metaphyseal dysostosis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Schwachman-Diamond syndrome is It is an inherited ribosomopathy and is the second most
common cause of pancreatic insufficiency after cystic fibrosis. It is characterized by the triad of:
• Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency → greasy stools/steatorrhea
• Bone marrow dysfunction → neutropenia or pancytopenia
• Skeletal abnormalities → metaphyseal dysostosis, short stature

Other options:
• B. Fanconi anemia – Causes pancytopenia and skeletal anomalies, but not pancreatic
insufficiency/greasy stools.
• C. Cystic fibrosis – Causes pancreatic insufficiency and recurrent infections, but metaphyseal
dysostosis is not typical.
• D. Pearson syndrome – Mitochondrial disorder with pancreatic dysfunction and sideroblastic
anemia.

Clinical Pearls:
• Shwachman-Diamond syndrome:
o Neutropenia most common
cytopenia
o Risk of MDS/AML
o Pancreatic insufficiency may
improve with age
• X-ray clue: metaphyseal dysostosis
• Management:
o Pancreatic enzyme replacement
o Nutritional support
o Hematology follow-up
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2390–2391; Pediatric OnSquares

stem:
A child with fever, cough, conjunctivitis, and a generalized maculopapular rash is diagnosed with
measles in a crowded pediatric waiting room.

105
Several unimmunized children and healthcare workers were exposed. What is the most appropriate
post-exposure management?

options.label: A
options.text: All unimmunized children older than 6 months should receive MMR vaccine

options.label: B
options.text: Give immune globulin to everyone in the waiting room

options.label: C
options.text: Give immune globulin to nurses who obtained a nasal swab

answer: A. All unimmunized children older than 6 months should receive MMR vaccine
explanation:
Measles is a highly contagious, viral infection that is most common in children. It is
characterized by fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, enanthem (Koplik's spots) on the buccal or labial
mucosa, and a maculopapular rash that spreads cephalocaudally. Diagnosis is usually clinical. Post-
exposure prophylaxis for measles depends on age, immune status, and timing:
• MMR vaccine within 72 hours of exposure can prevent or modify disease in susceptible individuals
≥6 months old.
• Immune globulin (IG) is reserved for high-risk exposed individuals:
o Infants <6 months
o Pregnant women without immunity
o Immunocompromised patients
• Healthcare workers without evidence of immunity should receive MMR vaccine, not routine IG.
• Measles (Rubeola) presents with fever, cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, and a confluent
maculopapular rash starting on the face




Other options:
• B. Give immune globulin to everyone – Incorrect; IG is only for selected high-risk individuals.
• C. Give IG to nurses – Healthcare workers should receive MMR if nonimmune.
• D. Nothing – Measles is highly contagious and requires prompt public health action.

★ Measles Post-Exposure Prophylaxis:
• MMR within 72 hrs, IG within 6 days
• ≤ 6 months: IGIM 0.5 mL/kg
• > 6 months:
106
o   6–11 months: MMR preferred; ≥12 months unvaccinated: MMR within 72 hrs
o   If exposed within 28 days from last measles vaccine: Nothing.
o   If exposed after 28 days from last measles vaccine: Give MMR vaccine (within 3 days).
•   Severe immunocompromise: IGIV 400 mg/kg
o SCID/PI, leukemia/chemo, transplant, high dose steroids (≥2 mg/kg/day for ≥14 days),
severe HIV
o If IG given → delay MMR 6 months
•   Infant receiving early MMR still needs routine 2-dose series later
•   MMR vaccine = live attenuated vaccine
•   Isolation: airborne + contact precautions → measles is one of the most contagious infections!


★ Varicella Post-Exposure Prophylaxis:
• Age <1 year:
o Give VariZIG/IVIG within 10 days if:
§ Mother exposed within 5 days before delivery OR mother or baby exposed within 2
days after delivery
§ Preterm ≥28 weeks and <34 weeks with nonimmune mother
§ Preterm <28 weeks/<1000 g regardless of maternal immunity
o If none of the above neonatal indications → observation/supportive care only
• Age ≥1 year:
o If fully vaccinated and exposure within 3 months of vaccine → nothing
o If unvaccinated/susceptible or vaccine >3 months ago → give varicella vaccine within 5
days of exposure
• VariZIG indications: immunocompromised, pregnant nonimmune patients, high-risk neonates,
certain preterm infants
• Varicella vaccine = live attenuated vaccine
• Isolation: airborne + contact precautions




Clinical Pearls:
• Measles contagious from 4 days before to 4 days after rash onset
• Classic features: cough, coryza, conjunctivitis, Koplik spots
• Vitamin A reduces morbidity in children with measles
• Airborne isolation required
• Defer live vaccines for 11 months after IVIG administration.
source: AAP Red Book 2021–2024; Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1542–1545; UpToDate

stem:
A 2-year-old boy is brought to clinic because of high fever up to 40°C (104°F) for the past 3 days. The
parents report that despite the fever, he appears playful and well between episodes. Today, the fever
suddenly resolved and he developed a faint pink maculopapular rash over the trunk, which was not
present earlier. The child is otherwise well appearing and nontoxic. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Roseola infantum (exanthem subitum), caused by HHV-6 (most commonly) or HHV-7,
classically presents with by 3 days of high fever (often up to 40°C), which disappears suddenly (rapid
defervescence) as the typical rash appears (fades within a few hours to 2 d) which is:
• Maculopapular or erythematous
• Typically beginning on the trunk and may spread to involve the neck and extremities
• Nonpruritic
• Blanches on pressure
• Child appearing relatively well between fevers

Other options:
• A. Rubella – Rash appears while fever is still present and is associated with postauricular
lymphadenopathy; typically only a 3-day illness and the rash would already be present.
• B. Erythema infectiosum (Fifth disease) – “Slapped cheek” rash; fever usually mild or absent once
rash appears.
• C. Erythema toxicum – Benign newborn rash seen in neonates only.

Clinical Pearls:
• Typical age: 6 months–2 years
• Most common complication: febrile seizures
• “Fever disappears → rash appears” = classic board clue
• Child usually looks better once rash develops




108
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1582–1583; UpToDate: “Roseola infantum (exanthem

stem:
A 28-week premature infant, now 3 weeks old, is receiving 100 kcal/kg/day from preterm formula (24
kcal/30 mL). He is being treated with diuretics and caffeine for patent ductus arteriosus. Despite
adequate feeding tolerance, his weight gain is only 5 g/day. What is the most likely cause of his poor
weight gain?

options.label: B
options.text: Preterm infants have twice the metabolic rate compared to term infants

options.label: C
options.text: Diuretics cause glucose and protein loss in the urine

explanation:
Preterm infants have significantly higher nutritional requirements than term infants. A caloric
intake of 100 kcal/kg/day is inadequate for a growing 28-week premature infant, who typically requires:
• 110–130 kcal/kg/day
• Sometimes higher if chronic lung disease or PDA is present
• Expected weight gain for preterm infants is approximately: 15–20 g/kg/day
• This infant’s gain of only 5 g/day strongly suggests insufficient caloric intake.

Other options:
• B. Preterm infants have twice the metabolic rate – They do have increased energy expenditure, but
not literally double; inadequate intake is the key issue.
• C. Diuretics cause glucose/protein loss – Not the primary mechanism of poor growth.
• D. Bradycardia/apnea require more calories – Mildly increases expenditure but would not fully
explain severe poor weight gain with suboptimal intake.

Clinical Pearls:
• Premature infants need higher:
o Calories
o Protein
o Calcium/phosphorus
• Poor growth in preterm infants:
o First assess caloric intake
o Then evaluate losses/increased expenditure
• Preterm formulas:
o Higher protein and mineral content
o Usually 22–24 kcal/oz
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 890–893;

stem:
A 6-month-old male infant presents with a history of recurrent ear infections and pneumonia. His
parents are concerned about persistent bleeding from minor injuries and frequent bruising. They also
report that he has a persistent itchy, red rash over his face and trunk that started at 2 months of age.
On examination, he has petechiae and eczema-like lesions on his cheeks and flexural areas.
Laboratory studies show thrombocytopenia with small platelets. Family history is notable for two
maternal uncles who died of severe infections in infancy. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
The clinical presentation is classic for Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome (WAS):
• Wiskott (eczema)
• Autoimmunity/infections (recurrent)
110
•   Thrombocytopenia with small platelets
•   X-linked recessive inheritance pattern with affected maternal male relatives
•   Quantitative immunoglobulin levels are the best initial test.
o IgM is usually low, while IgA and IgE are elevated → reflect the dysregulated immune
function seen early in WAS
•   Lymphocyte subsets may follow but are not the first test.
o T and B cell numbers may be normal or only mildly reduced early on
o The functional defect is more important than the absolute count
o Thus, subset analysis may miss the diagnosis initially
•   Other differentials:
o SCID would present earlier with lymphopenia and more severe opportunistic infections.
o CGD (diagnosed by oxidative burst) has normal platelets and no eczema.
o Complement disorders (diagnosed by low CH50)




Clinical pearls:
• WAS is an X-linked recessive primary immunodeficiency.
• Triad: eczema, recurrent infections, thrombocytopenia with small
platelets.
• Immunoglobulin levels are the best first-line screening test when
suspecting combined immunodeficiency with bleeding diathesis
• Labs: ↓ IgM, ↑ IgA and IgE, thrombocytopenia with small platelets
• Gene defect: WAS gene on Xp11.22
• Severe recurrent infections develop after transplacental maternal
IgG and maternal mucosal IgA are degraded around 6 months.
• Treatment: hematopoietic stem cell transplantation is curative.
• Avoid platelet transfusion unless bleeding is severe.
• Avoid live vaccines.
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 427

stem:
A 12-year-old boy with sickle cell disease presents with sudden onset difficulty walking and
weakness involving the right side of his body. His mother also reports new urinary incontinence since
this morning. On examination, he is alert and oriented. He has increased tone, hyperreflexia, and a
positive Babinski sign on the right side. Cranial nerve examination is normal. Which of the following
vessels is most likely affected?
explanation:
The anterior cerebral artery (ACA) supplies the medial frontal lobe, including the motor
cortex region responsible for lower extremity function and areas involved in micturition control. Therefore:
111
•   Infarction in the left ACA territory causes contralateral upper motor neuron weakness
•   ACA strokes may present with gait disturbance and urinary incontinence due to medial frontal
lobe involvement
•   Lesions above the corticospinal decussation produce contralateral hyperreflexia, spasticity, and
Babinski sign
•   Children with sickle cell disease are at increased risk of ischemic stroke secondary to vaso-
occlusive cerebral vasculopathy

Other options:
• B. Right anterior cerebral artery – Would cause left-sided weakness
• C/D. Middle cerebral artery lesions – More commonly cause face and upper extremity deficits,
aphasia (dominant hemisphere), or neglect (nondominant hemisphere)




Clinical Pearls:
• Cerebral hemisphere lesion → contralateral weakness
• Cerebellar lesion → ipsilateral ataxia
• Brainstem lesion may cause “crossed findings” (cranial nerve deficits + contralateral weakness)
• ACA stroke → gait disturbance, urinary incontinence, contralateral leg-predominant weakness
• MCA stroke → face/arm weakness predominance
• Medial frontal lobe involvement may impair bladder control
• Sickle cell disease is a major pediatric risk factor for ischemic stroke
• UMN signs include hyperreflexia, spasticity, and Babinski sign

Note: I believe this was the phrasing of the question, but just quickly brush up on your neuroanatomy.
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2820–2822;

stem:
A 12-hour-old newborn develops bluish discoloration of the entire body while crying, associated
with cyanosis of the extremities. The infant becomes less cyanotic when calm. Physical examination is
otherwise unremarkable, and vital signs are stable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF) is a cyanotic congenital heart disease characterized by right
ventricular outflow tract obstruction, which leads to decreased pulmonary blood flow and right-to-left
shunting. Cyanosis may worsen during episodes of crying or agitation (“tet spells”) due to increased right-
to-left shunting.
• Cyanosis that worsens with crying or agitation is characteristic of TOF
• Hypercyanotic (“tet”) spells occur due to increased right ventricular outflow obstruction
• Cyanotic episodes improve with calming or increased systemic vascular resistance

Other options:
• A. Transposition of the great arteries (TGA) typically presents with severe persistent cyanosis shortly
after birth that does not specifically worsen with crying

114
•     C. Ventricular septal defect (VSD) usually causes a left-to-right shunt and does not present with
early cyanosis
•     D. Truncus arteriosus causes cyanosis with signs of heart failure, but not classic hypercyanotic
spells triggered by crying

Clinical Pearls:
• TOF components:
o Ventricular septal defect
o Pulmonary stenosis
o Overriding aorta
o Right ventricular hypertrophy
• Tet spells are precipitated by crying, feeding, or agitation
• Squatting increases systemic vascular resistance and decreases right-to-left shunting
• Most common cyanotic congenital heart disease after infancy = TOF

★ Cyanotic Congenital Heart Disease:




Normal anatomy
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th Ed., p. 397–398; Osama Naga 3rd Ed., p. 374–375

stem: A term newborn receiving phototherapy for indirect hyperbilirubinemia has a repeat bilirubin level plotted on the nomogram, now below the treatment threshold. The infant is clinically well, term, and has no risk factors for hemolysis. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.label: B
options.text: Continue phototherapy and repeat bilirubin level

options.label: C
options.text: Discontinue phototherapy and repeat bilirubin level after 6–12 hours for rebound

options.label: D
options.text: Continue phototherapy and investigate for hemolysis Most likely answer: A. Discontinue phototherapy and discharge home IF: • Bilirubin is clearly below treatment threshold • No hemolysis risk • Infant clinically well • Reliable follow-up available High-yield rebound bilirubin pearls: • Routine rebound bilirubin checks are NOT needed in most healthy term infants • Rebound bilirubin is mainly considered if: o Hemolysis suspected (DAT+, G6PD, ABO incompatibility) o Phototherapy started <48 hrs of age o Prematurity o Bilirubin close to treatment threshold when stopping • Neurotoxicity Risk Factors o albumin < 3 g/dL o isoimmune hemolytic disease o G6PD deficiency o other hemolytic conditions o sepsis o clinical instability in previous 24 hours o (prematurity accounted for by distinct threshold curves) Always make sure you are using gestational age and risk factor specific charts! AAP phototherapy in newborns ≥35 weeks gestation with unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia in the absence of neurotoxicity risk factors (other than GA): ® Decisions to start phototherapy should be based upon TSB values; do not subtract direct (conjugated) bilirubin from the total value. ® In rare cases of severe hyperbilirubinemia in which direct (conjugated) bilirubin is >50% of the TSB, consult an expert. ® If bilirubin testing is performed with TcB, the value should be confirmed with TSB if the TcB value is >15 mg/dL (257 micromol/L) or within 3 mg/dL (51 micromol/L) of the phototherapy threshold. However, if the TcB level is at or above the treatment threshold, phototherapy should be initiated while awaiting TSB confirmation. ® Note that infants <24 hours old with a TSB at or above the phototherapy threshold are likely to have a hemolytic process and should have additional laboratory evaluation. Note: A very helpful tool for this during your time in the NICU is Bilitool; you can input and plot bilirubin, determine the risk factors to determine if phototherapy should be initiated. 116 ★ Neonatal Jaundice: 📚 Source: AAP Clinical Practice Guideline 2022: Management of Hyperbilirubinemia in the Newborn Infant ≥35 Weeks; UpToDate: “Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia in newborns ≥35 weeks of gestation: Initial management and phototherapy” ☆ NICU/Neonatology: Neonatal jaundice/hyperbilirubinemia

answer: C. Discontinue phototherapy and repeat bilirubin level after 6-12 hours for rebound

stem:
A healthy full-term newborn develops multiple erythematous macules and papules with small
central pustules over the trunk and face on day 2 of life. The infant is feeding well and has no fever. A
picture of the rash is shown below.




117
What is the best management?
explanation:
This is erythema toxicum neonatorum (ETN), a benign and self-limited neonatal rash
commonly appearing within the first few days of life. Infants are otherwise well appearing.
• Typical features:
o Erythematous macules/papules/pustules
o Face, trunk, proximal limbs
o Spares palms and soles
• No treatment is required, and the rash resolves spontaneously within days.

Other options:
• A. Oral antibiotics – Not indicated; ETN is not infectious.
• B. Topical corticosteroids – Unnecessary.
• D. Septic workup – Only if infant appears ill or febrile.

Clinical Pearls:
• Erythema toxicum neonatorum (ETN) is a very common benign newborn rash
• Appears in first 24–72 hrs of life
• Smear from pustules: eosinophils
• Palms/soles spared = classic clue

High-Yield Clinical Pearls:
• Erythema toxicum neonatorum (ETN) = eosinophils, spares palms/soles
• Transient neonatal pustular melanosis = present at birth, involves palms/soles
• Seborrheic dermatitis = greasy, nonpruritic
• Atopic dermatitis = pruritic
• Port-wine stain in V1 distribution → think Sturge-Weber
• ≥6 café-au-lait spots → evaluate for NF1
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1205–1206; Visual Dx

stem:
A child with known Hemophilia A presents with a painful swollen knee consistent with
hemarthrosis. He is treated with appropriate doses of factor VIII replacement, but the bleeding does
not improve as expected. What is the most common cause of treatment failure in this patient?
explanation:
The most common cause of poor response or refractory bleeding in a patient with Hemophilia
A receiving factor VIII therapy is the development of neutralizing antibodies (inhibitors) against factor VIII.
• High-risk patients:
o Severe hemophilia A
o Frequent factor exposure
o Family history of inhibitors
120
•   These antibodies neutralize infused factor VIII, making standard replacement ineffective.

Other options:
• A. HIV – Historically associated with factor products but does not cause acute factor resistance.
• B. Liver disease – Can worsen coagulopathy but is not the most common cause of refractory
hemarthrosis.
• D. Thrombocytopenia – May contribute to bleeding but does not explain failure of factor VIII
replacement.

Clinical Pearls:
• Hemophilia A = factor VIII deficiency
• Hemarthrosis is the classic presentation
• Confirm inhibitors with:
o Bethesda assay
• Treatment of inhibitor patients:
o Bypassing agents (rFVIIa, FEIBA)
o Emicizumab prophylaxis

★ Overview of Bleeding Disorders:
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 236–238

stem:
A child develops excessive bleeding several hours after a major surgical procedure despite normal
initial hemostasis. Laboratory evaluation shows:
• PT: 12 seconds (normal 11–14)
• aPTT: 31 seconds (normal 25–35)
• Bleeding time: normal
• Platelet count and platelet function studies: normal
Which coagulation factor deficiency is classically associated with this presentation?
explanation:
Factor XIII stabilizes fibrin by cross-linking fibrin polymers after clot formation. Deficiency leads to:

121
•   Delayed bleeding after trauma or surgery
•   Poor wound healing
•   Recurrent miscarriage
•   Umbilical stump bleeding in neonates

Because factor XIII acts after fibrin formation, routine coagulation studies are normal:
• Normal PT
• Normal aPTT
• Normal bleeding time
• Normal platelet studies

Other options:
• A. Factor V deficiency – Prolonged PT and aPTT.
• B. Factor VIII deficiency (Hemophilia A) – Prolonged aPTT.
• C. Factor VII deficiency – Prolonged PT.




Clinical Pearls:
• Normal coagulation studies + delayed bleeding = think Factor XIII deficiency
• Classic neonatal clue: delayed umbilical cord bleeding
• Diagnosis: factor XIII assay or clot solubility test
• Treatment: factor XIII concentrate or cryoprecipitate
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2381–2382;

stem:
A 6-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department after playing in the yard. She is drowsy
and has excessive lacrimation, salivation, and sweating. On examination, she has pinpoint pupils and
diffuse wheezing. What is the most likely cause of her symptoms?
explanation:
Organophosphate poisoning causes a cholinergic toxidrome due to acetylcholinesterase
inhibition and excess acetylcholine accumulation. Classic findings include salivation, lacrimation, miosis,
bronchorrhea, wheezing, bradycardia, and CNS depression.




124
•   Found in pesticides or insecticides (ex: parathion) and nerve gas → key word in vignette/Hx: farmer,
can also present in home gardeners
•   Exposure through skin, ingestion, or inhalation
•   Key word in vignette: garlic odor
•   Irreversible inhibitors of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) → ↑↑ ACh levels → ↑↑ cholinergic activity
o amplify parasympathetic NS — remember rest & digest; everything will be
contracting/secreting
§ diarrhea, drooling, incontinence
§ pinpoint pupils
§ wheezing & respiratory distress + BRADYCARDIA
o acute nicotinic toxicity includes fasciculations and muscle paralysis (also transient early
tachycardia and hypertension, especially in children).
•   The greatest danger in organophosphate poisoning is respiratory failure → patient may require
intubation & mechanical ventilation until toxidrome wears off (remember your ABCs!) → symptoms
last until the body can synthesize new AChE enzymes
o Never use succinylcholine to intubate these patients (even for RSI) → succinylcholine is
metabolized by AChE → this leads to further inhibition of AChE and will prolong the effects of
the poison
•   Antidote: ATROPINE!
o a competitive inhibitor of acetylcholine at the muscarinic receptor, which leads to the
resolution of muscarinic symptoms (eg, bradycardia, salivation)
o no effect on neuromuscular symptoms
•   Pralidoxime is a cholinesterase reactivating agent → must be given AFTER ATROPINE → causes
transient inactivation of AChE before it begins to work again
o if given alone, it could cause respiratory muscle paralysis
o improves both muscarinic and nicotinic symptoms
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 424–426; UWorld; AMBOSS

stem:
A 4-year-old girl presents with severe inspiratory stridor, fever, and a barking cough. She is
diagnosed with moderate-to-severe croup and receives one dose of nebulized racemic epinephrine and
dexamethasone. She continues to have inspiratory stridor at rest with moderate respiratory distress and
subcostal retractions. Her respiratory rate is 34/min. What is the next best step in management?
answer: B. Another dose of racemic epinephrine
explanation:
This child has persistent moderate-to-severe croup despite initial therapy. If respiratory
distress and stridor at rest continue after the first nebulized racemic epinephrine treatment, the next step is
to repeat nebulized racemic epinephrine while continuing observation.
• Dexamethasone reduces airway edema but takes several hours for maximal effect.
• Racemic epinephrine acts rapidly but its effect is temporary, so repeat dosing is often needed.
• Intubation is reserved for impending respiratory failure, exhaustion, altered mental status, or severe
hypoxemia.
• Salbutamol is ineffective because croup is an upper airway process, not bronchospasm.
• PICU admission may be needed if repeated epinephrine doses are required, but the immediate next
step is another dose.

Clinical Pearls:
• Croup = barking cough + inspiratory stridor
• Most common cause: parainfluenza virus
• First-line treatment: dexamethasone ± nebulized epinephrine
• Observe for rebound symptoms after epinephrine
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2154–2157; UWorld

stem:
A child is brought to the emergency department shortly after accidentally ingesting kerosene.
Immediately after ingestion, he developed coughing. On examination, he is alert with normal vital signs
and no respiratory distress. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
Kerosene ingestion is a hydrocarbon ingestion with high risk of aspiration pneumonitis, especially when
coughing occurs immediately after ingestion. A chest X-ray is indicated to evaluate for aspiration-related
lung injury, even if the initial examination is normal.
• Vomiting induction and gastric emptying are contraindicated because they increase aspiration risk.
• NG tube insertion is not recommended for hydrocarbon ingestion.
• Esophagoscopy is reserved for caustic ingestions, not kerosene.

Approach to ingestion of petroleum distillates and other aliphatic hydrocarbons (UpToDate):




126
Common aliphatic hydrocarbons include kerosene, mineral seal oil (furniture polish), gasoline, turpentine, naphtha (charcoal lighter fluid),
and mineral spirits. ¶ Epinephrine may precipitate catecholamine-induced ventricular arrhythmias in patients with hydrocarbon toxicity.

Clinical Pearls:
• Hydrocarbon aspiration → can cause chemical pneumonitis
• Immediate coughing/choking after ingestion = aspiration concern
• Avoid activated charcoal, emesis, and gastric lavage
• Observe symptomatic children for respiratory deterioration
• Stabilization:
o Most kerosene/hydrocarbon ingestions are mild and need supportive care only
o Intubate if: severe respiratory distress, worsening hypoxia, lethargy/coma, or inability to
protect airway
o Persistent wheeze/bronchospasm: trial albuterol
o Aromatic or halogenated hydrocarbon ingestion: ECG + cardiac monitoring (risk of
ventricular arrhythmias)
o If ventricular arrhythmias occur, follow PALS/ACLS, avoid epinephrine
o Seizures: treat with benzodiazepines (lorazepam/diazepam)
o Cholinergic symptoms (salivation, lacrimation, miosis, bronchorrhea, fasciculations)
suggest organophosphate co-ingestion → give atropine + pralidoxime
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 428–430; UpToDate: “Acute hydrocarbon exposure:

stem:
A 12-year-old child is brought to the emergency department with a 3-day history of upper
respiratory tract infection symptoms. While in the ER, he develops a generalized tonic-clonic seizure
lasting 4 minutes. After the seizure, he regains consciousness completely and has no focal neurologic
deficits on examination. What is the first investigation that should be performed?
explanation:
The first step in evaluating a child with an acute seizure is to identify reversible metabolic
causes, especially hypoglycemia and electrolyte abnormalities (Na, Ca, Mg)
• Rapid bedside glucose and serum electrolytes are essential initial investigations in any first-time
seizure or acute symptomatic seizure!
• Lumbar puncture is indicated if meningitis/encephalitis is suspected.
• EEG is useful later for epilepsy evaluation, not as the first emergency investigation.
• Brain CT is reserved for trauma, focal deficits, signs of increased ICP, or concern for intracranial
pathology.

Alternative question (Canadian Qbank QID 5483):
You see a 20-month-old male approximately 1 hour after he had a generalized seizure that lasted 2–3
minutes according to his mother. His past medical history is unremarkable except for two episodes of otitis
media. On examination, his temperature is 38.9°C (102.0°F), and he is awake, interactive, and consolable,
with obvious otitis media of the left ear. A neurologic examination is unremarkable, and there are no
meningeal signs.
Which one of the following would be most appropriate at this point?
A- Lumbar puncture
B- Electroencephalography
C- Neuroimaging studies
D- Serum levels of electrolytes, calcium, phosphate, magnesium, blood glucose, and CBC
E- No diagnostic studies at this time


127
Answer: E. No diagnostic studies at this time
Explanation: This patient has a classic simple febrile seizure:
• Age 6 months–5 years
• Generalized seizure
• Duration <15 minutes
• Single seizure in 24 hours
• Rapid return to baseline
• Normal neurologic examination
• No additional diagnostic studies are recommended in a well-appearing child with a simple febrile
seizure.

Other options (in this case):
• A. Lumbar puncture – Indicated if there are meningeal signs, suspicion of CNS infection, antibiotic
pretreatment masking meningitis, or incomplete Hib/pneumococcal immunization in infants 6–12
months.
• B. EEG – Does not predict recurrence or future epilepsy after a simple febrile seizure.
• C. Neuroimaging – Not indicated in a neurologically normal child with a simple febrile seizure.
• D. Routine blood tests – Generally unnecessary unless history suggests metabolic
abnormality/dehydration.

→ Note that the difference in these 2 cases is the age of presentation & history. A simple febrile seizure
requires no further investigations, whereas the first evaluation of new onset seizure in older age requires
identification of correctable causes (glucose, electrolytes).

Clinical Pearls:
• ABCs + bedside glucose are first priorities in seizures
• Febrile seizures occur between 6 months–5 years; this child is older → evaluate further
• Hyponatremia is a common reversible seizure cause in children
• Persistent altered mental status or meningeal signs → LP after stabilization
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 2810–2815;

stem:
A 7-year-old girl is brought to clinic because of a progressively enlarging rash on her chest for the
past 2 weeks. The lesion is mildly pruritic and began as a small red patch. Examination is shown below:




What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Tinea corporis (“ringworm”) is a superficial dermatophyte infection presenting as an annular
lesion with scaly raised border, central clearing, and mild pruritus.
• Diagnosis is usually clinical
• KOH preparation may show segmented fungal hyphae
• Skin contact, especially in warm and humid environments, is a common risk factor, and patients
often have concurrent infections in other body areas.
• Topical antifungal therapy (e.g., miconazole, terbinafine) is the first-line treatment for most
dermatophyte infections, including tinea corporis. Dermatophytes grow best in moist environments
such as public swimming pool facilities, which is where this patient works. The best initial test for
the diagnosis of dermatophyte infection is potassium hydroxide (KOH) preparation.
• Systemic antifungal therapy (e.g., oral terbinafine) is used to treat tinea capitis as well as other types
of dermatophyte infection in patients who do not respond to topical therapy, are
immunocompromised, and/or show extensive spread.




131
Other options:
• B. Atopic dermatitis – Usually poorly demarcated pruritic eczematous plaques.
• C. Psoriasis – Well-demarcated plaques with silvery scale.
• D. Impetigo – Honey-colored crusted lesions.

Clinical Pearls:
• Most common organisms: Trichophyton species
• Treatment:
o Topical azoles or terbinafine for localized disease
o Oral antifungals if extensive/scalp involvement
• Avoid topical steroids alone → may cause tinea incognito

stem:
The parents of a 5-year-old child bring him to clinic due to episodes where he stops talking mid-
sentence, stares blankly for a few seconds, then resumes talking. These episodes occur multiple
times per day. Neurologic examination is normal. EEG shows 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Absence seizures are brief episodes (usually <20 seconds) of impaired awareness, often
with staring, speech arrest, or automatisms
• Classic EEG finding: 3-Hz generalized spike-and-wave discharges
• Onset is typically between 4–10 years of age
• Distinct from daydreaming by sudden onset/offset, lack of awareness, and inability to be
interrupted
• No post ictal phase
• Can have simple automatisms → lip smacking, eye fluttering, head nodding
• Can be induced by hyperventilation
• EEG show classic 3-Hz spike-wave pattern.




•     Tx:
o   1st line = ethosuximide (Zarontin)
§ Mechanism: blocks T-type calcium channels in thalamic neurons.
§ Highly effective with fewer side effects compared to alternatives.
o   2nd line or if associated with generalized seizures = sodium valproate (Depakene)
o   3rd line = lamotrigine

Clinical pearls:
• Differentiate from focal or complex partial seizures which last longer and may have postictal
confusion
• Normal development between episodes
