stem:
A 9-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after falling backward while skateboarding.
He did not lose consciousness and is alert and oriented. There is no vomiting or seizure. On physical
145
examination, he has bruising over the mastoid area and blood behind the right tympanic membrane. No
focal neurologic deficits are noted. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Basilar skull fracture involves the bones at the base of the skull (e.g., petrous portion of
temporal bone)
• Key signs include:
o Hemotympanum (blood behind tympanic membrane)
o Battle sign (postauricular ecchymosis), raccoon eyes, CSF rhinorrhea or otorrhea
o May have cranial nerve deficits (CN VII palsy, hearing loss)
o CSF otorrhea
• Halo sign: Blood-tinged fluid from nose/ear forms a central red spot with a clear outer ring on
gauze, suggesting a CSF leak, typically from a basilar skull fracture.
• Even in absence of LOC or vomiting, hemotympanum in this setting is highly suggestive of basilar
skull fracture
• CT head is the preferred diagnostic modality
• Requires monitoring for CSF leaks and complications like meningitis




Clinical pearls:
• Avoid placing NG/ET tubes blindly in suspected basilar skull fracture
• Admit for observation even if GCS is normal if signs of basilar fracture are present
• Neurosurgery consultation may be warranted
• Monitor for CSF leaks and signs of meningitis
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 307–308; LANGE Q&A Pediatrics, 1st ed., p. 287

stem:
A 5-year-old girl presents to the emergency department with a chief complaint of swelling around
the eyes and ankles. You note the patient to have pitting edema up to the knees bilaterally and peri-
orbital edema with no erythema. You astutely send off a urine analysis and find that the patient has 4+
protein in the urine without signs of infection or red blood cells, and urine protein of 60 mg/m2/hr.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Classic: sudden edema + heavy proteinuria + bland urine sediment
• Most common in preschool children (2–6 years)
• Diagnosis often clinical; renal biopsy if atypical
• MCNS is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (80–90%), especially 2–6 yrs.
• Presents with: sudden-onset edema, heavy proteinuria (>40 mg/m²/hr), hypoalbuminemia.
• NO hematuria, HTN, or impaired renal function at onset.
• Typically responds rapidly to corticosteroids.




150
source: Nelson, Nephrology Ch. 474; Osama Naga 2024 p. 523–525; UWorld.

stem:
A 6-day-old newborn is brought for jaundice, poor feeding, vomiting, and lethargy. Examination
reveals hepatosplenomegaly, cataracts, and bleeding manifestations. Urine testing is positive for
reducing substances. Which organism is the most important cause of sepsis in this condition?
explanation:
• Galactosemia typically presents in the first weeks of life with:
o Lethargy, poor feeding, vomiting
o Jaundice, hepatomegaly, coagulopathy, sepsis (especially E. coli)
• Infants with galactosemia are particularly susceptible to severe E. coli sepsis, which is a classic
board association.
• A family history of sibling death may raise suspicion for an inborn error of metabolism
(autosomal recessive)

★ Type 1: Classic Galactosemia:


•       Definition: Autosomal recessive disorder caused by galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase
(GALT) deficiency
® Result: Accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate → toxic to liver, kidneys, brain
•       Onset: First few days to weeks of life after lactose ingestion (breast milk
or formula)
o Poor feeding, vomiting, lethargy, jaundice, hepatomegaly
o E. coli sepsis is a classic and life-threatening complication
o Hypoglycemia, coagulopathy, failure to thrive, cataracts
•       Diagnosis:
o Newborn screen
o Confirm with GALT enzyme assay or galactose-1-phosphate
o Positive urine reducing substances (non-glucose)
•       Management:
o Stop breast milk/formula (lactose-free/soy formula)
o Lifelong galactose restriction
•       Complications:
o Intellectual disability, speech delay
o Ovarian failure (in females)
o Liver dysfunction
o Sepsis (E. coli) – early and deadly

Type II – Galactokinase deficiency:
• Causes isolated cataracts in infancy without liver or kidney involvement Mnemonic: “Fruits from the Galapagos are
kind” (galactokinase and fructokinase
• Managed with dietary galactose restriction                               deficiencies present with mild symptoms).


Type III – UDP-galactose-4-epimerase deficiency:

152
•   Benign form is asymptomatic; severe form mimics classic galactosemia
•   Diagnosis via enzyme assay; treat severe form with galactose restriction

Clinical pearls:
• Galactosemia is diagnosed by measuring GALT enzyme activity or galactose-1-phosphate levels
• Classic neonatal association: E. coli sepsis
• Cataracts result from galactitol accumulation
• Treatment: immediate elimination of lactose/galactose from diet
• Early diagnosis prevents liver failure, sepsis, and death
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 241–243; UWorld

stem:
A full-term newborn has not passed stool for the first 36 hours of life. Physical examination is
otherwise normal. After a rectal examination, the infant passes a small meconium plug with temporary
relief of abdominal distension. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Delayed passage of meconium (>48 hours), abdominal distension, and explosive passage of
stool after rectal examination are classic clues for Hirschsprung disease. Rectal stimulation may
temporarily relieve obstruction.
• Meconium ileus is more strongly associated with cystic fibrosis and typically presents with thick,
inspissated meconium causing complete obstruction.
• The “squirt sign” after rectal exam is high yield for Hirschsprung disease.

Other options:
• B. Cystic fibrosis – Associated with meconium ileus, not classic explosive stool after rectal exam.
• C. Meconium ileus – Usually presents with marked obstruction and thick meconium.
• D. Intestinal atresia – Typically causes bilious vomiting and persistent obstruction.

Clinical Pearls:
• Hirschsprung = absent ganglion cells in distal colon
• Diagnosis: rectal suction biopsy
• Contrast enema: transition zone
• Complication: Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis
source: Nelson Textbook of Pediatrics, 21st ed., p. 1888–1891;

stem:
A 6-year-old previously healthy girl is brought to the emergency department with a 2-week history
of increased thirst, frequent urination, and daytime fatigue. Her mother also reports noticeable
weight loss over the last month. Today, the child has been more lethargic, and her breathing appears
deep and rapid. On exam, she is tachycardic, and dehydrated. What is the most appropriate next step
in management?

154
explanation:
• Suspected new-onset type 1 diabetes mellitus
• Best rapid test = check for glucosuria + ketonuria
• If positive, move to full labs, electrolytes, and ABG

★ Diabetic Ketoacidosis: (very high yield for wards & boards)
• Approximately 25% of children with T1DM present in diabetic
ketoacidosis (DKA).
• Pathophysiology: Triggered by inadequate insulin/increased insulin
need (stress from infection, steroids, etc.)
o ↓ insulin → glycogenolysis + gluconeogenesis + lipolysis
o ↑ glucose → polyuria → dehydration
o ↑ lipolysis leads to creation of ketone bodies, if ↑↑ → ↓ pH → electrolyte abnormalities
• Patients with DKA present initially with polyuria, polydipsia, nausea, and vomiting.
o Abdominal pain occurs frequently and can mimic an acute abdomen.
o The abdomen may be tender from vomiting or distended secondary to a paralytic ileus.
• Diagnosis: Glucose >200 mg/dL (11.1 mmol/L); Moderate–large ketones in urine,
betahydroxybutyrate in blood; AGAP metabolic acidosis

General principles of DKA Management:
• Initial labs: glucose, VBG, Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, HCO₃⁻, BUN, Cr, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, phosphate, urinalysis (ketones)
• Monitor: neuro status, vitals hourly, glucose q1hr & electrolytes q2–4h
• Start with fluids — insulin comes after volume resuscitation begins
o 1st start w/ a NS bolus 10 mg/kg
o 2nd continuous IV fluids at 1.5–2× maintenance (to rehydrate and avoid dehydration)
§ DO NOT USE HYPOTONIC FLUIDS – cerebral edema risk
§ ↑ dextrose-containing fluids once blood glucose <300 and ↓ nondextrose fluids
• Insulin infusion – Regular insulin (0.1 u/kg/hr) – start 1 hr after initial NS bolus
o Goal to ↓ glucose by 50–100 mg/dL/hr (2.8–5.6 mmol/L/hr)
• At risk of initial hypokalemia and hypophosphatemia 2º to urine losses, hypernatremia 2º to dehydration
(may present as pseudohyponatremia due to hyperglycemia)
o insulin shifts K⁺ and PO4 shift → hypokalemia & hypophosphatemia risk [total body depletion]
o K added to IV fluids [typically 20 mmol/L potassium phosphate + 20 mmol/L potassium chloride or
acetate]
o PO4 replaced only if symptomatic (e.g., muscle weakness, arrhythmia, hemolysis)
• Avoid bicarbonate unless pH <6.9 with life-threatening instability – risk of cerebral edema

Mild DKA: pH 7.2 – <7.3, HCO3 10 – <15     Moderate DKA: pH 7.1 – <7.2, HCO3 5 – <10     Severe DKA: pH <7.1, HCO3 <5
Usually well-appearing, alert              More dehydration, possible lethargy           High risk for cerebral edema, may be
Fluids:                                    Fluids:                                       obtunded or confused
-    10–20 mL/kg NS over 1–2 hr            -    10–20 mL/kg NS over 1–2 hr               Fluids:
-    Maintenance + deficit (5%) over 36–   -    Maintenance + 7–8% deficit over 48 hrs   -    10–20 mL/kg NS over 1–2 hr
48 hrs                                Insulin:                                      -    Replace 10% deficit over 48 hrs,
Insulin:                                   -    Start 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr IV regular         using 0.9% or 0.45% NaCl
-    Start 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr IV              insulin 1 hour after fluids                   depending on corrected Na⁺
regular insulin 1 hour after fluids   Potassium: Monitor K⁺ closely — total body    Insulin:
Potassium: Add once urine output           K⁺ is low even if serum is normal             -    Start 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr IV
confirmed                                  Glucose: Add D5% when BG < 250–300                 regular insulin 1 hour after fluids
Glucose: Add D5% when BG < 250–300         mg/dL (13.9–16.7 mmol/L)                      Potassium: Monitor K⁺ closely — total
mg/dL (13.9–16.7 mmol/L)                                                                 body K⁺ is low even if serum is normal
Glucose: Add D5% when BG < 250–
300 mg/dL (13.9–16.7 mmol/L)
Avoid bicarbonate unless pH <6.9
with cardiac instability or coma

155
Complications:
• Cerebral edema (0.5% to 1%)
o Signs/Sx: Headache, incontinence, altered mental status, emesis, hypertension, bradycardia, irregular
respirations
o Risk factors: age <5 yo, severe acidosis (pH <7.1, bicarb <5), severe dehydration (elevated BUN), severe
hyperosmolarity (corrected Na >155, glucose >1,000mg/dL [55.5 mmol/L]), longer duration of sx, new-onset
diabetes, aggressive initial fluid resuscitation, treatment with bicarbonate
o Management: Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg intravenously over 20 min or hypertonic saline (3% NS) 5–10 mL/kg over 30
min
§ repeated if there is no response in 30 minutes
o Respiratory compromise – Rare respiratory failure
• Electrolyte abnormalities
o Cardiac dysrhythmias
o Rapid correction has risk for CNS fluid shifts

When is the patient considered out of DKA?                      Next steps:
• Venous pH > 7.3                                                • Start subcutaneous insulin once DKA has
• Bicarbonate ≥ 15 mEq/L                                              resolved and the child is eating
• No ketonuria or ketonemia                                      • Give SC insulin 30–60 minutes before stopping
• Alert mental status                                                 the insulin drip to avoid rebound hyperglycemia
• Hemodynamically stable and well hydrated
• Tolerating oral intake
Clinical pearls:
• Always start DKA management with isotonic fluid bolus (10–20 mL/kg) based on severity
• Do not give bicarbonate unless pH <6.9 with life-threatening symptoms (e.g., arrhythmia)
• Replace deficit + maintenance over 36–48 hours; add insulin only after 1–2 hours of fluids
• Monitor glucose, electrolytes, pH, and mental status closely for cerebral edema

DKA mnemonic: The most important findings of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) are: Delirium/psychosis,
Dehydration, Kussmaul respirations, Abdominal pain/nausea/vomiting, fruity (Acetone) breath odor.
source: Nelson – Diabetes Mellitus; Osama Naga 2024 – Endocrinology

stem:
A 14-year-old boy is brought to clinic due to concerns about short stature. His height is below the
5th percentile, and he has no signs of pubertal development (Tanner stage l). His growth velocity
156
over the past year has been appropriate. The father mentions that he himself was also a "late bloomer"
and didn't reach his adult height until late adolescence. Examination is otherwise normal. Bone age is
consistent with a 12-year-old. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
• Family history + delayed puberty + delayed bone age = constitutional delay, not pathological
• Height typically catches up by late adolescence
• No treatment required unless psychosocial concern

Board Clues:
• Bone age is the key to narrow the cause:
o Normal bone age = likely familial
o Delayed bone age = constitutional, endocrine, systemic
• Normal growth velocity = variant of normal
• ↓ growth velocity = always pathologic
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, Endocrinology, Short stature

stem:
A 5-year-old boy is brought to clinic for progressive obesity despite parental attempts at dietary
restriction. His parents report that he is constantly asking for food and becomes irritable when meals
are limited. He has a history of neonatal hypotonia and poor feeding during infancy. On examination,
he has short stature, small hands and feet, hypotonia, and developmental delay. Which of the
following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) is a genetic imprinting disorder caused by loss of paternally
expressed genes on chromosome 15q11-q13. The syndrome classically presents with:
• Neonatal hypotonia and poor feeding during infancy
• Followed later by hyperphagia and severe obesity in early childhood
• Associated findings include developmental delay, short stature, hypogonadism, and small
hands/feet
• Behavioral problems and food-seeking behaviors are common

Other options:




158
•   Bardet-Biedl syndrome is associated with obesity, but also classically causes retinitis pigmentosa,
polydactyly, and renal anomalies
•   Congenital hypothyroidism causes hypotonia and
developmental delay, but not the characteristic
hyperphagia with food-seeking behavior
•   Cushing syndrome causes obesity and growth
failure, but patients typically have moon facies,
hypertension, violaceous striae, and proximal
muscle weakness

Clinical Pearls:
• Prader-Willi syndrome = neonatal hypotonia →
later hyperphagia and obesity
• Mnemonic: “P.R.A.D.E.R.”
Paternal (genes not imprinted)
Runt (short)
Almond eyes
Delay (mental)
Eats a lot
Right heart failure
• Caused by loss of paternal chromosome 15q11-
q13 expression
• Major complications include morbid obesity and
type 2 diabetes mellitus
• Angelman syndrome involves the same
chromosomal region but results from loss of the
maternal allele
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, PedsCases

stem:
A 6-week-old boy is brought to the emergency department for painful swelling of the right thigh.
His parents report that he became irritable after he fell while being with brother. On examination, the
infant cries with movement of the left leg. Radiologic imaging is shown below.




160
Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of injury?

options.label: B
options.text: Injury caused accidentally by a sibling

answer: A. Twisting injury to the leg
explanation:
A spiral fracture results from a rotational (torsional) force applied along the axis of a long
bone. In a non-ambulatory infant, a femoral spiral fracture is highly concerning for non-accidental trauma
(child abuse) because the infant is developmentally unable to generate sufficient force independently.
Therefore:
• Spiral fractures are classically caused by twisting mechanisms
• A non-ambulatory infant with a long bone fracture should raise strong suspicion for abuse!
• Rolling over or routine handling does not generate enough force to cause a femoral spiral fracture in
a young infant




161
Clinical Pearls:
• Spiral fracture → rotational/twisting force
• Any long bone fracture in a non-ambulatory infant should prompt evaluation for non-accidental
trauma
• Concerning features for abuse include:
o Multiple fractures in different stages of healing
o Rib fractures
o Metaphyseal “corner” fractures
o Delay in seeking medical care
• Early recognition and reporting are essential to protect the child
source: Nelson Essentials of Pediatrics, 7th ed., p. 94–95
